topics.title: 1D Signals: A General Introduction to Real-Time Signals - CT and DT Signals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST416 - SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** 1D Signals: A General Introduction to Real-Time Signals - CT and DT Signals ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 What is a Signal? In the formal language of **Signals and Systems**, a *signal* is any physical quantity that varies with one or more independent variables and contains information about the behavior or nature of a phenomenon. Mathematically, a signal is described as a **function of one or more independent variables**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Module 1 Anchor Concept):** > A signal is a mathematical description of a physical phenomenon expressed as a function of one or more independent variables (most commonly **time**). **Independent Variable:** The variable upon which the signal depends. For 1-D signals, this is overwhelmingly **time `t`** (continuous) or **sample index `n`** (discrete). **Dependent Variable:** The magnitude or value of the physical quantity being observed (e.g., voltage, pressure, temperature). ### 1.2 What is a 1-D Signal? A **One-Dimensional (1-D) signal** is a signal that is a function of a **single independent variable**. $$x = f(t) \quad \text{or} \quad x = f(n)$$ > [!NOTE] > A 1-D signal carries information along **one axis only**. The amplitude (vertical axis) varies as a function of a single variable (horizontal axis — typically time). **Real-World Examples of 1-D Signals:** - A speech waveform recorded by a microphone (amplitude vs. time) - A patient's ECG voltage trace - The daily closing price of a stock - The temperature of a city measured every hour - The output voltage of an amplifier **Comparison with Higher-Dimensional Signals:** | Dimension | Independent Variables | Example | | :--- | :---: | :--- | | 1-D | 1 | Audio signal `x(t)` | | 2-D | 2 | Still image `I(x, y)` | | 3-D | 3 | Video frame sequence `V(x, y, t)` | | 4-D | 4 | Volumetric medical scan over time | ### 1.3 Real-Time Signals — Conceptual Meaning A **real-time signal** is a signal whose values are generated, sampled, or processed **synchronously with the actual physical occurrence of the underlying phenomenon**, with negligible delay. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Module 1 Insight:** > A real-time signal is not just a "signal that exists" — it is a signal whose generation, transmission, and processing must satisfy strict timing constraints (deadlines) imposed by the physical world. ### 1.4 Intuitive Analogy — "The Heartbeat Monitor" 🫀 Imagine a patient connected to a heart monitor in an ICU: - The **physical phenomenon** is the beating of the heart. - The **transducer** (sensor) converts heart contractions into a voltage. - The **signal** `x(t)` is the voltage waveform displayed on the monitor. - This is a **1-D signal** because amplitude varies as a function of **one variable — time**. - It is **real-time** because every new sample of the waveform must be displayed within milliseconds — any delay could mean missing a cardiac event. - The signal is **continuous-time (analog)** at the sensor output, but becomes **discrete-time (digital)** after the Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC) samples it at regular intervals (e.g., 250 Hz). > [!NOTE] > **Key Takeaway from the Analogy:** A real-world signal is almost always **continuous in nature**, but as soon as a digital processor (DSP, microcontroller) touches it, it becomes **discrete-time**. The branch of engineering that bridges this gap is precisely *Signals and Systems*. ### 1.5 Continuous-Time (CT) vs. Discrete-Time (DT) Signals — The Foundational Classification The very first classification KTU demands in Module 1 is based on whether the independent variable is **continuous** or **discrete**. #### A. Continuous-Time (CT) Signals — `x(t)` A signal defined for **every instant of time** in some interval (or across the entire real line) is a continuous-time signal. $$x(t), \quad t \in \mathbb{R}$$ The independent variable `t` takes **any real value**, not just specific points. **Examples:** - Voltage across a capacitor in a live RC circuit - Speech waveform from a microphone - Velocity of a car measured by an analog speedometer > [!IMPORTANT] > **Do not confuse** *continuous-time* with *continuous-amplitude*. A CT signal can still have discrete amplitude values (e.g., a square wave toggles between `+V` and `0V` but is still continuous in time). #### B. Discrete-Time (DT) Signals — `x[n]` A signal defined **only at discrete instants of time** is a discrete-time signal. $$x[n], \quad n \in \mathbb{Z}$$ The independent variable `n` is an **integer index**, not a continuous real number. **Examples:** - Daily stock closing price — `x[0], x[1], x[2], \dots` - Digital audio stored in a `.wav` file (sampled at 44,100 Hz) - Pixel intensity values along a single horizontal scan-line of an image > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pitfall Avoidance:** The notation `x[n]` is **defined only at integer values of n**. Writing `x[2.5]` is mathematically meaningless unless the signal is being interpolated (which is a separate operation). ### 1.6 The Sampling Bridge — How CT becomes DT The most common way a CT signal becomes a DT signal is via **sampling** at a uniform rate `Fs` (samples per second), with sampling period `Ts = 1/Fs`. $$x[n] = x(nT_s) = x(t)\Big\vert_{t = nT_s}$$ > [!NOTE] > This equation is one of the **highest-weight formulas in KTU Module 1 and Module 2** (Sampling Theorem). The DT signal is essentially a sequence of *samples* extracted from the CT signal at regular intervals `Ts`. **Real-time example:** A digital thermometer samples the ambient air temperature once every minute — the temperature varies continuously in physics, but the device stores it as `T[0], T[1], T[2], \dots` — a DT signal. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Mathematical Representation of Signals #### A. Continuous-Time Signal A CT signal `x(t)` is a function: $$x : \mathbb{R} \rightarrow \mathbb{R} \quad (\text{or } \mathbb{C})$$ - Domain: All real numbers `t` (the independent variable). - Codomain: A real or complex value (the dependent variable, i.e., amplitude). **Functional Forms:** $$x(t) = A \sin(2\pi f t + \phi) \quad \text{(sinusoidal)}$$ $$x(t) = e^{-at} \cdot u(t) \quad \text{(decaying exponential)}$$ $$x(t) = \begin{cases} 1, & t \geq 0 \\ 0, & t < 0 \end{cases} \quad \text{(unit step)}$$ #### B. Discrete-Time Signal A DT signal `x[n]` is a sequence: $$x = \{\dots, x[-2], x[-1], x[0], x[1], x[2], \dots\}$$ - Domain: Integers `n ∈ ℤ`. - Codomain: Real or complex amplitude. **Explicit Notation:** $$x[n] = A \cos(\omega_0 n + \phi) \quad \text{(DT sinusoid)}$$ $$x[n] = a^n u[n] \quad \text{(DT exponential)}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > In the DT sinusoidal `cos(ω₀n + φ)`, the variable `ω₀` is a **normalized digital frequency** in **radians per sample**, **NOT** radians per second. This is a frequent source of confusion in KTU exams. ### 2.2 Independent Variable Classification (The Module-1 Decision Tree) ``` SIGNAL ├── Continuous-Time (CT) ── x(t), t ∈ ℝ │ ├── Continuous-Amplitude & Continuous-Time → Analog │ └── Discrete-Amplitude & Continuous-Time → Quantized CT │ └── Discrete-Time (DT) ── x[n], n ∈ ℤ ├── Continuous-Amplitude & Discrete-Time → Sampled signal └── Discrete-Amplitude & Discrete-Time → Digital signal ``` > [!NOTE] > **KTU Definition Anchor:** A signal that is **discrete in time AND discrete in amplitude** is called a **digital signal**. A signal that is **discrete in time but continuous in amplitude** is called a **sampled-data signal**. ### 2.3 Operations on 1-D Signals These are the **transformations that KTU Module 1** expects every student to be fluent in. | Operation | CT Form | DT Form | Effect | | :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- | | Time Shifting | `x(t − t₀)` | `x[n − n₀]` | Right-shift by `t₀` / `n₀` (delay) | | Time Scaling | `x(at)` | `x[kn]` | Compress (`a>1`) or expand (`a<1`) | | Time Reversal | `x(−t)` | `x[−n]` | Mirror about vertical axis | | Amplitude Scaling | `A·x(t)` | `A·x[n]` | Multiply amplitude by `A` | | Addition | `x(t) + y(t)` | `x[n] + y[n]` | Sum of two signals | | Multiplication | `x(t)·y(t)` | `x[n]·y[n]` | Point-wise product | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Combined Operation Rule (Examiner's Favorite):** > `x(−at + b)` is performed **right-to-left**: > 1. Shift right by `b/a` (replace `t` with `t − b/a`). > 2. Scale by `a` (replace `t` with `at`). > 3. Reverse (replace `t` with `−t`). ### 2.4 Elementary Signals — The "Building Blocks" These are the **standard CT and DT signals** that must be memorized for KTU 2024 exams. #### Unit Step Function $$u(t) = \begin{cases} 1, & t \geq 0 \\ 0, & t < 0 \end{cases}$$ $$u[n] = \begin{cases} 1, & n \geq 0 \\ 0, & n < 0 \end{cases}$$ #### Unit Impulse Function $$\delta(t) = \begin{cases} \infty, & t = 0 \\ 0, & t \neq 0 \end{cases} \quad \text{and} \quad \int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\delta(t)\,dt = 1$$ $$\delta[n] = \begin{cases} 1, & n = 0 \\ 0, & n \neq 0 \end{cases}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Critical Difference:** `δ(t)` is a **distribution** (not a regular function); `δ[n]` is a perfectly well-defined real number equal to `1` at `n=0` and `0` elsewhere. #### Unit Ramp Function $$r(t) = t \cdot u(t) = \begin{cases} t, & t \geq 0 \\ 0, & t < 0 \end{cases}$$ $$r[n] = n \cdot u[n]$$ #### Exponential Signal $$x(t) = e^{at} \quad \text{or} \quad x(t) = A e^{at}$$ $$x[n] = a^n$$ #### Sinusoidal Signal $$x(t) = A\sin(\omega t + \phi) \quad \text{or} \quad A\cos(\omega t + \phi)$$ $$x[n] = A\sin(\Omega n + \phi) \quad \text{or} \quad A\cos(\Omega n + \phi)$$ #### Complex Exponential $$x(t) = e^{j\omega t}$$ $$x[n] = e^{j\Omega n}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why `j`?** Using Euler's identity, `e^{jωt} = cos(ωt) + j·sin(ωt)`. This single complex form **simultaneously represents both sine and cosine** and is the **mathematical foundation of Fourier Analysis** (Module 2 onward). ### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Module 1 — CT and DT Signals) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master this table** — every formula here is examinable in either Part A (3 marks) or Part B (14 marks) of the End Semester Examination. | # | Concept | Continuous-Time `x(t)` | Discrete-Time `x[n]` | Units / Notes | | :---: | :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- | | 1 | Independent Variable | `t` | `n` | `t ∈ ℝ` ; `n ∈ ℤ` | | 2 | Sampling Relation | — | `x[n] = x(n·T_s)` | `T_s` in seconds | | 3 | Sampling Frequency | — | `F_s = 1/T_s` | Hz (samples/sec) | | 4 | Periodicity | `x(t+T) = x(t)` | `x[n+N] = x[n]` | `T, N > 0` | | 5 | Fundamental Period (sinusoid) | `T = 2π/ω` | `N = 2π·m/Ω` | `m, N ∈ ℤ` | | 6 | Energy | `\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t)\vert^2 dt` | `\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n]\vert^2` | Joules (normalized) | | 7 | Power | `\lim_{T\to\infty}\frac{1}{2T}\int_{-T}^{T}\vert x(t)\vert^2 dt` | `\lim_{N\to\infty}\frac{1}{2N+1}\sum_{n=-N}^{N}\vert x[n]\vert^2` | Watts (normalized) | | 8 | Unit Step | `u(t) = 1, t≥0` | `u[n] = 1, n≥0` | Discontinuous at `0` | | 9 | Unit Impulse | `δ(t)`, area = `1` | `δ[n] = 1` at `n=0` | CT: distribution | | 10 | Time Shift | `x(t − t₀)` | `x[n − n₀]` | Right = delay | | 11 | Time Reversal | `x(−t)` | `x[−n]` | Mirror flip | | 12 | Euler's Identity | `e^{jωt} = cos(ωt) + j·sin(ωt)` | `e^{jΩn} = cos(Ωn) + j·sin(Ωn)` | Foundation of Fourier | | 13 | Sampling Theorem (Nyquist) | `F_s ≥ 2·F_{max}` | — | Anti-aliasing condition | | 14 | Even Signal | `x(−t) = x(t)` | `x[−n] = x[n]` | Symmetric about 0 | | 15 | Odd Signal | `x(−t) = −x(t)` | `x[−n] = −x[n]` | Anti-symmetric about 0 | ### 2.6 Real-Time Engineering Utility - **Telecommunications:** Speech and data signals (CT at the antenna, DT inside the baseband processor). - **Biomedical Instrumentation:** ECG, EEG, EMG — all are real-time 1-D signals monitored in ICUs. - **Radar and Sonar:** Echo signals are CT at the receiver, but converted to DT for matched-filter processing. - **Audio and Music Production:** Microphones produce CT, mixers and DAWs (Digital Audio Workstations) work in DT. - **Control Systems:** Plant outputs are typically continuous (CT), but the controller (microcontroller) operates in DT. - **Financial Engineering:** Stock price ticks are inherently DT (irregularly spaced in time), but often resampled to a uniform DT stream. > [!NOTE] > **Why study BOTH CT and DT in the same course?** Because **every practical engineering system involves both**: the physical world is fundamentally CT, while the computational engine that processes it is fundamentally DT. Signals and Systems is the **mathematical bridge** that connects them. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation — The Sampling Equation **Goal:** Show that a DT signal `x[n]` is mathematically equivalent to a CT signal `x(t)` evaluated at discrete instants `t = nT_s`. **Step 1 — Start with a continuous-time signal.** A microphone outputs a CT voltage signal `x(t)` where `t` is a continuous real variable. $$x(t), \quad t \in \mathbb{R}$$ **Step 2 — Define a uniform sampling grid.** Choose a sampling period `T_s > 0` (in seconds). The grid of sampling instants is: $$t_n = n \cdot T_s, \quad n \in \mathbb{Z}$$ This grid is **uniform**: the spacing between any two consecutive samples is exactly `T_s`. **Step 3 — Extract values of `x(t)` at the grid points.** The DT signal is defined as the **restriction** of `x(t)` to the discrete grid: $$x[n] = x(t)\Big\vert_{t = nT_s} = x(nT_s)$$ **Step 4 — State the inverse relationship.** The sampling frequency is the reciprocal of the sampling period: $$F_s = \frac{1}{T_s}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Module-1 Exam Note:** KTU examiners frequently ask: *"A signal `x(t) = sin(20πt)` is sampled at `F_s = 100` Hz. Write the resulting discrete-time signal."* The answer is: > `T_s = 1/100 = 0.01 s`, so `x[n] = sin(20π·n·0.01) = sin(0.2πn)`. ### 3.2 Derivation — Relationship Between CT and DT Sinusoidal Frequencies **Goal:** Show how the digital frequency `Ω` of a DT sinusoid relates to the analog frequency `f` of its parent CT sinusoid. **Step 1 — Start with the CT sinusoid.** $$x(t) = A \sin(2\pi f t + \phi)$$ **Step 2 — Sample it at `F_s = 1/T_s` Hz.** Substitute `t = nT_s`: $$x[n] = A \sin\!\left(2\pi f \cdot nT_s + \phi\right)$$ **Step 3 — Recognize the inner argument as the DT digital frequency.** Define: $$\Omega = 2\pi f T_s = 2\pi \frac{f}{F_s}$$ **Step 4 — Rewrite in canonical DT form:** $$x[n] = A \sin(\Omega n + \phi)$$ **Step 5 — Express `Ω` in radians per sample:** $$\Omega = 2\pi \frac{f}{F_s} \quad \text{[radians per sample]}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Fundamental range of `Ω`:** Because `x[n]` only "sees" frequencies up to `F_s/2` (Nyquist limit), the equivalent range for `Ω` is: > `−π < Ω ≤ π` > A DT sinusoid with `Ω` outside this range **aliases** to an equivalent lower frequency. ### 3.3 Derivation — Periodicity Conditions for CT and DT Sinusoids **Goal:** Determine the conditions under which a sinusoidal signal is periodic, and compute its fundamental period. #### A. Continuous-Time Case A CT sinusoid `x(t) = A sin(ω₀t + φ)` is **periodic** if there exists `T > 0` such that: $$x(t + T) = A\sin(\omega_0(t+T) + \phi) = A\sin(\omega_0 t + \phi) = x(t)$$ This requires: $$\omega_0 T = 2\pi k, \quad k \in \mathbb{Z}$$ The **smallest positive `T`** (fundamental period) is obtained with `k = 1`: $$\boxed{T = \frac{2\pi}{\omega_0}}$$ > [!NOTE] > A CT sinusoid is **always periodic** for any `ω₀ > 0`. The fundamental period `T` is a real number and can be any positive real. #### B. Discrete-Time Case A DT sinusoid `x[n] = A sin(Ω₀n + φ)` is periodic only if there exists integer `N > 0` such that: $$x[n + N] = A\sin(\Omega_0(n+N) + \phi) = A\sin(\Omega_0 n + \phi) = x[n]$$ This requires: $$\Omega_0 N = 2\pi m, \quad m \in \mathbb{Z}$$ Solving for `N`: $$N = \frac{2\pi m}{\Omega_0}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Critical Insight:** A DT sinusoid is periodic **only if `2π/Ω₀` is a rational number** (i.e., `2π/Ω₀ = p/q` for integers `p, q`). Otherwise, the signal is **aperiodic**. > > Example: `x[n] = sin(n)` is **NOT periodic** because `2π/1` is irrational. The **fundamental period** is the **smallest positive integer `N`** satisfying the above. This is found by choosing the smallest integer `m` such that `N = 2πm/Ω₀` is a positive integer. ### 3.4 Derivation — Time Reversal and Time Shifting of a DT Signal **Goal:** Given `x[n] = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5}` where `x[0] = 3` (the middle element), derive `y[n] = x[−n + 2]`. **Step 1 — Identify the operation order.** The expression `x[−n + 2]` is read right-to-left: 1. **Shift** by `+2`: replace `n` with `n − 2` → `x[n − 2]` 2. **Reverse**: replace `n` with `−n` → `x[−n − 2]` Wait — let us carefully apply the standard rule. The expression `x[−n + 2]` means substituting `n` with `(−n + 2)`. So: $$y[n] = x[-n + 2]$$ **Step 2 — Compute `y[n]` for a few values of `n`.** | `n` | Argument `−n + 2` | `y[n] = x[−n+2]` | | :---: | :---: | :---: | | `−2` | `4` | `x[4] = 5` | | `−1` | `3` | `x[3] = 4` | | `0` | `2` | `x[2] = 3` | | `1` | `1` | `x[1] = 2` | | `2` | `0` | `x[0] = 3` (interpreted) | **Step 3 — Resulting DT signal:** $$y[n] = \{\dots, 5, 4, 3, 2, 3, \dots\} \quad \text{at } n = -2, -1, 0, 1, 2$$ > [!NOTE] > **Examiner's pattern:** KTU frequently combines *time shifting* and *time reversal* in a single sub-question. Always apply the **right-to-left** substitution order: replacement of `n` with `(−n + 2)` happens **directly and atomically** in the function argument — do NOT perform shift-then-reverse as two separate intermediate steps unless asked. ### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Sampling and Reconstruction Examples | Original CT Signal | Sampling Frequency `F_s` | Resulting DT Signal | Notes | | :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- | | `x(t) = cos(100πt)` | `200 Hz` | `x[n] = cos(0.5πn)` | `Ω = π/2` rad/sample | | `x(t) = sin(50πt)` | `100 Hz` | `x[n] = sin(0.5πn)` | `Ω = π/2` rad/sample | | `x(t) = cos(200πt)` | `100 Hz` | `x[n] = cos(2πn) = 1` | Aliased to DC! | | `x(t) = sin(300πt)` | `200 Hz` | `x[n] = sin(1.5πn)` | Above Nyquist → aliased | | `x(t) = e^{−10t}u(t)` | `50 Hz` | `x[n] = e^{−0.2n}u[n]` | `a = e^{−10T_s}` | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Row 3 Explanation:** The signal `cos(200πt)` has frequency `f = 100 Hz`. Sampling at `F_s = 100 Hz` violates the **Nyquist criterion** (`F_s < 2F_{max}`). The DT signal collapses to a constant `1`. This is the canonical KTU illustration of **aliasing**, which is exhaustively covered in Module 2. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram — The CT-to-DT Signal Conversion Chain (A Real-Time System) ```mermaid flowchart LR A["PHYSICAL WORLD
CT PHENOMENON"] --> B["TRANSDUCER
SENSOR"] B --> C["ANALOG SIGNAL
x of t
CONTINUOUS TIME AND AMPLITUDE"] C --> D["ANTI ALIASING
LOW PASS FILTER"] D --> E["ADC
SAMPLER AND QUANTIZER"] E --> F["DIGITAL SIGNAL
x of n
DISCRETE TIME AND AMPLITUDE"] F --> G["DSP
DIGITAL SIGNAL PROCESSOR"] G --> H["DAC
RECONSTRUCTOR"] H --> I["RECONSTRUCTION
FILTER"] I --> J["ANALOG OUTPUT
y of t"] J --> K["ACTUATOR
SPEAKER MOTOR DISPLAY"] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** A physical quantity (sound, light, pressure) is sensed, anti-alias filtered, sampled, quantized, processed digitally, reconstructed, and finally used to drive an actuator. This is the **universal real-time signal processing pipeline**. ### 4.2 Block Diagram — Time-Domain Operations on a CT Signal `x(t)` ```mermaid flowchart TD X["INPUT
x of t"] --> P1["OP1
TIME SHIFT
x of t minus t0"] P1 --> P2["OP2
TIME SCALE
x of a t"] P2 --> P3["OP3
TIME REVERSAL
x of minus t"] P3 --> P4["OP4
AMPLITUDE SCALE
A times x"] P4 --> Y["OUTPUT
y of t = A times x of minus a t plus b"] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Order of Operations (Right-to-Left Reading):** When a KTU question asks you to sketch `y(t) = A·x(−at + b)`, the transformations are applied in this order to the **argument of `x`**: > 1. **Time shift** by `b/a` (right by `b/a`) > 2. **Time scale** by factor `a` > 3. **Time reversal** about `t = 0` > 4. **Amplitude scale** by factor `A` ### 4.3 Block Diagram — Signal Classification Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD S["SIGNAL UNDER STUDY"] --> Q1{"IS THE INDEPENDENT
VARIABLE CONTINUOUS?"} Q1 -- YES --> CT["CONTINUOUS TIME
DEFINED AS x OF t"] Q1 -- NO --> DT["DISCRETE TIME
DEFINED AS x OF n"] CT --> Q2A{"IS THE AMPLITUDE
CONTINUOUS?"} Q2A -- YES --> ANA["ANALOG SIGNAL
CT AND CONTINUOUS AMP"] Q2A -- NO --> QCT["QUANTIZED CT
CT AND DISCRETE AMP"] DT --> Q2B{"IS THE AMPLITUDE
DISCRETE?"} Q2B -- YES --> DIG["DIGITAL SIGNAL
DT AND DISCRETE AMP"] Q2B -- NO --> SAM["SAMPLED DATA
DT AND CONTINUOUS AMP"] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Mnemonic for KTU Viva:** *"A signal can be either continuous-time or discrete-time, AND independently either continuous-amplitude or discrete-amplitude. The 2×2 combination gives four canonical classes."* ### 4.4 Schematic — Sampling as Multiplication by an Impulse Train ```mermaid flowchart LR CT["x of t
CONTINUOUS TIME SIGNAL"] --> MUL["MULTIPLIER
x t TIMES p t"] P["IMPULSE TRAIN
p t = SUM delta t minus nTs"] --> MUL MUL --> OUT["x sub s of t
SAMPLED SIGNAL
EQUAL TO SUM x nTs delta t minus nTs"] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Mathematical Model of Sampling:** The mathematically rigorous way to view the CT-to-DT conversion is: > $$x_s(t) = x(t) \cdot p(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT_s)\,\delta(t - nT_s)$$ > This is the **impulse-train sampling model** that KTU Module 2 (Sampling Theorem) builds upon. ### 4.5 Visual Comparison — A CT Signal vs. Its Sampled DT Counterpart ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph CT_PLOT["CT SIGNAL x of t — DRAWN AS A CONTINUOUS CURVE"] N1["x of 0 = 1.0"] N2["x of 1 = 0.5"] N3["x of 2 = minus 0.5"] N4["x of 3 = minus 1.0"] N5["x of 4 = minus 0.5"] end subgraph DT_PLOT["DT SIGNAL x of n — DRAWN AS DISCRETE STEMS"] M1["n = 0 — value 1.0"] M2["n = 1 — value 0.5"] M3["n = 2 — value minus 0.5"] M4["n = 3 — value minus 1.0"] M5["n = 4 — value minus 0.5"] end CT_PLOT --- DT_PLOT ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Plotting Convention:** A CT signal is sketched as a **continuous curve**; a DT signal is sketched as **discrete vertical stems (lollipops) with filled circles at the top** and **open circles at undefined points**. This convention appears in **every KTU question paper** involving signal plots. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Set A]** *Define a continuous-time signal and a discrete-time signal. Give one example of each from a real-world application.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** A **continuous-time (CT) signal** is a signal defined for every instant of the continuous time variable `t ∈ ℝ`. It is mathematically represented as a function `x(t)`, where `t` is a real number. - **Example:** The voltage across a live microphone — the voltage varies smoothly with time and is defined at every single instant. A **discrete-time (DT) signal** is a signal defined only at discrete instants of time. It is mathematically represented as a sequence `x[n]`, where the index `n` is an integer. - **Example:** The closing price of a stock recorded at the end of each trading day — only one value per day exists; intermediate values are not defined. The conversion between them is achieved by **sampling** at uniform intervals `T_s`, giving `x[n] = x(nT_s)`. *(Expected key terms for full marks: `t ∈ ℝ`, `n ∈ ℤ`, sampling, real-world example.)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Set B]** *What is a 1-D signal? Distinguish between a 1-D signal and a 2-D signal with suitable examples.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** A **1-D (one-dimensional) signal** is a signal that is a function of a **single independent variable** (typically time). It is written as `x(t)` for continuous-time or `x[n]` for discrete-time. The signal carries information along only one axis. - **Example:** An ECG waveform — amplitude varies as a function of time. A **2-D (two-dimensional) signal** is a function of **two independent variables** (typically two spatial coordinates). It is written as `I(x, y)`. - **Example:** A grayscale photograph — intensity varies as a function of horizontal position `x` and vertical position `y`. **Distinction:** 1-D signals depend on one variable (time), while 2-D signals depend on two variables (usually two spatial dimensions). 1-D signals are typically time-series, while 2-D signals are typically images. *(Expected key terms for full marks: function of one independent variable, `x(t)`, `I(x,y)`, ECG vs. image.)* --- ### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module-1 Internal Choice Pattern) --- ### **Question A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]** **Q.A (a)** Classify signals into continuous-time and discrete-time categories. For each category, sketch the unit step, unit impulse, and unit ramp functions, clearly labeling the axes and the value at the critical point. **(7 marks)** **Q.A (b)** A continuous-time signal is given by `x(t) = 2·sin(40πt + π/4)`. The signal is sampled at a rate of `F_s = 200` samples per second. **(i)** Write the resulting discrete-time signal `x[n]` in canonical form. **(ii)** Determine the digital frequency `Ω` in radians per sample. **(iii)** Is the resulting DT signal periodic? If yes, find the fundamental period. **(7 marks)** --- ### **Model Solution for Question A** #### **Part (a) — 7 Marks** **Step 1 — CT signals `x(t)`:** Defined for all real `t`; notation `x(t), t ∈ ℝ`. - **Unit step CT:** `u(t) = 1` for `t ≥ 0`, and `0` for `t < 0`. The signal jumps from `0` to `1` at `t = 0`. At `t = 0` it is defined as `1` (closed dot at `1`). - **Unit impulse CT:** `δ(t)` is `0` for all `t ≠ 0`, and is an impulse of area `1` at `t = 0` (vertical arrow of height `1` at the origin). - **Unit ramp CT:** `r(t) = t` for `t ≥ 0`, and `0` for `t < 0`. A straight line of slope `1` starting from the origin. **Step 2 — DT signals `x[n]`:** Defined only for integer `n ∈ ℤ`; notation `x[n]`. - **Unit step DT:** `u[n] = 1` for `n ≥ 0`, and `0` for `n < 0`. A sequence that is `0` for `n = −1, −2, …` and `1` for `n = 0, 1, 2, …`. - **Unit impulse DT:** `δ[n] = 1` at `n = 0`, and `0` otherwise. A single vertical stem of height `1` at `n = 0`. - **Unit ramp DT:** `r[n] = n` for `n ≥ 0`, and `0` for `n < 0`. A sequence of values `0, 1, 2, 3, …` for `n = 0, 1, 2, 3, …`. **Sketch (text representation):** | Signal | CT Sketch (continuous curve) | DT Sketch (discrete stems) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | `u` | Horizontal line at `1` for `t ≥ 0`, jumps from `0` at `t < 0` | Stem at `n=0,1,2,…` all of height `1`; `0` for `n<0` | | `δ` | Vertical arrow at `t=0` | Single stem of height `1` at `n=0` only | | `r` | Line of slope `1` from origin for `t ≥ 0` | Stems of heights `0, 1, 2, 3, …` for `n=0,1,2,3,…` | *(Awarding 2 marks for CT sketches, 2 marks for DT sketches, 1 mark for definitions, 2 marks for correct labelling of critical points.)* #### **Part (b) — 7 Marks** **Given:** `x(t) = 2·sin(40πt + π/4)`, `F_s = 200` Hz. **Sub-part (i):** Sampling period is $$T_s = \frac{1}{F_s} = \frac{1}{200} = 0.005 \text{ s}$$ Substituting `t = nT_s = 0.005n`: $$x[n] = 2\sin(40\pi \cdot 0.005 n + \pi/4) = 2\sin(0.2\pi n + \pi/4)$$ **Sub-part (ii):** Digital frequency in radians/sample is the coefficient of `n`: $$\Omega = 0.2\pi = \frac{\pi}{5} \text{ rad/sample}$$ **Sub-part (iii):** Periodicity test. A DT sinusoid is periodic iff `2π/Ω` is rational. $$\frac{2\pi}{\Omega} = \frac{2\pi}{0.2\pi} = \frac{2}{0.2} = 10$$ Since `2π/Ω = 10` is a **positive integer**, the signal IS periodic with fundamental period `N = 10` samples. **Verification:** `x[n + 10] = 2 sin(0.2π(n+10) + π/4) = 2 sin(0.2πn + 2π + π/4) = 2 sin(0.2πn + π/4) = x[n]`. ✓ *(Awarding 2 marks for sampling, 2 marks for digital frequency, 3 marks for periodicity analysis with verification.)* --- ### **Question B — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified for 2024 Scheme]** **Q.B (a)** Define a **1-D real-time signal**. With the help of a block diagram, describe the real-time signal processing chain that converts a physical phenomenon into a digital signal suitable for computer processing. Label each block clearly. **(7 marks)** **Q.B (b)** Consider a DT signal defined as `x[n] = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5}` where the **first sample (leftmost) corresponds to `n = −2`** and the value at `n = 0` is `3`. **(i)** Write `x[n]` in functional form using the unit step function. **(ii)** Find and sketch `y[n] = x[2 − n] + x[n]·u[−n]`. **(7 marks)** --- ### **Model Solution for Question B** #### **Part (a) — 7 Marks** **Definition (2 marks):** A **1-D real-time signal** is a single-variable function (typically of time) whose values are generated, sampled, and processed in synchronism with the physical phenomenon they represent, with a delay small enough to satisfy the timing constraints of the application. **Real-time signal processing chain (5 marks):** 1. **Physical Phenomenon** (e.g., sound wave, heart vibration, temperature variation) — this is the source, naturally **continuous-time** and **continuous-amplitude**. 2. **Transducer/Sensor** (e.g., microphone, ECG electrode, thermocouple) — converts the physical quantity into an **analog electrical signal** `x(t)`. 3. **Anti-Aliasing Filter** (analog low-pass filter) — removes frequency components above `F_s/2` (Nyquist frequency) to prevent aliasing during sampling. 4. **Sample-and-Hold Circuit** — captures the value of the signal at regular intervals `T_s`. 5. **ADC (Analog-to-Digital Converter)** — quantizes the sampled amplitudes into discrete levels, producing a **digital signal** `x[n]` (DT and discrete-amplitude). 6. **Digital Signal Processor (DSP) / Microcontroller** — performs the actual processing (filtering, compression, analysis) on the digital signal in real-time. > The corresponding block diagram is provided in **Section 4.1** of these notes. *(Awarding 2 marks for definition, 2 marks for diagram with labels, 3 marks for explaining the role of each block.)* #### **Part (b) — 7 Marks** **Given:** `x[n]` has the samples `x[−2]=1, x[−1]=2, x[0]=3, x[1]=4, x[2]=5`. **Sub-part (i) — Functional form using unit step (3 marks):** The unit step `u[n − n₀]` turns on at `n = n₀`. To represent `x[n]` we add scaled, shifted step "on" events that introduce each new sample value: $$x[n] = u[n+2] + u[n+1] + u[n] + u[n-1] + u[n-2]$$ **Verification:** - For `n = −2`: all `u[n − k] = 0` except `u[n+2] = 1` → `x[−2] = 1` ✓ - For `n = −1`: `u[n+2] = 1` and `u[n+1] = 1` → `x[−1] = 2` ✓ - For `n = 0`: three terms `= 3` → `x[0] = 3` ✓ - For `n = 1`: four terms `= 4` → `x[1] = 4` ✓ - For `n = 2`: five terms `= 5` → `x[2] = 5` ✓ **Sub-part (ii) — Compute `y[n] = x[2 − n] + x[n]·u[−n]` (4 marks):** First compute `x[2 − n]` (time-reversal and shift). The mapping is: | `n` | `2 − n` | `x[2 − n]` | `x[n]` | `u[−n]` | `x[n]·u[−n]` | `y[n]` | | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | | `−3` | `5` | `0` (out of range) | `0` | `1` | `0` | `0` | | `−2` | `4` | `0` | `1` | `1` | `1` | `1` | | `−1` | `3` | `0` | `2` | `1` | `2` | `2` | | `0` | `2` | `5` | `3` | `1` | `3` | `8` | | `1` | `1` | `4` | `4` | `0` | `0` | `4` | | `2` | `0` | `3` | `5` | `0` | `0` | `3` | | `3` | `−1` | `0` | `0` | `0` | `0` | `0` | **Resulting `y[n]`:** `y[−2]=1, y[−1]=2, y[0]=8, y[1]=4, y[2]=3`; all other values are `0`. **Sketch:** Stems of heights `1, 2, 8, 4, 3` at `n = −2, −1, 0, 1, 2` respectively, with `0` elsewhere. *(Awarding 2 marks for functional form, 1 mark for tabular computation, 1 mark for the final sketch.)* --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Five Marks-Losing Pitfalls in CT and DT Signal Questions — Examiner's Red Flags:** > > 1. **Conflating `δ(t)` and `δ[n]`:** `δ(t)` is a *generalized function (distribution)* of infinite height and unit area; `δ[n]` is a perfectly ordinary real number equal to `1` at `n=0` and `0` elsewhere. **Do NOT** write `δ(t) = 1` for `t = 0`. > > 2. **Mixing up digital frequency units:** A CT sinusoid uses `ω` in **radians/second**; a DT sinusoid uses `Ω` in **radians/sample**. Writing `ω = 200π rad/sample` is **an instant mark-loss**. > > 3. **Forgetting `u[0] = 1` and `δ[0] = 1` in DT:** The discrete unit step and impulse both equal `1` at the origin. The open/closed circle convention matters in sketches. > > 4. **Treating `x[2.5]` as defined:** A DT signal is **only defined at integer indices**. If a problem seems to require `x[2.5]`, re-read the problem — you have likely mis-substituted. > > 5. **Skipping the right-to-left substitution order** in combined time-shift + time-reversal operations (`x[−n + 2]`, `x[2 − n]`, etc.). KTU explicitly tests the substitution discipline in Module 1. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Module 1 — 1D CT and DT Signals: Rapid Revision Checklist** - **Signal:** A function of one or more independent variables; carries information. - **1-D Signal:** Function of a single variable, almost always **time** (continuous `t` or discrete `n`). - **Real-time signal:** A signal whose processing must satisfy timing deadlines imposed by the physical phenomenon. - **CT signal `x(t)`:** Defined for **all real `t ∈ ℝ`**; amplitude may be continuous or discrete. - **DT signal `x[n]`:** Defined for **integer `n ∈ ℤ`**; amplitude may be continuous (sampled) or discrete (digital). - **Sampling relation:** `x[n] = x(nT_s)`; sampling frequency `F_s = 1/T_s`. - **Nyquist criterion:** `F_s ≥ 2·F_{max}` to avoid aliasing (covered in detail in Module 2). - **Elementary signals to memorize:** `u(t)`, `u[n]`, `δ(t)`, `δ[n]`, `r(t)`, `r[n]`, `e^{at}`, `e^{jωt}`, `cos(ωt)`, `sin(Ωn)`. - **Standard operations:** Time shift, time scale, time reversal, amplitude scale, addition, multiplication — apply right-to-left in the argument. - **Periodicity:** CT sinusoid always periodic with `T = 2π/ω`; DT sinusoid periodic **only if `2π/Ω` is a rational number**, with `N = 2πm/Ω` the smallest positive integer. - **Euler's identity:** `e^{jωt} = cos(ωt) + j·sin(ωt)` — the foundation of Fourier analysis. - **Plotting convention:** CT as continuous curve; DT as discrete vertical stems with filled dots at defined values. - **Four canonical classes:** Analog (CT + continuous amplitude), Quantized CT (CT + discrete amplitude), Sampled-data (DT + continuous amplitude), Digital (DT + discrete amplitude). - **Real-time processing chain:** Physical source → Transducer → Anti-alias filter → Sampler → Quantizer → DSP → DAC → Reconstruction filter → Actuator. - **Examiner's mantra:** *"Always state the domain of the independent variable explicitly — `t ∈ ℝ` for CT, `n ∈ ℤ` for DT — and apply time-domain transformations strictly right-to-left in the function argument."*

topics.title: Sinusoids
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST416 - SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Sinusoids

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of a Sinusoid

A **sinusoid** is a smooth, infinitely repeating, periodic mathematical function whose waveform resembles a sine or cosine curve. In the context of Signals and Systems (KTU 2024 Scheme – PECST416), a sinusoid is the most fundamental **continuous-time (CT)** and **discrete-time (DT)** periodic signal used to model oscillating phenomena ranging from alternating current (AC) supply voltages to radio-frequency carriers and acoustic vibrations.

A general continuous-time sinusoid is defined as:

$$x(t) = A \cos(\omega_0 t + \phi)$$

or equivalently,

$$x(t) = A \sin(\omega_0 t + \phi)$$

A general discrete-time sinusoid is defined as:

$$x[n] = A \cos(\Omega_0 n + \phi)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The cosine form is the standard reference for sinusoids in KTU board examinations because of its direct relationship to the **Real Part of a Phasor** $\left(Re\{Ae^{j(\omega_0 t + \phi)}\}\right)$. Always default to the **cosine** form unless the problem statement explicitly specifies the sine form.

## 1.2 The Four Cardinal Parameters of a Sinusoid

Every sinusoid is fully described by exactly **four** parameters. A student who memorizes these four parameters can characterize any sinusoidal signal on sight:

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Unit | Physical Meaning |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| Amplitude | $A$ | Volts / Pascals (signal dependent) | Peak deviation from zero (DC offset is zero for pure sinusoid). |
| Angular Frequency | $\omega_0$ | **radians per second (rad/s)** | Rate of change of the argument; controls how fast the signal oscillates. |
| Initial Phase | $\phi$ | **radians (rad)** | Horizontal shift of the waveform at $t=0$ relative to a pure cosine. |
| Period | $T$ | **seconds (s)** | Time taken for one complete cycle. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Crucial Distinction — Frequency vs. Angular Frequency:**
> In CT signals, **$f_0$ (Hz)** and **$\omega_0$ (rad/s)** are related by $\omega_0 = 2\pi f_0$. Examiners frequently test whether students use the correct symbol — getting this wrong in an LCR/Phasor problem typically costs **2 marks** outright.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The Pendulum Clock

Imagine a grandfather clock's pendulum. The pendulum swings from the far left, to the far right, and back to the far left — this one round trip is one **period ($T$)**. The maximum displacement of the bob from its resting (center) position is the **amplitude ($A$)**. The number of complete round trips the pendulum makes in one second is the **frequency ($f_0$)**. If you walk up to the clock and start your stopwatch exactly when the pendulum is at its leftmost extreme, that initial angular position is the **phase ($\phi$)**. A pure sinusoid is the perfect mathematical fingerprint of this eternal, frictionless swing.

> [!TIP]
> A sinusoid is sometimes called a **"pure tone"** in audio engineering or a **"monochromatic wave"** in physics because it contains only **one** frequency component — unlike a musical chord or a speech signal which are sums of many sinusoids (Fourier's theorem).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Continuous-Time Sinusoid

The waveform $x(t) = A \cos(\omega_0 t + \phi)$ can be analyzed through three equivalent lenses:

### Lens 1: Amplitude–Time (Time-Domain) View
- The function reaches its maximum value $+A$ whenever the argument $(\omega_0 t + \phi) = 2\pi k$.
- The function reaches its minimum value $-A$ whenever the argument equals $(2k+1)\pi$.
- **Zero crossings** occur at $(\omega_0 t + \phi) = \frac{\pi}{2} + k\pi$.

### Lens 2: Phase–Time View
The signal can be rewritten to expose a **time-shift** $t_0$:

$$x(t) = A \cos(\omega_0(t - t_0)) \quad \text{where} \quad t_0 = -\frac{\phi}{\omega_0}$$

- If $\phi > 0$ (positive phase), the cosine is shifted **to the left** (advanced in time).
- If $\phi < 0$ (negative phase), the cosine is shifted **to the right** (delayed in time).

### Lens 3: Phasor (Rotating Vector) View
A sinusoid is the **real part of a uniformly rotating vector** in the complex plane. The vector of length $A$ rotates with angular velocity $\omega_0$ rad/s, and its initial angle with the real axis is $\phi$.

$$x(t) = \text{Re}\!\left\{ A e^{j(\omega_0 t + \phi)} \right\} = \text{Re}\!\left\{ \underbrace{A e^{j\phi}}_{\text{Phasor } \mathbf{X}} \cdot e^{j\omega_0 t} \right\}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Phasor $\mathbf{X} = A e^{j\phi}$** is a *complex number* (not a function of time). It captures the amplitude and phase of the sinusoid in a single entity. This is the foundation of **Phasor Analysis** used throughout AC circuit theory and Fourier Transform derivations.

## 2.2 Anatomy of a Discrete-Time Sinusoid

For the discrete-time signal $x[n] = A \cos(\Omega_0 n + \phi)$:

- $n \in \mathbb{Z}$ (integer sample indices).
- $\Omega_0$ is the **discrete-time angular frequency** in **radians per sample**.
- There is **no** direct "Hz" interpretation for $\Omega_0$ without knowing the sampling rate $f_s$.

### Periodicity Condition (Discrete-Time)

Unlike CT sinusoids which are *always* periodic, DT sinusoids are periodic **only if** $\frac{\Omega_0}{2\pi}$ is a **rational number**.

The fundamental period $N$ is the **smallest positive integer** $N$ satisfying:

$$\Omega_0 N = 2\pi k \quad \Rightarrow \quad N = \frac{2\pi k}{\Omega_0} \quad \text{for the smallest integer } k \text{ such that } N \in \mathbb{Z}^+$$

> [!WARNING]
> A common student mistake is to assume $N = \frac{2\pi}{\Omega_0}$ directly. This is **only valid if** $2\pi / \Omega_0$ happens to be an integer. If $2\pi / \Omega_0$ is irrational, the signal is **aperiodic** (a non-repeating infinite sequence).

### Frequency Uniqueness Range

Because cosine is $2\pi$-periodic, DT sinusoids are periodic in **frequency** as well:

$$A \cos((\Omega_0 + 2\pi k) n + \phi) = A \cos(\Omega_0 n + 2\pi k n + \phi) = A \cos(\Omega_0 n + \phi)$$

This means all frequencies are confined to a **principal range**:

$$-\pi < \Omega_0 \le \pi \quad \text{(or equivalently, } 0 \le \Omega_0 < 2\pi\text{)}$$

Frequencies outside this range are **aliases** of frequencies inside the range.

## 2.3 Sinusoids via Euler's Formula (The Foundation of Phasors)

A sinusoid is fundamentally a **complex exponential** in disguise. The forward and inverse Euler identities are:

$$e^{j\theta} = \cos\theta + j\sin\theta$$

$$\cos\theta = \frac{e^{j\theta} + e^{-j\theta}}{2}, \quad \sin\theta = \frac{e^{j\theta} - e^{-j\theta}}{2j}$$

Therefore, a CT sinusoid can be written as:

$$x(t) = A\cos(\omega_0 t + \phi) = \frac{A}{2}e^{j\phi} e^{j\omega_0 t} + \frac{A}{2}e^{-j\phi} e^{-j\omega_0 t}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This two-sided complex exponential decomposition is the **exact entry point to the Fourier Transform**. A real sinusoid of frequency $\omega_0$ contains **two** complex exponentials — one rotating at $+\omega_0$ and one at $-\omega_0$. The Fourier Transform of $x(t)$ is therefore two impulses at $\pm\omega_0$.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Property | Mathematical Statement | Engineering Use |
|:--|:--|:--|:--|
| 1 | CT Sinusoid | $x(t) = A \cos(\omega_0 t + \phi)$ | Reference waveform |
| 2 | DT Sinusoid | $x[n] = A \cos(\Omega_0 n + \phi)$ | Reference sampled sequence |
| 3 | Frequency Relation (CT) | $\omega_0 = 2\pi f_0 = \dfrac{2\pi}{T}$ | Convert between rad/s, Hz, and period |
| 4 | Phasor Representation | $x(t) = \text{Re}\{X e^{j\omega_0 t}\}, \quad X = A e^{j\phi}$ | AC circuit analysis, Fourier |
| 5 | Euler (Forward) | $e^{j\theta} = \cos\theta + j\sin\theta$ | Convert phasor to sinusoid |
| 6 | Euler (Cosine) | $\cos\theta = \dfrac{e^{j\theta} + e^{-j\theta}}{2}$ | Fourier series derivation |
| 7 | Euler (Sine) | $\sin\theta = \dfrac{e^{j\theta} - e^{-j\theta}}{2j}$ | Quadrature / Hilbert transform |
| 8 | Time-Shift Identity | $A\cos(\omega_0 t + \phi) = A\cos(\omega_0(t - t_0))$, $t_0 = -\dfrac{\phi}{\omega_0}$ | Delay/advance in time |
| 9 | DT Periodicity Condition | $\dfrac{\Omega_0}{2\pi} = \dfrac{k}{N}$ (rational) | Test if DT sinusoid repeats |
| 10 | DT Principal Frequency Range | $-\pi < \Omega_0 \le \pi$ | Avoid aliasing ambiguity |
| 11 | Even-Odd Decomposition | $A\cos(\omega_0 t + \phi) = A\cos\phi \cdot \cos(\omega_0 t) - A\sin\phi \cdot \sin(\omega_0 t)$ | Symmetry analysis in Fourier |
| 12 | Harmonic Relation | $x(t) = \sum_{k=1}^{N} A_k \cos(k\omega_0 t + \phi_k)$ is periodic with $T = \dfrac{2\pi}{\omega_0}$ | Fourier series synthesis |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **AC Power Systems:** The Indian domestic supply is $V(t) = 325 \cos(100\pi t)$ V (230 V RMS, 50 Hz). Phasor analysis makes power calculation trivial.
- **Telecommunications:** AM/FM radio carriers are high-frequency sinusoids amplitude- or frequency-modulated by audio signals.
- **Audio & Music:** A pure flute tone is nearly a perfect sinusoid. Synthesizers generate complex timbres by summing harmonically related sinusoids (Fourier synthesis).
- **Biomedical:** ECG, EEG, and EMG signals are analyzed by decomposing them into sinusoidal components of different frequencies (Fourier Transform).
- **Vibration Analysis:** Mechanical systems are excited at known sinusoidal frequencies to identify resonance peaks.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Sinusoid to Complex Exponential (Euler's Bridge)

**Goal:** Show that $x(t) = A\cos(\omega_0 t + \phi)$ is equivalent to the sum of two counter-rotating phasors.

**Step 1 — Apply the definition of cosine from Euler's identity.**

We begin with the well-known Euler identity:

$$e^{j\theta} = \cos\theta + j\sin\theta$$

Replacing $\theta$ with $-\theta$:

$$e^{-j\theta} = \cos\theta - j\sin\theta$$

**Step 2 — Add the two equations to isolate $\cos\theta$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
e^{j\theta} + e^{-j\theta} &= (\cos\theta + j\sin\theta) + (\cos\theta - j\sin\theta) \\
e^{j\theta} + e^{-j\theta} &= 2\cos\theta
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $\cos\theta$.**

$$
\cos\theta = \frac{e^{j\theta} + e^{-j\theta}}{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $\theta = \omega_0 t + \phi$ into the result.**

$$
\cos(\omega_0 t + \phi) = \frac{e^{j(\omega_0 t + \phi)} + e^{-j(\omega_0 t + \phi)}}{2}
$$

**Step 5 — Split the exponent using the rule $e^{a+b} = e^a \cdot e^b$.**

$$
\cos(\omega_0 t + \phi) = \frac{e^{j\phi} \cdot e^{j\omega_0 t} + e^{-j\phi} \cdot e^{-j\omega_0 t}}{2}
$$

**Step 6 — Multiply by the amplitude $A$ to obtain the full sinusoid.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x(t) = A\cos(\omega_0 t + \phi) &= A \cdot \frac{e^{j\phi} e^{j\omega_0 t} + e^{-j\phi} e^{-j\omega_0 t}}{2} \\
&= \frac{A}{2} e^{j\phi} e^{j\omega_0 t} + \frac{A}{2} e^{-j\phi} e^{-j\omega_0 t}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Interpret the result.**

- The first term is a **complex phasor** of magnitude $A/2$ and initial phase $+\phi$, rotating counter-clockwise at $\omega_0$ rad/s.
- The second term is the **complex conjugate** of the first, rotating clockwise at $\omega_0$ rad/s.
- The real sinusoid is the **sum of these two conjugate rotations**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is this derivation critical for KTU?** It is the foundational identity behind the **Fourier Series representation** of periodic signals. If a student cannot reproduce this on paper, they will fail Module 2 (Fourier Series) and Module 5 (Fourier Transform) derivations.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Discrete-Time Sinusoid Periodicity Condition

**Goal:** Find the smallest positive integer $N$ such that $x[n+N] = x[n]$ for all $n$.

**Step 1 — Write the periodicity requirement.**

$$
A \cos(\Omega_0 (n + N) + \phi) = A \cos(\Omega_0 n + \phi)
$$

**Step 2 — Cancel the amplitude $A$ and expand the left-hand side.**

$$
\cos(\Omega_0 n + \Omega_0 N + \phi) = \cos(\Omega_0 n + \phi)
$$

**Step 3 — Use the cosine shift identity $\cos(\alpha + \beta) = \cos\alpha \cos\beta - \sin\alpha \sin\beta$.**

$$
\cos(\Omega_0 n + \phi)\cos(\Omega_0 N) - \sin(\Omega_0 n + \phi)\sin(\Omega_0 N) = \cos(\Omega_0 n + \phi)
$$

**Step 4 — Equate coefficients of $\cos(\Omega_0 n + \phi)$ and $\sin(\Omega_0 n + \phi)$ on both sides.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Coefficient of } \cos(\Omega_0 n + \phi): \quad \cos(\Omega_0 N) &= 1 \\
\text{Coefficient of } \sin(\Omega_0 n + \phi): \quad \sin(\Omega_0 N) &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve the simultaneous trigonometric conditions.**

The smallest positive angle satisfying both $\cos\theta = 1$ and $\sin\theta = 0$ is $\theta = 2\pi k$ for integer $k$.

Therefore:

$$
\Omega_0 N = 2\pi k \quad \text{for some integer } k \ge 1
$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $N$ (the fundamental period).**

$$
N = \frac{2\pi k}{\Omega_0}
$$

**Step 7 — State the condition for periodicity.**

A discrete-time sinusoid is periodic **if and only if** $N$ is a positive integer for some choice of integer $k$. This requires $\frac{2\pi}{\Omega_0}$ to be a rational number, i.e.,

$$
\frac{\Omega_0}{2\pi} = \frac{k}{N} \in \mathbb{Q} \quad \text{(rational)}
$$

If $\frac{\Omega_0}{2\pi}$ is **irrational**, no integer $N$ can satisfy the equation, and the signal is **aperiodic**.

**Worked Numerical Example:** Let $\Omega_0 = \frac{2\pi}{3}$.

$$
N = \frac{2\pi k}{\Omega_0} = \frac{2\pi k}{2\pi / 3} = 3k
$$

Choosing the smallest positive integer $k=1$ gives $N=3$. So $x[n] = \cos(2\pi n/3)$ is periodic with fundamental period $N=3$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Even–Odd Decomposition of a Phase-Shifted Cosine

**Goal:** Express $A\cos(\omega_0 t + \phi)$ as a sum of an even part and an odd part. This is essential for Fourier Series coefficient computation.

**Step 1 — Apply the cosine addition formula $\cos(\alpha + \beta) = \cos\alpha\cos\beta - \sin\alpha\sin\beta$.**

$$
\cos(\omega_0 t + \phi) = \cos(\omega_0 t)\cos(\phi) - \sin(\omega_0 t)\sin(\phi)
$$

**Step 2 — Multiply by $A$.**

$$
A\cos(\omega_0 t + \phi) = A\cos(\phi)\cos(\omega_0 t) - A\sin(\phi)\sin(\omega_0 t)
$$

**Step 3 — Identify the even and odd parts.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Even part } x_e(t) &= A\cos(\phi) \cdot \cos(\omega_0 t) \quad \text{(even in } t \text{)} \\
\text{Odd part } x_o(t) &= -A\sin(\phi) \cdot \sin(\omega_0 t) \quad \text{(odd in } t \text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Trick:** A pure cosine $\cos(\omega_0 t)$ is purely even. A pure sine $\sin(\omega_0 t)$ is purely odd. A phase-shifted sinusoid $A\cos(\omega_0 t + \phi)$ is generally **neither** even nor odd — but it can be split into an even cosine part and an odd sine part, as derived above. This is how Fourier Cosine and Sine Series are derived.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example: Phasor Addition of Two Sinusoids

**Problem:** Combine $x_1(t) = 5\cos(100t)$ and $x_2(t) = 10\cos(100t + \pi/4)$ into a single sinusoid of the same frequency.

**Step 1 — Convert each to phasor form.**

$$
X_1 = 5 e^{j \cdot 0} = 5 + j0
$$

$$
X_2 = 10 e^{j\pi/4} = 10\left(\cos\frac{\pi}{4} + j\sin\frac{\pi}{4}\right) = 10\left(\frac{\sqrt{2}}{2} + j\frac{\sqrt{2}}{2}\right) = 5\sqrt{2} + j5\sqrt{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Sum the phasors (complex addition).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
X &= X_1 + X_2 \\
&= (5 + 5\sqrt{2}) + j(0 + 5\sqrt{2}) \\
&= (5 + 5\sqrt{2}) + j(5\sqrt{2})
\end{aligned}
$$

Numerically: $5\sqrt{2} \approx 7.071$, so

$$
X \approx 12.071 + j7.071
$$

**Step 3 — Compute magnitude and phase of the resultant phasor.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \vert X \vert = \sqrt{(5 + 5\sqrt{2})^2 + (5\sqrt{2})^2} \\
&= 5\sqrt{(1+\sqrt{2})^2 + (\sqrt{2})^2} \\
&= 5\sqrt{1 + 2\sqrt{2} + 2 + 2} \\
&= 5\sqrt{5 + 2\sqrt{2}} \\
&\approx 5 \times \sqrt{5 + 2.828} = 5 \times \sqrt{7.828} \approx 5 \times 2.798 \approx 13.99
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\phi = \angle X = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{5\sqrt{2}}{5 + 5\sqrt{2}}\right) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\sqrt{2}}{1+\sqrt{2}}\right) \approx \tan^{-1}(0.5858) \approx 0.529 \text{ rad} \approx 30.34^\circ
$$

**Step 4 — Write the combined sinusoid.**

$$
x(t) = x_1(t) + x_2(t) \approx 13.99 \cos(100t + 0.529) \text{ rad}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **high-yield KTU problem**. Sum of sinusoids of the same frequency is a standard 7-mark problem. The reverse — splitting a single sinusoid into components — is equally common.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Sinusoid Parameter Anatomy (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Sinusoid x of t equals A cos omega0 t plus phi"] --> B["Amplitude A"]
    A --> C["Angular Frequency omega0"]
    A --> D["Initial Phase phi"]
    A --> E["Period T equals 2 pi over omega0"]
    C --> F["Rotates Phasor in Complex Plane"]
    D --> G["Sets Initial Angle of Phasor"]
    B --> H["Magnitude of Phasor X equals A e to the j phi"]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I["Re of X times e to the j omega0 t"]
    I --> J["Real Sinusoidal Signal x of t"]
```

## 4.2 Phasor-to-Sinusoid Conceptual Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["Phasor X equals A e to the j phi"] --> P2["Multiply by e to the j omega0 t"]
    P2 --> P3["Complex Rotating Vector X e to the j omega0 t"]
    P3 --> P4["Take Real Part"]
    P3 --> P5["Take Imaginary Part"]
    P4 --> P6["Cosine Sinusoid A cos omega0 t plus phi"]
    P5 --> P7["Sine Sinusoid A sin omega0 t plus phi"]
```

## 4.3 Continuous-Time vs. Discrete-Time Sinusoid Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CT["Continuous Time Domain"]
        CT1["x of t equals A cos omega0 t plus phi"] --> CT2["Always Periodic"]
        CT2 --> CT3["Period T equals 2 pi over omega0"]
        CT3 --> CT4["Frequency Range: Zero to Infinity"]
    end
    subgraph DT["Discrete Time Domain"]
        DT1["x of n equals A cos Omega0 n plus phi"] --> DT2["Periodic Only if Omega0 over 2 pi is Rational"]
        DT2 --> DT3["Period N equals 2 pi k over Omega0"]
        DT3 --> DT4["Frequency Range: Negative Pi to Pi"]
    end
    CT4 --> CMP["Key Distinction: DT Frequencies are Bounded and Can Alias"]
    DT4 --> CMP
```

## 4.4 Euler Decomposition — Single Sinusoid as Two Counter-Rotating Phasors

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S["A cos omega0 t plus phi"] --> E1["Apply Euler Identity"]
    E1 --> R1["Term 1: A over 2 e to the j phi times e to the j omega0 t"]
    E1 --> R2["Term 2: A over 2 e to the minus j phi times e to the minus j omega0 t"]
    R1 --> C1["Counter Clockwise Rotation at plus omega0"]
    R2 --> C2["Clockwise Rotation at minus omega0"]
    C1 --> SUM["Sum of Conjugate Pair equals Real Sinusoid"]
    C2 --> SUM
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define a continuous-time sinusoidal signal. List its parameters with their units.**

**Model Answer:**

A continuous-time sinusoidal signal is a smooth, periodic function of the form:

$$x(t) = A \cos(\omega_0 t + \phi)$$

It is described by four parameters:

- **Amplitude $A$** — peak value of the signal (in Volts, Amperes, or signal-dependent units).
- **Angular frequency $\omega_0$** — rate of change of the argument, measured in **radians per second (rad/s)**.
- **Initial phase $\phi$** — angular offset of the signal at $t = 0$, measured in **radians (rad)**.
- **Period $T = \dfrac{2\pi}{\omega_0}$** — time for one complete cycle, measured in **seconds (s)**.

The signal is periodic for all real values of $\omega_0$ and $T$.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State and explain the conditions under which a discrete-time sinusoid is periodic.**

**Model Answer:**

A discrete-time sinusoid is given by $x[n] = A \cos(\Omega_0 n + \phi)$. For this signal to be periodic, there must exist a smallest positive integer $N$ such that:

$$x[n+N] = x[n] \quad \text{for all } n \in \mathbb{Z}$$

This requires $\Omega_0 N = 2\pi k$ for some positive integer $k$. Equivalently:

$$\frac{\Omega_0}{2\pi} = \frac{k}{N} \in \mathbb{Q}$$

The signal is **periodic** if and only if the ratio $\frac{\Omega_0}{2\pi}$ is a **rational number**. If this ratio is irrational, no integer $N$ exists, and the signal is **aperiodic**.

This is in stark contrast to CT sinusoids, which are periodic for **all** real $\omega_0$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Provided)

---

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

#### **(a) [7 Marks]** Express the continuous-time sinusoid $x(t) = 8\cos(50\pi t + \pi/6)$ as a sum of complex exponentials. Hence, find its phasor representation.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the parameters.**

$A = 8$, $\omega_0 = 50\pi$ rad/s, $\phi = \pi/6$ rad.

**Step 2 — Apply the Euler cosine identity.**

$$
x(t) = A \cos(\omega_0 t + \phi) = \frac{A}{2} e^{j\phi} e^{j\omega_0 t} + \frac{A}{2} e^{-j\phi} e^{-j\omega_0 t}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the numerical values.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x(t) &= \frac{8}{2} e^{j\pi/6} e^{j50\pi t} + \frac{8}{2} e^{-j\pi/6} e^{-j50\pi t} \\
&= 4 e^{j\pi/6} e^{j50\pi t} + 4 e^{-j\pi/6} e^{-j50\pi t}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Express the phasor.**

The phasor representation is:

$$
X = A e^{j\phi} = 8 e^{j\pi/6}
$$

**Step 5 — Verify by computing the real part.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Re}\{X e^{j\omega_0 t}\} &= \text{Re}\{8 e^{j\pi/6} e^{j50\pi t}\} \\
&= \text{Re}\{8 e^{j(50\pi t + \pi/6)}\} \\
&= 8\cos(50\pi t + \pi/6) = x(t) \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence, $x(t) = 4 e^{j\pi/6} e^{j50\pi t} + 4 e^{-j\pi/6} e^{-j50\pi t}$ and the phasor is $X = 8 e^{j\pi/6}$.

---

#### **(b) [7 Marks]** Determine whether the discrete-time sinusoid $x[n] = 3\cos(0.4\pi n + \pi/3)$ is periodic. If yes, find the fundamental period.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the discrete-time frequency.**

$\Omega_0 = 0.4\pi = \frac{2\pi}{5}$ rad/sample.

**Step 2 — Compute the ratio $\frac{\Omega_0}{2\pi}$.**

$$
\frac{\Omega_0}{2\pi} = \frac{2\pi/5}{2\pi} = \frac{1}{5} \in \mathbb{Q}
$$

Since the ratio is rational, the signal **is periodic**.

**Step 3 — Compute the fundamental period.**

$$
N = \frac{2\pi k}{\Omega_0} = \frac{2\pi k}{2\pi/5} = 5k
$$

Choosing the smallest positive integer $k=1$ gives the fundamental period:

$$
\boxed{N = 5}
$$

**Step 4 — Verification by direct substitution.**

$$
x[n+5] = 3\cos(0.4\pi(n+5) + \pi/3) = 3\cos(0.4\pi n + 2\pi + \pi/3) = 3\cos(0.4\pi n + \pi/3) = x[n] \quad \checkmark
$$

---

### **Question B (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

#### **(a) [7 Marks]** Using the cosine addition formula, decompose $x(t) = 10\cos(200t + \pi/3)$ into its even and odd components.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Recall the cosine addition identity.**

$$
\cos(\alpha + \beta) = \cos\alpha \cos\beta - \sin\alpha \sin\beta
$$

**Step 2 — Apply it to $x(t)$.**

$$
x(t) = 10\cos(200t + \pi/3) = 10[\cos(200t)\cos(\pi/3) - \sin(200t)\sin(\pi/3)]
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the trigonometric values $\cos(\pi/3) = 1/2$ and $\sin(\pi/3) = \sqrt{3}/2$.**

$$
x(t) = 10\left[\frac{1}{2}\cos(200t) - \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\sin(200t)\right] = 5\cos(200t) - 5\sqrt{3}\sin(200t)
$$

**Step 4 — Identify the even and odd parts.**

- **Even part** $x_e(t) = 5\cos(200t)$ (since cosine is an even function).
- **Odd part** $x_o(t) = -5\sqrt{3}\sin(200t)$ (since sine is an odd function).

**Step 5 — Verify.**

$x(-t) = 5\cos(-200t) - 5\sqrt{3}\sin(-200t) = 5\cos(200t) + 5\sqrt{3}\sin(200t) = x_e(t) - x_o(t)$. This matches the property $x(t) = x_e(t) + x_o(t)$.

---

#### **(b) [7 Marks]** Two sinusoids $x_1(t) = 6\cos(50t)$ and $x_2(t) = 8\cos(50t + \pi/3)$ are added. Find the equivalent single sinusoid.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Represent each as a phasor.**

$$
X_1 = 6 e^{j \cdot 0} = 6 + j0
$$

$$
X_2 = 8 e^{j\pi/3} = 8\left(\cos\frac{\pi}{3} + j\sin\frac{\pi}{3}\right) = 8\left(\frac{1}{2} + j\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right) = 4 + j4\sqrt{3}
$$

**Step 2 — Add the phasors (rectangular form).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
X &= X_1 + X_2 \\
&= (6 + 4) + j(0 + 4\sqrt{3}) \\
&= 10 + j4\sqrt{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to polar form to find magnitude $A$ and phase $\phi$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \vert X \vert = \sqrt{10^2 + (4\sqrt{3})^2} = \sqrt{100 + 48} = \sqrt{148} = 2\sqrt{37} \approx 12.166 \\
\phi &= \angle X = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{4\sqrt{3}}{10}\right) = \tan^{-1}(0.6928) \approx 0.6051 \text{ rad} \approx 34.7^\circ
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Write the equivalent single sinusoid.**

$$
\boxed{x(t) = x_1(t) + x_2(t) = 2\sqrt{37}\,\cos(50t + 0.6051) \approx 12.17 \cos(50t + 0.605)}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing $\omega_0$ with $f_0$:** Writing $\omega_0 = 50$ Hz instead of $\omega_0 = 50\pi$ rad/s. Examiners deduct **2 marks** for unit errors.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of $1/2$ in Euler decomposition:** The two-sided expansion has $A/2$, not $A$. This is a **1-mark** common slip.
> 3. **Assuming all DT sinusoids are periodic:** Many students answer "yes, period $N = 2\pi/\Omega_0$" without checking whether $N$ is actually an integer. A **3-mark** loss if the periodicity condition is misapplied.
> 4. **Sign error in phasor addition:** Mixing up $+\phi$ and $-\phi$ for the conjugate. Always verify $\text{Re}\{X e^{j\omega_0 t}\}$ at the end.
> 5. **Arithmetic error in phasor addition:** Mistaking the rectangular-to-polar conversion. Show **all** intermediate steps for the conversion — partial credit requires visible work.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Sinusoid Definition:** A periodic function of the form $A\cos(\omega_0 t + \phi)$ (CT) or $A\cos(\Omega_0 n + \phi)$ (DT). It is fully characterized by **amplitude**, **frequency**, and **phase**.
- **Frequency Conversion:** $\omega_0 = 2\pi f_0 = \dfrac{2\pi}{T}$ (CT). Always use **radians** for the angle units in $\omega_0$.
- **Euler's Bridge:** $A\cos(\omega_0 t + \phi) = \dfrac{A}{2}e^{j\phi}e^{j\omega_0 t} + \dfrac{A}{2}e^{-j\phi}e^{-j\omega_0 t}$ — the cornerstone identity of Fourier analysis.
- **Phasor Concept:** A sinusoid equals $\text{Re}\{X e^{j\omega_0 t}\}$ where $X = A e^{j\phi}$ is the **complex phasor**. This eliminates time from the equation for LTI system analysis.
- **CT vs. DT Periodicity:** CT sinusoids are **always** periodic. DT sinusoids are periodic **only if** $\frac{\Omega_0}{2\pi}$ is rational.
- **DT Principal Range:** $\Omega_0$ is unique only in the range $-\pi < \Omega_0 \le \pi$. Values outside this range are aliases.
- **Even–Odd Decomposition:** $A\cos(\omega_0 t + \phi) = A\cos\phi \cdot \cos(\omega_0 t) - A\sin\phi \cdot \sin(\omega_0 t)$. The first term is even, the second is odd.
- **Phasor Addition Rule:** Sinusoids of the **same frequency** can be combined into a single sinusoid by adding their phasors. Sinusoids of **different frequencies cannot** be combined this way.
- **Time Shift:** $A\cos(\omega_0 t + \phi) = A\cos(\omega_0(t - t_0))$ where $t_0 = -\phi / \omega_0$. A positive $\phi$ corresponds to a **left shift** (time advance).
- **Maximum 4 Parameters:** Amplitude, frequency, phase, period. If you can list all four, you understand the signal.
- **Default Form:** Always default to the **cosine** form in KTU exams unless told otherwise. Convert sine to cosine using $\sin\theta = \cos(\theta - \pi/2)$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Spectrum Representation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST416 - SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Spectrum Representation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Spectrum Representation** is the mathematical decomposition of a signal (in either continuous-time or discrete-time, periodic or aperiodic form) into a weighted sum or integral of complex exponential basis functions. It describes the signal in the **frequency domain**, revealing the amplitudes, phases, and frequencies of the constituent sinusoids or complex exponentials that combine to form the signal.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme, signals are classified along **two axes**:

1. **Time axis** → Continuous-Time (CT) or Discrete-Time (DT)
2. **Periodic nature** → Periodic or Aperiodic

This yields **four canonical Fourier representations**, each producing a specific type of *spectrum*:

| Signal Class | Representation | Resulting Spectrum |
|---|---|---|
| Continuous-Time, Periodic | **Fourier Series (CTFS)** | Discrete, non-periodic |
| Continuous-Time, Aperiodic | **Fourier Transform (CTFT)** | Continuous, non-periodic |
| Discrete-Time, Periodic | **Discrete-Time Fourier Series (DTFS)** | Discrete, periodic |
| Discrete-Time, Aperiodic | **Discrete-Time Fourier Transform (DTFT)** | Continuous, periodic |

> [!NOTE]
> The duality between time and frequency is the central idea of spectrum representation. A signal that is compact (short) in time spreads out broadly in frequency, and vice versa — a property formalized by the **time–frequency duality** and the **uncertainty principle**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world analogy — The Glass Prism:** Imagine white sunlight entering a glass prism. The prism does not *create* colors; it **decomposes** the light into its component wavelengths (red, orange, yellow, green, blue, indigo, violet). The resulting *rainbow* is the **spectrum** of white light.
>
> - The **white light** is the signal in the time domain.
> - The **prism** is the mathematical transform (Fourier).
> - The **rainbow** is the spectrum representation (frequency domain).
>
> Just as the rainbow tells you *exactly* which colors are present and in what proportion, the spectrum of a signal tells you *exactly* which frequencies are present and with what amplitude/phase.

### 1.3 Why Spectrum Representation Matters

- **Communication systems** use it to design filters and allocate frequency bands (e.g., $\mathbf{f_c = 2.4 \text{ GHz}}$ for Wi-Fi).
- **Audio engineering** relies on it for equalizers and noise-cancellation.
- **Image processing** uses the 2-D extension for JPEG/MPEG compression.
- **Control systems** analyze stability via frequency response (Bode plots).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Standard physical constants that frequently appear in this module:
> - **Fundamental angular frequency** $\omega_0 = \dfrac{2\pi}{T}$
> - **Sampling frequency** $f_s = \dfrac{1}{T_s}$
> - **Nyquist rate** $f_{\text{Nyquist}} = 2 f_{\max}$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Fourier Representations — Logic Steps

A complete spectrum representation is always a **pair** of equations: an **analysis equation** (signal → coefficients) and a **synthesis equation** (coefficients → signal). The four pairs differ in summation/integration limits and the discrete/continuous nature of time and frequency.

#### A. Continuous-Time Fourier Series (CTFS) — Periodic CT Signals

Used for a CT signal $x(t)$ with fundamental period $T_0$.

- **Analysis equation** (extract coefficients):
$$a_k = \frac{1}{T_0} \int_{T_0} x(t) \, e^{-jk\omega_0 t} \, dt$$

- **Synthesis equation** (reconstruct signal):
$$x(t) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{+\infty} a_k \, e^{jk\omega_0 t}, \quad \omega_0 = \frac{2\pi}{T_0}$$

- The $a_k$ are complex numbers forming a **discrete, non-periodic spectrum** indexed by integer $k$.

#### B. Continuous-Time Fourier Transform (CTFT) — Aperiodic CT Signals

Used for an aperiodic CT signal $x(t)$ satisfying the Dirichlet conditions.

- **Analysis equation**:
$$X(j\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(t) \, e^{-j\omega t} \, dt$$

- **Synthesis equation**:
$$x(t) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} X(j\omega) \, e^{j\omega t} \, d\omega$$

- $X(j\omega)$ is a **continuous, non-periodic** function of $\omega$.

#### C. Discrete-Time Fourier Series (DTFS) — Periodic DT Signals

Used for a DT signal $x[n]$ with fundamental period $N$.

- **Analysis equation**:
$$a_k = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=\langle N \rangle} x[n] \, e^{-jk\Omega_0 n}, \quad \Omega_0 = \frac{2\pi}{N}$$

- **Synthesis equation**:
$$x[n] = \sum_{k=\langle N \rangle} a_k \, e^{jk\Omega_0 n}$$

- The sequence $a_k$ is **discrete and periodic** with period $N$.

#### D. Discrete-Time Fourier Transform (DTFT) — Aperiodic DT Signals

Used for an aperiodic, summable DT signal $x[n]$.

- **Analysis equation**:
$$X(e^{j\Omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty} x[n] \, e^{-j\Omega n}$$

- **Synthesis equation**:
$$x[n] = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{2\pi} X(e^{j\Omega}) \, e^{j\Omega n} \, d\Omega$$

- $X(e^{j\Omega})$ is a **continuous, periodic** function of $\Omega$ with period $2\pi$.

### 2.2 Magnitude and Phase Spectrum

Because the coefficients $a_k$ (and $X(j\omega)$, $X(e^{j\Omega})$) are generally complex, two real plots are conventionally drawn:

$$\boxed{\,|X(j\omega)| \; \text{(Magnitude Spectrum)} \quad \text{and} \quad \angle X(j\omega) \; \text{(Phase Spectrum)}\,}$$

- **Even-symmetric signals** have purely real spectra → phase is either $0$ or $\pi$.
- **Odd-symmetric signals** have purely imaginary spectra.
- The squared magnitude $\vert X(j\omega)\vert^2$ is the **energy spectral density (ESD)**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarizes **every key formula** you must memorize for the Module 1 spectrum-representation problems in the KTU University Exam.

| # | Representation | Analysis (Signal → Coeff) | Synthesis (Coeff → Signal) | Spectrum Nature |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **CTFS** (periodic, CT) | $a_k = \dfrac{1}{T_0} \int_{T_0} x(t) e^{-jk\omega_0 t} dt$ | $x(t) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} a_k e^{jk\omega_0 t}$ | Discrete, non-periodic |
| 2 | **CTFT** (aperiodic, CT) | $X(j\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) e^{-j\omega t} dt$ | $x(t) = \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} X(j\omega) e^{j\omega t} d\omega$ | Continuous, non-periodic |
| 3 | **DTFS** (periodic, DT) | $a_k = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{n=\langle N \rangle} x[n] e^{-jk\Omega_0 n}$ | $x[n] = \sum_{k=\langle N \rangle} a_k e^{jk\Omega_0 n}$ | Discrete, periodic |
| 4 | **DTFT** (aperiodic, DT) | $X(e^{j\Omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] e^{-j\Omega n}$ | $x[n] = \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \int_{2\pi} X(e^{j\Omega}) e^{j\Omega n} d\Omega$ | Continuous, periodic |

**Essential supporting definitions:**

| Quantity | Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|
| Fundamental angular frequency (CT) | $\omega_0 = \dfrac{2\pi}{T_0}$ | rad/s |
| Fundamental frequency (DT) | $\Omega_0 = \dfrac{2\pi}{N}$ | rad/sample |
| Parseval's theorem (CTFS) | $\dfrac{1}{T_0}\int_{T_0}\vert x(t)\vert^2 dt = \sum_{k}\vert a_k\vert^2$ | W (power) |
| Parseval's theorem (DTFS) | $\dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{n=\langle N \rangle}\vert x[n]\vert^2 = \sum_{k=\langle N \rangle}\vert a_k\vert^2$ | W (power) |
| Rayleigh's energy theorem (CTFT) | $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\vert x(t)\vert^2 dt = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\vert X(j\omega)\vert^2 d\omega$ | J (energy) |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **CTFS** → analysis of AC power waveforms, vibration analysis in mechanical systems.
- **CTFT** → radar signal processing, analog filter design, optics (Fourier optics).
- **DTFS** → digital audio effects, OFDM subcarrier modulation in 4G/5G.
- **DTFT** → foundation for the **Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT)** and the **FFT algorithm** used in real-time DSP, MP3 encoding, MRI reconstruction.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the CTFS Synthesis Equation

**Starting premise:** Any periodic CT signal $x(t)$ with period $T_0$ can be expanded as a sum of harmonically related complex exponentials.

**Step 1:** Write the assumed form (this is the *definition* of the Fourier series representation):

$$x(t) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{+\infty} a_k \, e^{jk\omega_0 t}, \quad \omega_0 = \frac{2\pi}{T_0}$$

**Step 2:** Multiply both sides by $e^{-jm\omega_0 t}$ (the complex-conjugate basis) and integrate over one full period $T_0$:

$$\int_{T_0} x(t) \, e^{-jm\omega_0 t} \, dt = \int_{T_0} \left[ \sum_{k=-\infty}^{+\infty} a_k \, e^{jk\omega_0 t} \right] e^{-jm\omega_0 t} \, dt$$

**Step 3:** Exchange summation and integration (justified for absolutely summable series):

$$\int_{T_0} x(t) \, e^{-jm\omega_0 t} \, dt = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{+\infty} a_k \int_{T_0} e^{j(k-m)\omega_0 t} \, dt$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate the integral. Using the identity that exponentials over an integer number of full periods integrate to **zero unless the exponent is zero**:

$$\int_{T_0} e^{j(k-m)\omega_0 t} \, dt = \begin{cases} T_0, & k = m \\ 0, & k \neq m \end{cases}$$

This is called the **orthogonality of complex exponentials** — a cornerstone result.

**Step 5:** Substitute back. Only the term with $k = m$ survives:

$$\int_{T_0} x(t) \, e^{-jm\omega_0 t} \, dt = a_m \cdot T_0$$

**Step 6:** Solve for $a_m$ (rename $m \to k$):

$$\boxed{\,a_k = \frac{1}{T_0} \int_{T_0} x(t) \, e^{-jk\omega_0 t} \, dt\,}$$

This is the **CTFS analysis equation**, completing the derivation.

### 3.2 Worked Example — CTFS of a Rectangular Pulse Train

**Problem:** Find the Fourier series coefficients of the periodic CT signal

$$x(t) = \begin{cases} 1, & 0 < t < T_1 \\ 0, & T_1 < t < T_0 \end{cases}, \quad x(t+T_0) = x(t)$$

**Step 1:** Identify parameters. Fundamental frequency: $\omega_0 = \dfrac{2\pi}{T_0}$.

**Step 2:** Apply the analysis equation over the interval $[0, T_0]$ (signal is non-zero only on $[0, T_1]$):

$$a_k = \frac{1}{T_0} \int_{0}^{T_1} (1) \cdot e^{-jk\omega_0 t} \, dt$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate the integral. For $k \neq 0$:

$$a_k = \frac{1}{T_0} \left[ \frac{e^{-jk\omega_0 t}}{-jk\omega_0} \right]_{0}^{T_1} = \frac{1}{T_0} \cdot \frac{1 - e^{-jk\omega_0 T_1}}{jk\omega_0}$$

**Step 4:** Use $e^{-jk\omega_0 T_1} = e^{-jk(2\pi/T_0)T_1}$. Multiply numerator and denominator by $T_0$:

$$a_k = \frac{1 - e^{-jk\omega_0 T_1}}{jk\omega_0 T_0} = \frac{1}{jk\omega_0 T_0}\left(1 - e^{-jk\omega_0 T_1}\right)$$

**Step 5:** Factor $e^{-jk\omega_0 T_1/2}$ from the numerator:

$$a_k = \frac{e^{-jk\omega_0 T_1/2}}{jk\omega_0 T_0}\left(e^{jk\omega_0 T_1/2} - e^{-jk\omega_0 T_1/2}\right) = \frac{e^{-jk\omega_0 T_1/2}}{jk\omega_0 T_0} \cdot 2j\sin\!\left(\frac{k\omega_0 T_1}{2}\right)$$

**Step 6:** Simplify. The $j$'s cancel. Defining the **sinc-like envelope** $\text{Sa}(x) = \dfrac{\sin x}{x}$:

$$\boxed{\,a_k = \frac{T_1}{T_0} \cdot e^{-jk\omega_0 T_1/2} \cdot \text{Sa}\!\left(\frac{k\omega_0 T_1}{2}\right)\,}$$

**Step 7:** Special case $k = 0$ (limit as $k \to 0$): $a_0 = \dfrac{T_1}{T_0}$ — which is just the **duty cycle** (average value of the pulse train). ✓

### 3.3 Worked Example — DTFT of a Two-Point Signal

**Problem:** Compute the DTFT of $x[n] = \delta[n] + \delta[n-1]$.

**Step 1:** Apply the DTFT analysis equation:

$$X(e^{j\Omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty} x[n] \, e^{-j\Omega n} = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty} (\delta[n] + \delta[n-1]) e^{-j\Omega n}$$

**Step 2:** Use the sifting property $\sum f[n]\delta[n-n_0] = f[n_0]$:

$$X(e^{j\Omega}) = 1 + e^{-j\Omega}$$

**Step 3:** Factor out $e^{-j\Omega/2}$ to read magnitude and phase:

$$X(e^{j\Omega}) = e^{-j\Omega/2}\left(e^{j\Omega/2} + e^{-j\Omega/2}\right) = 2e^{-j\Omega/2}\cos\!\left(\frac{\Omega}{2}\right)$$

**Step 4:** Magnitude and phase:

$$|X(e^{j\Omega})| = 2\left|\cos\!\left(\frac{\Omega}{2}\right)\right|, \quad \angle X(e^{j\Omega}) = -\frac{\Omega}{2} + \pi \cdot u\!\left(-\cos(\Omega/2)\right)$$

**Step 5:** Verify periodicity: $X(e^{j(\Omega + 2\pi)}) = 1 + e^{-j(\Omega+2\pi)} = 1 + e^{-j\Omega} = X(e^{j\Omega})$ ✓ (period $2\pi$ as required).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Four-Fourier-Representations Decision Flow

The flowchart below maps **any** signal to its correct spectrum representation. This is the most important diagnostic chart for KTU Module 1 problems.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["INPUT SIGNAL x"] --> B{"Continuous or Discrete?"}
    B -- "Continuous-Time x(t)" --> C{"Periodic or Aperiodic?"}
    B -- "Discrete-Time x[n]" --> D{"Periodic or Aperiodic?"}
    C -- "Periodic, period T0" --> E["CTFS"]
    C -- "Aperiodic" --> F["CTFT"]
    D -- "Periodic, period N" --> G["DTFS"]
    D -- "Aperiodic" --> H["DTFT"]
    E --> I["Spectrum: Discrete, Non-Periodic"]
    F --> J["Spectrum: Continuous, Non-Periodic"]
    G --> K["Spectrum: Discrete, Periodic"]
    H --> L["Spectrum: Continuous, Periodic"]
    I --> M["Examples: AC mains 50Hz, square wave clock"]
    J --> N["Examples: Speech signal, music waveform"]
    K --> O["Examples: Sampled periodic waveform, OFDM symbol"]
    L --> P["Examples: Digital audio, image pixels"]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — From Time-Domain Signal to Spectrum Plot

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["x(t) or x[n] TIME-DOMAIN SIGNAL"] --> B["Apply Analysis Equation"]
    B --> C["Compute Coefficients a_k or X"]
    C --> D["Separate into Real and Imaginary Parts"]
    D --> E["Magnitude Spectrum |X| vs Frequency"]
    D --> F["Phase Spectrum angle X vs Frequency"]
    E --> G["PLOT 1: AMPLITUDE RESPONSE"]
    F --> H["PLOT 2: PHASE RESPONSE"]
    I["Reconstruct via Synthesis Equation"] --> J["Verify time-domain signal"]
    C --> I
```

### 4.3 Time–Frequency Duality Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Compact in Time"] --> B["Spread in Frequency"]
    B --> C["Many high frequencies needed"]
    C --> D["e.g., delta impulse: flat spectrum"]
    E["Spread in Time"] --> F["Compact in Frequency"]
    F --> G["Single-tone sinusoid: one spike"]
    G --> H["e.g., pure tone: 2 spikes at +/-f0"]
```

### 4.4 Magnitude–Phase Spectrum Visualization (Generic Plot Layout)

```mermaid
xychart-beta
    title "Generic Magnitude Spectrum of a Periodic Signal"
    x-axis "Frequency k omega0 (rad/s)"
    y-axis "|a_k|"
    line [0.5, 0.42, 0.32, 0.18, 0.05, -0.05, -0.18, -0.32, -0.42, -0.5]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Distinguish between Continuous-Time Fourier Series (CTFS) and Continuous-Time Fourier Transform (CTFT). When is each used?**

**Model Answer:**

- **CTFS** is used for **periodic continuous-time signals**. It produces a **discrete, non-periodic spectrum** of complex coefficients $a_k$ located at integer multiples of the fundamental frequency $\omega_0 = \frac{2\pi}{T_0}$.

$$a_k = \frac{1}{T_0}\int_{T_0} x(t)e^{-jk\omega_0 t}dt$$

- **CTFT** is used for **aperiodic continuous-time signals** that satisfy the Dirichlet conditions. It produces a **continuous, non-periodic** function $X(j\omega)$ defined over all $\omega \in (-\infty, \infty)$.

$$X(j\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) e^{-j\omega t} dt$$

In short, periodicity in time → **discrete** spectrum; periodicity in frequency → **periodic** spectrum. The two are duals of each other.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Define magnitude spectrum and phase spectrum. What is the significance of the energy spectral density (ESD)?**

**Model Answer:**

- The **magnitude spectrum** $\vert X(j\omega) \vert$ gives the strength (amplitude) of each frequency component.
- The **phase spectrum** $\angle X(j\omega)$ gives the relative phase alignment of each component.
- The **energy spectral density (ESD)** is defined as $E_{xx}(\omega) = \vert X(j\omega) \vert^2$ and satisfies **Parseval/Rayleigh's theorem**:

$$E = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^2 dt = \frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert X(j\omega) \vert^2 d\omega$$

Significance: it shows **how signal energy is distributed across frequencies** — vital in filter design, bandwidth analysis, and noise characterization.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]`

**(a)** Derive the **analysis and synthesis equations of the Continuous-Time Fourier Series (CTFS)** for a periodic signal $x(t)$ with period $T_0$. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** For the periodic square wave

$$x(t) = \begin{cases} +A, & 0 < t < T_0/2 \\ -A, & T_0/2 < t < T_0 \end{cases}$$

compute its Fourier series coefficients $a_k$, and sketch the magnitude spectrum $\vert a_k \vert$ vs $k$. **\[7 Marks\]**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Derivation:** (Write the **6-step derivation from Section 3.1** verbatim, including orthogonality argument. Each of the six steps is worth ~1 mark; explicitly state **"by orthogonality"** at Step 4 — KTU examiners award bonus marks for naming theorems.)

**Final boxed result:**
