$$a_k = \frac{1}{T_0}\int_{T_0} x(t) e^{-jk\omega_0 t}dt, \qquad x(t) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} a_k e^{jk\omega_0 t}$$

**(b) Computation:**

**Step 1:** Identify $\omega_0 = \dfrac{2\pi}{T_0}$. The signal is odd-symmetric about $t = T_0/2$ (a half-wave symmetric square wave), so $a_k$ is **purely imaginary**.

**Step 2:** Apply analysis equation:

$$a_k = \frac{1}{T_0}\left[ \int_{0}^{T_0/2} A\,e^{-jk\omega_0 t}dt + \int_{T_0/2}^{T_0}(-A)\,e^{-jk\omega_0 t}dt \right]$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate each integral. For the first:

$$I_1 = A\cdot \frac{1-e^{-jk\omega_0 T_0/2}}{jk\omega_0}$$

**Step 4:** For the second integral, substitute $u = t - T_0/2$:

$$I_2 = -A\cdot \frac{e^{-jk\omega_0 T_0/2} - e^{-jk\omega_0 T_0}}{jk\omega_0} = -A\cdot \frac{e^{-jk\pi} - 1}{jk\omega_0}$$

**Step 5:** Add $I_1 + I_2$. Since $e^{-jk\omega_0 T_0} = 1$:

$$a_k = \frac{A}{jk\omega_0 T_0}\left[ 1 - 2e^{-jk\pi} + 1 \right] = \frac{2A}{jk\omega_0 T_0}\left[ 1 - (-1)^k \right]$$

**Step 6:** Analyze two cases:

- If $k$ is even: $1 - (-1)^k = 0 \Rightarrow a_k = 0$.
- If $k$ is odd: $1 - (-1)^k = 2 \Rightarrow a_k = \dfrac{4A}{jk\omega_0 T_0} = \dfrac{2A}{jk\pi}$.

**Final answer:**

$$\boxed{\,a_k = \begin{cases} \dfrac{2A}{j\pi k}, & k \text{ odd} \\[4pt] 0, & k \text{ even} \end{cases}\,}$$

**Magnitude spectrum:**

$$|a_k| = \frac{2A}{\pi \vert k \vert}, \quad k = \pm 1, \pm 3, \pm 5, \ldots$$

The spectrum consists of **odd harmonics only** with envelope $\propto \dfrac{1}{\vert k \vert}$, characteristic of a square wave.

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Modified]`

**(a)** State and explain the **Discrete-Time Fourier Transform (DTFT) pair**. Discuss the **periodicity and continuity** of the resulting spectrum. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** Compute the DTFT of the causal exponential signal $x[n] = a^n u[n]$, where $\vert a \vert < 1$. Hence plot the magnitude spectrum. **\[7 Marks\]**

#### Model Solution:

**(a)** The DTFT pair:

$$X(e^{j\Omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] e^{-j\Omega n}, \qquad x[n] = \frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{2\pi} X(e^{j\Omega}) e^{j\Omega n} d\Omega$$

**Periodicity:** $X(e^{j(\Omega + 2\pi)}) = X(e^{j\Omega})$, so the DTFT is always **periodic with period $2\pi$** in $\Omega$. This is because discrete-time exponentials $e^{j\Omega n}$ are themselves $2\pi$-periodic in $\Omega$.

**Continuity:** If $x[n]$ is absolutely summable, $X(e^{j\Omega})$ is a **continuous** function of $\Omega$. (DTFT of a finite-duration sequence is always continuous.)

**(b) Computation:**

**Step 1:** Apply the DTFT analysis equation:

$$X(e^{j\Omega}) = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} a^n e^{-j\Omega n} = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} (a e^{-j\Omega})^n$$

**Step 2:** Recognize this as an infinite geometric series with ratio $r = a e^{-j\Omega}$. Converges iff $\vert r \vert = \vert a \vert < 1$, which is satisfied.

**Step 3:** Sum the series:

$$X(e^{j\Omega}) = \frac{1}{1 - a e^{-j\Omega}}$$

**Step 4:** Compute magnitude. Multiply numerator and denominator by $(1 - a e^{j\Omega})$:

$$X(e^{j\Omega}) = \frac{1 - a e^{j\Omega}}{(1 - a e^{-j\Omega})(1 - a e^{j\Omega})} = \frac{1 - a\cos\Omega + j a \sin\Omega}{1 - 2a\cos\Omega + a^2}$$

**Step 5:** Magnitude squared:

$$|X(e^{j\Omega})|^2 = \frac{1 - 2a\cos\Omega + a^2}{(1 - 2a\cos\Omega + a^2)^2} = \frac{1}{1 - 2a\cos\Omega + a^2}$$

$$\boxed{\,|X(e^{j\Omega})| = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 - 2a\cos\Omega + a^2}}\,}$$

**Plot behavior:**
- At $\Omega = 0$: $|X| = \dfrac{1}{1 - a}$ (maximum, low-frequency peak).
- At $\Omega = \pi$: $|X| = \dfrac{1}{1 + a}$ (minimum, high-frequency dip).
- Spectrum is **periodic with period $2\pi$** and **peaks near $\Omega = 0$**, confirming that low-frequency exponentials dominate for $\vert a \vert < 1$ (the signal is "smooth").

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mark-losing mistakes in spectrum-representation problems (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the $\frac{1}{2\pi}$ factor** in the CTFT/DTFT synthesis equation. KTU examiners deduct **1 full mark** for this. Memorize: analysis has $1$, synthesis has $\frac{1}{2\pi}$.
> 2. **Mixing up the period**: For CTFS, period is $T_0$ in **seconds**; for DTFS, period is $N$ in **samples**. The $\frac{1}{T_0}$ vs $\frac{1}{N}$ normalization differs.
> 3. **Forgetting to mention "orthogonality"** in CTFS derivations. Naming the principle earns **+1 mark bonus** in KTU valuations.
> 4. **Forgetting that DTFT is always periodic with period $2\pi$** — a classic 2-mark deduction.
> 5. **Not stating Dirichlet conditions** when asked to "compute the Fourier transform" of a general signal. Always list: (i) absolutely integrable, (ii) finite number of discontinuities, (iii) finite number of extrema — examiners love this.
> 6. **Plotting a continuous spectrum as discrete points or vice versa** — a 1-mark cosmetic deduction. Always state the spectrum nature first.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-density rapid-revision checklist for Spectrum Representation (Module 1):**
>
> ☐ **Four representations** — CTFS, CTFT, DTFS, DTFT — each with a clear time/frequency type.
>
> ☐ **CTFS**: periodic CT signal ↔ **discrete, non-periodic** spectrum $a_k$ at $\omega = k\omega_0$.
>
> ☐ **CTFT**: aperiodic CT signal ↔ **continuous, non-periodic** spectrum $X(j\omega)$.
>
> ☐ **DTFS**: periodic DT signal ↔ **discrete, periodic** spectrum $a_k$ (period $N$).
>
> ☐ **DTFT**: aperiodic DT signal ↔ **continuous, periodic** spectrum $X(e^{j\Omega})$ (period $2\pi$).
>
> ☐ **Two plots per spectrum** — magnitude $\vert \cdot \vert$ and phase $\angle(\cdot)$.
>
> ☐ **ESD** = $\vert X(j\omega) \vert^2$; **PSD** = $\frac{\vert a_k \vert^2}{T_0}$.
>
> ☐ **Parseval's theorem** — energy/power is preserved in both time and frequency domains.
>
> ☐ **Dirichlet conditions** — required for CTFT to exist; always quote them.
>
> ☐ **Orthogonality of complex exponentials** — the engine behind all CTFS/DTFS derivations.
>
> ☐ **Duality**: Compact in time ↔ spread in frequency; this is the **time–frequency uncertainty principle**.
>
> ☐ **Sinc function** $\text{Sa}(x) = \sin x / x$ — the canonical shape of rectangular-pulse spectra.
>
> ☐ **Half-wave symmetric** signals have **only odd harmonics** in their CTFS.
>
> ☐ **Causal exponential** $a^n u[n]$ ↔ rational DTFT with a pole at $z = a$ on the unit-circle approach.
>
> ☐ Always include the **$e^{-j\Omega/2}$-type phase factor** when factoring complex exponentials in DTFT problems.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sampling and Aliasing (Concept Only)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST416 - SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Sampling and Aliasing (Concept Only)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Sampling

**Sampling** is the process of converting a continuous-time (analog) signal $x(t)$ into a discrete-time signal $x[n]$ by recording the values of the continuous signal at uniformly spaced instants of time, governed by a parameter called the **Sampling Period ($T_s$)** or equivalently the **Sampling Frequency ($f_s$)**.

Mathematically, ideal (impulse) sampling is expressed as:

$$x_s(t) = x(t) \cdot \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t - nT_s)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Nyquist-Shannon Sampling Theorem):**
> A bandlimited continuous-time signal $x(t)$ having no frequency components above a maximum frequency $f_m$ (Hz) can be uniquely and completely reconstructed from its samples, **provided that the sampling frequency $f_s$ satisfies the condition**:
> $$f_s \ge 2 f_m$$
> The minimum allowed sampling rate, $f_s = 2f_m$, is known as the **Nyquist Rate**, and $f_s/2$ is known as the **Nyquist Frequency**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Movie Reel

Imagine watching a movie. A film is actually a sequence of still photographs (frames) shown rapidly. Each frame is a "sample" of reality. If the camera records 24 frames per second, your brain stitches them together into smooth motion.

- If the camera captures **too few frames per second** (e.g., a spinning wheel captured at 5 fps), the wheel looks like it is **rotating backwards or standing still**. This visual illusion is **aliasing** — high-frequency motion "folds" onto a false lower frequency.
- The **frame rate (samples per second)** must be at least **twice the fastest motion frequency** to reproduce it faithfully. This is the same idea as the Nyquist theorem.

## 1.3 What is Aliasing?

> [!NOTE]
> **Aliasing** is the phenomenon where two completely different continuous-time signals become **indistinguishable** when sampled at the same rate. Equivalently, high-frequency components of a signal "pretend" to be lower-frequency components after sampling, causing irreversible distortion.

A classic everyday example: the **wagon-wheel effect** in cinema, where a rotating wheel appears to rotate in the wrong direction due to insufficient frame rate.

## 1.4 Key Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Sampling Period | $T_s$ | Time gap between two consecutive samples (seconds) |
| Sampling Frequency | $f_s = 1/T_s$ | Number of samples per second (Hz) |
| Maximum Signal Frequency | $f_m$ | Highest frequency present in $x(t)$ |
| Nyquist Rate | $f_N = 2 f_m$ | Minimum sampling rate to avoid aliasing |
| Nyquist Frequency | $f_s/2$ | Highest frequency that can be represented uniquely |
| Folded Frequency | $f_a$ | The false frequency that appears due to aliasing |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Logic of Sampling

1. **Identify the Bandwidth of the Signal** — Find the maximum frequency $f_m$ present in the analog signal. If the signal is not strictly bandlimited, an **anti-aliasing filter** (low-pass filter) must be applied before sampling.
2. **Choose a Sampling Frequency** — Select $f_s$ such that $f_s \ge 2 f_m$. The closer $f_s$ is to $2f_m$, the more "ideal" the system; in practice, $f_s$ is chosen 3–5 times $f_m$.
3. **Capture Samples at Multiples of $T_s$** — Take the instantaneous amplitude of $x(t)$ at $t = 0, T_s, 2T_s, \ldots, nT_s$.
4. **Store / Transmit / Process** — The discrete samples $x[n] = x(nT_s)$ are the digital representation of the analog signal.
5. **Reconstruction (Optional)** — At the receiver, a low-pass filter is used to recover the analog signal from its samples (only if the Nyquist condition is satisfied).

> [!TIP]
> **Why does sampling work at all?**
> When a bandlimited signal is multiplied by an impulse train, the spectrum of $x(t)$ gets **replicated** at integer multiples of $f_s$. As long as these replicas do not overlap, a low-pass filter can isolate the original spectrum and perfectly reconstruct $x(t)$.

## 2.2 Types of Sampling (Conceptual Overview)

- **Impulse (Ideal) Sampling** — $x(t)$ is multiplied by an ideal impulse train. Mathematically clean, physically unrealizable.
- **Natural Sampling** — $x(t)$ is multiplied by a rectangular pulse train. The top of each sample follows the natural shape of the signal.
- **Flat-Top Sampling** — Each sample is held constant for a short duration using a Sample-and-Hold (S/H) circuit. Most common in real ADCs.

## 2.3 Understanding Aliasing — The "Fold-Over" Mechanism

When $f_s < 2f_m$, the spectral replicas in the frequency domain **overlap**. The overlapping region causes the high-frequency tail of one replica to add to the low-frequency tail of the next, producing a single distorted spectrum that **cannot be separated** by any filter.

The aliased (apparent) frequency of a true frequency $f$ when sampled at $f_s$ is given by:

$$f_a = \left\vert f - k f_s \right\vert, \quad k = \text{round}\left(\frac{f}{f_s}\right)$$

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sampling Period from Frequency | $T_s = \dfrac{1}{f_s}$ | Seconds |
| 2 | Nyquist Rate | $f_s \ge 2 f_m$ | Hz |
| 3 | Nyquist Frequency | $f_{Nyq} = \dfrac{f_s}{2}$ | Hz |
| 4 | Sampled Signal (Ideal) | $x_s(t) = x(t) \cdot \sum \delta(t - nT_s)$ | Impulse train modulation |
| 5 | Aliased Frequency | $f_a = \vert f - k f_s \vert$ | $k$ chosen to bring $f_a$ into $[0, f_s/2]$ |
| 6 | Reconstruction Condition | $f_m \le f_{Nyq}$ | Anti-aliasing pre-filter mandatory |
| 7 | Reconstruction Filter Cutoff | $f_c = f_s / 2$ | Ideal low-pass |
| 8 | Signal-to-Noise after sampling | Unchanged if Nyquist satisfied | — |
| 9 | Practical Sampling Choice | $f_s = (3 \text{ to } 5) \times f_m$ | Engineering rule of thumb |
| 10 | Units of $f_s$ | Hertz (Hz) or samples/sec | Same dimensionally |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Digital Audio (CD Quality)**: Audio signals have $f_m = 20$ kHz, so CDs use $f_s = 44.1$ kHz (slightly above $2f_m$).
- **Telecommunications**: Speech is bandlimited to 4 kHz and sampled at 8 kHz.
- **Medical Imaging (MRI/CT)**: Sampling theorem governs how finely we must scan a patient to avoid diagnostic artifacts (aliasing).
- **Oscilloscopes and ADCs**: Anti-aliasing filters are mandatory front-end components to enforce the Nyquist condition.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Since the topic is flagged as **"Concept Only"** in the KTU 2024 syllabus, the focus here is on intuitive derivations and conceptual proofs rather than full mathematical rigor. Key relationships are still derived step-by-step for board-level clarity.

## 3.1 Why Sampling Frequency Must Be at Least $2f_m$ — A Conceptual Derivation

Consider a pure sinusoid $x(t) = A \cos(2\pi f t)$ where $f$ is the signal frequency. We sample it at instants $t = nT_s$, so we observe:

$$x[n] = A \cos(2\pi f n T_s) = A \cos(2\pi n f / f_s)$$

Now, let us ask: **can a different sinusoid $y(t) = A \cos(2\pi f' t)$ produce the exact same samples?**

Setting $y[n] = x[n]$:

$$A \cos(2\pi n f' / f_s) = A \cos(2\pi n f / f_s)$$

This holds if the arguments differ by an integer multiple of $2\pi$:

$$\frac{2\pi n f'}{f_s} = \frac{2\pi n f}{f_s} \pm 2\pi k, \quad k \in \mathbb{Z}$$

Dividing by $2\pi n$:

$$f' = f \pm k f_s$$

**Interpretation:** A sinusoid of frequency $f$ is *indistinguishable* from another sinusoid of frequency $f' = f \pm k f_s$ after sampling.

To ensure **uniqueness** (only $k = 0$ is valid), we require that no other value of $k$ produces a frequency in the same band. Choosing the principal range $-\frac{f_s}{2} \le f \le \frac{f_s}{2}$, uniqueness is preserved **only if** all signal frequencies lie strictly within this band, i.e.:

$$f_m \le \frac{f_s}{2} \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad f_s \ge 2 f_m$$

This is the **Nyquist Criterion**, derived from first principles.

## 3.2 Computing the Aliased Frequency (Analytical Method)

**Problem Setup:** A signal of true frequency $f = 7$ kHz is sampled at $f_s = 4$ kHz. Find the apparent (aliased) frequency.

**Step 1 —** Check Nyquist condition: $f_s = 4$ kHz, $2f_m = 14$ kHz. Since $f_s < 2f_m$, aliasing is guaranteed.

**Step 2 —** Find integer $k$ such that the aliased frequency lies in the principal range $[0, f_s/2] = [0, 2]$ kHz.

$$\begin{aligned}
f_a &= \vert f - k f_s \vert = \vert 7 - k(4) \vert
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 —** Test values of $k$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
k = 0 &: \quad f_a = \vert 7 - 0 \vert = 7 \text{ kHz (out of range)} \\
k = 1 &: \quad f_a = \vert 7 - 4 \vert = 3 \text{ kHz (still > 2 kHz)} \\
k = 2 &: \quad f_a = \vert 7 - 8 \vert = 1 \text{ kHz} \;\; \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 —** Therefore, a 7 kHz sinusoid sampled at 4 kHz appears as a **1 kHz** sinusoid.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **General Rule for Aliased Frequency Calculation:**
> $$f_a = \left\{ \begin{array}{ll} f, & f \le f_s/2 \\ \vert f - k f_s \vert, & \text{otherwise}, \; k = \left\lfloor f / f_s + 0.5 \right\rfloor \end{array} \right.$$
> The aliased frequency is the original frequency **folded back** into the range $[0, f_s/2]$.

## 3.3 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Scenario | $f_s$ vs $2f_m$ | Spectral Replicas | Reconstruction | Result |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Oversampling | $f_s \gg 2f_m$ | Well-separated | Easy (LP filter) | Perfect |
| Critical Sampling | $f_s = 2f_m$ | Just touching | Theoretically possible | Ideal, impractical |
| Undersampling | $f_s < 2f_m$ | Overlapping | Impossible | **Aliasing occurs** |
| No Pre-filtering | $f_s < 2f_m$, signal not bandlimited | Severe overlap | Impossible | Severe aliasing |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Sampling and Reconstruction Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Analog Signal x of t] --> B[Anti Aliasing Low Pass Filter]
    B --> C[Sampler Switch at f sub s]
    C --> D[Discrete Samples x of n]
    D --> E[Digital Processing or Transmission]
    E --> F[Digital to Analog Converter DAC]
    F --> G[Reconstruction Low Pass Filter]
    G --> H[Reconstructed Signal y of t]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **Anti-Aliasing LPF** is the single most important block in any real-world sampling system. It enforces the bandlimited assumption required by the Nyquist theorem.

## 4.2 Conceptual Flowchart — Decision Logic for Sampling Validity

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Start Identify Signal] --> Q{Signal Bandlimited?}
    Q -- No --> R[Apply Anti Aliasing LPF]
    Q -- Yes --> S[Determine f sub m]
    R --> S
    S --> T{Choose f sub s greater than or equal 2 f sub m?}
    T -- Yes --> U[Sampling Valid No Aliasing]
    T -- No --> V[Increase f sub s OR Reject Signal]
    U --> W[Proceed to Reconstruction]
    V --> X[Aliasing Will Occur Distortion]
```

## 4.3 Frequency-Domain Picture — Effect of Sampling

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ORIGINAL[Original Spectrum]
        A1[X of f Centered at 0]
    end
    subgraph SAMPLED[Sampled Spectrum with f sub s greater than 2 f sub m]
        B1[Replica at 0]
        B2[Replica at f sub s]
        B3[Replica at 2 f sub s]
        B4[Replica at minus f sub s]
    end
    subgraph ALIASED[Sampled Spectrum with f sub s less than 2 f sub m]
        C1[Replica at 0 overlapping]
        C2[Replica at f sub s overlapping with main lobe]
    end
    ORIGINAL --> SAMPLED
    ORIGINAL --> ALIASED
```

**Reading the diagram:** The leftmost branch shows the original signal's spectrum. The middle branch illustrates correct sampling — clean, non-overlapping replicas. The rightmost branch shows aliasing — the replicas collide and merge into a single corrupted spectrum.

## 4.4 Visualization of the Aliasing "Fold-Over"

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[True Frequency 7 kHz] --> B[Sample at 4 kHz]
    B --> C[Apparent Frequency 1 kHz]
    D[True Frequency 9 kHz] --> B
    B --> E[Apparent Frequency 1 kHz]
    F[True Frequency 5 kHz] --> B
    B --> G[Apparent Frequency 1 kHz]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Notice how **three different true frequencies (5, 7, 9 kHz)** all map to the **same aliased frequency of 1 kHz** when sampled at 4 kHz. This is exactly why aliasing is called the **"indistinguishability problem"** — the sampled data is identical for these three different signals, making perfect reconstruction impossible.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define the Nyquist Rate and Nyquist Frequency. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**Model Answer:**

> **Nyquist Rate** is the *minimum* sampling frequency required to convert a bandlimited analog signal into its digital form without loss of information. It is defined as twice the maximum frequency component present in the signal:
> $$f_{Nyquist\ Rate} = 2 f_m$$
>
> **Nyquist Frequency** is the *highest* frequency that can be unambiguously represented in the discrete-time signal, equal to half the sampling frequency:
> $$f_{Nyquist\ Frequency} = \frac{f_s}{2}$$
>
> For faithful reconstruction, every frequency in the input signal must satisfy $f \le f_s/2$.

---

### Question 2: What is Aliasing? State one practical method to avoid it. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Model Answer:**

> **Aliasing** is the phenomenon in which high-frequency components of a continuous-time signal, when sampled at a rate below the Nyquist rate, appear as false (lower) frequencies in the sampled signal, causing irreversible distortion.
>
> **Practical method to avoid aliasing:** Apply an **anti-aliasing low-pass filter (pre-filter)** before the sampler. This filter removes all frequency components above $f_s/2$, ensuring the input signal is strictly bandlimited and the Nyquist condition is satisfied.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A: Sampling Theorem and Frequency Analysis [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Statement:** *(a)* State and prove the sampling theorem for low-pass bandlimited signals. *(7 marks)*
*(b)* A signal $x(t)$ contains frequency components up to 5 kHz. *(i)* Find the minimum sampling rate to avoid aliasing. *(ii)* If the signal is sampled at 7 kHz, what is the Nyquist frequency? *(iii)* Will aliasing occur? Justify. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — Statement and Proof Sketch (7 Marks)

**Statement:** A bandlimited signal $x(t)$ with maximum frequency $f_m$ Hz can be completely reconstructed from its samples $x(nT_s)$ if the sampling frequency satisfies $f_s \ge 2 f_m$.

**Proof Outline (Time-Domain Intuitive Approach):**

The sampled signal is modeled as $x(t)$ multiplied by an impulse train:

$$x_s(t) = x(t) \cdot \delta_{T_s}(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT_s)\, \delta(t - nT_s)$$

Taking the Fourier Transform, multiplication in time becomes **convolution in frequency** between the original spectrum $X(f)$ and the impulse train in frequency (spaced at $f_s$):

$$X_s(f) = f_s \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X(f - k f_s)$$

This means the spectrum $X(f)$ gets **replicated** at every integer multiple of $f_s$. When $f_s \ge 2f_m$, these replicas are non-overlapping. A low-pass filter with cutoff $f_c = f_s/2$ and gain $T_s$ then perfectly extracts $X(f)$, and the inverse Fourier transform yields the original $x(t)$.

Hence, reconstruction is possible **if and only if** $f_s \ge 2 f_m$. ∎

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks)

**Given:** $f_m = 5$ kHz.

**(i) Minimum sampling rate (Nyquist Rate):**

$$f_s \ge 2 f_m = 2 \times 5 = 10 \text{ kHz}$$

So the **minimum sampling rate is 10 kHz**.

**(ii) Nyquist frequency at $f_s = 7$ kHz:**

$$f_{Nyq} = \frac{f_s}{2} = \frac{7}{2} = 3.5 \text{ kHz}$$

**(iii) Will aliasing occur?**

Since the actual sampling rate $f_s = 7$ kHz is **less than** the required Nyquist rate of 10 kHz:

$$f_s = 7 \text{ kHz} < 2 f_m = 10 \text{ kHz}$$

The Nyquist condition is **violated**. The spectral replicas will overlap, and frequencies between 3.5 kHz and 5 kHz will **fold back** into the baseband, producing false lower frequencies. Therefore, **aliasing will occur**, and the original signal cannot be reconstructed.

---

### Question B: Aliasing in Action [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam — June 2023]

**Statement:** *(a)* Explain the concept of aliasing with a neat frequency-domain diagram. Describe how the aliasing effect can be prevented. *(7 marks)*
*(b)* A signal has a true frequency of 11 kHz. It is sampled at 8 kHz. Determine the apparent (aliased) frequency. If the same signal were sampled at 25 kHz, what would happen? *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — Aliasing Concept and Prevention (7 Marks)

**Concept:** When a bandlimited signal with maximum frequency $f_m$ is sampled at $f_s < 2f_m$, the periodic spectral replicas created by the sampling process **overlap** in the frequency domain. The overlapping region causes the high-frequency tail of one replica to mix with the low-frequency tail of the adjacent replica, creating a single, corrupted spectrum. This phenomenon is called **aliasing**. The high frequencies effectively "mask" themselves as low frequencies, and the resulting digital signal **cannot be inverted** to retrieve the original analog signal.

**Frequency-Domain Sketch (described):**

> - The original spectrum $X(f)$ is a single lobe centered at $f = 0$ extending up to $f_m$.
> - After sampling at $f_s < 2f_m$, replicas appear at $\pm f_s, \pm 2f_s, \ldots$
> - The baseband replica and the next replica **overlap** between $f_s - f_m$ and $f_m$.
> - The overlap region is shaded — this is the **aliased zone**.

**Prevention Methods:**

1. **Pre-filtering (Anti-Aliasing LPF):** Apply a low-pass filter with cutoff $f_c = f_s/2$ before sampling to enforce bandlimitedness.
2. **Increase sampling rate:** Choose $f_s$ to be at least $2f_m$ (in practice, 3 to 5 times $f_m$).
3. **Combined approach:** Use both filtering and oversampling for robust system design.

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — Aliased Frequency Computation (7 Marks)

**Case 1: Sampling at $f_s = 8$ kHz**

The true frequency is $f = 11$ kHz. The Nyquist frequency is $f_s/2 = 4$ kHz. Since $11 > 4$, aliasing will occur.

We use the formula:

$$f_a = \vert f - k f_s \vert \quad \text{where } k \text{ is chosen so that } f_a \le f_s/2$$

$$\begin{aligned}
k = 1 &: \quad f_a = \vert 11 - 8 \vert = 3 \text{ kHz} \;\; \checkmark \quad (\le 4 \text{ kHz})
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore, the **aliased frequency is 3 kHz**.

**Case 2: Sampling at $f_s = 25$ kHz**

Check Nyquist condition: $f_s = 25$ kHz, $2f_m = 22$ kHz (since $f_m = f = 11$ kHz).

$$f_s = 25 \text{ kHz} \ge 2 f_m = 22 \text{ kHz} \;\; \checkmark$$

The Nyquist criterion is satisfied. The Nyquist frequency is $f_s/2 = 12.5$ kHz, which is **greater than** 11 kHz. Hence the signal frequency lies within the representable band, **no aliasing occurs**, and the original 11 kHz signal can be **faithfully reconstructed**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in the Nyquist rate — many students write $f_s = f_m$ instead of $f_s = 2 f_m$. This is the **#1 marking deduction**.
> 2. **Confusing Nyquist Rate and Nyquist Frequency** — they are different quantities. Nyquist Rate is a *minimum sampling frequency*; Nyquist Frequency is *half the actual sampling frequency*.
> 3. **Wrong choice of $k$** in aliasing problems — always pick the $k$ that brings $f_a$ into the range $[0, f_s/2]$.
> 4. **Skipping units** in numerical answers — KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks for missing "Hz" or "kHz".
> 5. **Confusing $f_s$ with $f_m$** — students sometimes treat the signal's max frequency as if it were the sampling frequency. Read the question carefully.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Sampling Theorem (Nyquist–Shannon):** $f_s \ge 2 f_m$ is the *necessary and sufficient* condition for lossless reconstruction of a bandlimited signal.
- **Nyquist Rate** = $2 f_m$ (the *minimum* allowed $f_s$). **Nyquist Frequency** = $f_s/2$ (the *highest representable* signal frequency).
- **Aliasing** is the irreversible distortion caused by undersampling; it is *prevented* using an **anti-aliasing LPF** and/or **oversampling**.
- **Aliased frequency formula:** $f_a = \vert f - k f_s \vert$, with $k$ chosen to keep $f_a \in [0, f_s/2]$.
- The **sampling period** is $T_s = 1 / f_s$. The samples themselves are $x[n] = x(nT_s)$.
- In real systems, $f_s$ is chosen as **3 to 5 times** $f_m$ to allow for non-ideal (non-brick-wall) anti-aliasing filters.
- **Practical examples to remember:** Audio CD (44.1 kHz for 20 kHz audio), Telephone (8 kHz for 4 kHz voice), Digital oscilloscopes ($f_s$ at least 2× the bandwidth setting).
- **Aliasing is irreversible** — once it occurs in the sampled data, no filter or algorithm can recover the original high-frequency information. Prevention is the only cure.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Analog Frequency and Digital Frequency. Elementary Sequences: Real Sinusoidal Sequences
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST416 - SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Analog Frequency and Digital Frequency. Elementary Sequences: Real Sinusoidal Sequences

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Analog Frequency ($\Omega$)

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
Analog frequency, denoted by the Greek capital letter **Omega ($\Omega$)**, is the rate of oscillation of a **continuous-time (analog) sinusoidal signal** measured in **radians per second (rad/s)**. It represents the angular displacement per unit time of the rotating phasor that generates the sinusoid.

For a continuous-time sinusoid:

$$x(t) = A \cos(\Omega t + \phi)$$

where:
- $A$ → Amplitude of the signal
- $\Omega$ → Analog angular frequency in **rad/s**
- $\phi$ → Initial phase in radians
- $t$ → Continuous time in seconds

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU 2024 Highlight:** Analog frequency is associated strictly with **continuous-time signals**. The frequency axis of an analog sinusoid is **unbounded**, meaning $\Omega \in (-\infty, +\infty)$.

The relationship between analog angular frequency $\Omega$ and conventional analog frequency $f$ (in Hz) is:

$$\Omega = 2\pi f \quad \text{and} \quad f = \frac{1}{T_p}$$

where $T_p$ is the time period of the analog sinusoid in seconds.

---

### 1.2 Digital Frequency ($\omega$)

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
Digital frequency, denoted by the Greek lowercase letter **omega ($\omega$)**, is the rate of phase change of a **discrete-time (digital) sinusoidal sequence** measured in **radians per sample (rad/sample)**. It is the discrete-time counterpart of analog frequency and is defined only at integer time instants $n$.

For a discrete-time sinusoid:

$$x[n] = A \cos(\omega n + \phi)$$

where:
- $A$ → Amplitude of the sequence
- $\omega$ → Digital angular frequency in **rad/sample**
- $\phi$ → Initial phase in radians
- $n$ → Integer sample index (dimensionless, $n \in \mathbb{Z}$)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU 2024 Highlight:** Digital frequency is **bounded** and unique only within the principal range $\omega \in (-\pi, \pi]$ or equivalently $\omega \in [0, 2\pi)$. Outside this range, the sinusoid becomes **periodic repetitions** of the same waveform.

---

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Real-World Analogy — The Pendulum vs The Stroboscope:**

| Aspect | Analog Frequency ($\Omega$) | Digital Frequency ($\omega$) |
|---|---|---|
| Physical Analogy | A smooth, continuous pendulum swing in real time | A stroboscopic photograph capturing the pendulum at fixed intervals |
| Quantity Measured | Radians of swing per **second** | Radians of swing per **sample/frame** |
| Time Variable | Continuous, $t \in \mathbb{R}$ | Discrete, $n \in \mathbb{Z}$ |
| Frequency Range | $-\infty$ to $+\infty$ (unbounded) | $-\pi$ to $\pi$ (bounded & periodic) |
| Information Loss | None — all motion captured | Captures only the angular position at sample instants |

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Takeaway:** Imagine watching a ceiling fan. The *true* rotational speed is the **analog frequency**. Now imagine taking 30 photographs per second of the same fan and counting how many radians the blades appear to rotate between successive photos — that *apparent* per-photo rotation is the **digital frequency**. If you take photos slowly, the digital frequency can fool you (aliasing!).

---

### 1.4 Elementary Sequences — Real Sinusoidal Sequences

**Definition:**
A real sinusoidal sequence is a discrete-time signal of the form:

$$x[n] = A \cos(\omega_0 n + \phi)$$

where $A$ is a real amplitude, $\omega_0$ is the digital angular frequency in rad/sample, and $\phi$ is the initial phase. The sequence is **real-valued** since $A$ and $\phi$ are real.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Classification:** Real sinusoidal sequences are one of the **elementary sequences** along with the unit impulse $\delta[n]$, unit step $u[n]$, real exponential $A\alpha^n$, and complex exponential $A e^{j\omega n}$. They form the building blocks of all discrete-time signals through linear combinations.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fundamental Relationship Between Analog and Digital Frequency

When a continuous-time sinusoid $x(t) = A\cos(\Omega t + \phi)$ is sampled at a sampling period $T$ (sampling rate $f_s = 1/T$), the resulting discrete-time sequence is:

$$x[n] = x(nT) = A\cos(\Omega n T + \phi)$$

Comparing this with $x[n] = A\cos(\omega n + \phi)$, we obtain the **fundamental frequency mapping**:

$$\boxed{\omega = \Omega T = \frac{\Omega}{f_s}}$$

This is the **most important equation in Module 1** for KTU exams.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does this matter?** A single analog frequency $\Omega$ produces a digital frequency that is **modulated by the sampling period**. The same analog signal sampled at different rates produces different digital sequences. This is the mathematical origin of **aliasing**.

---

### 2.2 Periodicity of Discrete-Time Sinusoids

A discrete-time sinusoid $x[n] = A\cos(\omega_0 n + \phi)$ is periodic if and only if:

$$\boxed{\omega_0 = \frac{2\pi m}{N}}$$

for some integers $m, N$ with $N > 0$. The fundamental period is the **smallest positive integer** $N$ satisfying this.

**Periodic Condition Logic Steps:**

1. For periodicity: $x[n+N] = x[n]$ for all $n$
2. This requires: $\cos(\omega_0(n+N)+\phi) = \cos(\omega_0 n + \phi)$
3. Hence: $\omega_0 N = 2\pi m$ for some integer $m$
4. Therefore: $\omega_0 = 2\pi m / N$
5. **Fundamental period** $N$ = smallest integer satisfying the above.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Insight:** Unlike continuous-time sinusoids (which are periodic for **all** values of $\Omega$), a discrete-time sinusoid is periodic **only for specific rational multiples of $\pi$**.

---

### 2.3 Symmetry Properties of Real Sinusoidal Sequences

| Property | Mathematical Statement | Engineering Implication |
|---|---|---|
| Even Symmetry | $\cos(\omega n) = \cos(-\omega n)$ | Real cosines are symmetric about $n=0$ |
| Odd Symmetry | $\sin(\omega n) = -\sin(-\omega n)$ | Real sines are antisymmetric |
| Periodicity in $\omega$ | $\cos((\omega + 2\pi)n) = \cos(\omega n)$ | Digital frequency repeats every $2\pi$ |
| Conjugate Symmetry | $\cos(\omega n) = \text{Re}\{e^{j\omega n}\}$ | Bridge to complex exponentials |

---

### 2.4 Frequency Range and Uniqueness

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board-Favorite Concept:** The digital frequency $\omega$ is **unique only in the range** $(-\pi, \pi]$ (or $[0, 2\pi)$). Any frequency outside this range is mathematically equivalent to a frequency inside, differing only by an integer multiple of $2\pi$.

**Special Frequency Values:**

| $\omega$ (rad/sample) | Resulting Sequence | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| $\omega = 0$ | $x[n] = A$ (constant) | DC (zero frequency) |
| $\omega = \pi/2$ | $x[n] = A, 0, -A, 0, A, 0, \ldots$ | Period $N=4$ |
| $\omega = \pi$ | $x[n] = A, -A, A, -A, \ldots$ | **Highest** frequency, period $N=2$ |
| $\omega$ irrational multiple of $\pi$ | Aperiodic sequence | Never exactly repeats |

> [!NOTE]
> **The Highest Digital Frequency is $\omega = \pi$** — this corresponds to the **fastest oscillating discrete-time signal**, where samples alternate sign on every step. No discrete-time signal can oscillate faster than this.

---

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units | Key Boundary |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Analog sinusoid | $x(t)=A\cos(\Omega t + \phi)$ | $\Omega$: rad/s | $\Omega \in (-\infty,+\infty)$ |
| 2 | Digital sinusoid | $x[n]=A\cos(\omega n + \phi)$ | $\omega$: rad/sample | $\omega \in (-\pi, \pi]$ |
| 3 | Analog-digital mapping | $\omega = \Omega T$ | dimensionless ratio | $T = 1/f_s$ |
| 4 | Periodicity condition | $\omega_0 = 2\pi m / N$ | — | $N \in \mathbb{Z}^+$ |
| 5 | Fundamental period | $N = 2\pi m / \omega_0$ (smallest) | samples | Only if rational |
| 6 | Frequency conversion | $f = \Omega / (2\pi)$ | Hz | $f \in (-\infty,+\infty)$ |
| 7 | Aliasing foldover | $\omega_{\text{alias}} = \omega - 2\pi$ | rad/sample | $|\omega| \le \pi$ |
| 8 | Symmetry | $e^{j\omega n} = \cos(\omega n) + j\sin(\omega n)$ | — | Euler's relation |
| 9 | Highest DT frequency | $\omega_{\max} = \pi$ | rad/sample | $N=2$ |
| 10 | Lowest DT frequency | $\omega_{\min} = 0$ | rad/sample | DC, $N=\infty$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility:** Digital frequency is the heart of **Digital Signal Processing (DSP)**. Every DSP chip (in your phone, ECG machine, or 5G base station) processes signals using digital frequency. The bounded range $(-\pi, \pi]$ is the reason DSP systems use **finite-length filters** and **circular convolution** — concepts you will study in later modules.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Analog Sinusoid to Digital Frequency

**Starting Point:** Consider the continuous-time sinusoid:

$$x_a(t) = A\cos(\Omega_0 t + \phi)$$

**Step 1 — Sampling Operation:** Sample uniformly with period $T$ to obtain:

$$x[n] = x_a(nT) = A\cos(\Omega_0 (nT) + \phi)$$

**Step 2 — Group the constants:** Let $\omega_0 = \Omega_0 T$:

$$x[n] = A\cos(\omega_0 n + \phi)$$

**Step 3 — Final form matches discrete-time definition:**

$$\boxed{x[n] = A\cos(\omega_0 n + \phi), \quad \text{where} \quad \omega_0 = \Omega_0 T = \frac{\Omega_0}{f_s}}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Each analog frequency $\Omega_0$ maps to a digital frequency $\omega_0$ via the dimensionless product $\Omega_0 \cdot T$. The sampling period $T$ acts as the **scaling bridge** between continuous and discrete worlds.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Periodicity of Discrete-Time Sinusoid

**Statement:** The sequence $x[n] = A\cos(\omega_0 n + \phi)$ is periodic with period $N$ if and only if $x[n+N] = x[n]$ for all $n \in \mathbb{Z}$.

**Step 1 — Apply the periodicity condition:**

$$A\cos(\omega_0(n+N) + \phi) = A\cos(\omega_0 n + \phi)$$

**Step 2 — Expand the left-hand side:**

$$A\cos(\omega_0 n + \omega_0 N + \phi) = A\cos(\omega_0 n + \phi)$$

**Step 3 — Use the identity $\cos(\theta + 2\pi m) = \cos(\theta)$:**

This holds if and only if $\omega_0 N = 2\pi m$ for some integer $m$.

**Step 4 — Solve for $N$:**

$$N = \frac{2\pi m}{\omega_0}$$

**Step 5 — Fundamental period is the smallest positive integer $N$:**

$$\boxed{N = \min\left\{ \frac{2\pi m}{\omega_0} \,\Big\vert\, m \in \mathbb{Z}^+ \text{ and } N \in \mathbb{Z}^+ \right\}}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The condition $\omega_0 N = 2\pi m$ requires $\omega_0$ to be a **rational multiple of $\pi$** for an integer $N$ to exist. If $\omega_0 / 2\pi$ is irrational, no integer period exists — the sequence is **aperiodic**.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 1: Computing Digital Frequency from Analog

**Problem:** A 1 kHz analog sinusoid is sampled at $f_s = 8000$ samples/sec. Compute its digital frequency.

**Step 1 — Compute analog angular frequency:**

$$\Omega_0 = 2\pi f_0 = 2\pi (1000) = 2000\pi \;\; \text{rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute sampling period:**

$$T = \frac{1}{f_s} = \frac{1}{8000} = 1.25 \times 10^{-4} \;\; \text{s}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the mapping formula:**

$$\omega_0 = \Omega_0 T = 2000\pi \times 1.25 \times 10^{-4} = 0.25\pi = \frac{\pi}{4} \;\; \text{rad/sample}$$

**Step 4 — Verify periodicity:**

$$\omega_0 = \frac{\pi}{4} = \frac{2\pi m}{N} \implies N = 8, \;\; m = 1$$

The sequence is periodic with fundamental period $N = 8$ samples.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example 2: Periodicity Test

**Problem:** Determine whether $x[n] = 5\cos(0.3\pi n + \pi/6)$ is periodic. If yes, find the period.

**Step 1 — Apply periodicity condition:**

$$\omega_0 N = 2\pi m \implies 0.3\pi \cdot N = 2\pi m$$

**Step 2 — Simplify:**

$$N = \frac{2\pi m}{0.3\pi} = \frac{2m}{0.3} = \frac{20m}{3}$$

**Step 3 — Find smallest integer pair $(m, N)$:**

For $N$ to be an integer, $m$ must be a multiple of 3. The smallest $m = 3$ gives:

$$N = \frac{20(3)}{3} = 20$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion:** The sequence is **periodic with fundamental period $N = 20$** samples.

> **Conversion Logic:** When $\omega_0 = p\pi/q$ where $p, q$ are integers with $\gcd(p,q)=1$, the period is $N = 2q$ if $p$ is odd, and $N = q$ if $p$ is even. Here $0.3\pi = 3\pi/10$, so $p=3$ (odd), $q=10$, giving $N = 2(10) = 20$. ✓

---

### 3.5 Worked Example 3: Frequency Aliasing Demonstration

**Problem:** Two analog sinusoids with frequencies $f_1 = 1$ kHz and $f_2 = 9$ kHz are sampled at $f_s = 8$ kHz. Show that they produce identical digital sequences.

**Step 1 — Digital frequency of $f_1$:**

$$\omega_1 = 2\pi \frac{f_1}{f_s} = 2\pi \frac{1000}{8000} = \frac{\pi}{4} \;\; \text{rad/sample}$$

**Step 2 — Digital frequency of $f_2$:**

$$\omega_2 = 2\pi \frac{f_2}{f_s} = 2\pi \frac{9000}{8000} = \frac{9\pi}{4} \;\; \text{rad/sample}$$

**Step 3 — Reduce $\omega_2$ modulo $2\pi$:**

$$\omega_2 = \frac{9\pi}{4} - 2\pi = \frac{9\pi - 8\pi}{4} = \frac{\pi}{4} \;\; \text{rad/sample}$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion:** $\omega_1 = \omega_2 = \pi/4$. **Aliasing confirmed** — the two distinct analog frequencies map to the **same digital frequency**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Aliasing Pitfall:** When $\vert\omega\vert > \pi$, the digital frequency "folds back" into the principal range. This is why the **sampling theorem** (Nyquist criterion $f_s \ge 2f_{\max}$) must be strictly satisfied in all practical DSP systems.

---

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Exam Patterns

| Pattern | Given | Required | Key Formula | Marking Weightage |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Pattern 1 | Analog $f$ (Hz) and $f_s$ | Digital $\omega$ (rad/sample) | $\omega = 2\pi f / f_s$ | High (≥7 marks) |
| Pattern 2 | Digital sinusoid $x[n]$ | Periodicity test + period $N$ | $\omega N = 2\pi m$ | Very High (≥10 marks) |
| Pattern 3 | Complex $e^{j\omega n}$ | Real & Imaginary parts | Euler's formula | Medium (5 marks) |
| Pattern 4 | $\omega$ outside $(-\pi, \pi]$ | Reduce to principal range | Subtract $2\pi k$ | Medium (5 marks) |
| Pattern 5 | Plot of sinusoid | Identify $\omega$ and $\phi$ | Read first peaks | High (≥7 marks) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Analog-to-Digital Frequency Transformation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Analog Sinusoid x(t) = A cos(Omega t + phi)"] --> B["Sampling Switch at Period T"]
    B --> C["Sampler Output x(nT)"]
    C --> D["Discrete-Time Sequence x[n] = A cos(omega n + phi)"]
    D --> E["Apply Periodicity Test: omega N = 2 pi m"]
    E --> F{"omega inside (-pi, pi]?"}
    F -- "Yes" --> G["Direct Use: omega_unique = omega"]
    F -- "No" --> H["Fold: omega_unique = omega - 2 pi k"]
    H --> I["Final Digital Frequency omega_unique"]
    G --> I
    E --> J["Compute Fundamental Period N = 2 pi m / omega"]
```

### 4.2 Conceptual Map: Elementary Sequences Family

```mermaid
graph TD
    S["Elementary Sequences in DSP"]
    S --> S1["Unit Impulse delta n"]
    S --> S2["Unit Step u n"]
    S --> S3["Real Exponential A alpha to power n"]
    S --> S4["Real Sinusoidal A cos omega n + phi"]
    S --> S5["Complex Exponential A e to power j omega n"]
    S4 --> S4a["Frequency Range: omega in -pi to pi"]
    S4 --> S4b["Periodic only if omega = 2 pi m / N"]
    S4 --> S4c["Special case: omega = pi gives fastest alternation"]
    S4 --> S4d["Bridge to S5 via Euler: cos theta = Re of e to power j theta"]
    S5 --> S4
```

### 4.3 Frequency Domain Mapping Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph AX["Analog Frequency Axis (Unbounded)"]
        A1["Omega = -infinity"]
        A2["Omega = -2 pi f_s"]
        A3["Omega = 0 (DC)"]
        A4["Omega = pi f_s (Nyquist)"]
        A5["Omega = +infinity"]
    end
    subgraph DX["Digital Frequency Axis (Bounded -pi to pi)"]
        D1["omega = -pi"]
        D2["omega = -pi/2"]
        D3["omega = 0 (DC)"]
        D4["omega = +pi/2"]
        D5["omega = +pi"]
    end
    AX -- "Sampling: omega = Omega T" --> DX
    AX -- "Wrap by 2 pi" --> DX
    A2 -. "Folds to" .-> D5
    A4 -. "Maps to" .-> D5
```

### 4.4 Plot Schematic: Real Sinusoidal Sequence $x[n] = \cos(\pi n / 4)$

```mermaid
graph LR
    n0["n=0, x=1.000"]
    n1["n=1, x=0.707"]
    n2["n=2, x=0.000"]
    n3["n=3, x=-0.707"]
    n4["n=4, x=-1.000"]
    n5["n=5, x=-0.707"]
    n6["n=6, x=0.000"]
    n7["n=7, x=0.707"]
    n8["n=8, x=1.000 (Period N=8)"]
    n0 --> n1 --> n2 --> n3 --> n4 --> n5 --> n6 --> n7 --> n8
```

> **Reading the Plot:** The sequence traces one complete cycle of a cosine in 8 samples, with peaks at $n=0$ and $n=8$, confirming period $N=8$ and $\omega = \pi/4$ rad/sample.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** Define analog frequency and digital frequency. State the relationship between them.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Analog frequency ($\Omega$):** The rate of oscillation of a continuous-time sinusoidal signal, measured in **radians per second (rad/s)**. It is defined for $x(t) = A\cos(\Omega t + \phi)$ and has range $\Omega \in (-\infty, +\infty)$. **(1 mark)**
- **Digital frequency ($\omega$):** The rate of phase change of a discrete-time sinusoid, measured in **radians per sample (rad/sample)**. It is defined for $x[n] = A\cos(\omega n + \phi)$ and is bounded to $(-\pi, \pi]$. **(1 mark)**
- **Relationship:** $\omega = \Omega T = \Omega / f_s$, where $T$ is the sampling period and $f_s$ is the sampling frequency. **(1 mark)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** A discrete-time sinusoid is given by $x[n] = 5\cos(0.4\pi n)$. Determine whether it is periodic and find its fundamental period.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- Given: $\omega_0 = 0.4\pi = \frac{2\pi}{5}$. **(1 mark)**
- For periodicity, we need $\omega_0 N = 2\pi m$ for integer $N, m$.
- Substituting: $0.4\pi \cdot N = 2\pi m \Rightarrow N = 5m$. **(1 mark)**
- Smallest positive integer: $N = 5$ (with $m=1$).
- **Conclusion:** The sequence is **periodic with fundamental period $N = 5$ samples**. **(1 mark)**

---

### Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Derive the relationship between analog frequency and digital frequency. Show that the highest frequency a discrete-time sinusoid can represent is $\omega = \pi$ rad/sample. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A continuous-time signal $x(t) = 10\cos(200\pi t)$ is sampled at $f_s = 400$ Hz. Find (i) the digital frequency $\omega$, (ii) the fundamental period $N$ of the sampled sequence, and (iii) the first 8 sample values. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

Consider the continuous-time sinusoid:

$$x(t) = A\cos(\Omega_0 t + \phi)$$

Sampling at period $T$ gives:

$$x[n] = x(nT) = A\cos(\Omega_0 nT + \phi)$$

Define $\omega_0 = \Omega_0 T$, hence:

$$x[n] = A\cos(\omega_0 n + \phi)$$

$$\boxed{\omega_0 = \Omega_0 T = \frac{\Omega_0}{f_s}} \quad \textbf{(3 marks)}$$

**Highest Frequency Proof:** The digital frequency has the unique range $\omega \in (-\pi, \pi]$ due to the periodicity property:

$$\cos((\omega + 2\pi)n) = \cos(\omega n)$$

Hence any $|\omega| > \pi$ is equivalent to some $\omega' = \omega - 2\pi k$ within $(-\pi, \pi]$. The boundary value $\omega = \pi$ corresponds to:

$$x[n] = A\cos(\pi n) = A(-1)^n$$

This is the fastest oscillation possible (alternating sign), so $\omega_{\max} = \pi$ rad/sample. **$\square$ (4 marks)**

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

Given: $x(t) = 10\cos(200\pi t)$, $f_s = 400$ Hz.

**(i) Digital Frequency (2 marks):**

$$\Omega_0 = 200\pi \;\; \text{rad/s}, \quad T = 1/f_s = 1/400 \;\; \text{s}$$

$$\omega_0 = \Omega_0 T = 200\pi \times \frac{1}{400} = \frac{\pi}{2} \;\; \text{rad/sample}$$

**(ii) Fundamental Period (2 marks):**

$$\omega_0 = \frac{\pi}{2} = \frac{2\pi m}{N} \implies N = 4, \quad m = 1$$

**Period $N = 4$ samples.**

**(iii) First 8 Samples (3 marks):** $x[n] = 10\cos(\pi n / 2)$

| $n$ | $\pi n/2$ | $\cos(\pi n/2)$ | $x[n]$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | 1.000 | **10** |
| 1 | $\pi/2$ | 0.000 | **0** |
| 2 | $\pi$ | $-1.000$ | **-10** |
| 3 | $3\pi/2$ | 0.000 | **0** |
| 4 | $2\pi$ | 1.000 | **10** |
| 5 | $5\pi/2$ | 0.000 | **0** |
| 6 | $3\pi$ | $-1.000$ | **-10** |
| 7 | $7\pi/2$ | 0.000 | **0** |

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — **Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the concept of periodicity in discrete-time sinusoids. Prove that $x[n] = \cos(\omega_0 n)$ is periodic only if $\omega_0$ is a rational multiple of $2\pi$. Determine whether the sequence $x[n] = \cos(0.5 n)$ is periodic. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Two analog sinusoids with frequencies $f_1 = 500$ Hz and $f_2 = 1500$ Hz are sampled at $f_s = 1000$ Hz. Compute the digital frequencies and comment on the result. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Periodicity Proof (7 marks):**

A sequence $x[n]$ is periodic with period $N$ if $x[n+N] = x[n]$ for all $n$. For $x[n] = \cos(\omega_0 n)$:

$$\cos(\omega_0(n+N)) = \cos(\omega_0 n) \implies \omega_0 N = 2\pi m, \quad m \in \mathbb{Z}$$

Hence: $\omega_0 = \frac{2\pi m}{N}$, meaning $\omega_0$ must be a **rational multiple of $2\pi$**. **(4 marks)**

For $x[n] = \cos(0.5n)$: $\omega_0 = 0.5$ rad/sample. Periodicity requires $0.5 N = 2\pi m \Rightarrow N = 4\pi m$. Since $\pi$ is irrational, $N$ can never be a positive integer. **The sequence is aperiodic.** **(3 marks)**

---

**Part (b) — Aliasing Demonstration (7 marks):**

**For $f_1 = 500$ Hz, $f_s = 1000$ Hz:**

$$\omega_1 = 2\pi \frac{f_1}{f_s} = 2\pi \times \frac{500}{1000} = \pi \;\; \text{rad/sample}$$

**For $f_2 = 1500$ Hz, $f_s = 1000$ Hz:**

$$\omega_2 = 2\pi \frac{f_2}{f_s} = 2\pi \times \frac{1500}{1000} = 3\pi \;\; \text{rad/sample}$$

**Reduction to principal range:** $\omega_2 = 3\pi - 2\pi = \pi$ rad/sample. **(4 marks)**

**Comment:** Both analog frequencies map to the **same digital frequency** $\omega = \pi$. This is the classic **aliasing phenomenon** — the higher frequency $f_2 = 1500$ Hz is the "alias" of $f_1 = 500$ Hz because $f_2 = f_s - f_1$. The sampled sequences are **indistinguishable**. **(3 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Mix-up (–2 marks):** Students often write $\omega$ in rad/s or $\Omega$ in rad/sample. Always verify units before computing.
> 2. **Forgetting modulo $2\pi$ (–2 marks):** Digital frequencies outside $(-\pi, \pi]$ must be folded back. Writing $\omega = 9\pi/4$ as a final answer is incorrect.
> 3. **Aperiodicity Confusion (–3 marks):** If $\omega_0 / (2\pi)$ is irrational, the sequence is **aperiodic**, not "periodic with some large $N$".
> 4. **Forgetting the small $N$ requirement (–1 mark):** The fundamental period is the **smallest** positive integer $N$, not just any valid $N$.
> 5. **Missing the $\omega = \pi$ maximum (–1 mark):** Always explicitly state that $\pi$ rad/sample is the highest digital frequency.

---

### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Analog frequency** $\Omega$ is in **rad/s** and unbounded; **digital frequency** $\omega$ is in **rad/sample** and bounded to $(-\pi, \pi]$.
- ✅ The **bridge formula** $\omega = \Omega T$ connects the two domains — it is the single most important equation in this topic.
- ✅ Discrete-time sinusoids are periodic **only if** $\omega_0 = 2\pi m / N$ for some integers $m, N > 0$.
- ✅ The **fundamental period** is the **smallest** positive integer $N$ satisfying the periodicity condition.
- ✅ The **highest digital frequency** is $\omega = \pi$ (alternating sign, period 2); the **lowest** is $\omega = 0$ (DC, constant).
- ✅ Digital frequency is **periodic with period $2\pi$**: $\omega$ and $\omega \pm 2\pi$ represent the same sequence.
- ✅ **Aliasing** occurs when $|\omega| > \pi$ — frequencies fold back into the principal range, making distinct analog signals indistinguishable after sampling.
- ✅ Real sinusoidal sequences are elementary — they form the basis for **Fourier analysis** of discrete-time signals (Module 3 onwards).
- ✅ **Euler's relation** $e^{j\omega n} = \cos(\omega n) + j\sin(\omega n)$ links real sinusoids to complex exponentials — the foundation of DTFT in later modules.
- ✅ A discrete-time sinusoid with $\omega$ an **irrational multiple of $\pi$** is **never periodic** — a property unique to discrete-time systems.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Complex Exponential Sequences; Unit Impulse
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST416 - SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Complex Exponential Sequences; Unit Impulse

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Complex Exponential Sequences — Formal Definition

A **complex exponential sequence** is a discrete-time signal whose samples are governed by an exponential function in the complex plane. The most general form encountered in KTU Signals & Systems is:

$$x[n] = C \cdot \alpha^{n}$$

where $C$ and $\alpha$ are, in general, **complex numbers**. Expanding $\alpha$ in polar form gives the canonical KTU-board form:

$$x[n] = C \cdot \alpha^{n} = C \cdot \vert \alpha \vert^{n} \cdot e^{j \angle(\alpha)\, n}$$

Two special cases dominate the syllabus:

| Case | Form | Common Name |
|---|---|---|
| Pure imaginary | $\alpha = e^{j\omega}$ | **Complex exponential (oscillator)** |
| Pure real | $\alpha = a \in \mathbb{R}$ | **Real exponential** |
| General complex | $\alpha = r\,e^{j\omega}$ | **Complex geometric sequence** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus:** The complex exponential sequence $x[n] = e^{j\omega n}$ is the **fundamental building block** of all discrete-time signals. Every periodic sequence can be expressed as a sum of such exponentials via the **Discrete-Time Fourier Series (DTFS)**, and every finite-energy sequence can be built from them via the **Discrete-Time Fourier Transform (DTFT)**.

### Conceptual Analogy — Intuition for a First-Time Reader

Imagine you are standing at the centre of a giant clock face. A pointer starts at the "12 o'clock" position and rotates around the dial once every 24 hours. Now imagine that instead of continuous motion, the pointer **jumps to a new position at every whole hour** (discrete time $n = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$).

Each jump places the pointer at a new angle $\omega n$ around the clock. The complex number $e^{j\omega n} = \cos(\omega n) + j\sin(\omega n)$ is just a mathematical way of writing "the pointer's coordinates at the $n$-th hour" — the real part is its horizontal position, the imaginary part is its vertical position. As $n$ increases, the pointer traces out a **unit circle** in the complex plane, one jump at a time. That is exactly what a complex exponential sequence is: a sequence of points hopping around a circle.

If we change the radius to $\vert \alpha \vert \neq 1$, the pointer now hops along an inward or outward spiral. If $\vert \alpha \vert > 1$, the spiral expands; if $\vert \alpha \vert < 1$, it shrinks toward the origin.

## 1.2 Unit Impulse Sequence — Formal Definition

The **unit impulse sequence** (also called the **Kronecker delta** or **discrete-time unit sample**) is defined as:

$$\delta[n] = \begin{cases} 1, & n = 0 \\ 0, & n \neq 0 \end{cases}$$

It is the discrete-time analogue of the continuous-time Dirac delta $\delta(t)$. However, unlike the Dirac delta, $\delta[n]$ is an **ordinary, well-behaved function** at $n=0$ — it simply equals **1**. There is no infinite spike, no area-under-curve concept, and no distribution theory required.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the unit impulse is so important in KTU Signals & Systems:**
> 1. It is the **identity element for convolution**: $x[n] * \delta[n] = x[n]$.
> 2. Any sequence can be written as a weighted sum of shifted impulses: $x[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x[k]\,\delta[n-k]$ (**sifting property**).
> 3. It is the **discrete-time counterpart of the Dirac delta**, allowing LTI system analysis via impulse response $h[n]$.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Unit Impulse as a "Click"

Think of the unit impulse as a single, instantaneous **"click"** or **"tap"** at time $n=0$. Before the click ($n<0$) there is silence, at the moment of the click ($n=0$) there is a brief, perfect "pop" of amplitude **1**, and after the click ($n>0$) it is silent again. In music production, an impulse is exactly the short sharp sound an engineer uses to test the acoustics of a concert hall — one bang, then everything is quiet.

The genius of this signal is that by **adding together** many shifted-and-scaled copies of the click, you can construct *any* sound you want. A musical note is just a long sum of clicks of varying heights, spaced appropriately in time. This is the **sifting property** we will derive formally in Section 3.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Complex Exponential Sequence

Let us dissect the general form $x[n] = C \cdot \alpha^{n}$ step by step. Substituting $\alpha = r\,e^{j\Omega}$ (where $r = \vert \alpha \vert \geq 0$ and $\Omega = \angle \alpha$):

$$x[n] = C\, r^{n}\, e^{j\Omega n}$$

Multiplying $C$ in polar form as $C = \vert C \vert\, e^{j\phi}$:

$$x[n] = \vert C \vert\, r^{n}\, e^{j(\Omega n + \phi)}$$

The real and imaginary parts separate cleanly:

$$\text{Re}\{x[n]\} = \vert C \vert\, r^{n}\, \cos(\Omega n + \phi)$$

$$\text{Im}\{x[n]\} = \vert C \vert\, r^{n}\, \sin(\Omega n + \phi)$$

### 2.1.1 Why $\vert \alpha \vert$ Decides Growth or Decay

- If $\vert \alpha \vert = r > 1$: the sequence is a **growing** spiral (amplitude expands geometrically).
- If $\vert \alpha \vert = r = 1$: the sequence stays on a **constant** circle in the complex plane — it is **periodic** if $\Omega / 2\pi$ is rational.
- If $\vert \alpha \vert = r < 1$: the sequence is a **decaying** spiral (amplitude shrinks to zero).

### 2.1.2 Periodicity in Discrete Time — The $2\pi$ Mystery

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **A key KTU fact students must memorize:** The complex exponential $e^{j\omega n}$ is **periodic with period $2\pi$** in $\omega$. That is, $e^{j(\omega + 2\pi)n} = e^{j\omega n} e^{j2\pi n} = e^{j\omega n}$ since $e^{j2\pi n} = 1$ for integer $n$.

This means $\omega$ and $\omega + 2\pi$ produce **the exact same sequence** — they are indistinguishable in discrete time. This is a fundamental difference from the continuous-time case $e^{j\omega t}$, where different $\omega$ values always give different signals.

**Fundamental period of $e^{j\omega_0 n}$:**
- If $\omega_0 / 2\pi$ is **rational**, say $\omega_0 / 2\pi = p/q$ in lowest terms, then the period is $N = q$.
- If $\omega_0 / 2\pi$ is **irrational**, the sequence is **aperiodic** (never repeats exactly).

### 2.1.3 The Five Canonical Real Sequences Derived From the Complex Exponential

Using Euler's formula, every commonly tested real sequence is a special case:

| Real Sequence | KTU Form | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Constant | $x[n] = 1$ | $\alpha = 1$, $C=1$ |
| Alternating | $x[n] = (-1)^n$ | $\alpha = e^{j\pi}$ |
| Real cosine | $x[n] = \cos(\omega_0 n)$ | $\tfrac{1}{2}(e^{j\omega_0 n} + e^{-j\omega_0 n})$ |
| Real sine | $x[n] = \sin(\omega_0 n)$ | $\tfrac{1}{2j}(e^{j\omega_0 n} - e^{-j\omega_0 n})$ |
| Real exponential | $x[n] = a^n$ | $\alpha = a \in \mathbb{R}$ |

## 2.2 Anatomy of the Unit Impulse

### 2.2.1 Sifting (Sampling) Property — The Most Important Identity

For any sequence $x[n]$:

$$x[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x[k]\, \delta[n-k]$$

**Why this is true (conceptual explanation):**
- $\delta[n-k]$ is nonzero **only when** $n = k$, where it equals **1**.
- So the infinite sum collapses to a single surviving term: the one where $k = n$, which contributes $x[n] \cdot 1 = x[n]$.
- All other terms are zero.

> [!NOTE]
> This is called the **sifting property** because the impulse "sifts out" exactly the value of $x$ at the desired time index. It is the cornerstone for deriving the convolution sum in Module 2.

### 2.2.2 Shifted Unit Impulse $\delta[n-n_0]$

$$\delta[n-n_0] = \begin{cases} 1, & n = n_0 \\ 0, & n \neq n_0 \end{cases}$$

**Engineering interpretation:** A "click" occurring at time $n = n_0$ instead of $n = 0$.

### 2.2.3 Scaling Property

For any real constant $a$:

$$a\,\delta[n] = \begin{cases} a, & n = 0 \\ 0, & n \neq 0 \end{cases}$$

### 2.2.4 Time-Reversal

$$\delta[-n] = \delta[n] \quad \text{(the impulse is an even signal)}$$

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Identity | Name | KTU Use-Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $e^{j\omega n} = \cos(\omega n) + j\sin(\omega n)$ | Euler's formula | Convert between exp/cos/sin |
| 2 | $e^{j(\omega+2\pi)n} = e^{j\omega n}$ | $2\pi$ periodicity in $\omega$ | Identify identical sequences |
| 3 | $x[n] = C\,\alpha^{n}$, $\alpha = r\,e^{j\Omega}$ | General complex exp. | Standard form for all problems |
| 4 | $x[n] = \vert C \vert\, r^{n} e^{j(\Omega n + \phi)}$ | Polar expansion | Magnitude/phase extraction |
| 5 | $\cos(\omega_0 n) = \tfrac{1}{2}(e^{j\omega_0 n} + e^{-j\omega_0 n})$ | Cosine as sum of exp. | Convert problem to exponential form |
| 6 | $\sin(\omega_0 n) = \tfrac{1}{2j}(e^{j\omega_0 n} - e^{-j\omega_0 n})$ | Sine as sum of exp. | Convert problem to exponential form |
| 7 | $\delta[n] = 1$ at $n=0$, else $0$ | Unit impulse | Building block for any sequence |
| 8 | $x[n] = \sum_{k} x[k]\delta[n-k]$ | Sifting property | Derive convolution sum |
| 9 | $x[n]\,\delta[n-n_0] = x[n_0]\,\delta[n-n_0]$ | Multiplication identity | Simplify products involving impulses |
| 10 | $\delta[-n] = \delta[n]$ | Evenness | Time-reversal problems |

> [!NOTE]
> **Important KTU pitfall:** Formula 9 above says that **multiplying a sequence by a shifted impulse samples the sequence at the impulse's location**. Students frequently forget to substitute $n_0$ into $x[n]$ and write $x[0]$ instead, losing 1–2 marks.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Where this concept appears |
|---|---|
| **Digital filter design** | Impulse response $h[n]$ completely characterizes an LTI system |
| **Audio/Speech processing** | Each audio sample is one value of a complex exponential sum (Fourier) |
| **Communications** | Carrier signals are pure complex exponentials $e^{j\omega_c t}$ |
| **Image processing** | 2-D exponentials $e^{j(\omega_1 m + \omega_2 n)}$ form the DFT basis |
| **Control systems** | Stability checked by requiring $\vert \alpha \vert < 1$ for poles of $H(z)$ |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Real Sinusoid to Complex Exponential

**Problem statement:** Prove the Euler identity for discrete time:
$$\cos(\omega_0 n) = \frac{1}{2}\left(e^{j\omega_0 n} + e^{-j\omega_0 n}\right)$$

**Derivation:**

$$e^{j\theta} = \cos(\theta) + j\sin(\theta) \quad \text{[Taylor series / Euler]}$$

$$e^{-j\theta} = \cos(\theta) - j\sin(\theta) \quad \text{[Replace } \theta \to -\theta\text{]}$$

Adding these two equations:

$$e^{j\theta} + e^{-j\theta} = 2\cos(\theta)$$

Dividing by 2 and substituting $\theta = \omega_0 n$:

$$\cos(\omega_0 n) = \frac{e^{j\omega_0 n} + e^{-j\omega_0 n}}{2}$$

This is **verified for all integer $n$** since the formula is a pointwise identity. $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Derivation: Periodicity of $e^{j\omega n}$ in $\omega$

**Claim:** $e^{j(\omega + 2\pi) n} = e^{j\omega n}$ for all integer $n$.

**Proof:**

$$e^{j(\omega + 2\pi) n} = e^{j\omega n} \cdot e^{j 2\pi n}$$

By Euler, $e^{j 2\pi n} = \cos(2\pi n) + j\sin(2\pi n)$. For integer $n$, $\cos(2\pi n) = 1$ and $\sin(2\pi n) = 0$, so:

$$e^{j 2\pi n} = 1$$

Therefore:

$$e^{j(\omega + 2\pi) n} = e^{j\omega n} \cdot 1 = e^{j\omega n} \quad \blacksquare$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Consequence for KTU problems:** If a question asks for the "range of $\omega$" producing distinct sequences, the answer is **any interval of length $2\pi$**, conventionally $\omega \in [-\pi, \pi]$ or $\omega \in [0, 2\pi]$.

## 3.3 Derivation: Fundamental Period of $e^{j\omega_0 n}$

**Setup:** Suppose $\omega_0 / 2\pi$ is rational, i.e., $\omega_0 = 2\pi p/q$ with $p, q$ coprime positive integers. We claim the fundamental period is $N = q$.

**Proof:** We need the smallest $N \in \mathbb{Z}^+$ such that $e^{j\omega_0(n+N)} = e^{j\omega_0 n}$, i.e.,

$$e^{j\omega_0 N} = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega_0 N = 2\pi m \text{ for some } m \in \mathbb{Z}$$

Substituting $\omega_0 = 2\pi p/q$:

$$\frac{2\pi p N}{q} = 2\pi m \quad \Rightarrow \quad pN = mq$$

The smallest positive integer $N$ is obtained when $m = p$ (the smallest $m$ that yields integer $N$ given $\gcd(p,q)=1$):

$$N = q$$

**Verification:** For $N = q$:
$$e^{j\omega_0 q} = e^{j(2\pi p/q) q} = e^{j 2\pi p} = 1 \quad \checkmark$$

> [!NOTE]
> If $\omega_0 = \pi/3$ rad, then $p=1$, $q=6$, so the fundamental period is $N = 6$. This is a typical KTU problem.

## 3.4 Derivation: Sifting Property of $\delta[n]$

**Claim:** For any sequence $x[n]$,

$$x[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x[k]\, \delta[n-k]$$

**Proof:** Consider the right-hand side. For each fixed integer $n$, the term $\delta[n-k]$ is nonzero **only** when $n - k = 0$, i.e., $k = n$. At that single index, $\delta[0] = 1$, so the term becomes $x[n] \cdot 1 = x[n]$. For all other $k$, $\delta[n-k] = 0$, so those terms vanish. The infinite sum therefore reduces to a single term:

$$\sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x[k]\, \delta[n-k] = x[n] \quad \blacksquare$$

## 3.5 Derivation: Multiplication by a Shifted Impulse

**Claim:** $x[n]\, \delta[n-n_0] = x[n_0]\, \delta[n-n_0]$.

**Proof:** We evaluate both sides at every integer $n$:

- If $n \neq n_0$: $\delta[n-n_0] = 0$, so LHS $= x[n] \cdot 0 = 0$ and RHS $= x[n_0] \cdot 0 = 0$. Equal.
- If $n = n_0$: $\delta[n_0 - n_0] = \delta[0] = 1$, so LHS $= x[n_0] \cdot 1 = x[n_0]$ and RHS $= x[n_0] \cdot 1 = x[n_0]$. Equal.

Therefore the identity holds for all $n$. $\blacksquare$

## 3.6 Worked Example — A Typical KTU 14-Mark Style Problem

**Problem:** Express $x[n] = 2\cos\!\left(\tfrac{\pi}{4} n + \tfrac{\pi}{6}\right)$ as a sum of complex exponentials and hence determine its real magnitude, complex envelope, and fundamental period.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Expand the cosine using the angle-sum identity:**

$$2\cos(\omega_0 n + \phi) = e^{j(\omega_0 n + \phi)} + e^{-j(\omega_0 n + \phi)}$$

Here $\omega_0 = \pi/4$ and $\phi = \pi/6$.

**Step 2 — Separate into two complex exponentials:**

$$x[n] = e^{j(\pi/4 n + \pi/6)} + e^{-j(\pi/4 n + \pi/6)}$$

$$x[n] = e^{j\pi/6} \cdot e^{j\pi n/4} + e^{-j\pi/6} \cdot e^{-j\pi n/4}$$

**Step 3 — Identify the magnitude and phase:** Each term has $\vert \alpha \vert = 1$ (lies on unit circle), so the envelope is constant: $\vert C \vert = 1$.

**Step 4 — Find the fundamental period:** $\omega_0/2\pi = (\pi/4)/(2\pi) = 1/8$. So $p=1, q=8$ → period $N = 8$.

**Step 5 — Verify the real form:** Using $\cos(A+B) = \cos A \cos B - \sin A \sin B$ with $A=\pi n/4$, $B=\pi/6$:

$$x[n] = 2\cos(\pi n/4)\cos(\pi/6) - 2\sin(\pi n/4)\sin(\pi/6)$$

$$x[n] = \sqrt{3}\,\cos(\pi n/4) - \sin(\pi n/4)$$

(Using $\cos(\pi/6)=\sqrt{3}/2$, $\sin(\pi/6)=1/2$.) This matches the original expression and confirms the exponential decomposition. $\blacksquare$

## 3.7 Worked Example — Unit Impulse Manipulation

**Problem:** Simplify $y[n] = (2n+3)\,\delta[n-2] - 5\,\delta[n+1] + \delta[n]\,\cos(\pi n)$.

**Solution:**

Apply the multiplication identity from §3.5 term by term:

- **First term:** $(2n+3)\,\delta[n-2]$. Since $\delta[n-2] \neq 0$ only at $n=2$, the value of the polynomial at $n=2$ is $2(2)+3 = 7$. So $(2n+3)\delta[n-2] = 7\,\delta[n-2]$.

- **Second term:** $-5\,\delta[n+1]$ is already a scaled shifted impulse, no simplification needed.

- **Third term:** $\delta[n]\cos(\pi n) = \cos(\pi \cdot 0)\,\delta[n] = (1)\,\delta[n] = \delta[n]$, using the multiplication identity with $n_0=0$.

**Final simplified answer:**

$$y[n] = 7\,\delta[n-2] - 5\,\delta[n+1] + \delta[n] \quad \blacksquare$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Architecture — Generation of a Complex Exponential Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Index Generator n = 0, 1, 2, 3 ...] --> B{Multiply by Angular Freq omega}
    B --> C[Compute Phase theta = omega times n]
    C --> D[Euler Block: e to power j theta]
    D --> E[Splitter]
    E --> F[Real Part: cos theta times n]
    E --> G[Imag Part: sin theta times n]
    F --> H[Output x sub real]
    G --> I[Output x sub imag]
    H --> J[Complex Plane Plotter]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Unit Circle if alpha mag = 1]
    J --> L[Spiral In if alpha mag less than 1]
    J --> M[Spiral Out if alpha mag greater than 1]
```

## 4.2 Block Architecture — Decomposing Any Sequence into Impulses (Sifting Property)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Src[Input Sequence x of n] --> Splitter{Sifting Decomposer}
    Splitter --> P0[Weight x at 0 times delta n]
    Splitter --> P1[Weight x at 1 times delta n minus 1]
    Splitter --> P2[Weight x at 2 times delta n minus 2]
    Splitter --> Pk[Weight x at k times delta n minus k]
    Splitter --> PDots[Dots for all integer k]
    P0 --> Sum[Summation Node]
    P1 --> Sum
    P2 --> Sum
    Pk --> Sum
    PDots --> Sum
    Sum --> Out[Reconstructed Sequence x of n]
```

## 4.3 Flowchart — Classifying a Complex Exponential Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Given x of n = C alpha power n] --> A[Write alpha in polar form r e power j Omega]
    A --> B{Is r equals 1?}
    B -- Yes --> C{Is Omega over 2pi rational?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Periodic: compute N = denominator of reduced fraction]
    C -- No --> E[Aperiodic: never exactly repeats]
    B -- No --> F{Is r greater than 1?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Growing exponential envelope: magnitude expands]
    F -- No --> H[Decaying exponential envelope: magnitude shrinks to 0]
    D --> End[Identify sequence type]
    E --> End
    G --> End
    H --> End
```

## 4.4 Stem-Plot Schematic — Unit Impulse and Shifted Versions

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S1[Impulse at n equals 0]
        A1[n equals 0, height 1] -.->|arrow up| A0[Origin axis]
    end
    subgraph S2[Impulse at n equals 2]
        B1[n equals 2, height 1] -.->|arrow up| B0[Shifted axis]
    end
    subgraph S3[Impulse at n equals minus 1]
        C1[n equals minus 1, height 1] -.->|arrow up| C0[Shifted axis]
    end
    S1 -->|Sifting| Sum[Weighted sum reconstructs x of n]
    S2 --> Sum
    S3 --> Sum
```

## 4.5 Conceptual Map — Where This Topic Fits in KTU Module 1

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M1[Module 1: Signals and Systems] --> M1A[Continuous vs Discrete Signals]
    M1 --> M1B[Elementary Signals]
    M1 --> M1C[Complex Exponentials and Sinusoids]
    M1 --> M1D[Unit Impulse and Unit Step]
    M1A --> M1B
    M1B --> M1C
    M1C --> M1D
    M1D --> M1E[Systems Properties and Convolution: Module 2]
```
