Since $u[n] = 1$ for $n \geq 0$ and $0$ otherwise, and $x[n] = 2^n u[n]$:

| $n$ | $\leq -1$ | $0$ | $1$ | $2$ | $3$ | $4$ |
|-----|-----------|-----|-----|-----|-----|-----|
| $x[n]$ | $0$ | $1$ | $2$ | $4$ | $8$ | $16$ |

**Step 2 — Construct the folded signal $x[-n]$:**

| $n$ | $\leq -4$ | $-3$ | $-2$ | $-1$ | $0$ | $1$ | $2$ | $\geq 3$ |
|-----|-----------|------|------|------|-----|-----|-----|----------|
| $x[-n]$ | $16$ | $8$ | $4$ | $2$ | $1$ | $0$ | $0$ | $0$ |

**Step 3 — Compute the even part $x_e[n]$:**

For $n \geq 0$: $x_e[n] = \frac{2^n + 0}{2} = 2^{n-1}$.
For $n < 0$: Let $m = -n > 0$. Then $x_e[n] = \frac{0 + 2^{-n}}{2} = 2^{-n-1} = 2^{|n|-1}$.

Therefore:

$$x_e[n] = \frac{2^{\vert n \vert}}{2} \quad \text{for all } n \in \mathbb{Z}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the odd part $x_o[n]$:**

For $n \geq 0$: $x_o[n] = \frac{2^n - 0}{2} = 2^{n-1}$.
For $n < 0$: $x_o[n] = \frac{0 - 2^{-n}}{2} = -2^{-n-1}$.

Therefore:

$$x_o[n] = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 2^{\vert n \vert} \cdot \text{sgn}(n)$$

where $\text{sgn}(n)$ is the signum function.

**Step 5 — Compute the DC value:**

The DC value of a signal equals $x_e[0]$ (the value of the even part at the origin):

$$x_e[0] = \frac{2^0}{2} = \frac{1}{2}$$

**Step 6 — Verification:**

At $n = 0$: $x_e[0] + x_o[0] = 0.5 + 0 = 0.5$. The original signal has $x[0] = 2^0 \cdot 1 = 1$. Wait — the energy decomposition identity gives $x_e[n] + x_o[n] = x[n]$ pointwise. Let us re-verify:

$x_e[0] = \frac{x[0] + x[0]}{2} = \frac{1+1}{2} = 1$. ✓

(Correction: The even part at the origin is $\frac{1+1}{2} = 1$, since $x[-0] = x[0] = 1$.)

Therefore $x_o[0] = \frac{x[0] - x[0]}{2} = 0$ (forced zero, as expected).

The **DC value** of $x[n]$ is therefore $x_e[0] = 1$.

*(Mark Distribution: 2 marks for tabulation, 2 marks for even/odd derivations, 2 marks for DC computation, 1 mark for verification.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign error on the odd part:** Students frequently write $x_o[n] = \frac{x[-n] - x[n]}{2}$ (sign-flipped). The correct form is $x_o[n] = \frac{x[n] - x[-n]}{2}$. Lose 1 mark.
> 2. **Forgetting $x_o[0] = 0$:** Any answer that gives a non-zero odd component at the origin is **definitely wrong**. Lose 2 marks.
> 3. **Skipping the verification step:** The final check $x_e[n] + x_o[n] = x[n]$ is **mandatory** in KTU 14-mark answers. Lose 1 mark if missing.
> 4. **Failing to mention uniqueness:** In a 7-mark proof question, simply stating existence is not enough — uniqueness must be argued explicitly.
> 5. **Confusing $x[n]$ with $x[-n]$:** Always write the folded signal in a **separate table row** for clarity.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this section and stick it on your wall before the exam.**

- [x] **Even signal:** $x[n] = x[-n]$. Mirror match across the origin.
- [x] **Odd signal:** $x[n] = -x[-n]$. Mirror flip with sign inversion.
- [x] **Forced origin:** Every odd signal **must** have $x[0] = 0$.
- [x] **Universal decomposition:** $x[n] = x_e[n] + x_o[n]$ holds for **all** DT signals.
- [x] **Even extractor:** $x_e[n] = \frac{1}{2}\{x[n] + x[-n]\}$.
- [x] **Odd extractor:** $x_o[n] = \frac{1}{2}\{x[n] - x[-n]\}$.
- [x] **Uniqueness:** For every $x[n]$, there exists exactly **one** pair $(x_e, x_o)$.
- [x] **Sum rules:** $E + E = E$; $O + O = O$; $E + O =$ general (no symmetry).
- [x] **Product rules:** $E \cdot E = E$; $O \cdot O = E$; $E \cdot O = O$.
- [x] **Energy orthogonality:** $\sum x_e[n] \cdot x_o[n] = 0$.
- [x] **Energy decomposition:** $E_{\text{total}} = E_e + E_o$.
- [x] **DC value:** Equals $x_e[0]$, the value of the even part at the origin.
- [x] **DTFT link:** DTFT of a real, even sequence is **purely real**; DTFT of a real, odd sequence is **purely imaginary**.
- [x] **Common examples (even):** $\cos(\omega_0 n)$, $\delta[n]$, $\delta[n+n_0]+\delta[n-n_0]$.
- [x] **Common examples (odd):** $\sin(\omega_0 n)$, $n \cdot \text{rect}[n]$, $\text{sgn}(n)$.
- [x] **Exam trick:** Whenever you see a signal with $x[0] \neq 0$ and a non-zero average → it is **not** purely odd; it must have an even part.
- [x] **Memory aid:** "*E = Equation of mirror*"; "*O = Opposite*"; "*Decomposition is Divide and Conquer for Symmetry*".
- [x] **Time-shift property:** A time-shifted even signal $x_e[n - n_0]$ is generally **not** even, unless shifted back to the origin.
- [x] **Convolution preview:** Knowing even/odd reduces convolution integrals to half-range computations — useful in Module 2.

> **Final Exam Mantra:** *Decompose → Verify → State symmetry. Always perform these three steps in the same order.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Periodic and Non-Periodic Signals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST416 - SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Periodic and Non-Periodic Signals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the rigorous mathematical framework of **Signals and Systems** (as prescribed by the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for PECST416), a **continuous-time signal** $x(t)$ is classified as **periodic** if and only if there exists a strictly **positive real constant** $T > 0$ such that the following identity holds true for **every** value of the independent variable $t$:

$$x(t + T) = x(t) \quad \text{for all } t \in \mathbb{R}$$

The smallest positive value of $T$ that satisfies this condition is termed the **fundamental period** (denoted as $T_0$ or $T_f$), and its reciprocal $\frac{1}{T_0}$ is the **fundamental frequency** $f_0$. Consequently, the **angular fundamental frequency** is given by $\omega_0 = \frac{2\pi}{T_0}$.

If no such $T > 0$ exists, the signal is termed **aperiodic** (or non-periodic).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PECST416 - Module 1):**
> The classification of signals into periodic and aperiodic categories forms the foundational prerequisite for understanding the **Fourier Series** (Module 2) and **Fourier Transform** (Module 3). Periodic signals are analyzed using Fourier Series, while aperiodic signals are analyzed using the Fourier Transform — a duality that is a *high-weightage examination topic* every semester.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing on the side of a busy highway observing the passing of vehicles:

- **Periodic Signal Analogy**: A perfectly synchronized traffic signal at a city intersection cycles **Red → Green → Yellow → Red** every 90 seconds, *no matter what time of day you observe it*. If you check it at 10:00 AM, 10:01:30 AM, or 10:45:00 AM, the pattern is identical. This predictable, repeating behavior is exactly what a periodic signal exhibits on the time axis — it *resets itself* every $T_0$ seconds.

- **Non-Periodic (Aperiodic) Signal Analogy**: Now imagine a single clap of thunder. It happens *once*, decays rapidly, and never repeats again in exactly the same form. You cannot predict its occurrence based on a fixed interval. This single, non-repeating event is analogous to an **aperiodic signal** like $e^{-t}u(t)$ or a single rectangular pulse.

- **Almost-Periodic (Quasi-Periodic) Analogy**: Consider your heartbeat under stress — it might speed up and slow down in an irregular pattern. While you *can* identify a general rhythm, it is not mathematically exact. In engineering, when two periodic signals with **incommensurate periods** (irrational ratio) are summed, the resulting signal is *quasi-periodic* — it never exactly repeats.

### 1.3 Continuous-Time vs Discrete-Time Periodicity

For a **discrete-time signal** $x[n]$, periodicity requires:

$$x[n + N] = x[n] \quad \text{for all } n \in \mathbb{Z}$$

where $N > 0$ is an **integer**. Note that for discrete-time signals, $N$ must be a *positive integer* — a critical distinction from continuous-time signals where $T$ can be any positive real number.

> [!NOTE]
> **Discrete-Time Periodicity Constraint:**
> A continuous-time sinusoid $\cos(\pi t)$ has period $T = 2$, but the discrete-time sinusoid $\cos[\pi n]$ is **NOT periodic** because the required $N = 2$ exists and is valid, but $\cos[0.5\pi n]$ would require $N = 4$, which IS periodic. The key insight: discrete-time periodicity depends on whether $\frac{\omega}{2\pi}$ is a **rational number**.

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Fundamental Period | $T_0$ | Seconds (s) |
| Fundamental Frequency | $f_0 = \frac{1}{T_0}$ | Hertz (Hz) |
| Angular Frequency | $\omega_0 = 2\pi f_0$ | Radians per second (rad/s) |
| Discrete Period | $N_0$ | Samples (dimensionless) |
| Discrete Angular Frequency | $\Omega_0 = \frac{2\pi}{N_0}$ | Radians per sample |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mathematical Conditions for Periodicity — Structured Logic Steps

The decision-tree for testing periodicity of any continuous-time signal $x(t)$ follows this rigorous logical flow:

**Step 1 — Hypothesis Assumption:** Assume $x(t)$ is periodic with period $T$. Write:
$$x(t + T) = x(t)$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Definition:** Substitute $t \to t + T$ into the explicit mathematical expression of $x(t)$ and check if the resulting expression equals the original.

**Step 3 — Solve for T:** For standard sinusoidal forms $\sin(\omega t + \phi)$ or $\cos(\omega t + \phi)$, the periodicity condition reduces to:
$$\sin(\omega(t+T) + \phi) = \sin(\omega t + \phi)$$
This is satisfied when:
$$\omega T = 2\pi k, \quad k \in \mathbb{Z}^+$$
Thus:
$$T = \frac{2\pi k}{\omega}$$
The **fundamental period** corresponds to the smallest positive $T$, which occurs when $k = 1$:
$$T_0 = \frac{2\pi}{\omega} = \frac{2\pi}{2\pi f} = \frac{1}{f}$$

**Step 4 — Verify Fundamental Nature:** Ensure that no smaller positive $T' < T_0$ also satisfies the condition. If such $T'$ exists, then $T_0$ is not the fundamental period.

**Step 5 — Sum of Periodic Signals Test:** If $x(t) = x_1(t) + x_2(t)$ where $x_1$ has period $T_1$ and $x_2$ has period $T_2$, then $x(t)$ is periodic **if and only if**:
$$\frac{T_1}{T_2} = \frac{p}{q} \quad \text{where } p, q \in \mathbb{Z}^+$$
The fundamental period of the sum is:
$$T_0 = \text{LCM}(T_1, T_2) = p \cdot T_1 = q \cdot T_2$$

### 2.2 Critical Properties of Periodic Signals

- **Property P1 — Time Invariance of Periodicity:** If $T_0$ is a period, then any integer multiple $kT_0$ ($k \in \mathbb{Z}^+$) is also a period. The set of all periods is $\{kT_0 \mid k \in \mathbb{Z}^+\}$.

- **Property P2 — DC Signal as Special Case:** A constant signal $x(t) = C$ is periodic with **any** $T > 0$ as its period. By convention, its fundamental period is taken as $T_0 \to \infty$ or it is treated as a degenerate case.

- **Property P3 — Energy of Periodic Signals:** A periodic signal (excluding the zero signal) has **infinite energy** but **finite, non-zero power**. This is because it repeats indefinitely over $(-\infty, +\infty)$.

- **Property P4 — Sum Periodicity Rationality Condition:** For $N$ periodic signals with periods $T_1, T_2, \ldots, T_N$, the sum is periodic if and only if all ratios $\frac{T_i}{T_j}$ are rational numbers.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Condition | Mathematical Expression | Applicability / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Periodicity Definition | $x(t+T) = x(t)$ for all $t$ | Continuous-time, $T > 0$ |
| Discrete Periodicity | $x[n+N] = x[n]$ for all $n$ | Discrete-time, $N \in \mathbb{Z}^+$ |
| Fundamental Period of $\sin(\omega t)$ | $T_0 = \frac{2\pi}{\omega}$ | Smallest positive period |
| Fundamental Period of $\sin^2(\omega t)$ | $T_0 = \frac{\pi}{\omega}$ | Squaring halves the period |
| Fundamental Period of $\cos(\omega t + \phi)$ | $T_0 = \frac{2\pi}{\omega}$ | Phase shift $\phi$ does NOT affect period |
| Sum Periodicity | $T_0 = \text{LCM}(T_1, T_2)$ | Only if $\frac{T_1}{T_2} \in \mathbb{Q}$ |
| Aperiodic Condition | $\nexists T > 0 : x(t+T) = x(t)$ | Definition of non-periodic |
| Energy of Periodic Signal | $E = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt = \infty$ | Power signal |
| Power of Periodic Signal | $P = \frac{1}{T_0}\int_{0}^{T_0} \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt$ | Finite, non-zero |
| Discrete Periodicity Condition | $\frac{\Omega_0}{2\pi} = \frac{m}{N}$ | $m, N$ coprime integers |
| Period of $e^{j\omega_0 t}$ | $T_0 = \frac{2\pi}{\omega_0}$ | Complex exponential |
| Period of Constant $x(t)=C$ | $T_0 = $ any $T>0$ | DC signal special case |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The classification of signals into periodic and aperiodic categories is **not merely academic** — it directly determines which mathematical tools an engineer can deploy:

- **Power Systems Engineering:** The 50 Hz AC mains supply in India is a quintessential periodic signal. Engineers rely on its periodicity to design transformers, generators, and protection relays. Any deviation from periodicity (e.g., harmonics, transients) indicates a fault condition.

- **Communications Engineering:** Radio carriers (AM, FM) are periodic sinusoids modulated by aperiodic information signals (voice, data). The Fourier Series decomposition of the periodic carrier combined with the Fourier Transform of the aperiodic information forms the backbone of all wireless communication.

- **Biomedical Signal Processing:** ECG (Electrocardiogram) signals are *quasi-periodic* — they repeat with slight variations due to heart rate variability. Classifying them as periodic or aperiodic determines whether Fourier Series (for normal ECG) or Short-Time Fourier Transform (for abnormal ECG) is the appropriate analysis tool.

- **Vibration Analysis in Mechanical Engineering:** Periodic vibrations indicate rotating machinery imbalance, while aperiodic transients indicate impacts or faults. The classification guides maintenance decisions in industries.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Fundamental Period of $\sin(\omega t + \phi)$

**Problem Statement:** Prove that the fundamental period of the continuous-time sinusoid $x(t) = \sin(\omega t + \phi)$ is $T_0 = \frac{2\pi}{\omega}$, where $\omega > 0$ and $\phi \in \mathbb{R}$.

**Detailed Step-by-Step Derivation:**

By definition of periodicity, we require the smallest positive $T$ such that:
$$\sin(\omega(t+T) + \phi) = \sin(\omega t + \phi) \quad \forall t$$

Expanding the left-hand side using the angle-addition identity:
$$\sin(\omega t + \omega T + \phi) = \sin(\omega t + \phi)$$

Using the trigonometric identity $\sin(A + B) = \sin A \cos B + \cos A \sin B$, we can rewrite the equation equivalently as a condition on $\omega T$. The equality $\sin(\theta_1) = \sin(\theta_2)$ holds if and only if:
$$\theta_1 - \theta_2 = 2\pi k \quad \text{for some } k \in \mathbb{Z}$$

Applying this to our equation where $\theta_1 = \omega t + \omega T + \phi$ and $\theta_2 = \omega t + \phi$:
$$\omega T = 2\pi k \implies T = \frac{2\pi k}{\omega}$$

For the smallest positive value of $T$, we set $k = 1$:
$$\boxed{T_0 = \frac{2\pi}{\omega}}$$

**Textual Logic:** The result shows that the period depends *only* on $\omega$ (the angular frequency), and is completely independent of the phase $\phi$. This makes physical sense: shifting a sine wave horizontally does not change its repetition rate, only its starting point.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Period of $\sin^2(\omega t)$ (Half-Period Identity)

**Problem Statement:** Find the fundamental period of $x(t) = \sin^2(\omega t)$.

**Detailed Step-by-Step Derivation:**

We begin with the power-reduction trigonometric identity:
$$\sin^2(\theta) = \frac{1 - \cos(2\theta)}{2}$$

Substituting $\theta = \omega t$:
$$x(t) = \sin^2(\omega t) = \frac{1 - \cos(2\omega t)}{2}$$

The signal is now expressed as a sum of a DC component (constant $\frac{1}{2}$) and a cosine of frequency $2\omega$. The cosine term $\cos(2\omega t)$ has period:
$$T_{\cos} = \frac{2\pi}{2\omega} = \frac{\pi}{\omega}$$

The DC component $\frac{1}{2}$ has any period (effectively infinite period). The fundamental period of the sum is governed by the cosine term:
$$\boxed{T_0 = \frac{\pi}{\omega}}$$

**Textual Logic:** Squaring a sinusoid *doubles* its frequency and *halves* its period. This is because $\sin^2(\omega t)$ has two peaks (at $t=0$ and $t=\pi/\omega$) within one full cycle of $\sin(\omega t)$, since squaring makes the negative humps positive.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Periodicity of Sum: $\sin(4\pi t) + \cos(6\pi t)$

**Problem Statement:** Determine whether $x(t) = \sin(4\pi t) + \cos(6\pi t)$ is periodic, and if so, find its fundamental period.

**Detailed Step-by-Step Derivation:**

**Step A — Identify individual periods:**
- For $\sin(4\pi t)$: comparing with $\sin(\omega_1 t)$ where $\omega_1 = 4\pi$:
$$T_1 = \frac{2\pi}{\omega_1} = \frac{2\pi}{4\pi} = \frac{1}{2} \text{ seconds}$$

- For $\cos(6\pi t)$: comparing with $\cos(\omega_2 t)$ where $\omega_2 = 6\pi$:
$$T_2 = \frac{2\pi}{\omega_2} = \frac{2\pi}{6\pi} = \frac{1}{3} \text{ seconds}$$

**Step B — Test the rationality condition:**
$$\frac{T_1}{T_2} = \frac{1/2}{1/3} = \frac{3}{2}$$

Since $\frac{3}{2}$ is a rational number, the sum IS periodic.

**Step C — Compute the LCM:**
We need the smallest $T_0$ such that $\frac{T_0}{T_1}$ and $\frac{T_0}{T_2}$ are both integers:
$$\frac{T_0}{T_1} = \frac{T_0}{1/2} = 2T_0 \in \mathbb{Z}$$
$$\frac{T_0}{T_2} = \frac{T_0}{1/3} = 3T_0 \in \mathbb{Z}$$

The LCM of $2T_0$ and $3T_0$ being integers means $T_0$ must be a multiple of $\frac{1}{2}$ AND a multiple of $\frac{1}{3}$. The smallest positive such $T_0$ is the LCM of $\frac{1}{2}$ and $\frac{1}{3}$:

\begin{aligned}
T_0 &= \text{LCM}\left(\frac{1}{2}, \frac{1}{3}\right) \\
&= \frac{\text{LCM}(1,1)}{\text{GCD}(2,3)} \\
&= \frac{1}{1} \\
&= 1 \text{ second}
\end{aligned}

**Verification:** At $T_0 = 1$:
- $\sin(4\pi(t+1)) = \sin(4\pi t + 4\pi) = \sin(4\pi t)$ ✓ (since $\sin$ has period $2\pi$, and $4\pi = 2 \times 2\pi$)
- $\cos(6\pi(t+1)) = \cos(6\pi t + 6\pi) = \cos(6\pi t)$ ✓ (since $\cos$ has period $2\pi$, and $6\pi = 3 \times 2\pi$)

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{T_0 = 1 \text{ second}}$

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Energy vs Power for Periodic vs Aperiodic Signals

**Problem Statement:** Show that the signal $x(t) = \cos(\omega_0 t)$ is a **power signal** (finite power, infinite energy).

**Detailed Step-by-Step Derivation:**

**Energy Calculation:**
$$E = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \cos^2(\omega_0 t) \, dt$$

Using the identity $\cos^2(\theta) = \frac{1 + \cos(2\theta)}{2}$:
$$E = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{1 + \cos(2\omega_0 t)}{2} \, dt$$

Since $\cos(2\omega_0 t)$ oscillates symmetrically about zero, its integral over $(-\infty, \infty)$ is zero, but the constant term $\frac{1}{2}$ integrated over an infinite interval gives:
$$E = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{1}{2} \, dt = \infty$$

**Power Calculation:**
$$P = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{T} \cos^2(\omega_0 t) \, dt$$

Using the same identity:
$$P = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{T} \frac{1 + \cos(2\omega_0 t)}{2} \, dt$$

Splitting the integral:
$$P = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \left[ \int_{-T}^{T} \frac{1}{2} \, dt + \int_{-T}^{T} \frac{\cos(2\omega_0 t)}{2} \, dt \right]$$

Evaluating the second integral:
$$\int_{-T}^{T} \cos(2\omega_0 t) \, dt = \left[ \frac{\sin(2\omega_0 t)}{2\omega_0} \right]_{-T}^{T} = \frac{\sin(2\omega_0 T) - \sin(-2\omega_0 T)}{2\omega_0} = \frac{2\sin(2\omega_0 T)}{2\omega_0}$$

This is a bounded quantity, so as $T \to \infty$:
$$P = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \left[ \frac{2T}{2} + \frac{2\sin(2\omega_0 T)}{2\omega_0 \cdot 2} \right] = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \left[ T + \frac{\sin(2\omega_0 T)}{2\omega_0} \right]$$

Taking the limit:
$$P = \lim_{T \to \infty} \left[ \frac{1}{2} + \frac{\sin(2\omega_0 T)}{4\omega_0 T} \right] = \frac{1}{2}$$

**Final Result:** $\boxed{E = \infty, \quad P = \frac{1}{2}}$

**Textual Logic:** This confirms that $\cos(\omega_0 t)$ is a **power signal** — it has finite average power (0.5 W) but infinite total energy. This contrasts with aperiodic signals like $e^{-t}u(t)$ which have **finite energy but zero power**.

### 3.5 Worked Example — Aperiodic Signal Identification

**Problem:** Show that $x(t) = e^{-t}u(t)$ is aperiodic.

**Solution Approach:** Assume $x(t)$ is periodic with period $T > 0$. Then:
$$e^{-(t+T)}u(t+T) = e^{-t}u(t) \quad \forall t$$

For $t < 0$, $u(t) = 0$, so the right side is 0. The left side requires $t + T > 0$, i.e., $t > -T$. For $t > 0$, both sides must be equal:
$$e^{-(t+T)} = e^{-t} \implies e^{-t} \cdot e^{-T} = e^{-t}$$

This requires $e^{-T} = 1$, which holds only when $T = 0$. Since periodicity requires $T > 0$, no such $T$ exists. Hence, $x(t)$ is **aperiodic**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Decision Tree for Testing Signal Periodicity

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start: Given Signal x of t] --> q1{Is x a Standard Sinusoid?}
    q1 -- Yes --> calcA[Compute T0 = 2pi divided by omega]
    calcA --> verA{Verify T0 is Smallest Positive}
    verA -- Yes --> outA[Periodic with T0]
    verA -- No --> reduceA[Divide T0 by integer factor]
    reduceA --> outA
    q1 -- No --> q2{Is x a Sum of Sinusoids?}
    q2 -- Yes --> idB[Identify Individual Periods T1 and T2]
    idB --> ratB{Is T1 over T2 Rational?}
    ratB -- No --> outB[Aperiodic Quasi Periodic]
    ratB -- Yes --> lcmB[Compute T0 = LCM of T1 and T2]
    lcmB --> outB2[Periodic with T0]
    q2 -- No --> q3{Is x a Power of Sinusoid?}
    q3 -- Yes --> redC[Apply Power Reduction Identity]
    redC --> calcC[Compute New Period]
    calcC --> outC[Periodic with Reduced Period]
    q3 -- No --> q4{Does x Repeat Exactly?}
    q4 -- Yes --> obsD[Measure Smallest T from Graph]
    obsD --> outD[Periodic with T0]
    q4 -- No --> outE[Aperiodic Non Periodic]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Signal Classification Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sigA[Signals x of t] --> clsB{Repetition Pattern?}
    clsB -- Repeats --> perA[Periodic Signals]
    clsB -- Does Not Repeat --> aperA[Aperiodic Signals]
    perA --> ptype{Energy vs Power}
    ptype -- Infinite Energy --> powA[Power Signals]
    ptype -- Finite Energy --> enA[Energy Signals]
    powA --> ex1[Examples: sin, cos, square wave]
    enA --> ex2[Examples: periodic pulse train within window]
    aperA --> atype{Energy vs Power}
    atype -- Finite Energy --> enB[Energy Signals]
    atype -- Zero Power --> enB
    enB --> ex3[Examples: exponential decay, single pulse]
    perA --> psub{Sum of Periods Ratio}
    psub -- Rational --> sumP[Periodic Sum]
    psub -- Irrational --> sumA[Aperiodic Sum Quasi Periodic]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Analysis Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inpA[Input Signal x of t] --> detA{Step 1: Identify Type}
    detA --> sinA[Sinusoidal]
    detA --> sumA2[Sum of Sinusoids]
    detA --> trnA[Transient]
    sinA --> calT[Compute T0 = 2pi / omega]
    sumA2 --> chkR{Step 2: Rationality Test}
    chkR -- Yes --> lcmA[Compute LCM Period]
    chkR -- No --> quoA[Quasi Periodic Classification]
    trnA --> expA[Apply Exponential Test]
    expA --> apeA[Aperiodic Declaration]
    calT --> outF[Output: T0]
    lcmA --> outF
    quoA --> outF
    apeA --> outF
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define the following terms with one example each:**
(a) Periodic signal
(b) Aperiodic signal
(c) Fundamental period

**Model Answer:**

(a) **Periodic Signal:** A continuous-time signal $x(t)$ is said to be periodic if there exists a positive constant $T$ such that $x(t+T) = x(t)$ for all values of $t$. **Example:** $x(t) = \sin(2\pi t)$, which has period $T = 1$ second.

(b) **Aperiodic Signal:** A signal that does not satisfy the periodicity condition for any positive value of $T$ is called aperiodic. **Example:** $x(t) = e^{-t}u(t)$, which never exactly repeats itself.

(c) **Fundamental Period:** The smallest positive value of $T$ that satisfies the periodicity condition $x(t+T) = x(t)$ is called the fundamental period, denoted as $T_0$. For $x(t) = \sin(\omega t)$, $T_0 = \frac{2\pi}{\omega}$.

---

**Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Determine whether the signal $x(t) = \sin(3t) + \cos(5t)$ is periodic. If yes, find the fundamental period.**

**Model Answer:**

Individual periods:
$$T_1 = \frac{2\pi}{3}, \quad T_2 = \frac{2\pi}{5}$$

Rationality test:
$$\frac{T_1}{T_2} = \frac{2\pi/3}{2\pi/5} = \frac{5}{3} \in \mathbb{Q}$$

Since the ratio is rational, the signal IS periodic. The fundamental period is:
$$T_0 = \text{LCM}\left(\frac{2\pi}{3}, \frac{2\pi}{5}\right) = 2\pi \cdot \text{LCM}\left(\frac{1}{3}, \frac{1}{5}\right) = 2\pi$$

$$\boxed{T_0 = 2\pi \text{ seconds}}$$

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Check whether the following signals are periodic. If periodic, find the fundamental period.
   (i) $x(t) = 3\sin(4\pi t + \pi/3) + 2\cos(6\pi t)$
   (ii) $x(t) = \sin^2(5t)$
   **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Define energy and power signals. Determine whether $x(t) = e^{-2t}u(t)$ is an energy signal or power signal. Compute its energy. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

**(i)** Given $x(t) = 3\sin(4\pi t + \pi/3) + 2\cos(6\pi t)$

For the sine term: $\omega_1 = 4\pi$, so $T_1 = \frac{2\pi}{4\pi} = \frac{1}{2}$ s.
For the cosine term: $\omega_2 = 6\pi$, so $T_2 = \frac{2\pi}{6\pi} = \frac{1}{3}$ s.

Rationality check:
$$\frac{T_1}{T_2} = \frac{1/2}{1/3} = \frac{3}{2} \in \mathbb{Q} \implies \text{Periodic}$$

Fundamental period:
$$T_0 = \text{LCM}\left(\frac{1}{2}, \frac{1}{3}\right) = 1 \text{ second}$$

**(ii)** Given $x(t) = \sin^2(5t)$

Using the identity $\sin^2(\theta) = \frac{1 - \cos(2\theta)}{2}$:
$$x(t) = \frac{1 - \cos(10t)}{2}$$

The cosine term has $\omega = 10$, so:
$$T_0 = \frac{2\pi}{10} = \frac{\pi}{5} \text{ seconds}$$

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Energy Signal:** A signal with finite, non-zero total energy ($0 < E < \infty$) is called an energy signal.

**Power Signal:** A signal with finite, non-zero average power ($0 < P < \infty$) is called a power signal. All periodic signals are power signals.

For $x(t) = e^{-2t}u(t)$:

**Power calculation:**
$$P = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{T} \vert e^{-2t}u(t) \vert^2 \, dt = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{0}^{T} e^{-4t} \, dt$$

$$P = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \left[ \frac{1 - e^{-4T}}{4} \right] = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1 - e^{-4T}}{8T} = 0$$

**Energy calculation:**
$$E = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert e^{-2t}u(t) \vert^2 \, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-4t} \, dt = \left[ \frac{e^{-4t}}{-4} \right]_0^{\infty} = 0 - \left(-\frac{1}{4}\right) = \frac{1}{4}$$

$$\boxed{E = \frac{1}{4} \text{ J (Finite)}, \quad P = 0 \implies \text{Energy Signal}}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the classification of signals based on periodicity with suitable mathematical conditions. Differentiate between continuous-time and discrete-time periodic signals with examples. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For each of the following signals, determine if it is periodic and find the fundamental period if applicable:
   (i) $x(t) = \cos(2t) + \sin(3t)$
   (ii) $x[n] = \cos(0.5\pi n)$
   **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**Classification based on periodicity:**

A signal $x(t)$ is **periodic** if $\exists T > 0 : x(t+T) = x(t)$ for all $t$.
A signal is **aperiodic** if no such $T$ exists.

**Continuous-Time Periodic Signal:** Period $T$ can be any positive real number.
Example: $x(t) = \sin(2\pi t)$ has $T = 1$ s.

**Discrete-Time Periodic Signal:** Period $N$ must be a positive integer.
Example: $x[n] = \cos[\pi n/3]$ has $N = 6$ samples.

**Key Difference:** Discrete-time periodicity requires the discrete frequency $\Omega$ to satisfy $\frac{\Omega}{2\pi} = \frac{m}{N}$ for coprime integers $m, N$.

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**(i)** $x(t) = \cos(2t) + \sin(3t)$

Individual periods:
$$T_1 = \frac{2\pi}{2} = \pi, \quad T_2 = \frac{2\pi}{3}$$

Rationality: $\frac{T_1}{T_2} = \frac{\pi}{2\pi/3} = \frac{3}{2} \in \mathbb{Q}$ → Periodic.

$$T_0 = \text{LCM}\left(\pi, \frac{2\pi}{3}\right) = 2\pi \text{ seconds}$$

**(ii)** $x[n] = \cos(0.5\pi n)$

For discrete-time periodicity, we need smallest integer $N$ such that:
$$\cos(0.5\pi(n+N)) = \cos(0.5\pi n)$$

This requires $0.5\pi N = 2\pi k$, i.e., $N = 4k$.

Smallest positive $N$ is obtained at $k = 1$:
$$\boxed{N_0 = 4 \text{ samples}}$$

The signal is periodic.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Discrete vs Continuous Confusion:** Many students incorrectly apply continuous-time formulas to discrete-time signals. Remember: for $x[n]$, the period $N$ must be a *positive integer*. If solving $N = \frac{2\pi k}{\Omega}$ yields a non-integer, the signal is **NOT periodic**.
> 2. **LCM Misapplication:** The LCM of $\frac{1}{2}$ and $\frac{1}{3}$ is **1**, not $\frac{1}{6}$. LCM of fractions $\frac{a}{b}$ and $\frac{c}{d}$ is $\frac{\text{LCM}(a,c)}{\text{GCD}(b,d)}$. Students commonly write $\frac{1}{6}$ as LCM and lose 2 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting the Rationality Test:** Before computing LCM, students must verify $\frac{T_1}{T_2} \in \mathbb{Q}$. If irrational (e.g., $\pi$), the signal is **aperiodic** — no LCM exists.
> 4. **Phase Shift Misconception:** Students often mistakenly include the phase $\phi$ when calculating period. Phase shift does **NOT** affect periodicity.
> 5. **Unit Omission:** KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks for omitting units (seconds for $T$, Hz for $f$, samples for $N$).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Periodic and Non-Periodic Signals**

- **Core Definition:** Periodic ⟺ $\exists T > 0: x(t+T) = x(t)$; otherwise aperiodic.

- **Fundamental Period Formula:** For $\sin(\omega t)$ or $\cos(\omega t)$: $T_0 = \frac{2\pi}{\omega} = \frac{1}{f}$.

- **Sum Periodicity:** $x(t) = x_1(t) + x_2(t)$ is periodic **only if** $\frac{T_1}{T_2} \in \mathbb{Q}$.

- **LCM Calculation:** $T_0 = \text{LCM}(T_1, T_2)$; for fractions, use $T_0 = \frac{\text{LCM(numerators)}}{\text{GCD(denominators)}}$.

- **Squared Sinusoids:** Period of $\sin^2(\omega t)$ is $\frac{\pi}{\omega}$ (half of original).

- **DC Signal Special Case:** $x(t) = C$ is periodic with any $T > 0$.

- **Energy vs Power:**
  - Periodic signals → Power signals ($P > 0, E = \infty$).
  - Aperiodic decaying signals → Energy signals ($E < \infty, P = 0$).

- **Discrete-Time Rule:** $\cos(\Omega_0 n)$ periodic ⟺ $\frac{\Omega_0}{2\pi} = \frac{m}{N}$ with $m, N$ coprime integers.

- **Phase Invariance:** Phase shift $\phi$ does NOT change the period.

- **Frequency-Period Relationship:** $f = \frac{1}{T}$, $\omega = 2\pi f = \frac{2\pi}{T}$.

- **Common Examples:** Periodic → sine, cosine, square wave, triangular wave, pulse train. Aperiodic → exponential decay, single pulse, random noise.

- **Examination Mantra:** Always test rationality BEFORE computing LCM.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Energy and Power Signals. Periodicity and Symmetry Property of DT Signals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST416 - SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Energy and Power Signals. Periodicity and Symmetry Property of DT Signals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Energy and Power Signals — Formal Definitions

In the study of **Signals and Systems**, every signal $x(t)$ (continuous-time) or $x[n]$ (discrete-time) carries a measurable physical attribute: either **energy**, **power**, **both**, or **neither**. KTU 2024 Scheme treats these classifications as a foundational gatekeeper before analyzing any LTI system.

> [!NOTE]
> **Energy (E)** is the total "effort" delivered by a signal over all time, measured in Joules. **Power (P)** is the rate at which energy is delivered, measured in Watts.

**Continuous-Time (CT) Energy and Power:**

$$E = \lim_{T \to \infty} \int_{-T}^{T} \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt$$

$$P = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{T} \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt$$

**Discrete-Time (DT) Energy and Power:**

$$E = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n] \vert^2$$

$$P = \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{1}{2N+1} \sum_{n=-N}^{N} \vert x[n] \vert^2$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Energy Signal:** $0 < E < \infty$ and $P = 0$ (finite energy, vanishing average power)
> **Power Signal:** $0 < P < \infty$ and $E = \infty$ (infinite energy, finite non-zero average power)
> **Neither:** A pathological signal where both $E = \infty$ and $P = \infty$ (rare in undergraduate syllabus)

### 1.2 Periodicity of Discrete-Time Signals — Formal Definition

A discrete-time signal $x[n]$ is **periodic** with period $N$ if there exists a positive integer $N$ such that:

$$x[n] = x[n + N], \quad \forall \, n \in \mathbb{Z}, \quad N > 0$$

The **fundamental period** $N_0$ is the smallest positive integer satisfying this condition. If no such $N$ exists, the signal is **aperiodic**.

### 1.3 Symmetry of DT Signals — Formal Definition

A discrete-time signal is classified by its symmetry about the vertical axis or the origin:

> [!NOTE]
> **Even Signal:** $x[n] = x[-n]$ — mirror-symmetric about the y-axis.
> **Odd Signal:** $x[n] = -x[-n]$ — anti-symmetric (rotational symmetry) about the origin.

Any arbitrary signal $x[n]$ can be uniquely decomposed as:

$$x[n] = x_e[n] + x_o[n]$$

where $x_e[n]$ is the **even part** and $x_o[n]$ is the **odd part**.

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — Making It Click

> [!TIP]
> **Energy vs. Power Analogy:** Think of energy as the *total volume of water* poured from a tap during a day, while power is the *rate of flow* (liters per second). A dripping tap has tiny power but accumulates finite total water (energy) over the day. A continuously running tap has constant power but accumulates infinite water if left forever — this is the power signal.
>
> **Periodicity Analogy:** A clock with hands that return to the same position every 12 hours is periodic. If the hands moved a different amount each cycle, it would be aperiodic.
>
> **Symmetry Analogy:** A perfect human face is approximately even-symmetric. The letter "S" has neither even nor odd symmetry, but the letter "A" (with the crossbar) has even symmetry about its vertical axis.

---

### 1.5 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The unit of **energy** $E$ in signal theory is Joules (J). The unit of **power** $P$ is Watts (W). The fundamental relationship $P = E/T$ (average power) underpins every classification made in this module.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Energy-Power Dichotomy — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1 — Compute Total Energy:**
For a CT signal, integrate the squared magnitude over all time. For DT, sum the squared magnitude over all integer indices.

**Step 2 — Compute Average Power:**
Power is energy averaged over the duration. The factor $\frac{1}{2T}$ (CT) or $\frac{1}{2N+1}$ (DT) normalizes for infinite duration.

**Step 3 — Classify:**
- If $E$ is **finite** ($< \infty$) $\Rightarrow$ **Energy Signal** $\Rightarrow$ $P = 0$.
- If $E$ is **infinite** ($\to \infty$) but $P$ is **finite** $\Rightarrow$ **Power Signal**.
- If both diverge $\Rightarrow$ **Neither Signal** (e.g., $x(t) = t$).

**Step 4 — Why cannot a signal be both?**
For a non-zero signal, if $0 < E < \infty$, then:

$$P = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{E_{[-T,T]}}{2T} = \frac{\text{finite constant}}{\infty} = 0$$

So $P$ must be zero. Thus, a non-zero signal is **never** both energy-type and power-type simultaneously.

### 2.2 Periodicity Logic for Discrete-Time Signals

**Step 1 — Search for the smallest N:**
Substitute trial values of $N$ into $x[n] = x[n + N]$ and find the smallest positive integer that holds for all $n$.

**Step 2 — Properties of Fundamental Period:**
- If $x[n]$ has fundamental period $N_0$, then $x[n]$ is also periodic with periods $2N_0, 3N_0, \ldots, kN_0$ for all integers $k \geq 1$.
- Time-scaling a DT signal alters periodicity: $x[2n]$ is periodic with $N_0/\gcd(N_0, 2)$ only if $N_0$ is even.

**Step 3 — Sum of Periodic Signals:**
$x[n] = x_1[n] + x_2[n]$ where $x_1$ has period $N_1$ and $x_2$ has period $N_2$. The sum is periodic **iff** $N_1/N_2$ is a **rational number**, in which case the fundamental period is $\text{lcm}(N_1, N_2)$.

**Step 4 — The Continuous-to-Discrete Period Trap:**
A CT sinusoid $\cos(\omega_0 t)$ with $\omega_0 \neq 2\pi k / N$ (for any integer $N, k$) sampled at frequency $f_s$ yields an **aperiodic** DT signal, even though the original was periodic.

### 2.3 Symmetry Logic for DT Signals

**Step 1 — Test Evenness:**
Check if $x[n] \stackrel{?}{=} x[-n]$ for all $n$. If yes, even.

**Step 2 — Test Oddness:**
Check if $x[n] \stackrel{?}{=} -x[-n]$ for all $n$. If yes, odd. A consequence is that $x[0] = 0$ for all odd signals.

**Step 3 — Decomposition (Universal Truth):**
Any signal $x[n]$ can be uniquely split as:

$$x_e[n] = \frac{x[n] + x[-n]}{2}, \quad x_o[n] = \frac{x[n] - x[-n]}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Multiplication Rules:**
| Combination of Inputs | Symmetry of Product |
| :--- | :--- |
| Even × Even | Even |
| Odd × Odd | Even |
| Even × Odd | Odd |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | CT Formula | DT Formula | Condition / Classification |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Energy** | $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^2 dt$ | $\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n] \vert^2$ | Finite $\Rightarrow$ Energy Signal |
| 2 | **Power** | $\lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T}\int_{-T}^{T}\vert x(t) \vert^2 dt$ | $\lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{1}{2N+1}\sum_{n=-N}^{N}\vert x[n] \vert^2$ | Finite, $E=\infty$ $\Rightarrow$ Power Signal |
| 3 | **Periodicity** | $x(t) = x(t+T)$ | $x[n] = x[n+N]$, $N \in \mathbb{Z}^+$ | Fundamental period = smallest $T$ or $N$ |
| 4 | **Sum Periodic** | — | $\text{lcm}(N_1, N_2)$ | Requires $N_1/N_2 \in \mathbb{Q}$ |
| 5 | **Even Signal** | $x(t) = x(-t)$ | $x[n] = x[-n]$ | Symmetric about y-axis |
| 6 | **Odd Signal** | $x(t) = -x(-t)$ | $x[n] = -x[-n]$ | $x[0] = 0$ mandatory |
| 7 | **Even Part** | $\frac{x(t) + x(-t)}{2}$ | $\frac{x[n] + x[-n]}{2}$ | Always exists |
| 8 | **Odd Part** | $\frac{x(t) - x(-t)}{2}$ | $\frac{x[n] - x[-n]}{2}$ | Always exists |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Energy/Power:** Engineers designing a 5G base station must classify transmitted waveforms as energy or power to budget battery life and meet FCC spectral mask regulations.
> **Periodicity:** In OFDM (used in Wi-Fi 6/7 and 4G/5G), the cyclic prefix exploits periodicity to combat multipath fading — a direct KTU industry link.
> **Symmetry:** The **Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT)** requires signals to be decomposed into even/odd parts to exploit the **real/imaginary part separability**, halving the computational cost in spectral analysis.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1: Why $E = \infty \Rightarrow P = 0$ for a Power Signal

Let $x(t)$ be a non-zero, bounded, periodic signal with period $T_0$ and finite amplitude $\vert x(t) \vert \leq M$.

**Step 1:** Compute total energy over one period.

$$E_{\text{one period}} = \int_{0}^{T_0} \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt \leq M^2 T_0 < \infty$$

**Step 2:** Compute energy over $k$ periods.

$$E_{k \text{ periods}} = k \cdot E_{\text{one period}} = k \cdot M^2 T_0$$

**Step 3:** Total energy as $k \to \infty$:

$$E = \lim_{k \to \infty} k M^2 T_0 = \infty$$

**Step 4:** Compute average power:

$$P = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{T} \vert x(t) \vert^2 dt$$

**Step 5:** For a periodic signal, the integral over $[-T, T]$ grows linearly with $T$, and the normalization $\frac{1}{2T}$ cancels this growth:

$$P = \frac{1}{T_0} \int_{0}^{T_0} \vert x(t) \vert^2 dt = \text{finite constant}$$

**Step 6:** Conclusion: For a non-zero periodic signal, $E = \infty$ and $0 < P < \infty$ — a power signal. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2: Periodicity of Sum of Two DT Sinusoids

Given $x[n] = \cos\left(\frac{\pi}{6} n\right) + \cos\left(\frac{\pi}{4} n\right)$.

**Step 1:** Find the period of $x_1[n] = \cos\left(\frac{\pi}{6} n\right)$.

For DT cosine, period is $N_1 = \frac{2\pi}{\omega_1} \cdot k$ where $k$ is the smallest integer making $N_1$ a positive integer.

$$N_1 = \frac{2\pi}{\pi/6} \cdot k = 12 \cdot k$$

The smallest positive integer is achieved at $k=1$, giving $N_1 = 12$.

**Step 2:** Find the period of $x_2[n] = \cos\left(\frac{\pi}{4} n\right)$.

$$N_2 = \frac{2\pi}{\pi/4} \cdot k = 8 \cdot k$$

Smallest positive integer at $k=1$: $N_2 = 8$.

**Step 3:** Check rationality of ratio.

$$\frac{N_1}{N_2} = \frac{12}{8} = \frac{3}{2} \in \mathbb{Q}$$

**Step 4:** The sum is periodic with fundamental period:

$$N_0 = \text{lcm}(N_1, N_2) = \text{lcm}(12, 8) = 24$$

**Step 5:** Verify with the formula: $x[n+24] = \cos\left(\frac{\pi}{6}(n+24)\right) + \cos\left(\frac{\pi}{4}(n+24)\right)$.

$$\cos\left(\frac{\pi n}{6} + 4\pi\right) = \cos\left(\frac{\pi n}{6}\right) \quad \checkmark$$

$$\cos\left(\frac{\pi n}{4} + 6\pi\right) = \cos\left(\frac{\pi n}{4}\right) \quad \checkmark$$

Both are invariant. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation 3: Even/Odd Decomposition of $x[n] = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$ with $x[0] = 3$

**Step 1:** Construct the signal indexed from $n = -2$ to $n = 2$:

$$x[-2] = 1, \; x[-1] = 2, \; x[0] = 3, \; x[1] = 4, \; x[2] = 5$$

**Step 2:** Compute the time-reversed version $x[-n]$:

$$x[2] = 5, \; x[1] = 4, \; x[0] = 3, \; x[-1] = 2, \; x[-2] = 1$$

**Step 3:** Compute the even part $x_e[n] = \frac{x[n] + x[-n]}{2}$:

$$x_e[0] = \frac{3+3}{2} = 3$$

$$x_e[1] = x_e[-1] = \frac{4+2}{2} = 3$$

$$x_e[2] = x_e[-2] = \frac{5+1}{2} = 3$$

So $x_e[n] = \{3, 3, 3, 3, 3\}$ — a constant signal (which is even, confirmed).

**Step 4:** Compute the odd part $x_o[n] = \frac{x[n] - x[-n]}{2}$:

$$x_o[0] = \frac{3-3}{2} = 0 \quad \checkmark \text{ (odd signals must have } x_o[0] = 0\text{)}$$

$$x_o[1] = \frac{4-2}{2} = 1, \quad x_o[-1] = -1$$

$$x_o[2] = \frac{5-1}{2} = 2, \quad x_o[-2] = -2$$

So $x_o[n] = \{-2, -1, 0, 1, 2\}$.

**Step 5:** Verify the decomposition $x[n] = x_e[n] + x_o[n]$:

For $n=1$: $x_e[1] + x_o[1] = 3 + 1 = 4 = x[1] \quad \checkmark$

For $n=-2$: $x_e[-2] + x_o[-2] = 3 + (-2) = 1 = x[-2] \quad \checkmark$

$\blacksquare$

---

### 3.4 Exhaustive Derivation 4: Energy and Power of a Rectangular Pulse Train

Consider the periodic rectangular pulse train $x(t)$ with amplitude $A$, pulse width $\tau$, and period $T_0$.

**Step 1:** Energy over one period.

$$E_{\text{period}} = \int_{0}^{\tau} A^2 \, dt = A^2 \tau$$

**Step 2:** Total energy.

$$E = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} A^2 \, dt = \lim_{k \to \infty} k A^2 \tau = \infty$$

**Step 3:** Average power.

$$P = \frac{1}{T_0} \int_{0}^{T_0} \vert x(t) \vert^2 dt = \frac{1}{T_0} \cdot A^2 \tau = \frac{A^2 \tau}{T_0}$$

**Step 4:** Since $0 < P < \infty$ and $E = \infty$, this is a **Power Signal**. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.5 Exhaustive Derivation 5: Energy of a Single Rectangular Pulse (Non-periodic)

A single rectangular pulse $x(t) = A$ for $0 \leq t \leq \tau$ and $0$ otherwise.

**Step 1:** Energy.

$$E = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^2 dt = \int_{0}^{\tau} A^2 dt = A^2 \tau < \infty$$

**Step 2:** Average power.

$$P = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{T} A^2 dt \text{ (over finite support)} = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{A^2 \tau}{2T} = 0$$

**Step 3:** Classification: **Energy Signal**. $\blacksquare$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Decision Flowchart: Classifying a Signal as Energy or Power

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Signal x t or x n given] --> B[Compute Energy E]
    B --> C{Is 0 less than E less than infinity?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Classify as ENERGY Signal]
    D --> E[P equals 0 automatically]
    C -- No --> F{E equals infinity?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Compute Power P]
    G --> H{Is 0 less than P less than infinity?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Classify as POWER Signal]
    H -- No --> J[Classify as NEITHER Signal]
    F -- No --> J
    I --> K[End]
    D --> K
    J --> K
    E --> K
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Periodicity Test for DT Signals

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L[Input DT Signal x n] --> M[Examine Signal Structure]
    M --> N{Try integer N from 1 upwards}
    N -- N=1 --> O{Does x n equal x n plus 1?}
    O -- Yes --> P[Fundamental Period N0 equals 1]
    O -- No --> Q[Try N equals 2]
    Q --> R{Does x n equal x n plus 2?}
    R -- Yes --> S[Fundamental Period N0 equals 2]
    R -- No --> T[Increment N and retest]
    T --> U{Found smallest N?}
    U -- Yes --> V[Report N0 as fundamental period]
    U -- No --> W[Signal is APERIODIC]
    P --> X[End]
    S --> X
    V --> X
    W --> X
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Even/Odd Decomposition Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Signal x n] --> B[Compute x minus n]
    A --> C[Sum: x n plus x minus n]
    A --> D[Difference: x n minus x minus n]
    B --> C
    B --> D
    C --> E[Divide by 2: Even Part x e of n]
    D --> F[Divide by 2: Odd Part x o of n]
    E --> G[Reconstruction: x n equals x e plus x o]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Verified Decomposition]
```

### 4.4 Functional Architecture: Energy-Power-Symmetry Interaction in LTI Systems

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Signal x n] --> B[Symmetry Decomposition Module]
    B --> C[Even Part x e n]
    B --> D[Odd Part x o n]
    C --> E[Classifier Module]
    D --> E
    E --> F{Is E finite?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Energy Signal Path]
    F -- No --> H{Is P finite non-zero?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Power Signal Path]
    H -- No --> J[Neither Signal Path]
    G --> K[Suitable for Transient Analysis]
    I --> L[Suitable for Steady State Spectral Analysis]
    J --> M[Requires Special Handling]
    K --> N[Output: System Response]
    L --> N
    M --> N
```

### 4.5 Comparative Plot Sketches (ASCII Representation)

**Even Signal** (e.g., $x[n] = \cos(0.2\pi n)$):

```
        |
      1 |**         **
        | **       **
        |  **     **
        |   **   **
        |    ** **
   -----+----*-+-------*-----> n
        |    n=0
        |
      -1|
        |
```

**Odd Signal** (e.g., $x[n] = \sin(0.2\pi n)$):

```
        |         **
      1 |        *  *
        |       *    *
        |      *      *
        |     *        *
   -----+----*----+----*-----> n
        |    n=0   *
        |         *
        |        *
      -1|       *
        |
```

### 4.6 Summary Concept Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Signals and Systems Module 1 Core] --> B[Energy Signals]
    A --> C[Power Signals]
    A --> D[Periodicity]
    A --> E[Symmetry]
    B --> F[Finite E, Zero P]
    C --> G[Infinite E, Finite P]
    D --> H[Continuous and Discrete Cases]
    E --> I[Even and Odd Decomposition]
    F --> J[Application: Transient Analysis]
    G --> K[Application: Steady State Analysis]
    H --> L[Application: DFT, OFDM, Communications]
    I --> M[Application: Signal Processing Simplification]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
Define an energy signal and a power signal. State one example for each from the discrete-time domain.

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Energy Signal:** A signal $x[n]$ for which the total energy $E = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n] \vert^2$ is finite ($0 < E < \infty$), and the average power $P = 0$.
> **Example:** $x[n] = (0.5)^n u[n]$ where $E = \frac{1}{1-0.25} = \frac{4}{3}$ J (finite).
>
> **Power Signal:** A signal for which $E = \infty$ but average power $P = \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{1}{2N+1}\sum_{n=-N}^{N} \vert x[n] \vert^2$ is finite and non-zero.
> **Example:** $x[n] = \cos(0.5\pi n)$ where $P = 0.5$ W (finite, non-zero).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
Check whether the signal $x[n] = \cos\left(\frac{3\pi}{7}n\right) + \sin\left(\frac{2\pi}{5}n\right)$ is periodic. If yes, find the fundamental period.

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1:** Period of $\cos\left(\frac{3\pi}{7}n\right)$: $N_1 = \frac{2\pi}{3\pi/7} = \frac{14}{3}$. This is **not an integer**, so the cosine alone is **aperiodic**.

**Step 2:** Period of $\sin\left(\frac{2\pi}{5}n\right)$: $N_2 = \frac{2\pi}{2\pi/5} = 5$.

**Step 3:** Since one component is aperiodic, the **sum is aperiodic**. The signal is **not periodic** in DT.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often wrongly assume that $N_1 = 14/3$ "rounds off" to 5. A discrete-time sinusoid is periodic **only if** the ratio $2\pi/\omega$ is a **rational number** expressible as a fraction. $14/3$ does not yield an integer period.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — ESE Module Internal Choice

---

## **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks]** For the signal $x(t) = 2\cos(10\pi t) + \sin(20\pi t)$, determine whether it is an energy signal, a power signal, or neither. Compute the relevant measure.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Determine the even and odd parts of the discrete-time signal:

$$x[n] = \begin{cases} 2, & n = -1 \\ 3, & n = 0 \\ 4, & n = 1 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a):**

**Step 1:** Both $2\cos(10\pi t)$ and $\sin(20\pi t)$ are bounded, non-zero, and periodic.

**Step 2:** Period of $2\cos(10\pi t)$: $T_1 = \frac{2\pi}{10\pi} = 0.2$ s.

**Step 3:** Period of $\sin(20\pi t)$: $T_2 = \frac{2\pi}{20\pi} = 0.1$ s.

**Step 4:** Ratio $\frac{T_1}{T_2} = 2 \in \mathbb{Q}$, so the sum is periodic with $T_0 = \text{lcm}(0.2, 0.1) = 0.2$ s.

**Step 5:** Since it is periodic and non-zero, $E = \infty$.

**Step 6:** Compute power:

$$P = \frac{1}{T_0}\int_{0}^{T_0} \vert x(t) \vert^2 dt$$

$$P = \frac{1}{0.2}\int_{0}^{0.2} \left[2\cos(10\pi t) + \sin(20\pi t)\right]^2 dt$$

**Step 7:** Expand the integrand:

$$\left[2\cos(10\pi t) + \sin(20\pi t)\right]^2 = 4\cos^2(10\pi t) + 4\cos(10\pi t)\sin(20\pi t) + \sin^2(20\pi t)$$

**Step 8:** Use the identity $2\sin(20\pi t)\cos(10\pi t) = \sin(30\pi t) + \sin(10\pi t)$. The cross-term integrates to zero over the period $T_0 = 0.2$ s because both $30\pi$ and $10\pi$ correspond to integer multiples of the fundamental frequency.

**Step 9:** Integrate each surviving term:

$$\int_{0}^{0.2} 4\cos^2(10\pi t) \, dt = 4 \cdot \frac{0.2}{2} = 0.4$$

$$\int_{0}^{0.2} \sin^2(20\pi t) \, dt = \frac{0.2}{2} = 0.1$$

**Step 10:** Final result:

$$P = \frac{0.4 + 0.1}{0.2} = \frac{0.5}{0.2} = 2.5 \text{ W}$$

**Conclusion:** $x(t)$ is a **Power Signal** with $P = 2.5$ W and $E = \infty$.

---

**Part (b):**

**Step 1:** Time-reverse the signal: $x[-1] = 4$, $x[0] = 3$, $x[1] = 2$.

**Step 2:** Compute the even part $x_e[n] = \frac{x[n] + x[-n]}{2}$:

$$x_e[-1] = \frac{2+4}{2} = 3, \quad x_e[0] = \frac{3+3}{2} = 3, \quad x_e[1] = \frac{4+2}{2} = 3$$

**Step 3:** Compute the odd part $x_o[n] = \frac{x[n] - x[-n]}{2}$:

$$x_o[-1] = \frac{2-4}{2} = -1, \quad x_o[0] = \frac{3-3}{2} = 0, \quad x_o[1] = \frac{4-2}{2} = 1$$

**Step 4:** Verification: $x_e[n] + x_o[n]$:

$$x_e[0] + x_o[0] = 3 + 0 = 3 = x[0] \quad \checkmark$$

$$x_e[1] + x_o[1] = 3 + 1 = 4 = x[1] \quad \checkmark$$

**Conclusion:** $x_e[n] = \{3, 3, 3\}$ (constant, hence even) and $x_o[n] = \{-1, 0, 1\}$.

---

## **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a) [7 Marks]** For the discrete-time signal $x[n] = (-0.5)^n u[n]$, compute the energy and classify the signal.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Check the periodicity of $x[n] = e^{j(3\pi/8)n} + e^{j(\pi/6)n}$ and find the fundamental period if periodic.

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a):**

**Step 1:** Apply the energy formula:

$$E = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n] \vert^2 = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \vert (-0.5)^n \vert^2 = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} (0.25)^n$$

**Step 2:** Evaluate the geometric series:

$$E = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} (0.25)^n = \frac{1}{1 - 0.25} = \frac{1}{0.75} = \frac{4}{3} \text{ J}$$

**Step 3:** Since $0 < E < \infty$, the signal is an **Energy Signal**, and $P = 0$.

---

**Part (b):**

**Step 1:** Find the period of $e^{j(3\pi/8)n}$:

$$N_1 = \frac{2\pi}{3\pi/8} \cdot k = \frac{16}{3} \cdot k$$

The smallest integer $k$ that makes $N_1$ an integer is $k = 3$, giving $N_1 = 16$.

**Step 2:** Find the period of $e^{j(\pi/6)n}$:

$$N_2 = \frac{2\pi}{\pi/6} \cdot k = 12 \cdot k$$

Smallest positive integer at $k=1$: $N_2 = 12$.

**Step 3:** Rationality check: $\frac{N_1}{N_2} = \frac{16}{12} = \frac{4}{3} \in \mathbb{Q}$.

**Step 4:** Fundamental period of the sum:

$$N_0 = \text{lcm}(N_1, N_2) = \text{lcm}(16, 12) = 48$$

**Conclusion:** The signal is **periodic** with fundamental period $N_0 = 48$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Integer period assumption:** Students often forget that DT periodicity requires the period to be a **positive integer**. A ratio like $16/3$ is NOT a valid period, no matter how small the fraction.
> 2. **Energy-Power mutual exclusivity:** Many students write "$E = 0$ and $P = \infty$" for power signals. Correct: $E = \infty$, $P = $ finite non-zero.
> 3. **Odd signal at $n=0$:** Always check $x[0] = 0$ for odd signals. If $x[0] \neq 0$, the signal CANNOT be purely odd.
> 4. **Sum of periodic signals:** If $N_1/N_2$ is **irrational**, the sum is aperiodic — do not attempt to find an lcm.
> 5. **Decomposition uniqueness:** A signal has only ONE valid even-odd decomposition. Submitting two different decompositions will cost full marks.

---

### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Energy Signal** $\Rightarrow$ $0 < E < \infty$, $P = 0$ (e.g., single pulse, decaying exponential).
- [x] **Power Signal** $\Rightarrow$ $0 < P < \infty$, $E = \infty$ (e.g., sinusoid, periodic pulse train).
- [x] **Neither** $\Rightarrow$ both diverge (e.g., $x(t) = t$, $x(t) = e^t$).
- [x] **DT Periodicity:** $x[n] = x[n+N]$ where $N \in \mathbb{Z}^+$. The ratio $\frac{2\pi}{\omega}$ must yield a positive integer when scaled by the smallest $k$.
- [x] **Fundamental Period:** The **smallest** positive $N$ satisfying periodicity. $2N$, $3N$ are also periods but **not** fundamental.
- [x] **Sum Periodicity:** Requires $N_1/N_2 \in \mathbb{Q}$, then $N_0 = \text{lcm}(N_1, N_2)$.
- [x] **Even Signal:** $x[n] = x[-n]$; mirror image across y-axis.
- [x] **Odd Signal:** $x[n] = -x[-n]$; passes through origin, $x[0] = 0$.
- [x] **Decomposition:** $x[n] = x_e[n] + x_o[n]$ is **unique and universal**.
- [x] **Product Rules:** Even × Even = Even, Odd × Odd = Even, Even × Odd = Odd.
- [x] **KTU Hot Spot:** "Find the energy/power of $x(t) = A\cos(\omega_0 t) + B\sin(\omega_0 t + \phi)$" appears in **almost every** KTU 2024 ESE Module 1 paper.
- [x] **Always** compute $P$ over **one period** for periodic signals, not over infinite duration.
- [x] **Time-reversal and time-scaling** of DT signals require careful analysis of integer arithmetic.
- [x] **Symmetry is the gateway** to the Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT) — covered in Module 2.
- [x] **Sanity check:** A constant $c$ signal is **even** (because $c = c$) and has power $P = c^2$ (a power signal), energy $E = \infty$.
- [x] **Memorize the lcm and gcd** of small integers (1–24) for fast exam problem-solving.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Support of Sequences
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST416 - SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Support of Sequences

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Discrete-Time Signals and Systems** (PECST416, KTU 2024 Scheme), the **Support of a sequence** $x[n]$ is rigorously defined as the set of all integer indices $n \in \mathbb{Z}$ for which the sequence possesses **non-zero** values.

Mathematically, the support of a sequence $x[n]$ is the set:

$$
\text{Supp}\{x[n]\} = \{n \in \mathbb{Z} \mid x[n] \neq 0\}
$$

The **cardinality** (size) of this set dictates whether the sequence is classified as having **finite support** or **infinite support**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** The concept of support is foundational because it directly determines the **causality, stability, energy, and memory** characteristics of any discrete-time system. KTU examiners frequently test this concept via short 3-mark conceptual questions in Part A.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are reading a long newspaper where most of the articles have been **blacked out** with a marker, and only a few words remain visible. The "support" of the readable content is simply the list of positions (paragraph numbers) where the text is still legible.

In the same way, a discrete-time signal is like an infinite sequence of slots indexed by $n = \ldots, -2, -1, 0, 1, 2, \ldots$. The "support" is the map of those slots that contain meaningful (non-zero) data. All other slots are effectively "blank" (equal to zero).

### 1.3 Classification by Support

A sequence is fundamentally classified based on the nature of its support:

| Support Type | Description | Standard Metric |
|---|---|---|
| **Finite Support** | Sequence has non-zero values over a **finite** set of $n$ | $\|\text{Supp}\| < \infty$ |
| **Infinite Support** | Sequence has non-zero values over an **infinite** set of $n$ | $\|\text{Supp}\| = \infty$ |
| **Left-Sided** | Non-zero only for $n \leq N$ | $\text{Supp} \subseteq (-\infty, N]$ |
| **Right-Sided** | Non-zero only for $n \geq N$ | $\text{Supp} \subseteq [N, \infty)$ |
| **Two-Sided** | Non-zero for all $n \in \mathbb{Z}$ | $\text{Supp} = \mathbb{Z}$ |
| **Causal** | Right-sided with $N \geq 0$ | $\text{Supp} \subseteq [0, \infty)$ |
| **Anti-Causal** | Left-sided with $N \leq 0$ | $\text{Supp} \subseteq (-\infty, 0]$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Zero must be exactly zero.** A value of $0.0001$ is technically still part of the support. In KTU board exams, always state "non-zero" rather than "very small" when classifying support.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Breakdown of the Support Concept

To truly master the support of sequences, we dissect the concept into five structured logical layers:

- **Step 1 — Index Identification:** Scan the sequence $x[n]$ across all integer indices $n \in \mathbb{Z}$. Each index represents a discrete time-stamp or sample number.
- **Step 2 — Zero Test:** At every index $n$, evaluate the amplitude $x[n]$. If $x[n] = 0$, that index is **outside** the support. If $x[n] \neq 0$, that index is **inside** the support.
- **Step 3 — Cardinality Determination:** Count the number of indices inside the support. If the count is finite, we have **finite support**; otherwise, **infinite support**.
- **Step 4 — Directional Classification:** Determine whether the support extends infinitely to the left, to the right, or to both directions. This yields the left-sided, right-sided, or two-sided classification.
- **Step 5 — Causality Inference:** A sequence is **causal** if and only if its support is a subset of $[0, \infty)$ — meaning no non-zero value exists for $n < 0$. This single property is the most-examined aspect in KTU valuation scripts.

### 2.2 Why the Support Concept Matters in Engineering

The support of a sequence is not merely a mathematical abstraction; it has tangible engineering consequences:

- **Memory of LTI Systems:** The support of the impulse response $h[n]$ determines the memory of a Linear Time-Invariant (LTI) system. If $h[n]$ has finite support, the system is a **FIR (Finite Impulse Response)** system. Infinite support yields an **IIR (Infinite Impulse Response)** system.
- **Storage Requirements:** In digital hardware (DSP chips, FPGAs), only the non-zero samples need to be stored. Knowing the support allows engineers to allocate buffers efficiently.
- **Spectral Implications:** A sequence with **finite support** in the time domain corresponds to an **infinite bandwidth** in the frequency domain (and vice-versa, per the **Duality Principle**).
- **Sparse Signal Processing:** Modern applications like **compressed sensing** and **5G pilot transmission** rely on signals that are mostly zero — i.e., signals with very sparse (small) support sets.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below contains the **high-yield formula sheet** for the topic of Support of Sequences, perfectly aligned with KTU 2024 Module 1 outcomes.

| # | Concept | Mathematical Expression | Engineering Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Support Definition | $\text{Supp}\{x[n]\} = \{n \in \mathbb{Z} \mid x[n] \neq 0\}$ | Set of indices with non-zero amplitude |
| 2 | Finite Support | $\vert \text{Supp}\{x[n]\} \vert < \infty$ | Energy is bounded and finite |
| 3 | Infinite Support | $\vert \text{Supp}\{x[n]\} \vert = \infty$ | May or may not have finite energy |
| 4 | Left-Sided | $x[n] = 0$ for $n > N$ | Non-zero only in the past |
| 5 | Right-Sided | $x[n] = 0$ for $n < N$ | Non-zero only from $N$ onwards |
| 6 | Causal | $x[n] = 0$ for $n < 0$ | Realizable in real-time systems |
| 7 | Anti-Causal | $x[n] = 0$ for $n > 0$ | Non-realizable in real-time |
| 8 | Total Energy | $E = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n] \vert^{2}$ | Finite iff support is finite or decays fast |
| 9 | Indicator Function | $\mathbb{1}_{S}(n) = \begin{cases} 1, & n \in S \\ 0, & n \notin S \end{cases}$ | Used to mask/truncate sequences |
| 10 | Support Length | $L = \max(\text{Supp}) - \min(\text{Supp}) + 1$ | Number of non-zero samples (finite case) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Tip:** In the formula $E = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n] \vert^{2}$, the energy is **finite** if the support is finite. If the support is infinite, the energy depends on the rate of decay of $x[n]$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Example 1 — Finite Support Sequence (Rectangular Pulse)

**Problem:** Find the support of the sequence $x[n] = 2$ for $n = 0, 1, 2$ and $x[n] = 0$ otherwise.

**Step 1 — Write the Sequence Explicitly:**

$$
x[n] = \{\ldots, 0, 0, \underbrace{2}_{n=0}, \underbrace{2}_{n=1}, \underbrace{2}_{n=2}, 0, 0, \ldots\}
$$

**Step 2 — Identify Non-Zero Indices:**
At $n = 0$: $x[0] = 2 \neq 0$ → Include in support.
At $n = 1$: $x[1] = 2 \neq 0$ → Include in support.
At $n = 2$: $x[2] = 2 \neq 0$ → Include in support.
At all other $n$: $x[n] = 0$ → Exclude from support.

**Step 3 — Construct the Support Set:**

$$
\text{Supp}\{x[n]\} = \{0, 1, 2\}
$$

**Step 4 — Determine Cardinality:**

$$
\vert \text{Supp}\{x[n]\} \vert = 3 < \infty
$$

**Step 5 — Classify the Sequence:**

Since the support is finite, the sequence has **finite support** of length $L = 3$. Because all non-zero samples occur at $n \geq 0$, the sequence is also **causal**.

**Step 6 — Energy Computation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n] \vert^{2} \\
  &= \vert x[0] \vert^{2} + \vert x[1] \vert^{2} + \vert x[2] \vert^{2} \\
  &= (2)^{2} + (2)^{2} + (2)^{2} \\
  &= 4 + 4 + 4 = 12 \text{ units}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.2 Example 2 — Infinite Support Sequence (Exponential Decay)

**Problem:** Find the support of the sequence $x[n] = (0.5)^{n} u[n]$, where $u[n]$ is the unit step.

**Step 1 — Substitute the Unit Step Definition:**

$$
u[n] = \begin{cases} 1, & n \geq 0 \\ 0, & n < 0 \end{cases}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Values for $n \geq 0$:**

$$
x[0] = (0.5)^{0} = 1
$$
$$
x[1] = (0.5)^{1} = 0.5
$$
$$
x[2] = (0.5)^{2} = 0.25
$$
$$
x[3] = (0.5)^{3} = 0.125
$$

In general, for $n \geq 0$:

$$
x[n] = (0.5)^{n} > 0 \quad \text{(always non-zero)}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Values for $n < 0$:**

$$
x[-1] = 0, \quad x[-2] = 0, \quad x[-3] = 0, \ldots
$$

**Step 4 — Construct the Support Set:**

$$
\text{Supp}\{x[n]\} = \{0, 1, 2, 3, 4, \ldots\} = \mathbb{Z}_{\geq 0} = [0, \infty) \cap \mathbb{Z}
$$

**Step 5 — Determine Cardinality:**

$$
\vert \text{Supp}\{x[n]\} \vert = \infty
$$

**Step 6 — Classify the Sequence:**

The sequence has **infinite support**, is **right-sided** and **causal**. Although the support is infinite, the energy converges because of geometric decay.

**Step 7 — Energy Computation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \vert (0.5)^{n} \vert^{2} = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} (0.25)^{n}
\end{aligned}
$$

Using the geometric series formula $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} r^{n} = \frac{1}{1-r}$ for $\vert r \vert < 1$:

$$
E = \frac{1}{1 - 0.25} = \frac{1}{0.75} = \frac{4}{3} \text{ units}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Infinite support does **not** necessarily mean infinite energy. The convergence depends on the **rate of decay** of the sequence. A geometric sequence with $\vert r \vert < 1$ has finite energy despite infinite support.

---

### 3.3 Example 3 — Two-Sided Infinite Support

**Problem:** Find the support of $x[n] = \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{\vert n \vert}$ for all $n \in \mathbb{Z}$.

**Step 1 — Compute Values for Symmetric Indices:**

$$
x[0] = (0.5)^{0} = 1
$$
$$
x[1] = (0.5)^{1} = 0.5, \quad x[-1] = (0.5)^{1} = 0.5
$$
$$
x[2] = (0.5)^{2} = 0.25, \quad x[-2] = (0.5)^{2} = 0.25
$$

**Step 2 — Observe the Pattern:**

For all $n \in \mathbb{Z}$:

$$
x[n] = (0.5)^{\vert n \vert} > 0
$$

**Step 3 — Construct the Support Set:**

$$
\text{Supp}\{x[n]\} = \mathbb{Z} = \{\ldots, -2, -1, 0, 1, 2, \ldots\}
$$

**Step 4 — Classify the Sequence:**

The sequence is **two-sided** and **non-causal** (since non-zero values exist for both $n < 0$ and $n \geq 0$). It is **even-symmetric**: $x[n] = x[-n]$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Classification Flowchart of Sequence Support

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **decision hierarchy** used to classify a discrete-time sequence based on the structure of its support set.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start: Given Sequence x of n] --> testA{Is the set of non-zero indices finite?}
    testA -- Yes --> finiteNode[Finite Support]
    testA -- No --> testB{Are non-zero values bounded on the left side?}
    finiteNode --> finClass{Which side does support lie on?}
    finClass -- Both sides --> twoSideFin[Two-Sided Finite Support]
    finClass -- Only n greater than or equal 0 --> causalFin[Causal Finite Support]
    finClass -- Only n less than 0 --> antiCausalFin[Anti-Causal Finite Support]
    testB -- Yes --> testC{Are non-zero values bounded on the right side?}
    testB -- No --> testC
    testC -- Yes --> twoSideInf[Two-Sided Infinite Support Example cosine]
    testC -- No --> rightInf[Right-Sided Infinite Support Example step]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The classification starts at `startA`, checks whether the support is finite, then progressively narrows down the type based on directional boundaries.

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Support Detection in a DSP Pipeline

In a real-world **Digital Signal Processor**, identifying the support of a sequence is a critical sub-task. The block diagram below shows the functional architecture of an automatic support-detection module.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inSeq[Input Sequence Buffer] --> compUnit[Comparator with Zero Threshold]
    compUnit --> flagReg[Boolean Flag Array indexed by n]
    flagReg --> cardCalc[Cardinality Calculator]
    flagReg --> minMax[Min and Max Index Finder]
    cardCalc --> classOut[Classification Output Finite or Infinite]
    minMax --> dirDetect[Direction Detector Left Right Two-Sided]
    dirDetect --> causalOut[Causality Flag]
    classOut --> memCtrl[Memory Controller Allocates Buffer]
    causalOut --> memCtrl
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Memory Controller** block is the engineering consumer of the support information. It dynamically allocates only as many memory slots as the support size demands, which is the core principle behind **sparse signal storage**.

### 4.3 Visual Stem Plot of Different Support Types

The following Mermaid diagram shows a **timeline representation** of where non-zero samples appear for each support category.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    leftNode[Left-Sided: n less than or equal N] ---|gap| midZero1[Zeros for n greater than N]
    rightNode[Right-Sided: n greater than or equal N] ---|gap| midZero2[Zeros for n less than N]
    twoNode[Two-Sided: All n in Z] ---|infinite both ways| midZero3[No Zeros in middle]
    finNode[Finite Support: n0 to nN] ---|finite band| midZero4[Zeros outside finite interval]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the support of a discrete-time sequence. How is a sequence classified based on the nature of its support?**

**Model Answer:**
The support of a discrete-time sequence $x[n]$ is the set of all integer indices $n$ for which the sequence has a non-zero value, expressed as:

$$
\text{Supp}\{x[n]\} = \{n \in \mathbb{Z} \mid x[n] \neq 0\}
$$

A sequence is classified based on the cardinality of its support as follows:
- **Finite Support:** The set $\text{Supp}\{x[n]\}$ contains only a finite number of indices. For example, $x[n] = \{1, 2, 3\}$ at $n = 0, 1, 2$ has support $\{0, 1, 2\}$.
- **Infinite Support:** The set $\text{Supp}\{x[n]\}$ contains infinitely many indices. For example, $x[n] = (0.5)^{n} u[n]$ has support $\{0, 1, 2, \ldots\}$.
- **Left-Sided, Right-Sided, Two-Sided:** Based on whether the support is bounded on the right, left, or neither, respectively.

---

**Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is meant by a causal sequence? How does the support of a sequence determine its causality?**

**Model Answer:**
A sequence $x[n]$ is called **causal** if and only if it has zero values for all negative indices, i.e.:

$$
x[n] = 0 \quad \text{for all } n < 0
$$

The support of a sequence is directly linked to causality as follows:

$$
\text{Causal} \iff \text{Supp}\{x[n]\} \subseteq \{0, 1, 2, 3, \ldots\} = \mathbb{Z}_{\geq 0}
$$

This means the support of a causal sequence must lie entirely within the non-negative integer indices. If any non-zero value exists at $n < 0$, the support extends into the negative region and the sequence becomes **non-causal**, which is unrealizable in real-time physical systems.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### **Question A (Choice 1)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]`

**(a)** Define the support of a discrete-time sequence. For the sequence $x[n] = 2\delta[n] + 3\delta[n-1] + \delta[n-3]$, determine the support, its cardinality, the length of support, and the total energy. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the sequence $x[n] = (0.8)^{n} u[n] - (0.5)^{n} u[-n-1]$, find the support set, classify the sequence based on its support, and determine whether the sequence is causal. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question A(a):**

**Definition:** The support of a sequence $x[n]$ is the set of all integer indices $n$ where $x[n] \neq 0$:

$$
\text{Supp}\{x[n]\} = \{n \in \mathbb{Z} \mid x[n] \neq 0\}
$$

**Step 1 — Evaluate the Sequence:**

$$
x[0] = 2 \cdot \delta[0] = 2 \cdot 1 = 2
$$
$$
x[1] = 3 \cdot \delta[0] = 3 \cdot 1 = 3
$$
$$
x[3] = 1 \cdot \delta[0] = 1 \cdot 1 = 1
$$

All other $x[n] = 0$.

**Step 2 — Construct the Support Set:**

$$
\text{Supp}\{x[n]\} = \{0, 1, 3\}
$$

**Step 3 — Cardinality:**

$$
\vert \text{Supp}\{x[n]\} \vert = 3
$$

**Step 4 — Length of Support:**

$$
L = \max(\text{Supp}) - \min(\text{Supp}) + 1 = 3 - 0 + 1 = 4
$$

**Step 5 — Total Energy:**

$$
E = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n] \vert^{2} = (2)^{2} + (3)^{2} + (1)^{2} = 4 + 9 + 1 = 14 \text{ units}
$$

---

**Model Solution for Question A(b):**

**Step 1 — Decompose the Sequence:**

$$
x[n] = \underbrace{(0.8)^{n} u[n]}_{x_{1}[n]} - \underbrace{(0.5)^{n} u[-n-1]}_{x_{2}[n]}
$$

**Step 2 — Analyze $x_{1}[n]$:**

- For $n \geq 0$: $x_{1}[n] = (0.8)^{n} \neq 0$.
- For $n < 0$: $x_{1}[n] = 0$.

So $x_{1}[n]$ has support $\text{Supp}\{x_{1}[n]\} = \{0, 1, 2, \ldots\} = \mathbb{Z}_{\geq 0}$.

**Step 3 — Analyze $x_{2}[n]$:**

- For $n \leq -1$: $x_{2}[n] = (0.5)^{n} \neq 0$.
- For $n \geq 0$: $x_{2}[n] = 0$.

So $x_{2}[n]$ has support $\text{Supp}\{x_{2}[n]\} = \{\ldots, -3, -2, -1\} = \mathbb{Z}_{< 0}$.

**Step 4 — Combine Supports:**

Since $x[n] = x_{1}[n] - x_{2}[n]$ is a sum, the total support is the union:

$$
\text{Supp}\{x[n]\} = \mathbb{Z}_{\geq 0} \cup \mathbb{Z}_{< 0} = \mathbb{Z}
$$

**Step 5 — Classification:**

The support is the **entire integer set $\mathbb{Z}$**, so the sequence is **two-sided** and has **infinite support**.

**Step 6 — Causality Check:**

Since $\text{Supp}\{x[n]\}$ contains negative indices (from $x_{2}[n]$), the sequence is **non-causal**.

---

#### **Question B (Choice 2)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Modified]`

**(a)** For the sequence $x[n] = n \cdot (0.5)^{\vert n \vert}$ for all $n \in \mathbb{Z}$, determine the support set, classify the sequence as left-sided, right-sided, or two-sided, and explain whether it is causal. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A finite-duration sequence is defined as $x[n] = 5$ for $n = -2, -1, 0, 1$ and $x[n] = 0$ otherwise. Find its support, energy, and verify whether it is an energy signal or a power signal. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question B(a):**

**Step 1 — Evaluate for Various $n$:**

$$
x[0] = 0 \cdot (0.5)^{0} = 0
$$
$$
x[1] = 1 \cdot (0.5)^{1} = 0.5
$$
$$
x[-1] = -1 \cdot (0.5)^{1} = -0.5
$$
$$
x[2] = 2 \cdot (0.5)^{2} = 0.5
$$
$$
x[-2] = -2 \cdot (0.5)^{2} = -0.5
$$

**Step 2 — Identify Non-Zero Values:**

At $n = 0$: $x[0] = 0$ → **Excluded** from support.
For all $n \neq 0$: $x[n] = n \cdot (0.5)^{\vert n \vert} \neq 0$ → **Included** in support.

**Step 3 — Construct Support Set:**

$$
\text{Supp}\{x[n]\} = \mathbb{Z} \setminus \{0\}
$$

**Step 4 — Classification:**

The support extends infinitely in both directions, so the sequence is **two-sided** with **infinite support**.

**Step 5 — Causality:**

The support includes negative indices, so the sequence is **non-causal**.

---

**Model Solution for Question B(b):**

**Step 1 — Identify Non-Zero Indices:**

$x[-2] = 5$, $x[-1] = 5$, $x[0] = 5$, $x[1] = 5$ are non-zero.

**Step 2 — Support Set:**

$$
\text{Supp}\{x[n]\} = \{-2, -1, 0, 1\}
$$

**Step 3 — Energy Calculation:**

$$
E = \sum_{n=-2}^{1} (5)^{2} = 25 + 25 + 25 + 25 = 100 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 4 — Power Calculation:**

For a finite-energy sequence, the average power is:

$$
P = \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{1}{2N+1} \sum_{n=-N}^{N} \vert x[n] \vert^{2} = \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{100}{2N+1} = 0
$$

**Step 5 — Classification:**

Since $0 < E < \infty$ and $P = 0$, the sequence is an **Energy Signal** (and **not** a power signal).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> 1. **Confusing support length with cardinality:** Students often write the support length as $N - M$ instead of $N - M + 1$. The $+1$ is mandatory because the count is **inclusive** of both endpoints.
> 2. **Forgetting $n = 0$ in causal sequences:** A causal sequence is one with $x[n] = 0$ for $n < 0$. The index $n = 0$ is **included** in the support of a causal sequence. Markers often deduct marks for stating "causal means support is only for $n > 0$".
> 3. **Claiming "infinite support implies infinite energy":** This is **false**. A decaying exponential like $(0.5)^{n} u[n]$ has infinite support but finite energy $E = 4/3$. Always compute $E$ explicitly.
> 4. **Mistaking $x[0] = 0$ for "support excludes $n = 0$":** If a sequence happens to be zero at a specific index (e.g., $n = 0$), that index is not part of the support, but the rest of the sequence is unaffected. Do not generalize this to mean "the sequence is anti-causal".
> 5. **Using pipes $\vert x \vert$ inside markdown tables:** This breaks the table syntax. Always use $\vert x[n] \vert$ with explicit LaTeX delimiters or $\lvert \cdot \rvert$ for clean rendering.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Support Definition:** $\text{Supp}\{x[n]\} = \{n \in \mathbb{Z} \mid x[n] \neq 0\}$.
- **Finite Support:** The set of non-zero indices is finite. Such sequences always have **finite energy** and are **always energy signals**.
- **Infinite Support:** Non-zero indices form an infinite set. Energy may or may not be finite depending on decay.
- **Causal Sequence:** Support is a subset of $\{0, 1, 2, \ldots\}$. No non-zero value at $n < 0$.
- **Anti-Causal Sequence:** Support is a subset of $\{\ldots, -2, -1, 0\}$. Non-zero values only at $n \leq 0$.
- **Two-Sided Sequence:** Support is unbounded on both sides. Sequence is **non-causal**.
- **Length Formula:** For finite support spanning $[M, N]$, the length is $L = N - M + 1$.
- **Energy Formula:** $E = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n] \vert^{2}$ — finite for finite-support or fast-decaying sequences.
- **Power Formula:** $P = \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{1}{2N+1} \sum_{n=-N}^{N} \vert x[n] \vert^{2}$.
- **Causality vs. Support:** Causality is **purely a support property** — it depends only on whether indices $n < 0$ are excluded.
- **Impulse Sequences:** $\delta[n - k]$ has support exactly at the singleton $\{k\}$.
- **Unit Step $u[n]$:** Support is $\{0, 1, 2, \ldots\}$ — causal and right-sided.
- **Sinc and Sinusoidal Sequences:** Have infinite support on all of $\mathbb{Z}$ — two-sided and non-causal.
- **Engineering Connection:** Finite support $\leftrightarrow$ FIR systems; Infinite support $\leftrightarrow$ IIR systems.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bounded Sequences. Operations on Signals: Time Shifting (Translation)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST416 - SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Bounded Sequences. Operations on Signals: Time Shifting (Translation)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Bounded Sequences

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> A discrete-time signal $x(n)$ is said to be **bounded** if there exists a finite positive real number $M$ such that the magnitude of the signal never exceeds $M$ for any value of the discrete-time index $n$. Mathematically:
> $$\vert x(n) \vert \leq M < \infty \quad \text{for all } n \in \mathbb{Z}$$

The bound $M$ is a **finite** number. If such a finite $M$ exists, the sequence is bounded. Otherwise, it is **unbounded**.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy: The "Ceiling" of a Signal**
> Imagine a balloon floating inside a glass room. No matter how it moves or wiggles, the balloon **never breaks the glass ceiling**. The height of the glass ceiling is the "bound" $M$.
