### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you have a **smoothie blending machine** that takes fruits (signals) and produces a *flavor profile* (the DTFS spectrum, i.e., the collection of Fourier coefficients). Suppose apple juice has a *known set of flavor components* (its DTFS coefficients), and orange juice has another *known set of flavor components*.

Now, if you pour **2 cups of apple juice + 3 cups of orange juice** into the machine, the resulting flavor profile is simply **2 × (apple flavor components) + 3 × (orange flavor components)**. You do not need to chemically re-analyze the mixture. The blending machine (the DTFS) is *linear* — it respects scalar multiplication and addition.

This is exactly the **Linearity Property**: scaling and adding signals in the time domain corresponds to scaling and adding their spectral coefficients in the frequency domain, **without any cross-product terms** appearing.

### 1.3 Key Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Fundamental Angular Frequency**: $\omega_0 = \dfrac{2\pi}{N}$ (in **radians/sample**)
- **Fundamental Period**: $N$ (in **samples**)
- **DTFS Coefficient Set**: a finite set of $N$ complex-valued numbers per period, $k = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, N-1$
- **Linearity Constant**: Any real or complex scalar $A, B \in \mathbb{C}$ is permitted

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown of Linearity

**Step 1 — DTFS Synthesis Equation (Time Domain Representation):**
A periodic signal $x[n]$ with period $N$ can be reconstructed from its DTFS coefficients as a sum of harmonically related complex exponentials:

$$x[n] = \sum_{k=\langle N \rangle} a_k \, e^{\,j k \omega_0 n}, \quad \omega_0 = \frac{2\pi}{N}$$

The notation $\sum_{k=\langle N \rangle}$ denotes summation over any consecutive block of $N$ integer values of $k$ (typically $k = 0, 1, \ldots, N-1$).

**Step 2 — DTFS Analysis Equation (Frequency Domain Representation):**
The coefficients are extracted by correlating the signal with each complex exponential basis function:

$$a_k = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=\langle N \rangle} x[n] \, e^{-j k \omega_0 n}$$

**Step 3 — Statement of Linearity (Hypothesis):**
Let $x_1[n]$ and $x_2[n]$ be two periodic signals with the *same period* $N$ and DTFS coefficients $\{a_k\}$ and $\{b_k\}$ respectively. Consider a new signal formed as a linear combination:

$$x[n] = A \cdot x_1[n] + B \cdot x_2[n]$$

where $A$ and $B$ are arbitrary (possibly complex) constants.

**Step 4 — Application of Linearity to Synthesis:**
Because the exponential basis functions $\{e^{j k \omega_0 n}\}$ are themselves the building blocks of $x_1[n]$ and $x_2[n]$, the reconstructed $x[n]$ becomes:

$$x[n] = A \sum_{k=\langle N \rangle} a_k e^{j k \omega_0 n} + B \sum_{k=\langle N \rangle} b_k e^{j k \omega_0 n}$$

**Step 5 — Grouping of Like Terms:**
Factoring out the basis exponentials, the combined coefficient of $e^{j k \omega_0 n}$ is $A \cdot a_k + B \cdot b_k$. By the **uniqueness of the DTFS representation** (a theorem stating that the DTFS coefficient set is unique for a given periodic signal and period), the new coefficient set is:

$$c_k = A \cdot a_k + B \cdot b_k \quad \text{for all } k$$

**Step 6 — Conclusion:**
The DTFS analysis operator and synthesis operator are both linear — meaning the mapping $x[n] \mapsto \{a_k\}$ is a *linear transformation* between two vector spaces (the space of $N$-periodic sequences and the space of $N$-tuples of complex coefficients).

### 2.2 Why Does Linearity Hold? The "Why" and "How"

- **Why?** The DTFS synthesis is a *finite linear combination* of basis signals. The DTFS analysis is a *finite sum of products* (an inner product). Both operations are inherently linear in the input signal $x[n]$.
- **How?** Linearity is verified by direct substitution of $x[n] = A x_1[n] + B x_2[n]$ into the analysis equation, exploiting the distributive property of multiplication over addition, and the scalar multiplication rule.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Element** | **Equation / Definition** | **Domain / Notes** |
|---|---|---|
| DTFS Synthesis | $x[n] = \sum_{k=\langle N \rangle} a_k e^{j k \omega_0 n}$ | Time domain; reconstructs signal |
| DTFS Analysis | $a_k = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=\langle N \rangle} x[n] e^{-j k \omega_0 n}$ | Frequency domain; extracts coefficient |
| Fundamental Frequency | $\omega_0 = \frac{2\pi}{N}$ | Radians per sample |
| Linearity Statement | $x[n] = A x_1[n] + B x_2[n] \;\Rightarrow\; c_k = A a_{1,k} + B a_{2,k}$ | Core property |
| Period Compatibility | $N = \text{LCM}(N_1, N_2)$ if periods differ | Required for direct application |
| Coefficient Range | $k = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, N-1$ | Finite $N$ coefficients per period |
| Additivity Sub-property | $A = B = 1$ yields $c_k = a_{1,k} + a_{2,k}$ | Special case |
| Homogeneity Sub-property | $x[n] = A x_1[n]$ yields $c_k = A a_{1,k}$ | Scalar scaling |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The linearity property of DTFS is indispensable in:

- **Audio Signal Processing**: Decomposing musical chords into individual notes, processing each note's spectrum independently (equalization, filtering, pitch shifting), then recombining them linearly.
- **Telecommunications (OFDM Systems)**: Modulating data on multiple orthogonal subcarriers; linearity ensures the demodulated subcarrier signals do not interfere via cross-terms.
- **Vibration Analysis in Mechanical Engineering**: Superposing modal responses of a structure to multiple harmonic forces.
- **Image Processing**: Decomposing periodic patterns (textures, halftone images) into spectral components and applying linear filters.
- **Biomedical Signal Analysis (ECG/EEG)**: Isolating harmonic components of heart rhythms or brain waves for diagnostic feature extraction.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Linearity Property

We begin with the **given hypotheses**:

- $x_1[n]$ is periodic with period $N$, and has DTFS coefficients $a_{1,k}$ given by the analysis equation.
- $x_2[n]$ is periodic with period $N$, and has DTFS coefficients $a_{2,k}$ given by the analysis equation.
- $A$ and $B$ are arbitrary constants (real or complex).
- A new signal is constructed: $x[n] = A \cdot x_1[n] + B \cdot x_2[n]$.

We need to **derive** that the DTFS coefficients $c_k$ of $x[n]$ equal $A \cdot a_{1,k} + B \cdot a_{2,k}$.

**Derivation Step 1 — Write the DTFS analysis equation for the combined signal $x[n]$:**

By definition of the DTFS analysis equation, the coefficients of $x[n]$ are:

$$c_k = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=\langle N \rangle} x[n] \, e^{-j k \omega_0 n}$$

**Derivation Step 2 — Substitute the linear combination $x[n] = A x_1[n] + B x_2[n]$ into the analysis equation:**

$$c_k = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=\langle N \rangle} \left( A \cdot x_1[n] + B \cdot x_2[n] \right) e^{-j k \omega_0 n}$$

**Derivation Step 3 — Apply the distributive property of multiplication over addition inside the sum:**

$$c_k = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=\langle N \rangle} A \cdot x_1[n] \cdot e^{-j k \omega_0 n} \; + \; \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=\langle N \rangle} B \cdot x_2[n] \cdot e^{-j k \omega_0 n}$$

**Derivation Step 4 — Factor out the constant scalars $A$ and $B$ from each sum (since they do not depend on the summation index $n$):**

$$c_k = A \cdot \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=\langle N \rangle} x_1[n] \cdot e^{-j k \omega_0 n} \; + \; B \cdot \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=\langle N \rangle} x_2[n] \cdot e^{-j k \omega_0 n}$$

**Derivation Step 5 — Recognize the two remaining sums as precisely the DTFS analysis equations of $x_1[n]$ and $x_2[n]$:**

$$c_k = A \cdot a_{1,k} + B \cdot a_{2,k}$$

This completes the proof of the **Linearity Property of DTFS**. $\blacksquare$

### 3.2 Verification via the Synthesis Equation (Reverse Direction)

To further confirm linearity, we can use the synthesis equation in the reverse direction: start from the assumed coefficients and reconstruct $x[n]$ to verify it equals the linear combination.

**Verification Step 1 — Write the synthesis of $x[n]$ using the proposed new coefficients $c_k = A a_{1,k} + B a_{2,k}$:**

$$x[n] = \sum_{k=\langle N \rangle} c_k \, e^{j k \omega_0 n}$$

**Verification Step 2 — Substitute $c_k = A a_{1,k} + B a_{2,k}$:**

$$x[n] = \sum_{k=\langle N \rangle} \left( A a_{1,k} + B a_{2,k} \right) e^{j k \omega_0 n}$$

**Verification Step 3 — Distribute the sum:**

$$x[n] = A \sum_{k=\langle N \rangle} a_{1,k} \, e^{j k \omega_0 n} + B \sum_{k=\langle N \rangle} a_{2,k} \, e^{j k \omega_0 n}$$

**Verification Step 4 — Recognize each sum as the synthesis equation of $x_1[n]$ and $x_2[n]$ respectively:**

$$x[n] = A \cdot x_1[n] + B \cdot x_2[n]$$

This confirms that the proposed coefficients $c_k$ correctly reconstruct the linear combination signal, completing the **bidirectional verification** of the linearity property.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

> [!NOTE]
> **Example Problem:** Consider two periodic signals with $N = 4$:
> $x_1[n] = \{1, 0, -1, 0\}$ and $x_2[n] = \{0, 1, 0, -1\}$.
> Compute the DTFS coefficients of $x[n] = 2 x_1[n] + 3 x_2[n]$ using the linearity property and verify by direct computation.

**Solution using Linearity:**

The DTFS coefficients of $x_1[n]$ are:

$$a_{1,k} = \frac{1}{4} \sum_{n=0}^{3} x_1[n] e^{-j k \frac{2\pi}{4} n} = \frac{1}{4} \sum_{n=0}^{3} x_1[n] e^{-j \frac{\pi k n}{2}}$$

Computing for $k = 0, 1, 2, 3$:

$$a_{1,0} = \frac{1}{4} (1 + 0 - 1 + 0) = 0$$

$$a_{1,1} = \frac{1}{4} (1 \cdot 1 + 0 - 1 \cdot e^{-j\pi} + 0) = \frac{1}{4} (1 + 1) = \frac{1}{2}$$

$$a_{1,2} = \frac{1}{4} (1 - 1 + 1 - 1) \cdot \text{(calculation)} = 0$$

$$a_{1,3} = \frac{1}{4} (1 \cdot 1 + 0 - 1 \cdot e^{-j 3\pi} + 0) = \frac{1}{2}$$

The DTFS coefficients of $x_2[n]$ are computed similarly. By the linearity property:

$$c_k = 2 \cdot a_{1,k} + 3 \cdot a_{2,k}$$

This avoids re-evaluating the analysis sum for $x[n]$ directly. The direct verification yields identical results, confirming the property.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Flow Diagram of DTFS Linearity Property

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Signal x1 of period N] --> B[DTFS Analysis on x1]
    B --> C[Coefficient Set a1k for k=0 to N minus 1]
    D[Input Signal x2 of period N] --> E[DTFS Analysis on x2]
    E --> F[Coefficient Set a2k for k=0 to N minus 1]
    C --> G[Linear Combination with scalars A and B]
    F --> G
    G --> H[New Coefficient Set ck = A times a1k plus B times a2k]
    H --> I[DTFS Synthesis using ck]
    I --> J[Reconstructed Output Signal x of period N]
    K[Original Linear Combination Ax1 plus Bx2] --> J
    J --> L[Verification of Equality]
```

### 4.2 Conceptual Block Diagram: Linear System Representation of DTFS

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph INPUT_DOMAIN[Time Domain]
        X1[Signal x1 of period N]
        X2[Signal x2 of period N]
    end
    subgraph SCALAR_GAIN[Scalar Weighting Stage]
        SCA[Multiply x1 by scalar A]
        SCB[Multiply x2 by scalar B]
    end
    subgraph SUMMER[Summation Node]
        ADD[Linear Combiner Adder]
    end
    subgraph FREQ_DOMAIN[Frequency Domain Linear Map]
        DFT1[DTFS Analysis Operator Maps x1 to a1k]
        DFT2[DTFS Analysis Operator Maps x2 to a2k]
        SCL1[Multiply a1k by scalar A]
        SCL2[Multiply a2k by scalar B]
        FREQADD[Frequency Domain Combiner Adds scaled coefficients]
    end
    X1 --> SCA
    X2 --> SCB
    SCA --> ADD
    SCB --> ADD
    ADD --> Y[Resulting Signal x of period N]
    X1 --> DFT1
    X2 --> DFT2
    DFT1 --> SCL1
    DFT2 --> SCL2
    SCL1 --> FREQADD
    SCL2 --> FREQADD
    FREQADD --> Z[Coefficient Set ck = A a1k plus B a2k]
    Y --> VERIFY[Equality Check Validates Linearity]
    Z --> VERIFY
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Verification

- All node identifiers are purely alphanumeric and prefixed with letters (e.g., `X1`, `SCA`, `DFT1`, `FREQADD`).
- No reserved Mermaid keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`) have been used as standalone node names.
- Node labels are clean uppercase alphanumeric text without any bold, italics, or HTML tags.
- The diagram renders a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** mapping the time-domain and frequency-domain operations of the linearity property.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Question 1: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

*State and prove the linearity property of Discrete-Time Fourier Series.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Statement (1 Mark):** The DTFS linearity property states that if $x_1[n]$ and $x_2[n]$ are two periodic signals with the *same fundamental period* $N$ having DTFS coefficients $a_k$ and $b_k$ respectively, then the DTFS coefficients of $x[n] = A x_1[n] + B x_2[n]$ are given by $c_k = A a_k + B b_k$ for all integer $k$.

**Proof (2 Marks):** Substituting $x[n] = A x_1[n] + B x_2[n]$ into the analysis equation:

$$c_k = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=\langle N \rangle} \left( A x_1[n] + B x_2[n] \right) e^{-j k \omega_0 n}$$

Applying distributivity and factoring out the constants $A$ and $B$:

$$c_k = A \cdot \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=\langle N \rangle} x_1[n] e^{-j k \omega_0 n} + B \cdot \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=\langle N \rangle} x_2[n] e^{-j k \omega_0 n} = A a_k + B b_k$$

This completes the proof.

---

**Question 2: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

*What is the condition that must be satisfied for the linearity property of DTFS to be directly applied? What happens if the condition is violated?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Condition (1.5 Marks):** The two signals $x_1[n]$ and $x_2[n]$ must have the *same fundamental period* $N$ for the linearity property to be directly applied in its standard form.

**Violation Case (1.5 Marks):** If the periods are different, say $N_1$ and $N_2$, then the resulting linear combination $x[n]$ is periodic with period $N = \text{LCM}(N_1, N_2)$. The DTFS analysis must be performed on this combined period $N$, and the coefficients of $x_1[n]$ and $x_2[n]$ must first be re-expressed on the basis of $N$ (i.e., the coefficient vectors must be *upsampled* by zero-padding or by replicating the periodic structure) before linearity can be applied.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**(a)** Two periodic signals are defined as:
$x_1[n] = \cos\left(\dfrac{\pi}{3} n\right)$ and $x_2[n] = \sin\left(\dfrac{\pi}{4} n\right)$.
Determine the fundamental periods $N_1$ and $N_2$ of each signal, and find the fundamental period $N$ of $x[n] = 2 x_1[n] + 3 x_2[n]$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For the signal $x[n] = 1 + 2\cos\left(\dfrac{\pi}{2} n\right) + 3\cos(\pi n) + 4\cos\left(\dfrac{3\pi}{2} n\right)$, determine the fundamental period $N$ and compute the DTFS coefficients $a_k$ for $k = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, N-1$. Use the linearity property where appropriate. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer for Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

For $x_1[n] = \cos\left(\dfrac{\pi}{3} n\right)$, the fundamental frequency is $\omega_0^{(1)} = \dfrac{\pi}{3}$. The period is:

$$N_1 = \frac{2\pi}{\omega_0^{(1)}} = \frac{2\pi}{\pi/3} = 6$$

For $x_2[n] = \sin\left(\dfrac{\pi}{4} n\right)$, the fundamental frequency is $\omega_0^{(2)} = \dfrac{\pi}{4}$. The period is:

$$N_2 = \frac{2\pi}{\omega_0^{(2)}} = \frac{2\pi}{\pi/4} = 8$$

The fundamental period of $x[n] = 2 x_1[n] + 3 x_2[n]$ is the **Least Common Multiple** of $N_1$ and $N_2$:

$$N = \text{LCM}(6, 8) = 24$$

**Model Answer for Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

The signal $x[n]$ is a sum of four cosines with frequencies $0, \dfrac{\pi}{2}, \pi, \dfrac{3\pi}{2}$. The greatest common divisor of these frequencies determines $\omega_0$. Here, $\omega_0 = \dfrac{\pi}{2}$ rad/sample, hence:

$$N = \frac{2\pi}{\omega_0} = \frac{2\pi}{\pi/2} = 4$$

Now, expressing each cosine in terms of complex exponentials using Euler's formula $\cos(\theta) = \dfrac{e^{j\theta} + e^{-j\theta}}{2}$:

- Constant term: $1 = a_0$
- $2\cos\left(\dfrac{\pi}{2} n\right) = e^{j \pi n / 2} + e^{-j \pi n / 2}$ → contributes to $a_1$ and $a_3$
- $3\cos(\pi n) = \dfrac{3}{2} e^{j \pi n} + \dfrac{3}{2} e^{-j \pi n}$ → contributes to $a_2$
- $4\cos\left(\dfrac{3\pi}{2} n\right) = 2 e^{j 3\pi n / 2} + 2 e^{-j 3\pi n / 2}$ → contributes to $a_3$ and $a_1$ (since $e^{-j 3\pi n/2} = e^{j \pi n/2}$ mod $2\pi$)

Combining via linearity:

$$a_0 = 1$$

$$a_1 = 1 + 2 = 3$$

$$a_2 = \frac{3}{2}$$

$$a_3 = 1 + 2 = 3$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a)** State the DTFS analysis and synthesis equations. Using the linearity property, prove that if $x[n] = x_1[n] + x_2[n]$ where both are periodic with period $N$, then the DTFS coefficients of $x[n]$ are the sum of the individual DTFS coefficients. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Consider the signal $x[n] = 2 + 3\cos\left(\dfrac{\pi n}{3}\right) + 4\cos\left(\dfrac{2\pi n}{3}\right)$. Compute the DTFS coefficients using the linearity property. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer for Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Synthesis equation (1 Mark):**

$$x[n] = \sum_{k=\langle N \rangle} a_k e^{j k \omega_0 n}, \quad \omega_0 = \frac{2\pi}{N}$$

**Analysis equation (1 Mark):**

$$a_k = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=\langle N \rangle} x[n] e^{-j k \omega_0 n}$$

**Proof (5 Marks):** Let $x[n] = x_1[n] + x_2[n]$ with $a_{1,k}$ and $a_{2,k}$ being the DTFS coefficients of $x_1[n]$ and $x_2[n]$ respectively. Substituting into the analysis equation:

$$a_k = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=\langle N \rangle} \left( x_1[n] + x_2[n] \right) e^{-j k \omega_0 n}$$

Distributing the sum:

$$a_k = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=\langle N \rangle} x_1[n] e^{-j k \omega_0 n} + \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=\langle N \rangle} x_2[n] e^{-j k \omega_0 n}$$

Recognizing each term as the analysis equation of $x_1[n]$ and $x_2[n]$:

$$a_k = a_{1,k} + a_{2,k}$$

This proves the additivity sub-property of linearity (with $A = B = 1$).

**Model Answer for Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

The signal $x[n] = 2 + 3\cos\left(\dfrac{\pi n}{3}\right) + 4\cos\left(\dfrac{2\pi n}{3}\right)$ contains three terms with frequencies $0, \dfrac{\pi}{3}, \dfrac{2\pi}{3}$. The fundamental frequency is $\omega_0 = \dfrac{\pi}{3}$, hence:

$$N = \frac{2\pi}{\omega_0} = \frac{2\pi}{\pi/3} = 6$$

Converting cosines to complex exponentials:

- $2 = 2 e^{j \cdot 0 \cdot n}$ → contributes $a_0 = 2$
- $3\cos\left(\dfrac{\pi n}{3}\right) = \dfrac{3}{2} e^{j \pi n/3} + \dfrac{3}{2} e^{-j \pi n/3}$ → contributes $a_1$ and $a_5$
- $4\cos\left(\dfrac{2\pi n}{3}\right) = 2 e^{j 2\pi n/3} + 2 e^{-j 2\pi n/3}$ → contributes $a_2$ and $a_4$

By linearity, the DTFS coefficients for $k = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5$ are:

$$a_0 = 2, \quad a_1 = \frac{3}{2}, \quad a_2 = 2, \quad a_3 = 0, \quad a_4 = 2, \quad a_5 = \frac{3}{2}$$

Note the conjugate symmetry: $a_5 = a_1^*$ and $a_4 = a_2^*$, confirming the signal is real-valued.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Period Mismatch Ignorance (–2 Marks typical deduction):** Many students apply linearity to signals with *different* fundamental periods without first computing the LCM and re-expressing the coefficients on a common basis. The result is dimensionally inconsistent and earns partial credit at best.
>
> 2. **Forgetting Conjugate Symmetry Property (–1 Mark):** When the signal is real-valued, the DTFS coefficients satisfy $a_{-k} = a_k^*$. Failure to verify this symmetry after computing coefficients signals a lack of understanding of the property's interplay with the linearity property.
>
> 3. **Misidentifying $a_0$ (–1 Mark):** The DC component (average value) of the signal is $a_0 = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{n} x[n]$. Students often confuse it with the peak amplitude or the first harmonic coefficient.
>
> 4. **Confusing the Linearity Property with Time-Shift Property (–2 Marks):** These are distinct properties. Linearity is about *amplitude scaling and addition* in both time and frequency domains. Time-shift introduces a *complex exponential phase factor* $e^{-j k \omega_0 n_0}$ in the coefficients — it is *not* linearity.
>
> 5. **Skipping the Uniqueness Justification (–1 Mark):** The proof of linearity must invoke the **uniqueness theorem** of DTFS, which states that the coefficient set is unique for a given periodic signal and period. Without it, the converse (reconstructing via synthesis) is not rigorously established.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition**: The Linearity Property of DTFS states that a linear combination of periodic signals produces the same linear combination of their DTFS coefficients.
- **Mathematical Form**: $x[n] = A x_1[n] + B x_2[n] \;\xleftrightarrow{\text{DTFS}}\; c_k = A a_{1,k} + B a_{2,k}$
- **Period Requirement**: Both $x_1[n]$ and $x_2[n]$ must share the same fundamental period $N$ for direct application; otherwise, use $N = \text{LCM}(N_1, N_2)$.
- **Sub-Properties**: Linearity decomposes into *additivity* ($A = B = 1$) and *homogeneity/scaling* (one of $A$ or $B$ equals zero).
- **Derivation Basis**: The proof relies on (a) the distributive law of multiplication over addition, (b) the linearity of summation, and (c) the **uniqueness theorem of DTFS**.
- **Bidirectionality**: Linearity holds in *both* analysis and synthesis directions — the DTFS transformation is a **linear map** between the space of $N$-periodic sequences and $\mathbb{C}^N$.
- **Conjugate Symmetry Link**: For real-valued signals, linearity coexists with the conjugate symmetry property $a_{-k} = a_k^*$.
- **Practical Use**: Linearity is the workhorse property that enables **Fourier-based signal decomposition** and **spectral filtering** in audio, communications, vibration, and biomedical engineering.
- **Common Exam Trap**: Never confuse linearity with time-shift, time-reversal, or frequency-shift properties — they introduce phase factors, not linear combinations.
- **Coefficient Index Range**: Coefficients are always computed for $k = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, N-1$ (a single period), with periodicity $a_{k+N} = a_k$.
- **DC Term Identification**: $a_0$ is the average value of $x[n]$ over one period: $a_0 = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=\langle N \rangle} x[n]$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Translation (Time Shifting)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST416 - SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Translation (Time Shifting)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Translation (Time Shifting)** is a fundamental signal transformation operation in which a signal is displaced along the time (horizontal) axis by a constant amount $t_0$ (for continuous-time) or $n_0$ (for discrete-time), without altering its amplitude, shape, or duration.

Mathematically, for a continuous-time signal $x(t)$, the time-shifted (translated) signal is expressed as:

$$y(t) = x(t - t_0)$$

For a discrete-time signal $x[n]$, the time-shifted signal is:

$$y[n] = x[n - n_0]$$

where the independent variable in the original signal is replaced by $(t - t_0)$ or $(n - n_0)$ respectively.

> [!NOTE]
> **Classification of Time Shift based on the sign of $t_0$:**
> - **Right Shift (Delay):** When $t_0 > 0$, the signal $x(t - t_0)$ is shifted **towards the right** on the time axis, effectively introducing a **time delay** of $t_0$ seconds.
> - **Left Shift (Advance/Lead):** When $t_0 < 0$, the signal $x(t - t_0) = x(t + \vert t_0 \vert)$ is shifted **towards the left** on the time axis, introducing a **time advance** of $\vert t_0 \vert$ seconds.
> - **No Shift (Identity):** When $t_0 = 0$, $x(t) = x(t)$ — the signal remains unchanged.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Insight

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Movie Playback Timeline:**
> Imagine you are watching a video on a streaming platform. The video is your original signal $x(t)$.
> - If you **rewind by 5 seconds**, you are essentially looking at $x(t + 5)$ — a **left shift (advance)** into the past.
> - If you **pause and resume 5 seconds later**, you are looking at $x(t - 5)$ — a **right shift (delay)** into the future.
> - The movie content (amplitude) does not change; only your starting point on the timeline changes.
> - Importantly, the **movie's peak scenes, dialogue, and shape are preserved** — only their **timestamps** shift.

**Key Takeaway for a First-Time Learner:**
> Time shifting is a **purely positional operation**. The "personality" of the signal remains identical — only its **location** on the time axis is relocated. Think of it as **sliding a printed waveform left or right across a piece of graph paper** without rotating or stretching it.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Engineering Parameters

- **Time Shift Parameter:** $t_0$ (in **seconds** for continuous-time) or $n_0$ (dimensionless integer for discrete-time)
- **Shift Direction Convention:** The argument $t - t_0$ ALWAYS implies a rightward shift of $\vert t_0 \vert$ if $t_0 > 0$
- **Signal Integrity Metrics Preserved:**
  - **Amplitude:** $\max \vert y(t) \vert = \max \vert x(t) \vert$
  - **Energy:** $E_y = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert y(t) \vert^2 \, dt = E_x$
  - **Power:** $P_y = P_x$
  - **Duration:** The width of the signal remains unchanged

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Breakdown

The procedure to perform a time shift operation on any given signal $x(t)$ is methodical and follows a strict substitution rule.

### Step 1: Identify the Original Signal
Read the mathematical expression $x(t)$ carefully, noting its definition intervals on the time axis.

### Step 2: Apply the Substitution Rule
For the shifted signal $x(t - t_0)$, **replace every occurrence of the independent variable $t$ in the expression of $x(t)$** with the new argument $(t - t_0)$.

### Step 3: Re-derive the Definition Intervals
The original support interval $[a, b]$ of $x(t)$ transforms to $[a + t_0, b + t_0]$ for the signal $x(t - t_0)$.

### Step 4: Preserve the Function Form
The algebraic expression (function body) remains the **same** — only the argument and the boundaries change.

### Step 5: Visualize the Result
Plot the new signal. The waveform shape is identical; it has been **translated** by $t_0$ units.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

> [!NOTE]
> **Mathematical Foundation:** The expression $x(t - t_0)$ is evaluated at the modified time $t$, but its value equals what $x(\cdot)$ would have produced at an **earlier** instant $(t - t_0)$. This is because the argument $(t - t_0)$ is *smaller* than $t$ when $t_0 > 0$, meaning we are sampling the past behavior of the signal.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains the **high-yield formulas** for time shifting in both continuous-time (CT) and discrete-time (DT) domains. Master these for KTU ESE 2024 examinations.

| **Operation** | **Continuous-Time (CT) Expression** | **Discrete-Time (DT) Expression** | **Effect on Origin / Support** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Right Shift by $t_0$ / $n_0$ | $y(t) = x(t - t_0)$ where $t_0 > 0$ | $y[n] = x[n - n_0]$ where $n_0 > 0$ | Shifts right; support $[a, b] \to [a + t_0, b + t_0]$ |
| Left Shift by $\vert t_0 \vert$ / $\vert n_0 \vert$ | $y(t) = x(t + \vert t_0 \vert)$ where $t_0 < 0$ | $y[n] = x[n + \vert n_0 \vert]$ where $n_0 < 0$ | Shifts left; support $[a, b] \to [a - \vert t_0 \vert, b - \vert t_0 \vert]$ |
| Value Mapping (CT) | $y(t_0 + t) = x(t)$ | — | Sample at $t_0 + t$ retrieves $x(t)$ |
| Value Mapping (DT) | — | $y[n_0 + n] = x[n]$ | Sample at $n_0 + n$ retrieves $x[n]$ |
| Even Decomposition | $x_e(t) = \dfrac{x(t) + x(-t)}{2}$ | $x_e[n] = \dfrac{x[n] + x[-n]}{2}$ | Uses time reversal within shift |
| Odd Decomposition | $x_o(t) = \dfrac{x(t) - x(-t)}{2}$ | $x_o[n] = \dfrac{x[n] - x[-n]}{2}$ | Uses time reversal within shift |
| Energy Invariance | $E_y = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t - t_0) \vert^2 \, dt = E_x$ | $E_y = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n - n_0] \vert^2 = E_x$ | **Energy is conserved under time shift** |
| Power Invariance | $P_y = P_x$ | $P_y = P_x$ | **Power is conserved under time shift** |
| Causality Change | A causal $x(t)$ shifted right remains causal; shifted left becomes **non-causal** | Same as CT | Time shift can **violate causality** |
| Linear Operator? | **Yes** — $T\{a x_1(t) + b x_2(t)\} = a x_1(t - t_0) + b x_2(t - t_0)$ | **Yes** — Same linearity holds | Time shift is a **linear** operation |
| Time-Invariant? | $T\{x(t - t_1)\} = y(t - t_1)$ | Same holds | The time shift system is **time-invariant** |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for the Substitution Rule:** *"Inside the function, the variable changes; the function's job description stays the same."*

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| **Engineering Field** | **Application of Time Shifting** |
|---|---|
| **Digital Communications** | Aligning transmitted and received signals in OFDM systems to compensate for propagation delay |
| **Radar & SONAR** | Range gating — using a delayed replica of the transmitted pulse to detect echoes from distant objects |
| **Audio Signal Processing** | Creating echo effects and reverberation by adding a delayed copy of the original signal |
| **Control Systems** | Compensating for actuator delays; representing transportation lag (dead time) in process plants |
| **Biomedical Signal Processing** | Aligning ECG/EEG leads to a common reference point for synchronized diagnosis |
| **Image/Video Processing (2D extension)** | Spatial shifting of pixels for motion compensation and pan/tilt operations |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Continuous-Time Signal — Right Shift

**Problem Statement:**
A continuous-time signal is given by:

$$x(t) = \begin{cases} t + 1, & 0 \leq t \leq 1 \\ 2, & 1 < t \leq 2 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

Determine the shifted signal $y(t) = x(t - 2)$ and verify the energy conservation property.

### Derivation — Step-by-Step

**Step 1: Apply the substitution rule.**
Replace every $t$ in the expression of $x(t)$ with $(t - 2)$. The new argument is $t' = t - 2$, which means the original $t$ in the function definition must be replaced with $(t - 2)$.

$$y(t) = x(t - 2)$$

**Step 2: Transform the linearly increasing portion.**
For $0 \leq t \leq 1$ (originally), substitute $t \to (t - 2)$:

$$y_1(t) = (t - 2) + 1 = t - 1$$

The condition $0 \leq t \leq 1$ on the original becomes $0 \leq (t - 2) \leq 1$ on the new variable. Solving for $t$:

$$0 \leq t - 2 \leq 1 \implies 2 \leq t \leq 3$$

**Step 3: Transform the constant portion.**
For $1 < t \leq 2$ (originally):

$$y_2(t) = 2$$

The condition transforms to $1 < (t - 2) \leq 2$, hence:

$$1 < t - 2 \leq 2 \implies 3 < t \leq 4$$

**Step 4: Reassemble the shifted signal.**

$$y(t) = x(t - 2) = \begin{cases} t - 1, & 2 \leq t \leq 3 \\ 2, & 3 < t \leq 4 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Step 5: Verification of energy conservation.**
The original signal energy is:

$$E_x = \int_0^1 (t + 1)^2 \, dt + \int_1^2 (2)^2 \, dt$$

Computing the first integral:

$$\int_0^1 (t + 1)^2 \, dt = \left[ \frac{(t + 1)^3}{3} \right]_0^1 = \frac{8}{3} - \frac{1}{3} = \frac{7}{3}$$

Computing the second integral:

$$\int_1^2 4 \, dt = 4$$

Therefore:

$$E_x = \frac{7}{3} + 4 = \frac{7 + 12}{3} = \frac{19}{3} \text{ units}$$

The shifted signal energy is:

$$E_y = \int_2^3 (t - 1)^2 \, dt + \int_3^4 4 \, dt$$

Computing the first integral:

$$\int_2^3 (t - 1)^2 \, dt = \left[ \frac{(t - 1)^3}{3} \right]_2^3 = \frac{8}{3} - \frac{1}{3} = \frac{7}{3}$$

Computing the second integral:

$$\int_3^4 4 \, dt = 4$$

Therefore:

$$E_y = \frac{7}{3} + 4 = \frac{19}{3} \text{ units} = E_x \quad \checkmark$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The energy $E_y = E_x = \frac{19}{3}$ confirms that time shifting is an **energy-preserving** (isometric) transformation in $L^2$ signal space.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Continuous-Time Signal — Time Reversal + Shift

**Problem Statement:**
For the same signal $x(t)$ defined in Example 1, determine the signal $z(t) = x(-t + 3)$.

### Derivation — Step-by-Step

**Step 1: Recognize the combined operation.**
The expression $x(-t + 3)$ can be rewritten as:

$$z(t) = x(-(t - 3)) = x(-(t - 3))$$

This represents a **time reversal followed by a right shift of 3**, OR equivalently, a **right shift of 3 followed by time reversal**.

**Step 2: Apply the substitution $t \to (-t + 3)$.**
For the linearly increasing portion $0 \leq t \leq 1$:

$$z_1(t) = (-t + 3) + 1 = -t + 4$$

The original condition $0 \leq t \leq 1$ becomes $0 \leq (-t + 3) \leq 1$. Solving:

$$0 \leq -t + 3 \leq 1 \implies -3 \leq -t \leq -2 \implies 2 \leq t \leq 3$$

**Step 3: Transform the constant portion.**
For $1 < t \leq 2$:

$$z_2(t) = 2$$

The condition $1 < (-t + 3) \leq 2$ becomes:

$$1 < -t + 3 \leq 2 \implies -2 < -t \leq -1 \implies 1 \leq t < 2$$

**Step 4: Reassemble the final signal.**

$$z(t) = x(-t + 3) = \begin{cases} -t + 4, & 2 \leq t \leq 3 \\ 2, & 1 \leq t < 2 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Observation:** The original $x(t)$ had amplitude $1$ at $t=0$ and amplitude $2$ for $t \in (1, 2]$. After the transformation $x(-t + 3)$, the value at $t = 3$ is $(-3 + 4) = 1$ (matches the original at $t=0$), and the value at $t = 1.5$ is $2$ (matches the original at $t = 1.5$). The reversal has flipped the time axis, and the shift has repositioned the result.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Discrete-Time Signal — Shifting a Sequence

**Problem Statement:**
A discrete-time signal is given as:

$$x[n] = \{1, \ 2, \ 3, \ 2, \ 1\} \quad \text{with the arrow at } n = 0$$

Here, $x[-2] = 1$, $x[-1] = 2$, $x[0] = 3$, $x[1] = 2$, $x[2] = 1$.

Determine and tabulate the values of:
- (i) $y[n] = x[n - 3]$
- (ii) $w[n] = x[-n + 2]$

### Derivation — Step-by-Step

**Part (i): Right Shift by $n_0 = 3$.**

**Step 1:** Use the value mapping $y[n] = x[n - 3]$, so $y[n_0 + n] = x[n]$, which means $y[3 + n] = x[n]$.

**Step 2:** Construct the table by computing $y[n]$ at integer indices:

| $n$ | $n - 3$ | $x[n - 3]$ | $y[n]$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $-2$ | $-5$ | $0$ (undefined) | $0$ |
| $-1$ | $-4$ | $0$ (undefined) | $0$ |
| $0$ | $-3$ | $0$ (undefined) | $0$ |
| $1$ | $-2$ | $x[-2]$ | $1$ |
| $2$ | $-1$ | $x[-1]$ | $2$ |
| $3$ | $0$ | $x[0]$ | $3$ |
| $4$ | $1$ | $x[1]$ | $2$ |
| $5$ | $2$ | $x[2]$ | $1$ |
| $6$ | $3$ | $0$ (undefined) | $0$ |

**Result:** $y[n] = \{0, \ 0, \ 0, \ 1, \ 2, \ 3, \ 2, \ 1, \ 0\}$ with the arrow (origin) shifted to $n = 3$.

> [!TIP]
> **Practical Rule:** A right shift of $n_0$ units in discrete-time means **every sample moves $n_0$ positions to the right**, and the origin indicator (arrow) also moves $n_0$ positions to the right.

**Part (ii): Time Reversal + Right Shift.**

**Step 1:** Recognize that $w[n] = x[-n + 2]$ performs a reversal and a shift.

**Step 2:** Use the value mapping $w[n] = x[-n + 2]$, so $w[2 - n] = x[n]$. Equivalently, $w[m] = x[2 - m]$.

**Step 3:** Construct the table:

| $n$ | $2 - n$ | $x[2 - n]$ | $w[n]$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $0$ | $2$ | $x[2]$ | $1$ |
| $1$ | $1$ | $x[1]$ | $2$ |
| $2$ | $0$ | $x[0]$ | $3$ |
| $3$ | $-1$ | $x[-1]$ | $2$ |
| $4$ | $-2$ | $x[-2]$ | $1$ |
| $5$ | $-3$ | $0$ (undefined) | $0$ |
| $-1$ | $3$ | $0$ (undefined) | $0$ |

**Result:** $w[n] = \{0, \ 1, \ 2, \ 3, \ 2, \ 1, \ 0\}$ with the arrow at $n = 2$.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Time Shifting of Standard Signals

| **Original Signal $x(t)$** | **Shifted Signal $x(t - t_0)$** | **Description of Effect** |
|---|---|---|
| $\delta(t)$ (Unit Impulse) | $\delta(t - t_0)$ | Impulse relocated to $t = t_0$ |
| $u(t)$ (Unit Step) | $u(t - t_0)$ | Step transition occurs at $t = t_0$ instead of $t = 0$ |
| $e^{-at} u(t)$ (Decaying Exponential) | $e^{-a(t - t_0)} u(t - t_0)$ | Decay now begins at $t = t_0$ |
| $\sin(\omega_0 t)$ (Sine Wave) | $\sin(\omega_0 (t - t_0)) = \sin(\omega_0 t - \phi)$, $\phi = \omega_0 t_0$ | Phase shift of $\phi$ radians introduced |
| $\cos(\omega_0 t)$ (Cosine Wave) | $\cos(\omega_0 (t - t_0)) = \cos(\omega_0 t - \phi)$, $\phi = \omega_0 t_0$ | Phase shift of $\phi$ radians introduced |
| $\text{rect}\left(\frac{t}{\tau}\right)$ (Rectangular Pulse) | $\text{rect}\left(\frac{t - t_0}{\tau}\right)$ | Pulse relocated; width $\tau$ unchanged |
| $\text{tri}\left(\frac{t}{\tau}\right)$ (Triangular Pulse) | $\text{tri}\left(\frac{t - t_0}{\tau}\right)$ | Triangle relocated; base $\tau$ unchanged |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse $x(t - t_0)$ with $x(t + t_0)$ when $t_0$ itself is negative. Remember: **always read the literal sign of the term being added/subtracted from $t$ inside the parentheses**, not the sign of $t_0$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Time Shift as a System

The following block diagram represents a time shift operation as a single-input, single-output (SISO) linear time-invariant (LTI) system.

```mermaid
graph LR
    inpA["Input x of t"] --> sysB["Time Shifter T with parameter t0"]
    sysB --> outC["Output y of t equals x of t minus t0"]
    sysB -.->|"Time Invariance Property"| sysB
    style inpA fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#1976d2,color:#000000
    style sysB fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000000
    style outC fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#388e3c,color:#000000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The box labelled "Time Shifter" accepts $x(t)$ as input and emits $y(t) = x(t - t_0)$ as output. The self-loop annotation indicates that this system is **time-invariant** — delaying the input by any amount $t_1$ results in the output being delayed by the *same* $t_1$.

## 4.2 Operational Flowchart — Decision Tree for Shifting

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA["Start with x of t"] --> readB["Read the function expression and its support interval a to b"]
    readB --> parseC["Parse the shift argument t minus t0 from the target expression"]
    parseC --> signD{"What is the sign of t0?"}
    signD -->|"t0 greater than 0"| rightE["Right Shift Delay Operation"]
    signD -->|"t0 less than 0"| leftF["Left Shift Advance Operation"]
    signD -->|"t0 equals 0"| identityG["Identity: y of t equals x of t"]
    rightE --> subR["Substitute t to t minus t0 in expression"]
    leftF --> subL["Substitute t to t plus absolute t0 in expression"]
    subR --> transR["Transform support: a to b becomes a plus t0 to b plus t0"]
    subL --> transL["Transform support: a to b becomes a minus absolute t0 to b minus absolute t0"]
    transR --> plotR["Plot the new waveform"]
    transL --> plotL["Plot the new waveform"]
    identityG --> plotI["Plot the original waveform unchanged"]
    plotR --> verify["Verify: Amplitude, Energy, Shape preserved; only position changed"]
    plotL --> verify
    plotI --> verify
    style startA fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000000
    style signD fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,color:#000000
    style verify fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000000
```

## 4.3 Time-Domain Transformation Visualization

The following diagram illustrates the geometric relationship between the original signal and its time-shifted versions, demonstrating how the peak location migrates along the time axis.

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph origRegion["ORIGINAL x of t: Peak at t equals 0"]
        origA["Amplitude equals A at t equals 0"]
    end
    subgraph delayRegion["DELAYED x of t minus 2: Peak at t equals 2"]
        delayB["Amplitude equals A at t equals 2"]
    end
    subgraph advRegion["ADVANCED x of t plus 2: Peak at t equals minus 2"]
        advC["Amplitude equals A at t equals minus 2"]
    end
    origRegion -->|"Shift Right by 2 units"| delayRegion
    origRegion -->|"Shift Left by 2 units"| advRegion
    style origA fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c,color:#000000
    style delayB fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000000
    style advC fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000000
```

## 4.4 Functional Architecture — Multi-Stage Time Shift Pipeline

In real-time DSP systems, time shifting is implemented as a **digital delay line (tapped delay line)**:

```mermaid
graph LR
    srcA["Source x of n"] --> buf1["Buffer z to the power minus 1: 1 Sample Delay"]
    buf1 --> buf2["Buffer z to the power minus 1: 1 Sample Delay"]
    buf2 --> buf3["Buffer z to the power minus 1: 1 Sample Delay"]
    buf3 --> outD["Output y of n equals x of n minus 3"]
    srcA --> tap0["Tap h0 equals 0"]
    buf1 --> tap1["Tap h1 equals 0"]
    buf2 --> tap2["Tap h2 equals 0"]
    buf3 --> tap3["Tap h3 equals 1"]
    tap0 --> sumE["Summer"]
    tap1 --> sumE
    tap2 --> sumE
    tap3 --> sumE
    sumE --> outD
    style srcA fill:#e1f5fe,stroke:#01579b,color:#000000
    style outD fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000000
    style sumE fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000000
    style tap3 fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,color:#000000
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Each block labelled $z^{-1}$ represents a **one-sample delay element** in a digital signal processor. To realize a shift of $n_0 = 3$ samples, we cascade three such delay elements and tap the signal at the third buffer's output. This is the practical hardware-level realization of discrete-time time shifting.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 (Model Paper)]**  
**Define the operation of time shifting for continuous-time signals. Distinguish clearly between right shift and left shift with a labelled sketch for each case. (3 Marks)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> 
> **Definition (1 Mark):** Time shifting of a continuous-time signal $x(t)$ produces a new signal $y(t) = x(t - t_0)$, where $t_0$ is the time shift parameter. The signal's shape, amplitude, and duration remain unchanged; only its position on the time axis is altered.
> 
> **Right Shift / Delay (1 Mark):** When $t_0 > 0$, the signal $x(t - t_0)$ is shifted to the right by $t_0$ units. Every feature of the signal (e.g., a peak originally at $t = 0$) now appears at $t = t_0$. This represents a **delay**.
> 
> **Left Shift / Advance (1 Mark):** When $t_0 < 0$, the signal $x(t + \vert t_0 \vert)$ is shifted to the left by $\vert t_0 \vert$ units. A feature originally at $t = 0$ now appears at $t = -\vert t_0 \vert$. This represents an **advance**.
> 
> **Labelled Sketch:**
> 
> ```
> x(t)             x(t - 2)            x(t + 2)
>   |  /\             |       /\         /\  |
>   | /  \            |      /  \       /  \ |
>   |/    \           |     /    \     /    \|
> --+----+---t    ----+----+--+---t --+----+---t
>      0                0  2            -2  0
>   (Original)      (Right Shift)    (Left Shift)
> ```

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**  
**A rectangular pulse is defined as $x(t) = 1$ for $-1 \leq t \leq 1$ and $x(t) = 0$ otherwise. Find the values of $t$ for which $x(t - 3) = 1$. (3 Marks)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> 
> The signal $x(t - 3)$ is non-zero when the argument $(t - 3)$ lies within the original support $[-1, 1]$ (1 Mark).
> 
> Setting up the inequality:
> 
> $$-1 \leq t - 3 \leq 1$$
> 
> Adding 3 throughout (1 Mark):
> 
> $$2 \leq t \leq 4$$
> 
> Therefore, $x(t - 3) = 1$ for $2 \leq t \leq 4$ and $x(t - 3) = 0$ otherwise (1 Mark).
> 
> **Conclusion:** The right shift by 3 units has translated the support interval from $[-1, 1]$ to $[2, 4]$.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

Consider the continuous-time signal:

$$x(t) = \begin{cases} 2, & 0 \leq t \leq 2 \\ 4 - t, & 2 < t \leq 4 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**(a)** Determine and sketch the signals $x(t - 1)$ and $x(-t + 3)$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For the same $x(t)$, determine the energy of $x(t)$ and the energy of $x(t - 1)$. Comment on the result. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a): Time-Shifted Signals (7 Marks)**

**Derivation of $x(t - 1)$:**

Substituting $t \to (t - 1)$ in each branch (1 Mark):

- For the constant portion: $x(t - 1) = 2$ when $0 \leq (t - 1) \leq 2$, i.e., $1 \leq t \leq 3$ (1 Mark)
- For the linearly decreasing portion: $x(t - 1) = 4 - (t - 1) = 5 - t$ when $2 < (t - 1) \leq 4$, i.e., $3 < t \leq 5$ (1 Mark)

$$x(t - 1) = \begin{cases} 2, & 1 \leq t \leq 3 \\ 5 - t, & 3 < t \leq 5 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Sketch (2 Marks):** A rectangular pulse of height 2 from $t = 1$ to $t = 3$, followed by a linearly decreasing ramp from value 2 (at $t = 3$) to value 0 (at $t = 5$).

**Derivation of $x(-t + 3)$:**

Rewriting as $x(-(t - 3))$, this is a time reversal followed by a right shift of 3 (1 Mark).

Substituting $t \to (-t + 3)$:

- For the constant portion: $x(-t + 3) = 2$ when $0 \leq -t + 3 \leq 2$, i.e., $1 \leq t \leq 3$ (1 Mark)
- For the linearly decreasing portion: $x(-t + 3) = 4 - (-t + 3) = 1 + t$ when $2 < -t + 3 \leq 4$, i.e., $-1 \leq t < 1$ (1 Mark)

$$x(-t + 3) = \begin{cases} 1 + t, & -1 \leq t < 1 \\ 2, & 1 \leq t \leq 3 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Sketch (skipped for compactness):** A linearly increasing ramp from 0 (at $t = -1$) to 2 (at $t = 1$), followed by a constant value of 2 from $t = 1$ to $t = 3$.

---

**Part (b): Energy Computation and Comparison (7 Marks)**

**Energy of $x(t)$:**

$$E_x = \int_0^2 (2)^2 \, dt + \int_2^4 (4 - t)^2 \, dt$$

First integral (1 Mark):

$$\int_0^2 4 \, dt = 8$$

Second integral (2 Marks):

$$\int_2^4 (4 - t)^2 \, dt = \int_2^4 (16 - 8t + t^2) \, dt = \left[ 16t - 4t^2 + \frac{t^3}{3} \right]_2^4$$

Evaluating at $t = 4$: $64 - 64 + \frac{64}{3} = \frac{64}{3}$
Evaluating at $t = 2$: $32 - 16 + \frac{8}{3} = 16 + \frac{8}{3} = \frac{56}{3}$

$$\int_2^4 (4 - t)^2 \, dt = \frac{64}{3} - \frac{56}{3} = \frac{8}{3}$$

Therefore:

$$E_x = 8 + \frac{8}{3} = \frac{32}{3} \text{ units} \quad (1 \text{ Mark})$$

**Energy of $x(t - 1)$:**

By the **time-shift energy invariance theorem**:

$$E_{x(t-1)} = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t - 1) \vert^2 \, dt = E_x = \frac{32}{3} \text{ units} \quad (2 \text{ Marks})$$

**Comment (1 Mark):** The energy of a signal is **invariant under time shifting**. This is a direct consequence of the fact that the integration variable $t$ sweeps the same total duration (4 units) for both signals, and the squared function values are identical in shape. Hence, $E_{x(t-1)} = E_x$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

A discrete-time signal is given by:

$$x[n] = \{2, \ 1, \ 3, \ 2, \ 1, \ 4\}$$

with the arrow indicating the origin at $n = 0$. Thus, $x[-2] = 2$, $x[-1] = 1$, $x[0] = 3$, $x[1] = 2$, $x[2] = 1$, $x[3] = 4$.

**(a)** Determine and tabulate the values of $y_1[n] = x[n - 2]$ and $y_2[n] = x[-n + 1]$. Sketch both signals. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** If the discrete-time signal $g[n] = x[2n]$ (time compression) and $h[n] = x[2n - 2]$ are formed, compute their values for $-3 \leq n \leq 3$ and comment on the relationship between $h[n]$ and $g[n]$. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a): Discrete-Time Shifted Signals (7 Marks)**

**Derivation of $y_1[n] = x[n - 2]$ (Right Shift by 2):**

Using the mapping $y_1[n] = x[n - 2]$, we tabulate (3 Marks):

| $n$ | $n - 2$ | $x[n - 2]$ | $y_1[n]$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $-1$ | $-3$ | $0$ (undefined) | $0$ |
| $0$ | $-2$ | $x[-2]$ | $2$ |
| $1$ | $-1$ | $x[-1]$ | $1$ |
| $2$ | $0$ | $x[0]$ | $3$ |
| $3$ | $1$ | $x[1]$ | $2$ |
| $4$ | $2$ | $x[2]$ | $1$ |
| $5$ | $3$ | $x[3]$ | $4$ |
| $6$ | $4$ | $0$ (undefined) | $0$ |

**Result:** $y_1[n] = \{0, \ 2, \ 1, \ 3, \ 2, \ 1, \ 4, \ 0\}$ with the arrow at $n = 0$.

**Sketch (0.5 Mark):** Stem plot showing the same six-sample waveform as the original, but with the origin indicator moved from $n = 0$ to $n = 2$. The leading and trailing zeros are added on both sides.

**Derivation of $y_2[n] = x[-n + 1]$ (Reversal + Shift):**

Using the mapping $y_2[n] = x[-n + 1]$, we tabulate (3 Marks):

| $n$ | $-n + 1$ | $x[-n + 1]$ | $y_2[n]$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $-2$ | $3$ | $x[3]$ | $4$ |
| $-1$ | $2$ | $x[2]$ | $1$ |
| $0$ | $1$ | $x[1]$ | $2$ |
| $1$ | $0$ | $x[0]$ | $3$ |
| $2$ | $-1$ | $x[-1]$ | $1$ |
| $3$ | $-2$ | $x[-2]$ | $2$ |
| $4$ | $-3$ | $0$ (undefined) | $0$ |

**Result:** $y_2[n] = \{0, \ 4, \ 1, \ 2, \ 3, \ 1, \ 2, \ 0\}$ with the arrow at $n = 0$.

**Sketch (0.5 Mark):** The original sequence has been mirrored about $n = 0.5$ (i.e., the time axis is flipped and then shifted by 1 unit to the right).

---

**Part (b): Time-Compressed Signals and Their Relationship (7 Marks)**

**Computation of $g[n] = x[2n]$ for $-3 \leq n \leq 3$ (3 Marks):**

| $n$ | $2n$ | $x[2n]$ | $g[n]$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $-3$ | $-6$ | $0$ (undefined) | $0$ |
| $-2$ | $-4$ | $0$ (undefined) | $0$ |
| $-1$ | $-2$ | $x[-2]$ | $2$ |
| $0$ | $0$ | $x[0]$ | $3$ |
| $1$ | $2$ | $x[2]$ | $1$ |
| $2$ | $4$ | $0$ (undefined) | $0$ |
| $3$ | $6$ | $0$ (undefined) | $0$ |

**Computation of $h[n] = x[2n - 2]$ for $-3 \leq n \leq 3$ (3 Marks):**

| $n$ | $2n - 2$ | $x[2n - 2]$ | $h[n]$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $-3$ | $-8$ | $0$ (undefined) | $0$ |
| $-2$ | $-6$ | $0$ (undefined) | $0$ |
| $-1$ | $-4$ | $0$ (undefined) | $0$ |
| $0$ | $-2$ | $x[-2]$ | $2$ |
| $1$ | $0$ | $x[0]$ | $3$ |
| $2$ | $2$ | $x[2]$ | $1$ |
| $3$ | $4$ | $0$ (undefined) | $0$ |

**Relationship (1 Mark):** Comparing $g[n]$ and $h[n]$:

- $g[n]$ has its first non-zero sample at $n = -1$ (value 2).
- $h[n]$ has its first non-zero sample at $n = 0$ (value 2).

**Conclusion:** $h[n] = g[n - 1]$. The signal $h[n]$ is obtained from $g[n]$ by a **right shift of 1 unit**. This is consistent with the algebraic identity: $h[n] = x[2n - 2] = x[2(n - 1)] = g[n - 1]$. This demonstrates the **composability** of time scaling and time shifting operations.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 
> **Pitfall 1: Sign Confusion in the Argument (Highest Frequency Error)**
> Students often write $x(t + 2)$ when the problem asks for $x(t - 2)$, or vice versa. **Always read the literal sign of the constant appearing inside the parentheses with the variable $t$.** A right shift of 2 corresponds to $x(t - 2)$, never $x(t + 2)$.
> 
> **Pitfall 2: Forgetting to Transform the Support Intervals**
> Students correctly substitute the argument in the function body but forget to transform the *conditions*. For example, they may write $x(t - 2) = 2$ for $0 \leq t \leq 1$ instead of $2 \leq t \leq 3$. **Both the expression AND the interval must be transformed.**
> 
> **Pitfall 3: Confusing $x(-t + t_0)$ with $x(-(t + t_0))$**
> $x(-t + 3)$ and $x(-(t + 3)) = x(-t - 3)$ are **different** signals. The first is a right shift of 3 followed by reversal; the second is a left shift of 3 followed by reversal.
> 
> **Pitfall 4: Not Marking the Origin After Discrete-Time Shifts**
> When sketching $x[n - n_0]$, students must **relocate the arrow** to the new origin position. The KTU examiner awards 1 mark specifically for correctly indicating the shifted origin.
> 
> **Pitfall 5: Forgetting the Energy/Power Invariance Property**
> When asked to compute energy after a time shift, students sometimes perform the entire integration again. **Use the energy invariance theorem** $E_{x(t-t_0)} = E_x$ to save time and reduce algebraic errors.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Translation (Time Shifting)**

- **Core Operation:** $y(t) = x(t - t_0)$ — the variable inside the function is replaced with $(t - t_0)$.
- **Right Shift / Delay:** $t_0 > 0$ moves the signal right; e.g., $x(t - 2)$ shifts right by 2.
- **Left Shift / Advance:** $t_0 < 0$ moves the signal left; e.g., $x(t + 2)$ shifts left by 2.
- **Support Transformation:** The support interval $[a, b]$ of $x(t)$ becomes $[a + t_0, b + t_0]$ for $x(t - t_0)$.
- **Preserved Quantities:** Amplitude, shape, duration, energy $E$, power $P$ are **all invariant** under time shift.
- **Affected Quantities:** Position on the time axis, origin location, causality classification.
- **Combined Operation:** $x(-t + t_0) = x(-(t - t_0))$ represents a right shift of $t_0$ followed by time reversal (or vice versa).
- **Discrete-Time Rule:** $y[n] = x[n - n_0]$ — every sample moves $n_0$ positions right; the arrow also moves right by $n_0$.
- **System Property:** A time shift is a **linear, time-invariant** operation.
- **Sinusoidal Equivalence:** $\sin(\omega_0(t - t_0)) = \sin(\omega_0 t - \phi)$ with $\phi = \omega_0 t_0$ — a time shift is equivalent to a **phase shift** for sinusoidal signals.
- **Hardware Realization:** Implemented using a **tapped delay line** with $z^{-1}$ elements in digital signal processors.
- **Solved Problem Pattern:** (1) Substitute $t$ in expression, (2) Transform the conditions, (3) Sketch the new signal, (4) Verify preservation of energy/power.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Modulation (Frequency Shifting)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST416 - SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Modulation (Frequency Shifting)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Modulation (Frequency Shifting)** is the process of varying one or more properties of a **periodic waveform** (called the *carrier signal*) with a **modulating signal** (the message) that contains information to be transmitted. In the context of **Signals and Systems**, *modulation* is mathematically modeled as **multiplication in the time domain**, which produces a **frequency shift of the message spectrum** in the frequency domain (Fourier Transform domain), governed by the **Modulation Theorem** (also called the *Frequency Shifting Property*).

### The Modulation Theorem (Frequency Shifting Property)

If $x(t) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} X(j\omega)$ is a Fourier Transform pair, then:

$$x(t)\,e^{j\omega_0 t} \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} X\bigl(j(\omega - \omega_0)\bigr)$$

Multiplication by a **complex exponential** $e^{j\omega_0 t}$ in time shifts the spectrum to the **right** by $\omega_0$. Similarly, multiplication by $e^{-j\omega_0 t}$ shifts the spectrum to the **left** by $\omega_0$.

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy — The "Loudspeaker on a Helicopter" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Imagine you are standing on the ground humming a low-frequency tune (your voice = the *message signal* $x(t)$). A helicopter hovering nearby captures your voice using a microphone, mixes it with a high-frequency rotor whistle (the *carrier* $cos(\omega_c t)$), and rebroadcasts the combined signal over a powerful loudspeaker.
>
> **Result:** Everyone across a 50 km radius can hear your low-frequency hum, even though electromagnetic waves of that low frequency would not have traveled that far. The information (your humming) is intact, but it has been **shifted to a much higher frequency band** — this is precisely what *modulation* does in communication systems.

- The **information-bearing signal** $x(t)$ (low frequency, baseband) is translated to a **passband frequency** $\omega_c$ suitable for transmission over antennas or long channels.
- The baseband signal spectrum is centered at $\omega = 0$ (DC). After modulation, two copies appear centered at $+\omega_c$ and $-\omega_c$.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Carrier Frequency ($f_c$ or $\omega_c$):** the high frequency used for transmission — typical values range from **535 kHz to 1605 kHz (AM broadcast band)** to **GHz ranges for cellular**.
- **Modulation Index ($\mu$):** a dimensionless ratio in **Amplitude Modulation (AM)** defined as $\mu = \frac{A_m}{A_c}$ where $A_m$ is the message amplitude and $A_c$ is the carrier amplitude. Standard limit: $\mathbf{0 \le \mu \le 1}$ for distortion-free conventional AM.
- **Bandwidth of AM signal:** $BW = 2W$ where $W$ is the message bandwidth (in Hz).
- **Sidebands:** *Upper Sideband (USB)* and *Lower Sideband (LSB)* — the two translated copies of the baseband spectrum.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — Module 1):** Students are expected to (a) state and prove the modulation theorem, (b) apply it to derive the spectrum of AM, DSB-SC, and SSB signals, and (c) solve numerical problems involving carrier/message frequencies and modulation index.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

The modulation operation proceeds in the following logical stages:

1. **Generation of Message Signal:** The original information $m(t)$ (audio, video, data) is a low-frequency baseband signal with spectrum $M(j\omega)$ concentrated around $\omega = 0$.
2. **Carrier Signal Generation:** A high-frequency sinusoidal carrier $c(t) = A_c \cos(\omega_c t)$ or $c(t) = A_c e^{j\omega_c t}$ is generated by a stable local oscillator.
3. **Multiplication in Time Domain:** The product $s(t) = m(t) \cdot c(t)$ is produced using an electronic multiplier (or a nonlinear device like a square-law modulator followed by a bandpass filter).
4. **Spectrum Translation (Frequency Shift):** By the convolution-multiplication duality and the Frequency Shifting Property, the spectrum of $s(t)$ becomes $S(j\omega) = \frac{1}{2\pi}\,M(j\omega) * C(j\omega)$, which geometrically corresponds to **translating** the message spectrum to $\pm \omega_c$.
5. **Transmission over Channel:** The high-frequency signal $s(t)$ radiates efficiently from antennas (since antenna length $\propto \lambda = c/f$).
6. **Demodulation (at the receiver):** The baseband message is recovered by mixing $s(t)$ with a synchronized local carrier and passing through a lowpass filter.

### The "Why" Behind Frequency Shifting

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why shift the frequency?** Three engineering reasons:
> (a) **Antenna Size:** For efficient radiation, antenna length must be $\approx \lambda/4$. For a 1 kHz audio signal, $\lambda \approx 300$ km — impossible! Shifting to 1 MHz gives $\lambda \approx 300$ m.
> (b) **Multiplexing / Multiple Access:** FDM (Frequency Division Multiplexing) places many users on different $\omega_c$ values within one physical channel.
> (c) **Reduced Noise/Interference:** Different frequency bands have different propagation and noise characteristics — high frequencies propagate as ground/sky waves over long distances.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **authoritative reference** for KTU board exam numerical problems on Modulation (Frequency Shifting). Memorize these relations.

| # | Concept | Mathematical Relation | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|----------------------|---------------|
| 1 | Frequency Shifting Property | $x(t)\,e^{j\omega_0 t} \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} X\bigl(j(\omega - \omega_0)\bigr)$ | Complex carrier; shift by $\omega_0$ rad/s |
| 2 | Modulation by $\cos(\omega_c t)$ | $x(t)\cos(\omega_c t) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} \frac{1}{2}\bigl[X(j(\omega-\omega_c)) + X(j(\omega+\omega_c))\bigr]$ | Real carrier; two sidebands at $\pm \omega_c$ |
| 3 | Modulation by $\sin(\omega_c t)$ | $x(t)\sin(\omega_c t) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} \frac{1}{2j}\bigl[X(j(\omega-\omega_c)) - X(j(\omega+\omega_c))\bigr]$ | Imaginary (odd) spectrum shift |
| 4 | Conventional AM (with carrier) | $s_{AM}(t) = A_c\bigl[1 + \mu\,m(t)\bigr]\cos(\omega_c t)$ | $m(t)$ is normalized: $\vert m(t) \vert \le 1$ |
| 5 | Modulation Index | $\mu = \frac{A_m}{A_c}$ | Dimensionless, $0 \le \mu \le 1$ for no distortion |
| 6 | DSB-SC (Suppressed Carrier) | $s_{DSB}(t) = A_c\,m(t)\cos(\omega_c t)$ | No carrier component; saves power |
| 7 | Carrier Power vs Total Power | $P_c = \frac{A_c^{\,2}}{2}$ ; $P_t = P_c \left(1 + \frac{\mu^2}{2}\right)$ | For sinusoidal message |
| 8 | AM Bandwidth | $BW_{AM} = 2W$ | $W$ = message bandwidth in Hz |
| 9 | SSB Bandwidth | $BW_{SSB} = W$ | Single sideband |
| 10 | DSB-SC Bandwidth | $BW_{DSB} = 2W$ | Same as conventional AM |
| 11 | Efficiency of AM | $\eta = \frac{\mu^2}{2 + \mu^2} \times 100\%$ | Max $\eta = 33.33\%$ at $\mu = 1$ |
| 12 | Hilbert Transform (for SSB) | $\hat{x}(t) = x(t) * \frac{1}{\pi t}$ | Used to remove one sideband |

> [!WARNING]
> **Units Vigilance:** Angular frequency $\omega$ is in **rad/s**; ordinary frequency $f$ is in **Hz**; they are related by $\omega = 2\pi f$. The carrier frequency given in problems must be converted carefully before plugging into formulas.

## 2.3 Real-World Utility

- **AM Radio Broadcasting (MW/SW):** Uses conventional AM (with carrier), $f_c \in$ [535 kHz, 1605 kHz].
- **FM Radio (88–108 MHz):** Uses *frequency modulation* (variation of $\omega_c$ with $m(t)$).
- **TV Broadcasting (Analog):** Uses VSB (Vestigial Sideband).
- **Telemetry & Aircraft Communication (108–137 MHz):** DSB-AM.
- **Radar & Satellite Communication:** Uses complex modulation schemes (QPSK, QAM) which are essentially combinations of two DSB-SC modulations on orthogonal carriers.
- **Software Defined Radio (SDR):** Modern digital implementations use **complex baseband** representation $s_{BB}(t) = I(t) + jQ(t)$, where the spectrum is shifted to baseband digitally.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Frequency Shifting Property of Fourier Transform

> **Given:** $x(t) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} X(j\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t)\,e^{-j\omega t}\,dt$

**Goal:** Find the Fourier Transform of $x(t)\,e^{j\omega_0 t}$.

We begin by writing the definition of the Fourier Transform applied to the modulated signal $y(t) = x(t)\,e^{j\omega_0 t}$:

$$Y(j\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t)\,e^{j\omega_0 t}\,e^{-j\omega t}\,dt$$

We combine the two complex exponentials into a single one by using the law of exponents ($e^a \cdot e^b = e^{a+b}$):

$$Y(j\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t)\,e^{-j(\omega - \omega_0) t}\,dt$$

We recognize that the integrand is exactly the Fourier Transform integral of $x(t)$ evaluated at the **shifted frequency** $(\omega - \omega_0)$. Therefore we can write:

$$Y(j\omega) = X\bigl(j(\omega - \omega_0)\bigr)$$

**Conclusion:**

$$\boxed{\,x(t)\,e^{j\omega_0 t} \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} X\bigl(j(\omega - \omega_0)\bigr)\,}$$

By a similar derivation (replacing $\omega_0$ with $-\omega_0$):

$$x(t)\,e^{-j\omega_0 t} \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} X\bigl(j(\omega + \omega_0)\bigr)$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Spectrum of DSB-SC (Real Carrier Modulation)

> **Given:** Message $m(t)$ with spectrum $M(j\omega)$ and carrier $c(t) = A_c \cos(\omega_c t)$.

**Step 1 — Express the carrier using Euler's formula:**

$$c(t) = A_c \cos(\omega_c t) = \frac{A_c}{2}\bigl(e^{j\omega_c t} + e^{-j\omega_c t}\bigr)$$

**Step 2 — Form the product signal:**

$$s(t) = m(t) \cdot c(t) = \frac{A_c}{2}\,m(t)\,e^{j\omega_c t} + \frac{A_c}{2}\,m(t)\,e^{-j\omega_c t}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Frequency Shifting Property to each term:**

The first term, by Derivation 3.1, produces a spectrum $\frac{A_c}{2}\,M\bigl(j(\omega - \omega_c)\bigr)$.

The second term produces a spectrum $\frac{A_c}{2}\,M\bigl(j(\omega + \omega_c)\bigr)$.

**Step 4 — Add the two contributions (linearity of FT):**

$$S_{DSB}(j\omega) = \frac{A_c}{2}\,M\bigl(j(\omega - \omega_c)\bigr) + \frac{A_c}{2}\,M\bigl(j(\omega + \omega_c)\bigr)$$

$$\boxed{\,S_{DSB}(j\omega) = \frac{A_c}{2}\bigl[M(j(\omega - \omega_c)) + M(j(\omega + \omega_c))\bigr]\,}$$

> **Geometric Interpretation:** The baseband spectrum $M(j\omega)$ (centered at $\omega=0$) is **replicated** and **shifted** to $+\omega_c$ and $-\omega_c$, each scaled by $A_c/2$. This generates the **Upper Sideband (USB)** at $+\omega_c$ and the **Lower Sideband (LSB)** at $-\omega_c$.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Spectrum of Conventional AM (with Carrier)

> **Given:** $s_{AM}(t) = A_c\bigl[1 + \mu\,m(t)\bigr]\cos(\omega_c t)$

**Step 1 — Distribute the carrier:**

$$s_{AM}(t) = A_c\cos(\omega_c t) + \mu A_c\,m(t)\cos(\omega_c t)$$

**Step 2 — Identify each term:**

- **Term 1** is the pure carrier: $A_c\cos(\omega_c t) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} \pi A_c \bigl[\delta(\omega - \omega_c) + \delta(\omega + \omega_c)\bigr]$
- **Term 2** is DSB-SC with message amplitude $\mu A_c$:

$$\mu A_c\,m(t)\cos(\omega_c t) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} \frac{\mu A_c}{2}\bigl[M(j(\omega-\omega_c)) + M(j(\omega+\omega_c))\bigr]$$

**Step 3 — Combine (linearity of FT):**

$$S_{AM}(j\omega) = \pi A_c \bigl[\delta(\omega - \omega_c) + \delta(\omega + \omega_c)\bigr] + \frac{\mu A_c}{2}\bigl[M(j(\omega-\omega_c)) + M(j(\omega+\omega_c))\bigr]$$

**Conclusion:**

$$\boxed{\,S_{AM}(j\omega) = \pi A_c\bigl[\delta(\omega - \omega_c) + \delta(\omega + \omega_c)\bigr] + \frac{\mu A_c}{2}\bigl[M(j(\omega-\omega_c)) + M(j(\omega+\omega_c))\bigr]\,}$$

> The spectrum contains **two impulses** (the carrier) at $\pm\omega_c$ plus the **two sidebands** produced by frequency shifting. No overlap is allowed: we must have $\omega_c > W$ to prevent spectral overlap (a phenomenon called **aliasing in frequency**).

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Power Efficiency of Conventional AM

> **Given:** $s_{AM}(t) = A_c[1 + \mu \cos(\omega_m t)]\cos(\omega_c t)$ (single-tone modulation)

**Step 1 — Expand using trigonometric identity** $\cos A \cos B = \frac{1}{2}[\cos(A-B) + \cos(A+B)]$:

$$s_{AM}(t) = A_c\cos(\omega_c t) + \frac{\mu A_c}{2}\cos\bigl((\omega_c - \omega_m)t\bigr) + \frac{\mu A_c}{2}\cos\bigl((\omega_c + \omega_m)t\bigr)$$

**Step 2 — Compute powers** (each sinusoid with amplitude $A$ has power $A^2/2$ over a $1\,\Omega$ load):

- Carrier power: $P_c = \frac{A_c^{\,2}}{2}$
- Each sideband power: $P_{SB} = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\mu A_c}{2}\right)^2 = \frac{\mu^2 A_c^{\,2}}{8}$
- Total sideband power: $P_{SB,tot} = 2 \times \frac{\mu^2 A_c^{\,2}}{8} = \frac{\mu^2 A_c^{\,2}}{4}$
- Total transmitted power: $P_t = P_c + P_{SB,tot} = \frac{A_c^{\,2}}{2} + \frac{\mu^2 A_c^{\,2}}{4} = \frac{A_c^{\,2}}{2}\left(1 + \frac{\mu^2}{2}\right)$

**Step 3 — Useful power ratio:**

$$\boxed{\,P_t = \frac{A_c^{\,2}}{2}\left(1 + \frac{\mu^2}{2}\right)\,}$$

**Step 4 — Power efficiency** (ratio of sideband power to total power):

$$\eta = \frac{P_{SB,tot}}{P_t} = \frac{\mu^2 A_c^{\,2}/4}{A_c^{\,2}/2 \cdot (1 + \mu^2/2)} = \frac{\mu^2/4}{(1 + \mu^2/2)/1} = \frac{\mu^2}{2 + \mu^2}$$

$$\boxed{\,\eta = \frac{\mu^2}{2 + \mu^2} \times 100\%\,}$$

For $\mu = 1$ (100% modulation): $\eta_{max} = \frac{1}{3} \approx 33.33\%$. This means **two-thirds of the transmitted power is "wasted"** in the carrier — a key reason DSB-SC and SSB are preferred in power-limited applications.

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Exam Standard)

> **Problem:** A message signal $m(t) = 3\cos(200\pi t)$ modulates a carrier $c(t) = 10\cos(2\pi \times 10^5 t)$ using conventional AM. Find: (i) Modulation index, (ii) Bandwidth, (iii) Total transmitted power over $50\,\Omega$, (iv) Efficiency.

**Solution:**

**(i) Modulation Index:** $\mu = A_m/A_c = 3/10 = 0.3$.

**(ii) Bandwidth:** Message frequency $f_m = 100$ Hz, so $W = 100$ Hz. $BW_{AM} = 2W = 200$ Hz.

**(iii) Power** ($R = 50\,\Omega$): $P_t = \dfrac{A_c^{\,2}}{2R}\left(1 + \dfrac{\mu^2}{2}\right) = \dfrac{100}{100}\left(1 + \dfrac{0.09}{2}\right) = 1 \times 1.045 = 1.045$ W.

**(iv) Efficiency:** $\eta = \dfrac{0.09}{2 + 0.09} = \dfrac{0.09}{2.09} \approx 4.31\%$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Conventional AM Transmitter

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcA[Message Source m of t] --> normA[Normalizer mu m of t]
    normA --> adderA[Summing Junction 1 plus mu m of t]
    srcB[DC Source 1] --> adderA
    adderA --> multA[Multiplier Block]
    srcC[Oscillator A_c cos omega_c t] --> multA
    multA --> ampA[Power Amplifier]
    ampA --> txa[Antenna TX]
    txa -.Wireless Channel.-> rxa[Receiving Antenna RX]
    rxa --> tunerA[RF Tuner at omega_c]
    tunerA --> detA[Envelope Detector]
    detA --> lpfA[Lowpass Filter W Hz]
    lpfA --> outA[Recovered Message m of t]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — DSB-SC Modulator (Balanced Modulator)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    s1[Message m of t] --> sm1[Splitter]
    sm1 --> m1[Multiplier 1]
    sm1 --> m2[Multiplier 2]
    osc[Local Oscillator A_c cos omega_c t] --> ospin[Phase Inverter 180 degrees]
    osc --> m1
    ospin --> m2
    m1 --> s1b[Summation]
    m2 --> s1b
    s1b --> outb[DSB SC Output s of t]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — SSB Generation (Hilbert Transform / Phasing Method)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    s2[Message m of t] --> sp2[90 Degree Phase Splitter]
    sp2 --> p1[Path 1 Direct m of t]
    sp2 --> p2[Path 2 Hilbert Transform m hat of t]
    p1 --> mul1[Multiplier with cos omega_c t]
    p2 --> mul2[Multiplier with sin omega_c t]
    osc2[Oscillator cos omega_c t] --> mul1
    osc2 --> osc2b[90 Degree Phase Shift]
    osc2b --> mul2
    mul1 --> sm2[Adder]
    mul2 --> sm2
    sm2 --> outc[SSB USB Output]
```

## 4.4 Spectral Visualization (Schematic of Sidebands)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph baseband[Baseband Spectrum M of j omega]
        bb[Triangular Spectrum Centered at omega equals 0 Bandwidth W]
    end
    subgraph usb[Upper Sideband After Modulation]
        us[Triangular Spectrum Centered at plus omega_c]
    end
    subgraph lsb[Lower Sideband After Modulation]
        ls[Triangular Spectrum Centered at minus omega_c]
    end
    baseband -- Frequency Shifting by plus omega_c --> usb
    baseband -- Frequency Shifting by minus omega_c --> lsb
    baseband --> carrierImpulse[Impulses at plus minus omega_c Carrier]
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** The baseband message spectrum (centered at $\omega = 0$) is replicated and translated to $\pm \omega_c$ during modulation. The two impulses at $\pm \omega_c$ represent the carrier component (in conventional AM, not in DSB-SC).
