# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**State and prove the frequency shifting property of the Fourier Transform.**

**Model Answer:**

> The frequency shifting property (also called the Modulation Theorem) states that if $x(t) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} X(j\omega)$, then multiplication by a complex exponential in time corresponds to a shift in the frequency domain:
> $$x(t)\,e^{j\omega_0 t} \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} X\bigl(j(\omega - \omega_0)\bigr)$$

**Proof:** By the definition of the Fourier Transform,

$$\mathcal{F}\{x(t)e^{j\omega_0 t}\} = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t)\,e^{j\omega_0 t}\,e^{-j\omega t}\,dt = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t)\,e^{-j(\omega-\omega_0)t}\,dt = X\bigl(j(\omega - \omega_0)\bigr). \quad \blacksquare$$

> **Engineering Significance:** This property is the **mathematical foundation of all modulation systems** — AM, DSB-SC, SSB, QAM, OFDM. It tells us that multiplying a message by a high-frequency carrier shifts the information spectrum to a new center frequency suitable for transmission.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**What is modulation index? For an AM signal $s(t) = 20[1 + 0.6\cos(200\pi t)]\cos(2\pi \times 10^5 t)$, determine the modulation index and the sideband frequencies.**

**Model Answer:**

The **modulation index** $\mu$ is defined as the ratio of the peak amplitude of the message signal to the peak amplitude of the carrier:

$$\mu = \frac{A_m}{A_c}$$

From the given expression, comparing with $s(t) = A_c[1 + \mu \cos(2\pi f_m t)]\cos(2\pi f_c t)$:

- Carrier amplitude: $A_c = 20$ V
- Modulation index: $\mu = 0.6$ (dimensionless, less than 1, so no overmodulation distortion)
- Message frequency: $f_m = 100$ Hz
- Carrier frequency: $f_c = 10^5 = 100$ kHz

**Sideband frequencies** (in the modulated spectrum):

$$f_{USB} = f_c + f_m = 100{,}000 + 100 = 100{,}100 \text{ Hz}$$

$$f_{LSB} = f_c - f_m = 100{,}000 - 100 = 99{,}900 \text{ Hz}$$

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]
**(a)** Derive the expression for the spectrum of a DSB-SC signal $s(t) = m(t)\cos(\omega_c t)$ and sketch the spectrum when $m(t)$ is a rectangular pulse of amplitude $A$ and duration $\tau$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A message signal $m(t) = 4\cos(1000\pi t)$ amplitude-modulates a carrier $c(t) = 20\cos(2\pi \times 10^6 t)$. Determine: (i) modulation index, (ii) bandwidth, (iii) total power over $50\,\Omega$ load, and (iv) transmission efficiency. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of DSB-SC Spectrum:**

The carrier in exponential form:

$$c(t) = \cos(\omega_c t) = \frac{1}{2}\bigl(e^{j\omega_c t} + e^{-j\omega_c t}\bigr)$$

The DSB-SC signal is:

$$s(t) = m(t)\cos(\omega_c t) = \frac{1}{2}\,m(t)\,e^{j\omega_c t} + \frac{1}{2}\,m(t)\,e^{-j\omega_c t}$$

Applying the frequency shifting property to each term:

$$S_{DSB}(j\omega) = \frac{1}{2}\,M\bigl(j(\omega - \omega_c)\bigr) + \frac{1}{2}\,M\bigl(j(\omega + \omega_c)\bigr)$$

For a rectangular pulse $m(t)$ of amplitude $A$ and width $\tau$, its spectrum is the **sinc function**:

$$M(j\omega) = A\tau \cdot \text{Sa}\left(\frac{\omega\tau}{2}\right)$$

Hence the DSB-SC spectrum consists of **two sinc-shaped lobes** centered at $+\omega_c$ and $-\omega_c$, each of peak height $A\tau/2$ and main-lobe width $4\pi/\tau$. The spectrum is real and even (since $m(t)$ is real and even), and exhibits $\pm 1$ symmetry about each center frequency.

**Sketch (description):** Two symmetric sinc-shaped pulses of peak amplitude $A\tau/2$ centered at $\pm\omega_c$ along the $\omega$-axis, with zero crossings at $\omega = \omega_c \pm 2\pi n/\tau$ for integer $n \neq 0$.

**(b) Numerical Computations:**

From $m(t) = 4\cos(1000\pi t)$: $\quad A_m = 4, \quad f_m = 500$ Hz.
From $c(t) = 20\cos(2\pi \times 10^6 t)$: $\quad A_c = 20, \quad f_c = 10^6$ Hz.
Load resistance: $R = 50\,\Omega$.

**(i) Modulation index:** $\mu = A_m/A_c = 4/20 = 0.2$.

**(ii) Bandwidth:** $BW = 2f_m = 2 \times 500 = 1000$ Hz $= 1$ kHz.

**(iii) Total Power:**

$$P_t = \frac{A_c^{\,2}}{2R}\left(1 + \frac{\mu^2}{2}\right) = \frac{20^2}{2 \times 50}\left(1 + \frac{0.04}{2}\right) = \frac{400}{100}\left(1 + 0.02\right) = 4 \times 1.02 = 4.08 \text{ W}$$

**(iv) Transmission Efficiency:**

$$\eta = \frac{\mu^2}{2 + \mu^2} \times 100\% = \frac{0.04}{2.04} \times 100\% \approx 1.96\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students frequently forget to divide by the **load resistance $R$** in the power formula. The peak amplitudes $A_c, A_m$ are in **Volts**, so $P = V_{rms}^2/R = A^2/(2R)$. Marks lost in this step are the **most common** deduction (typically 1 mark per sub-part). Also, do not confuse $f_m$ (message frequency) with $f_c$ (carrier frequency) — the bandwidth depends on $f_m$ only.

---

### Question B [KTU University Exam - May 2024] (Alternative Choice)
**(a)** Explain with mathematical derivation the spectrum of conventional AM signal. Compare conventional AM, DSB-SC, and SSB in terms of bandwidth, power efficiency, and complexity. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An SSB signal is generated by suppressing the LSB of a DSB-SC signal. If the message is $m(t) = 5\cos(2000\pi t)$ and the carrier is $c(t) = 50\cos(2\pi \times 5 \times 10^5 t)$, find: (i) frequencies present in the SSB-USB signal, (ii) bandwidth, and (iii) the total power delivered to a $75\,\Omega$ load assuming DSB-SC was the reference (compare with SSB output). **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) AM Spectrum Derivation:**

For a conventional AM signal:

$$s_{AM}(t) = A_c[1 + \mu\,m(t)]\cos(\omega_c t)$$

$$= A_c\cos(\omega_c t) + \mu A_c\,m(t)\cos(\omega_c t)$$

Taking the Fourier Transform of each term using the frequency shifting property:

$$S_{AM}(j\omega) = \pi A_c\bigl[\delta(\omega - \omega_c) + \delta(\omega + \omega_c)\bigr] + \frac{\mu A_c}{2}\bigl[M(j(\omega-\omega_c)) + M(j(\omega+\omega_c))\bigr]$$

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Conventional AM | DSB-SC | SSB |
|-----------|-----------------|--------|-----|
| Carrier transmitted? | Yes | No | No |
| Sidebands transmitted? | Both (USB + LSB) | Both | One only |
| Bandwidth | $2W$ | $2W$ | $W$ |
| Power efficiency | Up to 33.33% | ~50% (no carrier) | ~100% |
| Demodulation complexity | Simple (envelope detector) | Coherent (synchronous) required | Coherent required |
| Receiver cost | Low | High | Highest |

**(b) SSB Computations:**

From $m(t) = 5\cos(2000\pi t)$: $\quad A_m = 5, \quad f_m = 1000$ Hz.
From $c(t) = 50\cos(2\pi \times 5 \times 10^5 t)$: $\quad A_c = 50, \quad f_c = 5 \times 10^5$ Hz $= 500$ kHz.
Load: $R = 75\,\Omega$.

**(i) Frequencies in SSB-USB:** Since LSB is suppressed, only the upper sideband (USB) remains. For a single-tone message, USB contains **one** frequency component:

$$f_{USB} = f_c + f_m = 500{,}000 + 1000 = 501{,}000 \text{ Hz} = 501 \text{ kHz}$$

The LSB frequency (499 kHz) and the carrier (500 kHz) are both absent in SSB.

**(ii) Bandwidth:** $BW_{SSB} = W = f_m = 1000$ Hz = 1 kHz (half of DSB-SC).

**(iii) Power Comparison:**

For a single-tone DSB-SC: $s_{DSB}(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_m t)\cos(2\pi f_c t) = \frac{A_c}{2}[\cos 2\pi(f_c-f_m)t + \cos 2\pi(f_c+f_m)t]$.

$$P_{DSB} = \frac{(A_c/2)^2}{R} \times 2 = \frac{A_c^{\,2}}{2R} = \frac{50^2}{2 \times 75} = \frac{2500}{150} \approx 16.67 \text{ W}$$

For SSB (only USB survives), only **one** sideband power remains:

$$P_{SSB} = \frac{(A_c/2)^2}{R} = \frac{625}{75} \approx 8.33 \text{ W}$$

Thus SSB transmits **half the power of DSB-SC** for the same carrier amplitude and message — a major power saving, in addition to halving the bandwidth.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — SSB Power:** A common error is to compute SSB power using the **conventional AM** formula (with $\mu$). Since SSB has **no carrier**, the correct reference is **DSB-SC** with **one** sideband surviving, giving $P_{SSB} = A_c^{\,2}/(4R)$ per sideband. Do not mix up AM, DSB-SC, and SSB power formulas in the same problem.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Frequency Shifting Property:** $x(t)\,e^{j\omega_0 t} \leftrightarrow X\bigl(j(\omega - \omega_0)\bigr)$ — memorize in both directions.
- **Modulation = Multiplication in Time = Convolution in Frequency = Spectral Shift.**
- **Real carrier modulation by $\cos(\omega_c t)$:** produces **two sidebands** (USB at $+\omega_c$, LSB at $-\omega_c$), each scaled by $A_c/2$.
- **Conventional AM (with carrier):** $s(t) = A_c[1 + \mu m(t)]\cos(\omega_c t)$ — contains carrier impulses plus two sidebands.
- **DSB-SC:** $s(t) = A_c m(t)\cos(\omega_c t)$ — no carrier impulses, but both sidebands present.
- **SSB:** only USB or only LSB — bandwidth is **half** of DSB-SC.
- **Modulation Index:** $\mu = A_m/A_c$. Valid range for distortion-free conventional AM: $\mathbf{0 \le \mu \le 1}$.
- **Overmodulation:** $\mu > 1$ causes envelope distortion (carrier phase reversal) — **must be avoided**.
- **Bandwidth Rule:** $BW_{AM} = BW_{DSB-SC} = 2W$, $\quad BW_{SSB} = W$.
- **AM Power Formula:** $P_t = \dfrac{A_c^{\,2}}{2R}\left(1 + \dfrac{\mu^2}{2}\right)$.
- **Efficiency:** $\eta = \dfrac{\mu^2}{2 + \mu^2} \times 100\%$, maximum $33.33\%$ at $\mu=1$.
- **Demodulation:**
  - Envelope Detector → conventional AM (cheap, no synchronization needed).
  - Synchronous (Coherent) Detector → DSB-SC, SSB (needs phase-locked carrier at receiver).
- **Units Vigilance:** $\omega$ (rad/s) vs $f$ (Hz): $\omega = 2\pi f$. Always verify.
- **KTU Common Mistakes:**
  1. Forgetting $1/2$ in cosine product: $A\cos\theta\cos\phi = \frac{A}{2}[\cos(\theta-\phi) + \cos(\theta+\phi)]$.
  2. Forgetting load resistance $R$ in power formulas.
  3. Confusing the message frequency $f_m$ with the carrier frequency $f_c$ in bandwidth calculation.
  4. Not stating the modulation index validity condition $0 \le \mu \le 1$.
  5. Drawing one-sided spectrum when the problem demands the two-sided spectrum (always sketch both $\pm\omega_c$).
- **Spectral Sketch Always Required:** KTU expects a clear, labeled spectrum sketch showing baseband, shifted sidebands, and carrier impulses (for AM) — failing to draw the sketch costs **at least 2 marks** in Part B.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the framework of continuous-time and discrete-time signals and systems, **Time Reversal** (also termed **Reflection** or **Folding**) is a fundamental signal transformation operation in which the independent variable (typically time, denoted by $t$ or $n$) is replaced by its negative. The operation produces a mirror image of the original signal about the vertical axis (the amplitude axis) corresponding to the origin.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition (Module 1):**
> The time-reversed (reflected) version of a continuous-time signal $x(t)$ is defined as a new signal $y(t)$ such that:
> $$y(t) = x(-t)$$
> For discrete-time signals, the analogous operation yields $y[n] = x[-n]$.
> This operation is classified under **elementary signal transformations** along with time-shifting, time-scaling, and amplitude-scaling.

Mathematically, the reflection operator $R$ acts on a signal as:
$$R\{x(t)\} = x(-t)$$
and is a non-causal, invertible, linear transformation.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Mirror of Time:**
> Imagine you are watching a movie of a ball being thrown upward and then falling back to the ground. If you **reverse the film reel** and play it backward, the ball appears to start from the ground and rise. This is precisely *time reversal* — the entire trajectory is mirrored about the instant when the ball was at its peak (the time axis origin $t = 0$).
> - The **shape** of the motion remains identical.
> - The **direction** of progression is reversed (past becomes future, future becomes past).
> - The **amplitude values** at corresponding mirrored instants remain the same.

**Plain-English Explanation for First-Time Learners:**
- Take any signal plot drawn on a graph paper with time on the horizontal axis and amplitude on the vertical axis.
- **Fold** the paper along the vertical (amplitude) axis.
- The image you see on the left side is the time-reversed version of what was on the right side.
- Every point originally at $t = t_0$ now appears at $t = -t_0$, with the **same amplitude**.

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Time reversal is one of the four primitive signal operations (the others being time-scaling, time-shifting, and amplitude-scaling) that students must master before tackling Linear Time-Invariant (LTI) systems, convolution, and Fourier analysis in subsequent modules of **PECST416**.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Properties

- **Dimensionality:** Time reversal preserves the units of the independent variable (e.g., *seconds*, *samples*).
- **Energy Invariance:** The total energy of the signal is **conserved** under time reversal:
  $$E_y = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(-t) \vert^{2}\,dt = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(\tau) \vert^{2}\,d\tau = E_x$$
  where the substitution $\tau = -t$ has been used.
- **Power Invariance:** Average power is similarly **preserved**.
- **Memory Requirement:** The operation is **non-causal** in real-time processing because generating $x(-t)$ for $t > 0$ requires knowledge of future samples $x(t)$ for $t > 0$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

The time-reversal transformation can be decomposed into a clear sequence of logical operations:

1. **Identify the Original Signal:** Start with the input signal $x(t)$ defined over its support $[t_1, t_2]$.
2. **Replace the Independent Variable:** Substitute $t \rightarrow -t$ in the functional form, yielding $x(-t)$.
3. **Mirror the Support Interval:** The new support becomes $[-t_2, -t_1]$. The interval is **flipped and negated**.
4. **Preserve Amplitude Values:** Every amplitude value at the original instant $t_0$ is now observed at the mirrored instant $-t_0$.
5. **Verify Signal Properties:** Test for even/odd symmetry, periodicity preservation, and energy conservation.

### 2.1.1 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why mirror the support?** Because the mapping $t \mapsto -t$ is a bijection that reverses the order of real numbers.
- **Why preserve amplitudes?** Because reflection is purely a transformation of the *time index*, not the *signal value*.
- **Why is it non-causal?** Because at output time $t = t_0 > 0$, we need the input value at $x(-t_0)$, which lies in the future of the original signal.

## 2.2 Algebraic Properties of Time Reversal

The time-reversal operator interacts with other signal transformations in a well-defined algebraic manner:

| Combined Operation | Result | Algebraic Identity |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Time Reversal of a Shifted Signal | $x(t - t_0) \xrightarrow{R} x(-t - t_0)$ | $R\{x(t - t_0)\} = x(-(t - t_0)) = x(-t + t_0)$ |
| Time Reversal then Time Shift | $x(t) \xrightarrow{R} x(-t) \xrightarrow{\text{shift by } t_0} x(-(t - t_0)) = x(-t + t_0)$ | Reversal and shift **commute in a sign-flipped manner** |
| Time Shift then Time Reversal | $x(t) \xrightarrow{\text{shift by } t_0} x(t - t_0) \xrightarrow{R} x(-t - t_0)$ | Order of operations **matters** |
| Time Reversal and Time Scaling | $x(at) \xrightarrow{R} x(-at)$ | Composed into a single scaling with negative factor $-a$ |
| Time Reversal and Amplitude Scaling | $A \cdot x(t) \xrightarrow{R} A \cdot x(-t)$ | Linearity preserved: $R\{A \cdot x(t)\} = A \cdot R\{x(t)\}$ |
| Double Time Reversal | $x(t) \xrightarrow{R} x(-t) \xrightarrow{R} x(-(-t)) = x(t)$ | $R \circ R = I$ (Identity operator) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction:** Time reversal is **NOT commutative** with time shifting in the simple sense. The expression $x(-t + t_0)$ is **not** the same as $x(-(t - t_0)) = x(-t + t_0)$. Wait — in fact, they **are equal**! The careful convention is: *time reversal followed by a shift of $t_0$* gives $x(-(t - t_0)) = x(-t + t_0)$, while *time shift of $t_0$ followed by time reversal* gives $x(-t - t_0)$. The sign of the shift changes.

## 2.3 Symmetry Classification of Signals

Time reversal plays a central role in defining signal symmetry:

### 2.3.1 Even Signals
A signal $x(t)$ is **even** if and only if:
$$x(-t) = x(t) \quad \forall \, t$$
Examples: $x(t) = \cos(\omega_0 t)$, $x(t) = e^{-\vert t \vert}$, $x(t) = t^{2}$.

### 2.3.2 Odd Signals
A signal $x(t)$ is **odd** if and only if:
$$x(-t) = -x(t) \quad \forall \, t$$
Examples: $x(t) = \sin(\omega_0 t)$, $x(t) = t^{3}$, $x(t) = t \cdot e^{-\vert t \vert}$.

### 2.3.3 General Decomposition
**Any** signal $x(t)$ can be uniquely decomposed into its even and odd parts:
$$x(t) = x_e(t) + x_o(t)$$
where:
$$x_e(t) = \frac{x(t) + x(-t)}{2}, \quad x_o(t) = \frac{x(t) - x(-t)}{2}$$

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **all essential formulas** for time-reversal problems frequently tested in KTU 2024 Scheme examinations for **PECST416**.

| # | Formula / Identity | Description | Domain |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $y(t) = x(-t)$ | Fundamental time-reversal definition | Continuous-time |
| 2 | $y[n] = x[-n]$ | Discrete-time time-reversal definition | Discrete-time |
| 3 | $R\{x(t)\} = x(-t)$ | Reflection operator notation | Operator algebra |
| 4 | $R \circ R = I$ | Double reflection yields original | Operator property |
| 5 | $R\{A x(t) + B y(t)\} = A x(-t) + B y(-t)$ | Linearity of reversal | LTI prerequisite |
| 6 | $E_{x(-t)} = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(-t) \vert^{2} dt = E_{x(t)}$ | Energy invariance | Energy signals |
| 7 | $P_{x(-t)} = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{T} \vert x(-t) \vert^{2} dt = P_{x(t)}$ | Power invariance | Power signals |
| 8 | $x_e(t) = \frac{x(t) + x(-t)}{2}$ | Even-part extraction | Symmetry analysis |
| 9 | $x_o(t) = \frac{x(t) - x(-t)}{2}$ | Odd-part extraction | Symmetry analysis |
| 10 | $R\{x(t - t_0)\} = x(-t - t_0)$ | Reversal of shifted signal | Combined operations |
| 11 | $R\{x(t)\} \text{ at } t = t_0 \text{ equals } x(-t_0)$ | Instantaneous value mapping | Graphical reading |
| 12 | Support $[t_1, t_2] \xrightarrow{R} [-t_2, -t_1]$ | Interval mapping rule | Support transformation |
| 13 | $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) dt = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(-t) d\tau$ | Area invariance under reversal | Integration property |
| 14 | $\mathcal{F}\{x(-t)\} = X(-\omega)$ | Fourier transform of reversed signal | Spectral analysis |
| 15 | $R\{x(at)\} = x(-at)$ | Reversal-scaling composition | Combined operations |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Time reversal underpins the design of **matched filters** in radar/sonar systems, **deconvolution** algorithms in geophysics, **image mirroring** in digital signal processing, and the foundational concept of **two-sided Laplace transforms** used in control systems analysis.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Energy Invariance Under Time Reversal

**Statement:** The total energy of a signal $x(t)$ is unchanged by the time-reversal operation.

### 3.1.1 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Write the energy of the reversed signal.**
The energy of $y(t) = x(-t)$ is defined as:
$$E_y = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert y(t) \vert^{2}\,dt$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the definition of $y(t)$.**
$$E_y = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(-t) \vert^{2}\,dt$$

**Step 3 — Apply the substitution $\tau = -t$.**
This gives $d\tau = -dt$, hence $dt = -d\tau$.
When $t \to -\infty$, then $\tau \to +\infty$.
When $t \to +\infty$, then $\tau \to -\infty$.

**Step 4 — Rewrite the integral with the new limits.**
$$E_y = \int_{+\infty}^{-\infty} \vert x(\tau) \vert^{2}\,(-d\tau)$$

**Step 5 — Swap the limits to remove the negative sign.**
Swapping the limits of integration introduces a negative sign:
$$E_y = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert x(\tau) \vert^{2}\,d\tau$$

**Step 6 — Recognize the original energy.**
This is precisely the energy of the original signal:
$$E_y = E_x$$

**Conclusion:** Energy is invariant under time reversal. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Even-Odd Decomposition of an Arbitrary Signal

**Statement:** Any real or complex signal $x(t)$ can be uniquely written as the sum of an even part $x_e(t)$ and an odd part $x_o(t)$.

### 3.2.1 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Write the time-reversed signal.**
$$x(-t)$$

**Step 2 — Form the sum $x(t) + x(-t)$.**
$$x(t) + x(-t)$$

**Step 3 — Check the symmetry of the sum.**
Replace $t$ with $-t$:
$$x(-t) + x(-(-t)) = x(-t) + x(t) = x(t) + x(-t)$$
The sum is unchanged, so the sum is **even**.

**Step 4 — Form the difference $x(t) - x(-t)$.**
$$x(t) - x(-t)$$

**Step 5 — Check the symmetry of the difference.**
Replace $t$ with $-t$:
$$x(-t) - x(-(-t)) = x(-t) - x(t) = -(x(t) - x(-t))$$
The difference flips sign, so the difference is **odd**.

**Step 6 — Define the even and odd parts via normalization.**
We want $x(t) = x_e(t) + x_o(t)$ and $x(-t) = x_e(t) - x_o(t)$ (since even parts are invariant and odd parts flip sign under reversal). Solving this linear system:
$$\begin{aligned} x_e(t) &= \frac{1}{2}\bigl[x(t) + x(-t)\bigr] \\[4pt] x_o(t) &= \frac{1}{2}\bigl[x(t) - x(-t)\bigr] \end{aligned}$$

**Step 7 — Verify by reconstruction.**
$$\begin{aligned} x_e(t) + x_o(t) &= \frac{1}{2}\bigl[x(t) + x(-t)\bigr] + \frac{1}{2}\bigl[x(t) - x(-t)\bigr] \\[4pt] &= \frac{1}{2}\bigl[2 x(t)\bigr] = x(t) \end{aligned}$$

**Conclusion:** The decomposition is verified. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example: Rectangular Pulse Under Time Reversal

**Problem:** Consider a rectangular pulse:
$$x(t) = \begin{cases} 1, & 1 \le t \le 3 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

Determine the time-reversed signal $y(t) = x(-t)$ and its support.

### 3.3.1 Analytical Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the support of $x(t)$.**
The signal $x(t)$ is non-zero on the interval $[1, 3]$.

**Step 2 — Apply the time-reversal definition.**
By definition, $y(t) = x(-t)$. This means we evaluate the original function at the point $-t$ instead of $t$.

**Step 3 — Determine when $x(-t) = 1$.**
We require $-t \in [1, 3]$ for $y(t)$ to equal $1$.
$$1 \le -t \le 3$$

**Step 4 — Solve the inequality for $t$.**
Multiplying by $-1$ and flipping the inequalities:
$$-3 \le t \le -1$$

**Step 5 — Write the final piecewise expression.**
$$y(t) = x(-t) = \begin{cases} 1, & -3 \le t \le -1 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Step 6 — Verify the support transformation rule.**
Original support $[1, 3]$ is transformed to $[-3, -1]$, which matches the rule $[t_1, t_2] \to [-t_2, -t_1] = [-3, -1]$. ✓

**Step 7 — Compute the energy of both signals for verification.**
$$E_x = \int_{1}^{3} 1^{2}\,dt = 2$$
$$E_y = \int_{-3}^{-1} 1^{2}\,dt = 2$$
Energy is preserved, as expected. ✓

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Time-Reversal of Common Test Signals

| Original Signal $x(t)$ | Definition | Time-Reversed $x(-t)$ | Support Shift | New Support |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Rectangular pulse | $1$ for $0 \le t \le 1$ | $1$ for $-1 \le t \le 0$ | Symmetric about $0$ | $[-1, 0]$ |
| Triangular pulse | $t$ for $0 \le t \le 1$ | $-t$ for $-1 \le t \le 0$ | Apex at $t = 0$ | $[-1, 0]$ |
| Exponential decay | $e^{-t} u(t)$ | $e^{t} u(-t)$ | Causal to anti-causal | $(-\infty, 0]$ |
| Sinusoid | $\sin(\omega_0 t)$ | $-\sin(\omega_0 t) = \sin(-\omega_0 t)$ | Odd symmetry | All $t$ |
| Cosine | $\cos(\omega_0 t)$ | $\cos(\omega_0 t)$ | Even symmetry | All $t$ |
| Ramp | $t \cdot u(t)$ | $-t \cdot u(-t)$ | Causal ramp to anti-causal | $(-\infty, 0]$ |
| Unit step | $u(t)$ | $u(-t)$ | Future step to past step | $(-\infty, 0]$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** A particularly common KTU question asks students to verify that $\sin(\omega_0 t)$ is **odd** by showing $x(-t) = -x(t)$. The substitution $\sin(-(-\omega_0 t)) = -\sin(\omega_0 t)$ is a one-line elegant proof.

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Block Diagram: Time-Reversal Operator

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the operational flow of a time-reversal system as implemented in digital signal processing hardware.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputA["Input Signal x of t"] --> memoryA["Storage Buffer"]
    memoryA --> readA["Reverse Read Order t to minus t"]
    readA --> outputA["Output Signal y of t equals x of minus t"]
    outputA --> verifyA["Energy and Symmetry Check"]
    verifyA --> resultA["Final Reversed Signal"]

    style inputA fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0277bd
    style memoryA fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style readA fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100
    style outputA fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32
    style verifyA fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#ad1457
    style resultA fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#4527a0
```

**Interpretation:**
- The **input signal** is first stored in a buffer (essential because time reversal is non-causal).
- The buffer is read in **reverse order** (last sample first).
- The **output** is the mirrored version of the input.
- The final stage performs **verification** through energy/symmetry checks.

---

The diagram below shows the systematic procedure for graphically time-reversing a signal, mapping each conceptual step to a visualization phase.

    plotX --> reflectX["Stage 3: Mirror about vertical axis at t equals 0"]
    reflectX --> shiftX["Stage 4: Read new amplitudes at negated time indices"]
    shiftX --> finalX["Stage 5: Final Signal x of minus t"]

## 4.3 Functional Architecture: Time Reversal in LTI System Context

The following architecture diagram shows how time reversal integrates with an LTI system — a foundational Module 2 concept, but introduced here for completeness.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Continuous Time Signal x of t"] --> nodeB["Time Reversal Block R"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Reversed Signal x of minus t"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["LTI System h of t"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Output y of t"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["Analysis: y of t equals integral x of minus tau h of t minus tau d tau"]

    style nodeA fill:#e1f5fe,stroke:#01579b
    style nodeB fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17
    style nodeC fill:#ffe082,stroke:#ff8f00
    style nodeD fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#1b5e20
    style nodeE fill:#80cbc4,stroke:#004d40
    style nodeF fill:#ce93d8,stroke:#4a148c
```

**Key Insight:** When a time-reversed signal is passed through an LTI system with impulse response $h(t)$, the output is the convolution:
$$y(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(-\tau) h(t - \tau) d\tau$$
This integral is the mathematical basis for understanding **deconvolution** and **inverse filtering** in later modules.

---

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define the time-reversal operation for a continuous-time signal $x(t)$. How does the operation affect the energy of the signal? Justify mathematically.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** The time-reversal (or reflection) of a continuous-time signal $x(t)$ is a new signal $y(t)$ defined by:
$$y(t) = x(-t)$$
This operation mirrors the original signal about the vertical (amplitude) axis, i.e., about $t = 0$.

**Effect on Energy (2 Marks):** The energy of a signal is invariant under time reversal. To prove this, consider:
$$E_y = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(-t) \vert^{2} dt$$
Substituting $\tau = -t$, $d\tau = -dt$, with the limits swapping from $(-\infty, \infty)$ to $(+\infty, -\infty)$:
$$E_y = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(\tau) \vert^{2} d\tau = E_x$$
Hence $E_y = E_x$. The energy is unchanged. $\blacksquare$

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Determine whether the signal $x(t) = t \cdot e^{-t^{2}}$ is even, odd, or neither. Show the relevant steps.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** Compute $x(-t)$:
$$x(-t) = (-t) \cdot e^{-(-t)^{2}} = -t \cdot e^{-t^{2}}$$

**Step 2 (1 Mark):** Compare $x(-t)$ with $x(t)$:
$$x(-t) = -t \cdot e^{-t^{2}} = -x(t)$$

**Step 3 (1 Mark):** Since $x(-t) = -x(t)$ for all $t$, the signal is **odd**.

---

## 5.2 Part B: ESE Module Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Consider the discrete-time signal $x[n] = \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$ defined for $n = 0, 1, 2, 3$. Perform the time-reversal operation to obtain $y[n] = x[-n]$ and plot both signals. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A continuous-time signal is defined as:
$$x(t) = \begin{cases} t + 1, & 0 \le t \le 2 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$
Find its time-reversed version $y(t) = x(-t)$, sketch both $x(t)$ and $y(t)$, and verify that the total energy is preserved. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a)

**Step 1 — Write the original signal with explicit indices (1 Mark):**
$$x[0] = 1, \quad x[1] = 2, \quad x[2] = 3, \quad x[3] = 4$$

**Step 2 — Apply the time-reversal definition $y[n] = x[-n]$ (2 Marks):**
For each value of $n$, we need to find $-n$ in the original support $\{0, 1, 2, 3\}$:
- $y[0] = x[-0] = x[0] = 1$
- $y[1] = x[-1]$. Since $-1$ is not in the original support, $y[1] = 0$.
- $y[-1] = x[1] = 2$
- $y[-2] = x[2] = 3$
- $y[-3] = x[3] = 4$

**Step 3 — Write the reversed signal in standard form (2 Marks):**
$$y[n] = \begin{cases} 4, & n = -3 \\ 3, & n = -2 \\ 2, & n = -1 \\ 1, & n = 0 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$
Or in arrow notation: $y[n] = \{\underset{n=-3}{4}, \underset{n=-2}{3}, \underset{n=-1}{2}, \underset{n=0}{1}\}$.

**Step 4 — Sketch (2 Marks):** Two stem plots — one for $x[n]$ at $n = 0, 1, 2, 3$ and one for $y[n]$ at $n = -3, -2, -1, 0$. The reversed signal is a mirror image of the original about the $y$-axis.

#### Model Solution for Part (b)

**Step 1 — Identify the support of $x(t)$ (1 Mark):**
The signal is non-zero on $[0, 2]$ and is a ramp with slope $1$ and $y$-intercept $1$.

**Step 2 — Apply $y(t) = x(-t)$ (2 Marks):**
We need $x(-t) \neq 0$ when $-t \in [0, 2]$, i.e., when $-2 \le t \le 0$.
For $t \in [-2, 0]$, we have $-t \in [0, 2]$, so:
$$y(t) = x(-t) = (-t) + 1 = 1 - t$$

**Step 3 — Write the final piecewise expression (1 Mark):**
$$y(t) = \begin{cases} 1 - t, & -2 \le t \le 0 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Step 4 — Sketch both signals (1 Mark):**
- $x(t)$: A ramp rising from $(0, 1)$ to $(2, 3)$ on the positive time axis.
- $y(t)$: A ramp descending from $(-2, 3)$ to $(0, 1)$ on the negative time axis.
- The two are mirror images about the vertical axis.

**Step 5 — Verify energy conservation (2 Marks):**
$$\begin{aligned} E_x &= \int_{0}^{2} (t + 1)^{2}\,dt \\ &= \left[\frac{(t+1)^{3}}{3}\right]_{0}^{2} = \frac{27}{3} - \frac{1}{3} = \frac{26}{3} \end{aligned}$$
$$\begin{aligned} E_y &= \int_{-2}^{0} (1 - t)^{2}\,dt \\ \text{Let } u &= 1 - t, \quad du = -dt. \text{ When } t = -2, u = 3; \text{ when } t = 0, u = 1. \\ &= \int_{3}^{1} u^{2} (-du) = \int_{1}^{3} u^{2}\,du = \left[\frac{u^{3}}{3}\right]_{1}^{3} = \frac{27}{3} - \frac{1}{3} = \frac{26}{3} \end{aligned}$$
Since $E_x = E_y = \frac{26}{3}$, energy is preserved. $\blacksquare$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** For the signal $x(t) = 2 \cos(3t) + \sin(2t)$, determine the even and odd components using the time-reversal decomposition formulas. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A rectangular pulse is given by:
$$x(t) = \begin{cases} A, & 2 \le t \le 5 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$
Sketch $x(t)$, $x(-t)$, $x(t - 2)$, and $x(-(t - 2))$ on separate axes. Comment on the difference between the time-reversed-then-shifted and shifted-then-time-reversed versions. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a)

**Step 1 — Compute $x(-t)$ (1 Mark):**
$$x(-t) = 2 \cos(-3t) + \sin(-2t) = 2 \cos(3t) - \sin(2t)$$
(Using $\cos(-\theta) = \cos(\theta)$ and $\sin(-\theta) = -\sin(\theta)$.)

**Step 2 — Apply the even-part formula (2 Marks):**
$$\begin{aligned} x_e(t) &= \frac{x(t) + x(-t)}{2} \\ &= \frac{[2 \cos(3t) + \sin(2t)] + [2 \cos(3t) - \sin(2t)]}{2} \\ &= \frac{4 \cos(3t)}{2} = 2 \cos(3t) \end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the odd-part formula (2 Marks):**
$$\begin{aligned} x_o(t) &= \frac{x(t) - x(-t)}{2} \\ &= \frac{[2 \cos(3t) + \sin(2t)] - [2 \cos(3t) - \sin(2t)]}{2} \\ &= \frac{2 \sin(2t)}{2} = \sin(2t) \end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Verify the decomposition (1 Mark):**
$$x_e(t) + x_o(t) = 2 \cos(3t) + \sin(2t) = x(t) \checkmark$$

**Step 5 — Verify symmetry (1 Mark):**
- $x_e(-t) = 2 \cos(-3t) = 2 \cos(3t) = x_e(t)$ (even) ✓
- $x_o(-t) = \sin(-2t) = -\sin(2t) = -x_o(t)$ (odd) ✓

---

#### Model Solution for Part (b)

**Step 1 — Sketch $x(t)$ (1 Mark):**
A rectangular pulse of amplitude $A$ from $t = 2$ to $t = 5$.

**Step 2 — Sketch $x(-t)$ (1 Mark):**
Mirror image of $x(t)$ about $t = 0$. The pulse now lies in $[-5, -2]$.

**Step 3 — Sketch $x(t - 2)$ (1 Mark):**
Right-shift of $x(t)$ by $2$ units. The pulse now lies in $[4, 7]$.

**Step 4 — Sketch $x(-(t - 2))$ (1 Mark):**
$$\begin{aligned} x(-(t - 2)) &= x(-t + 2) \end{aligned}$$
This is $x(-t)$ shifted right by $2$ units, OR equivalently $x(t - 2)$ reflected about $t = 0$. The pulse now lies in $[-3, 0]$.

**Step 5 — Compute the alternative — shift then reverse (1 Mark):**
$$\begin{aligned} x(t) &\xrightarrow{\text{shift by } +2} x(t - 2), \text{ support: } [4, 7] \\ x(t - 2) &\xrightarrow{R} x(-(t - 2)) \text{ applied: substitute } t \to -t \\ &= x(-t - 2), \text{ support: } [-7, -4] \end{aligned}$$
Wait — careful: applying time reversal $R$ to $x(t-2)$ gives $x(-(t) - 2) = x(-t-2)$, whose support is $[-7, -4]$. This is **different** from $x(-(t-2)) = x(-t+2)$ whose support is $[-3, 0]$.

**Step 6 — Comment on the difference (2 Marks):**
The two operations — *shift then reverse* versus *reverse then shift* — yield **different** signals:
- **Reverse then shift** $x(-t) \to x(-t + 2) = x(-(t-2))$: support $[-3, 0]$.
- **Shift then reverse** $x(t-2) \to x(-t - 2)$: support $[-7, -4]$.

This illustrates that time reversal and time shifting **do not commute** in the naive sense; the sign of the shift parameter changes depending on the order of operations.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Sign Error in Shift-Reverse Composition:** Many students write $x(-(t - t_0)) = x(-t - t_0)$ instead of the correct $x(-(t - t_0)) = x(-t + t_0)$. The minus sign is **inside** the parenthesis and distributes to both terms. **Always expand the parenthesis first.**
> 2. **Forgetting to Swap Integration Limits:** When proving energy/power invariance via substitution $\tau = -t$, students often forget to swap the limits. This leads to an erroneous negative sign in the final answer and a full loss of 2 marks.
> 3. **Confusing Reflection with Inversion:** Reflection ($x(-t)$) is **not** the same as amplitude inversion ($-x(t)$). Reflection mirrors the **time axis**; inversion flips the **amplitude axis**.
> 4. **Misidentifying Support Intervals:** The support $[t_1, t_2]$ transforms to $[-t_2, -t_1]$, **not** to $[-t_1, -t_2]$. Reversing the order of the endpoints is a frequent error.
> 5. **Treating Time Reversal as Causal:** In real-time systems, time reversal is **non-causal** and requires buffer storage. Students who claim "we can simply reverse the signal in real-time" lose marks for misunderstanding causality.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Time Reversal (Reflection):**
> - **Definition:** $y(t) = x(-t)$ (CT) or $y[n] = x[-n]$ (DT).
> - **Graphical Operation:** Mirror image about the vertical (amplitude) axis at $t = 0$.
> - **Support Mapping:** Interval $[t_1, t_2] \longrightarrow [-t_2, -t_1]$.
> - **Energy Invariance:** $E_{x(-t)} = E_{x(t)}$ — proven by substitution $\tau = -t$.
> - **Power Invariance:** $P_{x(-t)} = P_{x(t)}$.
> - **Linearity:** $R\{A x(t) + B y(t)\} = A x(-t) + B y(-t)$.
> - **Involution Property:** $R\{R\{x(t)\}\} = x(t)$ (double reversal = identity).
> - **Even Signal Test:** $x(-t) = x(t)$.
> - **Odd Signal Test:** $x(-t) = -x(t)$.
> - **Even-Odd Decomposition:**
>   - $x_e(t) = \frac{1}{2}[x(t) + x(-t)]$
>   - $x_o(t) = \frac{1}{2}[x(t) - x(-t)]$
> - **Combined with Time Shift:**
>   - $R\{x(t - t_0)\} = x(-t - t_0)$ — shift then reverse.
>   - Reverse then shift by $t_0$: $x(-(t - t_0)) = x(-t + t_0)$.
> - **Combined with Time Scaling:** $R\{x(at)\} = x(-at)$.
> - **Non-Causality:** Generating $x(-t)$ at $t > 0$ requires future samples; needs a buffer.
> - **Fourier Transform Pair:** $\mathcal{F}\{x(-t)\} = X(-j\omega)$, i.e., the spectrum is also reflected about $\omega = 0$.
> - **Common Test Signals:** $\cos(\omega_0 t)$ is even, $\sin(\omega_0 t)$ is odd, $e^{-at}u(t)$ becomes anti-causal $e^{at}u(-t)$ after reversal.
> - **Engineering Applications:** Matched filtering, deconvolution, image mirroring, two-sided Laplace analysis, radar/sonar signal processing.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Conjugation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST416 - SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Conjugation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

In **Signals and Systems**, **Conjugation** refers to the **Complex Conjugate** operation applied to a continuous-time or discrete-time signal. For a complex-valued signal $x(t)$, its complex conjugate is denoted as $x^*(t)$ (or sometimes $x^c(t)$) and is obtained by replacing every complex coefficient $j$ (where $j = \sqrt{-1}$) with $-j$, while keeping the real part unchanged.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PECST416 Module 1):**
> The complex conjugate of a signal $x(t)$, written as $x^*(t)$, is the signal formed by taking the complex conjugate of the signal's value at every instant $t$. If $x(t) = a(t) + j\,b(t)$, then $x^*(t) = a(t) - j\,b(t)$, where $a(t)$ and $b(t)$ are real-valued signals.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Mirror on the Imaginary Axis":**
> Imagine a complex signal as a 2D vector hovering above the time axis. The **real part** is the shadow cast on the ground (the horizontal floor), while the **imaginary part** is the height of the vector above the ground. Complex conjugation is like a **mirror placed horizontally along the real axis** — it flips the height (imaginary part) upside down but leaves the horizontal position (real part) completely untouched.

**Key Observations:**
- If $x(t)$ is purely **real** (i.e., $b(t) = 0$), then $x^*(t) = x(t)$. A real signal is its own conjugate.
- If $x(t)$ is purely **imaginary** (i.e., $a(t) = 0$), then $x^*(t) = -x(t)$. The conjugate negates the entire signal.
- **Magnitude** is preserved: $\vert x^*(t) \vert = \vert x(t) \vert$.
- **Phase** is negated: $\angle x^*(t) = -\angle x(t)$.

### Standard Notation Used in KTU Boards

| Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $x^*(t)$ | Complex conjugate of $x(t)$ |
| $x^*(-t)$ | Conjugate and time-reversed signal |
| $x^{H}(t)$ | Conjugate transpose (Hermitian) — used in vector contexts |
| $\Re\{x(t)\}$ | Real part of $x(t)$ |
| $\Im\{x(t)\}$ | Imaginary part of $x(t)$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### Fundamental Properties of Conjugation

1. **Double Conjugation (Involution Property):** Applying conjugation twice returns the original signal.
2. **Distributive over Addition:** The conjugate of a sum equals the sum of conjugates.
3. **Multiplicative with Scalar:** The conjugate of a product of scalar and signal equals the product of conjugate-scalar and signal.
4. **Conjugate of a Product:** The conjugate of a product equals the product of conjugates (order may reverse).
5. **Conjugate Symmetry of Power Spectral Density:** A foundational property used in spectral analysis.

### Conjugate Symmetric and Conjugate Antisymmetric Signals

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **most heavily tested sub-topic** on Conjugation in KTU exams. A student MUST master the decomposition theorem.

A signal $x(t)$ is called **Conjugate Symmetric (Even)** if:
$$x(t) = x^*(-t)$$

A signal $x(t)$ is called **Conjugate Antisymmetric (Odd)** if:
$$x(t) = -x^*(-t)$$

The **Decomposition Theorem** states that ANY complex signal can be uniquely expressed as:
$$x(t) = x_e(t) + x_o(t)$$

where $x_e(t)$ is the conjugate-symmetric (even) part and $x_o(t)$ is the conjugate-antisymmetric (odd) part, given by:

$$x_e(t) = \frac{1}{2}\left[x(t) + x^*(-t)\right]$$

$$x_o(t) = \frac{1}{2}\left[x(t) - x^*(-t)\right]$$

### KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Conjugation)

| Property | Mathematical Expression | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|
| Basic Definition | $x(t) = a(t) + jb(t) \Rightarrow x^*(t) = a(t) - jb(t)$ | Foundation of complex signal theory |
| Double Conjugate | $\left(x^*(t)\right)^* = x(t)$ | Simplification of nested expressions |
| Conjugate Sum | $(x(t) + y(t))^* = x^*(t) + y^*(t)$ | Linearity in spectral operations |
| Conjugate Product | $(x(t) \cdot y(t))^* = x^*(t) \cdot y^*(t)$ | Used in inner product calculations |
| Magnitude Invariance | $\vert x^*(t) \vert = \vert x(t) \vert$ | Power and energy are real-valued |
| Conjugate Symmetric | $x(t) = x^*(-t)$ | Real part of Fourier transform of real signals |
| Conjugate Antisymmetric | $x(t) = -x^*(-t)$ | Imaginary part characterization |
| Even Part | $x_e(t) = \frac{1}{2}\left[x(t) + x^*(-t)\right]$ | Decomposition tool |
| Odd Part | $x_o(t) = \frac{1}{2}\left[x(t) - x^*(-t)\right]$ | Decomposition tool |
| Energy of $x(t)$ | $E = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^2 dt = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) \cdot x^*(t) \, dt$ | Inner product uses conjugation |

### Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Telecommunications:** Used in quadrature modulation (I/Q channels) where conjugation is required to extract in-phase and quadrature components.
- **Radar & Sonar:** Matched filters rely on conjugating the transmitted signal template for correlation.
- **MRI & Medical Imaging:** Conjugate symmetry of the $k$-space data enables efficient image reconstruction.
- **Audio Signal Processing:** Used in phase vocoders and time-stretching algorithms.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Derivation 1: Decomposition into Even and Odd Conjugate Parts

**Statement to Prove:** Any complex signal $x(t)$ can be uniquely written as the sum of a conjugate-symmetric part $x_e(t)$ and a conjugate-antisymmetric part $x_o(t)$.

**Step 1 — Assume decomposition exists.**
Let $x(t) = x_e(t) + x_o(t)$, where by definition:
$$x_e(t) = x_e^*(-t) \quad \text{and} \quad x_o(t) = -x_o^*(-t)$$

**Step 2 — Apply time reversal and conjugate to the decomposition.**
Substituting $t \to -t$ and taking the complex conjugate of both sides of $x(t) = x_e(t) + x_o(t)$:
$$x^*(-t) = x_e^*(-t) + x_o^*(-t)$$

**Step 3 — Apply the symmetry definitions.**
Using $x_e^*(-t) = x_e(t)$ and $x_o^*(-t) = -x_o(t)$:
$$x^*(-t) = x_e(t) - x_o(t)$$

**Step 4 — Form a system of two linear equations.**
We now have two equations:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x(t)   &= x_e(t) + x_o(t) \\
x^*(-t) &= x_e(t) - x_o(t)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve the system for $x_e(t)$ and $x_o(t)$.**
Adding the two equations eliminates $x_o(t)$:
$$x(t) + x^*(-t) = 2\,x_e(t)$$

$$\boxed{\,x_e(t) = \frac{1}{2}\left[x(t) + x^*(-t)\right]\,}$$

Subtracting the two equations eliminates $x_e(t)$:
$$x(t) - x^*(-t) = 2\,x_o(t)$$

$$\boxed{\,x_o(t) = \frac{1}{2}\left[x(t) - x^*(-t)\right]\,}$$

**Step 6 — Verify uniqueness.**
Substituting back into $x(t) = x_e(t) + x_o(t)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_e(t) + x_o(t) &= \tfrac{1}{2}[x(t) + x^*(-t)] + \tfrac{1}{2}[x(t) - x^*(-t)] \\
&= \tfrac{1}{2}[2x(t)] = x(t) \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

This proves the **Decomposition Theorem**.

---

### Derivation 2: Energy of a Complex Signal

**Statement:** The total energy $E$ of a complex signal $x(t) = a(t) + jb(t)$ equals $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t)\,x^*(t)\,dt$.

**Step 1 — Magnitude squared.**
For any complex number $z = a + jb$, we have $z \cdot z^* = (a+jb)(a-jb) = a^2 + b^2 = \vert z \vert^2$.

**Step 2 — Apply to signal $x(t)$.**
$$\vert x(t) \vert^2 = x(t) \cdot x^*(t)$$

**Step 3 — Integrate over all time to obtain energy.**
By definition of signal energy for a complex signal:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt \\
  &= \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) \cdot x^*(t) \, dt \\
  &= \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \left(a^2(t) + b^2(t)\right) dt
\end{aligned}
$$

This shows why **conjugation is essential** for energy calculations — it ensures the integrand is always a non-negative real number, making the energy a real, measurable physical quantity.

---

### Derivation 3: Real Signals are Conjugate Symmetric

**Statement:** If $x(t)$ is real, then $x(t) = x^*(-t)$, i.e., every real signal is conjugate symmetric.

**Proof:**
If $x(t)$ is real, then $x^*(t) = x(t)$ (conjugation has no effect on real signals). Therefore:
$$x^*(-t) = x(-t)$$

For a real signal, the **time-reversal property of even functions** does not automatically hold unless the signal is even. However, for the conjugate-symmetric condition $x(t) = x^*(-t)$, substituting $x^*(t) = x(t)$:
$$x(t) = x(-t)$$

This is the **standard even-signal condition**. So a real signal is conjugate symmetric **if and only if it is an even function** of time. A general real signal can be decomposed into real-even and real-odd parts via the standard even/odd decomposition theorem.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Question]**
*Define the complex conjugate of a signal. State any four properties of complex conjugation.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The complex conjugate of a signal $x(t)$, denoted $x^*(t)$, is obtained by replacing $j$ with $-j$ at every instant. If $x(t) = a(t) + jb(t)$, then $x^*(t) = a(t) - jb(t)$.

**Four Properties:**
1. **Involution:** $(x^*(t))^* = x(t)$.
2. **Distributive:** $(x(t) + y(t))^* = x^*(t) + y^*(t)$.
3. **Magnitude invariance:** $\vert x^*(t) \vert = \vert x(t) \vert$.
4. **Product rule:** $(x(t) \cdot y(t))^* = x^*(t) \cdot y^*(t)$.

*(1 mark for definition + 0.5 each for 4 properties = 3 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Question]**
*Differentiate between conjugate symmetric and conjugate antisymmetric signals with one example each.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | Conjugate Symmetric | Conjugate Antisymmetric |
|---|---|---|
| Condition | $x(t) = x^*(-t)$ | $x(t) = -x^*(-t)$ |
| Time reversal | Conjugate equals original | Negative of conjugate equals original |
| Real signal equivalent | Even function | Odd function |
| Example | $x(t) = \cos(t) + j\sin(t)$ | $x(t) = \sin(t) + j\,0$ is **not** antisymmetric, but $x(t) = j\sin(t)$ is conjugate antisymmetric |

**Example 1 (Symmetric):** $x(t) = e^{j\omega t}$, since $x^*(-t) = e^{j\omega t} = x(t)$.
**Example 2 (Antisymmetric):** $x(t) = j\sin(\omega t)$, since $x^*(-t) = -j\sin(\omega t) = -x(t)$.

---

### Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

#### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Question]**
**(a)** State and prove the decomposition theorem for complex signals into conjugate symmetric and conjugate antisymmetric parts. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For the signal $x(t) = e^{(2+j5)t} \cdot u(t)$, determine:
  (i) The complex conjugate $x^*(t)$.
  (ii) The conjugate symmetric part $x_e(t)$.
  (iii) The conjugate antisymmetric part $x_o(t)$.
  (iv) Verify that $x(t) = x_e(t) + x_o(t)$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Decomposition Theorem Proof (7 Marks):**

*Statement:* Any complex signal $x(t)$ can be uniquely written as $x(t) = x_e(t) + x_o(t)$, where $x_e(t)$ is conjugate symmetric and $x_o(t)$ is conjugate antisymmetric.

*Proof:*

Assume $x(t) = x_e(t) + x_o(t)$ with $x_e(t) = x_e^*(-t)$ and $x_o(t) = -x_o^*(-t)$.

Take complex conjugate and time reverse:
$$x^*(-t) = x_e^*(-t) + x_o^*(-t) = x_e(t) - x_o(t)$$

Form the system:
$$x(t) = x_e(t) + x_o(t)$$
$$x^*(-t) = x_e(t) - x_o(t)$$

Solving (add and subtract):

$$x_e(t) = \tfrac{1}{2}[x(t) + x^*(-t)], \quad x_o(t) = \tfrac{1}{2}[x(t) - x^*(-t)]$$

Verification:
$$x_e(t) + x_o(t) = \tfrac{1}{2}[x(t) + x^*(-t)] + \tfrac{1}{2}[x(t) - x^*(-t)] = x(t) \quad \blacksquare$$

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 Marks):**

Given: $x(t) = e^{(2+j5)t} \cdot u(t)$ where $u(t)$ is the unit step.

**(i) Complex conjugate:**
$$x^*(t) = e^{(2-j5)t} \cdot u(t) \quad \text{(since } u(t) \text{ is real)}$$

**(ii) Conjugate symmetric part:**
First compute $x^*(-t)$:
$$x^*(-t) = e^{(2-j5)(-t)} \cdot u(-t) = e^{(-2+j5)t} \cdot u(-t)$$

Now:
$$x_e(t) = \tfrac{1}{2}\left[e^{(2+j5)t}\,u(t) + e^{(-2+j5)t}\,u(-t)\right]$$

**(iii) Conjugate antisymmetric part:**
$$x_o(t) = \tfrac{1}{2}\left[e^{(2+j5)t}\,u(t) - e^{(-2+j5)t}\,u(-t)\right]$$

**(iv) Verification:**
$$x_e(t) + x_o(t) = \tfrac{1}{2}[2 e^{(2+j5)t}\,u(t)] = e^{(2+j5)t}\,u(t) = x(t) \quad \checkmark$$

---

#### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Question]**
**(a)** Explain the concept of complex conjugate in signals. Derive the relationship between the magnitude, phase, and the conjugate of a complex signal $x(t) = a(t) + jb(t)$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Given $x(t) = 3e^{j\pi t} + 2e^{-j\pi t}$:
  (i) Express $x(t)$ in rectangular form $a(t) + jb(t)$.
  (ii) Compute $x^*(t)$ and verify the involution property.
  (iii) Determine the conjugate symmetric part $x_e(t)$.
  (iv) Comment on whether $x(t)$ is purely real, purely imaginary, or neither. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Magnitude-Phase Relationship (7 Marks):**

The signal $x(t) = a(t) + jb(t)$ in polar form is:
$$x(t) = \vert x(t) \vert e^{j\phi(t)}$$

where $\vert x(t) \vert = \sqrt{a^2(t) + b^2(t)}$ and $\phi(t) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{b(t)}{a(t)}\right)$.

Taking the complex conjugate (replace $j$ with $-j$):
$$x^*(t) = a(t) - jb(t) = \vert x(t) \vert e^{-j\phi(t)}$$

**Derived relationships:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert x^*(t) \vert &= \sqrt{a^2(t) + (-b(t))^2} = \sqrt{a^2(t) + b^2(t)} = \vert x(t) \vert \\
\angle x^*(t) &= \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{-b(t)}{a(t)}\right) = -\phi(t) = -\angle x(t)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** Conjugation preserves magnitude but negates phase.

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 Marks):**

Given: $x(t) = 3e^{j\pi t} + 2e^{-j\pi t}$.

**(i) Rectangular form:** Using Euler's formula $e^{j\theta} = \cos\theta + j\sin\theta$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x(t) &= 3[\cos(\pi t) + j\sin(\pi t)] + 2[\cos(\pi t) - j\sin(\pi t)] \\
     &= (3+2)\cos(\pi t) + j(3-2)\sin(\pi t) \\
     &= 5\cos(\pi t) + j\sin(\pi t)
\end{aligned}
$$

So $a(t) = 5\cos(\pi t)$ and $b(t) = \sin(\pi t)$.

**(ii) Complex conjugate and involution:**
$$x^*(t) = 5\cos(\pi t) - j\sin(\pi t)$$

**Involution check:**
$$(x^*(t))^* = 5\cos(\pi t) + j\sin(\pi t) = x(t) \quad \checkmark$$

**(iii) Conjugate symmetric part:**
$$x^*(-t) = 5\cos(-\pi t) - j\sin(-\pi t) = 5\cos(\pi t) + j\sin(\pi t)$$

$$x_e(t) = \tfrac{1}{2}[x(t) + x^*(-t)] = \tfrac{1}{2}[2 \cdot 5\cos(\pi t) + 2j\sin(\pi t)] = 5\cos(\pi t) + j\sin(\pi t) = x(t)$$

**(iv) Comment:** Since $x(t) = x^*(-t)$, the signal is **conjugate symmetric**. It is **neither purely real nor purely imaginary** because both $a(t) = 5\cos(\pi t)$ and $b(t) = \sin(\pi t)$ are non-zero in general.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of $\frac{1}{2}$:** Students often write $x_e(t) = x(t) + x^*(-t)$ without dividing by 2. This loses 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Sign error in odd part:** The odd part formula is $x_o(t) = \tfrac{1}{2}[x(t) - x^*(-t)]$, NOT $+$. Reverse sign loses full marks for the part.
> 3. **Conjugating $j$ vs. $-j$:** Always replace $j$ with $-j$. Do not change real coefficients.
> 4. **Mixing up conjugate symmetric with even:** Conjugate symmetry uses $x^*(-t)$, not $x(-t)$ — this distinction matters for complex signals and is the most common KTU trap.
> 5. **Not simplifying exponential expressions:** Always express $e^{j\theta} + e^{-j\theta} = 2\cos\theta$ for cleaner KTU board answers.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ Complex conjugate of $x(t) = a(t) + jb(t)$ is $x^*(t) = a(t) - jb(t)$.
- ✅ Conjugation is **involutive**: $(x^*)^* = x$.
- ✅ Conjugation is **distributive** over addition and compatible with multiplication.
- ✅ $\vert x^* \vert = \vert x \vert$ (magnitude preserved) and $\angle x^* = -\angle x$ (phase negated).
- ✅ A signal is **conjugate symmetric** if $x(t) = x^*(-t)$.
- ✅ A signal is **conjugate antisymmetric** if $x(t) = -x^*(-t)$.
- ✅ **Decomposition formulas (MEMORIZE):**
   - $x_e(t) = \tfrac{1}{2}[x(t) + x^*(-t)]$
   - $x_o(t) = \tfrac{1}{2}[x(t) - x^*(-t)]$
- ✅ Any complex signal can be uniquely decomposed into conjugate-symmetric + conjugate-antisymmetric parts.
- ✅ Energy of a complex signal is $E = \int x(t) x^*(t)\, dt$, which is always **real and non-negative**.
- ✅ For a real signal: $x^*(t) = x(t)$ — conjugation is the identity operation.
- ✅ Conjugate symmetry of $X(j\omega)$ for real signals is the foundation of Fourier Transform properties.
- ✅ Applications: matched filtering, QAM modulation, MRI reconstruction, spectral analysis.
- ✅ **Board tip:** Always simplify final answers; never leave exponential forms unexpanded if a real/imaginary form is requested.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Duality
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST416 - SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Duality # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology) The **Duality Property** (also called the **Duality Theorem** or **Symmetry Property**) of the Continuous-Time Fourier Transform (CTFT) states a profound reciprocal relationship between the **time domain** and the **frequency domain**. > [!IMPORTANT] **Duality Theorem (CTFT)** > If $x(t) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} X(\omega)$ is a valid Fourier Transform pair, then by the duality property: > $$X(t) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} 2\pi \, x(-\omega)$$ > This means: *interchanging the roles of time and frequency* in a transform pair yields another valid Fourier Transform pair, with a corresponding reflection in the frequency variable and a scaling factor of **2π** (the fundamental period of the radian-frequency domain). In other words, the transform of a *time-domain function that looks like a spectrum* is *2π times the time-reversed version of the original time-domain signal*, now expressed as a function of frequency. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain English Intuition Think of the duality property like a **mirror between two rooms** — Room A (Time Domain) and Room B (Frequency Domain). Whatever shape you see in Room A on the wall, you will see an *almost identical but mirrored* shape in Room B on the opposite wall. - Imagine you have a **rectangular pulse** of width 1 second in time → its Fourier Transform is a **sinc function** in frequency. - Now flip the question: If you have a **rectangular pulse in the "time" axis** (treating it as if it were a frequency-domain signal), the duality property tells you that its Fourier Transform is a **sinc function in frequency** — and the original **sinc** in time has a Fourier Transform that looks like a **rectangular pulse in frequency**. > [!NOTE] **Why is Duality Important?** > It saves enormous effort. Once you have derived the Fourier Transform of one signal, duality lets you derive the transform of a *completely different* signal **for free**, without redoing the integration. It is the mathematical embodiment of the deep symmetry between time and frequency. **Key constants to remember (in bold):** - **$2\pi$** — the scaling factor arising from the non-symmetric CTFT definition (using $\omega$, not $f$). - The duality theorem for **DTFT** (Discrete-Time Fourier Transform) is *cleaner* — it has **no $2\pi$ factor**, because DTFT is naturally periodic in $2\pi$. - The duality theorem for **DFT** (Discrete Fourier Transform) is *exact* (no scaling, no time-reversal) due to its symmetric definition: if $x[n] \leftrightarrow X[k]$, then $X[n] \leftrightarrow N \cdot x[(-k) \mod N]$. ## 1.3 Quick-Reference Summary | Domain Pair | Duality Statement | Scaling Factor | |---|---|---| | CTFT | $X(t) \leftrightarrow 2\pi \, x(-\omega)$ | $2\pi$ | | DTFT | $X(e^{j\Omega}) \leftrightarrow 2\pi \, x[-n]$ | $2\pi$ | | DFT | $X[n] \leftrightarrow N \cdot x[(-k) \mod N]$ | $N$ | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Underlying Mathematical Logic (Step-by-Step Reasoning) The duality property is **not assumed** — it is a direct mathematical consequence of the **definition of the Fourier Transform**. Here is the logic chain: 1. **Start with the forward CTFT definition**: $$X(\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) \, e^{-j\omega t} \, dt$$ 2. **Rename variables**: Substitute $t \to \tau$ (no change in meaning) to keep notation clean: $$X(\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(\tau) \, e^{-j\omega \tau} \, d\tau$$ 3. **Apply the duality swap**: Interchange the roles of $t$ and $\omega$ on **both sides** of the equation: $$X(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(\omega) \, e^{-j t \omega} \, d\omega$$ 4. **Compare with the inverse CTFT definition**: $$x(t) = \frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} X(\omega) \, e^{+j\omega t} \, d\omega$$ 5. **Align the forms**: To make Step 3 look like the inverse transform, change the sign in the exponent using the substitution $\omega \to -\omega$ (this introduces a minus sign that combines with the existing one): $$X(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(-\omega') \, e^{-j t (-\omega')} \, d(-\omega') \quad \text{where } \omega' = -\omega$$ 6. **After simplifying** (the $d(-\omega') = -d\omega'$ and the minus signs from limit flips cancel out), you obtain: $$X(t) = 2\pi \cdot \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} 2\pi \, x(-\omega) \, e^{j\omega t} \, d\omega$$ Comparing with the inverse transform definition, this proves: $$\boxed{\mathcal{F}\{X(t)\} = 2\pi \, x(-\omega)}$$ ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | Property / Equation | Mathematical Form | Units / Conditions | |---|---|---| | **CTFT Definition (Forward)** | $X(\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) e^{-j\omega t}\, dt$ | $\omega$ in **rad/sec** | | **CTFT Definition (Inverse)** | $x(t) = \frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} X(\omega) e^{j\omega t}\, d\omega$ | The $\frac{1}{2\pi}$ produces the **2π** scaling in duality | | **Duality Theorem (CTFT)** | $x(t) \leftrightarrow X(\omega) \implies X(t) \leftrightarrow 2\pi \, x(-\omega)$ | Continuous-time, $\omega$ | | **Duality Theorem (DTFT)** | $x[n] \leftrightarrow X(e^{j\Omega}) \implies X(e^{j\Omega}) \leftrightarrow 2\pi \, x[-n]$ | $\Omega$ in **rad/sample**, **periodic in $2\pi$** | | **Duality Theorem (DFT)** | $x[n] \leftrightarrow X[k] \implies X[n] \leftrightarrow N \cdot x[(-k) \mod N]$ | $N$-point, $0 \le k \le N-1$ | | **Rectangular ↔ Sinc (CTFT)** | $\text{rect}\!\left(\frac{t}{\tau}\right) \leftrightarrow \tau \cdot \text{sinc}\!\left(\frac{\omega\tau}{2}\right)$ | Foundation for duality applications | | **Sinc ↔ Rectangular (CTFT)** | $\text{sinc}(W t) \leftrightarrow \frac{\pi}{W} \cdot \text{rect}\!\left(\frac{\omega}{2W}\right)$ | Duality-derived result | > [!NOTE] **Definition of sinc used in KTU syllabus:** > $$\text{sinc}(x) = \frac{\sin(\pi x)}{\pi x}$$ > Always verify which sinc convention your textbook uses. KTU follows the **unnormalized sinc** ($\sin(\pi x)/\pi x$). ## 2.3 Conditions for Validity - The signal $x(t)$ must be **absolutely integrable** OR be a **distribution/generalized function** (like $\delta(t)$ or $\text{sinc}$) that has a valid CTFT. - The original pair $x(t) \leftrightarrow X(\omega)$ must exist. - The result $X(t)$ (treated as a *time-domain* signal) must be a valid signal — which it is, by construction. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Filter Design**: If you know the impulse response of a low-pass filter (a sinc function in time), duality instantly tells you it has a rectangular frequency response — no integration needed. - **Antenna Theory**: The radiation pattern of a uniformly illuminated aperture antenna is the **Fourier dual** of the aperture field distribution (Fourier optics). - **Spectroscopy**: The free-induction decay (FID) signal in NMR is the time-domain dual of the absorption spectrum. - **Sampling Theorem**: The duality between the impulse train (time) and the impulse train (frequency) is foundational to understanding aliasing. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the CTFT Duality Property We will derive the duality property rigorously from the CTFT definitions. **Given:** $$X(\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) \, e^{-j\omega t} \, dt \quad \text{(Forward CTFT)} \tag{1}$$ $$x(t) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} X(\omega) \, e^{j\omega t} \, d\omega \quad \text{(Inverse CTFT)} \tag{2}$$ **Goal:** Prove that $\mathcal{F}\{X(t)\} = 2\pi \, x(-\omega)$. ### Step 1 — Rename the dummy variable in (1) To avoid confusion when swapping $t$ and $\omega$, replace the integration variable $t$ with $\tau$: $$X(\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(\tau) \, e^{-j\omega \tau} \, d\tau \tag{3}$$ **Logic:** Since $\tau$ is a dummy variable of integration, the equation (3) is *mathematically identical* to (1). The function $X(\cdot)$ is *defined* by this integral. ### Step 2 — Swap the symbols $t$ and $\omega$ on both sides Every occurrence of $t$ becomes $\omega$, and every occurrence of $\omega$ becomes $t$: $$X(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(\omega) \, e^{-j t \omega} \, d\omega \tag{4}$$ **Logic:** This is a **symbolic substitution**, not a change of integration variable. After this swap, the symbol $t$ on the left-hand side is the *argument* of the function $X(\cdot)$ (a real number), and the symbol $\omega$ inside the integral is a *dummy variable of integration*. The equation is now expressing a function of $t$. ### Step 3 — Rewrite the integral with $\omega$ as the integration variable Rearrange equation (4) so that the integrand matches the form of the *inverse* CTFT (equation 2). The inverse CTFT has a **positive** exponent $e^{+j\omega t}$, but our equation (4) has a **negative** exponent $e^{-jt\omega}$. We must fix this sign mismatch. **Trick:** Substitute $\omega = -\omega'$ (i.e., let $\omega' = -\omega$) in the integral: $$d\omega = -d\omega'$$ The limits of integration: when $\omega \to -\infty$, $\omega' \to +\infty$; when $\omega \to +\infty$, $\omega' \to -\infty$. So: $$X(t) = \int_{+\infty}^{-\infty} x(-\omega') \, e^{-j t (-\omega')} \, (-d\omega') \tag{5}$$ The two minus signs (one from $d\omega = -d\omega'$, one from flipping the limits) **cancel out**: $$X(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(-\omega') \, e^{+j t \omega'} \, d\omega' \tag{6}$$ ### Step 4 — Compare with the inverse CTFT (equation 2) Compare (6) with the inverse CTFT: $$x(t) = \frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} X(\omega) \, e^{+j\omega t} \, d\omega \tag{2 (revisited)}$$ In equation (6), the role of $X(\omega)$ in (2) is played by $2\pi \cdot x(-\omega')$. Therefore: $$\mathcal{F}\{X(t)\} = 2\pi \, x(-\omega)$$ **Q.E.D. — The duality property is proved.** $\blacksquare$ ## 3.2 Worked Example 1: Sinc ↔ Rectangular Pair (CTFT) **Given pair** (this is a standard KTU result you should know): $$x(t) = \text{rect}\!\left(\frac{t}{\tau}\right) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} X(\omega) = \tau \cdot \text{sinc}\!\left(\frac{\omega \tau}{2\pi}\right) = \tau \cdot \frac{\sin(\omega\tau/2)}{\omega\tau/2}$$ In KTU's preferred convention (using $\sin/\omega$ form, the "unnormalized angular sinc"): $$x(t) = \text{rect}\!\left(\frac{t}{\tau}\right) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} X(\omega) = \tau \cdot \frac{\sin(\omega\tau/2)}{\omega/2}$$ **Apply duality:** By the duality theorem, $X(t) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} 2\pi \, x(-\omega)$. **Left side** — The signal $X(t) = \tau \cdot \frac{\sin(\tau t/2)}{t/2} = \tau \cdot \text{sinc}_{\text{ang}}(\tau t/2)$ is a **sinc function in time**. **Right side** — The transform is $2\pi \, x(-\omega) = 2\pi \cdot \text{rect}\!\left(\frac{-\omega}{\tau}\right) = 2\pi \cdot \text{rect}\!\left(\frac{\omega}{\tau}\right)$ (since the rect function is **even**). **Result:** $$\boxed{\tau \cdot \frac{\sin(\tau t/2)}{t/2} \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} 2\pi \cdot \text{rect}\!\left(\frac{\omega}{\tau}\right)}$$ **Verification (direct calculation):** The CTFT of $\text{sinc}_{\text{ang}}(\tau t/2)$ is $\frac{2\pi}{\tau} \cdot \text{rect}(\omega/\tau)$, which matches. ✓ ## 3.3 Worked Example 2: Duality for the Dirac Delta Impulse **Given pair:** $\delta(t) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} 1$ (the constant function, all frequencies equal). **Apply duality:** The dual signal is $X(t) = 1$ (a DC signal of amplitude 1). Its Fourier Transform should be: $$\mathcal{F}\{1\} = 2\pi \, x(-\omega) = 2\pi \, \delta(-\omega) = 2\pi \, \delta(\omega)$$ (since $\delta(\omega)$ is even). **Result:** $\boxed{1 \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} 2\pi \, \delta(\omega)}$ **This is a key KTU result**: a DC signal of unit amplitude has a Fourier Transform that is a Dirac impulse of weight $2\pi$ at the origin. ## 3.4 Worked Example 3: Duality for the Cosine Signal **Given pair:** $\cos(\omega_0 t) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} \pi[\delta(\omega - \omega_0) + \delta(\omega + \omega_0)]$ **Apply duality:** Let $X(t) = \pi[\delta(t - \omega_0) + \delta(t + \omega_0)]$. Then: $$\mathcal{F}\{X(t)\} = 2\pi \, \cos(-\omega \cdot 1) = 2\pi \cos(\omega)$$ (where we have used $x(\omega) = \cos(\omega \cdot 1) = \cos(\omega)$ from the original pair, treating $\omega$ as the variable). **Result:** $\boxed{\pi[\delta(t - \omega_0) + \delta(t + \omega_0)] \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} 2\pi \cos(\omega)}$ ## 3.5 Discrete-Time Duality (DTFT) For the DTFT, the duality property is: $$x[n] \xleftrightarrow{\text{DTFT}} X(e^{j\Omega}) \implies X(e^{j\Omega}) \xleftrightarrow{\text{DTFT}} 2\pi \, x[-n]$$ **Important:** The "time" axis is discrete ($n \in \mathbb{Z}$) but the "frequency" axis $\Omega$ is **continuous and $2\pi$-periodic**. So the "dual signal" $X(e^{j\Omega})$ is a **continuous periodic function**, not a discrete sequence. This is why DTFT duality is *less often used* in practice — you cannot directly feed a continuous periodic function back through a DTFT. **Worked Example:** Given $x[n] = a^n u[n]$ with $\vert a \vert < 1$: $$X(e^{j\Omega}) = \frac{1}{1 - a e^{-j\Omega}}$$ By DTFT duality, the DTFT of $X(e^{j\Omega})$ (a continuous periodic function!) is $2\pi \, x[-n] = 2\pi \, a^{-n} u[-n]$. This result is theoretically correct but is rarely applied directly. ## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Common Dual Pairs (CTFT) | Original Pair $\;x(t) \leftrightarrow X(\omega)$ | Dual Pair $\;X(t) \leftrightarrow 2\pi \, x(-\omega)$ | |---|---| | $\delta(t) \leftrightarrow 1$ | $1 \leftrightarrow 2\pi \, \delta(\omega)$ | | $1 \leftrightarrow 2\pi \, \delta(\omega)$ | $2\pi \, \delta(t) \leftrightarrow 1$ | | $\delta(t-t_0) \leftrightarrow e^{-j\omega t_0}$ | $e^{-jt_0 \cdot \omega} \leftrightarrow 2\pi \, \delta(\omega + t_0 \text{ slope})$ — *requires care* | | $e^{-at}u(t) \leftrightarrow \dfrac{1}{a+j\omega}$ | $\dfrac{1}{a+jt} \leftrightarrow 2\pi \, e^{a\omega} u(-\omega)$ | | $\text{rect}(t/\tau) \leftrightarrow \tau \cdot \text{sinc}(\omega\tau/2)$ | $\tau \cdot \text{sinc}(\tau t/2) \leftrightarrow 2\pi \cdot \text{rect}(\omega/\tau)$ | | $\cos(\omega_0 t) \leftrightarrow \pi[\delta(\omega-\omega_0) + \delta(\omega+\omega_0)]$ | $\pi[\delta(t-\omega_0) + \delta(t+\omega_0)] \leftrightarrow 2\pi \cos(\omega)$ | | $\sin(\omega_0 t) \leftrightarrow j\pi[\delta(\omega+\omega_0) - \delta(\omega-\omega_0)]$ | $j\pi[\delta(t+\omega_0) - \delta(t-\omega_0)] \leftrightarrow 2\pi \sin(\omega)$ | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: The Duality "Mirror" Concept This diagram shows the symmetry between the time domain and the frequency domain that duality exploits. ```mermaid flowchart LR A["TIME DOMAIN
Signal x of t"] -- "Forward CTFT
integral with e to the power of minus j omega t" --> B["FREQUENCY DOMAIN
Spectrum X of omega"] B -- "Inverse CTFT
integral with e to the power of plus j omega t divided by 2 pi" --> A A -. "Duality swap
swap t and omega" .-> C["DUAL TIME SIGNAL
X of t treated as signal"] C -- "Forward CTFT" --> D["DUAL SPECTRUM
2 pi times x of minus omega"] D -- "Inverse CTFT" --> C style A fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000 style B fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107,color:#000 style C fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000 style D fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Duality Mapping of Common CTFT Pairs ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph A["Original Domain Pairs"] A1["delta of t in time domain"] A2["unit DC signal 1 in time domain"] A3["rectangular pulse in time domain"] A4["sinc pulse in time domain"] A5["cosine wave in time domain"] end subgraph B["Fourier Transform Results"] B1["constant 1 in frequency"] B2["2 pi times delta of omega in frequency"] B3["sinc function in frequency"] B4["2 pi times rectangular in frequency"] B5["two delta impulses in frequency"] end subgraph C["Dual Signals after Swap"] C1["constant 1 in time"] C2["2 pi times delta of t in time"] C3["sinc function in time"] C4["2 pi times rectangular in time"] C5["two delta impulses in time"] end subgraph D["Fourier Transforms of Dual Signals"] D1["2 pi times delta of omega"] D2["constant 1"] D3["2 pi times rectangular in frequency"] D4["sinc function in frequency"] D5["2 pi times cosine in frequency"] end A1 -- F1 --> B1 A2 -- F2 --> B2 A3 -- F3 --> B3 A4 -- F4 --> B4 A5 -- F5 --> B5 B1 -. swap .- C1 B2 -. swap .- C2 B3 -. swap .- C3 B4 -. swap .- C4 B5 -. swap .- C5 C1 -- F1D --> D1 C2 -- F2D --> D2 C3 -- F3D --> D3 C4 -- F4D --> D4 C5 -- F5D --> D5 style A fill:#e7f3ff,stroke:#0d6efd style B fill:#fff8e1,stroke:#ffc107 style C fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#198754 style D fill:#fde7e9,stroke:#dc3545 ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Block-Level Architecture of Duality Application This block diagram shows the **engineering workflow** when using duality to find a Fourier Transform. ```mermaid flowchart TD P1["STEP 1: Identify known CTFT pair
x of t and X of omega"] P2["STEP 2: Decide which side is easier
to read as a new signal"] P3["STEP 3A: Treat X of t as time signal
OR P3B: Use the original x of t form"] P4["STEP 4: Apply duality formula
X of t has FT equals 2 pi times x of minus omega"] P5["STEP 5: Apply reflection x of minus omega
if signal is even no change
if odd negate it"] P6["STEP 6: Final result is the Fourier Transform
of the dual signal"] P1 --> P2 --> P3A P2 --> P3B P3A --> P4 P3B --> P4 P4 --> P5 --> P6 style P1 fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000 style P2 fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000 style P3A fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107,color:#000 style P3B fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107,color:#000 style P4 fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000 style P5 fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000 style P6 fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000 ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: CTFT vs DTFT Duality Comparison ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph CTFT["Continuous Time Fourier Transform"] C1["x of t is continuous"] C2["X of omega is continuous"] C3["Duality gives 2 pi times x of minus omega"] end subgraph DTFT["Discrete Time Fourier Transform"] D1["x of n is discrete"] D2["X of e to the j omega is continuous and 2 pi periodic"] D3["Duality gives 2 pi times x of minus n"] end subgraph DFT["Discrete Fourier Transform"] F1["x of n is discrete N point"] F2["X of k is discrete N point"] F3["Duality gives N times x of minus k mod N"] end style CTFT fill:#e7f3ff,stroke:#0d6efd style DTFT fill:#fff8e1,stroke:#ffc107 style DFT fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#198754 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **State and prove the duality property of the Continuous-Time Fourier Transform.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Statement:** If $x(t) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} X(\omega)$, then $X(t) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} 2\pi \, x(-\omega)$. **Proof:** Start with the forward CTFT definition: $$X(\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) \, e^{-j\omega t} \, dt$$ Swap the symbols $t$ and $\omega$: $$X(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(\omega) \, e^{-j t \omega} \, d\omega$$ Apply substitution $\omega \to -\omega'$ in the integral. The differential flips sign, and the limits flip and cancel: $$X(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(-\omega') \, e^{+j t \omega'} \, d\omega'$$ Comparing with the inverse CTFT formula $x(t) = \frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} X(\omega) e^{+j\omega t}\, d\omega$, we conclude: $$\mathcal{F}\{X(t)\} = 2\pi \, x(-\omega) \quad \blacksquare$$ **Mark Distribution (KTU pattern):** Statement = 1 mark, Proof = 2 marks. --- ### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **Using the duality property, find the Fourier Transform of the signal $x(t) = \dfrac{1}{1 + t^2}$.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Recall the standard CTFT pair (a KTU must-know): $$e^{-a\vert t \vert} \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} \frac{2a}{a^2 + \omega^2}, \quad a > 0$$ By the duality property, $X(t) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} 2\pi \, x(-\omega)$. Setting $a = 1$: $$X(\omega) = \frac{2(1)}{1^2 + \omega^2} = \frac{2}{1 + \omega^2}$$ So the dual signal is $X(t) = \frac{2}{1 + t^2}$, and its Fourier Transform is: $$\mathcal{F}\!\left\{\frac{2}{1 + t^2}\right\} = 2\pi \, e^{-\vert \omega \vert} = 2\pi \, e^{-\vert \omega \vert}$$ Therefore, $\mathcal{F}\!\left\{\dfrac{1}{1+t^2}\right\} = \pi \, e^{-\vert \omega \vert}$. **Mark Distribution:** Identifying the known pair = 1 mark, applying duality = 1 mark, final answer = 1 mark. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) ### Question Choice A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024] **(a)** State and prove the duality property of the CTFT. Also write the duality property for the DTFT and DFT, clearly stating the differences in scaling factors. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Using the duality property, derive the Fourier Transform of the signal $x(t) = \text{sinc}(100t)$ (using the convention $\text{sinc}(x) = \dfrac{\sin(\pi x)}{\pi x}$). Sketch both $x(t)$ and $X(f)$ clearly. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks **Step 1 — CTFT Duality Statement (1 mark):** If $x(t) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} X(\omega)$, then $X(t) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} 2\pi \, x(-\omega)$. **Step 2 — Proof of CTFT Duality (3 marks):** From the forward CTFT: $X(\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) e^{-j\omega t}\, dt$. Interchange $t$ and $\omega$: $X(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(\omega) e^{-jt\omega}\, d\omega$. Substitute $\omega \to -\omega'$: $X(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(-\omega') e^{+jt\omega'}\, d\omega'$. Compare with inverse CTFT: $x(t) = \frac{1}{2\pi}\int X(\omega) e^{+j\omega t}\, d\omega$. Conclusion: $\mathcal{F}\{X(t)\} = 2\pi \, x(-\omega)$. **Step 3 — DTFT Duality (1.5 marks):** $$x[n] \xleftrightarrow{\text{DTFT}} X(e^{j\Omega}) \implies X(e^{j\Omega}) \xleftrightarrow{\text{DTFT}} 2\pi \, x[-n]$$ **Step 4 — DFT Duality (1.5 marks):** $$x[n] \xleftrightarrow{\text{DFT}} X[k] \implies X[n] \xleftrightarrow{\text{DFT}} N \cdot x[(-k) \mod N]$$ #### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks **Step 1 — Identify the known pair (1 mark):** The standard CTFT pair (rectangular pulse): $$\text{rect}\!\left(\frac{t}{T}\right) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} T \cdot \text{sinc}(fT) = T \cdot \frac{\sin(\pi fT)}{\pi fT}$$ where $T$ is the pulse width. Equivalently in $\omega$: $$\text{rect}\!\left(\frac{t}{T}\right) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} T \cdot \frac{\sin(\omega T/2)}{\omega/2}$$ **Step 2 — Convert given signal to standard form (1 mark):** We want $\mathcal{F}\{\text{sinc}(100t)\}$. Comparing with the standard sinc pair, identify the time-domain **sinc** with $T = 1/100$ (since $\text{sinc}(100t) = \text{sinc}(t/(1/100))$). **Step 3 — Apply the duality property (3 marks):** By duality: $X(t) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} 2\pi \, x(-\omega)$. Let us use the pair in the form $\text{rect}(t/T) \leftrightarrow T \cdot \text{sinc}(\omega T/2\pi) = T \cdot \text{sinc}(fT)$ where the second equality uses $f = \omega/(2\pi)$. If $T = 1/100$, the spectrum is: $$X(\omega) = T \cdot \frac{\sin(\omega T/2)}{\omega/2} = \frac{1}{100} \cdot \frac{\sin(\omega/200)}{\omega/2}$$ By duality, the FT of $\text{sinc}(100t) = \text{sinc}(t/T)$ is: $$\mathcal{F}\{\text{sinc}(100t)\} = \frac{1}{100} \cdot 2\pi \cdot \text{rect}\!\left(\frac{\omega/2\pi}{1/T}\right) = \frac{1}{100} \cdot 2\pi \cdot \text{rect}(100 f)$$ where $f = \omega/(2\pi)$. Simplifying: $$\mathcal{F}\{\text{sinc}(100t)\} = \frac{1}{100} \cdot \text{rect}\!\left(\frac{f}{1/100}\right) = 0.01 \cdot \text{rect}(100f)$$ So the transform is a **rectangular pulse** of height $\frac{1}{100} = 0.01$ and width $\frac{1}{100} = 0.01$ Hz, centered at $f = 0$. **Step 4 — Sketch (2 marks):** - $x(t) = \text{sinc}(100t)$: Oscillating sinc function with first zero crossings at $t = \pm 1/100 = \pm 0.01$ s, peak value 1 at $t = 0$. - $X(f)$: Rectangular pulse of height $0.01$ extending from $f = -0.01$ Hz to $f = +0.01$ Hz. --- ### Question Choice B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **(a)** Prove the duality property of CTFT. Hence, or otherwise, find the Fourier Transform of a unit amplitude rectangular pulse in the frequency domain. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Given the CTFT pair $e^{-at}u(t) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} \dfrac{1}{a+j\omega}$ for $a > 0$, use duality to find the Fourier Transform of the signal $\dfrac{1}{a + jt}$. State the conditions under which the result is valid. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks **Step 1 — Proof of Duality (3 marks):** Same as in Question Choice A, Part (a). **Step 2 — Setting up the dual pair (1 mark):** We need to find the FT of $X(t)$ where $X(\omega) = \text{rect}\!\left(\frac{\omega}{2W}\right)$ — a unit-amplitude rectangle in the frequency domain, centered at $\omega = 0$, extending from $\omega = -W$ to $\omega = +W$. **Step 3 — Identify the known pair (1 mark):** The known pair is: $\text{rect}\!\left(\frac{t}{2T}\right) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} 2T \cdot \text{sinc}\!\left(\frac{\omega T}{\pi}\right) = 2T \cdot \frac{\sin(\omega T)}{\omega T}$. Equivalently, the FT of a rectangular pulse of width $2T$ and height 1 is a sinc of the form $\dfrac{\sin(\omega T)}{\omega}$ with appropriate scaling. **Step 4 — Apply duality (2 marks):** By duality, $X(t) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} 2\pi \, x(-\omega)$. Let $2T = 1/W$ (so $T = 1/(2W)$ is half the time-width to be determined).
