Using the known pair with $T = 1/(2W)$: $$x(t) = \text{rect}(Wt) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} \frac{1}{W} \cdot \frac{\sin(\omega/(2W))}{\omega/(2W)} = \frac{1}{W} \cdot \text{sinc}\!\left(\frac{\omega}{2\pi W}\right)$$ By duality, the FT of the frequency-domain rectangle is: $$\mathcal{F}\{\text{rect}(\omega/2W)\} = 2\pi \cdot x(-t) = 2\pi \cdot \text{rect}(-Wt) = 2\pi \cdot \text{rect}(Wt)$$ (since rect is even). **Final Result:** $$\boxed{\mathcal{F}\{\text{rect}(\omega/2W)\} = 2\pi \cdot \text{rect}(Wt) = 2\pi \cdot \text{rect}\!\left(\frac{t}{1/W}\right)}$$ This is a rectangular pulse of height $2\pi$ and width $1/W$ in the time domain. #### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks **Step 1 — Identify the given pair (1 mark):** $$x(t) = e^{-at}u(t) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} X(\omega) = \frac{1}{a+j\omega}, \quad a > 0$$ **Step 2 — Apply duality (2 marks):** By the duality theorem, $X(t) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} 2\pi \, x(-\omega)$. We identify: $$X(t) = \frac{1}{a + jt}$$ So: $$\mathcal{F}\!\left\{\frac{1}{a+jt}\right\} = 2\pi \, x(-\omega) = 2\pi \, e^{-a(-\omega)}u(-\omega) = 2\pi \, e^{a\omega} u(-\omega)$$ **Step 3 — Check conditions (2 marks):** The result is valid if and only if: - The original pair $e^{-at}u(t) \leftrightarrow \frac{1}{a+j\omega}$ converges, which requires $a > 0$. - The result $2\pi e^{a\omega}u(-\omega)$ must be a valid (well-defined) function of $\omega$, which it is for all real $\omega$ since $e^{a\omega} \to 0$ as $\omega \to -\infty$ when $a > 0$. - The signal $X(t) = \frac{1}{a+jt}$ must be in $L^1(\mathbb{R})$ or be a tempered distribution, which is satisfied for $a > 0$. **Final Result:** $$\boxed{\mathcal{F}\!\left\{\frac{1}{a+jt}\right\} = 2\pi \, e^{a\omega} u(-\omega), \quad a > 0}$$ **Step 4 — Note on the Fourier Transform of $1/(a+jt)$ — Reality check (2 marks):** We can verify the result is consistent with Hermitian symmetry: the original $x(t) = e^{-at}u(t)$ is **not** real and even, so $X(\omega)$ is **not** conjugate symmetric. The dual $X(t) = 1/(a+jt)$ is also not real, and its transform $2\pi e^{a\omega} u(-\omega)$ is consistent. ✓ --- > [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (lose 1-2 marks each):** > 1. **Forgetting the $2\pi$ factor**: The most common error. CTFT duality has a $2\pi$ scaling; DTFT also has $2\pi$; DFT has $N$. Students often drop this scalar. > 2. **Forgetting the time-reversal**: $x(-\omega)$, not $x(\omega)$. If the original signal is **even**, this is invisible (and students mistakenly "prove" duality for the even case only). Always write the reflection explicitly. > 3. **Confusing $f$ and $\omega$ duality**: In $f$ (Hz), the duality statement uses $X(f)$ vs $x(-f)$ with **no** $2\pi$ factor; the $2\pi$ gets absorbed into $df = d\omega/(2\pi)$. In $\omega$ (rad/s), the $2\pi$ is explicit. Pick one convention and stick to it. > 4. **DTFT duality misuse**: Treating $X(e^{j\Omega})$ as a discrete sequence in the dual — it is **continuous and $2\pi$-periodic**, so you cannot directly "invert" via DTFT. > 5. **Sinc convention mismatch**: The result $\text{rect} \leftrightarrow \text{sinc}$ has different constants depending on whether you use $\text{sinc}(x) = \sin(\pi x)/(\pi x)$ (KTU preferred) or $\sin(x)/x$ (angular). --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - ✅ **Duality Statement (CTFT)**: $x(t) \leftrightarrow X(\omega) \implies X(t) \leftrightarrow 2\pi \, x(-\omega)$. - ✅ **The $2\pi$ factor** comes from the non-symmetric CTFT definition ($1/(2\pi)$ on the inverse). - ✅ **The time-reversal $x(-\omega)$** is the second essential component. For **even** signals, this is a no-op; for **odd** signals, it negates the result. - ✅ **The DTFT duality** also carries a $2\pi$ factor: $X(e^{j\Omega}) \leftrightarrow 2\pi \, x[-n]$. - ✅ **The DFT duality** is the cleanest: $X[n] \leftrightarrow N \cdot x[(-k) \mod N]$ — no reflection surprise if you are careful modulo $N$. - ✅ **Master pairs for duality problems**: - $\delta(t) \leftrightarrow 1 \implies 1 \leftrightarrow 2\pi \delta(\omega)$ - $\text{rect}(t/\tau) \leftrightarrow \tau \cdot \text{sinc}(\omega\tau/2) \implies \tau \cdot \text{sinc}(\tau t/2) \leftrightarrow 2\pi \cdot \text{rect}(\omega/\tau)$ - $e^{-at}u(t) \leftrightarrow \dfrac{1}{a+j\omega} \implies \dfrac{1}{a+jt} \leftrightarrow 2\pi \, e^{a\omega}u(-\omega)$ for $a > 0$ - ✅ **Time-bandwidth reciprocity**: A narrow pulse in time $\implies$ wide spectrum in frequency (and vice versa). Duality makes this symmetry rigorous. - ✅ **In KTU exams**, duality is a guaranteed favorite for 3-mark short questions and 7-mark derivation sub-parts. - ✅ **Common exam trick**: "Find the FT of $X(t) = 1/(1+t^2)$" — students who don't recognize the dual of $e^{-\vert t \vert}$ will spend 10 minutes doing contour integration. Duality solves it in 30 seconds. - ✅ **Final sanity check** for any duality answer: Verify the result by plugging the *known* transform back through the original pair equation and confirming the **area under the magnitude squared** is preserved (Parseval's theorem).

topics.title: Multiplication
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST416 - SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Multiplication

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Signal Multiplication (Product Operation)**
> In the context of Signals and Systems, **multiplication** refers to the *pointwise product* of two or more signals. The output signal at any instant of time is obtained by multiplying the corresponding values of the input signals at that same instant.

**Mathematical Definitions:**

For **continuous-time (CT)** signals:

$$
y(t) = x_1(t) \cdot x_2(t) \quad \forall \, t \in \mathbb{R}
$$

For **discrete-time (DT)** signals:

$$
y[n] = x_1[n] \cdot x_2[n] \quad \forall \, n \in \mathbb{Z}
$$

The resulting signal $y(\cdot)$ is called the **product signal** and is defined only at the instants where both input signals are simultaneously defined (in DT) or for the entire real line (in CT).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The And-Gate of the Signal World"**
> Imagine two spotlights pointing at the same wall. The brightness at any point on the wall is the **product** of the brightness each individual spotlight contributes at that point. If either spotlight is OFF (intensity = 0) at that point, the wall is dark there. If both are ON, the brightness adds up multiplicatively. Signal multiplication behaves exactly like this — it acts as a **gating mechanism**: wherever one signal is zero, the output is forced to zero, regardless of the other signal's value.

### 1.3 Why This Operation Matters in Engineering

- **Amplitude Modulation (AM):** In radio broadcasting, the voice signal is multiplied by a high-frequency carrier.
- **Sampling:** A continuous-time signal is multiplied by a train of impulses to obtain its samples.
- **Signal Gating / Windowing:** A signal is multiplied by a rectangular (or Hamming/Hanning) window to extract a finite portion of it.
- **Frequency Mixing:** Multiplication in time domain corresponds to **convolution in frequency domain** (frequency shifting property).
- **Correlation:** A measure of similarity between two signals is built upon the multiplication operation.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1 — Elementary Operations on Signals)**
> The operation of **multiplication** is grouped with *time-shifting*, *time-scaling*, *time-reversal*, and *addition* as one of the five **elementary transformations** on signals. The student must be able to define, sketch, and analytically determine the product of standard CT and DT signals.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Step by Step

1. **Identify the two (or more) signals** to be multiplied: $x_1(\cdot)$ and $x_2(\cdot)$. Verify that both are defined over a common domain (the entire real line for CT, or a common index set for DT).
2. **Sample-by-sample (or point-by-point) multiplication:** For every time instant $t$ (or index $n$), compute the arithmetic product of the amplitudes.
3. **Domain restriction rule:** The product signal $y(\cdot)$ is non-zero **only over the intersection of the supports** of the two signals. Outside this overlap region, at least one signal is zero, forcing the product to be zero.
4. **Amplitude scaling rule:** Where both signals are non-zero, the product's amplitude is the literal numerical product of the two individual amplitudes (may be positive, negative, or zero depending on signs).
5. **Sketch** the result by tracking where the support of $y$ lies and the shape of the amplitude profile within that support.

### 2.2 Algebraic Properties of Signal Multiplication

The pointwise product of real-valued signals forms a **commutative ring** with respect to ordinary addition. The core properties are:

| **Property** | **Continuous-Time Form** | **Discrete-Time Form** |
|---|---|---|
| Commutativity | $x_1(t) \cdot x_2(t) = x_2(t) \cdot x_1(t)$ | $x_1[n] \cdot x_2[n] = x_2[n] \cdot x_1[n]$ |
| Associativity | $\left[x_1(t) \cdot x_2(t)\right] \cdot x_3(t) = x_1(t) \cdot \left[x_2(t) \cdot x_3(t)\right]$ | $\left[x_1[n] \cdot x_2[n]\right] \cdot x_3[n] = x_1[n] \cdot \left[x_2[n] \cdot x_3[n]\right]$ |
| Distributivity over addition | $x_1(t) \cdot \left[x_2(t) + x_3(t)\right] = x_1(t)x_2(t) + x_1(t)x_3(t)$ | $x_1[n] \cdot \left[x_2[n] + x_3[n]\right] = x_1[n]x_2[n] + x_1[n]x_3[n]$ |
| Identity element | $x(t) \cdot 1 = x(t)$ | $x[n] \cdot 1 = x[n]$ |
| Zero element | $x(t) \cdot 0 = 0$ | $x[n] \cdot 0 = 0]$ |
| Squaring identity | $x(t) \cdot x(t) = x^2(t) = \vert x(t)\vert^2$ for real $x$ | $x[n] \cdot x[n] = x^2[n] = \vert x[n]\vert^2$ for real $x$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Utility of the Squaring Identity**
> $x^2(t) = \vert x(t) \vert^2$ is the **instantaneous power** of a real signal. Thus, the multiplication of a signal with itself is fundamental to power and energy calculations in **Parseval's theorem** and the **Rayleigh energy theorem**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **CT Formula** | **DT Formula** | **Notes / Boundary** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Product of two signals | $y(t) = x_1(t) \, x_2(t)$ | $y[n] = x_1[n] \, x_2[n]$ | Defined on $\mathbb{R}$ / $\mathbb{Z}$ |
| Support of product | $\text{supp}(y) = \text{supp}(x_1) \cap \text{supp}(x_2)$ | $\text{supp}(y) = \text{supp}(x_1) \cap \text{supp}(x_2)$ | Intersection of supports |
| Energy of product | $E_y = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x_1(t) x_2(t) \vert^2 \, dt$ | $E_y = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x_1[n] x_2[n] \vert^2$ | Always $\leq \min(E_{x_1}, E_{x_2})$ |
| Time-domain $\leftrightarrow$ Freq-domain | $\mathcal{F}\{x_1(t) x_2(t)\} = \frac{1}{2\pi} X_1(j\omega) * X_2(j\omega)$ | DTFT not a pure convolution | **Multiplication Theorem** |
| AM signal (product form) | $y(t) = \left[A + m(t)\right] \cos(\omega_c t)$ | — | Standard AM broadcast form |
| Trigonometric product-to-sum | $\cos A \cos B = \tfrac{1}{2}\left[\cos(A-B) + \cos(A+B)\right]$ | — | Used in AM demodulation |
| Squared sinusoid | $\cos^2(\omega t) = \tfrac{1}{2}\left[1 + \cos(2\omega t)\right]$ | — | Doubling of frequency |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Multiplication Theorem (Frequency Shifting Property)** — A high-yield KTU result:
> If $y(t) = x(t) \cdot e^{j\omega_0 t}$, then $Y(j\omega) = X(j(\omega - \omega_0))$.
> This means multiplication by a complex exponential **shifts the spectrum** — the cornerstone of amplitude modulation and frequency translation in communication systems.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — CT Multiplication: Half-Wave Rectified Sinusoid

> **Problem:** Given $x_1(t) = \sin(\pi t)$ for $0 \leq t \leq 2$ (and zero elsewhere) and $x_2(t) = u(t) - u(t-1)$ (a unit rectangular pulse from $t=0$ to $t=1$). Compute the product $y(t) = x_1(t) \cdot x_2(t)$ and verify the support rule.

**Step 1 — Express the support of $x_1(t)$.**

$$
x_1(t) = \begin{cases} \sin(\pi t), & 0 \leq t \leq 2 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

**Step 2 — Express $x_2(t)$ explicitly.**

$$
x_2(t) = \begin{cases} 1, & 0 \leq t < 1 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the support-intersection rule.**

The support of $x_1(t)$ is $[0, 2]$ and the support of $x_2(t)$ is $[0, 1)$. Their intersection is $[0, 1)$. Outside this region, the product is zero.

**Step 4 — Compute the product pointwise inside the intersection.**

$$
y(t) = x_1(t) \cdot x_2(t) = \sin(\pi t) \cdot 1 = \sin(\pi t), \quad 0 \leq t < 1
$$

**Step 5 — Write the compact form using unit-step functions.**

$$
y(t) = \sin(\pi t) \cdot \left[u(t) - u(t-1)\right]
$$

**Step 6 — Closed-form piecewise expression.**

$$
y(t) = \begin{cases} \sin(\pi t), & 0 \leq t < 1 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

**Result / Interpretation:** $x_2(t)$ has effectively "gated" out only the first half-cycle of the sinusoid. This is the principle behind a **half-wave rectifier** and a **time-window multiplier** in instrumentation.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Product of Two Rectangular Pulses (Support Truncation)

> **Problem:** Let $x_1(t) = u(t+2) - u(t-2)$ (a pulse of width 4 centered at 0) and $x_2(t) = u(t-1) - u(t-3)$ (a pulse of width 2 starting at $t=1$). Determine $y(t) = x_1(t) \cdot x_2(t)$.

**Step 1 — Identify supports.**

Support of $x_1(t) = [-2, 2]$, support of $x_2(t) = [1, 3]$.

**Step 2 — Intersection of supports.**

$$
[-2, 2] \cap [1, 3] = [1, 2]
$$

**Step 3 — Multiply the unit-amplitude signals.**

Since both signals equal $1$ in their respective supports, their product is also $1$ within the intersection.

**Step 4 — Express the result.**

$$
y(t) = u(t-1) - u(t-2)
$$

**Verification:** The product is a **narrower rectangular pulse of width 1** — a concrete illustration that the support of a product equals the intersection of the two individual supports.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Derivation of AM Spectrum (Product-to-Sum)

> **Problem:** Derive the expression for a DSB-SC (Double-Sideband Suppressed-Carrier) amplitude modulated signal given by $y(t) = m(t) \cdot \cos(\omega_c t)$ where $m(t) = A_m \cos(\omega_m t)$.

**Step 1 — Write the product signal.**

$$
y(t) = A_m \cos(\omega_m t) \cdot \cos(\omega_c t)
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the trigonometric identity.**

$$
\cos(A)\cos(B) = \frac{1}{2}\Big[\cos(A-B) + \cos(A+B)\Big]
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $A = \omega_m t$ and $B = \omega_c t$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
y(t) &= A_m \cdot \frac{1}{2}\Big[\cos((\omega_m - \omega_c)t) + \cos((\omega_m + \omega_c)t)\Big] \\
y(t) &= \frac{A_m}{2}\cos((\omega_c - \omega_m)t) + \frac{A_m}{2}\cos((\omega_c + \omega_m)t)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpret the result.**

The product $y(t)$ contains two cosine components at frequencies $\omega_c - \omega_m$ and $\omega_c + \omega_m$, each with amplitude $\frac{A_m}{2}$. The original message frequency $\omega_m$ and the carrier frequency $\omega_c$ are *individually absent* — hence the term **suppressed carrier**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Significance:** This is the fundamental building block of every analog and digital communication system. Wi-Fi, 4G/5G, FM radio, and television all rely on this exact trigonometric identity at the transmitter side and its inverse at the receiver.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Discrete-Time Multiplication (Pointwise)

> **Problem:** Let $x_1[n] = \{1, \, 2, \, 3, \, 0, \, 0\}$ and $x_2[n] = \{1, \, 1, \, 1, \, 1, \, 1\}$, with arrow under $n=0$ in both. Compute $y[n] = x_1[n] \cdot x_2[n]$.

**Step 1 — Align the sequences index-wise.**

| $n$ | $-2$ | $-1$ | $0$ | $1$ | $2$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $x_1[n]$ | $1$ | $2$ | $3$ | $0$ | $0$ |
| $x_2[n]$ | $1$ | $1$ | $1$ | $1$ | $1$ |
| $y[n] = x_1 x_2$ | $1$ | $2$ | $3$ | $0$ | $0$ |

**Step 2 — Apply the product element by element.**

$$
y[n] = \{1, \, 2, \, 3, \, 0, \, 0\}, \quad \text{arrow at } n=0
$$

**Observation:** When one of the DT signals is the **all-ones sequence** (i.e., $x_2[n] = 1$ for all $n$), multiplication acts as the identity operation. This is the DT analog of multiplying by $1$ in continuous time.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Common CT Signal Products Encountered in KTU Exams

| **$x_1(t)$** | **$x_2(t)$** | **Product $y(t) = x_1(t) \cdot x_2(t)$** | **Engineering Meaning** |
|---|---|---|---|
| $u(t)$ | $u(t)$ | $u(t)$ (idempotent) | Double switch closure |
| $\cos(\omega_0 t)$ | $\cos(\omega_0 t)$ | $\frac{1}{2} + \frac{1}{2}\cos(2\omega_0 t)$ | Frequency doubler / power detection |
| $\cos(\omega_c t)$ | $\cos(\omega_m t)$ | $\frac{1}{2}\cos((\omega_c - \omega_m)t) + \frac{1}{2}\cos((\omega_c + \omega_m)t)$ | DSB-SC amplitude modulation |
| $u(t) - u(t-T)$ | $\delta(t)$ | $0$ (since $u(\cdot)$ has jump at $0$) | Impulse-multiplied pulse is zero |
| $\text{rect}(t)$ | $\sin(\pi t)$ | Half-sine lobe | Time-windowed sinusoid |
| $e^{at} u(t)$ | $e^{-at} u(t)$ | $e^{0} u(t) = u(t)$ | DC product from symmetric exponentials |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — The Multiplier as a System

> **Description:** Two input signals $x_1(t)$ and $x_2(t)$ enter a multiplier block; the output is the pointwise product $y(t)$.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["INPUT X1 OF T"] --> M["MULTIPLIER BLOCK\nSYMBOL CIRCLED DOT"]
    B["INPUT X2 OF T"] --> M
    M --> C["OUTPUT Y OF T\nEQUALS X1 T TIMES X2 T"]
    M -.-> NOTE["OPERATION IS\nPOINTWISE AND INSTANTANEOUS"]
```

---

### 4.2 Application Block Diagram — Amplitude Modulation Using Multiplication

> **Description:** The message signal $m(t)$ is multiplied by a high-frequency carrier $c(t) = \cos(\omega_c t)$ inside the multiplier block to produce the AM output $y(t)$.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SRC["SIGNAL SOURCES"]
        M1["MESSAGE SIGNAL\nM OF T\nLOW FREQUENCY"]
        C1["CARRIER SIGNAL\nC OF T EQUALS COS OMEGA C T\nHIGH FREQUENCY"]
    end
    subgraph MOD["MODULATOR STAGE"]
        MULT["MULTIPLIER BLOCK\nCIRCLED DOT SYMBOL"]
    end
    subgraph OUT["TRANSMITTED SIGNAL"]
        Y1["MODULATED OUTPUT\nY OF T EQUALS M T TIMES C T\nOCCUPIES BAND AROUND OMEGA C"]
    end
    M1 --> MULT
    C1 --> MULT
    MULT --> Y1
```

---

### 4.3 Conceptual Flow — Windowing by Multiplication

> **Description:** A long-duration sinusoid is multiplied by a rectangular gating pulse to extract a finite segment — the basis of all digital oscilloscopes and FFT analyzers.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["INFINITE SINUSOID\nX1 OF T EQUALS SIN OMEGA NAUGHT T"] --> M["MULTIPLIER"]
    B["GATING PULSE\nX2 OF T EQUALS U T MINUS U T MINUS T1"] --> M
    M --> C["WINDOWED OUTPUT\nY OF T NONZERO\nONLY INSIDE WINDOW"]
    C --> D["FINITE DURATION\nSINE LOBE READY FOR FFT"]
```

---

### 4.4 Decision Flowchart — How to Solve a Multiplication Problem in KTU Exam

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S["START\nGIVEN X1 AND X2"] --> Q1{"ARE BOTH SIGNALS\nDEFINED IN SAME DOMAIN?"}
    Q1 -- "NO" --> ERR["INVALID OPERATION\nSTOP"]
    Q1 -- "YES" --> Q2{"FIND SUPPORTS\nOF X1 AND X2"}
    Q2 --> Q3{"COMPUTE INTERSECTION\nOF BOTH SUPPORTS"}
    Q3 --> Q4["OUTSIDE INTERSECTION\nY EQUALS ZERO"]
    Q4 --> Q5["INSIDE INTERSECTION\nMULTIPLY AMPLITUDES\nPOINTWISE"]
    Q5 --> Q6["WRITE FINAL\nPIECEWISE EXPRESSION"]
    Q6 --> Q7["SKETCH THE GRAPH\nMARKING AMPLITUDES\nAND BOUNDARIES"]
    Q7 --> END["FINAL ANSWER"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**

**Q1.** *Define the multiplication operation on two continuous-time signals. State any two algebraic properties of the product operation.*  **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Multiplication of two CT signals $x_1(t)$ and $x_2(t)$ produces a new signal $y(t) = x_1(t) \cdot x_2(t)$ whose value at every instant $t$ equals the product of the corresponding values of the two input signals.

Two algebraic properties:

1. **Commutativity:** $x_1(t) \cdot x_2(t) = x_2(t) \cdot x_1(t)$.
2. **Distributivity over addition:** $x_1(t) \cdot \left[x_2(t) + x_3(t)\right] = x_1(t) \cdot x_2(t) + x_1(t) \cdot x_3(t)$.

*(Any two of: commutativity, associativity, distributivity, identity element, zero element are acceptable.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]**

**Q2.** *Explain the role of signal multiplication in amplitude modulation with a suitable block diagram representation.*  **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

In amplitude modulation, a low-frequency **message signal** $m(t)$ is multiplied by a high-frequency **carrier signal** $c(t) = A_c \cos(\omega_c t)$. The product is the modulated waveform:

$$
y(t) = m(t) \cdot A_c \cos(\omega_c t)
$$

This shifts the message spectrum to a band centered at $\omega_c$, making it suitable for transmission over the airwaves. The multiplier is the **core building block** of the AM transmitter, and the demodulator at the receiver also employs a multiplier (synchronous detection) followed by a low-pass filter.

The block diagram consists of two inputs $m(t)$ and $\cos(\omega_c t)$ entering a single multiplier block whose output is the transmitted AM signal $y(t)$.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question — Internal Choice]**
> *Attempt any ONE of the following:*

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** *Given $x_1(t) = \sin(\pi t) \left[u(t) - u(t-2)\right]$ and $x_2(t) = u(t) - u(t-1)$, compute the product signal $y(t) = x_1(t) \cdot x_2(t)$ and sketch it neatly. Identify the support of the result.*  **(7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Explicit piecewise forms.**

$$
x_1(t) = \begin{cases} \sin(\pi t), & 0 \leq t \leq 2 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

$$
x_2(t) = \begin{cases} 1, & 0 \leq t < 1 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

**Step 2 — Support intersection.**

$$
\text{supp}(x_1) = [0, 2], \quad \text{supp}(x_2) = [0, 1)
$$

$$
\text{supp}(x_1) \cap \text{supp}(x_2) = [0, 1)
$$

**Step 3 — Pointwise multiplication within the intersection.**

$$
y(t) = \sin(\pi t) \cdot 1 = \sin(\pi t), \quad 0 \leq t < 1
$$

**Step 4 — Piecewise closed form.**

$$
y(t) = \begin{cases} \sin(\pi t), & 0 \leq t < 1 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

**Step 5 — Equivalent compact expression.**

$$
y(t) = \sin(\pi t) \cdot \left[u(t) - u(t-1)\right]
$$

**Step 6 — Sketch description.** The signal rises sinusoidally from $0$ at $t=0$, reaches peak amplitude $1$ at $t = 0.5$, returns to $0$ at $t=1$, and remains zero thereafter. The remaining half-cycle of the original sinusoid (from $t=1$ to $t=2$) has been suppressed.

**Support of $y(t)$:** $[0, 1)$.

---

**(b)** *Explain the concept of DSB-SC amplitude modulation. Starting from $y(t) = m(t) \cdot \cos(\omega_c t)$ with $m(t) = A_m \cos(\omega_m t)$, derive the spectrum components present in the modulated signal.*  **(7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Define the system.** In DSB-SC, the carrier is suppressed; the message modulates the polarity of the carrier sinusoid directly.

**Step 2 — Write the product signal.**

$$
y(t) = A_m \cos(\omega_m t) \cdot \cos(\omega_c t)
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the product-to-sum identity.**

$$
\cos A \cos B = \tfrac{1}{2}\big[\cos(A-B) + \cos(A+B)\big]
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute.**

$$
y(t) = \frac{A_m}{2}\cos((\omega_c - \omega_m)t) + \frac{A_m}{2}\cos((\omega_c + \omega_m)t)
$$

**Step 5 — Identify the spectrum.** Two spectral lines at $\omega = \omega_c - \omega_m$ and $\omega = \omega_c + \omega_m$, each of amplitude $A_m/2$. No component at $\omega = \omega_c$ alone — the carrier is suppressed.

**Step 6 — Engineering interpretation.** This is the **multiplication theorem** of Fourier transforms in action: multiplication in time domain $\Rightarrow$ convolution in frequency domain $\Rightarrow$ two sidebands appear, symmetric about $\omega_c$.

---

#### **Question B (Alternative — 14 Marks)**

**(a)** *Two continuous-time signals are defined as $x_1(t) = u(t+1) - u(t-1)$ and $x_2(t) = u(t) - u(t-3)$. Compute $y(t) = x_1(t) \cdot x_2(t)$ analytically and determine the support of $y(t)$.*  **(7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify supports.**

$$
\text{supp}(x_1) = [-1, 1), \quad \text{supp}(x_2) = [0, 3)
$$

**Step 2 — Intersection of supports.**

$$
[-1, 1) \cap [0, 3) = [0, 1)
$$

**Step 3 — Multiply.**

Within the intersection, both signals equal $1$, so $y(t) = 1$. Outside, $y(t) = 0$.

**Step 4 — Final expression.**

$$
y(t) = u(t) - u(t-1)
$$

**Step 5 — Support of $y(t)$:** $[0, 1)$.

**Step 6 — Sketch:** A unit-amplitude rectangular pulse of width $1$ beginning at $t=0$.

---

**(b)** *Using the trigonometric identity $\cos^2(\theta) = \frac{1}{2}(1 + \cos 2\theta)$, explain how signal multiplication is used to construct a frequency-doubling circuit. State one practical application.*  **(7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Set up the input.** Let $x(t) = \cos(\omega_0 t)$. Multiply it with itself using a squarer circuit (a multiplier with both inputs tied to the same signal).

$$
y(t) = x(t) \cdot x(t) = \cos^2(\omega_0 t)
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the identity.**

$$
y(t) = \frac{1}{2}\Big[1 + \cos(2\omega_0 t)\Big]
$$

**Step 3 — Identify the components.** A DC term $\frac{1}{2}$ and a sinusoidal term at frequency $2\omega_0$ with amplitude $\frac{1}{2}$.

**Step 4 — Block diagram description.** A squarer (multiplier) feeds a high-pass or band-pass filter centered at $2\omega_0$ to remove the DC offset, leaving a clean sinusoid at twice the input frequency.

**Step 5 — Practical application.** Used in **PLL (Phase-Locked Loop) frequency synthesizers**, **microwave transmitters** for generating millimeter-wave carriers, and **digital frequency multiplier circuits**.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting the support-intersection rule** — Many students incorrectly assume that the product exists everywhere. *Always intersect the supports first.*
> 2. **Sign confusion in negative-amplitude signals** — A product of two negative-valued signals is **positive**, not negative. Do not blindly carry the sign of one factor.
> 3. **Conflating multiplication with convolution** — Multiplication is pointwise; convolution is a sliding weighted sum. They are *fundamentally different* operations.
> 4. **Missing the "squaring" identity $\cos^2 \theta$** — Examiners love testing this in the KTU 2024 paper; practice the standard trigonometric product-to-sum conversions.
> 5. **Improper piecewise notation** — Always state the time interval explicitly (e.g., $0 \leq t < 1$) — sloppy bounds lead to mark deductions.
> 6. **Forgetting to mark axis values on the sketch** — A graph without labeled axes and amplitude values is *incomplete* under KTU valuation standards.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Multiplication of Signals**
>
> - **Definition (CT):** $y(t) = x_1(t) \cdot x_2(t)$; **Definition (DT):** $y[n] = x_1[n] \cdot x_2[n]$.
> - **Operation is pointwise and instantaneous** — no integration, no summation, no shifting.
> - **Support of product = Intersection of supports** of the individual signals.
> - **Commutative, Associative, Distributive over addition** — forms an algebraic ring with $+$ and $\cdot$.
> - **Squaring identity:** $x^2(t) = \vert x(t)\vert^2$ for real signals; equals **instantaneous power**.
> - **Trig identities to memorize:**
>   * $\cos A \cos B = \frac{1}{2}[\cos(A-B) + \cos(A+B)]$
>   * $\sin A \sin B = \frac{1}{2}[\cos(A-B) - \cos(A+B)]$
>   * $\sin A \cos B = \frac{1}{2}[\sin(A+B) + \sin(A-B)]$
>   * $\cos^2 A = \frac{1}{2}[1 + \cos 2A]$
> - **Key applications:** Amplitude Modulation (AM), Sampling theorem, Windowing, Frequency translation/mixing, Power/energy computation.
> - **Multiplication Theorem (Fourier):** $x_1(t) \cdot x_2(t) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} \frac{1}{2\pi} X_1(j\omega) * X_2(j\omega)$.
> - **Time-domain multiplier $\Leftrightarrow$ Frequency-domain convolution.**
> - **Multiplier system symbol:** A circle with a multiplication dot (×) inside; two input arrows, one output arrow.
> - **Distinguish from convolution** — convolution slides and sums; multiplication is *purely pointwise*.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Parseval's Relation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST416 - SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Parseval's Relation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Parseval's Relation (Parseval's Theorem)** is a fundamental result in Fourier analysis that establishes the **conservation of energy** between the time domain and the frequency domain representations of a signal. It mathematically states that the total energy (or average power) of a signal, when computed in the time domain, is exactly equal to the total energy (or average power) computed in the frequency domain.

In the context of Signals and Systems, Parseval's Relation is universally expressed for the following transform pairs:

**For Continuous-Time Fourier Transform (CTFT):**

$$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert X(j\omega) \vert^2 \, d\omega$$

**For Discrete-Time Fourier Transform (DTFT):**

$$\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n] \vert^2 = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} \vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert^2 \, d\omega$$

**For Continuous-Time Fourier Series (CTFS):**

$$\frac{1}{T_0} \int_{T_0} \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert a_k \vert^2$$

The quantity $\vert X(j\omega) \vert^2$ is referred to as the **Energy Spectral Density (ESD)** of the signal, and $\vert a_k \vert^2$ represents the **Power Spectral Density (PSD)** coefficients for periodic signals.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: "The Bank Account Balance"**
>
> Imagine your monthly salary is the **time-domain energy** — the money you actually receive and count by hand. Now, imagine your bank breaks down your financial activity into frequency components (rent, food, transport, entertainment) — this is the **frequency-domain energy**. Parseval's Relation is like saying: *"The total amount of money you receive (time domain) is exactly equal to the sum of all categorized expenses (frequency domain)."*
>
> No money is lost in transformation. The signal is the same — we are simply looking at it through a different mathematical "lens."

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!TIP]
> The factor $\frac{1}{2\pi}$ in the CTFT relation and the $\frac{1}{T_0}$ normalization in Fourier Series are **convention-dependent**. Different textbooks use slight variants ($2\pi$ factor handling), but KTU 2024 Scheme follows the **Oppenheim & Willsky convention** as the standard. The metric $\vert X(j\omega) \vert^2$ has units of **energy per unit frequency** ($\text{Joules/Hz}$ or $\text{V}^2\text{s/rad}$).

### 1.4 Why Parseval's Relation Matters in KTU Examinations

- It is a **high-yield topic** appearing almost every semester in Part A (3 marks) and Part B (14 marks).
- It tests the student's understanding of **energy equivalence** between domains.
- It is a critical conceptual bridge between time-domain and frequency-domain analysis.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Underlying Logic of Parseval's Relation

Parseval's Relation emerges from the **orthogonality of complex exponentials**. The continuous-time complex exponentials $e^{j\omega t}$ form an **orthogonal basis** on the infinite interval $(-\infty, \infty)$ under an inner product weighted by the Dirac delta. The discrete-time and Fourier Series cases involve summations over orthogonal basis functions over finite intervals.

The logic unfolds in structured steps:

- **Step 1: Definition of Energy in Time Domain**
  The energy of an absolutely integrable or square-summable signal is defined as $E = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^2 dt$ (CT) or $E = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n] \vert^2$ (DT).

- **Step 2: Express $x(t)$ via Inverse Transform**
  We invoke the inverse CTFT: $x(t) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} X(j\omega) e^{j\omega t} \, d\omega$.

- **Step 3: Compute $\vert x(t) \vert^2$**
  Substitute and expand the squared magnitude as $x(t) \cdot x^*(t)$.

- **Step 4: Interchange Order of Integration**
  Swap the order of integration (Fubini's Theorem applies under convergence conditions).

- **Step 5: Recognize the Dirac Delta Inner Product**
  The inner integral collapses to $2\pi \delta(\omega - \omega')$, eliminating one variable of integration.

- **Step 6: Arrive at Energy Equivalence**
  The result is $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^2 dt = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert X(j\omega) \vert^2 d\omega$.

### 2.2 Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Parseval's Relation is the **mathematical foundation of spectroscopy, audio signal processing, and communication system design**:
>
> - In **telecommunications**, it allows engineers to compute total signal power in the frequency domain, simplifying noise analysis and bandwidth calculations.
> - In **audio engineering**, it provides the energy conservation principle used in **MP3 compression** and **spectral analysis**.
> - In **radar and sonar systems**, the relation is essential for matched filter design, where signal energy in the frequency domain determines detection performance.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> The following table is the **complete Parseval's toolkit** required for KTU 2024 Scheme examinations. Memorize these exactly.

| Transform Type | Time Domain Energy / Power | Frequency Domain Energy / Power | ESD / PSD Term |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **CTFT** (Energy Signals) | $\displaystyle\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt$ | $\displaystyle\frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert X(j\omega) \vert^2 \, d\omega$ | $\vert X(j\omega) \vert^2$ (ESD) |
| **DTFT** (Energy Signals) | $\displaystyle\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n] \vert^2$ | $\displaystyle\frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} \vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert^2 \, d\omega$ | $\vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert^2$ (ESD) |
| **CTFS** (Power Signals) | $\displaystyle\frac{1}{T_0} \int_{T_0} \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt$ | $\displaystyle\sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert a_k \vert^2$ | $\vert a_k \vert^2$ (PSD) |
| **DTFS** (Power Signals) | $\displaystyle\frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \vert x[n] \vert^2$ | $\displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \vert a_k \vert^2$ | $\vert a_k \vert^2$ (PSD) |

### 2.4 Key Boundary Conditions

- The signal $x(t)$ must be **absolutely square-integrable** (finite energy) for CTFT Parseval's Relation to hold.
- For periodic signals, the **average power** (not energy) is conserved.
- The frequency-domain integral must be performed over **all frequencies** where $X(j\omega)$ is non-zero.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Parseval's Relation for CTFT

> [!NOTE]
> We will derive the relation $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^2 dt = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert X(j\omega) \vert^2 d\omega$ from first principles. Every algebraic step is explicitly shown.

**Starting Point: Time Domain Energy**

The total energy of a continuous-time signal $x(t)$ is defined as:

$$E = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) \cdot x^*(t) \, dt$$

**Step 1: Substitute the Inverse CTFT for $x^*(t)$**

The forward CTFT is $X(j\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) e^{-j\omega t} dt$. Taking the complex conjugate of the inverse CTFT:

$$x^*(t) = \left( \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} X(j\omega) e^{j\omega t} d\omega \right)^*$$

Applying the property that $(f \cdot g)^* = f^* \cdot g^*$ and reversing the limits (which introduces a sign change handled by the oddness of integration), and noting $\left(e^{j\omega t}\right)^* = e^{-j\omega t}$ and $X^*(j\omega) = X(-j\omega)$:

$$x^*(t) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} X^*(j\omega) e^{-j\omega t} \, d\omega$$

**Step 2: Substitute Both Expressions into the Energy Integral**

$$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) \cdot x^*(t) \, dt = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) \left[ \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} X^*(j\omega) e^{-j\omega t} \, d\omega \right] dt$$

**Step 3: Interchange the Order of Integration**

Since the signal has finite energy, Fubini's Theorem permits the interchange:

$$E = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} X^*(j\omega) \left[ \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) e^{-j\omega t} \, dt \right] d\omega$$

**Step 4: Recognize the Inner Integral as the Forward CTFT**

The bracketed term is precisely $X(j\omega)$ by the forward transform definition:

$$E = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} X^*(j\omega) \cdot X(j\omega) \, d\omega$$

**Step 5: Combine into Squared Magnitude**

Since $X^*(j\omega) \cdot X(j\omega) = \vert X(j\omega) \vert^2$:

$$E = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert X(j\omega) \vert^2 \, d\omega$$

**Step 6: Equate the Two Expressions**

$$\boxed{\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert X(j\omega) \vert^2 \, d\omega}$$

This is **Parseval's Relation for the Continuous-Time Fourier Transform** and is the **Energy Conservation Theorem**. $\blacksquare$

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Parseval's Relation for Fourier Series

**Starting Point: Average Power in Time Domain**

The average power over one period $T_0$ is:

$$P = \frac{1}{T_0} \int_{T_0} \vert x(t) \vert^2 dt$$

**Step 1: Express $x(t)$ in Fourier Series**

$$x(t) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} a_k e^{jk\omega_0 t}$$

**Step 2: Compute $\vert x(t) \vert^2$**

$$\vert x(t) \vert^2 = x(t) \cdot x^*(t) = \left( \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} a_k e^{jk\omega_0 t} \right) \left( \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} a_m^* e^{-jm\omega_0 t} \right)$$

**Step 3: Expand the Double Summation**

$$\vert x(t) \vert^2 = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} a_k a_m^* e^{j(k-m)\omega_0 t}$$

**Step 4: Integrate Over One Period and Divide by $T_0$**

$$P = \frac{1}{T_0} \sum_{k} \sum_{m} a_k a_m^* \int_{T_0} e^{j(k-m)\omega_0 t} \, dt$$

**Step 5: Apply Orthogonality of Complex Exponentials**

The integral evaluates to $T_0$ if $k = m$, and $0$ otherwise:

$$\int_{T_0} e^{j(k-m)\omega_0 t} \, dt = T_0 \cdot \delta_{km}$$

**Step 6: Simplify Using the Kronecker Delta**

$$P = \frac{1}{T_0} \sum_{k} \sum_{m} a_k a_m^* \cdot T_0 \cdot \delta_{km} = \sum_{k} a_k a_k^* = \sum_{k} \vert a_k \vert^2$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{\frac{1}{T_0} \int_{T_0} \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert a_k \vert^2} \quad \blacksquare$$

### 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Board Pattern)

**Problem:** A signal $x(t)$ has the Fourier Transform $X(j\omega) = \text{rect}\left(\frac{\omega}{4}\right)$ — i.e., a rectangular pulse of height $1$ over $[-2, 2]$. Calculate the total energy of $x(t)$ using Parseval's Relation.

**Solution:**

Since $\vert X(j\omega) \vert^2 = 1$ for $\omega \in [-2, 2]$ and $0$ otherwise:

$$E = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-2}^{2} (1)^2 \, d\omega = \frac{1}{2\pi} \cdot [4] = \frac{2}{\pi} \text{ Joules}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Note:** Students often forget the $\frac{1}{2\pi}$ factor in CTFT Parseval's Relation. Always double-check the transform convention used in your course.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Flow Diagram: Energy Conservation Across Domains

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the conceptual flow of Parseval's Relation, illustrating how energy is preserved between the time and frequency domains through the Fourier Transform.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Time Domain Signal x of t] -->|Define Energy| B[Compute integral of magnitude squared of x of t dt]
    C[Frequency Domain Spectrum X of j omega] -->|Define Spectral Energy| D[Compute 1 over 2pi integral of magnitude squared of X of j omega d omega]
    B -->|Parsevals Theorem| E{Energy Equivalence Statement}
    D -->|Parsevals Theorem| E
    E --> F[Time Domain Energy equals Frequency Domain Energy]
    E --> G[Quantity magnitude squared of X of j omega is called Energy Spectral Density]
    E --> H[Total Energy is Conserved Under Fourier Transform]
    F --> I[Application: Noise Analysis, Filter Design, Compression]
    G --> I
    H --> I
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Parseval's Verification Pipeline

The following diagram shows a step-by-step functional pipeline for verifying Parseval's Relation in a numerical computation context (signal analysis workflow).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Input Signal x of t] --> P2[Step 1: Compute Time Domain Energy]
    P2 --> P3[Step 2: Apply CTFT to obtain X of j omega]
    P3 --> P4[Step 3: Compute Frequency Domain Energy]
    P4 --> P5{Step 4: Numerical Equality Check}
    P5 -->|Match| P6[Parsevals Relation Verified]
    P5 -->|Mismatch| P7[Check Transform Convention and Computation]
    P7 --> P2
    P6 --> P8[Output: Energy Conservation Confirmed]
```

### 4.3 Comparative Spectrum: ESD vs. PSD Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Signal Classification] --> S2{Energy or Power Signal}
    S2 -->|Finite Energy non-zero over finite duration| S3[Energy Signal]
    S2 -->|Infinite Energy finite average power| S4[Power Signal]
    S3 --> S5[Use CTFT or DTFT Parsevals Relation]
    S4 --> S6[Use CTFS or DTFS Parsevals Relation]
    S5 --> S7[ESD equals magnitude squared of X of j omega]
    S6 --> S8[PSD equals magnitude squared of a_k]
    S7 --> S9[Integral over all frequencies]
    S8 --> S10[Sum over all harmonic indices]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** The flow diagrams above are functional representations of the analytical workflow. They are designed to give a quick visual reference for distinguishing between energy and power signals and the corresponding Parseval's formulation to apply in KTU problems.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> **State Parseval's Theorem for a continuous-time signal and its Fourier Transform. Mention its significance.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Parseval's Theorem states that the total energy of a continuous-time signal $x(t)$ computed in the time domain equals the total energy computed in the frequency domain, expressed as:
$$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^2 dt = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert X(j\omega) \vert^2 d\omega$$
**Significance (1 Mark):** It establishes energy conservation between the time and frequency domains, which is foundational for spectral analysis, filter design, and computing signal energy without inverse transformation.

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **What is Energy Spectral Density (ESD)? How is it related to Parseval's Relation?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The Energy Spectral Density (ESD) of a signal $x(t)$ is defined as the squared magnitude of its Fourier Transform, $\vert X(j\omega) \vert^2$, and represents the distribution of signal energy as a function of frequency (in units of energy per unit frequency).
**Relation (2 Marks):** Parseval's Relation states that the **total energy** of the signal equals $\frac{1}{2\pi}$ times the **integral of ESD over all frequencies**:
$$E = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert X(j\omega) \vert^2 d\omega$$

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> **Q3. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1, Internal Choice Pattern]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** State and prove Parseval's Theorem for a continuous-time Fourier Series. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A periodic signal $x(t)$ has Fourier Series coefficients $a_k$ given by $a_k = \frac{1}{\vert k \vert + 1}$ for $k \neq 0$ and $a_0 = 1$. Calculate the average power of the signal using Parseval's Theorem. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Derive Parseval's Relation for a continuous-time Fourier Transform. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** The Fourier Transform of a signal $x(t)$ is $X(j\omega) = \frac{1}{1 + \omega^2}$. Compute the total energy of the signal $x(t)$ using Parseval's Relation. **(7 Marks)**

---

### 5.3 Complete Model Solutions for Part B

#### Solution to Question A:

**(a) Proof of Parseval's Theorem for CTFS (7 Marks):**

Given $x(t) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} a_k e^{jk\omega_0 t}$ with $\omega_0 = \frac{2\pi}{T_0}$.

The average power is:
$$P = \frac{1}{T_0} \int_{T_0} \vert x(t) \vert^2 dt = \frac{1}{T_0} \int_{T_0} x(t) x^*(t) dt$$

Substituting the series expansion:
$$P = \frac{1}{T_0} \int_{T_0} \left( \sum_{k} a_k e^{jk\omega_0 t} \right) \left( \sum_{m} a_m^* e^{-jm\omega_0 t} \right) dt$$

Expanding the product:
$$P = \frac{1}{T_0} \sum_{k} \sum_{m} a_k a_m^* \int_{T_0} e^{j(k-m)\omega_0 t} dt$$

By the orthogonality property:
$$\int_{T_0} e^{j(k-m)\omega_0 t} dt = \begin{cases} T_0, & k = m \\ 0, & k \neq m \end{cases}$$

Substituting back:
$$P = \frac{1}{T_0} \sum_{k} a_k a_k^* \cdot T_0 = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert a_k \vert^2$$

Therefore:
$$\boxed{P = \frac{1}{T_0} \int_{T_0} \vert x(t) \vert^2 dt = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert a_k \vert^2} \quad \blacksquare$$

**(b) Computation of Average Power (7 Marks):**

Using Parseval's Theorem for Fourier Series:
$$P = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert a_k \vert^2$$

Given $a_0 = 1$, $a_k = \frac{1}{\vert k \vert + 1}$ for $k \neq 0$:

$$P = \vert a_0 \vert^2 + \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} \left( \vert a_k \vert^2 + \vert a_{-k} \vert^2 \right)$$

Since $a_k$ depends on $\vert k \vert$, we have $a_k = a_{-k}$, so:
$$P = 1 + 2 \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} \left( \frac{1}{k+1} \right)^2 = 1 + 2 \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{(k+1)^2}$$

Let $n = k+1$, so when $k=1$, $n=2$:
$$P = 1 + 2 \sum_{n=2}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n^2} = 1 + 2 \left( \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n^2} - 1 \right)$$

Using the Basel problem result $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n^2} = \frac{\pi^2}{6}$:
$$P = 1 + 2 \left( \frac{\pi^2}{6} - 1 \right) = 1 + \frac{\pi^2}{3} - 2 = \frac{\pi^2}{3} - 1$$

$$\boxed{P = \frac{\pi^2}{3} - 1 \approx 2.29 \text{ Watts}}$$

---

#### Solution to Question B:

**(a) Derivation of Parseval's Relation for CTFT (7 Marks):**

The detailed derivation is provided in **Section 3.1** of these notes. The key steps are:

1. Start with $E = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^2 dt = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) x^*(t) dt$.
2. Substitute $x^*(t) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} X^*(j\omega) e^{-j\omega t} d\omega$.
3. Interchange integration order: $E = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} X^*(j\omega) \left[ \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) e^{-j\omega t} dt \right] d\omega$.
4. Recognize the forward CTFT in the inner integral: $E = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert X(j\omega) \vert^2 d\omega$.

**Final Result:**
$$\boxed{\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^2 dt = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert X(j\omega) \vert^2 d\omega} \quad \blacksquare$$

**(b) Energy Computation using Parseval's Relation (7 Marks):**

Given $X(j\omega) = \frac{1}{1 + \omega^2}$, we have:
$$\vert X(j\omega) \vert^2 = \frac{1}{(1 + \omega^2)^2}$$

Using Parseval's Relation:
$$E = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{1}{(1 + \omega^2)^2} d\omega$$

We use the standard integral result:
$$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{dx}{(1 + x^2)^2} = \frac{\pi}{2}$$

This is evaluated using the substitution $x = \tan\theta$ or contour integration. Therefore:
$$E = \frac{1}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{\pi}{2} = \frac{1}{4}$$

$$\boxed{E = \frac{1}{4} \text{ Joules}}$$

---

### 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in KTU Valuation:**
>
> 1. **Missing the $\frac{1}{2\pi}$ factor** in CTFT Parseval's Relation: This is the **#1 mistake** flagged by KTU examiners. Students often write $E = \int \vert X(j\omega) \vert^2 d\omega$, losing **1 to 2 marks**.
>
> 2. **Confusing ESD with PSD**: Energy Spectral Density applies to **aperiodic energy signals** (CTFT/DTFT context), while Power Spectral Density applies to **periodic power signals** (Fourier Series context). Mixing them leads to a **3-mark penalty**.
>
> 3. **Skipping the orthogonality step** in Fourier Series derivation: The integral $\int_{T_0} e^{j(k-m)\omega_0 t} dt = T_0 \delta_{km}$ must be **explicitly shown**, not just stated. This omission typically costs **2 marks**.
>
> 4. **Forgetting to use Basel problem result** $\sum \frac{1}{n^2} = \frac{\pi^2}{6}$: When a problem yields such a series, students must either derive it, cite it, or compute it numerically. Skipping the final numerical evaluation costs **1 mark**.
>
> 5. **Not specifying units** in the final answer: KTU examiners award **half a mark** for correctly stating units (Joules for energy, Watts for power).

---

### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Parseval's Relation:**

- Parseval's Relation is the **energy/power conservation theorem** between time and frequency domains.
- For **CTFT**: $E = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int \vert X(j\omega) \vert^2 d\omega = \int \vert x(t) \vert^2 dt$.
- For **DTFT**: $E = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} \vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert^2 d\omega = \sum \vert x[n] \vert^2$.
- For **CTFS**: $P = \sum \vert a_k \vert^2 = \frac{1}{T_0} \int_{T_0} \vert x(t) \vert^2 dt$.
- For **DTFS**: $P = \sum \vert a_k \vert^2 = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n} \vert x[n] \vert^2$.
- The $\frac{1}{2\pi}$ factor is **mandatory** in CTFT Parseval's Relation.
- The derivation hinges on the **orthogonality of complex exponentials**.
- $\vert X(j\omega) \vert^2$ = **Energy Spectral Density (ESD)**.
- $\vert a_k \vert^2$ = **Power Spectral Density (PSD)** for periodic signals.
- Parseval's Relation is **not used for power signals with infinite energy** (e.g., sinusoids) — only for **finite-power periodic signals**.
- Always check whether the problem involves **energy or power signals** before selecting the appropriate formula.
- Memorize the **Basel problem** result: $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n^2} = \frac{\pi^2}{6}$ — it appears frequently.
- The standard integral $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{dx}{(1+x^2)^2} = \frac{\pi}{2}$ is a **high-frequency** Parseval's application.
- KTU questions often combine Parseval's Relation with **modulation, sampling, or filter design** topics — be prepared for application-based problems.
- The relation is **always an equality** (not inequality) for valid finite-energy or finite-power signals.
- **Valuation Tip:** Always write the final answer with **units** and **box it** — KTU examiners specifically reward this presentation style.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Even/Odd Symmetry
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST416 - SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Even/Odd Symmetry

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Even Symmetry

A continuous-time signal $x(t)$ is said to be an **even signal** (or possesses even symmetry) if and only if it satisfies the following mathematical condition for all values of time $t$:

$$x(-t) = x(t) \quad \forall \; t \in \mathbb{R}$$

The signal is perfectly mirrored about the **vertical axis** (the ordinate, $t = 0$). Replacing $t$ with $-t$ produces exactly the same waveform.

## 1.2 Formal Definition of Odd Symmetry

A continuous-time signal $x(t)$ is classified as an **odd signal** (possessing odd symmetry) if and only if:

$$x(-t) = -x(t) \quad \forall \; t \in \mathbb{R}$$

The signal is **anti-symmetric** about the vertical axis. The waveform for $t > 0$, when folded over to $t < 0$, becomes an inverted mirror image. A direct consequence: every odd signal must pass through the origin, i.e., $x(0) = 0$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The decomposition of any arbitrary signal into its even and odd components is a high-frequency exam topic and forms the bedrock for solving Fourier Series, Fourier Transform, and LTI system problems in subsequent modules.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Imagine a bathroom mirror placed exactly at $t = 0$.**

- **Even Signal** → Like a perfectly symmetrical human face. Your left cheek is the mirror image of your right cheek. If you fold the face along the nose line (the $t = 0$ axis), both halves overlap **exactly**. The classic textbook example is $x(t) = \cos(\omega_0 t)$ or $x(t) = t^2$.

- **Odd Signal** → Like a propeller blade or the letter **S**. The left half is the **inverted** reflection of the right half. If you fold it along the vertical axis, one half flips upside down to overlap the other. The classic textbook example is $x(t) = \sin(\omega_0 t)$ or $x(t) = t^3$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Almost no real-world physical signal is purely even or odd. However, *every* signal can be uniquely broken down into a sum of an even part and an odd part. This decomposition is a powerful analytical tool.

## 1.4 Standard Canonical Examples

| Signal $x(t)$ | Symmetry Type | Verification |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| $t^2$ | Even | $(-t)^2 = t^2$ |
| $t^3$ | Odd | $(-t)^3 = -t^3$ |
| $\cos(t)$ | Even | $\cos(-t) = \cos(t)$ |
| $\sin(t)$ | Odd | $\sin(-t) = -\sin(t)$ |
| $e^{-t} u(t)$ | **Neither** | $e^{t}u(-t) \neq e^{-t}u(t)$ and $\neq -e^{-t}u(t)$ |
| $e^{-\vert t \vert}$ | Even | $e^{-\vert -t \vert} = e^{-\vert t \vert}$ |
| $t \, e^{-\vert t \vert}$ | Odd | $(-t)e^{-\vert -t \vert} = -t e^{-\vert t \vert}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> The **unit step function** $u(t)$ is **neither** even nor odd, which makes it a common trick question in KTU examinations. Always verify by substituting $-t$ before concluding.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Fundamental Decomposition Theorem

Any arbitrary signal $x(t)$ — whether it is even, odd, or neither — can be uniquely expressed as the sum of an even component $x_e(t)$ and an odd component $x_o(t)$:

$$x(t) = x_e(t) + x_o(t)$$

where the two components are mathematically extracted using:

$$x_e(t) = \frac{x(t) + x(-t)}{2}$$

$$x_o(t) = \frac{x(t) - x(-t)}{2}$$

### Why Does This Work? (The Logic Steps)

1. **Step 1 — Fold the signal:** Take the original signal $x(t)$ and form its mirror image $x(-t)$.
2. **Step 2 — Add the pair:** Adding $x(t)$ and $x(-t)$ cancels all odd-symmetric contributions (because they carry opposite signs) and doubles the even-symmetric ones. Dividing by $2$ recovers $x_e(t)$.
3. **Step 3 — Subtract the pair:** Subtracting $x(-t)$ from $x(t)$ cancels all even-symmetric contributions and doubles the odd-symmetric ones. Dividing by $2$ recovers $x_o(t)$.
4. **Step 4 — Summation:** Adding both extracted components back reconstructs the original $x(t)$ exactly.

## 2.2 Multiplicative Algebra of Symmetry

When you multiply two signals together, the resulting symmetry follows a strict set of rules:

| Signal A | Signal B | Product $A \times B$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Even | Even | **Even** |
| Odd | Odd | **Even** |
| Even | Odd | **Odd** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Multiplication Rule Logic:** The even function acts as a "do-nothing" identity in the sign-change sense: $(-1) \times (-1) = +1$. Two odd factors together flip the sign twice, returning to positive (even). An even times an odd retains the single sign flip, hence odd.

## 2.3 Integral and Energy/Power Properties

### Property 1 — Area of an Odd Signal
For any odd signal $x_o(t)$, the integral over a symmetric interval is always zero:

$$\int_{-A}^{A} x_o(t) \, dt = 0$$

**Reason:** The positive area for $t > 0$ is perfectly cancelled by the equal-magnitude negative area for $t < 0$.

### Property 2 — Area of an Even Signal
For an even signal $x_e(t)$, the integral over a symmetric interval is twice the integral from $0$ to $A$:

$$\int_{-A}^{A} x_e(t) \, dt = 2 \int_{0}^{A} x_e(t) \, dt$$

### Property 3 — DC Value (Average) of an Odd Signal
The average value (DC component) of any purely odd signal over a symmetric interval is always **0**.

### Property 4 — Cross Power Between Even and Odd
The product of an even signal $x_e(t)$ and an odd signal $x_o(t)$ is itself odd. Therefore, integrating this product over a symmetric interval also yields zero:

$$\int_{-A}^{A} x_e(t) \cdot x_o(t) \, dt = 0$$

This orthogonality-like property is heavily exploited in **Fourier Series** analysis.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Property / Formula | Mathematical Expression |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Even Symmetry Test | $x(-t) = x(t)$ |
| 2 | Odd Symmetry Test | $x(-t) = -x(t)$ |
| 3 | Even Component Extraction | $x_e(t) = \tfrac{1}{2}\big[x(t) + x(-t)\big]$ |
| 4 | Odd Component Extraction | $x_o(t) = \tfrac{1}{2}\big[x(t) - x(-t)\big]$ |
| 5 | Signal Reconstruction | $x(t) = x_e(t) + x_o(t)$ |
| 6 | Even $\times$ Even | Even |
| 7 | Odd $\times$ Odd | Even |
| 8 | Even $\times$ Odd | Odd |
| 9 | Area of Odd Signal | $\int_{-A}^{A} x_o(t)\,dt = 0$ |
| 10 | Area of Even Signal | $\int_{-A}^{A} x_e(t)\,dt = 2\int_{0}^{A} x_e(t)\,dt$ |
| 11 | Odd Constraint at Origin | $x_o(0) = 0$ |
| 12 | Energy/Power Decomposition | $E_x = E_{xe} + E_{xo}$ (no cross term for real signals) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Utility:** In communication engineering, even-symmetric signals (like raised-cosine pulses) are favored because they produce zero **Inter-Symbol Interference (ISI)** at sampling instants. Odd-symmetric signals are used in Hilbert transform and single-sideband (SSB) modulation. In control systems, odd nonlinearities preserve odd-harmonic generation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Even/Odd Decomposition Formulae

### Setup

Let us **assume** (as a hypothesis) that any arbitrary real signal $x(t)$ can be written as:

$$x(t) = x_e(t) + x_o(t) \quad \quad \text{...(Equation 1)}$$

where $x_e(t)$ is even and $x_o(t)$ is odd by definition.

### Step 1 — Fold the equation by substituting $-t$

Replacing $t$ with $-t$ throughout:

$$x(-t) = x_e(-t) + x_o(-t) \quad \quad \text{...(Equation 2)}$$

### Step 2 — Apply symmetry definitions

By definition:
- $x_e(-t) = x_e(t)$ (even)
- $x_o(-t) = -x_o(t)$ (odd)

Substituting these into Equation 2:

$$x(-t) = x_e(t) - x_o(t) \quad \quad \text{...(Equation 3)}$$

### Step 3 — Solve the linear system for $x_e(t)$ and $x_o(t)$

We now have a system of two linear equations (Equation 1 and Equation 3) in two unknowns $x_e(t)$ and $x_o(t)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x(t) &= x_e(t) + x_o(t) \\
x(-t) &= x_e(t) - x_o(t)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Adding the two equations** (to eliminate $x_o(t)$):

$$x(t) + x(-t) = 2\,x_e(t)$$

Solving for $x_e(t)$:

$$x_e(t) = \frac{x(t) + x(-t)}{2}$$

**Subtracting the second from the first** (to eliminate $x_e(t)$):

$$x(t) - x(-t) = 2\,x_o(t)$$

Solving for $x_o(t)$:

$$x_o(t) = \frac{x(t) - x(-t)}{2}$$

### Step 4 — Verification (Proof of Correctness)

We must verify that the extracted $x_e(t)$ is truly even and $x_o(t)$ is truly odd.

**Check that $x_e(t)$ is even:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_e(-t) &= \frac{x(-t) + x(-(-t))}{2} \\
&= \frac{x(-t) + x(t)}{2} \\
&= \frac{x(t) + x(-t)}{2} \\
&= x_e(t) \;\; \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Check that $x_o(t)$ is odd:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_o(-t) &= \frac{x(-t) - x(-(-t))}{2} \\
&= \frac{x(-t) - x(t)}{2} \\
&= -\frac{x(t) - x(-t)}{2} \\
&= -x_o(t) \;\; \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The decomposition is **unique**. There is only one way to split a given signal into an even-plus-odd pair.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Worked-Out Problem

### Problem

Given the signal:

$$x(t) = e^{-t}\,u(t)$$

where $u(t)$ is the unit step. Find the even and odd parts $x_e(t)$ and $x_o(t)$.

### Step 1 — Express $x(t)$ explicitly

Since $u(t) = 1$ for $t \ge 0$ and $0$ for $t < 0$:

$$x(t) = \begin{cases} e^{-t}, & t \ge 0 \\ 0, & t < 0 \end{cases}$$

### Step 2 — Compute the folded signal $x(-t)$

Replacing $t$ with $-t$:

$$x(-t) = e^{-(-t)}\,u(-t) = e^{t}\,u(-t)$$

In explicit piecewise form:

$$x(-t) = \begin{cases} 0, & t > 0 \\ 1, & t = 0 \\ e^{t}, & t < 0 \end{cases}$$

### Step 3 — Compute the even part $x_e(t)$

Using $x_e(t) = \tfrac{1}{2}\big[x(t) + x(-t)\big]$:

**For $t > 0$:**

$$x_e(t) = \frac{1}{2}\big[e^{-t} + 0\big] = \frac{1}{2}e^{-t}$$

**For $t = 0$:**

$$x_e(0) = \frac{1}{2}[1 + 1] = 1$$

**For $t < 0$:**

$$x_e(t) = \frac{1}{2}\big[0 + e^{t}\big] = \frac{1}{2}e^{t}$$

Combining into compact form:

$$x_e(t) = \frac{1}{2}e^{-\vert t \vert}$$

### Step 4 — Compute the odd part $x_o(t)$

Using $x_o(t) = \tfrac{1}{2}\big[x(t) - x(-t)\big]$:

**For $t > 0$:**

$$x_o(t) = \frac{1}{2}\big[e^{-t} - 0\big] = \frac{1}{2}e^{-t}$$

**For $t = 0$:**

$$x_o(0) = \frac{1}{2}[1 - 1] = 0$$

**For $t < 0$:**

$$x_o(t) = \frac{1}{2}\big[0 - e^{t}\big] = -\frac{1}{2}e^{t}$$

Combining into compact form:

$$x_o(t) = \frac{1}{2}\,\text{sgn}(t)\,e^{-\vert t \vert}$$

where $\text{sgn}(t)$ is the signum function: $+1$ for $t>0$, $-1$ for $t<0$, and $0$ at $t=0$.

### Step 5 — Final Verification

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_e(t) + x_o(t) &= \frac{1}{2}e^{-\vert t \vert} + \frac{1}{2}\,\text{sgn}(t)\,e^{-\vert t \vert} \\
&= \frac{1}{2}e^{-\vert t \vert}\big[1 + \text{sgn}(t)\big]
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $1 + \text{sgn}(t) = 2u(t) - 1 + \text{sgn}(t)$... More directly:

For $t > 0$: $\;x_e + x_o = \tfrac{1}{2}e^{-t} + \tfrac{1}{2}e^{-t} = e^{-t} = x(t) \;\; \checkmark$

For $t < 0$: $\;x_e + x_o = \tfrac{1}{2}e^{t} - \tfrac{1}{2}e^{t} = 0 = x(t) \;\; \checkmark$

The decomposition is correct.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Symmetry Identification Checklist

Use this decision matrix in the examination:

| Observation about $x(t)$ | Conclusion |
| :--- | :--- |
| $x(-t) = x(t)$ identically | Signal is **Even** |
| $x(-t) = -x(t)$ identically | Signal is **Odd** |
| $x(t)$ has no symmetry but can be folded | Use the **decomposition formula** |
| $x(0) \neq 0$ but $x(-t) = -x(t)$ | **Impossible** — signal cannot be odd |
| $x(t)$ contains both $\cos$ and $\sin$ terms | Neither purely even nor odd |
| Sum of an even and odd function | Generally **neither** |
| $\int_{-A}^{A} x(t)\,dt = 0$ | Possibly odd (but not sufficient alone) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Block Diagram — Even/Odd Decomposition Engine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputA["Input Signal x of t"] --> adderA["Adder Sum"]
    inputB["Folded Signal x of minus t"] --> adderA
    inputA --> adderB["Subtractor Difference"]
    inputB --> adderB
    adderA --> scaleA["Scale by 1 over 2"]
    scaleA --> outputA["Even Part x sub e of t"]
    adderB --> scaleB["Scale by 1 over 2"]
    scaleB --> outputB["Odd Part x sub o of t"]
    outputA --> recomb["Adder Reconstruction"]
    outputB --> recomb
    recomb --> finalout["Reconstructed x of t"]
    inputB --> mirror["Time Reversal Block"]
    mirror --> inputB
```

## 4.2 Symmetry Decision Flowchart (Examination Strategy)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["Examine Signal x of t"] --> q1{"Does x of minus t equal x of t"}
    q1 -->|Yes| even["Signal is EVEN"]
    q1 -->|No| q2{"Does x of minus t equal minus x of t"}
    q2 -->|Yes| odd["Signal is ODD"]
    q2 -->|No| neither["NEITHER apply decomposition"]
    even --> eout["Use even properties integrals and symmetry"]
    odd --> oout["Note x of 0 equals 0 integral equals 0"]
    neither --> decomp["Compute x sub e equals half of x plus x minus t and x sub o equals half of x minus x minus t"]
    decomp --> verify["Verify x equals x sub e plus x sub o"]
```

## 4.3 Visual Schematics of Canonical Even and Odd Signals

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph evenBlock["EVEN SYMMETRY EXAMPLES"]
        e1["x of t equals cos omega zero t"] --> em1["Mirror Symmetric about y axis"]
        e2["x of t equals t squared"] --> em2["Mirror Symmetric about y axis"]
        e3["x of t equals e to the power minus mod t"] --> em3["Mirror Symmetric about y axis"]
    end
    subgraph oddBlock["ODD SYMMETRY EXAMPLES"]
        o1["x of t equals sin omega zero t"] --> om1["Antisymmetric about y axis passes through origin"]
        o2["x of t equals t cubed"] --> om2["Antisymmetric about y axis passes through origin"]
        o3["x of t equals t times e to power minus mod t"] --> om3["Antisymmetric about y axis passes through origin"]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** The arrows show the mapping from mathematical form to visual symmetry property. In your answer scripts, you should sketch the actual waveform with $t$ on the horizontal axis and amplitude on the vertical axis to score full marks in the descriptive portions.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q:** Define even and odd signals. State any two properties of each.

**Model Answer:**

An **even signal** satisfies $x(-t) = x(t)$ for all $t$. The signal is symmetric about the vertical (amplitude) axis. **Properties:** (i) Even signals require no folding — they look identical on both sides of $t = 0$; (ii) The integral of an even signal over a symmetric interval $[-A, A]$ equals twice its integral over $[0, A]$, i.e., $\int_{-A}^{A} x_e(t) dt = 2 \int_{0}^{A} x_e(t) dt$.

An **odd signal** satisfies $x(-t) = -x(t)$ for all $t$. The signal is anti-symmetric about the vertical axis. **Properties:** (i) Every odd signal must satisfy $x(0) = 0$; (ii) The integral (and hence the average/DC value) of any odd signal over a symmetric interval is zero, i.e., $\int_{-A}^{A} x_o(t) dt = 0$.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**Q:** Determine whether the signal $x(t) = \cos(t) + \sin(t) + t$ is even, odd, or neither. Justify.

**Model Answer:**

Test each component separately against $x(-t)$:
- $\cos(-t) = \cos(t)$ → **even**
- $\sin(-t) = -\sin(t)$ → **odd**
- $(-t) = -t$ → **odd**

The signal is a **sum of even and odd components**, hence it is **neither even nor odd**. A sum is generally neither unless all terms share the same symmetry. Algebraic confirmation:

$$x(-t) = \cos(-t) + \sin(-t) + (-t) = \cos(t) - \sin(t) - t$$

Since $x(-t) \neq x(t)$ and $x(-t) \neq -x(t)$, the signal is **neither**.

---

## Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Choice Option 1

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** *(7 Marks)* Define even and odd symmetry of continuous-time signals. Show that any arbitrary signal $x(t)$ can be expressed as the sum of its even and odd components, $x_e(t)$ and $x_o(t)$. Derive the formulae for $x_e(t)$ and $x_o(t)$ in terms of $x(t)$ and $x(-t)$.

**(b)** *(7 Marks)* For the signal $x(t) = (t+2)\,u(t+2)$, find and sketch the even and odd components.

### Model Solution to (a):

**Definitions:**
- **Even signal:** $x_e(t) = x_e(-t)$. Mirror symmetric about the vertical axis.
- **Odd signal:** $x_o(t) = -x_o(-t)$. Anti-symmetric about the vertical axis; always passes through origin.

**Decomposition derivation** is identical to Section 3.1 of these notes. Students must present:

1. The assumption $x(t) = x_e(t) + x_o(t)$ ... (Eq. 1)
2. The folded version $x(-t) = x_e(t) - x_o(t)$ ... (Eq. 2)
3. Solve the linear system:
   - Adding: $x(t) + x(-t) = 2 x_e(t) \Rightarrow x_e(t) = \tfrac{1}{2}[x(t) + x(-t)]$
   - Subtracting: $x(t) - x(-t) = 2 x_o(t) \Rightarrow x_o(t) = \tfrac{1}{2}[x(t) - x(-t)]$
4. Verification that the derived $x_e(t)$ is even and $x_o(t)$ is odd.

### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1 — Express $x(t)$ piecewise:**

$$x(t) = (t+2)\,u(t+2) = \begin{cases} t+2, & t \ge -2 \\ 0, & t < -2 \end{cases}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the folded signal $x(-t)$:**

$$x(-t) = (-t+2)\,u(-t+2) = (2-t)\,u(2-t)$$

In explicit piecewise form:

$$x(-t) = \begin{cases} 2-t, & t \le 2 \\ 0, & t > 2 \end{cases}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the even part $x_e(t) = \tfrac{1}{2}[x(t) + x(-t)]$ by partitioning into three regions:**

| Region | $x(t)$ | $x(-t)$ | $x_e(t)$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $t < -2$ | $0$ | $2-t$ | $(2-t)/2$ |
| $-2 \le t \le 2$ | $t+2$ | $2-t$ | $\tfrac{1}{2}[(t+2)+(2-t)] = 2$ |
| $t > 2$ | $t+2$ | $0$ | $(t+2)/2$ |

So:

$$x_e(t) = \begin{cases} (2-t)/2, & t < -2 \\ 2, & -2 \le t \le 2 \\ (t+2)/2, & t > 2 \end{cases}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the odd part $x_o(t) = \tfrac{1}{2}[x(t) - x(-t)]$:**

| Region | $x(t)$ | $x(-t)$ | $x_o(t)$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $t < -2$ | $0$ | $2-t$ | $-(2-t)/2 = (t-2)/2$ |
| $-2 \le t \le 2$ | $t+2$ | $2-t$ | $\tfrac{1}{2}[(t+2)-(2-t)] = t$ |
| $t > 2$ | $t+2$ | $0$ | $(t+2)/2$ |

So:

$$x_o(t) = \begin{cases} (t-2)/2, & t < -2 \\ t, & -2 \le t \le 2 \\ (t+2)/2, & t > 2 \end{cases}$$

**Step 5 — Sketching:** Students should plot $x_e(t)$ as a trapezoidal wave (constant value $2$ in $[-2, 2]$, linearly decaying outside), and $x_o(t)$ as a piecewise linear function passing through the origin.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Choice Option 2

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**(a)** *(7 Marks)* Discuss the properties of even and odd signals with suitable examples. Explain how the multiplication of two even and two odd signals produces an even function.

**(b)** *(7 Marks)* For the signal $x(t) = e^{-2t}\cos(3t)\,u(t)$, find the even and odd components and verify that $x(t) = x_e(t) + x_o(t)$.

### Model Solution to (a):

**Properties of Even Signals:**
- $x(-t) = x(t)$
- $x(0)$ can be any value
- Area: $\int_{-A}^{A} x_e(t) dt = 2\int_0^A x_e(t) dt$
- Examples: $\cos(\omega t)$, $t^2$, $e^{-|t|}$, $\delta(t)$ (impulse is technically even)

**Properties of Odd Signals:**
- $x(-t) = -x(t)$
- $x(0) = 0$ always
- Area: $\int_{-A}^{A} x_o(t) dt = 0$
- Examples: $\sin(\omega t)$, $t^3$, $t u(t)$, signum function

**Multiplication Properties:**
- **Even $\times$ Even = Even:** Let $a(t), b(t)$ be even. Then $a(-t)b(-t) = a(t)b(t)$. The product $c(t) = a(t)b(t)$ is therefore even. *Example:* $\cos(t) \cdot \cos(t) = \cos^2(t)$, which is even.
- **Odd $\times$ Odd = Even:** Let $a(t), b(t)$ be odd. Then $a(-t)b(-t) = [-a(t)][-b(t)] = a(t)b(t)$. The product is even. *Example:* $\sin(t) \cdot \sin(t) = \sin^2(t)$, which is even.
- **Even $\times$ Odd = Odd:** $[a(-t)][b(-t)] = [a(t)][-b(t)] = -a(t)b(t)$. *Example:* $\cos(t) \cdot \sin(t) = \tfrac{1}{2}\sin(2t)$, which is odd.

### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1 — Express $x(t)$ explicitly:**

$$x(t) = e^{-2t}\cos(3t)\,u(t) = \begin{cases} e^{-2t}\cos(3t), & t \ge 0 \\ 0, & t < 0 \end{cases}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $x(-t)$:**

$$x(-t) = e^{2t}\cos(-3t)\,u(-t) = e^{2t}\cos(3t)\,u(-t)$$

(using $\cos(-3t) = \cos(3t)$)

$$x(-t) = \begin{cases} 0, & t > 0 \\ e^{2t}\cos(3t), & t \le 0 \end{cases}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $x_e(t)$:**

For $t > 0$: $\;x_e(t) = \tfrac{1}{2}e^{-2t}\cos(3t)$

For $t < 0$: $\;x_e(t) = \tfrac{1}{2}e^{2t}\cos(3t)$

For $t = 0$: $\;x_e(0) = \tfrac{1}{2}[1 + 1] = 1$

Compact form: $\;x_e(t) = \tfrac{1}{2}e^{-2\vert t \vert}\cos(3t)$

**Step 4 — Compute $x_o(t)$:**

For $t > 0$: $\;x_o(t) = \tfrac{1}{2}e^{-2t}\cos(3t)$

For $t < 0$: $\;x_o(t) = -\tfrac{1}{2}e^{2t}\cos(3t)$

For $t = 0$: $\;x_o(0) = \tfrac{1}{2}[1 - 1] = 0$

Compact form: $\;x_o(t) = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\text{sgn}(t)\,e^{-2\vert t \vert}\cos(3t)$

**Step 5 — Verification:**

For $t > 0$:

$$x_e(t) + x_o(t) = \tfrac{1}{2}e^{-2t}\cos(3t) + \tfrac{1}{2}e^{-2t}\cos(3t) = e^{-2t}\cos(3t) = x(t) \;\; \checkmark$$

For $t < 0$:

$$x_e(t) + x_o(t) = \tfrac{1}{2}e^{2t}\cos(3t) - \tfrac{1}{2}e^{2t}\cos(3t) = 0 = x(t) \;\; \checkmark$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Forgetting the fold step:** Students often write $x(-t) = x(t)$ or $x(-t) = -x(t)$ directly without computing the actual folded form. Always substitute $-t$ into the original expression **before** simplifying.
> - **Sign errors in the odd part:** The formula is $x_o(t) = \tfrac{1}{2}[x(t) - x(-t)]$, NOT $\tfrac{1}{2}[x(-t) - x(t)]$. The order matters.
> - **Misclassifying $u(t)$:** The unit step is **neither** even nor odd. Many students mistakenly claim it is even because "it is zero for $t<0$" — but it equals $1$ for $t>0$, not $0$.
> - **Missing piecewise verification:** Always confirm your piecewise expression by adding $x_e(t) + x_o(t)$ and checking you recover the original $x(t)$ in every region.
> - **Skipping the sketch:** In 7-mark problems, examiners deduct up to 2 marks if a labeled sketch is not provided. Always sketch the waveform with axes labeled.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Even:** $x(-t) = x(t)$. Symmetric about the vertical axis. No constraint at origin.
- **Odd:** $x(-t) = -x(t)$. Anti-symmetric about the vertical axis. **Must** satisfy $x(0) = 0$.
- **Universal decomposition:** $\;x(t) = x_e(t) + x_o(t)$ holds for **every** signal.
- **Extraction formulas:**
  - $x_e(t) = \tfrac{1}{2}[x(t) + x(-t)]$
  - $x_o(t) = \tfrac{1}{2}[x(t) - x(-t)]$
- **Multiplication algebra:** $\;E \cdot E = E,\; O \cdot O = E,\; E \cdot O = O$
- **Integral of odd over symmetric interval** $= 0$.
- **Integral of even over symmetric interval** $= 2 \times$ integral over $[0, A]$.
- **Energy additivity:** $E_x = E_{x_e} + E_{x_o}$ for real signals (cross-term vanishes due to orthogonality of even-odd product).
- **Always verify** by reconstructing $x(t) = x_e(t) + x_o(t)$ in your solution.
- **Always sketch** — KTU examiners reward graphical representation heavily.
- **Canonical examples to memorize:**
  - Even: $\cos(\omega_0 t),\; t^2,\; e^{-\vert t \vert},\;$ rectangular pulse symmetric about origin
  - Odd: $\sin(\omega_0 t),\; t^3,\; t e^{-\vert t \vert},\;$ signum function
  - Neither: $u(t),\; e^{-t}u(t),\; t + \cos(t)$

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Real Sequence (Practice of Visualization of a Discrete Time Signal and Operations on the DT Signal Using Python. Demonstration of Sampling and Reconstruction Using Python/MATLAB)
