> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST416 - SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Real Sequence (Practice of Visualization of a Discrete Time Signal and Operations on the DT Signal Using Python. Demonstration of Sampling and Reconstruction Using Python/MATLAB)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 1: REAL SEQUENCE — VISUALIZATION, OPERATIONS & SAMPLING/RECONSTRUCTION

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of a Real Sequence

A **real sequence** is a mathematical function whose domain is the set of integers (or a subset of integers) and whose range is a subset of the real numbers $\mathbb{R}$. In the context of **Signals and Systems**, a real sequence is the most fundamental representation of a **Discrete-Time (DT) signal**.

Formally, a real sequence is denoted as $x[n]$, where $n \in \mathbb{Z}$ and $x[n] \in \mathbb{R}$.

$$x[n] = \{x[0], x[1], x[2], \ldots\}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PECST416):** A discrete-time signal is a function defined on the integer index set $n$, mapping each integer to a real amplitude. When the amplitude is real-valued, the sequence is called a **real sequence**.

**Notation Convention:**
- Sequence value at index $n$ is written as $x[n]$.
- Bracket `[ ]` denotes discrete-time (sample index).
- Parenthesis `( )` denotes continuous-time (real time $t$).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are taking a photograph of a moving object every **0.1 second**. You do not have the full continuous motion — you only have a finite set of "snapshots" stored in your camera's memory. Each snapshot is one sample, and the ordered list of samples (with their timestamps) is a **discrete-time signal**, or a **sequence**.

| Real-World Analogy | Signal-Theory Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Sampling an audio song at 44,100 Hz | $x[n] = \cos(2\pi \cdot 1000 \cdot n/44100)$ |
| Daily closing price of a stock | $x[0], x[1], \ldots, x[364]$ (one year of data) |
| Hourly temperature reading | A finite-duration real sequence |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Real"?** The word "real" distinguishes it from a **complex sequence** $x[n] = a[n] + jb[n]$. Real sequences have only one value (magnitude) per index, whereas complex sequences carry both magnitude and phase information at every index.

### 1.3 Standard Real Sequences (Must Memorize for KTU)

The following elementary sequences form the **vocabulary** of discrete-time signal processing. KTU questions almost always begin with plotting one of these.

| Sequence | Mathematical Form | KTU Importance |
|---|---|---|
| Unit Impulse $\delta[n]$ | $\delta[n] = \begin{cases} 1, & n=0 \\ 0, & n \neq 0 \end{cases}$ | **High** (Frequent in exams) |
| Unit Step $u[n]$ | $u[n] = \begin{cases} 1, & n \geq 0 \\ 0, & n < 0 \end{cases}$ | **High** |
| Unit Ramp $r[n]$ | $r[n] = n \cdot u[n]$ | Medium |
| Exponential $x[n] = a^n u[n]$ | $a \in \mathbb{R}$ | **High** |
| Sinusoidal $x[n] = A\cos(\omega_0 n + \phi)$ | $\omega_0$ = digital frequency (rad/sample) | **Very High** |
| Rectangular pulse $x[n] = u[n] - u[n-N]$ | Length $N$ | Medium |

### 1.4 Graphical Visualization — The Stem Plot

A continuous-time signal is drawn with smooth lines because the variable is continuous. A discrete-time signal **must never be connected by lines** between samples. The proper visualization is a **stem plot**, where each sample is represented as a vertical line topped with a marker (a filled circle) at the amplitude value.

**Visualization rule:** If you see a line connecting two samples in a DT signal plot in your KTU answer script, you will **lose 1 mark** — the examiner's key explicitly penalizes this.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Rule (KTU Valuation):** In discrete-time signal plots:
> - Vertical stems rise from the $n$-axis up to the sample value.
> - A filled circle is placed at the top of each stem.
> - **No line** is drawn between consecutive samples.
> - If a sample value is zero, no stem is drawn (or a stem of zero height).

### 1.5 Operations on Discrete-Time Signals

The following are the fundamental operations KTU tests on real sequences:

| Operation | Definition | Plain-English Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Time Shifting** | $y[n] = x[n - n_0]$ | The signal slides to the right (delay) if $n_0 > 0$, or to the left (advance) if $n_0 < 0$ |
| **Time Folding (Reversal)** | $y[n] = x[-n]$ | The signal is mirrored about the vertical axis (the $n = 0$ axis) |
| **Time Scaling** | $y[n] = x[kn]$ (downsampling by $k$) | The signal is "sped up" — samples become closer together |
| **Amplitude Scaling** | $y[n] = A \cdot x[n]$ | The signal gets taller ($A>1$) or shorter ($0<A<1$) |
| **Addition** | $y[n] = x_1[n] + x_2[n]$ | Sample-by-sample sum |
| **Multiplication** | $y[n] = x_1[n] \cdot x_2[n]$ | Sample-by-sample product (modulation) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Folding + Shifting is NOT commutative.** Always fold first, then shift: $x[-(n-n_0)]$ is interpreted as fold $x[n]$ to get $x[-n]$, then shift right by $n_0$. This trap question is a KTU favourite.

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Decomposition of an Arbitrary Sequence

Any arbitrary real sequence can be written as a **weighted sum of shifted impulses**. This is called the **Sifting Property** of the unit impulse, and it is foundational for convolution, LTI systems, and Z-transform derivations.

$$x[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x[k] \cdot \delta[n-k]$$

**Why this matters:** It tells us that if we know the response of an LTI system to $\delta[n]$ (the impulse response $h[n]$), we can compute the response to **any** input $x[n]$ using convolution:

$$y[n] = x[n] * h[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x[k] \cdot h[n-k]$$

### 2.2 Decomposition of Unit Step into Impulses

$$u[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \delta[n-k] = \delta[n] + \delta[n-1] + \delta[n-2] + \ldots$$

Conversely:

$$\delta[n] = u[n] - u[n-1]$$

This duality is a KTU short-answer favourite.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The table below contains the **must-know** formulas for this topic. Memorize the boundary conditions exactly.

| # | Concept | Formula | Domain / Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Real sequence definition | $x[n] \in \mathbb{R}, \; n \in \mathbb{Z}$ | All $n$ or specified range |
| 2 | Unit impulse | $\delta[n] = 1$ if $n=0$ else $0$ | Energy $E = 1$ |
| 3 | Unit step | $u[n] = 1$ for $n \geq 0$ | Power $P = 0.5$ for $n \in [0, N]$ |
| 4 | Sign function | $\text{sgn}[n] = u[n] - u[-n-1]$ | Returns $+1, 0, -1$ |
| 5 | Sifting property | $x[n] = \sum_k x[k]\delta[n-k]$ | Infinite summation form |
| 6 | Time shifting | $y[n] = x[n-n_0]$ | $n_0 > 0$: right shift (delay) |
| 7 | Time folding | $y[n] = x[-n]$ | Mirror about $n=0$ |
| 8 | Time scaling | $y[n] = x[an]$, $a$ integer $>1$ | Decimation / downsampling |
| 9 | Sampling (CT → DT) | $x[n] = x_c(nT)$ | $T$ = sampling period (sec/sample) |
| 10 | Nyquist rate | $f_s \geq 2 f_{\max}$ | $f_s$ in Hz |
| 11 | Nyquist frequency | $f_N = f_s / 2$ | Highest recoverable freq |
| 12 | Reconstruction (ideal) | $x_c(t) = \sum_n x[n] \cdot \text{sinc}\!\left(\frac{t - nT}{T}\right)$ | Whittaker-Shannon formula |
| 13 | Convolution (DT) | $y[n] = \sum_k x[k]h[n-k]$ | LTI system response |
| 14 | Energy of sequence | $E = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n] \vert^2$ | For finite-energy signals |
| 15 | Power of sequence | $P = \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{1}{2N+1} \sum_{n=-N}^{N} \vert x[n] \vert^2$ | For periodic/power signals |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** The vertical bars in $|\cdot|$ have been written using `\vert` to preserve the markdown table. **Do not** type them as the pipe character `|` in your answer script — the table will break.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Where Sequences Appear |
|---|---|
| **Digital Audio (MP3, AAC)** | Audio sampled at 44.1 kHz → sequence of amplitude values |
| **Digital Images (JPEG)** | 2D array of pixel intensities — a 2D real sequence |
| **Biomedical (ECG, EEG)** | Sampled electrical signals from the body |
| **Radar / Sonar** | Discrete-time reflected pulses |
| **5G Communication** | OFDM symbols sampled at tens of MHz |
| **Control Systems** | Discrete PID controller output as a sequence |

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Sifting Property of $\delta[n]$

We want to show that any real sequence can be reconstructed as a weighted sum of shifted impulses.

**Step 1 — Consider an arbitrary sample $x[k]$ of the sequence.** The value $x[k]$ exists only at index $n = k$.

**Step 2 — Multiply $x[k]$ by a shifted impulse $\delta[n-k]$.** By the definition of the unit impulse:

$$x[k] \cdot \delta[n-k] = \begin{cases} x[k], & n = k \\ 0, & n \neq k \end{cases}$$

**Step 3 — Sum over all $k$:**

$$\sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x[k] \cdot \delta[n-k] = x[n]$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion.** The sifting property is proved. This result is the **theoretical foundation of digital convolution**.

$$\boxed{x[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x[k] \cdot \delta[n-k]}$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Sampling of a Continuous-Time Sinusoid

Consider the continuous-time signal:

$$x_c(t) = \cos(2\pi f_0 t), \quad f_0 = 50 \text{ Hz}$$

We sample it uniformly with sampling period $T$ (or sampling frequency $f_s = 1/T$).

**Step 1 — Substitute $t = nT$:**

$$x[n] = x_c(nT) = \cos(2\pi f_0 n T) = \cos\!\left(2\pi \frac{f_0}{f_s} n\right)$$

**Step 2 — Define the digital (normalized) frequency $\omega_0$:**

$$\omega_0 = 2\pi \frac{f_0}{f_s} \quad \text{(in radians per sample)}$$

**Step 3 — Final sampled form:**

$$x[n] = \cos(\omega_0 n)$$

**Step 4 — Numerical example (KTU-favourite).** Let $f_0 = 50$ Hz, $f_s = 200$ Hz:

$$\omega_0 = 2\pi \cdot \frac{50}{200} = \frac{\pi}{2} \text{ rad/sample}$$

So $x[n] = \cos(\pi n / 2) = \{1, 0, -1, 0, 1, 0, -1, 0, \ldots\}$.

This is a **period-4** sequence. Note that the original analog signal has period $1/50 = 20$ ms, but the sampled sequence has period $4$ samples $= 20$ ms. Sampling preserved the periodicity — a healthy case.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Reconstruction via Whittaker-Shannon Interpolation

The problem: given samples $x[n] = x_c(nT)$, recover $x_c(t)$ for any continuous $t$.

**Step 1 — The ideal reconstruction filter** is a low-pass filter with cutoff at $f_c = f_s / 2$ (the Nyquist frequency) and gain $T$.

**Step 2 — Its impulse response** is a sinc function:

$$h(t) = T \cdot \text{sinc}\!\left(\frac{t}{T}\right) = T \cdot \frac{\sin(\pi t / T)}{\pi t / T}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The unnormalized sinc is defined as $\text{sinc}(x) = \frac{\sin(\pi x)}{\pi x}$, which equals $1$ at $x = 0$. This convention is the standard in DSP and is used in MATLAB's `sinc` function.

**Step 3 — The output of the filter** is the convolution of the sampled signal (an impulse train modulated by $x[n]$) with $h(t)$:

$$x_c(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] \cdot h(t - nT)$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $h(t)$:**

$$x_c(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] \cdot \text{sinc}\!\left(\frac{t - nT}{T}\right)$$

This is the **Whittaker-Shannon interpolation formula**. It exactly reconstructs $x_c(t)$ if and only if $f_s \geq 2 f_{\max}$ (Nyquist condition).

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Operations

Let $x[n] = \{0, 1, 2, 3, 2, 1, 0\}$ for $n = -1, 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5$. The table below shows the result of each operation. This is the **canonical KTU lab-style problem**.

| Operation | Resulting Sequence $y[n]$ | Indexing |
|---|---|---|
| Original $x[n]$ | $\{0, 1, 2, 3, 2, 1, 0\}$ | $n = -1, 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5$ |
| Right shift by 2: $x[n-2]$ | $\{0, 0, 1, 2, 3, 2, 1, 0\}$ | $n = 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7$ |
| Left shift by 1: $x[n+1]$ | $\{1, 2, 3, 2, 1, 0\}$ | $n = -2, -1, 0, 1, 2, 3$ |
| Folding: $x[-n]$ | $\{0, 1, 2, 3, 2, 1, 0\}$ | $n = -5, -4, -3, -2, -1, 0, 1$ |
| Fold + shift: $x[-(n-2)] = x[-n+2]$ | $\{0, 1, 2, 3, 2, 1, 0\}$ | $n = 7, 6, 5, 4, 3, 2, 1$ |
| Amplitude scale $A=2$: $2x[n]$ | $\{0, 2, 4, 6, 4, 2, 0\}$ | $n = -1, 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5$ |

### 3.5 Python Code Block — Complete Visualization Toolkit

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following Python code is part of the **practical / lab component** of PECST416. KTU lab examinations directly test plotting and signal generation. Run this in a Jupyter notebook or `.py` file.

```python
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# ---------- 1. Standard Sequences ----------
n = np.arange(-5, 11)  # n = -5, -4, ..., 10

# Unit impulse
impulse = np.where(n == 0, 1, 0)

# Unit step
step = np.where(n >= 0, 1, 0)

# Ramp
ramp = n * (n >= 0)

# Exponential decay
expo = (0.8) ** n * (n >= 0)

# Sinusoidal (digital frequency omega = pi/8)
omega = np.pi / 8
sine_seq = np.sin(omega * n)

fig, axs = plt.subplots(2, 2, figsize=(12, 7))
axs[0, 0].stem(n, impulse, basefmt=" "); axs[0, 0].set_title("Unit Impulse δ[n]")
axs[0, 1].stem(n, step, basefmt=" ");    axs[0, 1].set_title("Unit Step u[n]")
axs[1, 0].stem(n, ramp, basefmt=" ");    axs[1, 0].set_title("Unit Ramp r[n]")
axs[1, 1].stem(n, expo, basefmt=" ");    axs[1, 1].set_title("Exponential 0.8^n u[n]")
for ax in axs.flat: ax.grid(True); ax.set_xlabel("n")
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()
```

```python
# ---------- 2. Operations on a Real Sequence ----------
n = np.arange(-3, 8)
x = np.array([0, 1, 2, 3, 2, 1, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0])  # triangular sequence

# Shifting:  y1[n] = x[n - 2]   (right shift by 2)
y_shift = np.concatenate((np.zeros(2), x[:-2]))

# Folding:   y2[n] = x[-n]
y_fold  = x[::-1]

# Add a sinusoid:  y3[n] = x[n] + 0.5*sin(pi n / 4)
y_add   = x + 0.5 * np.sin(np.pi * n / 4)

fig, axs = plt.subplots(3, 1, figsize=(10, 8))
axs[0].stem(n, x,       basefmt=" "); axs[0].set_title("Original x[n]")
axs[1].stem(n, y_shift, basefmt=" "); axs[1].set_title("Shifted x[n-2]")
axs[2].stem(n, y_fold,  basefmt=" "); axs[2].set_title("Folded x[-n]")
for ax in axs: ax.grid(True); ax.set_xlabel("n")
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()
```

```python
# ---------- 3. Sampling Demonstration ----------
# Continuous signal:  x_c(t) = sin(2*pi*5*t)   (5 Hz sinusoid)
# Sampling frequencies:  30 Hz, 12 Hz, 8 Hz, 6 Hz

f0 = 5                              # signal frequency (Hz)
t  = np.linspace(0, 1, 2000)         # dense time grid for CT signal
xc = np.sin(2 * np.pi * f0 * t)

fs_list = [30, 12, 8, 6]            # four sampling rates
fig, axs = plt.subplots(2, 2, figsize=(12, 7))
for ax, fs in zip(axs.flat, fs_list):
    n  = np.arange(0, int(fs * 1) + 1)
    xs = np.sin(2 * np.pi * f0 * n / fs)
    ax.plot(t, xc, 'b-',  label="CT signal", linewidth=1.2)
    ax.stem(n/fs, xs, linefmt='r-', markerfmt='ro', basefmt=" ", label="samples")
    ax.set_title(f"Sampling at fs = {fs} Hz  (Nyquist = {2*f0} Hz)")
    ax.set_xlabel("t (s)"); ax.set_ylim(-1.5, 1.5); ax.grid(True); ax.legend()
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()
```

> [!WARNING]
> When $f_s = 6$ Hz (less than $2 f_0 = 10$ Hz), **aliasing** occurs. The reconstructed signal will look like a much lower-frequency sinusoid. This is the central visual takeaway of the sampling lab.

```python
# ---------- 4. Reconstruction (Whittaker-Shannon) ----------
from scipy.signal import resample

f0, fs = 5, 30                                 # 5 Hz signal, 30 Hz sampling
T      = 1 / fs
n_idx  = np.arange(0, 41)
x_n    = np.sin(2 * np.pi * f0 * n_idx / fs)   # sampled sequence

# Reconstruct at higher rate using sinc interpolation
t_rec  = np.linspace(0, n_idx[-1]*T, 1000)
x_rec  = np.zeros_like(t_rec)
for n_k, x_k in zip(n_idx, x_n):
    x_rec += x_k * np.sinc((t_rec - n_k*T) / T)

plt.figure(figsize=(10, 4))
plt.plot(t_rec, x_rec, 'r-', label="Reconstructed x_c(t)")
plt.stem(n_idx*T, x_n, linefmt='b-', markerfmt='bo', basefmt=" ", label="Samples")
plt.title("Reconstruction via Whittaker-Shannon (Sinc Interpolation)")
plt.xlabel("t (s)"); plt.grid(True); plt.legend(); plt.show()
```

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Workflow: From Continuous Signal to Reconstructed Signal

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Continuous-Time\nSignal x_c(t)"] --> B["Anti-Aliasing\nFilter (LPF)"]
    B --> C["Sampler\n(Switch / ADC)"]
    C --> D["Discrete-Time\nSequence x[n]"]
    D --> E["Digital\nProcessing\n(Filter / DFT / etc.)"]
    E --> F["DAC /\nReconstruction\nFilter"]
    F --> G["Reconstructed\nContinuous Signal\nx_hat(t)"]
    D -. "If ideal and fs ≥ 2fmax" .-> G
    style A fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F77B4
    style D fill:#FFF4E6,stroke:#FF7F0E
    style G fill:#E8F8E8,stroke:#2CA02C
    style B fill:#FDEAEA,stroke:#D62728
    style F fill:#FDEAEA,stroke:#D62728
```

**Block interpretation:**
- **Anti-Aliasing Filter:** Limits the bandwidth of $x_c(t)$ to $f_{\max} \leq f_s / 2$ before sampling.
- **Sampler:** Produces $x[n] = x_c(nT)$.
- **Digital Processing:** Any operation — filtering, DFT, modulation.
- **Reconstruction Filter:** Sinc-interpolation (ideal) or zero-order hold (practical).

### 4.2 Concept Map — All Operations on a Real Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R["Real Sequence x[n]"] --> S1["Time Shifting\nx[n - n0]"]
    R --> S2["Time Folding\nx[-n]"]
    R --> S3["Time Scaling\nx[kn] (decimation)"]
    R --> S4["Amplitude Scaling\nA * x[n]"]
    R --> S5["Addition\nx1[n] + x2[n]"]
    R --> S6["Multiplication\nx1[n] * x2[n]"]
    S1 --> C["Combined:\nx[-n + n0]"]
    S2 --> C
    C --> O["Output Sequence\ny[n]"]
    S5 --> O
    S6 --> O
    style R fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F77B4
    style O fill:#E8F8E8,stroke:#2CA02C
    style C fill:#FFF4E6,stroke:#FF7F0E
```

### 4.3 Sampling Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Signal x_c(t) with\nmax freq f_max"] --> B{"Choose\nsampling\nfrequency f_s"}
    B -->|fs >= 2f_max| C["No Aliasing\nReconstruction is perfect\n(Whittaker-Shannon)"]
    B -->|fs < 2f_max| D["Aliasing Occurs\nHigh frequencies fold\ninto low-frequency ghosts"]
    D --> E["Cannot recover\noriginal signal exactly"]
    C --> F["Lab Output:\nReconstructed curve\nmatches x_c(t)"]
    style A fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F77B4
    style C fill:#E8F8E8,stroke:#2CA02C
    style D fill:#FDEAEA,stroke:#D62728
    style E fill:#FDEAEA,stroke:#D62728
```

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define a real sequence. With a neat sketch, plot the sequence $x[n] = \{1, 2, 3, 2, 1\}$ for $n = -1, 0, 1, 2, 3$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A real sequence is a function $x[n]$ that maps an integer index $n \in \mathbb{Z}$ to a real-valued amplitude $x[n] \in \mathbb{R}$. It is the discrete-time counterpart of a continuous-time signal, and is represented as an ordered list of numbers.

| $n$ | $-1$ | $0$ | $1$ | $2$ | $3$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $x[n]$ | $1$ | $2$ | $3$ | $2$ | $1$ |

**Stem plot description (for the answer script):**
- Draw a horizontal $n$-axis from $n=-1$ to $n=3$.
- Mark the origin at $n=0$.
- Draw five vertical stems at $n=-1, 0, 1, 2, 3$ with heights $1, 2, 3, 2, 1$ respectively.
- Place a filled circle at the top of each stem.
- **Do not** connect the samples with lines.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Connecting samples with straight lines instead of using stems = **−1 mark** (discrete-time signals are not continuous).

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**State the Nyquist sampling theorem. A signal $x(t) = 5\cos(100\pi t)$ is sampled at $f_s = 200$ Hz. Determine whether the sampling satisfies the theorem.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Nyquist Sampling Theorem:** A band-limited continuous-time signal $x_c(t)$ with maximum frequency component $f_{\max}$ can be uniquely reconstructed from its samples $x[n] = x_c(nT)$ provided the sampling frequency satisfies:

$$f_s \geq 2 f_{\max}$$

The minimum sampling rate, $f_s = 2 f_{\max}$, is called the **Nyquist rate**.

**Solution:**
- Signal: $x_c(t) = 5\cos(100\pi t)$
- Standard form: $x_c(t) = A\cos(2\pi f_0 t)$, so $2\pi f_0 = 100\pi \Rightarrow f_0 = 50$ Hz.
- Maximum frequency: $f_{\max} = 50$ Hz.
- Nyquist rate: $2 f_{\max} = 100$ Hz.
- Given: $f_s = 200$ Hz $\geq 100$ Hz.
- **Conclusion:** The sampling **satisfies** the Nyquist criterion, and the signal can be perfectly reconstructed.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU Part B questions on real sequences and sampling are typically allotted **14 marks** with sub-parts (a) **7 marks** and (b) **7 marks**. The internal choice is between two completely different questions (not just an "OR" within sub-parts).

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Define the unit impulse and unit step sequences. Show that $\delta[n] = u[n] - u[n-1]$ and $u[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \delta[n-k]$. Plot both sequences for $n \in \{-3, -2, -1, 0, 1, 2, 3\}$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a Python program to generate the following sequences and display them using stem plots: (i) $x_1[n] = 3\delta[n+2] - 2\delta[n] + \delta[n-2]$, (ii) $x_2[n] = (0.5)^n u[n]$, (iii) $x_3[n] = \cos(\pi n / 4) \cdot u[n]$. Comment on the periodicity of $x_3[n]$. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution

**(a) [7 marks]**

**Definition 1 (1 mark):** The unit impulse sequence $\delta[n]$ is defined as:

$$\delta[n] = \begin{cases} 1, & n = 0 \\ 0, & n \neq 0 \end{cases}$$

**Definition 2 (1 mark):** The unit step sequence $u[n]$ is defined as:

$$u[n] = \begin{cases} 1, & n \geq 0 \\ 0, & n < 0 \end{cases}$$

**Proof of $\delta[n] = u[n] - u[n-1]$ (2 marks):**

Consider $u[n] - u[n-1]$ at three cases:
- If $n < 0$: both $u[n] = 0$ and $u[n-1] = 0$ (since $n-1 < 0$). Difference $= 0$.
- If $n = 0$: $u[0] = 1$ and $u[-1] = 0$. Difference $= 1$.
- If $n > 0$: both $u[n] = 1$ and $u[n-1] = 1$. Difference $= 0$.

Therefore:

$$u[n] - u[n-1] = \begin{cases} 1, & n=0 \\ 0, & n \neq 0 \end{cases} = \delta[n]$$

**Proof of $u[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \delta[n-k]$ (2 marks):**

At index $n = 0$: sum is $\delta[0] = 1$.
At index $n = 1$: sum is $\delta[1] + \delta[0] = 0 + 1 = 1$.
At any $n \geq 0$: the term $k = n$ contributes $1$, all others contribute $0$. Sum $= 1$.
At any $n < 0$: no term $k \geq 0$ gives $n - k = 0$, so sum $= 0$.

Hence the summation equals $1$ for $n \geq 0$ and $0$ for $n < 0$, which is exactly $u[n]$.

**Plot (1 mark):** A tabular stem plot with filled circles at $n = 0$ for impulse (height 1), and at $n = 0, 1, 2, 3$ (heights 1) for step.

**(b) [7 marks]**

**Python code (5 marks):**

```python
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

n = np.arange(-5, 11)

# (i) Impulse combination
x1 = 3*np.where(n == -2, 1, 0) - 2*np.where(n == 0, 1, 0) + np.where(n == 2, 1, 0)

# (ii) Exponential decay
x2 = (0.5)**n * (n >= 0)

# (iii) Cosine windowed by step
x3 = np.cos(np.pi * n / 4) * (n >= 0)

fig, axs = plt.subplots(3, 1, figsize=(10, 9))
axs[0].stem(n, x1, basefmt=" "); axs[0].set_title("x1[n] = 3δ[n+2] - 2δ[n] + δ[n-2]")
axs[1].stem(n, x2, basefmt=" "); axs[1].set_title("x2[n] = (0.5)^n u[n]")
axs[2].stem(n, x3, basefmt=" "); axs[2].set_title("x3[n] = cos(πn/4) u[n]")
for ax in axs: ax.grid(True); ax.set_xlabel("n")
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()
```

**Periodicity comment (2 marks):** $x_3[n] = \cos(\pi n/4)$ has digital frequency $\omega_0 = \pi/4$. The period in samples is:

$$N = \frac{2\pi}{\omega_0} \cdot m, \quad \text{choose smallest integer } m \text{ s.t. } N \in \mathbb{Z}$$

$$\frac{2\pi}{\pi/4} = 8 \Rightarrow N = 8 \text{ samples}$$

So $x_3[n]$ is **periodic with fundamental period $N = 8$ samples**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the process of sampling and reconstruction of a continuous-time signal. State and prove the Nyquist sampling theorem. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A continuous-time signal $x_c(t) = \cos(200\pi t) + 0.5\cos(400\pi t)$ is sampled at $f_s = 300$ Hz. Determine (i) the Nyquist rate, (ii) whether the signal is recoverable, (iii) the digital frequency in rad/sample corresponding to the **aliased** component (if any), and (iv) write a Python program to demonstrate the aliasing effect. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution

**(a) [7 marks]**

**Block diagram (2 marks):** Draw the diagram from Section 4.1 — continuous signal → anti-aliasing filter → sampler → discrete sequence → DAC/reconstruction filter → reconstructed continuous signal.

**Statement of Nyquist theorem (2 marks):**
A bandlimited signal $x_c(t)$ with highest frequency $f_{\max}$ can be uniquely recovered from its samples $x[n] = x_c(nT)$ if and only if the sampling frequency $f_s = 1/T$ satisfies:

$$f_s \geq 2 f_{\max}$$

**Proof sketch (3 marks):**
- The Fourier transform of the sampled signal $x_s(t) = \sum_n x[n]\delta(t - nT)$ is a periodic replication of $X_c(j\omega)$ with period $\omega_s = 2\pi/T$.
- If $\omega_{\max} < \omega_s / 2$ (i.e., $f_{\max} < f_s/2$), the spectral replicas do not overlap.
- An ideal low-pass filter with cutoff $\omega_c = \omega_s / 2$ and gain $T$ extracts the baseband replica, recovering $X_c(j\omega)$ exactly.
- Inverse Fourier transform gives back $x_c(t)$. The impulse response of the reconstruction filter is $h(t) = T\,\text{sinc}(t/T)$, leading to the Whittaker-Shannon formula.

**(b) [7 marks]**

**(i) Nyquist rate (1 mark):**
- Frequencies in $x_c(t)$: $f_1 = 100$ Hz, $f_2 = 200$ Hz.
- $f_{\max} = 200$ Hz.
- Nyquist rate $= 2 f_{\max} = 400$ Hz.

**(ii) Recoverability (1 mark):** Given $f_s = 300$ Hz $< 400$ Hz $\Rightarrow$ **aliasing occurs**. The signal is **not** perfectly recoverable.

**(iii) Aliased digital frequencies (2 marks):** The aliased frequency is computed as the difference between the actual frequency and the nearest multiple of $f_s$:

$$f_{\text{alias}} = \vert f - k f_s \vert, \quad k = \text{round}(f / f_s)$$

- For $f_1 = 100$ Hz: $k = 0$, $f_{\text{alias,1}} = 100$ Hz. Digital freq: $\omega_1 = 2\pi \cdot 100/300 = 2\pi/3$ rad/sample.
- For $f_2 = 200$ Hz: $k = 1$, $f_{\text{alias,2}} = \vert 200 - 300 \vert = 100$ Hz. Digital freq: $\omega_2 = 2\pi/3$ rad/sample.

Both components appear at the **same aliased frequency 100 Hz**! The reconstructed signal will look like a single sinusoid at 100 Hz with amplitude $1 + 0.5 = 1.5$ (after constructive interference). This is a classic KTU trap answer.

**(iv) Python program (3 marks):**

```python
import numpy as np, matplotlib.pyplot as plt

f1, f2, fs = 100, 200, 300
T = 1 / fs
n = np.arange(0, 31)
t_cont = np.linspace(0, n[-1]*T, 2000)

x_cont  = np.cos(2*np.pi*f1*t_cont) + 0.5*np.cos(2*np.pi*f2*t_cont)
x_samp  = np.cos(2*np.pi*f1*n*T)    + 0.5*np.cos(2*np.pi*f2*n*T)

# Naive reconstruction by connecting samples (wrong, but illustrative)
t_rec = np.linspace(0, n[-1]*T, 2000)
x_rec = np.cos(2*np.pi*f1*t_rec) + 0.5*np.cos(2*np.pi*f2*t_rec)

plt.figure(figsize=(10, 4))
plt.plot(t_cont, x_cont, 'b-', label="Original x_c(t)")
plt.stem(n*T, x_samp, linefmt='r-', markerfmt='ro', basefmt=" ", label="Samples")
plt.plot(t_rec, x_rec, 'g--', label="What a bandlimited reconstruction would give")
plt.title("Aliasing: fs=300 Hz < Nyquist=400 Hz → 100 Hz & 200 Hz collapse to 100 Hz")
plt.xlabel("t (s)"); plt.grid(True); plt.legend(); plt.show()
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting to check $f_{\max}$:** Many students compute the Nyquist rate for the *first* component only. Always use the **highest** frequency in the signal.
> 2. **Confusing analog and digital frequencies:** $\omega_0 = 2\pi f_0 / f_s$ is in **radians per sample**, not radians per second. Mixing units loses **2 marks**.
> 3. **Adding aliased amplitudes incorrectly:** When aliased components land on the same frequency, their amplitudes add (or subtract, if one is inverted due to a $k$ giving a negative coefficient). Forgetting this loses **1 mark**.
> 4. **Plotting continuous lines for a discrete signal:** In the script, use `stem()`, not `plot()`. Continuous lines for DT signals lose **1 mark**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep)**

- [ ] A **real sequence** $x[n]$ has real-valued amplitudes at integer indices $n \in \mathbb{Z}$.
- [ ] Always plot DT signals with **stem plots** — never with continuous lines.
- [ ] The unit impulse $\delta[n]$ is non-zero **only** at $n = 0$, where it equals $1$.
- [ ] The unit step $u[n]$ equals $1$ for $n \geq 0$ and $0$ for $n < 0$.
- [ ] **Sifting property:** $x[n] = \sum_k x[k]\delta[n-k]$ — foundation of convolution.
- [ ] **Time shifting:** $x[n - n_0]$ with $n_0 > 0$ is a **right shift** (delay).
- [ ] **Time folding:** $x[-n]$ is a mirror image about the $n = 0$ axis.
- [ ] **Fold + shift order matters:** Apply folding **first**, then shift.
- [ ] **Sampling:** $x[n] = x_c(nT)$, where $T$ is the sampling period and $f_s = 1/T$ the sampling frequency.
- [ ] **Nyquist rate:** $f_s \geq 2 f_{\max}$.
- [ ] **Digital frequency:** $\omega_0 = 2\pi f_0 / f_s$ (rad/sample).
- [ ] **Aliasing** occurs when $f_s < 2 f_{\max}$ — high frequencies fold into the baseband.
- [ ] **Whittaker-Shannon reconstruction formula:** $x_c(t) = \sum_n x[n] \cdot \text{sinc}((t - nT)/T)$.
- [ ] **Anti-aliasing filter** must precede the sampler to band-limit the input.
- [ ] Python essentials: `np.where`, `np.arange`, `plt.stem`, `np.sin/cos`, `np.sinc`.
- [ ] Use `sinc` from NumPy/SciPy which uses the **normalized** definition $\text{sinc}(x) = \sin(\pi x)/(\pi x)$.
- [ ] For a period-$N$ discrete sinusoid, $N = 2\pi m / \omega_0$ with smallest integer $m$.
- [ ] Energy $E = \sum_n \vert x[n] \vert^2$; Power $P = \lim (1/(2N+1)) \sum \vert x[n] \vert^2$.
- [ ] KTU expects you to **comment on aliasing visually** in lab reports — annotate plots with $f_s$ vs $2f_{\max}$.
- [ ] For multi-component signals, the **highest** frequency determines the Nyquist rate, not the dominant or first-listed one.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Discrete-Time Fourier Transform for Aperiodic Sequences. Properties of the Discrete-Time Fourier Transform (Periodicity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST416 - SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Discrete-Time Fourier Transform for Aperiodic Sequences. Properties of the Discrete-Time Fourier Transform (Periodicity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of the Discrete-Time Fourier Transform (DTFT)

For an **aperiodic discrete-time signal** $x[n]$, the **Discrete-Time Fourier Transform (DTFT)** $X(e^{j\omega})$ is formally defined as a continuous function of the angular frequency variable $\omega$ (in radians per sample):

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n]\, e^{-j\omega n}$$

The corresponding **Inverse Discrete-Time Fourier Transform (IDTFT)** that recovers $x[n]$ from $X(e^{j\omega})$ is given by:

$$x[n] = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{2\pi} X(e^{j\omega})\, e^{j\omega n}\, d\omega$$

The integration interval is any contiguous span of length $2\pi$ (for example, $-\pi$ to $\pi$), because the spectrum is itself periodic (as we shall see in the periodicity property).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Unlike the Continuous-Time Fourier Transform (CTFT), the DTFT is **always periodic in $\omega$** with period $2\pi$. This is the single most distinguishing feature students must remember.

## 1.2 Existence Conditions (Sufficient Conditions)

The DTFT converges if the sequence $x[n]$ satisfies any one of the following:

- **Absolutely summable:** $\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n] \vert < \infty$  
- **Square summable (finite energy):** $\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n] \vert^{2} < \infty$ (convergence in the mean-square sense)
- **Dirichlet conditions:** The sequence has a finite number of maxima, minima, and discontinuities within any finite interval.

> [!NOTE]
> Periodic sequences do **not** possess a convergent DTFT in the ordinary sense; they are handled by the **Discrete Fourier Series (DFS)**. This module focuses exclusively on **aperiodic** sequences.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy: The Spectroscope of Digital Signals

Imagine shining white light through a glass prism — the prism **spreads** the light into its constituent colours, giving you a complete colour "fingerprint" of the source. The DTFT plays the **same role for digital signals**:

- The **time-domain signal** $x[n]$ is the "white light" (a complex mixture of many sinusoidal components).
- The **DTFT** $X(e^{j\omega})$ is the "rainbow" — it tells you **exactly how much** of each digital frequency $\omega$ is present in $x[n]$.
- Because digital frequencies only "wrap around" every $2\pi$ radians, you only need to look at one full revolution (one full circle on the unit circle) to see the entire spectrum — the rest is just a repeat.

## 1.4 Why Periodicity? — A First-Principle Intuition

A digital signal can only meaningfully contain frequencies in the range $\omega \in [-\pi, \pi]$ (or equivalently $0$ to $2\pi$). Why? Because the complex exponential $e^{j\omega n}$ is **$2\pi$-periodic in $\omega$ for any fixed integer $n$**:

$$e^{j(\omega + 2\pi)n} = e^{j\omega n}\cdot e^{j2\pi n} = e^{j\omega n}\cdot 1 = e^{j\omega n}$$

Hence, a sinusoid of digital frequency $\omega + 2\pi$ is **indistinguishable** from one of frequency $\omega$. The DTFT must therefore also repeat every $2\pi$, because the underlying basis functions do. We will formalise this in Section 3.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Logical Breakdown of the DTFT Operation

The DTFT pair can be decomposed into a sequence of four conceptual steps:

- **Step 1 — Decomposition:** $x[n]$ is decomposed into an infinite continuum of complex exponentials $e^{j\omega n}$, each weighted by a complex amplitude $\dfrac{X(e^{j\omega})}{2\pi}\, d\omega$.
- **Step 2 — Forward Transform:** Each weight is computed by projecting $x[n]$ onto $e^{j\omega n}$ using an inner product (correlation) across all time $n \in \mathbb{Z}$.
- **Step 3 — Synthesis:** The inverse integral recombines these weighted exponentials, integrating over **one** $2\pi$ interval (any interval) since the spectrum is periodic.
- **Step 4 — Uniqueness & Invertibility:** Under Dirichlet conditions, the pair is unique — no information is lost, and $x[n]$ can be exactly reconstructed from $X(e^{j\omega})$.

## 2.2 The Periodicity Property — The Heart of This Module

**Statement:** If $x[n] \xleftrightarrow{\text{DTFT}} X(e^{j\omega})$, then

$$X(e^{j(\omega + 2\pi)}) = X(e^{j\omega}) \quad \text{for all } \omega$$

Equivalently, $X(e^{j\omega})$ is **periodic with period $2\pi$** in the angular frequency variable $\omega$.

### 2.2.1 Why the Periodicity is $2\pi$ (Not $2\omega_{s}$ Like CTFT)

In the **Continuous-Time Fourier Transform (CTFT)**, the spectrum is **not periodic** — the frequency variable $\omega$ can take any real value from $-\infty$ to $+\infty$. In the **DTFT**, however, the frequency variable is bounded and the spectrum is necessarily periodic with period $2\pi$ because:

- The independent variable is the integer index $n$ (discrete time).
- $e^{j\omega n}$ is $2\pi$-periodic in $\omega$ when $n$ is an integer.
- Summation over integer $n$ preserves this periodicity.

### 2.2.2 Geometric Interpretation on the Unit Circle

Plot $e^{j\omega}$ on the complex plane as $\omega$ varies from $-\pi$ to $\pi$: this traces the **unit circle exactly once**. Because $X(e^{j\omega})$ repeats every $2\pi$, the entire DTFT is completely described by its values on **any single $2\pi$ interval**, most commonly $\omega \in [-\pi, \pi]$ or $\omega \in [0, 2\pi]$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Consequence:** When we plot $|X(e^{j\omega})|$, we only need to draw it over $[0, \pi]$ (or $[-\pi, \pi]$) for real signals. This is why DTFT magnitude plots in textbooks never extend beyond $\pi$.

## 2.3 Secondary Properties Built on Periodicity

Although the module focus is periodicity, the following properties are **direct consequences** of (or intimately related to) it and frequently appear in KTU questions:

- **Linearity:** $a\,x_{1}[n] + b\,x_{2}[n] \;\xleftrightarrow{\text{DTFT}}\; a\,X_{1}(e^{j\omega}) + b\,X_{2}(e^{j\omega})$
- **Time Shifting:** $x[n - n_{0}] \;\xleftrightarrow{\text{DTFT}}\; e^{-j\omega n_{0}}\, X(e^{j\omega})$
- **Frequency Shifting:** $e^{j\omega_{0} n}\, x[n] \;\xleftrightarrow{\text{DTFT}}\; X(e^{j(\omega - \omega_{0})})$ — note that the shifted spectrum is still $2\pi$-periodic, only **re-centred** at $\omega_{0}$.
- **Conjugation & Conjugate Symmetry:** For real $x[n]$, $X(e^{-j\omega}) = X^{*}(e^{j\omega})$, meaning $|X(e^{j\omega})|$ is **even** and $\angle X(e^{j\omega})$ is **odd** over $[-\pi, \pi]$.
- **Parseval's Theorem:** $\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n] \vert^{2} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} \vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert^{2}\, d\omega$ — integrates over **one period only** thanks to periodicity.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **DTFT Pair / Property** | **Mathematical Statement** | **Periodicity Note** |
|---|---|---|
| Forward DTFT | $X(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n]\, e^{-j\omega n}$ | Result is $2\pi$-periodic in $\omega$ |
| Inverse DTFT | $x[n] = \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \int_{2\pi} X(e^{j\omega})\, e^{j\omega n}\, d\omega$ | Integral over any $2\pi$ interval |
| Periodicity Property | $X(e^{j(\omega + 2\pi)}) = X(e^{j\omega})$ | Fundamental period in $\omega$ is **$2\pi$** |
| Periodicity of $e^{j\omega n}$ | $e^{j(\omega+2\pi)n} = e^{j\omega n}$ for integer $n$ | Underlying cause of DTFT periodicity |
| Linearity | $a x_{1}[n] + b x_{2}[n] \;\leftrightarrow\; a X_{1} + b X_{2}$ | Preserved under periodicity |
| Frequency Shifting | $e^{j\omega_{0}n} x[n] \;\leftrightarrow\; X(e^{j(\omega-\omega_{0})})$ | Periodicity shifts by $\omega_{0}$ |
| Time Shifting | $x[n-n_{0}] \;\leftrightarrow\; e^{-j\omega n_{0}} X(e^{j\omega})$ | Phase factor is $2\pi$-periodic in $\omega$ |
| Conjugate Symmetry (real $x$) | $X(e^{-j\omega}) = X^{*}(e^{j\omega})$ | Combined with periodicity gives full symmetry over $[-\pi,\pi]$ |
| Parseval's Theorem | $\sum \vert x[n] \vert^{2} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} \vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert^{2} d\omega$ | Integration over **one** period |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key constant to remember:** The fundamental period in the $\omega$-domain is $\mathbf{2\pi}$ **radians/sample** (not $2\pi$ in Hz — digital frequency is always in radians per sample).

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Digital Audio Processing (MP3, AAC, FLAC codecs):** Speech and music signals are aperiodic in time but their DTFT is periodic in $\omega$, which is exploited in filter banks and the Modified Discrete Cosine Transform (MDCT).
- **Digital Filter Design:** FIR and IIR filter frequency responses $H(e^{j\omega})$ are inherently $2\pi$-periodic. Designers only need to specify the response over $[0, \pi]$.
- **OFDM in 4G/5G/Wi-Fi:** Sub-carriers are placed at frequencies that differ by multiples of $2\pi/N$ on the unit circle — directly leveraging the periodicity and the discrete (DFS) viewpoint.
- **Spectrum Analysers & Software-Defined Radio (SDR):** Real-time FFT analysers plot only the principal interval $[-\pi, \pi]$ (or $[0, \pi]$ for real signals) knowing the rest is redundant.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Periodicity Property

**Given:** $x[n] \;\xleftrightarrow{\text{DTFT}}\; X(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n]\, e^{-j\omega n}$

**To Prove:** $X(e^{j(\omega + 2\pi)}) = X(e^{j\omega})$ for all real $\omega$.

---

### Step 1 — Write the DTFT with argument shifted by $2\pi$

$$X(e^{j(\omega + 2\pi)}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n]\, e^{-j(\omega + 2\pi)n}$$

*Logic:* We simply substitute $(\omega + 2\pi)$ in place of $\omega$ inside the definition of the forward DTFT.

---

### Step 2 — Split the exponential using the product rule for exponents

$$\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n]\, e^{-j(\omega + 2\pi)n} = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n]\, e^{-j\omega n}\cdot e^{-j2\pi n}$$

*Logic:* The exponent law $e^{a+b} = e^{a}\cdot e^{b}$ allows us to factorise the term.

---

### Step 3 — Evaluate the constant complex exponential $e^{-j2\pi n}$

$$e^{-j2\pi n} = \cos(2\pi n) - j\sin(2\pi n)$$

Since $n$ is an **integer**, $2\pi n$ is an integer multiple of $2\pi$, hence:

$$\cos(2\pi n) = 1 \quad \text{and} \quad \sin(2\pi n) = 0$$

Therefore:

$$e^{-j2\pi n} = 1 - j(0) = 1$$

*Logic:* The complex exponential at integer multiples of $2\pi$ is identically equal to **1** by Euler's identity. This is the lynchpin of the entire periodicity proof.

---

### Step 4 — Substitute back and simplify

$$X(e^{j(\omega + 2\pi)}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n]\, e^{-j\omega n}\cdot (1) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n]\, e^{-j\omega n}$$

*Logic:* The factor of 1 can be removed from each term of the summation.

---

### Step 5 — Recognise the original DTFT expression

$$\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n]\, e^{-j\omega n} \;=\; X(e^{j\omega})$$

*Logic:* This is exactly the definition of the forward DTFT. The summation is unchanged.

---

### Step 6 — Conclusion

$$\boxed{\,X(e^{j(\omega + 2\pi)}) = X(e^{j\omega}) \quad \text{for all } \omega \in \mathbb{R}\,}$$

Hence the DTFT $X(e^{j\omega})$ is **periodic in $\omega$ with fundamental period $2\pi$**. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — DTFT of a One-Sided Decaying Exponential

**Problem:** Compute the DTFT of $x[n] = a^{n} u[n]$ where $\vert a \vert < 1$, and verify its periodicity.

### Step 1 — Apply the forward DTFT definition

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} a^{n} u[n]\, e^{-j\omega n} = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} a^{n} e^{-j\omega n}$$

*Logic:* $u[n] = 1$ for $n \geq 0$ and $0$ otherwise, so the lower limit becomes $n=0$.

---

### Step 2 — Combine terms into a single geometric series

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \bigl(a\, e^{-j\omega}\bigr)^{n}$$

*Logic:* The product $a^{n} e^{-j\omega n} = (a e^{-j\omega})^{n}$ allows a clean geometric-series form.

---

### Step 3 — Apply the infinite geometric series formula

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{1}{1 - a\, e^{-j\omega}}, \quad \text{provided } \vert a\, e^{-j\omega} \vert = \vert a \vert < 1$$

*Logic:* The series $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} r^{n} = \dfrac{1}{1-r}$ converges when $\vert r \vert < 1$. Here $r = a e^{-j\omega}$ and $\vert r \vert = \vert a \vert < 1$ (assumed).

---

### Step 4 — Verify periodicity by shifting $\omega \to \omega + 2\pi$

$$X(e^{j(\omega + 2\pi)}) = \frac{1}{1 - a\, e^{-j(\omega + 2\pi)}} = \frac{1}{1 - a\, e^{-j\omega}\, e^{-j2\pi}} = \frac{1}{1 - a\, e^{-j\omega}\cdot 1} = X(e^{j\omega})$$

*Logic:* Since $e^{-j2\pi} = 1$, the denominator is unchanged, confirming periodicity.

---

### Step 5 — Final Result

$$\boxed{\,X(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{1}{1 - a\, e^{-j\omega}}, \quad X(e^{j(\omega+2\pi)}) = X(e^{j\omega})\,}$$

This is a **complex-valued, $2\pi$-periodic** rational function of $e^{j\omega}$, with a pole at $z = a$ on the real axis of the $z$-plane (inside the unit circle since $\vert a \vert < 1$).

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Verifying Periodicity for a Rectangular Pulse

**Problem:** For $x[n] = 1$ for $0 \leq n \leq N-1$ and $0$ otherwise, find $X(e^{j\omega})$ and confirm periodicity.

### Step 1 — Apply the forward DTFT

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} (1)\, e^{-j\omega n} = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \bigl(e^{-j\omega}\bigr)^{n}$$

---

### Step 2 — Sum the finite geometric series

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{1 - e^{-j\omega N}}{1 - e^{-j\omega}}, \quad \omega \neq 2\pi k$$

At $\omega = 0$ (or multiples of $2\pi$), the limit gives $X(e^{j0}) = N$ by L'Hôpital's rule.

---

### Step 3 — Verify periodicity

$$X(e^{j(\omega+2\pi)}) = \frac{1 - e^{-j(\omega+2\pi)N}}{1 - e^{-j(\omega+2\pi)}} = \frac{1 - e^{-j\omega N}\, e^{-j2\pi N}}{1 - e^{-j\omega}\, e^{-j2\pi}} = \frac{1 - e^{-j\omega N}\cdot 1}{1 - e^{-j\omega}\cdot 1} = X(e^{j\omega})$$

*Logic:* Both $e^{-j2\pi N} = 1$ (integer $N$) and $e^{-j2\pi} = 1$ ensure the expression is unchanged.

---

### Step 4 — Final Result (closed form, often simplified)

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{\sin(\omega N/2)}{\sin(\omega/2)}\, e^{-j\omega(N-1)/2}$$

This is the **Dirichlet kernel**, periodic in $\omega$ with period $2\pi$, as required.

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the conceptual pipeline of computing the DTFT of an aperiodic sequence, evaluating it over one $2\pi$ interval, and recovering the original signal via the IDTFT.

```mermaid

    B -->|Sample one 2 pi period omega in minus pi to pi| C["Principal Spectrum Slice X(e^j omega) omega in minus pi to pi"]
    C -->|Recognise periodic repetition| D["Extended 2 pi Periodic Spectrum for all omega"]
    D -->|Inverse DTFT Integral over one 2 pi span| A
    B -->|Property Test Shift omega by 2 pi| E["Verify X(e^j(omega plus 2 pi)) equals X(e^j omega)"]
    E -->|Result holds for all integer n because e to minus j 2 pi n equals 1| F["Conclusion DTFT is 2 pi Periodic"]
```

## 4.4 Visual Schematic of Periodicity Along the Unit Circle

The unit circle in the $z$-plane is traversed **once** as $\omega$ sweeps from $-\pi$ to $\pi$. Beyond that, the DTFT simply retraces identical values. This block diagram captures the idea:

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    P1 --> P2["omega equals 0 rightmost point z equals 1"]
    P2 --> P3["omega equals plus pi over 2 top"]
    P3 --> P4["omega equals plus pi back to bottom"]
    P4 -.->|periodicity restart identical values| P0
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]`

**State and prove the periodicity property of the Discrete-Time Fourier Transform.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Statement (1 mark):** If $x[n] \;\xleftrightarrow{\text{DTFT}}\; X(e^{j\omega})$, then $X(e^{j(\omega + 2\pi)}) = X(e^{j\omega})$. The DTFT is periodic in $\omega$ with fundamental period $2\pi$.

**Proof (2 marks):**

$$X(e^{j(\omega + 2\pi)}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n]\, e^{-j(\omega+2\pi)n} = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n]\, e^{-j\omega n}\, e^{-j2\pi n}$$

Since $n$ is an integer, $e^{-j2\pi n} = 1$. Hence:

$$X(e^{j(\omega + 2\pi)}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n]\, e^{-j\omega n} = X(e^{j\omega}) \quad \blacksquare$$

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]`

**Why is the DTFT always periodic in $\omega$, whereas the Continuous-Time Fourier Transform (CTFT) is not?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The DTFT is periodic with period $2\pi$ because its basis functions are complex exponentials $e^{j\omega n}$ in which the time index $n$ is restricted to **integers**. For any integer $n$:

$$e^{j(\omega + 2\pi)n} = e^{j\omega n}\, e^{j2\pi n} = e^{j\omega n} \cdot 1 = e^{j\omega n}$$

Hence the spectrum must repeat every $2\pi$ (1 mark). In contrast, the CTFT uses $e^{j\Omega t}$ with $t$ continuous and real, so $e^{j(\Omega + 2\pi)t} \neq e^{j\Omega t}$ in general and the CTFT spectrum is **not periodic** (2 marks).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not confuse the periodicity of DTFT ($2\pi$ in $\omega$) with the periodicity of a discrete-time sinusoid ($2\pi$ in $\omega$, but $2\pi/N$ for finite-length DFT).** Examiners will deduct 2 marks instantly if you mix these up.
> 2. **Always state the condition "$n$ is an integer"** when invoking $e^{-j2\pi n} = 1$. Omitting this earns partial credit only.
> 3. **Integration limits in the IDTFT are "any $2\pi$ interval"** — writing $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}$ is a critical error and loses 2 marks.
> 4. **Do not plot the spectrum beyond $\omega = \pi$** unless explicitly asked — examiners expect you to recognise that the rest is redundant.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Model]`

**A periodic exponential sequence is given by $x[n] = a^{\vert n \vert},\ \vert a \vert < 1$.**

**(a)** Determine the DTFT $X(e^{j\omega})$ of the sequence. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Show explicitly that the spectrum $X(e^{j\omega})$ is periodic with period $2\pi$ and plot its magnitude $\vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert$ over the interval $-\pi \leq \omega \leq \pi$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Split the two-sided exponential into causal and anti-causal parts**

$$x[n] = a^{\vert n \vert} = a^{n}\, u[n] + a^{-n}\, u[-n-1]$$

*Logic:* For $n \geq 0$, $\vert n \vert = n$. For $n < 0$, $\vert n \vert = -n$, and $u[-n-1] = 1$ only when $n \leq -1$.

---

**Step 2 — Apply the DTFT to the causal part**

$$X_{1}(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} a^{n}\, e^{-j\omega n} = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \bigl(a\, e^{-j\omega}\bigr)^{n} = \frac{1}{1 - a\, e^{-j\omega}}, \quad \vert a \vert < 1$$

---

**Step 3 — Apply the DTFT to the anti-causal part**

$$X_{2}(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{-1} a^{-n}\, e^{-j\omega n}$$

Let $m = -n$, so as $n$ goes from $-\infty$ to $-1$, $m$ goes from $\infty$ to $1$:

$$X_{2}(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{m=1}^{\infty} a^{m}\, e^{j\omega m} = \sum_{m=0}^{\infty} \bigl(a\, e^{j\omega}\bigr)^{m} - 1 = \frac{1}{1 - a\, e^{j\omega}} - 1 = \frac{a\, e^{j\omega}}{1 - a\, e^{j\omega}}$$

---

**Step 4 — Combine both parts**

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = X_{1}(e^{j\omega}) + X_{2}(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{1}{1 - a\, e^{-j\omega}} + \frac{a\, e^{j\omega}}{1 - a\, e^{j\omega}}$$

---

**Step 5 — Simplify into a single closed-form expression**

Putting both fractions over a common denominator $1 - a(e^{j\omega} + e^{-j\omega}) + a^{2}$:

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{1 - a^{2}}{1 - 2a\cos\omega + a^{2}}$$

**Final Answer (Part a):**

$$\boxed{\,X(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{1 - a^{2}}{1 - 2a\cos\omega + a^{2}}\,}$$

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Substitute $\omega \to \omega + 2\pi$ in the closed-form expression**

$$X(e^{j(\omega + 2\pi)}) = \frac{1 - a^{2}}{1 - 2a\cos(\omega + 2\pi) + a^{2}}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the periodicity of cosine**

Since $\cos(\omega + 2\pi) = \cos\omega$ (cosine is $2\pi$-periodic):

$$X(e^{j(\omega + 2\pi)}) = \frac{1 - a^{2}}{1 - 2a\cos\omega + a^{2}} = X(e^{j\omega})$$

*Logic:* This explicitly verifies the $2\pi$ periodicity. (4 marks)

**Step 3 — Magnitude plot description (3 marks)**

- The magnitude $\vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert = \dfrac{1 - a^{2}}{1 - 2a\cos\omega + a^{2}}$ is **real and positive** for all $\omega$ (since $\vert a \vert < 1$).
- It is **maximum at $\omega = 0$**: $\vert X(e^{j0}) \vert = \dfrac{1 - a^{2}}{(1 - a)^{2}} = \dfrac{1 + a}{1 - a}$.
- It is **minimum at $\omega = \pm\pi$**: $\vert X(e^{j\pi}) \vert = \dfrac{1 - a^{2}}{(1 + a)^{2}} = \dfrac{1 - a}{1 + a}$.
- The plot is **symmetric about $\omega = 0$** (even function) and **periodic with period $2\pi$**, so values outside $[-\pi, \pi]$ are exact repetitions of the principal interval.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Model]`

**(a)** Define the Discrete-Time Fourier Transform and the Inverse DTFT. State any **two sufficient conditions** for the existence of the DTFT. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the rectangular pulse $x[n] = 1$ for $0 \leq n \leq N-1$ and $0$ otherwise, derive $X(e^{j\omega})$ and prove that it is periodic with period $2\pi$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Forward DTFT definition (2 marks)**

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n]\, e^{-j\omega n}$$

This expresses the spectrum as a sum (in the time index $n$) of complex exponentials $e^{-j\omega n}$ weighted by the signal samples.

---

**Step 2 — Inverse DTFT definition (2 marks)**

$$x[n] = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{2\pi} X(e^{j\omega})\, e^{j\omega n}\, d\omega$$

The integration spans any contiguous $2\pi$ interval. The factor $\dfrac{1}{2\pi}$ compensates for the integration over one full period.

---

**Step 3 — Two sufficient conditions for existence (3 marks)**

**Condition 1 — Absolute summability:** If $\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n] \vert < \infty$, then the DTFT converges uniformly and $X(e^{j\omega})$ is continuous and bounded.

**Condition 2 — Square summability (finite energy):** If $\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n] \vert^{2} < \infty$, then $X(e^{j\omega})$ exists in the **mean-square sense**, and Parseval's theorem holds:

$$\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n] \vert^{2} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} \vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert^{2} d\omega$$

*Logic:* These are *sufficient* (not necessary) — many useful signals (e.g., periodic sequences) have a DTFT only in the generalised sense (using delta functions).

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Write the DTFT for the rectangular pulse**

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n]\, e^{-j\omega n} = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} e^{-j\omega n} = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \bigl(e^{-j\omega}\bigr)^{n}$$

---

**Step 2 — Sum the finite geometric series**

For $\omega \neq 2\pi k$:

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{1 - (e^{-j\omega})^{N}}{1 - e^{-j\omega}} = \frac{1 - e^{-j\omega N}}{1 - e^{-j\omega}}$$

(At $\omega = 0$, by L'Hôpital's rule, $X(e^{j0}) = N$.)

---

**Step 3 — Convert to a more compact trigonometric form**

Multiply numerator and denominator by $e^{j\omega/2}$ and use the identity $1 - e^{-j\theta} = 2j\sin(\theta/2)\, e^{-j\theta/2}$:

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{e^{j\omega N/2}\,(e^{-j\omega N/2} - e^{j\omega N/2})}{e^{j\omega/2}\,(e^{-j\omega/2} - e^{j\omega/2})} = \frac{\sin(\omega N/2)}{\sin(\omega/2)}\, e^{-j\omega(N-1)/2}$$

---

**Step 4 — Prove periodicity by direct substitution**

$$X(e^{j(\omega + 2\pi)}) = \frac{\sin\bigl((\omega + 2\pi)N/2\bigr)}{\sin\bigl((\omega + 2\pi)/2\bigr)}\, e^{-j(\omega + 2\pi)(N-1)/2}$$

Using $\sin(\theta + \pi N) = (-1)^{N}\sin\theta$ and $e^{-j\pi(N-1)} = (-1)^{N-1}$ for integer $N$, both the numerator sine and the exponential pick up cancelling factors of $(-1)^{N}$:

$$X(e^{j(\omega + 2\pi)}) = \frac{(-1)^{N}\sin(\omega N/2)}{(-1)\sin(\omega/2)}\, e^{-j\omega(N-1)/2}\, e^{-j\pi(N-1)} = \frac{\sin(\omega N/2)}{\sin(\omega/2)}\, e^{-j\omega(N-1)/2}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\,X(e^{j(\omega + 2\pi)}) = X(e^{j\omega})\,}$$

confirming the $2\pi$ periodicity of the DTFT of the rectangular pulse. $\blacksquare$

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2 Periodicity Focus**

- **DTFT Definition (Forward):** $X(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n]\, e^{-j\omega n}$ — continuous in $\omega$, sum over integer $n$.
- **IDTFT Definition (Inverse):** $x[n] = \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \int_{2\pi} X(e^{j\omega})\, e^{j\omega n}\, d\omega$ — integral over **one $2\pi$ interval only**.
- **Periodicity is the defining feature of DTFT:** $X(e^{j(\omega + 2\pi)}) = X(e^{j\omega})$, period = $\mathbf{2\pi}$ **radians per sample**.
- **Root cause of periodicity:** $e^{-j2\pi n} = 1$ for every integer $n$ — always state the integer condition in proofs.
- **Existence:** Absolute summability $\Rightarrow$ uniform convergence; square summability $\Rightarrow$ mean-square convergence; Dirichlet conditions suffice in general.
- **Plotting convention:** For real signals, plot $\vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert$ over $[0, \pi]$ or $[-\pi, \pi]$ — values outside are redundant periodic copies.
- **Conjugate symmetry (real signals):** $X(e^{-j\omega}) = X^{*}(e^{j\omega})$, giving even magnitude and odd phase.
- **Parseval's theorem:** Energy in time = average energy in one period of the spectrum, integrated over $[-\pi, \pi]$.
- **Distinguish carefully:**
  - **DTFT** (aperiodic in time $\to$ periodic in $\omega$) ← *this module*.
  - **DFS / DTFS** (periodic in time $\to$ discrete & periodic in $\omega$).
  - **DFT** (finite-length, sampled version of DTFT).
  - **CTFT** (aperiodic in time $\to$ aperiodic & continuous in $\Omega$).
- **Canonical examples to remember:**
  - $a^{n}u[n] \;\leftrightarrow\; \dfrac{1}{1 - a\, e^{-j\omega}}$
  - $a^{\vert n \vert} \;\leftrightarrow\; \dfrac{1 - a^{2}}{1 - 2a\cos\omega + a^{2}}$
  - Rectangular pulse of length $N \;\leftrightarrow\; \dfrac{\sin(\omega N/2)}{\sin(\omega/2)}\, e^{-j\omega(N-1)/2}$ (Dirichlet kernel).
- **Common student pitfall:** Writing "$2\pi$" without specifying "radians per sample" loses 1 mark; confusing $2\pi$ periodicity with $2\pi/N$ spacing (DFT) loses 2 marks.
- **Engineering link:** The periodicity property is what makes digital filter design, OFDM, and spectrum analysers practical — you only ever need to design or display one $2\pi$ window.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Linearity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST416 - SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Linearity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Signals and Systems (PECST416)**, a system $\mathcal{T}$ is said to be **Linear** if and only if it satisfies the **Principle of Superposition**. The principle of superposition is the joint combination of two fundamental properties:

1. **Additivity (Homogeneity over Sum)**
2. **Homogeneity (Scaling Property)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Critical Terminology:** A system is linear **ONLY IF** it satisfies **BOTH** additivity and homogeneity (scaling). Failure of either property makes the system **non-linear**. This is a frequently tested 3-mark definition.

Mathematically, a system $\mathcal{T}$ is linear if for **any arbitrary constants** $a$ and $b$ and **any arbitrary input signals** $x_1(t)$ and $x_2(t)$:

$$\mathcal{T}\{a \cdot x_1(t) + b \cdot x_2(t)\} = a \cdot \mathcal{T}\{x_1(t)\} + b \cdot \mathcal{T}\{x_2(t)\}$$

For discrete-time systems, the analogous expression is:

$$\mathcal{T}\{a \cdot x_1[n] + b \cdot x_2[n]\} = a \cdot \mathcal{T}\{x_1[n]\} + b \cdot \mathcal{T}\{x_2[n]\}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Distinction:** Do not confuse the **Signals & Systems definition of linearity** (based on superposition) with the high-school **"y = mx + c"** definition of a linear equation. Under superposition, a system described by $y(t) = x(t) + 5$ is **LINEAR**, because the "5" is an input-independent offset that is allowed in the strict mathematical definition used in this course.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a perfectly calibrated **production machine** in a factory:
- If you feed in **1 kg of raw material**, it produces **1 unit of output**.
- If you feed in **5 kg of raw material**, it produces exactly **5 units of output** (scaling works).
- If you feed in a mix of two different raw materials simultaneously, the output is exactly the sum of what each would produce separately (additivity works).

This machine is **linear**. Now imagine a second machine that, due to friction, has a "**warm-up threshold**" — you must input at least 1 kg before it produces any output. If you input 0.5 kg, you get 0 output (not 0.5 units). This second machine is **non-linear** because scaling fails for small inputs.

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Mental Test:** A system is linear if **doubling the input exactly doubles the output**, and **adding two inputs produces the sum of their individual outputs**. If either rule breaks, the system is non-linear.

---

### 1.3 Why Linearity Matters (Engineering Relevance)

Linearity is the **single most important property** in signals and systems engineering because:

- **LTI (Linear Time-Invariant)** systems can be fully characterized by their **impulse response** $h(t)$.
- Linear systems allow **frequency-domain analysis** using the **Fourier Transform**, **Laplace Transform**, and **Z-Transform**.
- Any linear system preserves the shape of sinusoids (only amplitude and phase may change).
- Circuit analysis (resistors, capacitors, inductors operating in their linear regions) relies entirely on linearity.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Decomposition of the Superposition Principle

The **single test equation** for linearity can be decomposed into two independent, simpler tests:

#### Test 1 — Additivity (Sum Rule)
For any two inputs $x_1(t)$ and $x_2(t)$:

$$\mathcal{T}\{x_1(t) + x_2(t)\} = \mathcal{T}\{x_1(t)\} + \mathcal{T}\{x_2(t)\}$$

The system response to a **sum of inputs equals the sum of individual responses**.

#### Test 2 — Homogeneity (Scaling Rule)
For any scalar $a$ and any input $x(t)$:

$$\mathcal{T}\{a \cdot x(t)\} = a \cdot \mathcal{T}\{x(t)\}$$

The system response to a **scaled input equals the scaled response**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examination Tip:** When asked to "test for linearity", you must verify **BOTH** additivity and homogeneity (or equivalently, the single combined superposition equation). Showing only one is worth **partial marks (typically 50%)**, not full marks.

---

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **#** | **Property** | **Mathematical Condition** | **Required For Linearity?** |
| :---: | :--- | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | Additivity | $\mathcal{T}\{x_1 + x_2\} = \mathcal{T}\{x_1\} + \mathcal{T}\{x_2\}$ | ✅ Yes (Mandatory) |
| 2 | Homogeneity | $\mathcal{T}\{a \cdot x\} = a \cdot \mathcal{T}\{x\}$ | ✅ Yes (Mandatory) |
| 3 | Superposition (Combined) | $\mathcal{T}\{a x_1 + b x_2\} = a\mathcal{T}\{x_1\} + b\mathcal{T}\{x_2\}$ | ✅ Yes (Mandatory) |
| 4 | Zero-Input → Zero-Output | $\mathcal{T}\{0\} = 0$ | ✅ Yes (Consequence) |
| 5 | Time-Invariance | $y(t-t_0) = \mathcal{T}\{x(t-t_0)\}$ | ❌ No (Separate Test) |
| 6 | Causality | $y(t)$ depends only on $x(\tau)$ for $\tau \le t$ | ❌ No (Separate Test) |

> [!NOTE]
> The fourth property ($\mathcal{T}\{0\} = 0$) is a **necessary consequence** of homogeneity (set $a = 0$). It is a useful **quick-rejection test**: if a system produces a non-zero output for zero input, it is **definitely non-linear**. This is a very common KTU 3-mark shortcut question.

---

### 2.3 Classification of Common Systems (Quick Reference)

| **System Equation** | **Linear?** | **Reason** |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| $y(t) = 2x(t)$ | ✅ Linear | Passes both additivity and homogeneity |
| $y(t) = x(t) + 5$ | ✅ Linear | Constant offset is allowed (passes superposition) |
| $y(t) = x^2(t)$ | ❌ Non-linear | Squaring violates homogeneity (scaling gives $a^2 x^2$, not $a x^2$) |
| $y(t) = \sin(x(t))$ | ❌ Non-linear | $\sin(a \cdot x) \neq a \cdot \sin(x)$ in general |
| $y(t) = x(t) \cdot x(t-1)$ | ❌ Non-linear | Product of signals violates superposition |
| $y[n] = n \cdot x[n]$ | ❌ Non-linear | Time-varying coefficient is non-linear (also time-varying) |
| $y(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{t} x(\tau)\,d\tau$ | ✅ Linear | Integration is a linear operator |
| $y(t) = \dfrac{dx(t)}{dt}$ | ✅ Linear | Differentiation is a linear operator |

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In practice, the test for linearity is the **first checkpoint** engineers apply when analyzing any physical system:
- **Communication systems**: Modulators, filters, and amplifiers are designed to operate linearly to avoid distortion.
- **Control systems**: The Laplace transform (and hence stability/response analysis) only works for linear systems.
- **Signal processing**: Algorithms like **Fourier analysis**, **convolution**, and **sampling theorem** all require linear system assumptions.
- **Circuit analysis**: **Superposition theorem** in electrical circuits is a direct application of linearity.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Proof: Why $\mathcal{T}\{0\} = 0$ is a Consequence of Linearity

This derivation is **frequently asked as a 3-mark question** in KTU exams.

**Given:** A system $\mathcal{T}$ is linear, meaning for any input $x(t)$ and any scalar $a$:

$$\mathcal{T}\{a \cdot x(t)\} = a \cdot \mathcal{T}\{x(t)\}$$

**To Prove:** $\mathcal{T}\{0\} = 0$

**Step 1 — Apply the homogeneity property** with $a = 0$:

$$\mathcal{T}\{0 \cdot x(t)\} = 0 \cdot \mathcal{T}\{x(t)\}$$

**Step 2 — Simplify the left side** (since $0 \cdot x(t) = 0$ for any signal $x(t)$):

$$\mathcal{T}\{0\} = 0 \cdot \mathcal{T}\{x(t)\}$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the right side** (since $0$ times anything is $0$):

$$\mathcal{T}\{0\} = 0$$

**Conclusion:** The property $\mathcal{T}\{0\} = 0$ is a **necessary consequence** of the homogeneity (scaling) property. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 1: Test Linearity of $y(t) = 2x(t) + 3$

**Given System:** $y(t) = 2x(t) + 3$

**Step 1 — Set up the superposition test** with arbitrary constants $a, b$ and inputs $x_1(t), x_2(t)$:

$$\text{LHS} = \mathcal{T}\{a \cdot x_1(t) + b \cdot x_2(t)\}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the system definition to the combined input**:

$$\text{LHS} = 2 \cdot [a \cdot x_1(t) + b \cdot x_2(t)] + 3$$

**Step 3 — Distribute the multiplication**:

$$\text{LHS} = 2a \cdot x_1(t) + 2b \cdot x_2(t) + 3$$

**Step 4 — Apply the system definition to each individual input separately**:

$$\text{RHS} = a \cdot \mathcal{T}\{x_1(t)\} + b \cdot \mathcal{T}\{x_2(t)\}$$

$$\text{RHS} = a \cdot [2x_1(t) + 3] + b \cdot [2x_2(t) + 3]$$

**Step 5 — Expand the right-hand side**:

$$\text{RHS} = 2a \cdot x_1(t) + 3a + 2b \cdot x_2(t) + 3b$$

$$\text{RHS} = 2a \cdot x_1(t) + 2b \cdot x_2(t) + 3(a + b)$$

**Step 6 — Compare LHS and RHS**:

$$\text{LHS} = 2a \cdot x_1(t) + 2b \cdot x_2(t) + 3$$
$$\text{RHS} = 2a \cdot x_1(t) + 2b \cdot x_2(t) + 3(a+b)$$

**Step 7 — Conclusion**:

Since $\text{LHS} \neq \text{RHS}$ in general (the constant terms differ: $3$ vs $3(a+b)$), the system **fails the superposition test**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Student Mistake:** Students often wrongly conclude that $y(t) = 2x(t) + 3$ is **non-linear** because of the "**+ 3**" term. **This is INCORRECT** under the Signals & Systems definition. The correct approach is to use the superposition test as shown above. A simpler trick: the system passes the $\mathcal{T}\{0\} = 0$ test ($y = 3 \neq 0$), so it is non-linear — but the formal superposition proof is the full-mark answer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Correct Classification:** $y(t) = 2x(t) + 3$ is **NON-LINEAR** under the strict superposition definition. (The "$y=mx+c$" intuition of linearity from school mathematics does NOT apply here.)

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 2: Test Linearity of $y(t) = \sin(x(t))$

**Given System:** $y(t) = \sin(x(t))$

**Step 1 — Apply the homogeneity test with scalar $a$ and input $x(t)$**:

$$\text{LHS} = \mathcal{T}\{a \cdot x(t)\} = \sin(a \cdot x(t))$$

**Step 2 — Apply the system definition scaled by $a$**:

$$\text{RHS} = a \cdot \mathcal{T}\{x(t)\} = a \cdot \sin(x(t))$$

**Step 3 — Compare LHS and RHS**:

The trigonometric identity $\sin(a \cdot x(t)) = a \cdot \sin(x(t))$ is **NOT true** in general (it only holds for small $a$ and special values, e.g., $a = 1$ or $a = 0$).

For example, let $a = 2$ and $x(t) = \pi/2$:
- LHS: $\sin(2 \cdot \pi/2) = \sin(\pi) = 0$
- RHS: $2 \cdot \sin(\pi/2) = 2 \cdot 1 = 2$

Since $0 \neq 2$, homogeneity fails.

**Step 4 — Conclusion**: The system $y(t) = \sin(x(t))$ is **NON-LINEAR**.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example 3: Test Linearity of an LTI System (Continuous-Time)

**Given System:** $y(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{t} x(\tau) \, d\tau$

**Step 1 — Set up the combined superposition test**:

$$\text{LHS} = \mathcal{T}\{a \cdot x_1(t) + b \cdot x_2(t)\} = \int_{-\infty}^{t} [a \cdot x_1(\tau) + b \cdot x_2(\tau)] \, d\tau$$

**Step 2 — Use the linearity of integration to split the integral**:

$$\text{LHS} = a \cdot \int_{-\infty}^{t} x_1(\tau) \, d\tau + b \cdot \int_{-\infty}^{t} x_2(\tau) \, d\tau$$

**Step 3 — Recognize the individual system responses**:

$$\text{RHS} = a \cdot \mathcal{T}\{x_1(t)\} + b \cdot \mathcal{T}\{x_2(t)\} = a \cdot \int_{-\infty}^{t} x_1(\tau) \, d\tau + b \cdot \int_{-\infty}^{t} x_2(\tau) \, d\tau$$

**Step 4 — Compare LHS and RHS**:

$$\text{LHS} = \text{RHS}$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion**: The system is **LINEAR** (and is in fact the LTI **accumulator/integrator** system with impulse response $h(t) = u(t)$).

---

### 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix (Exam Strategy)

| **Observation When Testing** | **Verdict** | **Action** |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| System contains $x^2(t)$, $x^3(t)$, $\sqrt{x(t)}$, $\vert x(t) \vert$ | ❌ Non-Linear | Stop and declare non-linear |
| System contains $\sin(x)$, $\cos(x)$, $e^{x}$, $\log(x)$ | ❌ Non-Linear | Stop and declare non-linear |
| System contains $x(t) \cdot x(t-\tau)$ (product of signals) | ❌ Non-Linear | Stop and declare non-linear |
| System has time-varying coefficient (e.g., $t \cdot x(t)$) | ❌ Non-Linear (also Time-Variant) | Stop and declare non-linear |
| System has a constant offset ($+C$) | ❌ Non-Linear | Verify using $\mathcal{T}\{0\} = 0$ test |
| System is $y = kx$, $y = $ sum of scaled/delayed inputs, $y = \int x$, $y = \frac{dx}{dt}$ | ✅ Linear | Confirm with full superposition proof |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Linearity Test Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Given System y = T{x}] --> B{Quick Check 1: Contains x^2, sin x, product x1*x2, time-varying coefficient?}
    B -- YES --> C[VERDICT: NON-LINEAR]
    B -- NO --> D{Quick Check 2: T{0} = 0?}
    D -- NO --> E[VERDICT: NON-LINEAR constant offset detected]
    D -- YES --> F[Apply Superposition Test: T{a*x1 + b*x2}]
    F --> G[Compute LHS using system equation]
    G --> H[Compute RHS as a*T{x1} + b*T{x2}]
    H --> I{LHS equals RHS for ALL a, b, x1, x2?}
    I -- YES --> J[VERDICT: LINEAR System]
    I -- NO --> K[VERDICT: NON-LINEAR System]
    E --> Z[End]
    C --> Z
    J --> Z
    K --> Z
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Flowchart:** Always perform the **quick-rejection checks** (squares, trig, products, constant offsets) **first** to save time in the KTU examination hall. Only run the full superposition test when the quick checks pass.

---

### 4.2 Block-Level Architecture of a Linear System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    input1[Input x1] --> sumNode
    input2[Input x2] --> sumNode
    coeffA[Scalar a] --> mulA
    sumNode --> mulA
    mulA --> combSum((+))
    input1b[Input x1] --> sysT1[System T]
    input2b[Input x2] --> sysT2[System T]
    sysT1 --> combSum
    coeffB[Scalar b] --> mulB
    sysT2 --> mulB
    mulB --> combSum
    combSum --> output[Output y]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** This diagram shows the **two equivalent paths** for a linear system. Path 1 (top): combine inputs first, then pass through $\mathcal{T}$. Path 2 (bottom): pass each input through $\mathcal{T}$ separately, then combine. **For a linear system, both paths produce identical output.** This is the visual essence of superposition.

---

### 4.3 Schematic: Impulse Response Decomposition (LTI Foundation)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    delta[Impulse delta t] --> h[Impulse Response h t]
    h --> conv[Convolution Operation]
    x[Input Signal x t] --> conv
    conv --> y[Output y t]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Connection to Linearity:** Once a system is verified to be **linear** and **time-invariant (LTI)**, its complete behavior is fully described by the **impulse response $h(t)$** shown above. The output is the convolution $y(t) = x(t) * h(t)$. This is why linearity testing is always the **first step** in KTU problems on LTI systems.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**
*Define linearity in the context of signals and systems. State the two conditions a system must satisfy to be called linear.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A system $\mathcal{T}$ is **linear** if it satisfies the **principle of superposition**, which has two mandatory conditions:

1. **Additivity:** $\mathcal{T}\{x_1(t) + x_2(t)\} = \mathcal{T}\{x_1(t)\} + \mathcal{T}\{x_2(t)\}$
2. **Homogeneity (Scaling):** $\mathcal{T}\{a \cdot x(t)\} = a \cdot \mathcal{T}\{x(t)\}$ for any scalar $a$.

Both conditions must hold **simultaneously** for **all** inputs and scalars. If either fails, the system is non-linear. *(1 mark for stating the principle, 1 mark for additivity, 1 mark for homogeneity.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Expected]**
*Show that a necessary condition for a system to be linear is that its response to a zero input is zero.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Given a linear system, by the **homogeneity property**:

$$\mathcal{T}\{a \cdot x(t)\} = a \cdot \mathcal{T}\{x(t)\}$$

Substituting $a = 0$:

$$\mathcal{T}\{0 \cdot x(t)\} = 0 \cdot \mathcal{T}\{x(t)\}$$

$$\mathcal{T}\{0\} = 0 \quad \blacksquare$$

Hence, a zero input must produce a zero output for any linear system. *(1 mark for stating homogeneity, 1 mark for substitution, 1 mark for the final conclusion.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

---

**Q3. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** *Test whether the system described by* $y(t) = 2x(t) + 5$ *is linear. Justify your answer with a mathematical proof. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *Test whether the system described by* $y(t) = x^2(t)$ *is linear. Justify your answer with a counter-example. (7 marks)*

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — System:** $y(t) = 2x(t) + 5$

**Method: Using the $\mathcal{T}\{0\} = 0$ quick test:**

For zero input, $x(t) = 0$:

$$y(t) = 2(0) + 5 = 5 \neq 0$$

Since $\mathcal{T}\{0\} \neq 0$, the system **violates the necessary condition for linearity**.

**Verification via full superposition test:**

Let input be $a \cdot x_1(t) + b \cdot x_2(t)$:

$$\text{LHS} = 2[a \cdot x_1(t) + b \cdot x_2(t)] + 5 = 2a \cdot x_1(t) + 2b \cdot x_2(t) + 5$$

$$\text{RHS} = a[2x_1(t) + 5] + b[2x_2(t) + 5] = 2a \cdot x_1(t) + 2b \cdot x_2(t) + 5(a+b)$$

Since $5 \neq 5(a+b)$ for arbitrary $a, b$, **LHS ≠ RHS**.

**Conclusion:** The system is **NON-LINEAR** because of the constant offset $5$. *(7 marks: 2 for quick test, 3 for superposition setup, 1 for comparison, 1 for final conclusion.)*

---

**Part (b) — System:** $y(t) = x^2(t)$

**Homogeneity Test:** Let input be $a \cdot x(t)$:

$$\text{LHS} = \mathcal{T}\{a \cdot x(t)\} = (a \cdot x(t))^2 = a^2 \cdot x^2(t)$$

$$\text{RHS} = a \cdot \mathcal{T}\{x(t)\} = a \cdot x^2(t)$$

**Counter-example:** Let $a = 2$ and $x(t) = 1$ for all $t$:

$$\text{LHS} = (2 \cdot 1)^2 = 4$$

$$\text{RHS} = 2 \cdot (1)^2 = 2$$

Since $\text{LHS} \neq \text{RHS}$ (i.e., $4 \neq 2$), **homogeneity fails**.

**Conclusion:** The system $y(t) = x^2(t)$ is **NON-LINEAR**. *(7 marks: 2 for setting up homogeneity, 2 for computing LHS, 2 for counter-example showing failure, 1 for conclusion.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Showing only ONE of the two tests** (e.g., only additivity OR only homogeneity) — Deduct **50% marks**.
> 2. **Using a single specific input** (e.g., $x(t) = 1$) and a single specific scalar to "prove" linearity — this is **not a valid proof**. Linearity must hold for **ALL** inputs and **ALL** scalars.
> 3. **Confusing linearity with the $y = mx + c$ school definition** — a constant offset DOES make a system non-linear under the KTU S&S definition.
> 4. **Forgetting to state the conclusion explicitly** — always end with "**Therefore, the system is LINEAR / NON-LINEAR**".

---

#### **Alternative Question Choice (Internal Choice):**

**Q4. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Expected]**

**(a)** *Define the principle of superposition. Show that the system* $y(t) = 3x(t) - 2x(t-1)$ *is linear using the superposition principle. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *State and prove the homogeneity property of linear systems. Hence, deduce that a linear system must produce zero output for zero input. (7 marks)*

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a):**

**Definition:** The principle of superposition states that the response of a system to a **weighted sum of inputs** equals the **weighted sum of the responses** to each input individually. *(2 marks)*

Mathematically, for any scalars $a, b$ and inputs $x_1(t), x_2(t)$:

$$\mathcal{T}\{a \cdot x_1(t) + b \cdot x_2(t)\} = a \cdot \mathcal{T}\{x_1(t)\} + b \cdot \mathcal{T}\{x_2(t)\}$$

**Verification for** $y(t) = 3x(t) - 2x(t-1)$:

LHS:

$$\text{LHS} = 3[a x_1(t) + b x_2(t)] - 2[a x_1(t-1) + b x_2(t-1)]$$

$$\text{LHS} = 3a x_1(t) + 3b x_2(t) - 2a x_1(t-1) - 2b x_2(t-1)$$

RHS:

$$\text{RHS} = a[3x_1(t) - 2x_1(t-1)] + b[3x_2(t) - 2x_2(t-1)]$$

$$\text{RHS} = 3a x_1(t) - 2a x_1(t-1) + 3b x_2(t) - 2b x_2(t-1)$$

Comparing term-by-term, $\text{LHS} = \text{RHS}$. Hence, the system is **LINEAR**. *(5 marks for verification, 2 marks for definition.)*

---

**Part (b):**

**Statement:** The homogeneity property states that if the input to a linear system is scaled by a constant factor $a$, then the output is also scaled by the same factor $a$. *(2 marks)*

Mathematically:

$$\mathcal{T}\{a \cdot x(t)\} = a \cdot \mathcal{T}\{x(t)\}$$

**Proof:** This is a direct consequence of the superposition principle. Setting $b = 0$ and replacing $x_1(t)$ with $x(t)$ in the superposition equation:

$$\mathcal{T}\{a \cdot x(t) + 0 \cdot x_2(t)\} = a \cdot \mathcal{T}\{x(t)\} + 0 \cdot \mathcal{T}\{x_2(t)\}$$

Simplifying:

$$\mathcal{T}\{a \cdot x(t)\} = a \cdot \mathcal{T}\{x(t)\} \quad \blacksquare$$

**Deduction (Zero-Input Property):** Substituting $a = 0$ in the homogeneity equation:

$$\mathcal{T}\{0 \cdot x(t)\} = 0 \cdot \mathcal{T}\{x(t)\}$$

$$\mathcal{T}\{0\} = 0 \quad \blacksquare$$

Hence, a linear system **must** produce zero output for zero input. *(2 marks for statement, 2 marks for proof, 1 mark for substitution, 1 mark for conclusion.)*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist — Linearity**

- ✅ **Definition**: A system is linear if it satisfies **superposition** = **additivity** + **homogeneity**.
- ✅ **Additivity**: $\mathcal{T}\{x_1 + x_2\} = \mathcal{T}\{x_1\} + \mathcal{T}\{x_2\}$
- ✅ **Homogeneity**: $\mathcal{T}\{a \cdot x\} = a \cdot \mathcal{T}\{x\}$
- ✅ **Superposition (combined)**: $\mathcal{T}\{a x_1 + b x_2\} = a \mathcal{T}\{x_1\} + b \mathcal{T}\{x_2\}$
- ✅ **Zero-Input Test**: $\mathcal{T}\{0\} = 0$ is a **necessary** condition (quick-rejection tool).
- ✅ **Non-linear indicators**: $x^2$, $x^3$, $\vert x \vert$, $\sin(x)$, $\cos(x)$, $e^x$, $\log(x)$, signal products, square roots, time-varying coefficients.
- ✅ **Linear operators**: Multiplication by constant, addition, time-shift, integration, differentiation, convolution.
- ✅ **Constant offset trap**: $y = x + C$ is **NON-LINEAR** in the KTU S&S definition (NOT $y = mx + c$ from school).
- ✅ **Always show BOTH additivity AND homogeneity** for full marks in 14-mark questions.
- ✅ **Use generic variables** ($a, b, x_1(t), x_2(t)$) — never prove linearity with a single specific input value.
- ✅ **Always conclude explicitly**: "Therefore, the system is **linear / non-linear**."
- ✅ **Linearity ≠ Time-Invariance**: These are **independent** properties. A system can be linear but time-variant (e.g., $y(t) = t \cdot x(t)$) or non-linear but time-invariant (e.g., $y(t) = x^2(t)$).
- ✅ **LTI systems** (Linear + Time-Invariant) are the most important class — fully characterized by impulse response $h(t)$.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Translation (Time Shifting)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST416 - SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Translation (Time Shifting)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
