| Time Reversal + Shift | $x(-t + T)$ or $x(-(t - T))$ | Mirror about $t = 0$, then shift right by $T$. |
| Time Shift + Reversal | $x(-t - T)$ or $x(-(t + T))$ | Shift left by $T$, then mirror about $t = 0$. |

> [!WARNING]
> These are **NOT the same**! The order of operations matters. $x(-t+T) \neq x(-t-T)$. They differ by a sign on the shift amount.

---

### 🔁 Special Cases — Even and Odd Signals

The structure of a signal under time reversal is the cornerstone of a major classification:

> [!NOTE]
> **Even Signal:** A signal is **even** if it satisfies:
> $$x(-t) = x(t) \quad \text{for all } t$$
> Examples: $\cos(\omega t)$, $t^2$, $u(t) + u(-t)$.
> *Effect of time reversal:* The signal is **completely unchanged**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Odd Signal:** A signal is **odd** if it satisfies:
> $$x(-t) = -x(t) \quad \text{for all } t$$
> Examples: $\sin(\omega t)$, $t$, $t^3$.
> *Effect of time reversal:* The shape is mirrored AND the amplitude is **inverted** (sign-flipped).

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** Whenever asked to "sketch $x(-t)$", draw the original signal, then **fold it like a piece of paper along the $y$-axis**. If the signal is even, the sketch is identical; if odd, it flips vertically too.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Mathematical Logic — Step-by-Step Breakdown

To understand time reversal rigorously, follow this reasoning chain:

1. **Substitution Rule:** The transformation $t \rightarrow -t$ is applied to the argument. For composite signals like $x(t) = f(g(t))$, time reversal yields $x(-t) = f(g(-t))$.
2. **Sample Mapping:** A sample at $t = t_0$ with amplitude $x(t_0)$ appears at $t = -t_0$ with the **same amplitude**.
3. **Mirror Property:** The signal axis becomes $\tau = -t$. As $t$ increases (forward in original), $\tau$ decreases — hence the mirror.
4. **Composition with Shifting:** When combined with time shifting, the order of application determines the final form.
5. **Invariants:** Certain signal measures (energy, power, area) remain unchanged under reversal because of the absolute value or because of symmetry of integration.

---

## 2.2 Non-Commutativity of Time Shift and Time Reversal

This is a **favourite KTU question**. The two operations **do not commute**.

### Path 1: Shift First, Then Reverse
Start: $x(t)$
1. Apply time shift by $T$: $\;y_1(t) = x(t - T)$
2. Apply time reversal: $\;y_1(-t) = x(-t - T)$

$$
\boxed{\text{Shift then Reverse: } x(t) \longrightarrow x(t-T) \longrightarrow x(-t-T)}
$$

### Path 2: Reverse First, Then Shift
Start: $x(t)$
1. Apply time reversal: $\;y_2(t) = x(-t)$
2. Apply time shift by $T$: $\;y_2(t-T) = x(-(t-T)) = x(-t+T)$

$$
\boxed{\text{Reverse then Shift: } x(t) \longrightarrow x(-t) \longrightarrow x(-t+T)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** $x(-t-T) \neq x(-t+T)$. The **order matters**. Only if $T = 0$ do they coincide.

---

## 2.3 Even–Odd Decomposition (Fundamental Theorem)

> [!NOTE]
> **Any arbitrary signal** $x(t)$ can be **uniquely expressed** as the sum of an even component and an odd component:
> $$x(t) = x_e(t) + x_o(t)$$

The components are extracted as:

$$
x_e(t) = \frac{x(t) + x(-t)}{2} \quad \text{(Even Part)}
$$

$$
x_o(t) = \frac{x(t) - x(-t)}{2} \quad \text{(Odd Part)}
$$

**Verification:**
- $x_e(-t) = \dfrac{x(-t) + x(t)}{2} = x_e(t)$ → Even ✓
- $x_o(-t) = \dfrac{x(-t) - x(t)}{2} = -x_o(t)$ → Odd ✓
- $x_e(t) + x_o(t) = \dfrac{x(t)+x(-t)}{2} + \dfrac{x(t)-x(-t)}{2} = x(t)$ ✓

---

## 2.4 📊 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Quick Reference)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use `\vert` for absolute value in tables to preserve markdown formatting.**

| # | Property / Identity | Formula | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Basic Time Reversal** | $x(t) \rightarrow x(-t)$ | Signal mirroring |
| 2 | **Shift then Reverse** | $x(t-T) \rightarrow x(-t-T)$ | Convolution kernel flipping |
| 3 | **Reverse then Shift** | $x(-t) \rightarrow x(-t+T)$ | Symmetry-based analysis |
| 4 | **Even Component** | $x_e(t) = \dfrac{x(t) + x(-t)}{2}$ | Fourier Cosine Transform setup |
| 5 | **Odd Component** | $x_o(t) = \dfrac{x(t) - x(-t)}{2}$ | Fourier Sine Transform setup |
| 6 | **Even Signal Test** | $x(-t) = x(t)$ | Symmetry check for DFT |
| 7 | **Odd Signal Test** | $x(-t) = -x(t)$ | Symmetry check for DFT |
| 8 | **Area Invariance** | $\displaystyle\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(-t)\,dt = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(\tau)\,d\tau$ | DC component unchanged |
| 9 | **Energy Invariance** | $\displaystyle\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(-t) \vert^2\,dt = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(\tau) \vert^2\,d\tau = E$ | Total energy preserved |
| 10 | **Power Invariance** | $\displaystyle\lim_{T\to\infty}\frac{1}{2T}\int_{-T}^{T} \vert x(-t) \vert^2\,dt = P$ | Total power preserved |
| 11 | **DC Value from Odd Part** | $x_o(0) = 0$ always | Quick sanity check |
| 12 | **Even Part at Origin** | $x_e(0) = x(0)$ | Quick sanity check |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Time reversal is **not a textbook curiosity** — it is deeply embedded in modern engineering:

- **Radar & Sonar (Matched Filtering):** To detect a target, the received signal $x(t)$ is correlated with a **time-reversed template** $x(-t)$, maximizing the Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR).
- **Ultrasound Imaging (Time-Reversal Mirrors):** A transducer array records waves, reverses them in time electronically, and re-emits them to **focus back on the original source** — used in non-invasive medical imaging and defect detection in metals.
- **Convolution Operation:** $y(t) = x(t) * h(t) = \int x(\tau)h(t-\tau)\,d\tau$. The term $h(t-\tau)$ is the time-reversed and shifted impulse response — the very foundation of LTI system analysis.
- **Fourier Transform Symmetry:** Real even signals $\leftrightarrow$ Real even Fourier transforms; Real odd signals $\leftrightarrow$ Imaginary odd Fourier transforms.
- **Digital Audio Effects:** Reverse reverb, backward masking, and "flip" effects are direct applications of time reversal.
- **Autocorrelation Function:** $R_x(\tau) = \int x(t)x(t+\tau)\,dt$ is **always an even function** of $\tau$ — a direct consequence of time-reversal symmetry.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I: Even and Odd Decomposition of an Arbitrary Signal

**Goal:** Show that any signal $x(t)$ can be written as $x(t) = x_e(t) + x_o(t)$.

### Step 1 — Start with the conjecture
We propose that there exist functions $x_e(t)$ and $x_o(t)$ such that:

$$
x(t) = x_e(t) + x_o(t)
$$

### Step 2 — Apply time reversal to the equation
Replace $t$ with $-t$ in the above equation:

$$
x(-t) = x_e(-t) + x_o(-t)
$$

### Step 3 — Apply the definitions of even and odd
By definition, an even function satisfies $x_e(-t) = x_e(t)$ and an odd function satisfies $x_o(-t) = -x_o(t)$. Substituting:

$$
x(-t) = x_e(t) - x_o(t)
$$

### Step 4 — Solve the linear system
We now have a system of two equations in two unknowns ($x_e(t)$ and $x_o(t)$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
x(t) &= x_e(t) + x_o(t) \quad &\text{(Equation A)} \\
x(-t) &= x_e(t) - x_o(t) \quad &\text{(Equation B)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Add Equation A and Equation B:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x(t) + x(-t) &= \bigl[x_e(t) + x_o(t)\bigr] + \bigl[x_e(t) - x_o(t)\bigr] \\
&= 2\,x_e(t)
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\,x_e(t) = \frac{x(t) + x(-t)}{2}\,}
$$

**Subtract Equation B from Equation A:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x(t) - x(-t) &= \bigl[x_e(t) + x_o(t)\bigr] - \bigl[x_e(t) - x_o(t)\bigr] \\
&= 2\,x_o(t)
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\,x_o(t) = \frac{x(t) - x(-t)}{2}\,}
$$

This completes the proof. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Derivation II: Non-Commutativity of Time Shifting and Time Reversal

**Goal:** Prove algebraically that reversing after shifting yields a different result than shifting after reversing.

### Path A — Shift first, then reverse
Let $y_1(t)$ be the result after shifting $x(t)$ by $T$:

$$
y_1(t) = x(t - T)
$$

Now apply time reversal: replace $t$ with $-t$:

$$
y_1(-t) = x(-t - T)
$$

### Path B — Reverse first, then shift
Let $y_2(t)$ be the result after reversing $x(t)$:

$$
y_2(t) = x(-t)
$$

Now apply a time shift by $T$: replace $t$ with $(t - T)$:

$$
y_2(t - T) = x(-(t - T)) = x(-t + T)
$$

### Comparison

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Path A:} \quad & x(-t - T) \\
\text{Path B:} \quad & x(-t + T) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

The two differ by the sign in front of $T$. Hence:

$$
\boxed{\,x(-t - T) \neq x(-t + T) \quad \text{for } T \neq 0\,}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Interpretation:** In Path A, the signal is shifted *first* (say, to the right) and then mirrored; in Path B, it is mirrored *first* and then shifted to the right. The "anchor point" about which the signal is folded changes, producing two different results.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III: Energy Invariance Under Time Reversal

**Goal:** Show that $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(-t) \vert^2\,dt = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^2\,dt$.

### Step 1 — Define the energy of the original signal

$$
E_x = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^2\,dt
$$

### Step 2 — Define the energy of the time-reversed signal

$$
E_{x_r} = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(-t) \vert^2\,dt
$$

### Step 3 — Apply the substitution $\tau = -t$
When $t \rightarrow -\infty$, $\tau \rightarrow +\infty$, and when $t \rightarrow +\infty$, $\tau \rightarrow -\infty$. Also, $d\tau = -dt$, so $dt = -d\tau$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{x_r} &= \int_{+\infty}^{-\infty} \vert x(\tau) \vert^2 \cdot (-d\tau) \\
&= \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert x(\tau) \vert^2\,d\tau \quad &\text{(flipping limits cancels the minus sign)} \\
&= E_x
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\,E_{x_r} = E_x\,}
$$

The total energy of the signal is **invariant** under time reversal. The same proof (with a limit) holds for **average power** $P$.

---

## 3.4 Derivation IV: Area Invariance Under Time Reversal

**Goal:** Show that $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(-t)\,dt = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t)\,dt$.

Using the same substitution $\tau = -t$, $dt = -d\tau$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(-t)\,dt &= \int_{+\infty}^{-\infty} x(\tau) \cdot (-d\tau) \\
&= \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(\tau)\,d\tau
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence:

$$
\boxed{\,\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(-t)\,dt = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t)\,dt = A\,}
$$

The net signed area is preserved. **However**, the time-reversed waveform starts where the original ended — a critical observation in signal sketching.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example — Even/Odd Decomposition of $x(t) = e^{-2t}u(t)$

Given the signal $x(t) = e^{-2t}u(t)$, find its even and odd parts.

### Step 1 — Compute the time-reversed signal
By definition $u(t) \rightarrow u(-t)$, so:

$$
x(-t) = e^{2t}u(-t)
$$

### Step 2 — Apply the even-part formula

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_e(t) &= \frac{x(t) + x(-t)}{2} \\
&= \frac{1}{2}\bigl[e^{-2t}u(t) + e^{2t}u(-t)\bigr]
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Apply the odd-part formula

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_o(t) &= \frac{x(t) - x(-t)}{2} \\
&= \frac{1}{2}\bigl[e^{-2t}u(t) - e^{2t}u(-t)\bigr]
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Verify by reconstruction

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_e(t) + x_o(t) &= \frac{1}{2}\bigl[e^{-2t}u(t) + e^{2t}u(-t) + e^{-2t}u(t) - e^{2t}u(-t)\bigr] \\
&= \frac{1}{2}\bigl[2\,e^{-2t}u(t)\bigr] \\
&= e^{-2t}u(t) = x(t) \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Note:** $x_e(t)$ here is a **two-sided decaying exponential** (symmetric about $t=0$), and $x_o(t)$ is a **two-sided anti-symmetric** waveform. Both extend over $(-\infty, \infty)$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Time Reversal as a Signal-Processing Operation

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates how a signal flows through a "Time Reversal" processing block, and how it differs from a "Time Shifting" block.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputA["Input Signal x of t"] --> shiftBlock["Time Shifter T greater than zero"]
    shiftBlock --> outputA["Output x of t minus T"]
    inputA --> reverseBlock["Time Reverser"]
    reverseBlock --> outputB["Output x of minus t"]
    reverseBlock --> shiftAfter["Time Shifter T"]
    shiftAfter --> outputC["Output x of minus t plus T"]
    shiftBlock --> reverseAfter["Time Reverser"]
    reverseAfter --> outputD["Output x of minus t minus T"]
    outputC -. NOT EQUAL .-> outputD
    outputD -. NOT EQUAL .-> outputC
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The central claim is the inequality — Path B (`x(-t+T)`) and Path A (`x(-t-T)`) are **NOT equal**. This visually captures the non-commutativity of the two operations.

---

## 4.2 Flowchart — Decision Tree for Sketching $x(-t)$

A student should follow this exact decision tree when asked to sketch the time reversal of any signal in the exam.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startNode["Given signal x of t"] --> q1{"Is signal symmetric about y axis?"}
    q1 -- "Yes, even" --> ansEven["Draw x of minus t equal to x of t unchanged"]
    q1 -- "No" --> q2{"Is signal anti symmetric about y axis?"}
    q2 -- "Yes, odd" --> ansOdd["Mirror about y axis AND flip vertically"]
    q2 -- "No, neither" --> ansGeneral["Fold entire waveform about vertical axis y axis"]
    ansGeneral --> checkStep["Verify: sample at t0 appears at minus t0 with same amplitude"]
    ansOdd --> checkStep
    ansEven --> checkStep
    checkStep --> endNode["Final sketch x of minus t complete"]
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid State Diagram — Even/Odd Decomposition Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    s1["Start: Original signal x of t"] --> s2["Compute time reversed signal x of minus t"]
    s2 --> s3["Add x of t and x of minus t pointwise"]
    s3 --> s4["Divide the sum by 2 to get Even Part x sub e of t"]
    s2 --> s5["Subtract x of minus t from x of t pointwise"]
    s5 --> s6["Divide the difference by 2 to get Odd Part x sub o of t"]
    s4 --> s7["Verify x sub e minus t equals x sub e of t"]
    s5 --> s8["Verify x sub o minus t equals negative x sub o of t"]
    s7 --> s9["Add x sub e of t and x sub o of t to recover x of t"]
    s8 --> s9
    s9 --> s10["Decomposition successful"]
```

---

## 4.4 Schematic — Time Reversal of a Rectangular Pulse

Suppose $x(t)$ is a rectangular pulse of height $A$ extending from $t = 1$ to $t = 3$:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subA["Subplot 1: Original x of t - Pulse from t equals 1 to t equals 3, height A"]
    subB["Subplot 2: Reversed x of minus t - Pulse from t equals minus 3 to t equals minus 1, height A"]
    subA --> arrow1["Folding line at t equals zero"]
    arrow1 --> subB
```

> [!TIP]
> **Visualisation rule:** For a pulse defined on $[a, b]$, the time-reversed pulse $x(-t)$ is defined on $[-b, -a]$. Note the **order of the interval is reversed**, but the **height/width is preserved**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 | Module 2 | 3 Marks]**
Define **time reversal** of a continuous-time signal. State the effect of this operation on the **total energy** and **average power** of the signal.

**Model Answer:**

Time reversal of a continuous-time signal $x(t)$ produces a new signal $x(-t)$, obtained by replacing $t$ with $-t$. This operation mirrors the signal about the vertical axis ($t = 0$). A sample originally at $t = t_0$ now appears at $t = -t_0$.

**Effect on Energy:** Time reversal preserves the total energy of the signal. Mathematically:

$$
E_{x_r} = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(-t) \vert^2\,dt = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(\tau) \vert^2\,d\tau = E_x
$$

**Effect on Power:** Time reversal also preserves the average power:

$$
P_{x_r} = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T}\int_{-T}^{T} \vert x(-t) \vert^2\,dt = P_x
$$

This invariance follows from the substitution $\tau = -t$, which maps the integration range bijectively onto itself.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 | Module 2 | 3 Marks]**
What are **even** and **odd** signals? Express an arbitrary signal $x(t)$ as the sum of its even and odd components.

**Model Answer:**

An **even signal** satisfies $x(-t) = x(t)$ for all $t$. Such a signal is symmetric about the vertical axis. Example: $\cos(\omega t)$.

An **odd signal** satisfies $x(-t) = -x(t)$ for all $t$. Such a signal is anti-symmetric about the vertical axis. Example: $\sin(\omega t)$.

Any arbitrary signal $x(t)$ can be **uniquely decomposed** as:

$$
\boxed{\,x(t) = x_e(t) + x_o(t)\,}
$$

where:

$$
x_e(t) = \frac{x(t) + x(-t)}{2}, \qquad x_o(t) = \frac{x(t) - x(-t)}{2}
$$

The even part $x_e(t)$ is obtained by averaging the signal with its time-reversed version, while the odd part $x_o(t)$ is obtained by taking half their difference.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

---

### 📝 Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — May 2023 | Module 2 | 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Explain the operations of **time shifting** and **time reversal** on continuous-time signals with suitable examples. Show that the two operations are **not commutative**. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** A signal $x(t)$ is defined as a rectangular pulse of amplitude $2$ extending from $t = -1$ to $t = 2$. Sketch the following:
- (i) $x(t)$
- (ii) $x(-t)$
- (iii) $x(t - 3)$
- (iv) $x(-t + 2)$ *(7 Marks)*

#### Model Solution for Part (a)

**Time Shifting:** The operation $x(t) \rightarrow x(t - T)$ slides the signal by $T$ units along the time axis. If $T > 0$, the signal shifts to the **right** (delayed); if $T < 0$, it shifts to the **left** (advanced). For example, if $x(t) = \sin(t)$, then $x(t - \pi/2) = \sin(t - \pi/2)$ is delayed by $\pi/2$ seconds.

**Time Reversal:** The operation $x(t) \rightarrow x(-t)$ mirrors the signal about the vertical axis. Every sample at $t = t_0$ is mapped to $t = -t_0$. For $x(t) = e^{-t}u(t)$ (decaying right-sided exponential), $x(-t) = e^{t}u(-t)$ is a growing **left-sided** exponential.

**Non-Commutativity Proof:**

*Path 1 — Shift first, then reverse:*

$$
x(t) \xrightarrow{\text{shift by } T} x(t - T) \xrightarrow{\text{reverse}} x(-t - T)
$$

*Path 2 — Reverse first, then shift:*

$$
x(t) \xrightarrow{\text{reverse}} x(-t) \xrightarrow{\text{shift by } T} x(-(t - T)) = x(-t + T)
$$

Since $x(-t - T) \neq x(-t + T)$ for $T \neq 0$, the two operations **do not commute**. The order of application changes the final result. The conceptual reason: the "folding axis" of the reversal operation (which is $t=0$) is fixed; shifting the signal before folding changes the alignment of the signal with respect to this fixed axis.

#### Model Solution for Part (b)

Given: $x(t) = 2 \cdot \text{rect}\bigl((t - 0.5)/3\bigr)$, a rectangular pulse of amplitude $2$ on $[-1, 2]$.

- **(i) $x(t)$:** Rectangular pulse, amplitude $2$, from $t = -1$ to $t = 2$.

- **(ii) $x(-t)$:** Mirror of $x(t)$ about $t = 0$. The pulse now lies from $t = -2$ to $t = 1$, with the same amplitude $2$. (Interval $[a,b]$ becomes $[-b,-a]$.)

- **(iii) $x(t - 3)$:** Shift the original $x(t)$ right by $3$ units. The pulse now lies from $t = 2$ to $t = 5$, with amplitude $2$.

- **(iv) $x(-t + 2)$:** This equals $x(-(t - 2))$. So first reverse the signal, then shift right by $2$. The pulse is on $[-2, 1]$ after reversal, then shifted right by $2$, giving a pulse on $[0, 3]$ with amplitude $2$.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    s1["Plot 1: x of t - Pulse on minus 1 to 2, height 2"] --> s2["Plot 2: x of minus t - Pulse on minus 2 to 1, height 2"]
    s1 --> s3["Plot 3: x of t minus 3 - Pulse on 2 to 5, height 2"]
    s1 --> s4["Plot 4: x of minus t plus 2 - Pulse on 0 to 3, height 2"]
```

---

### 📝 Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 | Module 2 | 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Find the **even** and **odd** components of the signal $x(t) = e^{-t}u(t)$. Verify your answer. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** A signal $x(t)$ has total energy $E = 10$ J and average power $P = 0$. If we time-reverse the signal to obtain $x(-t)$, what are the new energy and power? Justify with a short derivation. *(7 Marks)*

#### Model Solution for Part (a)

**Step 1 — Compute $x(-t)$:**
For $x(t) = e^{-t}u(t)$, the time-reversed signal is:

$$
x(-t) = e^{t}u(-t)
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the even part:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_e(t) &= \frac{x(t) + x(-t)}{2} \\
&= \frac{1}{2}\bigl[e^{-t}u(t) + e^{t}u(-t)\bigr]
\end{aligned}
$$

In explicit form:
$$
x_e(t) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{1}{2} e^{-t}, & t \geq 0 \\[6pt] \dfrac{1}{2} e^{t}, & t < 0 \end{cases}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the odd part:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_o(t) &= \frac{x(t) - x(-t)}{2} \\
&= \frac{1}{2}\bigl[e^{-t}u(t) - e^{t}u(-t)\bigr]
\end{aligned}
$$

In explicit form:
$$
x_o(t) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{1}{2} e^{-t}, & t \geq 0 \\[6pt] -\dfrac{1}{2} e^{t}, & t < 0 \end{cases}
$$

**Step 4 — Verification:**
Check that $x_e(-t) = x_e(t)$:
- For $t > 0$: $x_e(-t) = \frac{1}{2}e^{-t} = x_e(t)$ ✓
- For $t < 0$: $x_e(-t) = \frac{1}{2}e^{t} = x_e(t)$ ✓

Check that $x_o(-t) = -x_o(t)$:
- For $t > 0$: $x_o(-t) = -\frac{1}{2}e^{t}$ and $-x_o(t) = -\frac{1}{2}e^{-t}$. These are **not equal** in form — they equal at corresponding points. ✓
- For $t < 0$: $x_o(-t) = \frac{1}{2}e^{-t}$ and $-x_o(t) = \frac{1}{2}e^{t}$. Similarly holds pointwise. ✓

**Step 5 — Reconstruction:**

$$
x_e(t) + x_o(t) = \frac{1}{2}e^{-t} + \frac{1}{2}e^{-t} = e^{-t} \quad \text{for } t \geq 0
$$

And for $t < 0$: $x_e + x_o = \frac{1}{2}e^{t} - \frac{1}{2}e^{t} = 0$, which matches $x(t) = 0$ for $t < 0$. ✓

#### Model Solution for Part (b)

**Statement:** The new energy is $E' = 10$ J and the new power is $P' = 0$.

**Derivation for Energy:**

The total energy of $x(-t)$ is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E' &= \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(-t) \vert^2\,dt
\end{aligned}
$$

Apply the substitution $\tau = -t$, so $dt = -d\tau$. When $t \to -\infty$, $\tau \to +\infty$; when $t \to +\infty$, $\tau \to -\infty$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E' &= \int_{+\infty}^{-\infty} \vert x(\tau) \vert^2 \cdot (-d\tau) \\
&= \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert x(\tau) \vert^2\,d\tau \\
&= 10 \text{ J}
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence $E' = 10$ J — energy is **invariant** under time reversal.

**Derivation for Power:**

The average power is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P' &= \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T}\int_{-T}^{T} \vert x(-t) \vert^2\,dt
\end{aligned}
$$

With the same substitution $\tau = -t$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P' &= \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T}\int_{T}^{-T} \vert x(\tau) \vert^2 \cdot (-d\tau) \\
&= \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T}\int_{-T}^{T} \vert x(\tau) \vert^2\,d\tau \\
&= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence $P' = 0$ — power is also **invariant**. Physically, time reversal is just a horizontal mirror; it does not change the magnitudes of the signal samples, hence total energy/power content is preserved.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Mistakes (KTU Board Examiner's Perspective):**
>
> 1. **Sign Confusion in Combined Operations:** The single most common error is writing $x(-t-T)$ when the question asks for $x(-(t-T))$. Always remember: $x(-(t-T)) = x(-t+T)$, NOT $x(-t-T)$. **This single sign error can cost 3–4 marks.**
>
> 2. **Forgetting the Order of Operations:** When a question states "first shift by $T$, then reverse," students often apply reversal first because they "see $-t$ first." Always translate the verbal sequence into mathematical steps **in the order given**.
>
> 3. **Skipping the Verification Step:** In even-odd decomposition problems, KTU examiners often award a **dedicated 1–2 mark sub-credit** for verifying that $x_e(-t) = x_e(t)$ and $x_o(-t) = -x_o(t)$. Skipping this loses easy marks.
>
> 4. **Ignoring the Domain of $u(t)$:** When decomposing $x(t) = e^{-t}u(t)$, students often write $x(-t) = e^{t}u(t)$ (incorrect domain). The correct answer is $x(-t) = e^{t}u(-t)$ — the step function's argument **also** flips.
>
> 5. **Confusing Area with Energy:** Area ($\int x(t)\,dt$) is preserved under time reversal (assuming finite), but the **time-ordering** of the area accumulation is reversed. Energy uses $|x|^2$, so it's preserved trivially. Do not mix the two.
>
> 6. **Missing "Sketch the signal" sub-questions:** Drawing $x(-t)$ is mandatory in Part B questions. A correct mathematical answer with a missing or wrong sketch loses **at least 2–3 marks**.
>
> 7. **Unit Step Function in Even/Odd Parts:** Even/Odd components of a one-sided signal $e^{-t}u(t)$ are **two-sided** signals. Students often incorrectly think $x_e(t) = 0$ for $t < 0$. This is wrong.

---

## 5.4 🧾 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Time Reversal (Module 2)**
>
> - **Definition:** $x(t) \rightarrow x(-t)$ — mirror about the $y$-axis.
> - **Discrete form:** $x[n] \rightarrow x[-n]$.
> - **Shift + Reverse:** $x(t-T) \rightarrow x(-t-T)$ (anchor stays put).
> - **Reverse + Shift:** $x(-t) \rightarrow x(-(t-T)) = x(-t+T)$ (anchor moves).
> - **Non-Commutativity:** Time shift and time reversal **do not commute** for $T \neq 0$.
> - **Even Signal:** $x(-t) = x(t)$ — time reversal leaves it **unchanged**.
> - **Odd Signal:** $x(-t) = -x(t)$ — time reversal mirrors AND inverts amplitude.
> - **Even-Odd Decomposition:** $x(t) = \dfrac{x(t)+x(-t)}{2} + \dfrac{x(t)-x(-t)}{2} = x_e(t) + x_o(t)$.
> - **Energy Invariance:** $\int \vert x(-t) \vert^2 dt = E_x$ — proven by substitution $\tau = -t$.
> - **Power Invariance:** Time reversal does not change average power.
> - **Area Invariance:** $\int x(-t)\,dt = \int x(t)\,dt$ — proven similarly.
> - **Property:** $x_o(0) = 0$ always; $x_e(0) = x(0)$ always.
> - **Sketching rule:** Pulse on $[a, b]$ reverses to $[-b, -a]$.
> - **Composite signal rule:** $f(g(t))$ reverses to $f(g(-t))$ — apply to inner function first.
> - **Real-world link:** Convolution, matched filtering, time-reversal mirrors in ultrasound, Fourier transform symmetry.
> - **KTU favourite question:** "Show that time shifting and time reversal are not commutative." Memorize the two paths.
> - **Common pitfall:** Domain of $u(t)$ also flips under time reversal: $u(t) \rightarrow u(-t)$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Convolution
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST416 - SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Convolution

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

**Convolution** is a mathematical operation that combines two signals to produce a third signal, expressing how the shape of one signal is modified by the other. In the context of **Linear Time-Invariant (LTI)** systems, it is the cornerstone operation that determines the output of any LTI system when the input and the system's impulse response are known.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Exact Board Terminology):**
> The convolution of two continuous-time signals $x(t)$ and $h(t)$ is defined as the **convolution integral**:
>
> $$y(t) = x(t) * h(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(\tau)\, h(t - \tau)\, d\tau$$
>
> The convolution of two discrete-time signals $x[n]$ and $h[n]$ is defined as the **convolution sum**:
>
> $$y[n] = x[n] * h[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{+\infty} x[k]\, h[n - k]$$

The asterisk symbol $*$ denotes the convolution operator (it is **NOT** standard multiplication).

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **🎯 "The Smudge Fingerprint" Analogy**
>
> Imagine you dip your finger in **blue ink** and press it firmly onto a long roll of moving **white paper** moving at constant speed. Now imagine someone else dips their finger in **red ink** and does the same, but slightly delayed.
>
> - The **paper** = the time axis $t$
> - **Blue ink smear** = input signal $x(t)$
> - **Red ink smear** = impulse response $h(t)$ (system's reaction)
> - The **final purple stain on the paper** = output $y(t)$ — a blend of both smears
>
> Convolution simply asks: *"At every instant $t$ on the paper, how much of the red smear overlaps with the time-reversed, shifted blue smear?"* The amount of overlap (area of intersection) at that instant gives the output value.

**A simpler physical analogy:** Think of convolution as a **sliding weighted average** — at every time $t$, you flip one signal, slide it across the other, multiply the overlapping portions point-by-point, and add (integrate) the result.

## 1.3 Why Convolution Matters in Engineering

- **LTI System Analysis:** Output of any LTI system = Input $\ast$ Impulse Response
- **Communications:** Filtering, modulation, channel equalization
- **Image Processing:** Blurring, sharpening, edge detection
- **Control Systems:** System identification and response prediction
- **Audio/Speech Processing:** Reverberation, echo modeling

The "golden rule" of LTI systems: **Knowing $h(t)$ completely characterizes the system** for any input.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four-Step Graphical Convolution Procedure

The graphical method is the most heavily tested convolution technique in KTU exams. Master these four steps:

| Step | Operation | Mathematical Equivalent | Description |
|:----:|:----------|:------------------------|:------------|
| **1** | **Folding (Time-Reversal)** | Replace $t \rightarrow -t$ | Flip $h(\tau)$ to get $h(-\tau)$ |
| **2** | **Shifting** | Replace $t \rightarrow t - t_0$ | Slide $h(-\tau)$ by $t_0$ to get $h(t_0 - \tau)$ |
| **3** | **Multiplication** | Compute $x(\tau) \cdot h(t_0 - \tau)$ | Multiply overlapping portions point-by-point |
| **4** | **Integration (Area)** | Integrate over overlap region | Area under product curve = $y(t_0)$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic:** **"F-S-M-I"** = **F**old, **S**hift, **M**ultiply, **I**ntegrate.
> This is the algorithm that earns full marks in KTU board exams.

## 2.2 Mathematical Properties of Convolution

For KTU 2024, the following algebraic properties are **high-yield** (frequently asked for 7-mark derivations):

| Property | Continuous-Time | Discrete-Time |
|:---------|:----------------|:--------------|
| **Commutative** | $x(t) * h(t) = h(t) * x(t)$ | $x[n] * h[n] = h[n] * x[n]$ |
| **Associative** | $\big(x(t) * h_1(t)\big) * h_2(t) = x(t) * \big(h_1(t) * h_2(t)\big)$ | Same form with $[\cdot]$ |
| **Distributive** | $x(t) * \big(h_1(t) + h_2(t)\big) = x(t)*h_1(t) + x(t)*h_2(t)$ | Same form with $[\cdot]$ |
| **Identity Element** | $x(t) * \delta(t) = x(t)$ | $x[n] * \delta[n] = x[n]$ |
| **Zero Element** | $x(t) * 0 = 0$ | $x[n] * 0[n] = 0$ |
| **Shift Property** | $x(t-t_0) * h(t) = y(t-t_0)$ | $x[n-n_0] * h[n] = y[n-n_0]$ |
| **Derivative Property** | $\dfrac{d}{dt}\big(x(t) * h(t)\big) = \dfrac{dx(t)}{dt} * h(t)$ | $x[n] * h[n]$: difference properties |
| **Integral Property** | $\displaystyle\int_{-\infty}^{t} x(\tau)d\tau * h(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{t} y(\tau)d\tau$ | Running sum property |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The complete convolution toolbox for the board exam:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Continuous Convolution Integral:} \quad y(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(\tau)\, h(t - \tau)\, d\tau \\[6pt]
&\text{Discrete Convolution Sum:} \quad y[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{+\infty} x[k]\, h[n - k] \\[6pt]
&\text{Convolution with Unit Impulse:} \quad x(t) * \delta(t - t_0) = x(t - t_0) \\[6pt]
&\text{Causality Condition (CT):} \quad h(t) = 0 \text{ for } t < 0 \\[6pt]
&\text{LTI Output via Impulse Response:} \quad y(t) = x(t) * h(t) \\[6pt]
&\text{Convolution of Two Rectangular Pulses:} \quad \text{rect}\!\left(\frac{t}{T}\right) * \text{rect}\!\(\frac{t}{T}\) = T \cdot \text{tri}\!\left(\frac{t}{T}\right) \\[6pt]
&\text{Convolution of Two Unit Steps:} \quad u(t) * u(t) = r(t) = t\,u(t) \\[6pt]
&\text{Convolution of Exponential with Step:} \quad e^{-at}u(t) * u(t) = \frac{1 - e^{-at}}{a}\,u(t)
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Table Cell Warning:** Inside the tables above, the unit-rectangle notation `rect` is written using escaped characters. KTU expects $\text{rect}(t/T)$ — a rectangle of width $T$ centered at origin. The convolution of two rectangles yields a **triangle** of base $2T$ and peak $T$.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application |
|:------|:------------|
| **Telecommunications** | Multipath channel modeling; matched filtering |
| **Biomedical Signal Processing** | ECG/EEG de-noising via FIR filters |
| **Image Processing** | 2-D convolution for Gaussian blurring and edge kernels |
| **Speech Processing** | Modeling vocal tract as convolution (source $\ast$ filter) |
| **Control Systems** | Determining step response from impulse response |
| **Geophysics** | Seismic signal deconvolution for subsurface imaging |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Output of an LTI System as a Convolution

**Starting Point:** Any arbitrary continuous-time signal $x(t)$ can be approximated as a sum of scaled, shifted impulses.

**Step 1 — Approximate $x(t)$ as a sum of rectangular pulses:**

$$
x(t) \approx \sum_{k=-\infty}^{+\infty} x(k\Delta\tau)\, \text{rect}_\Delta(t - k\Delta\tau)
$$

where $\text{rect}_\Delta(t)$ is a narrow rectangle of width $\Delta\tau$ and unit area, and $x(k\Delta\tau)$ is the height of the $k^{th}$ rectangle.

**Step 2 — Apply LTI system to each pulse:**

Let the system's response to $\delta(t)$ (impulse) be $h(t)$. By the **scaling property** of LTI systems, the response to a rectangle of area $x(k\Delta\tau)\Delta\tau$ is:

$$
x(k\Delta\tau)\Delta\tau \cdot h(t - k\Delta\tau)
$$

**Step 3 — Superpose all responses (by linearity):**

$$
y(t) \approx \sum_{k=-\infty}^{+\infty} x(k\Delta\tau)\, h(t - k\Delta\tau)\, \Delta\tau
$$

**Step 4 — Take the limit as $\Delta\tau \rightarrow 0$:**

The summation becomes a Riemann integral:

$$
\begin{aligned}
y(t) &= \lim_{\Delta\tau \to 0} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{+\infty} x(k\Delta\tau)\, h(t - k\Delta\tau)\, \Delta\tau \\[6pt]
y(t) &= \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(\tau)\, h(t - \tau)\, d\tau \\[6pt]
\therefore\ y(t) &= x(t) * h(t) \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Worked Example #1: Convolution of Two Rectangular Pulses (CT)

**Problem:** Compute $y(t) = x(t) * h(t)$ where
$x(t) = \text{rect}(t/2)$ (rectangle of width 2, from $t = -1$ to $t = 1$)
and $h(t) = \text{rect}(t/2)$ (same).

**Step 1 — Write the convolution integral:**

$$
y(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(\tau)\, h(t - \tau)\, d\tau
$$

**Step 2 — Identify non-zero regions:**

$x(\tau) \neq 0$ for $-1 \leq \tau \leq 1$
$h(t - \tau) \neq 0$ for $-1 \leq t - \tau \leq 1 \implies (t-1) \leq \tau \leq (t+1)$

**Step 3 — Find overlap interval as $t$ varies:**

Overlap exists when $\max(-1, t-1) \leq \tau \leq \min(1, t+1)$.

**Case 1:** $t < -2$. No overlap. $\quad y(t) = 0$

**Case 2:** $-2 \leq t \leq 0$. Lower limit $= -1$, upper limit $= t+1$. Length of overlap $= t + 2$.

$$
y(t) = \int_{-1}^{t+1} 1\, d\tau = (t+1) - (-1) = t + 2
$$

**Case 3:** $0 \leq t \leq 2$. Lower limit $= t-1$, upper limit $= 1$. Length of overlap $= 2 - t$.

$$
y(t) = \int_{t-1}^{1} 1\, d\tau = 1 - (t-1) = 2 - t
$$

**Case 4:** $t > 2$. No overlap. $\quad y(t) = 0$

**Step 4 — Final result (triangular pulse):**

$$
\boxed{\,y(t) = \begin{cases} t + 2, & -2 \leq t \leq 0 \\ 2 - t, & 0 \leq t \leq 2 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}\,}
$$

**Sanity Check:** Peak value at $t = 0$ is $y(0) = 2$, base width is $4$. This is a **triangle** centered at origin — consistent with the rule that rect $\ast$ rect = triangle.

## 3.3 Worked Example #2: Discrete Convolution (FIR Filter Style)

**Problem:** Compute $y[n] = x[n] * h[n]$ where
$x[n] = \{1,\ 2,\ 3\} \quad (x[0]=1,\ x[1]=2,\ x[2]=3)$
$h[n] = \{1,\ 1,\ 1\} \quad (h[0]=1,\ h[1]=1,\ h[2]=1)$
(arrows indicate $n = 0$.)

**Step 1 — Use convolution sum formula:**

$$
y[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{2} x[k]\, h[n - k]
$$

**Step 2 — Output length:** $N_y = N_x + N_h - 1 = 3 + 3 - 1 = 5$ values: $n = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4$.

**Step 3 — Compute each value:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
y[0] &= x[0]\,h[0] = (1)(1) = 1 \\[4pt]
y[1] &= x[0]\,h[1] + x[1]\,h[0] = (1)(1) + (2)(1) = 3 \\[4pt]
y[2] &= x[0]\,h[2] + x[1]\,h[1] + x[2]\,h[0] = (1)(1) + (2)(1) + (3)(1) = 6 \\[4pt]
y[3] &= x[1]\,h[2] + x[2]\,h[1] = (2)(1) + (3)(1) = 5 \\[4pt]
y[4] &= x[2]\,h[2] = (3)(1) = 3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Final result:**

$$
\boxed{\,y[n] = \{1,\ 3,\ 6,\ 5,\ 3\}\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification via Tabular Method (preferred in KTU exams):**

| $x[k] \backslash h[n-k]$ | $h[0]=1$ | $h[1]=1$ | $h[2]=1$ | Row Sum = $y[n]$ |
|:------------------------:|:--------:|:--------:|:--------:|:----------------:|
| $x[0]=1$ | **1** | 1 | 1 | 1 |
| $x[1]=2$ | 2 | **2** | 2 | 3 |
| $x[2]=3$ | 3 | 3 | **3** | 6 |
| Col 2 sum | — | — | — | $y[2]=6$ etc. |

## 3.4 Worked Example #3: Convolution of Causal Exponential with Unit Step

**Problem:** Find $y(t) = e^{-at}u(t) * u(t)$.

**Step 1 — Write integral:**

$$
y(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} e^{-a\tau}u(\tau)\, u(t - \tau)\, d\tau
$$

**Step 2 — Apply unit step constraints:**

$u(\tau) = 1$ for $\tau \geq 0$, and $u(t-\tau) = 1$ for $\tau \leq t$.

**Step 3 — Determine limits:**

- If $t < 0$: No value of $\tau$ satisfies both. $\quad y(t) = 0$
- If $t \geq 0$: Integration limits are $0 \leq \tau \leq t$.

**Step 4 — Evaluate:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
y(t) &= \int_{0}^{t} e^{-a\tau}\, d\tau \\[6pt]
     &= \left[ \frac{e^{-a\tau}}{-a} \right]_{0}^{t} \\[6pt]
     &= \frac{1}{-a}\left(e^{-at} - e^{0}\right) \\[6pt]
     &= \frac{1 - e^{-at}}{a} \quad \text{for } t \geq 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Final causal answer:**

$$
\boxed{\,y(t) = \frac{1 - e^{-at}}{a}\, u(t)\,}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Convolution Process Flow (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

This Mermaid diagram maps the exact algorithmic flow that KTU expects when solving a convolution problem using the graphical method.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Input Signal x(t)"] --> B["Define dummy variable tau via t to tau"]
    C["Impulse Response h(t)"] --> D["Step 1 FOLD - Replace t with minus tau to get h of minus tau"]
    B --> E["Step 2 SHIFT - Slide h by amount t0 to get h of t0 minus tau"]
    D --> E
    E --> F["Step 3 MULTIPLY - Pointwise product x of tau times h of t0 minus tau"]
    F --> G["Step 4 INTEGRATE - Compute area under the product curve"]
    G --> H{"Is product nonzero over any interval?"}
    H -->|"Yes"| I["Output value y of t0 equals integral of product over overlap region"]
    H -->|"No overlap"| J["Output value y of t0 equals zero"]
    I --> K["Slide t0 continuously from minus infinity to plus infinity"]
    J --> K
    K --> L["Final Output Signal y of t"]
    L --> M["Plot y of t versus t - triangular or piecewise shape"]
```

## 4.2 LTI System Block Diagram — Convolution as the System Operator

This block diagram clarifies *why* convolution is the central operation in LTI systems.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Input x of t"] -->|"x of t"| B["LTI SYSTEM with Impulse Response h of t"]
    B -->|"x of t asterisk h of t"| C["Output y of t"]
    D["Impulse delta of t"] -->|"delta of t"| B
    B -->|"h of t"| E["Impulse Response"]
    F["Step Function u of t"] -->|"u of t"| B
    B -->|"u of t asterisk h of t equals s of t"| G["Step Response s of t"]
```

## 4.3 Comparison: Continuous vs. Discrete Convolution Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph CT["Continuous Time Domain"]
        A1["x of t - continuous signal"] --> A2["Integral from minus inf to plus inf"]
        A3["h of t - continuous impulse response"] --> A2
        A2 --> A4["y of t - continuous output"]
    end
    subgraph DT["Discrete Time Domain"]
        B1["x of n - discrete samples"] --> B2["Summation from k equals minus inf to plus inf"]
        B3["h of n - discrete impulse response"] --> B2
        B2 --> B4["y of n - discrete output sequence"]
    end
```

## 4.4 Graphical Sliding-Window Interpretation (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["x of tau - fixed stationary signal on tau axis"] --> C
    B["h of minus tau - folded copy of h"] --> D["Slide h of minus tau by amount t0 along tau axis"]
    D --> C["Compute overlap with x of tau"]
    C --> E["y of t0 equals area of intersection at position t0"]
    E --> F["Repeat for all t0 to trace complete output curve"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Tip for KTU Students:** The four-step procedure (Fold → Shift → Multiply → Integrate) maps **1-to-1** onto the Mermaid flow above. In your exam answer, draw the four sketches (step 1 to step 4) sequentially and label each region's overlap limits. Examiners award full marks only when **all four sketches are shown**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define convolution integral for two continuous-time signals. State and prove the commutative property of convolution.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (1 Mark):** The convolution of $x(t)$ and $h(t)$ is
>
> $$y(t) = x(t) * h(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(\tau)\, h(t - \tau)\, d\tau$$
>
> **Commutative Property (1 Mark):** $x(t) * h(t) = h(t) * x(t)$.
>
> **Proof (1 Mark):**
>
> $$x(t) * h(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(\tau)\, h(t - \tau)\, d\tau$$
>
> Substitute $u = t - \tau \implies \tau = t - u,\ d\tau = -du$. Limits flip:
>
> $$\begin{aligned} x(t) * h(t) &= \int_{+\infty}^{-\infty} x(t - u)\, h(u)\, (-du) \\ &= \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} h(u)\, x(t - u)\, du \\ &= h(t) * x(t) \quad \blacksquare \end{aligned}$$

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Check whether the system $y(t) = x(t) * h(t)$ is linear and time-invariant.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Linearity (1.5 Marks):** Let $x_1(t) \to y_1(t) = x_1 * h$ and $x_2(t) \to y_2(t) = x_2 * h$. For input $x_3(t) = a\,x_1(t) + b\,x_2(t)$:
>
> $$y_3(t) = (a x_1 + b x_2) * h = a(x_1 * h) + b(x_2 * h) = a\, y_1(t) + b\, y_2(t)$$
>
> So the system is **linear**.
>
> **Time-Invariance (1.5 Marks):** Let $x_1(t) = x(t - t_0)$. Then
>
> $$y_1(t) = x(t - t_0) * h(t) = \int x(\tau - t_0)\, h(t - \tau)\, d\tau = y(t - t_0)$$
>
> So the system is **time-invariant**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module 2 — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part-B question in Module 2 carries **14 marks**, split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Students must answer **either OR**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]`

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**
Find the convolution $y(t) = x(t) * h(t)$ where
$x(t) = u(t) - u(t - 3)$ (rectangular pulse of width 3, height 1) and
$h(t) = e^{-2t}\, u(t)$.

**Model Solution:**

> Write the convolution integral:
>
> $$y(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(\tau)\, h(t - \tau)\, d\tau = \int_{0}^{3} 1 \cdot e^{-2(t - \tau)}\, u(t - \tau)\, d\tau$$
>
> **Case 1: $t < 0$.** Since $\tau \geq 0$, the integrand has $u(t - \tau) = 0$. So $y(t) = 0$.
>
> **Case 2: $0 \leq t \leq 3$.** Here $\tau \leq t \leq 3$ is allowed. Limits: $0 \leq \tau \leq t$.
>
> $$y(t) = \int_{0}^{t} e^{-2(t - \tau)}\, d\tau = e^{-2t}\int_{0}^{t} e^{2\tau}\, d\tau = e^{-2t} \cdot \frac{e^{2t} - 1}{2} = \frac{1 - e^{-2t}}{2}$$
>
> **Case 3: $t > 3$.** Full pulse is within the sliding window. Limits: $0 \leq \tau \leq 3$.
>
> $$y(t) = \int_{0}^{3} e^{-2(t - \tau)}\, d\tau = e^{-2t}\cdot \frac{e^{6} - 1}{2} = \frac{1 - e^{-6}}{2}\, e^{-2(t-3)}$$
>
> **Final Result:**
>
> $$\boxed{\,y(t) = \begin{cases} 0, & t < 0 \\[4pt] \dfrac{1 - e^{-2t}}{2}, & 0 \leq t \leq 3 \\[8pt] \dfrac{1 - e^{-6}}{2}\, e^{-2(t-3)}, & t > 3 \end{cases}\,}$$

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**
State and prove the **associative property** of convolution. Show that the cascade of two LTI systems is equivalent to a single LTI system whose impulse response is the convolution of the individual impulse responses.

**Model Solution:**

> **Statement (1 Mark):** $x(t) * [h_1(t) * h_2(t)] = [x(t) * h_1(t)] * h_2(t)$
>
> **Proof (4 Marks):** Let $g(t) = h_1(t) * h_2(t)$. Then
>
> $$x(t) * g(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(\tau)\, g(t - \tau)\, d\tau = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(\tau)\left[\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} h_1(\lambda)\, h_2(t - \tau - \lambda)\, d\lambda\right] d\tau$$
>
> Swap integrals (Fubini's theorem, valid for causal LTI systems):
>
> $$= \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} h_1(\lambda)\left[\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(\tau)\, h_2((t-\lambda) - \tau)\, d\tau\right] d\lambda$$
>
> Inner integral is $x(t - \lambda) * h_2(t - \lambda)$ — this is a shifted version of $x * h_2$. So:
>
> $$\begin{aligned} &= \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} h_1(\lambda)\,[x * h_2](t - \lambda)\, d\lambda \\ &= h_1(t) * [x * h_2](t) = [x * h_1] * h_2 \quad \blacksquare \end{aligned}$$
>
> **Cascade Interpretation (2 Marks):** Two LTI systems with impulse responses $h_1(t)$ and $h_2(t)$ in cascade produce an output
>
> $$y(t) = [x(t) * h_1(t)] * h_2(t) = x(t) * [h_1(t) * h_2(t)]$$
>
> Hence the cascade is equivalent to a single LTI system with impulse response $h(t) = h_1(t) * h_2(t)$. **Order does not matter.**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**
Compute the convolution $y[n] = x[n] * h[n]$ for
$x[n] = \{1, 2, 1\}$ (with $x[0]=1$) and
$h[n] = \{1, 1, 1, 1\}$ (with $h[0]=1$). Plot $y[n]$.

**Model Solution:**

> **Output length:** $N_y = 3 + 4 - 1 = 6$, so $n = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5$.
>
> **Apply the discrete convolution sum:**
>
> $$\begin{aligned} y[0] &= x[0]\,h[0] = 1 \cdot 1 = 1 \\ y[1] &= x[0]\,h[1] + x[1]\,h[0] = 1 + 2 = 3 \\ y[2] &= x[0]\,h[2] + x[1]\,h[1] + x[2]\,h[0] = 1 + 2 + 1 = 4 \\ y[3] &= x[0]\,h[3] + x[1]\,h[2] + x[2]\,h[1] = 1 + 2 + 1 = 4 \\ y[4] &= x[1]\,h[3] + x[2]\,h[2] = 2 + 1 = 3 \\ y[5] &= x[2]\,h[3] = 1 \cdot 1 = 1 \end{aligned}$$
>
> **Final Result:**
>
> $$\boxed{\,y[n] = \{1, 3, 4, 4, 3, 1\}\,}$$
>
> **Shape:** Discrete triangle — confirms that convolution of two rectangular sequences produces a triangular sequence.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**
The impulse response of a causal LTI system is $h(t) = e^{-t} u(t)$. Find the output $y(t)$ for input $x(t) = e^{-2t} u(t)$ using convolution. Hence, find the value of $y(1)$.

**Model Solution:**

> **Convolution integral:**
>
> $$y(t) = \int_{0}^{t} e^{-2\tau}\, e^{-(t - \tau)}\, d\tau = e^{-t}\int_{0}^{t} e^{-\tau}\, d\tau$$
>
> **Evaluate:**
>
> $$\begin{aligned} y(t) &= e^{-t}\left[-e^{-\tau}\right]_{0}^{t} = e^{-t}(1 - e^{-t}) \\ &= e^{-t} - e^{-2t} \quad \text{for } t \geq 0 \end{aligned}$$
>
> **Final Result:**
>
> $$\boxed{\,y(t) = \left(e^{-t} - e^{-2t}\right) u(t)\,}$$
>
> **At $t = 1$:**
>
> $$y(1) = e^{-1} - e^{-2} = 0.3679 - 0.1353 = 0.2326$$

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE MARKS in convolution problems:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the FOLD step (–1 to –2 marks):** Many students directly shift $h(t)$ without flipping it first. Always write $h(-\tau)$ explicitly before substituting the shift.
>
> 2. **Incorrect overlap region (–2 marks):** When the limits of integration are written, students often forget to update them as $t$ changes. You **must** split the answer into **cases** based on the value of $t$.
>
> 3. **Missing the unit step multiplier in final answer (–1 mark):** The final causal answer must always be multiplied by $u(t)$. Writing $y(t) = (1 - e^{-2t})/2$ **without** $u(t)$ is incomplete.
>
> 4. **Confusing $*$ with regular multiplication:** The symbol $*$ means convolution, NOT multiplication. Examiners WILL deduct a mark if it is used ambiguously.
>
> 5. **Not labeling sketches in graphical convolution:** KTU examiners expect **four** labeled diagrams — one for each step. Skipping sketches = –2 marks minimum.
>
> 6. **Discrete output length errors:** Forgetting that $N_y = N_x + N_h - 1$ leads to off-by-one errors in $y[n]$ indexing.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 2: Convolution**
>
> - **Definition:** $y(t) = \int x(\tau)h(t-\tau)d\tau$ (CT); $y[n] = \sum x[k]h[n-k]$ (DT).
> - **Four-step graphical procedure:** Fold → Shift → Multiply → Integrate (**F-S-M-I**).
> - **Output length for DT:** $N_y = N_x + N_h - 1$.
> - **Identity element:** Convolution with $\delta(t)$ or $\delta[n]$ returns the original signal.
> - **Commutative, Associative, Distributive** properties are **all** valid for convolution.
> - **LTI System Output:** $y(t) = x(t) * h(t)$ — this is the **defining equation** of LTI behavior.
> - **Causality Condition:** $h(t) = 0$ for $t < 0$ in continuous-time, $h[n] = 0$ for $n < 0$ in discrete-time.
> - **rect $\ast$ rect = triangle** (peak = $T$, base = $2T$).
> - **$u(t) \ast u(t) = t\,u(t) = r(t)$** — ramp function.
> - **$e^{-at}u(t) \ast u(t) = \dfrac{1-e^{-at}}{a}\,u(t)$** — a heavily tested identity.
> - **Convolution in cascade:** $h_{eq}(t) = h_1(t) * h_2(t)$; order is irrelevant.
> - **Convolution in parallel:** $h_{eq}(t) = h_1(t) + h_2(t)$.
> - **Always multiply the final causal result by $u(t)$.**
> - **Always split the answer into cases** based on the time variable $t$.
> - **KTU's most-tested convolution question:** graphical method for two simple exponentials or two rectangles.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Multiplication
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST416 - SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Multiplication

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of **Signals and Systems**, **Multiplication** (also called **point-wise product** or **modulation** in a broader sense) is a fundamental pointwise operation performed between two continuous-time or discrete-time signals. Given two signals $x_1(t)$ and $x_2(t)$ defined over the same time domain, their product signal is denoted as $y(t) = x_1(t) \cdot x_2(t)$, where the value of the output at every instant $t$ is the algebraic product of the values of the two input signals at that same instant.

For discrete-time signals, the operation is defined as:
$$y[n] = x_1[n] \cdot x_2[n]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECST416 – Module 2):** Multiplication of signals in the time domain is the dual of **Convolution in the frequency domain**. This duality, known as the **Multiplication Theorem** (or **Modulation Theorem**), is a high-weightage topic for both Continuous-Time and Discrete-Time Fourier Transform problems.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have **two transparent slides** of a graph placed exactly one on top of the other. The first slide has the waveform of $x_1(t)$ drawn on it, and the second has the waveform of $x_2(t)$. If a beam of light is allowed to pass through the overlap region, the **brightness (intensity) of the emerging beam at every point is proportional to the product** of the opacity (amplitude) of the two slides at that point.

In simpler terms:
- Where **both signals are non-zero** → product is non-zero.
- Where **even one signal is zero** → product is zero.
- Negative × Negative = Positive (signal gets inverted where overlaps occur with sign changes).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** Time-domain multiplication is essentially a **gating** or **windowing** operation — it lets one signal pass through only when another signal allows it. This is the foundational principle behind **Amplitude Modulation (AM)**, **Sampling Theorem**, and **Windowing in DSP**.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- The continuous-time product signal is measured in the **product of the units of the two parent signals** (e.g., Volts × Volts = V² when both are voltage signals).
- The fundamental time constant remains the support interval of the parent signals.
- **Standard metric used in KTU problems:** product of amplitudes, energy, and the resulting spectrum's bandwidth.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown

The point-wise multiplication of two signals can be broken down into a structured logical flow:

1. **Domain Alignment**: Both signals must be defined over the **same domain** (continuous-time $t$ or discrete-time $n$).
2. **Sample-wise Pairing**: For every time instant (or sample index), pair the corresponding values.
3. **Algebraic Multiplication**: Multiply the paired values using standard scalar multiplication rules (including sign handling).
4. **Result Construction**: The new signal $y(t)$ (or $y[n]$) has the same time support as the inputs, but the amplitude is the product.

### 2.2 The Multiplication Theorem (Modulation Theorem)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **most important concept** related to multiplication in the KTU Signals and Systems syllabus.

The **Multiplication Theorem** states that **multiplication in the time domain corresponds to convolution (scaled by $\frac{1}{2\pi}$) in the frequency domain**, and vice-versa.

**Continuous-Time Form (CTFT):**

$$\mathcal{F}\{x(t) \cdot y(t)\} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \big[ X(j\omega) * Y(j\omega) \big]$$

**Discrete-Time Form (DTFT):**

$$X(z) \cdot Y(z) \quad \xrightarrow{\text{Inverse Z}} \quad \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x[k] \, y[n-k]$$

For DTFT specifically:
$$X(e^{j\omega}) \cdot Y(e^{j\omega}) \quad \longleftrightarrow \quad \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} X(e^{j\theta}) \, Y(e^{j(\omega - \theta)}) \, d\theta$$

### 2.3 Algebraic Properties of Signal Multiplication

The point-wise product of two signals exhibits the following algebraic properties:

- **Commutative:** $x(t) \cdot y(t) = y(t) \cdot x(t)$
- **Associative:** $[x(t) \cdot y(t)] \cdot z(t) = x(t) \cdot [y(t) \cdot z(t)]$
- **Distributive over Addition:** $x(t) \cdot [y(t) + z(t)] = x(t)\cdot y(t) + x(t)\cdot z(t)$
- **Identity Element:** Multiplication by a constant (DC signal $c$) scales the signal: $c \cdot x(t) = c\,x(t)$.
- **Nullifying Element:** Multiplication by zero yields the zero signal: $0 \cdot x(t) = 0$.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Mathematical Expression** | **Description** |
|---|---|---|
| CT Multiplication | $y(t) = x_1(t) \cdot x_2(t)$ | Point-wise product (continuous-time) |
| DT Multiplication | $y[n] = x_1[n] \cdot x_2[n]$ | Point-wise product (discrete-time) |
| CT Multiplication Theorem | $X(j\omega) * Y(j\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} X(j\tau) Y(j(\omega - \tau)) d\tau$ | Convolution integral in frequency |
| DTFT Product Property | $\mathcal{F}\{x[n] y[n]\} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} X(e^{j\theta}) Y(e^{j(\omega - \theta)}) d\theta$ | Periodic convolution |
| AM Modulation (Standard) | $y(t) = m(t) \cos(\omega_c t)$ | Carrier modulated by message $m(t)$ |
| Energy of Product | $E_y = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x_1(t) x_2(t) \vert^{2} dt$ | Squared magnitude product integrated |
| Power of Product | $P_y = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{T} \vert x_1(t) x_2(t) \vert^{2} dt$ | Time-averaged power |
| Bandwidth (AM DSB-SC) | $B_{y} = 2 B_{m}$ | Double the message bandwidth |

> [!NOTE]
> In the formula table above, the **vertical bar for absolute value** has been deliberately replaced by `\vert` (in LaTeX rendering) or the word "magnitude" to prevent breaking the markdown table structure — this is a KTU-engine formatting safeguard.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Amplitude Modulation (AM):** Radio transmission — message signal multiplied with a high-frequency carrier.
- **Sampling Theorem:** Continuous signal multiplied by an impulse train to obtain discrete samples.
- **Windowing in DSP:** Truncating an infinite-duration signal by multiplying with a finite-duration window (Rectangular, Hamming, Hanning).
- **Mixers in Communication:** Used to shift signals between frequency bands in superheterodyne receivers.
- **Image Processing:** Point-wise multiplication of two images for masking, blending, or filtering.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Continuous-Time Multiplication Theorem

**Statement:** If $x(t) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} X(j\omega)$ and $y(t) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} Y(j\omega)$, then

$$\mathcal{F}\{x(t) \cdot y(t)\} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \big[ X(j\omega) * Y(j\omega) \big]$$

**Proof (Line-by-Line Exhaustive Derivation):**

**Step 1:** Begin with the definition of the Continuous-Time Fourier Transform (CTFT) of the product signal $p(t) = x(t) \cdot y(t)$:

$$P(j\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) \, y(t) \, e^{-j\omega t} \, dt$$

**Step 2:** Apply the **Inverse Fourier Transform** to $y(t)$ to express it in terms of its spectrum $Y(j\omega)$:

$$y(t) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} Y(j\tau) \, e^{j\tau t} \, d\tau$$

**Step 3:** Substitute Step 2 into the expression in Step 1:

$$P(j\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) \left[ \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} Y(j\tau) \, e^{j\tau t} \, d\tau \right] e^{-j\omega t} \, dt$$

**Step 4:** Interchange the order of integration (Fubini's Theorem, valid as signals are absolutely integrable):

$$P(j\omega) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} Y(j\tau) \left[ \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) \, e^{-j(\omega - \tau) t} \, dt \right] d\tau$$

**Step 5:** Recognize the inner integral as the Fourier Transform of $x(t)$ evaluated at frequency $(\omega - \tau)$:

$$P(j\omega) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} Y(j\tau) \, X(j(\omega - \tau)) \, d\tau$$

**Step 6:** Recognize the right-hand side as the convolution integral between $X(j\omega)$ and $Y(j\omega)$ scaled by $\frac{1}{2\pi}$:

$$P(j\omega) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \big[ X(j\omega) * Y(j\omega) \big]$$

**Step 7 (Conclusion):** Hence proved. Time-domain multiplication maps to frequency-domain convolution scaled by $\frac{1}{2\pi}$.

$$\boxed{\mathcal{F}\{x(t) \cdot y(t)\} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \big[ X(j\omega) * Y(j\omega) \big]}$$

---

### 3.2 Analytical Worked Example — Standard AM Signal

**Problem:** Let the message signal be $m(t) = A_m \cos(\omega_m t)$ and the carrier be $c(t) = A_c \cos(\omega_c t)$. Find the Fourier Transform of the AM signal $y(t) = m(t) \cdot c(t)$.

**Solution (Exhaustive Step-by-Step):**

**Step 1:** Write down the product signal:

$$y(t) = A_m A_c \cos(\omega_m t) \cos(\omega_c t)$$

**Step 2:** Apply the trigonometric product-to-sum identity:

$$y(t) = \frac{A_m A_c}{2} \big[ \cos((\omega_c - \omega_m)t) + \cos((\omega_c + \omega_m)t) \big]$$

> Reasoning: Using $\cos A \cos B = \frac{1}{2}[\cos(A-B) + \cos(A+B)]$.

**Step 3:** Take the Fourier Transform of each cosine term using the standard CTFT pair $\cos(\omega_0 t) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} \pi[\delta(\omega - \omega_0) + \delta(\omega + \omega_0)]$:

\begin{aligned}
Y(j\omega) &= \frac{A_m A_c}{2} \cdot \pi \big[ \delta(\omega - (\omega_c - \omega_m)) + \delta(\omega + (\omega_c - \omega_m)) \big] \\
&\quad + \frac{A_m A_c}{2} \cdot \pi \big[ \delta(\omega - (\omega_c + \omega_m)) + \delta(\omega + (\omega_c + \omega_m)) \big]
\end{aligned}

**Step 4 (Final Simplified Result):**

$$Y(j\omega) = \frac{\pi A_m A_c}{2} \big[ \delta(\omega - \omega_c + \omega_m) + \delta(\omega + \omega_c - \omega_m) + \delta(\omega - \omega_c - \omega_m) + \delta(\omega + \omega_c + \omega_m) \big]$$

> **Interpretation:** The product of two cosines in time creates **two pairs of impulses** in the frequency domain, centered at $\pm \omega_c$ and separated by $\pm \omega_m$. This directly demonstrates the Multiplication Theorem in action.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Worked Example — Rectangular Windowing

**Problem:** A signal $x(t) = \cos(100\pi t)$ is multiplied by a rectangular pulse $w(t) = 1$ for $0 \le t \le 1$ and $0$ elsewhere. Sketch the resulting product signal.

**Solution (Step-by-Step):**

**Step 1:** Identify the support of $w(t)$: $[0, 1]$.

**Step 2:** Apply the product rule:

$$y(t) = x(t) \cdot w(t) = \begin{cases} \cos(100\pi t), & 0 \le t \le 1 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Step 3:** Count the cycles in $[0, 1]$: Frequency = $50$ Hz, Period = $0.02$ s, so number of cycles = $1/0.02 = 50$ cycles.

> **Result:** The product is a finite-duration burst of exactly 50 cosine cycles, otherwise zero everywhere — a textbook **windowed sinusoid**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
