$$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{d\omega}{a^2 + \omega^2} = \frac{\pi}{a}, \quad \text{with } a = 2$$

$$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{d\omega}{4 + \omega^2} = \frac{\pi}{2}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute back:

$$E = \frac{1}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{\pi}{2} = \frac{1}{4} \text{ Joules}$$

**Step 6 (Verification):** Time-domain check:

$$E = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-4t} dt = \left[ \frac{e^{-4t}}{-4} \right]_0^{\infty} = \frac{1}{4} \text{ Joules} \quad \checkmark$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]**

> **(a)** Derive Parseval's Theorem for a continuous-time periodic signal in terms of its Fourier Series coefficients. **\[7 Marks\]**
>
> **(b)** A periodic signal $x(t)$ with period $T = \pi$ has Fourier Series coefficients $a_k = \frac{1}{1 + k^2}$. Using Parseval's Theorem, find the average power of $x(t)$. **\[7 Marks\]**

#### Model Solution to Part (a)

**Step 1:** Represent $x(t)$ using its exponential Fourier Series:

$$x(t) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} a_k \, e^{jk\omega_0 t}, \quad \omega_0 = \frac{2\pi}{T}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the average power over one period:

$$P = \frac{1}{T} \int_{T} \vert x(t)\vert^2 \, dt = \frac{1}{T} \int_{T} x(t)\, x^*(t) \, dt$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the FS expansion for both $x(t)$ and $x^*(t)$:

$$P = \frac{1}{T} \int_{T} \left[ \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} a_k \, e^{jk\omega_0 t} \right] \left[ \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} a_m^* \, e^{-jm\omega_0 t} \right] dt$$

**Step 4:** Exchange summation and integration:

$$P = \frac{1}{T} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} a_k \, a_m^* \int_{T} e^{j(k-m)\omega_0 t} \, dt$$

**Step 5:** Apply the orthogonality principle — the integral is $T$ when $k = m$ and $0$ otherwise:

$$\int_{T} e^{j(k-m)\omega_0 t} \, dt = \begin{cases} T, & k = m \\ 0, & k \neq m \end{cases}$$

**Step 6:** The Kronecker delta collapses the double sum, leaving:

$$P = \frac{1}{T} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} a_k \, a_k^* \cdot T = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert a_k\vert^2$$

$$\boxed{\frac{1}{T} \int_{T} \vert x(t)\vert^2 \, dt = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert a_k\vert^2} \qquad \blacksquare$$

#### Model Solution to Part (b)

**Step 1:** Apply the periodic Parseval's identity directly:

$$P = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert a_k\vert^2 = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{1}{(1 + k^2)^2}$$

**Step 2:** Evaluate the symmetric infinite sum.

Split the sum at $k = 0$:

$$P = \frac{1}{(1+0)^2} + 2 \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{(1+k^2)^2} = 1 + 2 \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{(1+k^2)^2}$$

**Step 3:** Use the known closed-form result for this series:

$$\sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{1}{(1+k^2)^2} = \frac{\pi}{2} \cdot \frac{e^{\pi} + e^{-\pi}}{e^{\pi} - e^{-\pi}} + 1 = \frac{\pi}{2} \coth(\pi) + 1$$

(Equivalent forms include $\frac{\pi \coth \pi}{2} + 1$, which is derived from the residue theorem in complex analysis.)

**Step 4:** Numerical evaluation:

$$\coth(\pi) = \frac{e^{\pi} + e^{-\pi}}{e^{\pi} - e^{-\pi}} \approx \frac{23.1407 + 0.0432}{23.1407 - 0.0432} \approx 1.0037$$

$$P \approx \frac{\pi \times 1.0037}{2} + 1 \approx 1.5766 + 1 = 2.5766 \text{ Watts}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> * **Omitting the $\frac{1}{2\pi}$ factor:** Students frequently forget the normalization constant in the aperiodic Parseval's identity, leading to answers off by a factor of $2\pi$. This is the **single most common error** and costs full marks.
> * **Confusing energy and power forms:** Mixing up the aperiodic (energy) and periodic (power) versions is a classic blunder. Always check: is the signal **aperiodic** (use FT and energy) or **periodic** (use FS and power)?
> * **Forgetting the complex conjugate:** Writing $X(j\omega)^2$ instead of $\vert X(j\omega)\vert^2 = X(j\omega) X^*(j\omega)$. The squared magnitude is essential.
> * **Incorrect integration limits in DTFT form:** The DTFT Parseval's identity integrates over one period $[-\pi, \pi]$, not $(-\infty, \infty)$.
> * **Not checking the time-domain verification:** KTU examiners award bonus marks when students verify their answer by independently computing energy/power in the other domain.
> * **Failing to apply Fubini's Theorem justification:** In derivations, briefly note that interchange of integrals is valid because the signal has finite energy (absolute convergence).

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

* **Core Identity (CT Aperiodic):** $\int \vert x(t)\vert^2 dt = \frac{1}{2\pi}\int \vert X(j\omega)\vert^2 d\omega$ — always remember the $\frac{1}{2\pi}$ factor.
* **Core Identity (DT Aperiodic):** $\sum \vert x[n]\vert^2 = \frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^{\pi} \vert X(e^{j\omega})\vert^2 d\omega$ — integration is over one DTFT period.
* **Core Identity (CT Periodic):** $\frac{1}{T}\int_T \vert x(t)\vert^2 dt = \sum \vert a_k\vert^2$ — conserves power, not energy.
* **Core Identity (DT Periodic):** $\frac{1}{N}\sum_N \vert x[n]\vert^2 = \sum_N \vert a_k\vert^2$ — average power per sample.
* **Physical Meaning:** Energy/Power conservation between time and frequency domains — the foundation of spectral analysis.
* **Key Derivation Tool:** Substituting the IFT into the energy/power integral and exploiting orthogonality of complex exponentials.
* **Key Identity Used:** $X^*(j\omega) = \int x^*(t) e^{j\omega t} dt$ (complex conjugate of forward FT).
* **Standard Integral:** $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{d\omega}{a^2 + \omega^2} = \frac{\pi}{a}$ — appears frequently in problems.
* **Energy Spectral Density:** $E_{xd}(\omega) = \vert X(j\omega)\vert^2$ — energy per unit bandwidth.
* **Bandwidth Energy Calculation:** Fractional energy in $[\omega_1, \omega_2]$ is $\frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{\omega_1}^{\omega_2} \vert X(j\omega)\vert^2 d\omega$.
* **Engineering Applications:** Filter design, SNR computation, bandwidth allocation, spectrum analysis, modulation/demodulation.
* **Exam Tip:** Always verify Parseval's identity by computing energy in *both* domains — this guarantees full marks and confirms the answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Even/Odd Symmetry
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST416 - SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Even/Odd Symmetry

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU Definition

In the study of continuous-time (CT) and discrete-time (DT) signals, **symmetry** is a classification property based on how the signal behaves when the independent variable (time `t` or sample index `n`) is reflected about the origin (`0`). KTU 2024 scheme strictly categorizes symmetry into three types: **Even Symmetry**, **Odd Symmetry**, and **Neither** (general case with no symmetry).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition — Even Signal**
> A signal $x(t)$ (or $x[n]$) is called an **even signal** if and only if it satisfies the condition:
> $$x(-t) = x(t) \quad \text{(for all } t \in \mathbb{R}\text{)}$$
> $$x[-n] = x[n] \quad \text{(for all } n \in \mathbb{Z}\text{)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition — Odd Signal**
> A signal $x(t)$ (or $x[n]$) is called an **odd signal** if and only if it satisfies the condition:
> $$x(-t) = -x(t) \quad \text{(for all } t \in \mathbb{R}\text{)}$$
> $$x[-n] = -x[n] \quad \text{(for all } n \in \mathbb{Z}\text{)}$$

**Mandatory properties derived directly from these definitions:**
- For every **odd signal**: $x(0) = 0$ (continuous-time) and $x[0] = 0$ (discrete-time).
- For every **odd signal**: the area under the curve from $-\infty$ to $+\infty$ is **exactly zero** (assuming the signal is integrable in the Riemann sense).
- An **even signal** is always symmetric about the vertical axis (the y-axis).

### 1.2 Real-World Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are standing in front of a tall, still lake at sunset:

- **Even Signal Analogy — The Mountain Reflection**: Look at a perfectly symmetrical mountain reflected in a calm lake. The left half of the mountain is an **exact mirror image** of the right half. If you fold the picture along the vertical centerline (the y-axis), both halves overlap perfectly. This is *even symmetry* — the signal looks identical whether you walk forward in time or backward in time.

- **Odd Signal Analogy — The Seesaw**: Now picture a playground seesaw in perfect balance about the pivot (origin). The left arm goes up by exactly the same amount that the right arm goes **down**. If you reflect the seesaw across the y-axis, it gets **upside down** — that is, it becomes the **negative** of its original position. This is *odd symmetry* — anti-symmetric mirror behavior with a sign flip.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this matter in Engineering?** Many physical systems and standard test inputs exhibit these symmetries. For example, $x(t) = \cos(\omega t)$ is even, $x(t) = \sin(\omega t)$ is odd. Recognizing symmetry allows engineers to simplify Fourier analysis, halve computational load, and predict system responses efficiently.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Core Decomposition Theorem

This is the **most heavily tested concept in KTU exams** regarding symmetry. KTU examiners frequently frame this as: *"Any arbitrary signal can be uniquely broken into an even part and an odd part."*

**Step-by-step logic:**
1. Start with any arbitrary signal $x(t)$. We want to express it as $x(t) = x_e(t) + x_o(t)$.
2. Substitute $-t$ into both sides: $x(-t) = x_e(-t) + x_o(-t)$.
3. By definition of even/odd: $x_e(-t) = x_e(t)$ and $x_o(-t) = -x_o(t)$.
4. Therefore: $x(-t) = x_e(t) - x_o(t)$.
5. Now add the two equations: $x(t) + x(-t) = 2 x_e(t)$ → solve for $x_e(t)$.
6. Subtract them: $x(t) - x(-t) = 2 x_o(t)$ → solve for $x_o(t)$.

### 2.2 Product and Sum Algebra Rules

When two signals are multiplied or added, their symmetry follows strict algebraic rules:

| Operation | Resulting Symmetry |
| :--- | :--- |
| Even $\times$ Even | Even |
| Odd $\times$ Odd | Even |
| Even $\times$ Odd | Odd |
| Even $+$ Even | Even |
| Odd $+$ Odd | Odd |
| Even $+$ Odd | Neither (general signal) |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Property | Mathematical Expression | Domain |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Even signal condition | $x(-t) = x(t)$ | CT |
| Odd signal condition | $x(-t) = -x(t)$ | CT |
| Discrete even signal | $x[-n] = x[n]$ | DT |
| Discrete odd signal | $x[-n] = -x[n]$ | DT |
| Even part extraction | $x_e(t) = \tfrac{1}{2}\left[x(t) + x(-t)\right]$ | CT |
| Odd part extraction | $x_o(t) = \tfrac{1}{2}\left[x(t) - x(-t)\right]$ | CT |
| Discrete even part | $x_e[n] = \tfrac{1}{2}\left[x[n] + x[-n]\right]$ | DT |
| Discrete odd part | $x_o[n] = \tfrac{1}{2}\left[x[n] - x[-n]\right]$ | DT |
| Area of odd signal | $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x_o(t)\,dt = 0$ | CT |
| Energy relation | $E_{\text{total}} = E_{\text{even}} + E_{\text{odd}}$ | Both |
| Power relation | $P_{\text{total}} = P_{\text{even}} + P_{\text{odd}}$ | Both |
| Energy of cross term | $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x_e(t) x_o(t)\, dt = 0$ | CT |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility**: In communication systems (like AM/FM modulation), recognizing whether $x(t)\cos(\omega_c t)$ produces an even or odd signal determines whether the resulting spectrum is real and even, or imaginary and odd — directly impacting demodulator design.

### 2.4 Cross-Term Property — The Hidden Gem

When computing energy $E = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x^2(t)\, dt = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} [x_e(t) + x_o(t)]^2\, dt$, the cross-term vanishes because the product of an even and an odd signal is always **odd**, and the area under any odd signal over a symmetric interval is **zero**.

$$E = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x_e^2(t)\, dt + \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x_o^2(t)\, dt + 2\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x_e(t) x_o(t)\, dt$$

The last integral is **always zero**, giving $E = E_{\text{even}} + E_{\text{odd}}$. This is a recurring KTU 14-mark question pattern.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Even-Odd Decomposition Theorem

**Objective**: Prove that any arbitrary signal $x(t)$ can be uniquely expressed as the sum of an even component and an odd component.

**Given**: $x(t)$ is any arbitrary CT signal (no symmetry assumed).

**Assumption**: We assume $x(t) = x_e(t) + x_o(t)$ exists, where $x_e(t)$ is even and $x_o(t)$ is odd.

**Step 1**: Substitute $-t$ into the assumption equation. Because $x_e$ is even and $x_o$ is odd, the function $x(t)$ evaluated at $-t$ becomes:

$$x(-t) = x_e(-t) + x_o(-t)$$

**Step 2**: Apply the symmetry definitions. Even function mirrored is itself: $x_e(-t) = x_e(t)$. Odd function mirrored is its negative: $x_o(-t) = -x_o(t)$.

$$x(-t) = x_e(t) - x_o(t)$$

**Step 3**: We now have a system of two linear equations with two unknowns ($x_e(t)$ and $x_o(t)$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
x(t) &= x_e(t) + x_o(t) \\
x(-t) &= x_e(t) - x_o(t)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4**: Add the two equations. The $x_o(t)$ terms cancel, leaving $x(t) + x(-t) = 2 x_e(t)$.

$$x_e(t) = \frac{x(t) + x(-t)}{2}$$

**Step 5**: Subtract the second equation from the first. The $x_e(t)$ terms cancel, leaving $x(t) - x(-t) = 2 x_o(t)$.

$$x_o(t) = \frac{x(t) - x(-t)}{2}$$

**Step 6 (Verification)**: Confirm $x_e(t)$ is truly even by substituting $-t$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_e(-t) &= \frac{x(-t) + x(-(-t))}{2} \\
&= \frac{x(-t) + x(t)}{2} \\
&= x_e(t) \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 (Verification)**: Confirm $x_o(t)$ is truly odd:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_o(-t) &= \frac{x(-t) - x(-(-t))}{2} \\
&= \frac{x(-t) - x(t)}{2} \\
&= -\left[\frac{x(t) - x(-t)}{2}\right] \\
&= -x_o(t) \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion**: Every signal $x(t)$ has a **unique** even-odd decomposition given by the formulas above. $\blacksquare$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem**: Consider the discrete-time signal $x[n] = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$ with the arrow indicating $n = 0$ at the value **3** (i.e., $x[-2] = 1$, $x[-1] = 2$, $x[0] = 3$, $x[1] = 4$, $x[2] = 5$). Find the even and odd components.

**Step 1**: Construct $x[-n]$ by time-reversing the sequence. The original sequence maps to $n = -2, -1, 0, 1, 2$. Reversing means swapping the endpoints: $x[-2] = 5$, $x[-1] = 4$, $x[0] = 3$, $x[1] = 2$, $x[2] = 1$.

**Step 2**: Compute $x_e[n] = \frac{1}{2}\big(x[n] + x[-n]\big)$ element-wise:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_e[-2] &= \tfrac{1}{2}(1 + 5) = 3 \\
x_e[-1] &= \tfrac{1}{2}(2 + 4) = 3 \\
x_e[0]  &= \tfrac{1}{2}(3 + 3) = 3 \\
x_e[1]  &= \tfrac{1}{2}(4 + 2) = 3 \\
x_e[2]  &= \tfrac{1}{2}(5 + 1) = 3
\end{aligned}
$$

So $x_e[n] = \{3, 3, 3, 3, 3\}$ — a constant DC signal, which is **even** as expected.

**Step 3**: Compute $x_o[n] = \frac{1}{2}\big(x[n] - x[-n]\big)$ element-wise:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_o[-2] &= \tfrac{1}{2}(1 - 5) = -2 \\
x_o[-1] &= \tfrac{1}{2}(2 - 4) = -1 \\
x_o[0]  &= \tfrac{1}{2}(3 - 3) = 0 \\
x_o[1]  &= \tfrac{1}{2}(4 - 2) = 1 \\
x_o[2]  &= \tfrac{1}{2}(5 - 1) = 2
\end{aligned}
$$

So $x_o[n] = \{-2, -1, 0, 1, 2\}$ — anti-symmetric about the origin, confirming it is **odd**.

**Step 4 (Sanity check)**: $x_e[n] + x_o[n] = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$ — matches the original signal exactly. $\blacksquare$

### 3.3 Proof that the Area of an Odd Signal is Zero

**Given**: $x(t)$ is odd, so $x(-t) = -x(t)$.

**Step 1**: Evaluate the integral over a symmetric interval $[-T, T]$:

$$I = \int_{-T}^{T} x(t)\, dt$$

**Step 2**: Substitute $u = -t$, so $du = -dt$. The limits flip: when $t = -T$, $u = T$; when $t = T$, $u = -T$.

$$I = \int_{T}^{-T} x(-u)(-du) = \int_{-T}^{T} x(-u)\, du$$

**Step 3**: Since $x$ is odd, $x(-u) = -x(u)$:

$$I = \int_{-T}^{T} -x(u)\, du = -I$$

**Step 4**: The only way $I = -I$ is if $I = 0$. Therefore:

$$\int_{-T}^{T} x(t)\, dt = 0 \quad \blacksquare$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Signal Symmetry Classification Tree

The following Mermaid flowchart shows how KTU examiners mentally classify any signal based on its behavior under time-reversal ($t \to -t$ or $n \to -n$):

```mermaid
graph TD
    startA["Input Signal x of t"] --> checkA{"Check x of -t"}
    checkA -- "Equals x of t" --> evenA["Even Signal"]
    checkA -- "Equals -x of t" --> oddA["Odd Signal"]
    checkA -- "Neither condition holds" --> generalA["General Signal"]
    evenA --> exA["Example cos of omega t"]
    oddA --> exO["Example sin of omega t"]
    generalA --> decompA["Decompose using formulas"]
    decompA --> partEvenA["Even Part x_e of t"]
    decompA --> partOddA["Odd Part x_o of t"]
    partEvenA --> sumA["x of t equals x_e plus x_o"]
    partOddA --> sumA
    sumA --> checkB{"Verify x_e even and x_o odd"}
    checkB -- "Yes" --> doneA["Decomposition Successful"]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Even-Odd Decomposition Pipeline

This block diagram represents the operational pipeline used in signal processing software and hardware to split any arbitrary signal into its symmetric components:

```mermaid
graph LR
    inputX["x of t original signal"] --> delayLine["Time Reversal Block: Replace t with -t"]
    delayLine --> xNegT["x of -t"]
    inputX --> sumBlockA["Adder Node"]
    xNegT --> sumBlockA
    sumBlockA --> divideTwoA["Divide by 2"]
    divideTwoA --> evenOut["Output: x_e of t even part"]
    inputX --> subtractBlock["Subtractor Node"]
    xNegT --> subtractBlock
    subtractBlock --> divideTwoB["Divide by 2"]
    divideTwoB --> oddOut["Output: x_o of t odd part"]
    evenOut --> recombiner["Signal Recombiner: Add"]
    oddOut --> recombiner
    recombiner --> reconstructed["Reconstructed x of t verification"]
```

### 4.3 Product Algebra Visual Map

```mermaid
graph TD
    propA["Even Multiply Even"] --> resA["Result is Even"]
    propB["Odd Multiply Odd"] --> resA
    propC["Even Multiply Odd"] --> resB["Result is Odd"]
    propD["Odd Multiply Even"] --> resB
    sumA["Even Add Even"] --> resC["Result is Even"]
    sumB["Odd Add Odd"] --> resD["Result is Odd"]
    sumC["Even Add Odd"] --> resE["Result is General Neither"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip**: In all the flowcharts above, observe that the **recombiner** node always reconstructs the original signal exactly. This is the practical embodiment of the mathematical identity $x(t) = x_e(t) + x_o(t)$.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**Q1.** Define an even signal and an odd signal. Give one example of each for continuous-time and discrete-time domains.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
An **even signal** satisfies $x(-t) = x(t)$ (or $x[-n] = x[n]$ in DT). It is symmetric about the vertical axis. Example (CT): $x(t) = \cos(t)$; Example (DT): $x[n] = \delta[n]$ (impulse is its own mirror).

An **odd signal** satisfies $x(-t) = -x(t)$ (or $x[-n] = -x[n]$). It is anti-symmetric. Example (CT): $x(t) = \sin(t)$; Example (DT): $x[n] = -n \cdot u[n]$ shifted properly. The value at origin must be zero: $x(0) = 0$.

---

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]**

**Q2.** State the even-odd decomposition theorem. Write the formulas for extracting even and odd parts of a signal $x(t)$.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The even-odd decomposition theorem states that any arbitrary signal $x(t)$ can be uniquely expressed as the sum of an even part $x_e(t)$ and an odd part $x_o(t)$.

Formulas:
$$x_e(t) = \frac{x(t) + x(-t)}{2} \qquad x_o(t) = \frac{x(t) - x(-t)}{2}$$

The signal then satisfies $x(t) = x_e(t) + x_o(t)$.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A — Even-Odd Decomposition and Energy Analysis** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**Part (a) [7 Marks]**: Consider the continuous-time signal defined as:
$$x(t) = \begin{cases} t+1, & -1 \le t \le 0 \\ -t+1, & 0 < t \le 1 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

Determine the even and odd parts of $x(t)$ and sketch all three waveforms.

**Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1**: Find $x(-t)$. Substitute $-t$ everywhere:

For $-1 \le -t \le 0$ (i.e., $0 \le t \le 1$): $x(-t) = (-t) + 1 = 1 - t$.
For $0 < -t \le 1$ (i.e., $-1 \le t < 0$): $x(-t) = -(-t) + 1 = t + 1$.

So:
$$x(-t) = \begin{cases} t+1, & -1 \le t < 0 \\ 1-t, & 0 \le t \le 1 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Step 2**: Compute even part:
$$x_e(t) = \frac{x(t) + x(-t)}{2}$$

For $-1 \le t \le 0$: $x_e(t) = \frac{(t+1) + (t+1)}{2} = t+1$.
For $0 < t \le 1$: $x_e(t) = \frac{(1-t) + (1-t)}{2} = 1-t$.
At $t = 0$: $x_e(0) = 1$.

Therefore $x_e(t)$ is a symmetric triangular pulse peaking at $(0, 1)$.

**Step 3**: Compute odd part:
$$x_o(t) = \frac{x(t) - x(-t)}{2}$$

For $-1 \le t \le 0$: $x_o(t) = \frac{(t+1) - (t+1)}{2} = 0$.
For $0 < t \le 1$: $x_o(t) = \frac{(1-t) - (1-t)}{2} = 0$.

Therefore $x_o(t) = 0$ for all $t$. The original signal was **purely even**, which makes sense because the piecewise definition is symmetric. $\blacksquare$

**Part (b) [7 Marks]**: For a general signal $x(t)$ with energy $E$, prove that the cross-energy integral between its even and odd parts is zero. Hence show that $E = E_e + E_o$.

**Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1**: Express total energy:
$$E = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} |x(t)|^2\, dt = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x^2(t)\, dt$$

**Step 2**: Substitute $x(t) = x_e(t) + x_o(t)$:
$$E = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \big[x_e(t) + x_o(t)\big]^2\, dt$$

**Step 3**: Expand the square:
$$E = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x_e^2(t)\, dt + \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x_o^2(t)\, dt + 2\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x_e(t)\, x_o(t)\, dt$$

**Step 4**: Identify cross-term $I_c = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x_e(t) x_o(t)\, dt$. The product of an even and odd signal is odd. The integral of any odd signal over symmetric limits is zero (proved in Section 3.3). Hence $I_c = 0$.

**Step 5**: Therefore:
$$E = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x_e^2(t)\, dt + \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x_o^2(t)\, dt = E_e + E_o \quad \blacksquare$$

---

#### **Question B — Algebraic Symmetry Properties** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Part (a) [7 Marks]**: Show that:
(i) The product of two even signals is even.
(ii) The product of two odd signals is even.
(iii) The product of an even and an odd signal is odd.

**Solution (7 Marks):**

**(i) Let $x(t), y(t)$ be even.** Then $x(-t) = x(t)$ and $y(-t) = y(t)$.

Define $z(t) = x(t) y(t)$. Then:
$$z(-t) = x(-t) y(-t) = x(t) y(t) = z(t)$$
Therefore $z(t)$ is **even**. $\checkmark$

**(ii) Let $x(t), y(t)$ be odd.** Then $x(-t) = -x(t)$ and $y(-t) = -y(t)$.

Define $z(t) = x(t) y(t)$. Then:
$$z(-t) = x(-t) y(-t) = [-x(t)][-y(t)] = x(t) y(t) = z(t)$$
Therefore $z(t)$ is **even**. $\checkmark$

**(iii) Let $x(t)$ be even and $y(t)$ be odd.** Then $x(-t) = x(t)$ and $y(-t) = -y(t)$.

Define $z(t) = x(t) y(t)$. Then:
$$z(-t) = x(-t) y(-t) = x(t) \cdot [-y(t)] = -x(t) y(t) = -z(t)$$
Therefore $z(t)$ is **odd**. $\checkmark$

**Part (b) [7 Marks]**: Given $x(t) = e^{-2t} u(t)$ (a right-sided exponential, neither even nor odd), determine:
(i) Its even part $x_e(t)$.
(ii) Its odd part $x_o(t)$.
(iii) Verify that $x(t) = x_e(t) + x_o(t)$.

**Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1**: Determine $x(-t)$. Since $u(-t) = 1$ for $t \le 0$ and $0$ otherwise:
$$x(-t) = e^{-2(-t)} u(-t) = e^{2t} u(-t)$$

**Step 2**: Compute even part:
$$x_e(t) = \frac{1}{2}\big[e^{-2t} u(t) + e^{2t} u(-t)\big]$$

This is a two-sided exponential that is symmetric about the y-axis. It is non-zero everywhere on the real line.

**Step 3**: Compute odd part:
$$x_o(t) = \frac{1}{2}\big[e^{-2t} u(t) - e^{2t} u(-t)\big]$$

**Step 4 (Verification)**: For $t > 0$: $u(t) = 1$, $u(-t) = 0$.

$$x_e(t) + x_o(t) = \tfrac{1}{2} e^{-2t} + \tfrac{1}{2} e^{-2t} = e^{-2t} = x(t) \quad \checkmark$$

For $t < 0$: $u(t) = 0$, $u(-t) = 1$.

$$x_e(t) + x_o(t) = \tfrac{1}{2} e^{2t} - \tfrac{1}{2} e^{2t} = 0 = x(t) \quad \checkmark$$

For $t = 0$: $u(0)$ is conventionally taken as $1/2$, but both sides evaluate to $1/2$ consistently. $\blacksquare$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Sign confusion in odd part formula**: Many students write $x_o(t) = \tfrac{1}{2}[x(-t) - x(t)]$ with the order swapped. The correct order is $x(t) - x(-t)$. A sign error here **cascades** through the entire problem and costs 4–6 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting to verify**: KTU 2024 scheme strictly requires verification that the extracted parts actually satisfy $x_e(-t) = x_e(t)$ and $x_o(-t) = -x_o(t)$. Skipping verification costs **2 marks minimum**.
> 3. **Cross-term integral**: In energy/power problems, students often forget that $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x_e(t) x_o(t) dt = 0$. This is the **make-or-break step** for full marks in 14-mark questions.
> 4. **Discrete vs Continuous**: Mixing up $n$ and $t$, or applying CT formulas to DT signals (or vice versa), loses 2–3 marks. Always declare the domain explicitly.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Even signal**: $x(-t) = x(t)$ — symmetric about y-axis, like a mountain.
- **Odd signal**: $x(-t) = -x(t)$ — anti-symmetric, like a seesaw; always satisfies $x(0) = 0$.
- **Universal decomposition**: $x(t) = x_e(t) + x_o(t)$ holds for **every** signal — this is the single most important KTU result.
- **Extraction formulas**: $x_e(t) = \tfrac{1}{2}[x(t) + x(-t)]$, $x_o(t) = \tfrac{1}{2}[x(t) - x(-t)]$.
- **Discrete-time equivalents**: Replace $t$ with $n$ and use square brackets $x[n]$, $x[-n]$.
- **Product algebra**: Even×Even = Even, Odd×Odd = Even, Even×Odd = Odd.
- **Sum algebra**: Even+Even = Even, Odd+Odd = Odd, Even+Odd = Neither (general).
- **Area rule**: $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x_o(t) dt = 0$ — area under odd signals vanishes.
- **Energy rule**: $E = E_e + E_o$ — cross-term vanishes due to odd symmetry.
- **Verification mandate**: After decomposition, always check that $x_e(-t) = x_e(t)$ and $x_o(-t) = -x_o(t)$.
- **Common KTU test signals**: $\cos(\omega t)$ is even, $\sin(\omega t)$ is odd, $\delta(t)$ is even, $u(t)$ is neither, $e^{-at}u(t)$ (with $a > 0$) is neither.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Real Sequences)
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST416 - SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Real Sequences)

# Real Sequences – Foundational Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme** for Signals and Systems, a **real sequence** is formally defined as a mathematical function whose domain is the set of integers $\mathbb{Z}$ (or a subset of integers) and whose codomain is the set of real numbers $\mathbb{R}$.

$$
x : \mathbb{Z} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}, \quad x[n] \in \mathbb{R} \quad \forall n \in \mathbb{Z}
$$

The variable $n$ is an integer representing the sample index, while $x[n]$ denotes the value of the sequence at the $n^{\text{th}}$ instant. Since every output is strictly a **real number**, the sequence contains no imaginary or complex components; hence the qualifier "Real."

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):**
> A sequence is the most fundamental discrete-time signal. All discrete-time systems operate on sequences, making this concept the building block for convolution, Z-transform, and DFT studied in later modules.

## 1.2 Intuitive Conceptual Analogy

Imagine a **movie reel** that has been cut into individual frames. The film itself is a *continuous signal* $x(t)$, but once you separate it into numbered photographs, each photograph has a specific frame number $n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$ and each has a measurable brightness value $x[n]$. The complete set of frame-number-to-brightness-value pairings is your **real sequence**.

A simpler analogy: think of the **daily closing temperature** of your city recorded every day. Day index = $n$, Temperature in °C = $x[n]$. The ordered collection $\{x[0], x[1], x[2], \ldots\}$ is a real sequence. It is "real" because temperature is a real-valued physical quantity (never $3 + 4i$ °C).

## 1.3 Graphical Representation

A sequence is conventionally represented as a **stem plot**, where the horizontal axis is the integer index $n$ and the vertical axis is the amplitude $x[n]$.

- **Stem:** A vertical line from the $n$-axis up to the value $x[n]$.
- **Marker:** A filled circle (or dot) at the top of each stem.
- **Implicit zero:** Any integer $n$ without a marker has $x[n] = 0$.

## 1.4 Classification of Real Sequences

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU students must remember the following classification tree, as questions are frequently framed directly from this structure.

**A. Based on Duration (Support):**

| Type | Definition | Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Finite-Length Sequence** | $x[n]$ is defined only for $N_1 \le n \le N_2$ | $x[n] = \{1, 2, 3\}$, $\uparrow$ at $n=0$ |
| **Infinite-Length Sequence** | $x[n]$ is defined for all $n \in \mathbb{Z}$ | $x[n] = (0.5)^n u[n]$ |

**B. Based on Symmetry:**

| Type | Condition | Property |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Even Sequence** | $x[-n] = x[n]$ | Symmetric about the vertical axis ($n = 0$) |
| **Odd Sequence** | $x[-n] = -x[n]$ | Anti-symmetric; $x[0] = 0$ always |

**C. Based on Repetition:**

| Type | Condition |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Periodic Sequence** | $x[n + N] = x[n]$ for some smallest integer $N > 0$ |
| **Aperiodic Sequence** | No finite $N$ satisfies the periodicity condition |

**D. Based on Energy/Power:**

| Type | Condition |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Energy Signal** | $0 < E < \infty$ (finite energy, average power $= 0$) |
| **Power Signal** | $0 < P < \infty$ (finite power, infinite energy) |
| **Neither** | Neither energy nor power is finite |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Standard Elementary Real Sequences

These four sequences appear in **every KTU exam cycle**, often as 3-mark direct questions. Master their definitions and stem plots.

### (i) Unit Impulse Sequence $\delta[n]$
$$
\delta[n] = \begin{cases} 1, & n = 0 \\ 0, & n \neq 0 \end{cases}
$$
- Acts as the **identity element** for discrete-time convolution.
- Sampling property: $x[n]\,\delta[n - n_0] = x[n_0]\,\delta[n - n_0]$.

### (ii) Unit Step Sequence $u[n]$
$$
u[n] = \begin{cases} 1, & n \ge 0 \\ 0, & n < 0 \end{cases}
$$
- Cumulative relation: $\displaystyle u[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{n} \delta[k]$.
- Difference relation: $\delta[n] = u[n] - u[n-1]$.

### (iii) Unit Ramp Sequence $r[n]$
$$
r[n] = \begin{cases} n, & n \ge 0 \\ 0, & n < 0 \end{cases}
$$
- Equivalently, $r[n] = n\,u[n]$.

### (iv) Real Exponential Sequence $x[n] = a^n$
- If $\vert a \vert < 1$: sequence **decays** toward zero as $n \to \infty$.
- If $\vert a \vert > 1$: sequence **grows** without bound.
- If $a$ is negative: sequence **alternates** sign each step.

### (v) Real Sinusoidal Sequence $x[n] = A\cos(\omega_0 n + \phi)$
- Always **periodic** in discrete time if $\omega_0$ is a rational multiple of $2\pi$.
- Fundamental period $N = \dfrac{2\pi}{\omega_0} \cdot m$, where $m$ is the smallest integer making $N$ an integer.

## 2.2 Basic Operations on Sequences

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All transformations below are evaluated on a **per-sample basis**. KTU problems frequently ask for the transformed sequence $y[n]$ given an operation applied to $x[n]$.

### Time Shifting (Delay / Advance)
$$
y[n] = x[n - n_0]
$$
- If $n_0 > 0$: the sequence is **delayed** (shifted to the right).
- If $n_0 < 0$: the sequence is **advanced** (shifted to the left).

### Time Reversal (Folding)
$$
y[n] = x[-n]
$$
- The sequence is mirrored about the vertical axis $n = 0$.

### Time Scaling (Down-sampling / Up-sampling)
$$
y[n] = x[kn] \quad (\text{Down-sampling by factor } k), \qquad y[n] = x[n/k] \quad (\text{Up-sampling})
$$

### Amplitude Scaling
$$
y[n] = A \cdot x[n]
$$
- Every sample is multiplied by the scalar $A \in \mathbb{R}$.

### Addition and Multiplication
$$
y[n] = x_1[n] + x_2[n], \qquad y[n] = x_1[n] \cdot x_2[n]
$$
- Performed **sample-by-sample** at each index $n$.

## 2.3 Symmetry Decomposition Theorem

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Theorem:** Any real sequence can be uniquely decomposed into its even and odd parts.

$$
x[n] = x_e[n] + x_o[n]
$$

where the components are given by:

$$
x_e[n] = \frac{x[n] + x[-n]}{2} \qquad \text{(Even Part)}
$$

$$
x_o[n] = \frac{x[n] - x[-n]}{2} \qquad \text{(Odd Part)}
$$

Verification: $x_e[-n] = \frac{x[-n] + x[n]}{2} = x_e[n]$ (even), and $x_o[-n] = \frac{x[-n] - x[n]}{2} = -x_o[n]$ (odd). $\blacksquare$

## 2.4 Energy and Power of Real Sequences

The total **energy** $E$ of a real sequence is:

$$
E = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n] \vert^2 = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} (x[n])^2 \quad \text{(since } x[n] \in \mathbb{R}\text{)}
$$

The average **power** $P$ over $N$ samples (or over all time) is:

$$
P = \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{1}{2N+1} \sum_{n=-N}^{N} \vert x[n] \vert^2
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Rule of Thumb:**
> - A **periodic** sequence is almost always a **power signal**.
> - A **decaying** (aperiodic, finite-support) sequence is almost always an **energy signal**.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Concept | Formula / Condition | Key Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Unit Impulse | $\delta[n] = 1$ at $n=0$, else $0$ | Identity for convolution |
| 2 | Unit Step | $u[n] = 1$ for $n \ge 0$ | $u[n] = \sum \delta[k]$ |
| 3 | Ramp | $r[n] = n\,u[n]$ | Zero for negative $n$ |
| 4 | Time Shift | $y[n] = x[n - n_0]$ | $n_0 > 0$: right shift |
| 5 | Time Reversal | $y[n] = x[-n]$ | Mirror about $n=0$ |
| 6 | Even Part | $x_e[n] = \frac{x[n]+x[-n]}{2}$ | Always $x_e[-n] = x_e[n]$ |
| 7 | Odd Part | $x_o[n] = \frac{x[n]-x[-n]}{2}$ | Always $x_o[0] = 0$ |
| 8 | Energy | $E = \sum (x[n])^2$ | Finite for energy signals |
| 9 | Power | $P = \lim \frac{1}{2N+1} \sum (x[n])^2$ | Finite for power signals |
| 10 | Periodicity | $x[n+N] = x[n]$ | Smallest $N>0$ is fundamental period |
| 11 | Sinusoid Period | $N = \frac{2\pi m}{\omega_0}$, $m \in \mathbb{Z}^+$ | $N$ must be a positive integer |
| 12 | Exponential | $x[n] = a^n$ | Decays if $\vert a \vert < 1$ |
| 13 | Real Sequence | $x[n] \in \mathbb{R}$ for all $n$ | No imaginary component |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Digital Audio Processing:** An MP3 song sampled at 44.1 kHz is a real sequence where each $x[n]$ is the air-pressure amplitude.
- **Biomedical Signals:** ECG and EEG recordings are stored as real sequences for cardiac and neurological analysis.
- **Stock Market Analytics:** Daily closing prices form a real sequence used in time-series forecasting and trend decomposition.
- **Communication Systems:** Real baseband signals (audio, sensor data) are real sequences before modulation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Periodicity of a Discrete-Time Sinusoid

**Problem Setup:** Determine whether $x[n] = \cos\!\left(\dfrac{\pi}{6}n\right)$ is periodic. If yes, find the fundamental period $N$.

### Step 1 – Write the Periodicity Condition
By definition, a sequence is periodic with period $N$ if:
$$
x[n + N] = x[n] \quad \text{for all } n \in \mathbb{Z}
$$

### Step 2 – Substitute the Given Sequence
$$
\cos\!\left(\frac{\pi}{6}(n + N)\right) = \cos\!\left(\frac{\pi}{6}n\right)
$$

### Step 3 – Apply the Cosine Identity
Using $\cos(A + B) = \cos A \cos B - \sin A \sin B$, we require:
$$
\cos\!\left(\frac{\pi}{6}n\right)\cos\!\left(\frac{\pi}{6}N\right) - \sin\!\left(\frac{\pi}{6}n\right)\sin\!\left(\frac{\pi}{6}N\right) = \cos\!\left(\frac{\pi}{6}n\right)
$$

### Step 4 – Equate Coefficients of $\cos$ and $\sin$
For the identity to hold for **all** $n$, we must have:
$$
\cos\!\left(\frac{\pi}{6}N\right) = 1 \quad \text{AND} \quad \sin\!\left(\frac{\pi}{6}N\right) = 0
$$

### Step 5 – Solve for the Smallest Positive $N$
Both conditions are satisfied simultaneously when:
$$
\frac{\pi}{6}N = 2\pi k, \quad k \in \mathbb{Z}
$$

Solving for $N$:
$$
N = \frac{2\pi k \cdot 6}{\pi} = 12k
$$

The smallest positive integer occurs at $k = 1$, giving:
$$
\boxed{N = 12}
$$

**Conclusion:** The sequence is periodic with fundamental period $N = 12$ samples. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Even–Odd Decomposition of a Given Sequence

**Problem Setup:** Given $x[n] = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$ with the arrow $\uparrow$ at $n = 0$, find $x_e[n]$ and $x_o[n]$.

### Step 1 – Write the Sequence in Index Form
Aligning the arrow at $n=0$:
$$
x[-2]=1,\ x[-1]=2,\ x[0]=3,\ x[1]=4,\ x[2]=5
$$

### Step 2 – Construct $x[-n]$ by Mirroring
By reversing the index:
$$
x[2]=1,\ x[1]=2,\ x[0]=3,\ x[-1]=4,\ x[-2]=5
$$

### Step 3 – Apply the Even-Part Formula
$$
x_e[n] = \frac{x[n] + x[-n]}{2}
$$

Computing each sample:
$$
x_e[-2] = \frac{x[-2] + x[2]}{2} = \frac{1 + 5}{2} = 3
$$

$$
x_e[-1] = \frac{x[-1] + x[1]}{2} = \frac{2 + 4}{2} = 3
$$

$$
x_e[0] = \frac{x[0] + x[0]}{2} = \frac{3 + 3}{2} = 3
$$

$$
x_e[1] = \frac{x[1] + x[-1]}{2} = \frac{4 + 2}{2} = 3
$$

$$
x_e[2] = \frac{x[2] + x[-2]}{2} = \frac{5 + 1}{2} = 3
$$

Result:
$$
\boxed{x_e[n] = \{3, 3, 3, 3, 3\},\ \uparrow\ \text{at}\ n=0}
$$

### Step 4 – Apply the Odd-Part Formula
$$
x_o[n] = \frac{x[n] - x[-n]}{2}
$$

Computing each sample:
$$
x_o[-2] = \frac{1 - 5}{2} = -2
$$

$$
x_o[-1] = \frac{2 - 4}{2} = -1
$$

$$
x_o[0] = \frac{3 - 3}{2} = 0
$$

$$
x_o[1] = \frac{4 - 2}{2} = 1
$$

$$
x_o[2] = \frac{5 - 1}{2} = 2
$$

Result:
$$
\boxed{x_o[n] = \{-2, -1, 0, 1, 2\},\ \uparrow\ \text{at}\ n=0}
$$

### Step 5 – Verification
Adding both parts:
$$
x_e[n] + x_o[n] = \{3-2,\ 3-1,\ 3+0,\ 3+1,\ 3+2\} = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\} = x[n] \quad \checkmark
$$

Note that $x_o[0] = 0$, confirming the mandatory property of any odd sequence. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Energy and Power of an Exponential Decay

**Problem Setup:** For the causal exponential $x[n] = (0.5)^n\,u[n]$, compute the energy $E$ and average power $P$.

### Step 1 – Energy Formula for a Causal Sequence
$$
E = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} (x[n])^2 = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \left((0.5)^n\right)^2 = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} (0.5)^{2n} = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} (0.25)^n
$$

### Step 2 – Recognize the Geometric Series
The infinite geometric series $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} r^n$ converges to $\dfrac{1}{1-r}$ when $\vert r \vert < 1$.

Here $r = 0.25$, so:
$$
E = \frac{1}{1 - 0.25} = \frac{1}{0.75} = \frac{4}{3} \approx 1.333
$$

### Step 3 – Average Power
$$
P = \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{1}{2N+1} \sum_{n=-N}^{N} (x[n])^2 = \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{E}{2N+1} = 0
$$

### Step 4 – Classification
$$
\boxed{E = \frac{4}{3} < \infty, \qquad P = 0}
$$

**Conclusion:** $x[n] = (0.5)^n u[n]$ is an **Energy Signal** (but not a power signal). $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Operation Composition (Shift + Fold + Scale)

**Problem Setup:** Given $x[n] = \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$ with $\uparrow$ at $n=0$, compute $y[n] = 2\,x[-(n-1)]$.

### Step 1 – Decompose the Compound Operation
The expression is read right-to-left:
1. **Inner:** $x[n-1]$ — shift right by 1 (delay by 1).
2. **Middle:** $x[-(n-1)] = x[1-n]$ — fold the delayed sequence.
3. **Outer:** $y[n] = 2\,x[1-n]$ — amplitude scale by 2.

### Step 2 – Step-by-Step Table

| Step | Operation | Result $w[n]$ (with $\uparrow$ at $n=0$) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Start | $x[n]$ | $\{1, 2, 3, 4\}$ |
| Shift | $x[n-1]$ | $\{1, 2, 3, 4\}$ but $\uparrow$ at $n=1$ |
| Fold | $x[-(n-1)]$ | $\{1, 2, 3, 4\}$ but $\uparrow$ at $n=-1$ |
| Scale | $y[n] = 2x[-(n-1)]$ | $\{2, 4, 6, 8\}$ but $\uparrow$ at $n=-1$ |

### Step 3 – Final Answer
Writing in standard form with arrow at $n = -1$:
$$
\boxed{y[n] = \{2, 4, 6, 8\},\ \uparrow\ \text{at}\ n = -1}
$$

The amplitudes are doubled because of the multiplicative scalar $2$, and the arrow position reflects the net $-1$ shift introduced by the combined fold-and-delay operations. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Operation Result Table

Let $x[n] = \{a,\ b,\ c\}$ with $\uparrow$ at $n = 0$. Then the same sequence under different operations is:

| Operation | Resulting Sequence | Arrow Position |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $x[n]$ | $\{a, b, c\}$ | $n = 0$ |
| $x[n-2]$ | $\{a, b, c\}$ | $n = 2$ |
| $x[n+1]$ | $\{a, b, c\}$ | $n = -1$ |
| $x[-n]$ | $\{c, b, a\}$ | $n = 0$ |
| $x[-n+2]$ | $\{c, b, a\}$ | $n = 2$ |
| $x[2n]$ | Subsample every 2nd | depends on parity |
| $2\,x[n]$ | $\{2a, 2b, 2c\}$ | $n = 0$ |
| $x[-n-1]$ | $\{c, b, a\}$ | $n = -1$ |

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Periodicity Quick Reference

| $x[n]$ | Periodic? | Fundamental Period $N$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\cos\!\left(\dfrac{\pi}{8}n\right)$ | Yes | $16$ |
| $\cos\!\left(\dfrac{1}{6}n\right)$ | **No** | $\frac{2\pi}{1/6} = 12\pi$ is not an integer |
| $\sin\!\left(\dfrac{\pi}{3}n + \dfrac{\pi}{4}\right)$ | Yes | $6$ |
| $(-1)^n$ | Yes | $2$ |
| $(0.5)^n u[n]$ | No | Aperiodic (energy signal) |
| $e^{j\pi n/5}$ | Yes | $10$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Stem Plot of a Generic Real Sequence

The following Mermaid block visualizes the **stem-plot representation** of a generic real sequence, highlighting sample indices, amplitudes, and the implicit zeros.

```mermaid
graph LR
    classDef stemOn fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e3a8a,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef stemOff fill:#ffffff,stroke:#94a3b8,stroke-width:1px,color:#0f172a
    classDef axis fill:#f1f5f9,stroke:#94a3b8,color:#0f172a
    classDef label fill:#fef9c3,stroke:#facc15,color:#0f172a

    axisN["n axis (horizontal)"]:::axis
    axisA["Amplitude axis (vertical)"]:::axis

    sN2["n = -2 : x[n] = 0"]:::stemOff
    sN1["n = -1 : x[n] = 0"]:::stemOff
    s0["n = 0 : x[n] = 2 STEM ON"]:::stemOn
    s1["n = 1 : x[n] = 3 STEM ON"]:::stemOn
    s2["n = 2 : x[n] = 1 STEM ON"]:::stemOn
    s3["n = 3 : x[n] = 0"]:::stemOff
    s4["n = 4 : x[n] = 0"]:::stemOff

    sN2 --- sN1
    sN1 --- s0
    s0 --- s1
    s1 --- s2
    s2 --- s3
    s3 --- s4
```

**Reading guide:** A "STEM ON" node indicates that a filled-circle marker is drawn at $(n, x[n])$. A plain node indicates the implicit zero value.

## 4.2 Functional Architecture: Transformation Pipeline for Sequences

This block diagram shows how an input sequence $x[n]$ flows through standard KTU transformations to produce output sequences $y[n]$.

```mermaid
graph TD
    classDef src fill:#0f766e,stroke:#0f766e,color:#ffffff
    classDef op fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff
    classDef out fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff

    A["INPUT SEQUENCE x n"]:::src

    B["SHIFT BLOCK: y1 n equals x of n minus n0"]:::op
    C["FOLD BLOCK: y2 n equals x of minus n"]:::op
    D["SCALE BLOCK: y3 n equals A times x n"]:::op
    E["ADD BLOCK: y4 n equals x1 n plus x2 n"]:::op

    F["OUTPUT: y1 n"]:::out
    G["OUTPUT: y2 n"]:::out
    H["OUTPUT: y3 n"]:::out
    I["OUTPUT: y4 n"]:::out

    A --> B --> F
    A --> C --> G
    A --> D --> H
    A --> E --> I

    J["SECOND INPUT x2 n"]:::src
    J --> E
```

## 4.3 Even–Odd Decomposition Flowchart

This figure illustrates the canonical decomposition of any real sequence into its symmetric components.

```mermaid
graph TD
    classDef io fill:#0f172a,stroke:#0f172a,color:#ffffff
    classDef proc fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff
    classDef res fill:#15803d,stroke:#15803d,color:#ffffff

    A["INPUT: x n arbitrary real sequence"]:::io

    B["STEP 1: Construct x of minus n via folding"]:::proc
    C["STEP 2: Add x n plus x of minus n"]:::proc
    D["STEP 3: Subtract x n minus x of minus n"]:::proc
    E["STEP 4: Divide both by 2"]:::proc

    F["EVEN PART x even n symmetric"]:::res
    G["ODD PART x odd n antisymmetric"]:::res

    A --> B
    B --> C
    B --> D
    C --> E
    D --> E
    E --> F
    E --> G

    F --> H["VERIFICATION: x even plus x odd equals x n"]
    G --> H
```

## 4.4 Energy vs Power Decision Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    classDef q fill:#fef9c3,stroke:#facc15,color:#0f172a
    classDef a fill:#0f766e,stroke:#0f766e,color:#ffffff
    classDef r fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff

    Q1{"Is energy E finite?"}:::q
    Q2{"Is power P finite?"}:::q

    A1["ENERGY SIGNAL: 0 less than E less than infinity, P equals 0"]:::a
    A2["POWER SIGNAL: E equals infinity, 0 less than P less than infinity"]:::a
    A3["NEITHER: E and P both infinite"]:::r

    Q1 -- yes --> A1
    Q1 -- no --> Q2
    Q2 -- yes --> A2
    Q2 -- no --> A3
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define a real sequence. State any four standard elementary sequences with their mathematical expressions.**

**Model Answer:**
A real sequence is a function $x[n]$ defined for integer indices $n \in \mathbb{Z}$ whose values belong to the real number set, i.e., $x[n] \in \mathbb{R}$.

Four standard elementary sequences are:

1. **Unit Impulse:** $\delta[n] = 1$ for $n = 0$, else $0$.
2. **Unit Step:** $u[n] = 1$ for $n \ge 0$, else $0$.
3. **Unit Ramp:** $r[n] = n$ for $n \ge 0$, else $0$.
4. **Real Exponential:** $x[n] = a^n$, where $a \in \mathbb{R}$.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**What is the condition for a sequence to be periodic? Check whether $x[n] = \cos(0.5\pi n)$ is periodic and find its fundamental period.**

**Model Answer:**
A sequence is periodic if $x[n+N] = x[n]$ for all $n$, with the smallest such positive integer $N$ being the fundamental period.

For $x[n] = \cos(0.5\pi n)$, we require:
$$
\cos(0.5\pi(n+N)) = \cos(0.5\pi n) \implies 0.5\pi N = 2\pi k
$$
$$
N = 4k,\ \text{smallest positive integer}\ N = 4
$$

**Result:** Periodic with $N = 4$.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a) For the sequence $x[n] = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$ with arrow at $n=0$, determine its even and odd parts. Verify that $x[n] = x_e[n] + x_o[n]$.** `[7 Marks]`

**Step 1 — Tabulate $x[n]$ with index:**
$$
x[-2]=1,\ x[-1]=2,\ x[0]=3,\ x[1]=4,\ x[2]=5
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $x[-n]$ by mirroring:**
$$
x[2]=1,\ x[1]=2,\ x[0]=3,\ x[-1]=4,\ x[-2]=5
$$

**Step 3 — Apply even formula $x_e[n] = \dfrac{x[n]+x[-n]}{2}$:**

| $n$ | $x[n]$ | $x[-n]$ | $x_e[n]$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $-2$ | $1$ | $5$ | $3$ |
| $-1$ | $2$ | $4$ | $3$ |
| $0$ | $3$ | $3$ | $3$ |
| $1$ | $4$ | $2$ | $3$ |
| $2$ | $5$ | $1$ | $3$ |

Result: $x_e[n] = \{3, 3, 3, 3, 3\}$, $\uparrow$ at $n=0$.

**Step 4 — Apply odd formula $x_o[n] = \dfrac{x[n]-x[-n]}{2}$:**

| $n$ | $x[n]$ | $x[-n]$ | $x_o[n]$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $-2$ | $1$ | $5$ | $-2$ |
| $-1$ | $2$ | $4$ | $-1$ |
| $0$ | $3$ | $3$ | $0$ |
| $1$ | $4$ | $2$ | $1$ |
| $2$ | $5$ | $1$ | $2$ |

Result: $x_o[n] = \{-2, -1, 0, 1, 2\}$, $\uparrow$ at $n=0$.

**Step 5 — Verify:** $x_e[n] + x_o[n] = \{3-2, 3-1, 3+0, 3+1, 3+2\} = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\} = x[n]$ $\checkmark$

**(b) Compute the energy and average power of $x[n] = (0.5)^n u[n]$ and classify the signal.** `[7 Marks]`

**Step 1 — Energy:**
$$
E = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \left((0.5)^n\right)^2 = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} (0.25)^n = \frac{1}{1-0.25} = \frac{4}{3}
$$

**Step 2 — Power:**
$$
P = \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{1}{2N+1}\cdot \frac{4}{3} = 0
$$

**Step 3 — Classification:** Since $0 < E = \dfrac{4}{3} < \infty$, the signal is an **Energy Signal**.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a) Given $x[n] = \{2, 4, 6, 8\}$ with arrow at $n=0$, find the sequence $y[n] = x[2-n]$. Sketch both stem plots.** `[7 Marks]`

**Step 1 — Identify shift and fold composition:**
$y[n] = x[2-n]$ can be written as $x[-(n-2)]$, i.e., shift by $+2$ then fold (or fold then shift by $-2$).

**Step 2 — Compose using intermediate table:**

| Original $n$ | $x[n]$ | After shift $x[n-2]$ at $n=2$ | After fold $x[-(n-2)] = x[2-n]$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $-2$ | $0$ | — | $0$ |
| $-1$ | $0$ | — | $0$ |
| $0$ | $2$ | at $n=2$: $2$ | $0$ |
| $1$ | $4$ | — | $0$ |
| $2$ | $6$ | — | $2$ |
| $3$ | $8$ | — | $4$ |
| $4$ | $0$ | — | $6$ |
| $5$ | $0$ | — | $8$ |

**Step 3 — Final answer:**
$$
\boxed{y[n] = \{2, 4, 6, 8\},\ \uparrow\ \text{at}\ n = 2}
$$

**Step 4 — Sketch description:** $x[n]$ has amplitudes $2, 4, 6, 8$ at $n = 0, 1, 2, 3$ respectively (rising staircase). $y[n]$ has the same amplitudes but mirrored, peaking at $n=2$ and decaying for $n > 2$.

**(b) Determine whether $x[n] = \cos(0.3\pi n)$ is periodic. Justify using the rational multiple criterion.** `[7 Marks]`

**Step 1 — State criterion:**
A discrete sinusoid $\cos(\omega_0 n)$ is periodic **if and only if** $\dfrac{\omega_0}{2\pi}$ is a rational number, i.e., $\dfrac{\omega_0}{2\pi} = \dfrac{p}{q}$ for some integers $p, q$. The fundamental period is then $N = q$.

**Step 2 — Apply to the given signal:**
$$
\frac{\omega_0}{2\pi} = \frac{0.3\pi}{2\pi} = \frac{0.3}{2} = \frac{3}{20}
$$

**Step 3 — Rationality check:**
$\dfrac{3}{20}$ is a rational number (ratio of two integers), so the sequence is **periodic** with $N = 20$.

**Step 4 — Verification:**
$$
x[n+20] = \cos(0.3\pi(n+20)) = \cos(0.3\pi n + 6\pi) = \cos(0.3\pi n) = x[n] \quad \checkmark
$$

**Conclusion:** Periodic with fundamental period $N = 20$.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Arrow Misplacement:** Always re-check the index of the arrow $\uparrow$ after every shift/fold operation. Many students lose 2–3 marks by writing the correct values but at the wrong starting index.
> 2. **Forgetting $x_o[0] = 0$:** If your computed odd part has a non-zero value at $n = 0$, it is automatically wrong — full 7-mark loss possible on symmetry problems.
> 3. **Conflating Continuous vs Discrete Periodicity:** Unlike continuous sinusoids which are *always* periodic, discrete sinusoids are periodic **only when** $\omega_0 / 2\pi$ is rational. KTU examiners explicitly test this conceptual distinction.
> 4. **Energy vs Power Confusion:** Remember $P = \lim \frac{E}{2N+1}$ — for any decaying exponential, $E$ is finite but $P = 0$. Do not classify such signals as power signals.
> 5. **Skipping the Geometric Series Convergence Check:** Always verify $\vert r \vert < 1$ before applying $\sum r^n = \frac{1}{1-r}$.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **real sequence** maps integers to real numbers; no imaginary part exists.
- Standard sequences: $\delta[n]$, $u[n]$, $r[n]$, $a^n$, $\cos(\omega_0 n)$.
- **Time shift:** $x[n-n_0]$, positive $n_0$ → right shift (delay).
- **Folding:** $x[-n]$ mirrors the sequence about $n = 0$.
- **Amplitude scaling:** $y[n] = A \cdot x[n]$ multiplies every sample by $A$.
- **Even part** formula: $x_e[n] = \dfrac{x[n] + x[-n]}{2}$.
- **Odd part** formula: $x_o[n] = \dfrac{x[n] - x[-n]}{2}$.
- **Mandatory check:** Any valid odd sequence must satisfy $x_o[0] = 0$.
- **Decomposition identity:** $x[n] = x_e[n] + x_o[n]$ is **unique**.
- **Energy:** $E = \sum (x[n])^2$ — finite only for energy signals.
- **Power:** $P = \lim_{N \to \infty} \dfrac{1}{2N+1}\sum (x[n])^2$ — finite only for power signals.
- **Periodicity condition:** $x[n+N] = x[n]$ for the smallest positive integer $N$.
- **Discrete sinusoid periodicity:** $\cos(\omega_0 n)$ is periodic iff $\dfrac{\omega_0}{2\pi} \in \mathbb{Q}$.
- **Decaying exponentials** ($\vert a \vert < 1$) are typically energy signals.
- **Periodic sequences** are typically power signals.
- **Operations order matters:** Always read composite expressions from the innermost bracket outward.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: DTFT of Periodic Sequences - Frequency Spectra of Sequences
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST416 - SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** DTFT of Periodic Sequences - Frequency Spectra of Sequences

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

The **Discrete-Time Fourier Transform (DTFT)** of a discrete-time sequence $x(n)$ is defined as:

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(n)\, e^{-j\omega n}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Periodic Sequences**
> A sequence is called **periodic** if there exists a smallest positive integer $N$ such that $x(n+N) = x(n)$ for all $n$. The DTFT of a periodic sequence exists only in a **generalized function** sense — it is not a regular function but a **train of impulses** in the frequency domain.

The **DTFT of a periodic sequence** is mathematically expressed as:

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{2\pi}{N} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} a_k\, \delta\!\left(\omega - \frac{2\pi k}{N}\right)$$

where the coefficients $a_k$ are the **Discrete-Time Fourier Series (DTFS)** coefficients of the periodic sequence.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Heartbeat Monitor

Imagine a doctor listening to a patient's **periodic heartbeat** through a stethoscope. The heartbeat is a repeating signal (periodic in time). If you were to pass this heartbeat through a spectrum analyzer, instead of getting a smooth curve, you would see **sharp spikes** at specific frequencies — these spikes correspond to the fundamental heart rate and its harmonics.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Mapping**
> - **Time domain** — the periodic heartbeat (repeating every $N$ beats).
> - **Frequency domain** — sharp impulses at exact harmonic frequencies $\omega = \frac{2\pi k}{N}$.
> - **Coefficient $a_k$** — the "weight" or "loudness" of each harmonic component, identical to the amplitude of that harmonic in the Fourier series expansion.

This is precisely how a periodic discrete-time signal behaves under the DTFT: it produces a **discrete, impulse-train spectrum** rather than a continuous spectrum.

## 1.3 Frequency Spectra of Sequences — Definition

The **Frequency Spectrum** of a sequence is the complete description of its frequency content, consisting of two components:

- **Magnitude Spectrum:** $\vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert$ — represents the amplitude distribution across frequencies.
- **Phase Spectrum:** $\angle X(e^{j\omega})$ — represents the phase distribution across frequencies.

For a **periodic sequence**, both spectra are **discrete impulse trains** spaced at intervals of $\frac{2\pi}{N}$ in the frequency domain.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DTFT is ALWAYS periodic in $\omega$ with period $2\pi$** — this is a fundamental property distinguishing DTFT from the Continuous-Time Fourier Transform (CTFT).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Mathematical Foundation — Why Impulses?

For a **periodic sequence** $x(n)$ with period $N$, the **Discrete-Time Fourier Series (DTFS)** synthesis equation gives:

$$x(n) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=\langle N \rangle} a_k\, e^{j k \omega_0 n}, \quad \omega_0 = \frac{2\pi}{N}$$

The DTFS coefficients are computed as:

$$a_k = \sum_{n=\langle N \rangle} x(n)\, e^{-j k \omega_0 n}$$

### The 'Why' Behind the Impulse Train

1. **Step 1 — Substitute DTFS into DTFT definition:**
   Apply DTFT to the synthesis expression of the periodic sequence.

2. **Step 2 — Recognize the discrete spectrum:**
   A complex exponential $e^{j k \omega_0 n}$ has a DTFT equal to an impulse train:
   
   $$e^{j k \omega_0 n} \xleftrightarrow{\text{DTFT}} 2\pi \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(\omega - k\omega_0 - 2\pi m)$$

3. **Step 3 — Linearity and scaling:**
   Multiply by the coefficients and sum — yielding a **weighted impulse train**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The DTFT of a periodic sequence is **non-summable** in the classical sense; it exists only as a **distribution** (generalized function), analogous to how the Fourier transform of $\cos(\omega_0 t)$ is a pair of Dirac deltas.

## 2.2 Properties of DTFT — KTU High-Yield Summary

| # | Property | Time Domain | Frequency Domain |
|---|----------|-------------|------------------|
| 1 | Linearity | $a\,x_1(n) + b\,x_2(n)$ | $a\,X_1(e^{j\omega}) + b\,X_2(e^{j\omega})$ |
| 2 | Time Shifting | $x(n-n_0)$ | $e^{-j\omega n_0}\,X(e^{j\omega})$ |
| 3 | Frequency Shifting | $e^{j\omega_0 n}\,x(n)$ | $X(e^{j(\omega-\omega_0)})$ |
| 4 | Time Reversal | $x(-n)$ | $X(e^{-j\omega})$ |
| 5 | Conjugation | $x^*(n)$ | $X^*(e^{-j\omega})$ |
| 6 | Convolution | $x_1(n) * x_2(n)$ | $X_1(e^{j\omega})\,X_2(e^{j\omega})$ |
| 7 | Multiplication | $x_1(n)\,x_2(n)$ | $\frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} X_1(e^{j\theta})\,X_2(e^{j(\omega-\theta)})\,d\theta$ |
| 8 | Differentiate-in-Freq | $n\,x(n)$ | $j \frac{d}{d\omega} X(e^{j\omega})$ |
| 9 | Parseval's Theorem | $\sum_{n} \vert x(n) \vert^2$ | $\frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} \vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert^2 d\omega$ |
| 10 | Periodicity | — | $X(e^{j\omega}) = X(e^{j(\omega+2\pi)})$ |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application |
|--------|-------------|
| **Digital Audio Processing** | MP3 codecs rely on spectral analysis to identify periodic pitches (e.g., musical notes). |
| **OFDM Communication** | 4G/5G/6G systems use periodic pilot sequences whose impulse-train spectra help channel estimation. |
| **Biomedical Signals** | ECG/EEG periodic components are analyzed using DTFT to detect arrhythmia patterns. |
| **Speech Processing** | Voiced speech is quasi-periodic; impulse-train spectra model the vocal-tract excitation. |
| **Radar Systems** | Pulsed radar returns are periodic in time; their spectra reveal target velocity (Doppler). |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 EXHAUSTIVE DERIVATION — DTFT of a Periodic Sequence

### Setup
Let $x(n)$ be a **periodic sequence** with fundamental period $N$. Its DTFS representation is:

$$x(n) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} a_k\, e^{j k \omega_0 n}, \quad \text{where} \quad \omega_0 = \frac{2\pi}{N}$$

### Step 1 — Take the DTFT of Both Sides

By definition:

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(n)\, e^{-j\omega n}$$

Substitute the DTFS expression for $x(n)$:

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \left[ \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} a_k\, e^{j k \omega_0 n} \right] e^{-j\omega n}$$

### Step 2 — Interchange the Order of Summation

Since both sums are finite/infinite and convergent in the distribution sense, we can swap:

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} a_k \left[ \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} e^{-j(\omega - k\omega_0)n} \right]$$

### Step 3 — Recognize the Inner Sum as an Impulse Train

The inner sum is the DTFT of a constant sequence $1$ (i.e., $x(n) = 1$ for all $n$). This sum is a **train of Dirac deltas**:

$$\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} e^{-j(\omega - k\omega_0)n} = 2\pi \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(\omega - k\omega_0 - 2\pi m)$$

### Step 4 — Substitute Back

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{2\pi}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} a_k \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(\omega - k\omega_0 - 2\pi m)$$

### Step 5 — Combine into a Single Sum

By letting $k$ range over all integers (since $a_k$ is periodic with period $N$):

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{2\pi}{N} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} a_k\, \delta\!\left(\omega - \frac{2\pi k}{N}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **This is the KTU-canonical result** — the DTFT of a periodic sequence is a train of impulses weighted by $\frac{2\pi}{N} a_k$ located at frequencies $\omega = \frac{2\pi k}{N}$.

## 3.2 DERIVATION — DTFT of Complex Exponential $e^{j\omega_0 n}$

We derive a special case to strengthen understanding.

### Step 1 — Apply Definition

$$\mathcal{F}\{e^{j\omega_0 n}\} = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} e^{j\omega_0 n}\, e^{-j\omega n} = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} e^{-j(\omega - \omega_0)n}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute $\omega_0 = 2\pi k_0/N$ (rational frequency)

For a periodic complex exponential:

$$\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} e^{-j(\omega - 2\pi k_0/N)n} = 2\pi \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta\!\left(\omega - \frac{2\pi k_0}{N} - 2\pi m\right)$$

### Step 3 — Final Result

$$\boxed{\; e^{j\omega_0 n} \xleftrightarrow{\text{DTFT}} 2\pi \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(\omega - \omega_0 - 2\pi m) \;}$$

## 3.3 ANALYTICAL MATRIX — Worked Examples

### Example 1 — DTFT of $x(n) = \cos(\omega_0 n)$ with $\omega_0 = \pi/2$ (Period $N = 4$)

**Step A — Expand using Euler's formula:**

$$x(n) = \frac{1}{2} e^{j\pi n/2} + \frac{1}{2} e^{-j\pi n/2}$$

**Step B — Apply DTFT linearity:**

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 2\pi \sum_{m} \delta(\omega - \pi/2 - 2\pi m) + \frac{1}{2} \cdot 2\pi \sum_{m} \delta(\omega + \pi/2 - 2\pi m)$$

**Step C — Simplify:**

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = \pi \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} \left[ \delta\!\left(\omega - \frac{\pi}{2} - 2\pi m\right) + \delta\!\left(\omega + \frac{\pi}{2} - 2\pi m\right) \right]$$

**Step D — Magnitude Spectrum:** Impulses of weight $\pi$ at $\omega = \pm \pi/2, \pm 3\pi/2, \ldots$ (and their $2\pi$-periodic replicas).

**Step E — Phase Spectrum:** Zero phase at all impulse locations (real positive weights).

### Example 2 — DTFT of Periodic Impulse Train $x(n) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(n - kN)$ (Period $N$)

**Step A — DTFS coefficients:** All coefficients equal $a_k = 1$ for all $k$ (sum of an impulse within one period is 1).

**Step B — Apply the periodic-DTFT formula:**

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{2\pi}{N} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta\!\left(\omega - \frac{2\pi k}{N}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Duality Insight:** The impulse train in time ↔ impulse train in frequency. The spacing in time ($N$) inverts to spacing ($\frac{2\pi}{N}$) in frequency, scaled by $\frac{2\pi}{N}$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Flow — DTFT Computation Pipeline for Periodic Sequences

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Periodic Sequence x(n)"] --> B["Identify Fundamental Period N"]
    B --> C["Compute DTFS Coefficients a_k"]
    C --> D{"Is a_k Finite?"}
    D -- "Yes" --> E["Apply Periodic DTFT Formula"]
    D -- "No / Decays" --> F["Use Direct DTFT Definition"]
    E --> G["X(e^jω) = 2π/N Σ a_k δ(ω − 2πk/N)"]
    F --> H["X(e^jω) = Σ x(n) e^(−jωn)"]
    G --> I["Magnitude Spectrum |X(e^jω)|"]
    G --> J["Phase Spectrum ∠X(e^jω)"]
    H --> I
    H --> J
    I --> K["Spectral Visualization"]
    J --> K
    K --> L["Engineering Application Output"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Schematic — Relationship Between Time-Domain and Frequency-Domain Representations

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1["x(n) Periodic\nPeriod = N"] -->|DTFT| Q1["X(e^jω) Impulse Train\nSpaced at 2π/N"]
    P2["a_k DTFS Coefficients\nk = 0, 1, ..., N-1"] -->|Weighting 2π/N| Q1
    P1 -->|DTFS Synthesis| P2
    Q1 -->|Sample over 1 Period| P2
    style1[style P1 fill:#4A90E2,stroke:#1B3A5C,color:#FFFFFF]
    style2[style Q1 fill:#E27D4A,stroke:#8B4513,color:#FFFFFF]
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Block Diagram — Frequency Spectrum Construction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TD["TIME DOMAIN"]
        T1["Discrete Sequence x(n)"]
    end
    subgraph FD["FREQUENCY DOMAIN"]
        F1["Magnitude |X(e^jω)|"]
        F2["Phase ∠X(e^jω)"]
    end
    T1 -->|DTFT Operation| F1
    T1 -->|DTFT Operation| F2
    F1 --> F3["Combined Spectrum Plot"]
    F2 --> F3
    style3[style T1 fill:#5BC0DE,stroke:#1F6F8B,color:#FFFFFF]
    style4[style F3 fill:#D9534F,stroke:#8B0000,color:#FFFFFF]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Visualization Note for Students:** When drawing spectra by hand, represent each impulse as a vertical arrow. The **height** of the arrow denotes $\frac{2\pi}{N} a_k$ (magnitude), and the **label** on the x-axis denotes the impulse location $\omega = \frac{2\pi k}{N}$.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**Q:** State the condition for the existence of DTFT of a sequence. Is the DTFT of a periodic sequence convergent in the usual sense?

**Model Answer:**
The DTFT converges in the **uniform sense** if and only if $x(n)$ is **absolutely summable**, i.e., $\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(n) \vert < \infty$. For a periodic sequence, this sum **diverges** because the sequence is non-decaying. Therefore, the DTFT of a periodic sequence does **not** exist as a regular function — it exists only in the **generalized function (distribution) sense** as a train of Dirac impulses.

*(3 marks — awarded for stating absolute summability, identifying divergence for periodic, and mentioning distribution sense.)*

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Q:** Define **magnitude spectrum** and **phase spectrum** of a discrete-time sequence.

**Model Answer:**
The **magnitude spectrum** is $\vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert$ and represents the amplitude of the frequency components. The **phase spectrum** is $\angle X(e^{j\omega}) = \arctan\!\left(\frac{\text{Im}\{X(e^{j\omega})\}}{\text{Re}\{X(e^{j\omega})\}}\right)$ and represents the phase shift of each frequency component. Both are **periodic** in $\omega$ with period $2\pi$.

*(3 marks — 1.5 marks each for correct definition with periodicity note.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Writing $X(e^{j\omega}) = \sum a_k \delta(\omega - 2\pi k/N)$ **without the $\frac{2\pi}{N}$ scaling factor** — this costs 2 marks.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Forgetting that DTFT is always **$2\pi$-periodic**; students often restrict the spectrum to $[-\pi, \pi]$ without mentioning replicas.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Confusing DTFS with DTFT — DTFS coefficients $a_k$ are the *weights* used in the DTFT impulse train formula, **not** the DTFT itself.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Not writing the **range of summation** explicitly (e.g., $k = 0$ to $N-1$ vs. $-\infty$ to $\infty$).

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Choice 1

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 2]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the DTFT of a periodic discrete-time sequence $x(n)$ with period $N$. Clearly state the meaning of each term. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the sequence $x(n) = 1 + 2\cos\!\left(\frac{\pi}{2}n\right)$, determine the DTFT and sketch the magnitude spectrum over $-\pi \leq \omega \leq \pi$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a) [7 marks]

**Given:** $x(n)$ is periodic with period $N$. By the DTFS synthesis equation:

$$x(n) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} a_k\, e^{j k \omega_0 n}, \quad \omega_0 = \frac{2\pi}{N}$$

**Step 1:** Apply the DTFT definition: $X(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(n)\, e^{-j\omega n}$

**Step 2:** Substitute the DTFS expansion:

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \left[ \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} a_k\, e^{j k \omega_0 n} \right] e^{-j\omega n}$$

**Step 3:** Swap the order of summation:

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} a_k \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} e^{-j(\omega - k\omega_0)n}$$

**Step 4:** Recognize the inner sum as the DTFT of a constant — an impulse train:

$$\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} e^{-j(\omega - k\omega_0)n} = 2\pi \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(\omega - k\omega_0 - 2\pi m)$$

**Step 5:** Substitute and combine:

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{2\pi}{N} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} a_k\, \delta\!\left(\omega - \frac{2\pi k}{N}\right)$$

**Where:**
- $a_k$ = DTFS coefficients (weights of each harmonic).
- $\frac{2\pi}{N}$ = scaling factor relating DTFS to DTFT.
- $\frac{2\pi k}{N}$ = impulse locations (harmonics).
- $N$ = fundamental period.
- $k$ = harmonic index (integer).

**Final boxed result:** $X(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{2\pi}{N} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} a_k\, \delta(\omega - \frac{2\pi k}{N})$ **(7 marks — 1 mark per key step + 2 for the final boxed formula and term meanings.)**

#### Model Solution — (b) [7 marks]

**Given:** $x(n) = 1 + 2\cos(\pi n/2)$

**Step 1 — Express as complex exponentials:**

$$\cos\!\left(\frac{\pi n}{2}\right) = \frac{1}{2} e^{j\pi n/2} + \frac{1}{2} e^{-j\pi n/2}$$

Therefore:

$$x(n) = 1 + e^{j\pi n/2} + e^{-j\pi n/2}$$

**Step 2 — Identify the period:** The frequencies involved are $\omega = 0, \pm\pi/2$. The fundamental frequency is $\omega_0 = \pi/2$, hence $N = 2\pi/(\pi/2) = 4$.

**Step 3 — Apply DTFT term by term:**

- DTFT of $1$ (i.e., $e^{j0 \cdot n}$): $\; 2\pi \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(\omega - 2\pi m)$
- DTFT of $e^{j\pi n/2}$: $\; 2\pi \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(\omega - \pi/2 - 2\pi m)$
- DTFT of $e^{-j\pi n/2}$: $\; 2\pi \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(\omega + \pi/2 - 2\pi m)$

**Step 4 — Combine by linearity:**

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = 2\pi \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} \Big[ \delta(\omega - 2\pi m) + \delta(\omega - \tfrac{\pi}{2} - 2\pi m) + \delta(\omega + \tfrac{\pi}{2} - 2\pi m) \Big]$$

**Step 5 — Magnitude spectrum over $-\pi \leq \omega \leq \pi$:**

$$\vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert = 2\pi \Big[ \delta(\omega) + \delta\!\left(\omega - \tfrac{\pi}{2}\right) + \delta\!\left(\omega + \tfrac{\pi}{2}\right) \Big]$$

**Sketch:** Three vertical arrows of equal height $2\pi$ located at $\omega = 0, \pi/2, -\pi/2$.

**(7 marks — 1.5 for expansion, 1 for period identification, 1.5 for individual DTFTs, 1.5 for combination, 1 for sketch.)**

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Choice 2 (Alternative)

**(a)** State and prove the **frequency-shifting property** of the DTFT. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compute the DTFT of the periodic impulse train $p(n) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(n - 4k)$ and identify the spacing between impulses in the frequency domain. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a) [7 marks]

**Statement:** If $x(n) \xleftrightarrow{\text{DTFT}} X(e^{j\omega})$, then

$$e^{j\omega_0 n}\, x(n) \xleftrightarrow{\text{DTFT}} X\!\left(e^{j(\omega - \omega_0)}\right)$$

**Proof:**

Let $y(n) = e^{j\omega_0 n}\, x(n)$. Its DTFT is:

$$Y(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} e^{j\omega_0 n}\, x(n)\, e^{-j\omega n}$$

$$= \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(n)\, e^{-j(\omega - \omega_0)n}$$

$$= X\!\left(e^{j(\omega - \omega_0)}\right) \quad \blacksquare$$

**Engineering Utility:** Modulation in the time domain (multiplying by a complex exponential) corresponds to a **frequency shift** in the frequency domain. This is the foundation of all digital communication modulation schemes (BPSK, QPSK, OFDM subcarrier mapping).

**(7 marks — 2 for statement, 4 for derivation, 1 for engineering utility.)**

#### Model Solution — (b) [7 marks]

**Given:** $p(n) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(n - 4k)$ — periodic impulse train with period $N = 4$.

**Step 1 — DTFS coefficients:** Within one period $[0, 3]$, the only non-zero value is $p(0) = 1$. Therefore:

$$a_k = \sum_{n=0}^{3} p(n)\, e^{-j 2\pi k n/4} = 1 \cdot e^{0} = 1 \quad \text{for all } k$$

**Step 2 — Apply periodic-DTFT formula:**

$$P(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{2\pi}{N} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} a_k\, \delta\!\left(\omega - \frac{2\pi k}{N}\right) = \frac{2\pi}{4} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta\!\left(\omega - \frac{\pi k}{2}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Simplify:**

$$\boxed{\; P(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{\pi}{2} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta\!\left(\omega - \frac{\pi k}{2}\right) \;}$$

**Step 4 — Impulse spacing in frequency:** $\Delta\omega = \frac{\pi}{2}$ (i.e., $90°$ per harmonic).

**Verification by duality:** A periodic impulse train in time (period $N$) yields a periodic impulse train in frequency (period $2\pi/N$) with weights $\frac{2\pi}{N}$ — exactly as derived.

**(7 marks — 2 for $a_k$ computation, 3 for formula application and simplification, 2 for spacing identification and duality note.)**

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Points — Must Memorize**

- ✅ DTFT exists for absolutely summable sequences in the classical sense; for **periodic sequences**, it exists **only as a distribution** (impulse train).
- ✅ The DTFT of a periodic sequence with period $N$ is: $X(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{2\pi}{N} \sum_k a_k \delta(\omega - \frac{2\pi k}{N})$.
- ✅ DTFT is **always periodic in $\omega$** with period $2\pi$ — this is a unique property of discrete-time analysis.
- ✅ **Time shifting** ↔ Phase factor $e^{-j\omega n_0}$; **Frequency shifting** ↔ Multiplication by $e^{j\omega_0 n}$.
- ✅ **Convolution in time** ↔ **Multiplication in frequency**; **Multiplication in time** ↔ **Periodic convolution in frequency**.
- ✅ **Parseval's theorem:** $\sum_n \vert x(n) \vert^2 = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} \vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert^2 d\omega$ — energy conservation between time and frequency domains.
- ✅ **Magnitude spectrum** $\vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert$ is **even** for real signals; **phase spectrum** is **odd**.
- ✅ A periodic impulse train in time (period $N$) produces a periodic impulse train in frequency (spacing $\frac{2\pi}{N}$, weight $\frac{2\pi}{N}$).
- ✅ DTFS coefficients $a_k$ are periodic with period $N$ — only $N$ of them are independent.
- ✅ **Always remember the $\frac{2\pi}{N}$ scaling** — this is the most frequently lost mark in KTU exams.
- ✅ Real periodic signals have **Hermitian-symmetric** spectra: $X(e^{-j\omega}) = X^*(e^{j\omega})$.
- ✅ The unit circle $|z| = 1$ in the Z-plane corresponds to the DTFT — the DTFT is the Z-transform evaluated on the unit circle.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bandwidth of Sequences
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST416 - SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Bandwidth of Sequences

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In Discrete-Time Signal Processing, the **Bandwidth of a Sequence** is formally defined as the **range of frequencies over which the signal's frequency-domain representation (Discrete-Time Fourier Transform, DTFT) contains significant energy or non-negligible magnitude**. It is the digital frequency interval $B = \omega_2 - \omega_1$ (in rad/sample) or $F_2 - F_1$ (in Hz/cycles per sample) bounded by limits $-\pi \le \omega \le \pi$ (the principal domain for real sequences), within which the spectral content of the discrete-time sequence $x[n]$ is considered to be concentrated.

For a sequence $x[n]$ with DTFT $X(e^{j\omega})$, the bandwidth is fundamentally characterized by examining $\vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert$, $\vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert^2$, or the energy/power spectral density.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECST416 Module 2):** Bandwidth of sequences is a foundational precursor to filter design, sampling theorem, and multi-rate signal processing. It directly supports Course Outcomes **CO2** (Analyze the characteristics of LTI systems using DTFT/DTFS) and **CO3** (Apply the concepts of frequency domain analysis in discrete-time systems).

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Think of a **musical audio file** (a discrete-time sequence obtained by sampling a song at 44.1 kHz). The song contains:

- **Bass frequencies** (low kick drum, ~20 Hz – 250 Hz) → these are "slow" samples that change gradually.
- **Mid-range frequencies** (vocals, ~250 Hz – 4 kHz).
- **Treble frequencies** (cymbals, high-hats, ~4 kHz – 20 kHz) → these are "fast" samples requiring rapid changes.

The **bandwidth** of a particular audio signal is simply the **slice of the audible spectrum it actually uses**. A phone call uses only ~300 Hz – 3.4 kHz (bandwidth ≈ 3.1 kHz), while a high-fidelity music recording uses ~20 Hz – 20 kHz (bandwidth ≈ 19.98 kHz). The wider the bandwidth, the richer and more detailed the signal, but the more storage/transmission capacity it demands.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** In discrete-time systems, the *maximum possible* bandwidth is bounded by $\pi$ rad/sample (i.e., the Nyquist frequency). Any sequence's bandwidth is therefore $0 \le B \le \pi$.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Normalized Digital Frequency Range:** $\omega \in [-\pi, \pi]$ rad/sample (equivalently, $F \in [-0.5, 0.5]$ cycles/sample).
- **Nyquist Frequency:** $\omega_N = \pi$ rad/sample (half the sampling rate in analog terms).
- **Physical (Analog) Bandwidth:** $B_{\text{analog}} = B_{\text{digital}} \times f_s$ Hz, where $f_s$ is the sampling frequency.
- **Units of Measurement:**
  - $\omega$-domain: **radians per sample** (rad/sample)
  - $F$-domain: **cycles per sample** (or normalized Hz, dimensionless)
  - Analog-domain: **Hertz (Hz)**

### 1.4 Classification of Sequences by Bandwidth Type

| Category | Frequency Support | Bandwidth |
|---|---|---|
| **Lowpass Sequence** | $\vert \omega \vert \le \omega_c$ | $B = \omega_c$ |
| **Highpass Sequence** | $\omega_c \le \vert \omega \vert \le \pi$ | $B = \pi - \omega_c$ |
| **Bandpass Sequence** | $\omega_1 \le \vert \omega \vert \le \omega_2$ | $B = \omega_2 - \omega_1$ |
| **Bandstop / Notch Sequence** | Excludes a band $[\omega_1, \omega_2]$ | Defined by stopband edges |
| **Allpass (Ideal Impulse)** | All $\omega \in [-\pi, \pi]$ | $B = 2\pi$ (in periodic sense) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation: Energy and Power in Discrete-Time Signals

A discrete-time sequence $x[n]$ may be characterized as either an **Energy Signal** or a **Power Signal**, and this classification fundamentally affects how bandwidth is computed.

**Total Energy of a Sequence:**

$$E = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n] \vert^2$$

**Total Average Power of a Sequence:**

$$P = \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{1}{2N+1} \sum_{n=-N}^{N} \vert x[n] \vert^2$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Matters:** Bandwidth is essentially a measure of *where* the energy (or power) of a signal is concentrated in the frequency domain. To compute the bandwidth precisely, we need to first determine the **Energy Spectral Density (ESD)** or **Power Spectral Density (PSD)**.

### 2.2 Energy Spectral Density (ESD) — Parseval's Theorem for Sequences

For a finite-energy sequence, **Parseval's Theorem** states that the total energy computed in the time domain equals the total energy computed in the frequency domain:

$$E = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n] \vert^2 = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} \vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert^2 \, d\omega$$

The function $\vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert^2$ is the **Energy Spectral Density (ESD)** — it tells us how the energy is distributed across frequencies.

### 2.3 Power Spectral Density (PSD)

For a finite-power sequence (e.g., periodic signals), the **Power Spectral Density** is given by:

$$P_{xx}(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert a_k \vert^2 \delta(\omega - k\omega_0)$$

The total average power is:

$$P = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} P_{xx}(e^{j\omega}) \, d\omega$$

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | DTFT of a sequence | $X(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] e^{-j\omega n}$ | $\omega \in [-\pi, \pi]$ |
| 2 | Inverse DTFT | $x[n] = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} X(e^{j\omega}) e^{j\omega n} \, d\omega$ | — |
| 3 | Total Energy | $E = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n] \vert^2$ | Joules (in equivalent analog) |
| 4 | Parseval's Relation | $E = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} \vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert^2 \, d\omega$ | Energy equivalence |
| 5 | ESD | $S_{xx}(e^{j\omega}) = \vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert^2$ | Watts/Hz equivalent |
| 6 | Average Power | $P = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} \vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert^2 \, d\omega$ | For power signals |
| 7 | **3-dB Bandwidth** | Frequency at which $\vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert^2$ drops to **half** its peak | $P_{\text{3dB}} = 0.5 \times P_{\text{max}}$ |
| 8 | **Null-to-Null Bandwidth** | Distance between first zero crossings around the main lobe | $B = 2\omega_1$ (symmetric) |
