| 9 | **RMS Bandwidth** | $B_{\text{rms}} = \sqrt{\frac{\int_{-\pi}^{\pi} \omega^2 \vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert^2 d\omega}{\int_{-\pi}^{\pi} \vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert^2 d\omega}}$ | Statistical measure |
| 10 | **Fractional Power Bandwidth** | $\frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\omega_0}^{\omega_0} \vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert^2 d\omega = \alpha P$ | $\alpha$ = power fraction (e.g., 0.95) |
| 11 | Lowpass Bandwidth | $B = \omega_c$ where $\vert X(e^{j\omega_c}) \vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \vert X(0) \vert$ | rad/sample |
| 12 | Analog Bandwidth | $B_{\text{Hz}} = B_{\text{rad/sample}} \times \frac{f_s}{2\pi}$ | Hz |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Notational Tip:** In all table entries above, the magnitude $\vert \cdot \vert$ uses LaTeX `\vert` to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. The exam valuation expects $\vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert$ for magnitude.

### 2.5 Definitions of Bandwidth (KTU High-Yield)

There are **four primary engineering definitions** of bandwidth for discrete-time sequences:

#### (A) 3-dB Bandwidth (Half-Power Bandwidth)
The frequency $\omega_c$ at which the squared magnitude of the spectrum drops to **half** of its maximum (i.e., the power falls to half):

$$\vert X(e^{j\omega_c}) \vert^2 = \frac{1}{2} \vert X(e^{j0}) \vert^2$$

Or equivalently: $\vert X(e^{j\omega_c}) \vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \vert X(e^{j0}) \vert$ (≈ 0.7071 of peak amplitude).

#### (B) Null-to-Null Bandwidth
The width of the **main lobe** of $\vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert$, measured between the first zero crossings on either side of the peak. Most common for signals with sinc-shaped spectra (e.g., rectangular pulses).

#### (C) RMS Bandwidth
A statistical measure of spectral spread, analogous to standard deviation in probability:

$$B_{\text{rms}}^2 = \frac{\int_{-\pi}^{\pi} \omega^2 \vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert^2 d\omega}{\int_{-\pi}^{\pi} \vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert^2 d\omega}$$

#### (D) Fractional Power Bandwidth
The smallest interval $[-\omega_0, \omega_0]$ containing a specified fraction (e.g., 95%, 99%) of the total signal power. This is the most rigorous definition but requires numerical computation.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application of Bandwidth Concept |
|---|---|
| **Telecommunications** | Channel allocation, link budget, Shannon capacity $C = B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$ |
| **Audio Engineering** | MP3/AAC codec design; high-fidelity audio requires 20 kHz analog bandwidth |
| **Radar / SONAR** | Range resolution $\Delta R = c / (2B)$ — wider bandwidth means finer range resolution |
| **Biomedical (ECG/EEG)** | Detecting pathological signals; typical ECG bandwidth ≈ 0.05–150 Hz |
| **Image Processing** | Each row of an image is a 1-D sequence whose bandwidth determines sharpness |
| **Wireless (4G/5G)** | LTE channel bandwidths: 1.4, 3, 5, 10, 15, 20 MHz |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering "Why":** Bandwidth is the **currency of information**. A system with larger bandwidth can transmit more data per second. Every DSP filter, every communication channel, every storage medium is fundamentally constrained by its bandwidth.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Parseval's Theorem for Discrete-Time Sequences (Energy Equivalence)

**Goal:** Show that the energy computed in the time domain equals the energy computed in the frequency domain.

**Starting Point:** We begin with the inverse DTFT of $X(e^{j\omega})$:

$$x[n] = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} X(e^{j\omega}) e^{j\omega n} \, d\omega$$

**Step 1:** Compute the squared magnitude $\vert x[n] \vert^2$. Since $x[n]$ is generally complex, $\vert x[n] \vert^2 = x[n] \cdot x^*[n]$:

$$\vert x[n] \vert^2 = x[n] \cdot x^*[n] = \left[ \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} X(e^{j\omega}) e^{j\omega n} d\omega \right] \cdot \left[ \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} X^*(e^{j\theta}) e^{-j\theta n} d\theta \right]$$

**Step 2:** Substitute this into the total energy expression $E = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n] \vert^2$:

$$E = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \left[ \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} X(e^{j\omega}) e^{j\omega n} d\omega \right] \cdot \left[ \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} X^*(e^{j\theta}) e^{-j\theta n} d\theta \right]$$

**Step 3:** Swap the order of summation and integration (justified by Fubini's theorem since $x[n]$ is absolutely summable for finite-energy signals):

$$E = \frac{1}{(2\pi)^2} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} X(e^{j\omega}) X^*(e^{j\theta}) \left[ \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} e^{j(\omega - \theta) n} \right] d\omega \, d\theta$$

**Step 4:** Recognize the inner summation as the **discrete-time counterpart of the Dirac delta**:

$$\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} e^{j(\omega - \theta) n} = 2\pi \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(\omega - \theta - 2\pi k)$$

**Step 5:** Substitute the impulse train back into the integral. The integration over $\theta$ from $-\pi$ to $\pi$ captures exactly one impulse (since $\omega \in [-\pi, \pi]$), giving $\theta = \omega$:

$$E = \frac{1}{(2\pi)^2} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} X(e^{j\omega}) X^*(e^{j\omega}) \cdot 2\pi \, d\omega$$

**Step 6:** Simplify to obtain **Parseval's Theorem** for sequences:

$$E = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} \vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert^2 \, d\omega$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** This proves the energy equivalence between the time domain and the frequency domain. The integrand $\vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert^2$ is the **Energy Spectral Density (ESD)**, and integrating it over $[-\pi, \pi]$ yields the total energy.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Bandwidth of a Rectangular Pulse Sequence

**Problem:** Consider a finite-length sequence $x[n] = 1$ for $-N \le n \le N$, and $x[n] = 0$ elsewhere. Compute its bandwidth.

**Step 1:** Find the DTFT of the rectangular pulse.

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=-N}^{N} e^{-j\omega n}$$

**Step 2:** Factor out the term $e^{j\omega N}$ from the lower bound to make the sum symmetric:

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = e^{j\omega N} \sum_{n=0}^{2N} e^{-j\omega n}$$

**Step 3:** Use the finite geometric series formula $\sum_{k=0}^{M-1} r^k = \frac{1 - r^M}{1 - r}$ with $M = 2N + 1$ and $r = e^{-j\omega}$:

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = e^{j\omega N} \cdot \frac{1 - e^{-j\omega(2N+1)}}{1 - e^{-j\omega}}$$

**Step 4:** Multiply numerator and denominator by $e^{-j\omega(N + 0.5)}$ to obtain a centered, real-valued form:

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{\sin\left[\omega\left(N + \frac{1}{2}\right)\right]}{\sin\left(\frac{\omega}{2}\right)}$$

**Step 5:** Identify the **magnitude spectrum**:

$$\vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert = \left\vert \frac{\sin\left[\omega\left(N + \frac{1}{2}\right)\right]}{\sin\left(\frac{\omega}{2}\right)} \right\vert$$

**Step 6:** Locate the **first zero crossing** (null) of the magnitude spectrum. Zeros occur when the numerator vanishes but the denominator does not:

$$\omega\left(N + \frac{1}{2}\right) = \pm \pi \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega = \pm \frac{\pi}{N + \frac{1}{2}}$$

**Step 7:** Compute the **Null-to-Null Bandwidth**:

$$B_{\text{null-to-null}} = 2 \times \frac{\pi}{N + \frac{1}{2}} = \frac{2\pi}{N + 0.5}$$

**Step 8:** Approximate for large $N$ (a common KTU exam shortcut):

$$B_{\text{null-to-null}} \approx \frac{2\pi}{N} \quad \text{for } N \gg 1$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** As $N \to \infty$ (infinite-length rectangular pulse), the bandwidth $B \to 0$, but the signal becomes a constant DC component (which makes sense — a constant has zero frequency content). Conversely, as $N \to 1$ (very short pulse), $B \to 2\pi$, meaning the signal contains all frequencies.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: 3-dB Bandwidth of an Ideal Discrete-Time Lowpass Filter

**Problem:** An ideal lowpass filter has magnitude response $\vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert = 1$ for $\vert \omega \vert \le \omega_c$ and $\vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert = 0$ for $\omega_c < \vert \omega \vert \le \pi$. Find the 3-dB bandwidth.

**Step 1:** Identify the peak (maximum) magnitude:

$$\vert H(e^{j0}) \vert = 1$$

**Step 2:** The 3-dB point occurs where the magnitude squared drops to half:

$$\vert H(e^{j\omega_c}) \vert^2 = \frac{1}{2} \vert H(e^{j0}) \vert^2 = 0.5$$

**Step 3:** For the ideal filter, $\vert H(e^{j\omega_c}) \vert^2 = 1$ (at the cutoff, magnitude is still 1 for ideal case), so the 3-dB bandwidth equals the cutoff frequency:

$$B_{\text{3dB}} = \omega_c \text{ rad/sample}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For non-ideal (realizable) filters, the 3-dB bandwidth is slightly *less* than the nominal cutoff frequency due to the roll-off in the transition band.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Bandwidth Comparison of Common Sequences

| Sequence $x[n]$ | DTFT $X(e^{j\omega})$ | Spectrum Type | Null-to-Null BW | 3-dB BW |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $\delta[n]$ (unit impulse) | $1$ for all $\omega$ | Flat | $2\pi$ | $2\pi$ |
| Constant $1$ | $2\pi \delta(\omega)$ | DC line | $0$ | $0$ |
| $e^{j\omega_0 n}$ | $2\pi \delta(\omega - \omega_0)$ | Single line | $0$ | $0$ |
| Rectangular pulse (length $2N+1$) | $\frac{\sin[\omega(N+0.5)]}{\sin(\omega/2)}$ | Sinc-like | $\frac{2\pi}{N+0.5}$ | $\approx \frac{0.886 \cdot 2\pi}{N}$ |
| Two-sided exponential $a^{\vert n\vert}$ | $\frac{1 - a^2}{1 - 2a\cos\omega + a^2}$ | Lowpass (Lorentzian) | $\approx 2\sqrt{1 - a}$ for $a \approx 1$ | Depends on $a$ |
| Single-frequency cosine $\cos(\omega_0 n)$ | $\pi[\delta(\omega - \omega_0) + \delta(\omega + \omega_0)]$ | Two lines | $0$ | $0$ |
| Chirp / FM signal | Time-varying spectrum | Wide | $B \approx \mu N$ | — |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Classification of Sequences by Bandwidth

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Discrete-Time Sequence x of n"] --> B{"Finite Energy E less than infinity"}
    B -- Yes --> C["Energy Signal"]
    B -- No --> D{"Finite Power P between 0 and infinity"}
    D -- Yes --> E["Power Signal"]
    D -- No --> F["Neither Energy nor Power"]

    C --> G["Compute DTFT X of e raised to j omega"]
    E --> H["Compute DTFS or use PSD"]

    G --> I{"Shape of magnitude spectrum"}
    H --> I

    I --> J["Lowpass: support in omega less than omega_c"]
    I --> K["Highpass: support in omega greater than omega_c"]
    I --> L["Bandpass: support in omega_1 to omega_2"]
    I --> M["Allpass: support entire range minus pi to pi"]

    J --> N["Bandwidth equals omega_c"]
    K --> O["Bandwidth equals pi minus omega_c"]
    L --> P["Bandwidth equals omega_2 minus omega_1"]
    M --> Q["Bandwidth equals 2 pi"]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Architecture: Bandwidth Computation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Input["Input Sequence x of n"] --> Step1["Step 1: Compute DTFT X of e raised to j omega"]
    Step1 --> Step2["Step 2: Find Magnitude Spectrum magnitude of X"]
    Step2 --> Step3["Step 3: Choose Bandwidth Definition"]
    Step3 --> Def1["3 dB BW: Find omega where magnitude squared drops to half peak"]
    Step3 --> Def2["Null to Null BW: Measure main lobe width"]
    Step3 --> Def3["RMS BW: Use second moment formula"]
    Step3 --> Def4["Fractional Power BW: Find smallest interval containing 95 percent power"]
    Def1 --> Output["Output: Bandwidth B in rad per sample"]
    Def2 --> Output
    Def3 --> Output
    Def4 --> Output
    Output --> Convert["Convert to Hz using B_Hz equals B times fs over 2 pi"]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Spectrum of an Ideal Lowpass Discrete-Time Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Frequency_Domain
    direction LR
    A1["omega equal to minus pi: 0"] --> A2["omega from minus omega_c to omega_c: 1"]
    A2 --> A3["omega equal to omega_c: transition"] --> A4["omega greater than omega_c: 0"]
    A4 --> A5["omega equal to pi: 0"]
    end
    subgraph Bandwidth_Region
    direction LR
    B1["Stopband: magnitude equals 0"] --> B2["Passband: magnitude equals 1"] --> B3["Stopband: magnitude equals 0"]
    end
    Frequency_Domain --> Bandwidth_Region
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Bandwidth Categories in Filter Specifications

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Spec["Filter Specification"] --> Pass["Passband: 0 to omega_p, ripple less than delta_p"]
    Spec --> Trans["Transition Band: omega_p to omega_s"]
    Spec --> Stop["Stopband: omega_s to pi, attenuation greater than A_s"]
    Pass --> B1["Passband Edge omega_p"]
    Trans --> B2["Transition Width: omega_s minus omega_p"]
    Stop --> B3["Stopband Edge omega_s"]
    B1 --> Result["Effective Bandwidth approximately omega_p to omega_s"]
    B2 --> Result
    B3 --> Result
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** In practical filter design, the "bandwidth" is not a single sharp number but a *specification region* bounded by the passband edge, transition band width, and stopband edge. KTU exam problems often test this distinction between *ideal* and *practical* bandwidths.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A: 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer / Definition Type)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

Define the term **"bandwidth of a sequence"**. Mention any two commonly used definitions of bandwidth in discrete-time signal processing.

**Model Answer:**

The bandwidth of a sequence is the range of frequencies over which its Discrete-Time Fourier Transform (DTFT) has significant magnitude or energy. It quantifies the spectral extent of a discrete-time signal and is bounded by $0 \le B \le \pi$ rad/sample (for lowpass-type sequences).

**Two commonly used definitions:**

1. **3-dB Bandwidth (Half-Power Bandwidth):** The frequency $\omega_c$ at which the squared magnitude of the DTFT drops to half of its peak value, i.e., $\vert X(e^{j\omega_c}) \vert^2 = \frac{1}{2} \vert X(e^{j0}) \vert^2$.

2. **Null-to-Null Bandwidth:** The width of the main lobe of the magnitude spectrum, measured between the first zero crossings on either side of the peak frequency.

*(2 marks for definition + 1 mark for naming two types with one-line description.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

State **Parseval's theorem for discrete-time sequences** and explain its significance in computing the bandwidth of a sequence.

**Model Answer:**

Parseval's theorem for discrete-time sequences relates the energy in the time domain to the energy in the frequency domain:

$$E = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n] \vert^2 = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} \vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert^2 \, d\omega$$

**Significance in bandwidth computation:** The function $\vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert^2$ is the **Energy Spectral Density (ESD)**. It allows us to determine the fraction of total signal energy contained within any frequency interval $[\omega_1, \omega_2]$. This is the basis of the **fractional-power bandwidth** definition: we find the smallest interval $[-\omega_0, \omega_0]$ that contains a specified fraction (e.g., 95% or 99%) of the total energy.

*(1.5 marks for the formula + 1.5 marks for the significance/explanation.)*

---

### Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Full Descriptive — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Question]**

**(a)** For the discrete-time rectangular pulse $x[n] = 1$ for $-N \le n \le N$ and $x[n] = 0$ otherwise, derive the expression for its DTFT and compute the **null-to-null bandwidth**. Discuss how the bandwidth varies with $N$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A sequence has Energy Spectral Density $S(e^{j\omega}) = 1$ for $\vert \omega \vert \le 0.4\pi$ and $S(e^{j\omega}) = 0$ for $0.4\pi < \vert \omega \vert \le \pi$. Compute the total energy and find the **fractional power bandwidth** that contains 90% of the total energy. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a):

**Step 1: Write the DTFT of the rectangular pulse.**

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=-N}^{N} e^{-j\omega n} = \sum_{n=-N}^{N} 1 \cdot e^{-j\omega n}$$

**Step 2: Apply the geometric series formula.**

Using $\sum_{n=-N}^{N} e^{-j\omega n} = e^{j\omega N} \cdot \frac{1 - e^{-j\omega(2N+1)}}{1 - e^{-j\omega}}$ and simplifying:

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{\sin\left[\omega\left(N + \frac{1}{2}\right)\right]}{\sin\left(\frac{\omega}{2}\right)}$$

**Step 3: Find the first zeros of the magnitude spectrum.**

The magnitude is zero whenever $\sin[\omega(N + 0.5)] = 0$ but $\sin(\omega/2) \neq 0$:

$$\omega(N + 0.5) = \pm \pi \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega = \pm \frac{\pi}{N + 0.5}$$

**Step 4: Compute the null-to-null bandwidth.**

$$B = 2 \times \frac{\pi}{N + 0.5} = \frac{2\pi}{N + 0.5}$$

**Step 5: Discuss the variation with $N$.**

- For $N = 1$ (length-3 pulse): $B = \frac{2\pi}{1.5} \approx 1.333\pi$ — very wide bandwidth.
- For $N = 10$ (length-21 pulse): $B = \frac{2\pi}{10.5} \approx 0.190\pi$ — narrow bandwidth.
- As $N \to \infty$: $B \to 0$ — the signal becomes a constant DC level.
- **Conclusion:** Bandwidth is **inversely proportional** to the pulse duration — a fundamental time-frequency duality in DSP.

#### Model Solution — Part (b):

**Step 1: Compute the total energy using Parseval's theorem.**

$$E = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} S(e^{j\omega}) \, d\omega = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-0.4\pi}^{0.4\pi} 1 \, d\omega$$

$$E = \frac{1}{2\pi} \cdot 0.8\pi = 0.4 \text{ (normalized units)}$$

**Step 2: Set up the equation for 90% energy containment.**

The 90% energy condition:

$$\frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\omega_0}^{\omega_0} 1 \, d\omega = 0.9 \times 0.4 = 0.36$$

**Step 3: Solve for $\omega_0$.**

$$\frac{1}{2\pi} \cdot 2\omega_0 = 0.36 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega_0 = 0.36\pi$$

**Step 4: Report the fractional power bandwidth.**

$$B_{90\%} = 2\omega_0 = 0.72\pi \text{ rad/sample}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning #1:** Many students forget to multiply by 2 for the *two-sided* bandwidth and report only $0.36\pi$ instead of $0.72\pi$. Always remember that the bandwidth is the *width* of the interval, not its endpoint.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning #2:** When the ESD is constant over a band (as in this problem), students often incorrectly integrate over the full $[-\pi, \pi]$ range instead of the actual support of the spectrum. **Read the problem carefully** — $S(e^{j\omega}) = 0$ outside the support matters.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**(a)** Define the **RMS bandwidth** of a sequence. For a sequence with ESD $S(e^{j\omega}) = 2 \cdot \text{rect}(\omega / 0.5\pi)$ (a rectangular passband of width $0.5\pi$ centered at $\omega = 0$), compute the RMS bandwidth. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Differentiate clearly between **3-dB bandwidth**, **null-to-null bandwidth**, and **fractional-power bandwidth** for a discrete-time sequence. Give one example for each. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a):

**Step 1: Definition of RMS Bandwidth.**

The RMS (Root Mean Square) bandwidth is a statistical measure of spectral spread, defined as:

$$B_{\text{rms}}^2 = \frac{\int_{-\pi}^{\pi} \omega^2 \vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert^2 d\omega}{\int_{-\pi}^{\pi} \vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert^2 d\omega}$$

For a zero-mean signal (centered spectrum), this is analogous to the standard deviation of the energy distribution in the frequency domain.

**Step 2: Identify the support and ESD of the given sequence.**

The ESD is $S(e^{j\omega}) = 2$ for $\vert \omega \vert \le 0.25\pi$ and $S(e^{j\omega}) = 0$ otherwise.

**Step 3: Compute the denominator (total energy).**

$$\int_{-\pi}^{\pi} S(e^{j\omega}) d\omega = \int_{-0.25\pi}^{0.25\pi} 2 \, d\omega = 2 \cdot 0.5\pi = \pi$$

**Step 4: Compute the numerator (second moment).**

$$\int_{-\pi}^{\pi} \omega^2 S(e^{j\omega}) d\omega = \int_{-0.25\pi}^{0.25\pi} 2\omega^2 \, d\omega = 2 \cdot \left[ \frac{\omega^3}{3} \right]_{-0.25\pi}^{0.25\pi}$$

$$= 2 \cdot \frac{2 (0.25\pi)^3}{3} = \frac{4 \cdot (0.015625 \pi^3)}{3} = \frac{0.0625 \pi^3}{3} = \frac{\pi^3}{48}$$

**Step 5: Compute $B_{\text{rms}}$.**

$$B_{\text{rms}}^2 = \frac{\pi^3 / 48}{\pi} = \frac{\pi^2}{48}$$

$$B_{\text{rms}} = \frac{\pi}{\sqrt{48}} = \frac{\pi}{4\sqrt{3}} \approx 0.1443\pi \text{ rad/sample}$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b):

| Bandwidth Type | Definition | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **3-dB Bandwidth** | Frequency at which $\vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert^2$ drops to **half** of its peak value. | A lowpass filter with peak gain 1: 3-dB BW = $\omega_c$ where gain = $0.707$. |
| **Null-to-Null Bandwidth** | Width of the **main lobe** of $\vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert$ between the first two zero crossings. | Rectangular pulse of length $2N+1$: $B = \frac{2\pi}{N + 0.5}$. |
| **Fractional-Power Bandwidth** | Smallest interval containing a specified fraction (e.g., 90%, 95%) of total signal power/energy. | An ESD $S(e^{j\omega}) = 1$ over $\vert \omega \vert \le 0.4\pi$: 90% energy BW = $0.72\pi$. |

**Distinguishing Features:**

- The **3-dB BW** is amplitude-based and simple to measure experimentally.
- The **Null-to-Null BW** is geometric, suitable for signals with clear main lobes.
- The **Fractional-Power BW** is energy-based, most rigorous, but requires numerical integration.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning #3:** For RMS bandwidth, students often forget to **square** the result or forget to **subtract the squared mean frequency** (for non-zero-center spectra). Always use the centered formula $B_{\text{rms}}^2 = \int (\omega - \mu_\omega)^2 S(\omega) d\omega / \int S(\omega) d\omega$ where $\mu_\omega$ is the mean frequency.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning #4:** A common mistake in Part (b) is to give only *formulas* without providing a *concrete example*. KTU valuations explicitly award 1–2 marks for the example column. Always include a numerical example.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Must-Know Definitions (2-mark weightage questions):**
> - Bandwidth = spectral extent of a sequence's DTFT, bounded by $[-\pi, \pi]$.
> - Energy signal: $0 < E < \infty$, $P = 0$.
> - Power signal: $0 < P < \infty$, $E = \infty$.
> - Energy Spectral Density (ESD): $S(e^{j\omega}) = \vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert^2$.
> - Power Spectral Density (PSD): $P_{xx}(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_k \vert a_k \vert^2 \delta(\omega - k\omega_0)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Must-Know Formulas (carry into exam hall):**
> - DTFT: $X(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_n x[n] e^{-j\omega n}$.
> - Parseval: $E = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} \vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert^2 d\omega$.
> - Rectangular pulse (length $2N+1$) DTFT: $\frac{\sin[\omega(N+0.5)]}{\sin(\omega/2)}$.
> - Rectangular pulse null-to-null BW: $\frac{2\pi}{N+0.5}$.
> - 3-dB point: $\vert X(\omega_c) \vert^2 = 0.5 \cdot \vert X(0) \vert^2$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Time-Frequency Duality (Frequently Asked):**
> - **Shorter time-domain signal** (smaller $N$) $\Rightarrow$ **wider bandwidth**.
> - **Longer time-domain signal** (larger $N$) $\Rightarrow$ **narrower bandwidth**.
> - Product of time-duration and bandwidth is approximately constant (uncertainty principle).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bandwidth Conversion (most common error):**
> - $B_{\text{Hz}} = B_{\text{rad/sample}} \times \frac{f_s}{2\pi}$ (note the $2\pi$).
> - $B_{\text{Hz}} = B_{\text{cycles/sample}} \times f_s$ (simpler form).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common Exam Traps:**
> 1. Confusing *one-sided* bandwidth (just $\omega_c$) with *two-sided* bandwidth ($2\omega_c$).
> 2. Forgetting that real sequences have *symmetric* spectra about $\omega = 0$.
> 3. Reporting the 3-dB frequency $\omega_c$ instead of the bandwidth $B = \omega_c$ (lowpass case).
> 4. Using the wrong form of Parseval's theorem (energy vs. power versions).
> 5. Treating the *analog* bandwidth and *digital* bandwidth as identical.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Module-2 Connection Map:**
> - **Module 2 focus:** Frequency analysis of discrete-time signals.
> - **Predecessor topic:** DTFT, properties, sampling theorem.
> - **Successor topic:** Digital filter design, LTI system analysis, multi-rate systems.
> - **Bandwidth** acts as the **bridge** between time-domain features (pulse width, decay rate) and frequency-domain features (spectral spread, filter selectivity).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Energy Density Spectra
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST416 - SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Energy Density Spectra # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the rigorous framework of **Signals and Systems** as prescribed by the KTU 2024 Scheme, an **Energy Signal** is defined as a signal $x(t)$ (continuous-time) or $x[n]$ (discrete-time) for which the total normalized energy is finite and non-zero, i.e., $0 < E < \infty$. The **Energy Density Spectrum (EDS)**, also equivalently called the **Energy Spectral Density (ESD)** or **Energy Spectrum**, is a frequency-domain function that quantifies *how the total signal energy is distributed as a function of frequency* $\omega$ (or $f$). Formally, for a continuous-time energy signal $x(t)$, the Energy Density Spectrum $\Psi_x(\omega)$ is mathematically defined as the **squared magnitude of the Continuous-Time Fourier Transform (CTFT)** of the signal: $$\Psi_x(\omega) \triangleq \left| X(\omega) \right|^2 = X(\omega) \cdot X^*(\omega)$$ where: - $X(\omega) = \mathcal{F}\{x(t)\} = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t)\, e^{-j\omega t}\, dt$ is the Fourier Transform of $x(t)$. - $X^*(\omega)$ denotes the **complex conjugate** of $X(\omega)$. The corresponding discrete-time expression is: $$\Psi_x(\Omega) = \left| X(e^{j\Omega}) \right|^2$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Energy Density Spectrum is a **non-negative**, **real-valued**, and **even function** of $\omega$ (i.e., $\Psi_x(\omega) = \Psi_x(-\omega)$). It is measured in units of **Joules per radian per second** ($\text{J}\cdot\text{s/rad}$). --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain English Intuition Imagine you walk into a sweet shop with a mixed box of assorted sweets (chocolates, toffees, jalebis). The **total weight of the box** represents the **total energy** of a signal — a single scalar number that says nothing about *what kinds* of sweets are inside. Now, suppose you sort every sweet by its **type** (chocolate, toffee, etc.) and weigh each pile separately. The **weight per sweet-type** is analogous to the **Energy Density Spectrum** — it tells you exactly **how much energy is sitting at each frequency band**. - **Total Energy** $E$ $\longrightarrow$ Total weight of all sweets. - **Energy Density Spectrum** $\Psi_x(\omega)$ $\longrightarrow$ Weight contributed *per unit frequency interval* around $\omega$. So, the EDS is essentially a **frequency-wise breakup of the energy** of a signal. > [!NOTE] > **Intuition Recap:** Fourier Transform gives *amplitude per frequency*; Energy Density Spectrum gives *energy per frequency*. Squaring the magnitude is what converts amplitude into energy. --- ## 1.3 Why is Energy Density Spectra Important? The EDS is crucial because it allows engineers to: 1. **Analyze the spectral content** of finite-energy signals (e.g., a radar pulse, a speech utterance, a biomedical ECG signal). 2. **Filter design verification** — to check whether a filter passes the desired energy bands. 3. **Signal classification** — distinguish narrowband vs. broadband signals. 4. **Noise characterization** — quantify noise power per Hz in communication channels. > [!IMPORTANT] > The Energy Density Spectrum is the **bridge between the time-domain energy integral and the frequency domain**, made precise by **Parseval's (Rayleigh's) Energy Theorem**. # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown The construction of the Energy Density Spectrum involves a logical, four-step pipeline that every KTU student must internalize: ### Step 1 — Verify the Signal is an Energy Signal Confirm that the signal $x(t)$ has finite, non-zero energy: $$E_x = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \left| x(t) \right|^2 dt < \infty$$ If this integral diverges, the signal is a **Power Signal**, not an Energy Signal, and EDS does **not** apply (Power Spectral Density applies instead). ### Step 2 — Compute the Fourier Transform Apply the CTFT to obtain the complex-valued frequency representation: $$X(\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t)\, e^{-j\omega t}\, dt$$ This produces a (generally) complex function containing both **amplitude spectrum** $\vert X(\omega) \vert$ and **phase spectrum** $\angle X(\omega)$. ### Step 3 — Discard Phase and Square the Magnitude The phase information is intentionally **thrown away** because: - The EDS represents **real, physical energy** — energy has no notion of "angle." - Multiplying $X(\omega)$ by its own conjugate $X^*(\omega)$ ensures a **real, non-negative** quantity: $$\Psi_x(\omega) = X(\omega)\, X^*(\omega) = \left| X(\omega) \right|^2$$ ### Step 4 — Integrate to Recover Total Energy The total energy is recovered by integrating the EDS over all frequencies, scaled by $\dfrac{1}{2\pi}$ (from Parseval's theorem): $$E_x = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \Psi_x(\omega)\, d\omega$$ --- ## 2.2 Why These Steps? (The "Why & How") | Logical Step | Why It Is Done | Engineering Justification | |---|---|---| | Verify finite energy | EDS only meaningful for energy signals | Power signals require PSD instead | | Take Fourier Transform | Decompose signal into orthogonal sinusoids | Sinusoids are eigenfunctions of LTI systems | | Drop the phase | Energy is a scalar, not directional | Phase is preserved separately in $X(\omega)$ | | Square the magnitude | Energy ∝ amplitude² in physics | Voltage across resistor $P = V^2/R$ | --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mastery Mandate:** Every formula below is a high-yield item for KTU End Semester Examinations. Memorize the column **Expression** and the corresponding **Units**. | # | Concept | Continuous-Time Expression | Discrete-Time Expression | Units | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Energy of signal | $E = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^2 dt$ | $E = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n] \vert^2$ | Joules | | 2 | Fourier Transform | $X(\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) e^{-j\omega t} dt$ | $X(e^{j\Omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] e^{-j\Omega n}$ | varies | | 3 | Energy Density Spectrum | $\Psi_x(\omega) = \vert X(\omega) \vert^2$ | $\Psi_x(\Omega) = \vert X(e^{j\Omega}) \vert^2$ | J·s/rad | | 4 | Parseval's Theorem (CT) | $E = \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \Psi_x(\omega)\, d\omega$ | $E = \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} \Psi_x(\Omega)\, d\Omega$ | Joules | | 5 | Autocorrelation $R_x(\tau)$ | $R_x(\tau) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) x(t+\tau) dt$ | $R_x[m] = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] x[n+m]$ | Joules | | 6 | Wiener–Khinchin Relation | $\Psi_x(\omega) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} R_x(\tau)$ | $\Psi_x(\Omega) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} R_x[m]$ | — | | 7 | EDS via Autocorrelation | $\Psi_x(\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} R_x(\tau) e^{-j\omega \tau} d\tau$ | $\Psi_x(\Omega) = \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} R_x[m] e^{-j\Omega m}$ | J·s/rad | | 8 | Even-Symmetry Property | $\Psi_x(\omega) = \Psi_x(-\omega)$ | $\Psi_x(\Omega) = \Psi_x(-\Omega)$ | — | | 9 | Non-Negativity | $\Psi_x(\omega) \geq 0 \;\; \forall \omega$ | $\Psi_x(\Omega) \geq 0 \;\; \forall \Omega$ | — | --- ## 2.4 Key Properties of Energy Density Spectrum 1. **Non-negativity:** $\Psi_x(\omega) \geq 0$ for all $\omega$. 2. **Real-valued:** $\Psi_x(\omega) \in \mathbb{R}$. 3. **Even function:** $\Psi_x(\omega) = \Psi_x(-\omega)$. 4. **Phase-independent:** Phase information is lost in EDS. 5. **Units:** Joules per radian per second (CT), Joules per radian (DT). 6. **Total Energy Recovery:** $E = \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \Psi_x(\omega)\, d\omega$. --- ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility | Domain | Application | |---|---| | **Wireless Communication** | Verifying channel bandwidth occupancy of a transmitted pulse | | **Biomedical Engineering** | ECG/EEG signal energy distribution across frequencies | | **Audio Engineering** | Equalizer design based on energy per frequency band | | **Radar Systems** | Matched filter design using energy of the transmitted chirp | | **Vibration Analysis** | Identifying resonant frequencies in mechanical structures | | **Image Processing** | 2-D extension of EDS for texture analysis | # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Parseval's Energy Theorem (CT) > **Goal:** Prove that $E = \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert X(\omega) \vert^2\, d\omega$. ### Step 1 — Start with the Time-Domain Energy Integral The total energy of a continuous-time signal $x(t)$ is: $$E = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t)\, x^*(t)\, dt = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^2 dt$$ **Logic:** Energy is defined as the integral of squared magnitude (analogous to $P = V^2 / R$ in electrical circuits). --- ### Step 2 — Substitute the Inverse Fourier Transform for One of the $x(t)$ Terms The inverse CTFT gives: $$x(t) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} X(\omega)\, e^{j\omega t}\, d\omega$$ Plugging this in: $$E = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) \left[ \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} X(\omega)\, e^{j\omega t}\, d\omega \right]^* dt$$ --- ### Step 3 — Simplify the Conjugate Since $(e^{j\omega t})^* = e^{-j\omega t}$ and $X^*(\omega)$ is the conjugate of $X(\omega)$: $$E = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t)\, X^*(\omega)\, e^{-j\omega t}\, d\omega\, dt$$ **Logic:** The constant $\frac{1}{2\pi}$ has been pulled out of the inner integral since it is independent of $t$. --- ### Step 4 — Swap the Order of Integration Using Fubini's theorem (valid because the energy is finite): $$E = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} X^*(\omega) \left[ \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t)\, e^{-j\omega t}\, dt \right] d\omega$$ **Logic:** Both integrals are absolutely convergent because $x(t)$ is an energy signal. --- ### Step 5 — Recognize the Forward Fourier Transform The inner integral is precisely $X(\omega)$: $$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t)\, e^{-j\omega t}\, dt = X(\omega)$$ Therefore: $$E = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} X^*(\omega)\, X(\omega)\, d\omega = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert X(\omega) \vert^2\, d\omega$$ --- ### Step 6 — Recognize the Energy Density Spectrum By definition, $\Psi_x(\omega) = \vert X(\omega) \vert^2$. Hence: $$\boxed{\, E = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \Psi_x(\omega)\, d\omega \,}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conclusion:** Integrating the Energy Density Spectrum over all frequencies (and dividing by $2\pi$) exactly recovers the total time-domain energy. This is **Parseval's Energy Theorem** (or Rayleigh's Theorem). --- ## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Wiener–Khinchin Theorem (EDS ↔ Autocorrelation) ### Step 1 — Definition of Autocorrelation $$R_x(\tau) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t)\, x(t+\tau)\, dt$$ --- ### Step 2 — Take the Fourier Transform of $R_x(\tau)$ $$\mathcal{F}\{R_x(\tau)\} = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} R_x(\tau)\, e^{-j\omega \tau}\, d\tau$$ Substituting the definition of $R_x(\tau)$: $$\mathcal{F}\{R_x(\tau)\} = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \left[ \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t)\, x(t+\tau)\, dt \right] e^{-j\omega \tau}\, d\tau$$ --- ### Step 3 — Change of Variable Let $\sigma = t + \tau \Rightarrow \tau = \sigma - t,\; d\tau = d\sigma$. The bounds remain $(-\infty, \infty)$: $$\mathcal{F}\{R_x(\tau)\} = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t)\, x(\sigma)\, e^{-j\omega (\sigma - t)}\, dt\, d\sigma$$ --- ### Step 4 — Separate the Exponential $$\mathcal{F}\{R_x(\tau)\} = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t)\, x(\sigma)\, e^{j\omega t}\, e^{-j\omega \sigma}\, dt\, d\sigma$$ --- ### Step 5 — Separate into Two Fourier Integrals $$\mathcal{F}\{R_x(\tau)\} = \left[ \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t)\, e^{j\omega t}\, dt \right] \cdot \left[ \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(\sigma)\, e^{-j\omega \sigma}\, d\sigma \right]$$ --- ### Step 6 — Identify Each Integral The first integral is the **inverse Fourier Transform kernel**: $$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t)\, e^{j\omega t}\, dt = X(-\omega)$$ The second integral is the **forward Fourier Transform**: $$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(\sigma)\, e^{-j\omega \sigma}\, d\sigma = X(\omega)$$ --- ### Step 7 — Combine and Apply Modulus $$\mathcal{F}\{R_x(\tau)\} = X(-\omega) \cdot X(\omega) = X^*(\omega) \cdot X(\omega)$$ (using the property $X(-\omega) = X^*(\omega)$ for real signals) Therefore: $$\boxed{\, \mathcal{F}\{R_x(\tau)\} = \vert X(\omega) \vert^2 = \Psi_x(\omega) \,}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Wiener–Khinchin Theorem (KTU Favorite):** The Energy Density Spectrum $\Psi_x(\omega)$ and the Autocorrelation function $R_x(\tau)$ form a **Fourier Transform pair**. --- ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Pattern) **Problem:** Find the Energy Density Spectrum of $x(t) = e^{-at} u(t)$ for $a > 0$. **Solution:** ### Step 1 — Compute the Fourier Transform $$X(\omega) = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-at}\, e^{-j\omega t}\, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-(a+j\omega)t}\, dt$$ $$X(\omega) = \left[ \frac{-e^{-(a+j\omega)t}}{a + j\omega} \right]_0^{\infty} = \frac{1}{a + j\omega}$$ (Valid since $a > 0$ ensures convergence.) --- ### Step 2 — Compute the Magnitude Squared $$\vert X(\omega) \vert^2 = \frac{1}{\vert a + j\omega \vert^2} = \frac{1}{a^2 + \omega^2}$$ --- ### Step 3 — State the Energy Density Spectrum $$\boxed{\, \Psi_x(\omega) = \frac{1}{a^2 + \omega^2} \,}$$ **Verification using Parseval's theorem:** $$E = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{1}{a^2 + \omega^2}\, d\omega = \frac{1}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{\pi}{a} = \frac{1}{2a}$$ This matches the direct time-domain energy calculation $\int_0^{\infty} e^{-2at}\, dt = \frac{1}{2a}$. ✓ --- ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: EDS vs PSD | Feature | Energy Density Spectrum (EDS) | Power Spectral Density (PSD) | |---|---|---| | Applicable to | Energy signals | Power signals | | Definition | $\Psi_x(\omega) = \vert X(\omega) \vert^2$ | $S_x(\omega) = \lim_{T \to \infty} \dfrac{\vert X_T(\omega) \vert^2}{T}$ | | Units | Joules / (rad/s) | Watts / (rad/s) | | Recoverable Quantity | $\dfrac{1}{2\pi}\int \Psi_x d\omega = E$ | $\dfrac{1}{2\pi}\int S_x d\omega = P$ | | Time-domain counterpart | Autocorrelation $R_x(\tau)$ | Autocorrelation $R_x(\tau)$ | | KTU Module | **Module 2** | Module 3 (typically) | # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Conceptual Block Diagram — EDS Construction Pipeline The following Mermaid **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** illustrates the complete transformation pipeline from a time-domain energy signal to its Energy Density Spectrum, and the dual pathway via autocorrelation (Wiener–Khinchin). ```mermaid flowchart LR A[xtime domain signal] --> B{Is Energy
Finite E greater than 0} B -- Yes --> C[Compute Fourier
Transform X omega] B -- No --> Z[Use Power Spectral
Density instead] C --> D[Compute Conjugate
X star omega] D --> E[Multiply
X times X star] E --> F[Psi x omega equals
Energy Density Spectrum] F --> G[Integrate over omega
divided by 2 pi] G --> H[Total Energy E] F --> I[Autocorrelation
R x tau] I --> J[Fourier Transform
Wiener Khinchin] J --> F ``` **Reading the Diagram:** - Left vertical path: Time-domain signal $x(t)$ → Fourier Transform → EDS via $\vert X(\omega) \vert^2$ → Integrate to get $E$. - Bottom horizontal path: EDS ↔ Autocorrelation via the **Wiener–Khinchin theorem** (bidirectional Fourier pair). - Right branch: If energy is not finite, the framework redirects to Power Spectral Density (PSD). --- ## 4.2 Frequency-Domain Schematic — EDS of a Rectangular Pulse Consider $x(t) = \text{rect}(t/T)$ (rectangular pulse of width $T$). Its Fourier Transform is the **sinc function**: $$X(\omega) = T \cdot \text{sinc}\left(\frac{\omega T}{2}\right) = T \cdot \frac{\sin(\omega T / 2)}{\omega T / 2}$$ The corresponding EDS is: $$\Psi_x(\omega) = T^2 \cdot \text{sinc}^2\left(\frac{\omega T}{2}\right)$$ **Key Observations (KTU Visual Insight):** | Feature | Behavior | |---|---| | Peak value of $\Psi_x$ | $T^2$ at $\omega = 0$ | | Zero crossings | $\omega = \pm \dfrac{2\pi n}{T},\; n \neq 0$ | | Lobes | Main lobe (centered) + decaying side lobes | | Symmetry | Even function of $\omega$ | | Total Energy | $E = \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \int T^2 \text{sinc}^2(\omega T / 2) d\omega = T$ ✓ | --- ## 4.3 State Diagram — Properties of EDS ```mermaid flowchart TD P1[Psi x omega] --> Q1[Non negative
for all omega] P1 --> Q2[Real valued
no imaginary part] P1 --> Q3[Even function
Psi equals Psi at minus omega] P1 --> Q4[Loses phase
information] P1 --> Q5[Total energy E
recovered by integration] P1 --> Q6[Fourier Pair with
Autocorrelation R tau] ``` --- ## 4.4 EDS Spectral Profiles for Common Signals | Signal $x(t)$ | Fourier Transform $X(\omega)$ | Energy Density Spectrum $\Psi_x(\omega)$ | |---|---|---| | $\delta(t)$ (impulse) | $1$ | $1$ (flat white spectrum) | | $e^{-at} u(t)$ | $\dfrac{1}{a + j\omega}$ | $\dfrac{1}{a^2 + \omega^2}$ | | $\text{rect}(t/T)$ | $T\,\text{sinc}(\omega T / 2)$ | $T^2\,\text{sinc}^2(\omega T / 2)$ | | $e^{-a \vert t \vert}$ | $\dfrac{2a}{a^2 + \omega^2}$ | $\dfrac{4a^2}{(a^2 + \omega^2)^2}$ | | $\cos(\omega_0 t)$ w/ window | $\frac{1}{2}[\delta(\omega - \omega_0) + \delta(\omega + \omega_0)]$-shaped lobe | lobes at $\pm\omega_0$ | > [!NOTE] > The EDS profile of the **double exponential** $e^{-a\vert t\vert}$ falls off as $1/\omega^4$, much faster than the single-sided exponential's $1/\omega^2$ — making it more band-limited. # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define Energy Density Spectrum. Mention any two of its properties.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The **Energy Density Spectrum (EDS)** of a continuous-time energy signal $x(t)$ is defined as the squared magnitude of its Fourier Transform: $$\Psi_x(\omega) = \vert X(\omega) \vert^2 = X(\omega) X^*(\omega)$$ where $X(\omega) = \mathcal{F}\{x(t)\}$. **Two Properties (1.5 marks each):** 1. **Non-negativity:** $\Psi_x(\omega) \geq 0$ for all $\omega$ — since it is a squared magnitude, it can never be negative. 2. **Even function of $\omega$:** $\Psi_x(\omega) = \Psi_x(-\omega)$ — the EDS is symmetric about the origin because $\vert X(\omega) \vert$ is an even function for real signals. *(Alternative property: It is real-valued; it loses phase information; total energy recoverable by integration.)* --- ### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **State and explain Parseval's Energy Theorem for continuous-time signals.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Statement:** Parseval's Energy Theorem states that the total energy of a signal computed in the time domain equals the energy computed (via the EDS) in the frequency domain, scaled by $\dfrac{1}{2\pi}$. **Mathematical Expression:** $$E = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^2 dt = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert X(\omega) \vert^2 d\omega = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \Psi_x(\omega) d\omega$$ **Significance (1 Mark):** It justifies the physical interpretation of $\Psi_x(\omega) = \vert X(\omega) \vert^2$ as the **energy density per unit angular frequency**, because integrating it (with $d\omega / 2\pi$) recovers total energy. This is the energy-domain analogue of conservation of energy in physics. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern) > [!WARNING] > **KTU 2024 Internal-Choice Convention:** For each 14-mark question, the examiner provides TWO alternative questions. **You must answer ONE.** The KTU 2024 Scheme strictly follows this (a)-(b) sub-structure with each part carrying **7 marks**. --- ### **Question B1 (Option A)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]` **(a)** Derive the relation between Energy Density Spectrum and Autocorrelation function for a continuous-time energy signal. State the theorem you used. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A signal $x(t) = e^{-2t} u(t)$ is passed through an LTI system with impulse response $h(t) = e^{-3t} u(t)$. Find the Energy Density Spectrum of the output $y(t)$. **(7 Marks)** --- ### **Model Solution — Question B1 (Option A)** #### **Part (a) Solution [7 Marks]** **Statement of the Theorem (1 Mark):** The relation between the Energy Density Spectrum (EDS) and the Autocorrelation function is established by the **Wiener–Khinchin Theorem**, which states that they form a Fourier Transform pair. **Autocorrelation Function Definition (1 Mark):** $$R_x(\tau) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t)\, x(t+\tau)\, dt$$ **Derivation (5 Marks):** Take the Fourier Transform of $R_x(\tau)$: $$\mathcal{F}\{R_x(\tau)\} = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} R_x(\tau)\, e^{-j\omega \tau}\, d\tau$$ Substitute the definition of $R_x(\tau)$: $$= \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \left[ \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t)\, x(t+\tau)\, dt \right] e^{-j\omega \tau}\, d\tau$$ Apply the change of variable $\sigma = t + \tau \Rightarrow \tau = \sigma - t$: $$= \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t)\, x(\sigma)\, e^{-j\omega (\sigma - t)}\, dt\, d\sigma$$ Separate the exponential: $$= \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t)\, x(\sigma)\, e^{j\omega t}\, e^{-j\omega \sigma}\, dt\, d\sigma$$ $$= \left[ \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t)\, e^{j\omega t}\, dt \right] \cdot \left[ \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(\sigma)\, e^{-j\omega \sigma}\, d\sigma \right]$$ Recognize each integral as Fourier Transforms: $$= X(-\omega) \cdot X(\omega)$$ For real $x(t)$, using $X(-\omega) = X^*(\omega)$: $$\mathcal{F}\{R_x(\tau)\} = X^*(\omega) \cdot X(\omega) = \vert X(\omega) \vert^2 = \Psi_x(\omega)$$ **Final Result (boxed for clarity):** $$\boxed{\, \Psi_x(\omega) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} R_x(\tau) \,}$$ --- #### **Part (b) Solution [7 Marks]** **Step 1 — Compute $X(\omega)$ [2 Marks]:** $$X(\omega) = \mathcal{F}\{e^{-2t} u(t)\} = \frac{1}{2 + j\omega}$$ **Step 2 — Compute $H(\omega)$ [2 Marks]:** $$H(\omega) = \mathcal{F}\{e^{-3t} u(t)\} = \frac{1}{3 + j\omega}$$ **Step 3 — Compute $Y(\omega) = X(\omega) H(\omega)$ [1 Mark]:** $$Y(\omega) = \frac{1}{(2 + j\omega)(3 + j\omega)}$$ **Step 4 — Energy Density Spectrum of $y(t)$ [2 Marks]:** $$\Psi_y(\omega) = \vert Y(\omega) \vert^2 = \frac{1}{\vert 2 + j\omega \vert^2 \cdot \vert 3 + j\omega \vert^2}$$ $$\boxed{\, \Psi_y(\omega) = \frac{1}{(4 + \omega^2)(9 + \omega^2)} \,}$$ --- ### **Question B2 (Option B — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]` **(a)** For a continuous-time signal $x(t) = A\,\text{rect}\!\left(\dfrac{t}{T}\right)$, derive the expression for its Energy Density Spectrum. Sketch its magnitude spectrum. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Verify Parseval's Theorem for the signal $x(t) = e^{-at} u(t)$ using both time-domain and frequency-domain methods. **(7 Marks)** --- ### **Model Solution — Question B2 (Option B)** #### **Part (a) Solution [7 Marks]** **Step 1 — Express $x(t)$ explicitly [1 Mark]:** $$x(t) = \begin{cases} A, & \vert t \vert \leq T/2 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$ **Step 2 — Compute the Fourier Transform [3 Marks]:** $$X(\omega) = \int_{-T/2}^{T/2} A\, e^{-j\omega t}\, dt = A \left[ \frac{e^{-j\omega t}}{-j\omega} \right]_{-T/2}^{T/2}$$ $$= \frac{A}{-j\omega} \left[ e^{-j\omega T/2} - e^{j\omega T/2} \right] = \frac{2A}{\omega} \sin\!\left(\frac{\omega T}{2}\right)$$ $$\Rightarrow X(\omega) = A T \cdot \text{sinc}\!\left(\frac{\omega T}{2}\right)$$ where $\text{sinc}(x) = \dfrac{\sin(x)}{x}$. **Step 3 — Compute EDS [2 Marks]:** $$\boxed{\, \Psi_x(\omega) = \vert X(\omega) \vert^2 = A^2 T^2 \cdot \text{sinc}^2\!\left(\frac{\omega T}{2}\right) \,}$$ **Step 4 — Sketch description [1 Mark]:** A sinc-squared function with peak $A^2 T^2$ at $\omega = 0$, zero crossings at $\omega = \pm \dfrac{2\pi n}{T}$ for $n = 1, 2, 3, \dots$, and decaying oscillatory side lobes. Symmetric about $\omega = 0$. --- #### **Part (b) Solution [7 Marks]** **Step 1 — Time-Domain Energy [2 Marks]:** $$E = \int_{0}^{\infty} \left( e^{-at} \right)^2 dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-2at}\, dt = \left[ \frac{-e^{-2at}}{2a} \right]_0^{\infty} = \frac{1}{2a}$$ **Step 2 — Frequency-Domain EDS [2 Marks]:** From the earlier example: $$X(\omega) = \frac{1}{a + j\omega} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \Psi_x(\omega) = \frac{1}{a^2 + \omega^2}$$ **Step 3 — Apply Parseval [2 Marks]:** $$E = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{1}{a^2 + \omega^2}\, d\omega$$ Using the standard integral $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \dfrac{d\omega}{a^2 + \omega^2} = \dfrac{\pi}{a}$: $$E = \frac{1}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{\pi}{a} = \frac{1}{2a}$$ **Step 4 — Verification [1 Mark]:** $$E_{\text{time}} = \frac{1}{2a} = E_{\text{frequency}} \quad \checkmark$$ **Parseval's Theorem is verified.** --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls — Read Carefully!** | # | Mistake Committed | Marks Lost | Examiner's Tip | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Forgetting the $\dfrac{1}{2\pi}$ factor in Parseval's theorem | **1–2 marks** | Write the full formula every time; never skip $1/(2\pi)$. | | 2 | Writing EDS as $X(\omega)$ instead of $\vert X(\omega) \vert^2$ | **2 marks** | EDS is always *squared magnitude* — never just the transform. | | 3 | Confusing EDS with Power Spectral Density (PSD) | **2–3 marks** | EDS = $\vert X(\omega) \vert^2$ for *energy signals*; PSD uses averaging and limit. | | 4 | Writing $\Psi_x(\omega)$ as a complex quantity | **1 mark** | EDS is strictly **real and non-negative**. | | 5 | Omitting the $\mathcal{F}\{R_x(\tau)\}$ notation in the Wiener–Khinchin answer | **1 mark** | Always mention it is a **Fourier Transform pair**, not just "equal to". | | 6 | Applying EDS to a power signal (e.g., $\sin(\omega_0 t)$) | **3 marks** | First check finite-energy; otherwise switch to PSD. | | 7 | Computing $\vert X(\omega) \vert$ instead of $\vert X(\omega) \vert^2$ for EDS | **2 marks** | "Density spectrum" implies *squared* magnitude. | --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Read 5 minutes before exam.** - **Definition:** $\Psi_x(\omega) = \vert X(\omega) \vert^2$ — squared magnitude of the Fourier Transform. - **Parseval's Theorem (CT):** $E = \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \Psi_x(\omega)\, d\omega$. - **Parseval's Theorem (DT):** $E = \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} \Psi_x(\Omega)\, d\Omega$. - **Wiener–Khinchin Theorem:** $\Psi_x(\omega) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} R_x(\tau)$ — EDS and Autocorrelation are Fourier pairs. - **Autocorrelation Definition (CT):** $R_x(\tau) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) x(t+\tau) dt$. - **Autocorrelation Definition (DT):** $R_x[m] = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] x[n+m]$. - **Properties:** Non-negative, real-valued, even function, loses phase information. - **Units (CT):** Joules per radian per second. - **Units (DT):** Joules per radian. - **EDS applies to:** Only energy signals ($0 < E < \infty$); for power signals use PSD. - **Standard EDS Profiles to Memorize:** - Exponential $e^{-at} u(t)$: $\Psi_x(\omega) = \dfrac{1}{a^2 + \omega^2}$. - Double exponential $e^{-a\vert t\vert}$: $\Psi_x(\omega) = \dfrac{4a^2}{(a^2 + \omega^2)^2}$. - Rectangular pulse: $\Psi_x(\omega) = A^2 T^2 \,\text{sinc}^2(\omega T / 2)$. - Impulse $\delta(t)$: $\Psi_x(\omega) = 1$ (flat spectrum). - **Integration Trick:** $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \dfrac{d\omega}{a^2 + \omega^2} = \dfrac{\pi}{a}$ — memorize this for Parseval-based verification. - **For cascaded LTI systems:** $\Psi_y(\omega) = \Psi_x(\omega) \cdot \vert H(\omega) \vert^2$ — EDS multiplies by the system's energy transfer function. - **Symmetry reminder:** For real signals, $X(-\omega) = X^*(\omega)$ — this is what makes $\vert X(\omega) \vert$ even. --- > [!NOTE] > **End of Module 2 — Energy Density Spectra Notes.** > **All the Best for Your KTU 2024 Examinations!**

topics.title: Characterizing LTI Systems Using the Fourier Transform
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST416 - SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Characterizing LTI Systems Using the Fourier Transform

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Frequency Response of an LTI System:** For a continuous-time Linear Time-Invariant (LTI) system with impulse response $h(t)$, the **frequency response** $H(j\omega)$ is defined as the **Fourier Transform of the impulse response**:
> $$H(j\omega) = \mathcal{F}\{h(t)\} = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} h(t)\, e^{-j\omega t}\, dt$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Eigenfunction Property:** A complex exponential $e^{j\omega_0 t}$ is an **eigenfunction** of any LTI system. This means that when $e^{j\omega_0 t}$ is the input, the output is the *same* complex exponential scaled by a complex constant — the **eigenvalue** — which equals $H(j\omega_0)$.

The output $y(t)$ of an LTI system for any input $x(t)$ in the **frequency domain** is given by the product of the Fourier transforms:
$$Y(j\omega) = H(j\omega)\, X(j\omega)$$

This is the cornerstone of Fourier-domain analysis: convolution in the time domain becomes **multiplication in the frequency domain**.

---

### 1.2 Intuitive Overview — "The Equalizer Analogy"

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — Audio Graphic Equalizer:**
> Imagine your music player has a graphic equalizer with multiple frequency sliders (bass, mid, treble). Each slider is a knob that controls how much the system amplifies or attenuates a *specific frequency band*.
>
> - The **input** is the original music signal (a sum of many pure tones at different frequencies).
> - The **system** is the equalizer, which acts on each frequency independently.
> - The **frequency response** $H(j\omega)$ is the exact gain/phase curve that tells you what the equalizer does to *each* frequency.
> - The **output** is the music you hear, with the bass boosted, treble reduced, or whatever you set.
>
> The crucial insight: **an LTI system is just a fancy equalizer that scales the magnitude and shifts the phase of every frequency component** of the input by a known amount. The Fourier transform simply lets you see *which* frequency is *which* inside the input signal, and the frequency response tells you *what the system does to that frequency*.

### 1.3 Key Physical Quantities and Units

| Quantity | Symbol | SI Unit | Description |
|----------|--------|---------|-------------|
| **Frequency response** | $H(j\omega)$ | dimensionless (ratio) | Complex-valued function of $\omega$ |
| **Magnitude response** | $\vert H(j\omega)\vert$ | dimensionless (ratio) | Gain at frequency $\omega$ |
| **Phase response** | $\angle H(j\omega)$ | radians (or degrees) | Phase shift at $\omega$ |
| **Group delay** | $\tau_g(\omega) = -\dfrac{d}{d\omega}\angle H(j\omega)$ | seconds (s) | Time delay of envelope |
| **Cutoff frequency** | $\omega_c$ | rad/s | Boundary between passband and stopband |
| **Bandwidth** | $BW$ | rad/s | Width of the passband |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Eigenfunction Property — Why It Works

Any LTI system obeys two axioms — **linearity** and **time-invariance**. These together imply a striking result when the input is a complex exponential $e^{j\omega_0 t}$:

**Step 1 — Decompose the input using the Inverse Fourier Transform:**
$$x(t) = \frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} X(j\omega)\, e^{j\omega t}\, d\omega$$

**Step 2 — Each infinitesimal slice $X(j\omega)\, e^{j\omega t}$ acts as a tiny complex exponential input.** By linearity, the system response to each slice can be computed independently and summed.

**Step 3 — Time-invariance guarantees the response of a complex exponential at frequency $\omega$ depends only on $\omega$, not on $t$.** Therefore, the system is fully described by a single complex function $H(j\omega)$ that gives the "gain-and-phase" applied to each frequency.

This is the deep reason the **convolution integral collapses to a simple product** in the frequency domain.

---

### 2.2 The Convolution Theorem (The Heart of Fourier Analysis)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Convolution in Time ↔ Multiplication in Frequency**
> $$y(t) = h(t) * x(t) \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad Y(j\omega) = H(j\omega)\, X(j\omega)$$

The reverse is also true:
$$H(j\omega)\, X(j\omega) \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad \frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} H(j\theta)\, X(j(\omega-\theta))\, d\theta$$

---

### 2.3 Response to a Sinusoidal Input (Steady-State + Transient)

For an input $x(t) = \cos(\omega_0 t) = \tfrac{1}{2}\left(e^{j\omega_0 t} + e^{-j\omega_0 t}\right)$, the output is:

$$y(t) = \tfrac{1}{2}\left[H(j\omega_0)\, e^{j\omega_0 t} + H(-j\omega_0)\, e^{-j\omega_0 t}\right]$$

For **real impulse responses**, $H(-j\omega_0) = H^*(j\omega_0)$, so:

$$y(t) = \text{Re}\{H(j\omega_0)\, e^{j\omega_0 t}\} = \vert H(j\omega_0)\vert \cos\!\big(\omega_0 t + \angle H(j\omega_0)\big)$$

This shows that **a sinusoid in, a sinusoid out** — only amplitude and phase change. **No new frequencies are generated** by an LTI system (linearity forbids it; this is a key KTU result).

> [!NOTE]
> **Causality constraint:** If the system is causal and stable, the impulse response is absolutely integrable, and therefore $H(j\omega)$ exists (i.e., is well-defined and bounded for all $\omega$).

---

### 2.4 Distortionless Transmission

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A system is **distortionless** if the output is a delayed, scaled replica of the input:
> $$y(t) = K\, x(t - t_0)$$
> where $K > 0$ is a real constant gain and $t_0$ is the propagation delay.
>
> In the frequency domain, this requires:
> $$H(j\omega) = K\, e^{-j\omega t_0}$$
> Equivalently:
> - $\vert H(j\omega)\vert = K$ (constant for all $\omega$ — **flat magnitude**)
> - $\angle H(j\omega) = -\omega t_0$ (linear phase — **constant group delay**)

Any deviation from constant $\vert H(j\omega)\vert$ produces **amplitude (magnitude) distortion**. Any deviation from linear phase produces **phase distortion**.

---

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Print this table. It contains the entire arsenal you need to solve any KTU problem on this topic.**

| # | Formula / Property | Statement |
|---|--------------------|-----------|
| 1 | Frequency response | $H(j\omega) = \displaystyle\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} h(t)\, e^{-j\omega t}\, dt$ |
| 2 | Convolution theorem | $y(t) = h(t) * x(t) \;\Longleftrightarrow\; Y(j\omega) = H(j\omega)\, X(j\omega)$ |
| 3 | Eigenfunction property | $e^{j\omega_0 t} \xrightarrow{\text{LTI}} H(j\omega_0)\, e^{j\omega_0 t}$ |
| 4 | Sinusoidal response | $\cos(\omega_0 t) \rightarrow \vert H(j\omega_0)\vert \cos\!\big(\omega_0 t + \angle H(j\omega_0)\big)$ |
| 5 | Magnitude response | $\vert H(j\omega)\vert = \sqrt{[\text{Re}\{H(j\omega)\}]^2 + [\text{Im}\{H(j\omega)\}]^2}$ |
| 6 | Phase response | $\angle H(j\omega) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\text{Im}\{H(j\omega)\}}{\text{Re}\{H(j\omega)\}}\right)$ |
| 7 | Distortionless system | $H(j\omega) = K\, e^{-j\omega t_0}$ |
| 8 | Group delay | $\tau_g(\omega) = -\dfrac{d\,\angle H(j\omega)}{d\omega}$ |
| 9 | LPF ideal | $H(j\omega) = \begin{cases} 1, & \vert\omega\vert < \omega_c \\ 0, & \vert\omega\vert > \omega_c \end{cases}$ |
| 10 | HPF ideal | $H(j\omega) = \begin{cases} 0, & \vert\omega\vert < \omega_c \\ 1, & \vert\omega\vert > \omega_c \end{cases}$ |
| 11 | System bandwidth | Frequency range where $\vert H(j\omega)\vert \geq \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\, \vert H(j\omega)\vert_{\max}$ (i.e., $-3\,\text{dB}$ point) |
| 12 | Parseval's relation | $\displaystyle\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert x(t)\vert^2\, dt = \frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert X(j\omega)\vert^2\, d\omega$ |

---

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Audio amplifiers & speakers:** Designed to have flat magnitude response across the audible band (20 Hz – 20 kHz) for *fidelity*.
- **Telecommunications channel equalizers:** Compensate the channel's $H(j\omega)$ so the *cascade* becomes distortionless.
- **ECG/biomedical filters:** Remove $50/60\,\text{Hz}$ power-line interference using a notch (band-stop) filter.
- **Radar/sonar matched filters:** Designed so $H(j\omega) = X^*(j\omega)$ for optimal signal detection under noise.
- **Control systems:** Bode plots of $H(j\omega)$ are used daily to check stability margins (gain crossover, phase margin).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Output of an LTI System in the Frequency Domain

We start from the **convolution integral** that defines the output of an LTI system:

$$y(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} h(\tau)\, x(t - \tau)\, d\tau$$

**Step 1:** Take the Fourier Transform of both sides.

$$Y(j\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} y(t)\, e^{-j\omega t}\, dt$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the expression for $y(t)$ into the integral.

$$Y(j\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty}\!\left[\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} h(\tau)\, x(t - \tau)\, d\tau\right] e^{-j\omega t}\, dt$$

**Step 3:** Swap the order of integration (justified by Fubini's theorem, since $h$ and $x$ are absolutely integrable for stable systems).

$$Y(j\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} h(\tau)\!\left[\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(t - \tau)\, e^{-j\omega t}\, dt\right] d\tau$$

**Step 4:** Use the **time-shift property of the Fourier transform** on the inner integral. Replacing $u = t - \tau \;\Rightarrow\; du = dt$:

$$\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(t - \tau)\, e^{-j\omega t}\, dt = e^{-j\omega \tau}\! \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(u)\, e^{-j\omega u}\, du = e^{-j\omega \tau}\, X(j\omega)$$

**Step 5:** Substitute back. Note that $X(j\omega)$ is independent of $\tau$ and can be pulled out of the inner integral.

$$Y(j\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} h(\tau)\, e^{-j\omega \tau}\, X(j\omega)\, d\tau = X(j\omega)\!\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} h(\tau)\, e^{-j\omega \tau}\, d\tau$$

**Step 6:** Recognize the remaining integral as the **frequency response** $H(j\omega)$.

$$\boxed{\,Y(j\omega) = H(j\omega)\, X(j\omega)\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the single most important result in Module 2. It transforms a difficult convolution integral into simple multiplication.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 1 — LTI System with Exponential Impulse Response

**Problem (KTU-style):** An LTI system has impulse response $h(t) = e^{-2t}\, u(t)$. Find the response $y(t)$ to the input $x(t) = e^{-t}\, u(t)$.

**Step A — Compute $H(j\omega)$:**

$$H(j\omega) = \int_{0}^{+\infty} e^{-2t}\, e^{-j\omega t}\, dt = \int_{0}^{+\infty} e^{-(2 + j\omega)t}\, dt$$

$$H(j\omega) = \left[\frac{e^{-(2 + j\omega)t}}{-(2 + j\omega)}\right]_{0}^{+\infty} = \frac{1}{2 + j\omega}$$

**Step B — Compute $X(j\omega)$:**

$$X(j\omega) = \int_{0}^{+\infty} e^{-t}\, e^{-j\omega t}\, dt = \int_{0}^{+\infty} e^{-(1 + j\omega)t}\, dt = \frac{1}{1 + j\omega}$$

**Step C — Multiply:**

$$Y(j\omega) = H(j\omega)\, X(j\omega) = \frac{1}{(2 + j\omega)(1 + j\omega)}$$

**Step D — Partial fraction expansion:**

$$\frac{1}{(2 + j\omega)(1 + j\omega)} = \frac{A}{2 + j\omega} + \frac{B}{1 + j\omega}$$

Multiply both sides by $(2 + j\omega)(1 + j\omega)$:

$$1 = A(1 + j\omega) + B(2 + j\omega)$$

- Set $j\omega = -1$: $\;1 = A(0) + B(1) \;\Rightarrow\; B = 1$
- Set $j\omega = -2$: $\;1 = A(-1) + B(0) \;\Rightarrow\; A = -1$

Therefore:

$$Y(j\omega) = \frac{-1}{2 + j\omega} + \frac{1}{1 + j\omega}$$

**Step E — Inverse Fourier Transform (use $\dfrac{1}{a + j\omega} \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}^{-1}} e^{-at}\, u(t)$):**

$$\boxed{\,y(t) = \left(-e^{-2t} + e^{-t}\right) u(t) = \left(e^{-t} - e^{-2t}\right) u(t)\,}$$

**Verification (time-domain convolution):** $y(t) = h(t) * x(t) = \int_0^t e^{-2\tau}\, e^{-(t-\tau)}\, d\tau = e^{-t}\int_0^t e^{-\tau}\, d\tau = e^{-t}(1 - e^{-t}) = e^{-t} - e^{-2t}$ for $t \geq 0$. ✓

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Sinusoidal Steady-State Response

**Problem:** Given $H(j\omega) = \dfrac{1}{2 + j\omega}$, find the output for $x(t) = \cos(3t)$.

**Step 1 — Evaluate $H$ at $\omega_0 = 3$:**

$$H(j3) = \frac{1}{2 + j3} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{4 + 9}}\, e^{-j\tan^{-1}(3/2)} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{13}}\, e^{-j\tan^{-1}(1.5)}$$

**Step 2 — Magnitude and phase:**

$$\vert H(j3)\vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{13}} \approx 0.2774, \quad \angle H(j3) = -\tan^{-1}(1.5) \approx -56.31^\circ$$

**Step 3 — Apply the sinusoidal rule:**

$$\boxed{\,y(t) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{13}}\,\cos\!\left(3t - 56.31^\circ\right)\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Notice: the **frequency is unchanged** ($\omega = 3$ rad/s). Only magnitude is scaled by $1/\sqrt{13}$ and phase is shifted by $-56.31^\circ$. This is the trademark behavior of an LTI system driven by a sinusoid.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Distortionless Transmission Check

**Problem:** Determine whether the system $H(j\omega) = \dfrac{1}{1 + j\omega}$ is distortionless.

**Step 1 — Magnitude response:**

$$\vert H(j\omega)\vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + \omega^2}}$$

This is **not constant** — it falls off as $1/\omega$ for large $\omega$. So **magnitude distortion is present**.

**Step 2 — Phase response:**

$$\angle H(j\omega) = -\tan^{-1}(\omega)$$

This is **not linear** in $\omega$ — it saturates at $-90^\circ$. So **phase distortion is present**.

**Conclusion:** The system is **NOT** distortionless. The output $y(t)$ will not be a scaled and delayed copy of any arbitrary input.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Fourier Domain Characterization of an LTI System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Input Signal x of t"] --> B["Fourier Transform X of j omega"]
    B --> C["Multiplication by H of j omega"]
    D["Impulse Response h of t"] --> E["Fourier Transform H of j omega"]
    E --> C
    C --> F["Output Spectrum Y of j omega"]
    F --> G["Inverse Fourier Transform"]
    G --> H["Output Signal y of t"]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Sinusoidal Steady-State Response Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P["Cosine Input cos omega zero t"] --> Q["Evaluate H at j omega zero"]
    Q --> R["Compute magnitude H"]
    Q --> S["Compute phase H"]
    R --> T["Scale amplitude"]
    S --> T
    T --> U["Output: H mag times cos of omega zero t plus H phase"]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Flowchart — Distortionless Transmission Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Given H of j omega"] --> B{"Magnitude constant for all omega?"}
    B -- Yes --> C{"Phase linear in omega?"}
    B -- No --> M1["Magnitude distortion present"]
    C -- Yes --> D["DISTORTIONLESS: y of t equals K times x of t minus t zero"]
    C -- No --> M2["Phase distortion present"]
    M1 --> E["NOT Distortionless"]
    M2 --> E
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — Filter Classification

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["H of j omega"] --> B["Magnitude response"]
    A --> C["Phase response"]
    B --> D1["LPF: passes low freq"]
    B --> D2["HPF: passes high freq"]
    B --> D3["BPF: passes mid band"]
    B --> D4["BSF: rejects mid band"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. Define the frequency response of an LTI system. Why is it called the system's "transfer function" in the Fourier domain?** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The frequency response of a continuous-time LTI system with impulse response $h(t)$ is defined as the Fourier transform of the impulse response:

$$H(j\omega) = \mathcal{F}\{h(t)\} = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} h(t)\, e^{-j\omega t}\, dt$$

It is called the transfer function because, just as the impulse response *transfers* an input to the output in the time domain via convolution, the frequency response *transfers* the input spectrum to the output spectrum via simple multiplication: $Y(j\omega) = H(j\omega)\, X(j\omega)$. In this sense, $H(j\omega)$ completely characterizes how the system "transfers" each sinusoidal component from input to output. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. State the convolution theorem for the Fourier transform and explain its significance in analyzing LTI systems.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Statement:** If $y(t) = h(t) * x(t)$, then $Y(j\omega) = H(j\omega)\, X(j\omega)$, and conversely.

**Significance:**
1. It converts the **complex convolution integral** in the time domain into a **simple algebraic product** in the frequency domain.
2. It reveals the **eigenfunction property** — complex exponentials $e^{j\omega t}$ pass through an LTI system unchanged in shape, scaled only by $H(j\omega)$.
3. It makes filter design, system analysis, and stability assessment (via Bode plots) far more tractable. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the output $Y(j\omega)$ of a continuous-time LTI system in terms of its input $X(j\omega)$ and frequency response $H(j\omega)$, starting from the convolution integral. State the convolution theorem clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An LTI system has impulse response $h(t) = e^{-3t}\, u(t)$. Find the response of the system to the input $x(t) = e^{-2t}\, u(t)$ using the Fourier transform method. Verify your answer. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question A

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

Starting from the convolution integral:
$$y(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} h(\tau)\, x(t - \tau)\, d\tau \quad\text{(1 mark)}$$

Taking the Fourier transform:
$$Y(j\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} y(t)\, e^{-j\omega t}\, dt \quad\text{(1 mark)}$$

Substituting and swapping order of integration:
$$Y(j\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} h(\tau)\!\left[\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(t - \tau)\, e^{-j\omega t}\, dt\right] d\tau \quad\text{(1 mark)}$$

Applying the time-shift property of the Fourier transform (substituting $u = t - \tau$):
$$\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(t - \tau)\, e^{-j\omega t}\, dt = e^{-j\omega \tau}\, X(j\omega) \quad\text{(2 marks)}$$

Factoring out $X(j\omega)$ and recognizing the remaining integral as $H(j\omega)$:

$$\boxed{\,Y(j\omega) = H(j\omega)\, X(j\omega)\,} \quad\text{(2 marks)}$$

**Convolution Theorem Statement (embedded above):** $y(t) = h(t) * x(t) \;\Longleftrightarrow\; Y(j\omega) = H(j\omega)\, X(j\omega)$.

---

**(b) Numerical solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute $H(j\omega)$:** **(1 mark)**

$$H(j\omega) = \int_{0}^{+\infty} e^{-3t}\, e^{-j\omega t}\, dt = \frac{1}{3 + j\omega}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $X(j\omega)$:** **(1 mark)**

$$X(j\omega) = \int_{0}^{+\infty} e^{-2t}\, e^{-j\omega t}\, dt = \frac{1}{2 + j\omega}$$

**Step 3 — Multiply:** **(1 mark)**

$$Y(j\omega) = H(j\omega)\, X(j\omega) = \frac{1}{(3 + j\omega)(2 + j\omega)}$$

**Step 4 — Partial fraction expansion:** **(2 marks)**

Let $\dfrac{1}{(3 + j\omega)(2 + j\omega)} = \dfrac{A}{3 + j\omega} + \dfrac{B}{2 + j\omega}$.

Then $1 = A(2 + j\omega) + B(3 + j\omega)$.

- Setting $j\omega = -3$: $\;1 = A(-1) \;\Rightarrow\; A = -1$
- Setting $j\omega = -2$: $\;1 = B(1) \;\Rightarrow\; B = 1$

So $Y(j\omega) = \dfrac{-1}{3 + j\omega} + \dfrac{1}{2 + j\omega}$.

**Step 5 — Inverse Fourier Transform:** **(1 mark)**

Using $\dfrac{1}{a + j\omega} \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}^{-1}} e^{-at}\, u(t)$:

$$y(t) = \left(-e^{-3t} + e^{-2t}\right) u(t) = \left(e^{-2t} - e^{-3t}\right) u(t)$$

**Step 6 — Verification by direct convolution:** **(1 mark)**

$$y(t) = \int_0^t e^{-3\tau}\, e^{-2(t-\tau)}\, d\tau = e^{-2t}\int_0^t e^{-\tau}\, d\tau = e^{-2t}(1 - e^{-t}) = e^{-2t} - e^{-3t} \;\checkmark$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of distortionless transmission through an LTI system. Derive the conditions on the magnitude and phase responses for distortionless behavior. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An LTI system has frequency response $H(j\omega) = \dfrac{1}{1 + j\omega}$. Determine whether the system is distortionless. If not, find the output when the input is $x(t) = \cos(2t)$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question B

**(a) Distortionless transmission (7 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** A system is said to be distortionless if the output is an exact replica of the input, scaled by a constant $K$ and delayed by a time $t_0$:

$$y(t) = K\, x(t - t_0)$$

where $K > 0$ is a real constant (gain) and $t_0 \geq 0$ is the propagation delay.

**Frequency-domain derivation (3 marks):** Taking the Fourier transform of both sides and applying the time-shift property:

$$Y(j\omega) = K\, e^{-j\omega t_0}\, X(j\omega)$$

But since $Y(j\omega) = H(j\omega)\, X(j\omega)$, we have:

$$H(j\omega) = K\, e^{-j\omega t_0}$$

**Conditions (2 marks):**
- **Magnitude condition:** $\vert H(j\omega)\vert = K$ (a constant independent of $\omega$)
- **Phase condition:** $\angle H(j\omega) = -\omega t_0$ (a linear function of $\omega$ with slope $-t_0$)

If both conditions hold for all $\omega$, the system is distortionless. The constant group delay equals the propagation delay: $\tau_g = t_0$.

---

**(b) Numerical solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Magnitude check (1 mark):**

$$\vert H(j\omega)\vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + \omega^2}}$$

This depends on $\omega$ (decreases as $\omega$ increases), so **magnitude distortion is present**.

**Step 2 — Phase check (1 mark):**

$$\angle H(j\omega) = -\tan^{-1}(\omega)$$

This is **not linear** in $\omega$ (it asymptotes to $-90^\circ$), so **phase distortion is present**.

**Step 3 — Conclusion (1 mark):** The system is **NOT distortionless**.

**Step 4 — Output for $x(t) = \cos(2t)$ (4 marks):**

Evaluate $H$ at $\omega_0 = 2$:

$$H(j2) = \frac{1}{1 + j2} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{5}}\, e^{-j\tan^{-1}(2)}$$

$$\vert H(j2)\vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{5}} \approx 0.4472, \quad \angle H(j2) = -\tan^{-1}(2) \approx -63.43^\circ$$

Applying the sinusoidal rule:

$$\boxed{\,y(t) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{5}}\,\cos\!\left(2t - 63.43^\circ\right)\,}$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (Read Carefully Before Exam Day):**
> 1. **Forgetting the $u(t)$ factor in inverse transforms.** When you write $y(t) = e^{-at}\, u(t)$, the unit step MUST be included. Examiners routinely deduct 0.5 – 1 mark for this.
> 2. **Using $\omega$ as frequency in Hz vs. rad/s.** KTU problems almost always work in **rad/s**. If the problem gives $f$ in Hz, **convert first**: $\omega = 2\pi f$.
> 3. **Sign errors in the phase.** $\angle H(j\omega) = -\tan^{-1}(\text{Im}/\text{Re})$ — the negative sign comes from the denominator. Mixing this up gives wrong phases and lost marks.
> 4. **Skipping the "swap order of integration" justification** in derivations. Examiners expect at least a one-line mention of Fubini's theorem or absolute integrability.
> 5. **Conflating $H(s)$ (Laplace) with $H(j\omega)$ (Fourier).** For stable, causal systems, $H(j\omega) = H(s)\vert_{s = j\omega}$. Many students drop the $j$ accidentally.
> 6. **Not stating both conditions** for distortionless transmission. You need **flat magnitude AND linear phase** — saying just one is incomplete and costs at least 1 mark.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Read This 30 Minutes Before the Exam):**

- **Definition:** $H(j\omega) = \mathcal{F}\{h(t)\}$ — the Fourier transform of the impulse response.
- **Core relation:** $Y(j\omega) = H(j\omega)\, X(j\omega)$ — convolution in time $\Leftrightarrow$ multiplication in frequency.
- **Eigenfunction property:** $e^{j\omega_0 t} \xrightarrow{\text{LTI}} H(j\omega_0)\, e^{j\omega_0 t}$. Complex exponentials are eigenfunctions of *every* LTI system.
- **Sinusoidal input:** Output is *same* sinusoid with scaled magnitude $\vert H(j\omega_0)\vert$ and shifted phase $\angle H(j\omega_0)$. **Frequency is unchanged.**
- **Magnitude response:** $\vert H(j\omega)\vert = \sqrt{[\text{Re}]^2 + [\text{Im}]^2}$
- **Phase response:** $\angle H(j\omega) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\text{Im}}{\text{Re}}\right)$ with correct quadrant.
- **Group delay:** $\tau_g(\omega) = -\dfrac{d\,\angle H(j\omega)}{d\omega}$
- **Distortionless system:** $H(j\omega) = K\, e^{-j\omega t_0}$ — **flat magnitude + linear phase**.
- **3-dB bandwidth:** Frequency at which $\vert H(j\omega)\vert = \vert H(j\omega)\vert_{\max}/\sqrt{2}$.
- **Inverse transform pair to memorize:** $\dfrac{1}{a + j\omega} \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}^{-1}} e^{-at}\, u(t)$ (for $a > 0$).
- **Cascade property:** $H_{\text{total}}(j\omega) = H_1(j\omega) \cdot H_2(j\omega)$ for LTI systems in series.
- **Parseval's theorem:** Useful for energy calculations and for relating time-domain and frequency-domain energy.
- **Filter families:** LPF, HPF, BPF, BSF — know the ideal magnitude response sketch for each.
- **Why Fourier?** Solves differential equations via algebra; reveals the spectral content of signals; enables filter design.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Discrete Time Systems: Block Diagram Representation and Mathematical Representation of Discrete-Time Systems. Some Common Elements of Discrete-Time Systems (Adder
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST416 - SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Discrete Time Systems: Block Diagram Representation and Mathematical Representation of Discrete-Time Systems. Some Common Elements of Discrete-Time Systems (Adder

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Discrete-Time System?

A **Discrete-Time (DT) System** is a mathematical operator (or transformation) that maps an input discrete-time signal $x[n]$ to an output discrete-time signal $y[n]$. Formally, it is defined as a function:

$$T\{\,\cdot\,\} : x[n] \;\longmapsto\; y[n]$$

where $n \in \mathbb{Z}$ represents the discrete time index (integer-valued). The transformation $T\{\,\cdot\,\}$ is governed by a set of rules — most commonly a **Linear Constant Coefficient Difference Equation (LCCDE)** — that relates present and past samples of the input and output.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim):** A discrete-time system is a device or algorithm that operates on a discrete-time signal (input) according to a well-defined rule to produce another discrete-time signal (output). When the rule is fixed, the system is called **time-invariant**; when the rule satisfies superposition, the system is **linear**; and when the output depends only on present and past inputs, the system is **causal**.

## 1.2 Two Equivalent Ways to Represent a DT System

A discrete-time system can be fully described by **two complementary representations**:

| Representation | Form | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Mathematical** | Difference equation, operator equation, convolution sum | Analytical manipulation, solving for $y[n]$, stability analysis |
| **Block Diagram** | Interconnection of elementary graphical blocks | Hardware/software implementation, intuitive visualization |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Insight:** In KTU exams, questions of 7–14 marks frequently ask you to **convert from a difference equation to a block diagram** or **vice versa**. Mastering both representations in tandem is non-negotiable.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The Factory Assembly Line

Imagine a small digital-signal factory:

- **Raw material** entering the factory = input samples $x[n], x[n-1], x[n-2], \dots$
- **Machines** on the assembly line = the *adders*, *multipliers*, and *delay units* (the common elements).
- **Finished product** leaving the factory = output samples $y[n]$.
- **The factory blueprint** = block diagram; **The factory's operating manual (rulebook)** = mathematical representation.
