# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question Pattern, 2024 Scheme]**

### Question 1: Define the unit ramp function and state its relation with the unit step function.

**Model Answer:**

The unit ramp function is defined as:
$$r(t) = \begin{cases} t, & t \geq 0 \\ 0, & t < 0 \end{cases} = t \cdot u(t)$$

**Relation with unit step:**
- $r(t)$ is the *integral* of $u(t)$: $\displaystyle r(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{t} u(\tau)\,d\tau$
- $u(t)$ is the *derivative* of $r(t)$: $\displaystyle u(t) = \frac{dr(t)}{dt}$ (for $t \neq 0$)

**Significance:** The ramp models linearly increasing inputs, and its derivative gives the step — establishing a fundamental chain in LTI system analysis.

---

### Question 2: Express the rectangular pulse of amplitude $A$ and width $T$ starting at $t = 0$ using singularity functions.

**Model Answer:**

The rectangular pulse can be constructed as the difference of two time-shifted unit steps:
$$p(t) = A\,[u(t) - u(t - T)]$$

This is interpreted as: switch ON at $t = 0$ (add $A \cdot u(t)$), then switch OFF at $t = T$ (subtract $A \cdot u(t - T)$). The result is a pulse that is $A$ for $0 \leq t < T$ and zero elsewhere.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Pattern (Module 3 Internal Choice):** Answer **ONE** of the following.

---

### ❖ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Pattern]**

> **(a)** Define the unit step function $u(t)$ and unit ramp function $r(t)$ with neat sketches. Derive the relationship $r(t) = \displaystyle\int_{-\infty}^{t} u(\tau)\,d\tau$. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Construct a triangular pulse of peak amplitude $5\,\text{V}$ at $t = 3\,\text{s}$ with base from $t = 0$ to $t = 6\,\text{s}$ using ramp functions. Verify your answer at $t = 0$, $t = 1.5$, $t = 3$, and $t = 6$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Definition of $u(t)$:**
$$u(t) = \begin{cases} 1, & t > 0 \\ 0, & t < 0 \end{cases}$$

The graph is a step jumping from 0 to 1 at the origin.

**Definition of $r(t)$:**
$$r(t) = \begin{cases} t, & t \geq 0 \\ 0, & t < 0 \end{cases}$$

The graph is a straight line through the origin with slope 1 for $t \geq 0$.

**Derivation of the integral relationship:**

For $t \geq 0$:
$$\int_{-\infty}^{t} u(\tau)\,d\tau = \int_{-\infty}^{0} u(\tau)\,d\tau + \int_{0}^{t} u(\tau)\,d\tau$$

Since $u(\tau) = 0$ for $\tau < 0$:
$$\int_{-\infty}^{0} u(\tau)\,d\tau = 0$$

Since $u(\tau) = 1$ for $0 \leq \tau \leq t$:
$$\int_{0}^{t} u(\tau)\,d\tau = [\tau]_0^t = t$$

Therefore:
$$\int_{-\infty}^{t} u(\tau)\,d\tau = t = r(t) \quad \text{for } t \geq 0$$

For $t < 0$:
$$\int_{-\infty}^{t} u(\tau)\,d\tau = 0 = r(t) \quad \text{(since } u(\tau) = 0 \text{ throughout)}$$

Hence proved. $\blacksquare$

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:** Peak $A = 5\,\text{V}$, peak time $T = 3\,\text{s}$, base width $= 2T = 6\,\text{s}$.

**Standard form** for a triangle with peak $A$ at time $T$, base from $0$ to $2T$:
$$x(t) = \frac{A}{T} \left[ r(t) - 2r(t - T) + r(t - 2T) \right]$$

**Substituting** $A = 5$, $T = 3$:
$$x(t) = \frac{5}{3} \left[ r(t) - 2r(t - 3) + r(t - 6) \right]$$

**Verification:**

At $t = 0$:
$$x(0) = \frac{5}{3}\left[0 - 2(0) + 0\right] = 0 \quad \checkmark$$

At $t = 1.5$ (midway up the rising edge, expected value $= 2.5$):
$$x(1.5) = \frac{5}{3}\left[1.5 - 2(0) + 0\right] = \frac{5}{3} \cdot 1.5 = 2.5 \quad \checkmark$$

At $t = 3$ (peak, expected value $= 5$):
$$x(3) = \frac{5}{3}\left[3 - 2(0) + 0\right] = \frac{5}{3} \cdot 3 = 5 \quad \checkmark$$

At $t = 6$ (end, expected value $= 0$):
$$x(6) = \frac{5}{3}\left[6 - 2(3) + 0\right] = \frac{5}{3} \cdot 0 = 0 \quad \checkmark$$

The construction is verified.

---

### ❖ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Pattern]**

> **(a)** With neat diagrams, explain the unit step, unit ramp, and unit impulse functions. Show that $\delta(t) = \dfrac{du(t)}{dt}$ in the distributional sense. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A first-order RC low-pass filter has $R = 2\,\text{k}\Omega$ and $C = 0.5\,\mu\text{F}$. Find the step response $v_{\text{out}}(t)$ when $v_{\text{in}}(t) = 5\,u(t)$ V. Compute the value of $v_{\text{out}}$ at $t = \tau$, $3\tau$, and $5\tau$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Unit Step $u(t)$:** Defined as $u(t) = 1$ for $t > 0$ and $0$ for $t < 0$. The graph shows a discontinuous jump of magnitude 1 at $t = 0$.

**Unit Ramp $r(t)$:** Defined as $r(t) = t$ for $t \geq 0$ and $0$ for $t < 0$. The graph shows a linearly rising line with slope 1 starting from origin.

**Unit Impulse $\delta(t)$:** Defined as $\delta(t) = 0$ for $t \neq 0$ and $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t)\,dt = 1$. It is an idealized spike of infinite height and zero width, with unit area.

**Derivation:** $\delta(t) = \dfrac{du(t)}{dt}$:

Since $u(t)$ jumps by exactly 1 unit at $t = 0$, the *rate of change* at that instant is infinite. By the definition of the derivative in the distributional sense:

$$\frac{du(t)}{dt} = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{u(t) - u(t - \Delta t)}{\Delta t}$$

This gives an infinitesimally thin spike of unit area, which is precisely the unit impulse $\delta(t)$. $\blacksquare$

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:** $R = 2 \times 10^3\,\Omega$, $C = 0.5 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{F}$, $v_{\text{in}}(t) = 5u(t)$ V.

**Time constant:**
$$\tau = RC = (2 \times 10^3)(0.5 \times 10^{-6}) = 10^{-3}\,\text{s} = 1\,\text{ms}$$

**Standard step response of RC circuit** (output across capacitor):
$$v_{\text{out}}(t) = V_{\text{final}}\,(1 - e^{-t/\tau})\,u(t)$$

**Substituting** $V_{\text{final}} = 5$ V:
$$v_{\text{out}}(t) = 5(1 - e^{-1000\,t})\,u(t)\,\text{V}$$

**Evaluation at key instants:**

At $t = \tau = 1\,\text{ms}$:
$$v_{\text{out}}(\tau) = 5(1 - e^{-1}) = 5(1 - 0.3679) = 5(0.6321) = 3.16\,\text{V}$$

At $t = 3\tau = 3\,\text{ms}$:
$$v_{\text{out}}(3\tau) = 5(1 - e^{-3}) = 5(1 - 0.0498) = 5(0.9502) = 4.75\,\text{V}$$

At $t = 5\tau = 5\,\text{ms}$:
$$v_{\text{out}}(5\tau) = 5(1 - e^{-5}) = 5(1 - 0.0067) = 5(0.9933) = 4.97\,\text{V}$$

**Result Summary Table:**

| Time | $v_{\text{out}}$ (V) | % of Final |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $\tau$ | $3.16$ | $63.2\%$ |
| $3\tau$ | $4.75$ | $95.0\%$ |
| $5\tau$ | $4.97$ | $99.3\%$ |

The capacitor effectively charges to its steady-state value within $5\tau$ (settling time).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 Board Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the $u(t)$ factor** in the final answer for synthesized signals. A triangular pulse $x(t) = \frac{5}{3}[r(t) - 2r(t-3) + r(t-6)]$ is **incomplete** — the proper causal form must explicitly include $u(t)$ in each term, or state that all ramp functions are causal.
>
> 2. **Sign error in the derivative of $u(t)$:** Students sometimes write $\frac{du(t)}{dt} = -\delta(t)$ instead of $+\delta(t)$. Remember: $u(t)$ *increases* by 1, so the rate of change is *positive*.
>
> 3. **Confusion between $u(t)$ and $u(-t)$:** $u(t)$ is right-sided (causal). $u(-t)$ is left-sided (anti-causal). This distinction is critical in signal classification.
>
> 4. **Skipping the verification step:** In KTU 2024, sub-parts asking "verify your answer" carry **1–2 marks**. Skipping the verification table will cost you easy points.
>
> 5. **Forgetting the $u(t)$ in step response of RC filter:** The output $v_{\text{out}}(t) = 5(1 - e^{-t/\tau})$ is *valid only for $t \geq 0$*. Always multiply by $u(t)$ to make the answer rigorous.
>
> 6. **Mixing up $r(t)$ with $t \cdot u(t)$:** While they are equivalent, examiners expect the more explicit $r(t)$ notation in construction problems.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3, Unit Advance**

- [ ] **Unit Step $u(t)$** is defined as $1$ for $t > 0$ and $0$ for $t < 0$, with a unit jump discontinuity at the origin.
- [ ] **Unit Ramp $r(t) = t \cdot u(t)$** rises linearly with slope $1$ for $t \geq 0$.
- [ ] **The integration chain:** $\delta(t) \to u(t) \to r(t) \to \frac{t^2}{2}u(t)$ — each is the integral of the previous.
- [ ] **The differentiation chain** is the reverse: $\frac{d}{dt}r(t) = u(t)$, $\frac{d}{dt}u(t) = \delta(t)$ (in distributional sense).
- [ ] **Rectangular pulse** = $A[u(t) - u(t - T)]$.
- [ ] **Triangular pulse** of peak $A$ at $t = T$, base $0$ to $2T$: $\frac{A}{T}[r(t) - 2r(t-T) + r(t-2T)]$.
- [ ] **Time-shifted** $u(t - t_0)$ is a step activated at $t = t_0$.
- [ ] **Time-reversed** $u(-t)$ is an anti-causal step (left-sided).
- [ ] **Step response** of a first-order system: $y(t) = K(1 - e^{-t/\tau})u(t)$.
- [ ] **At one time constant** $\tau$, the step response reaches **63.2%** of its final value.
- [ ] **At $5\tau$**, the system is considered to have **settled** (within 99.3%).
- [ ] **Higher-order singularities** follow the $u_{-n}(t) = \frac{t^{n-1}}{(n-1)!}u(t)$ formula.
- [ ] **Laplace transform pairs:** $u(t) \leftrightarrow \frac{1}{s}$, $r(t) \leftrightarrow \frac{1}{s^2}$, $\delta(t) \leftrightarrow 1$.
- [ ] **Fourier transform of $u(t)$** contains a Dirac comb component: $u(t) \leftrightarrow \frac{1}{j\omega} + \pi\delta(\omega)$.
- [ ] **Energy of $u(t)$ is infinite** — it is a *power signal*, not an energy signal.
- [ ] **KTU ESE weightage:** This topic typically carries **3 marks in Part A** and appears as a **7-mark sub-part** of a 14-mark Module 3 question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Recursive DT Systems and Non-Recursive Discrete Time Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST416 - SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Recursive DT Systems and Non-Recursive Discrete Time Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Discrete-Time (DT) system** processes a discrete-time input sequence $x[n]$ to produce a discrete-time output sequence $y[n]$ according to a well-defined mathematical rule. When this rule is expressed in the form of a **linear constant-coefficient difference equation (LCCDE)**, two fundamental structural families emerge:

### (a) Recursive Discrete-Time System
A **Recursive DT system** (also called an **IIR — Infinite Impulse Response system**) is one in which the present output $y[n]$ depends on **one or more past values of the output** $y[n-1], y[n-2], \dots$ in addition to present/past inputs.

$$y[n] = -\sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k \, y[n-k] + \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k \, x[n-k]$$

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> A recursive system has **feedback** — the output is fed back to influence its own future values. The order $N$ of the system is the highest delay on the output side.

### (b) Non-Recursive Discrete-Time System
A **Non-Recursive DT system** (also called an **FIR — Finite Impulse Response system**) is one in which the present output $y[n]$ depends **exclusively on present and past input samples** $x[n], x[n-1], \dots, x[n-M]$ and **not on any past outputs**.

$$y[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k \, x[n-k]$$

> [!NOTE] **Key Distinction:**
> Non-recursive = **No feedback term** = all output-coefficients $a_k$ are zero. The output is a finite, weighted sum of inputs.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!TIP] **Plain-English Analogy — The Two Bank Accounts**
>
> Imagine two types of savings accounts:
>
> - **Recursive Account (IIR):** Your interest this month = current deposit + a small fraction of *last month's balance*. Your balance *feeds back* into its own future growth. The system "remembers" itself through feedback.
>
> - **Non-Recursive Account (FIR):** Your interest this month = only a weighted sum of the *last 12 months' deposits*. There is **no feedback** from previous interest. The system "remembers" only inputs over a fixed window.
>
> Just like the recursive account carries a *running memory* through feedback, a recursive DT system carries memory of all past outputs. The non-recursive account uses a *sliding window* of inputs, exactly mirroring a non-recursive DT system.

---

## 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics

- **Order of the system (N):** Highest output delay term — dictates system complexity and number of memory elements.
- **Memory length (M+1):** Number of input samples required to compute one output.
- **System function $H(z)$:** Z-domain transfer function of the system.
- **BIBO Stability criterion:** A recursive system is stable **iff all poles of $H(z)$ lie strictly inside the unit circle** $\vert z \vert < 1$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The General Linear Constant-Coefficient Difference Equation (LCCDE)

Any **LTI discrete-time system** can be characterized by the *N-th order* difference equation:

$$\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k \, y[n-k] = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k \, x[n-k], \quad a_0 = 1$$

**Why this form?**
- The left-hand side (LHS) carries all *output terms* (with $a_0$ normalized to 1).
- The right-hand side (RHS) carries all *input terms*.
- The equation is **linear** (signals are not multiplied with each other) and **time-invariant** (coefficients $a_k, b_k$ are constants, independent of $n$).

### Rearranged (causal) form — solves for $y[n]$:

$$y[n] = -\sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k \, y[n-k] + \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k \, x[n-k]$$

**Classification rule:**

| Condition | System Type | Alias |
|---|---|---|
| Any $a_k \neq 0$ for $k \geq 1$ | **Recursive** | IIR (Infinite Impulse Response) |
| All $a_k = 0$ for $k \geq 1$ | **Non-Recursive** | FIR (Finite Impulse Response) |

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Formula / Property | Description | Boundary / Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $y[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k \, x[n-k]$ | Non-recursive (FIR) output equation | $a_k = 0, \; \forall k \geq 1$ |
| 2 | $y[n] = -\sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k \, y[n-k] + \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k \, x[n-k]$ | Recursive (IIR) output equation | $N \geq 1$ |
| 3 | $H(z) = \dfrac{Y(z)}{X(z)} = \dfrac{\sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k \, z^{-k}}{\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k \, z^{-k}}$ | System function (transfer function) | $a_0 = 1$ normalization |
| 4 | $H(z) = \dfrac{B(z)}{A(z)}$ | Pole-zero form | Poles = roots of $A(z)$ |
| 5 | $h[n] = \mathcal{Z}^{-1}\{H(z)\}$ | Impulse response | Input: $x[n] = \delta[n]$ |
| 6 | **FIR Length:** $h[n]$ is non-zero for $0 \leq n \leq M$ | FIR has finite length $M+1$ | Always stable |
| 7 | **IIR Length:** $h[n]$ has infinite support | IIR has infinitely many non-zero samples | BIBO stable iff all poles satisfy $\vert p_k \vert < 1$ |
| 8 | BIBO Stability: $\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert h[n] \vert < \infty$ | Absolutely summable impulse response | Necessary and sufficient for LTI BIBO stability |
| 9 | $y[n] = x[n] + y[n-1]$ | Accumulator (simple IIR) | $a_1 = -1, b_0 = 1$ |
| 10 | $y[n] = \dfrac{1}{M+1}\sum_{k=0}^{M} x[n-k]$ | Moving average filter (FIR) | All $b_k = \dfrac{1}{M+1}$ |

> [!NOTE] **Mermaid Table Tip for Students:** Memorize formula #3 and #7 — they appear in nearly every KTU board exam question on this topic.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT] **Why does this matter in industry?**
>
> - **Recursive (IIR) systems** are used in **audio equalizers, digital audio crossovers, biomedical signal processors (ECG/EEG filters), and control systems** where sharp frequency-selective response is needed with minimal computational cost. Example: a 2nd-order Butterworth filter achieves equivalent selectivity to a 50-tap FIR with only 5 multiplications per sample.
> - **Non-recursive (FIR) systems** dominate **data transmission, image processing, and phase-critical applications** (e.g., modem pulse-shaping, HDTV video filters) because they can be designed to have **exactly linear phase**, meaning all frequency components are delayed by the same amount — critical for preserving signal waveform shape.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: General Difference Equation → System Function $H(z)$

**Goal:** Convert the LCCDE to a transfer function in the z-domain.

### Starting Point
Take the standard LCCDE form:

$$\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k \, y[n-k] = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k \, x[n-k]$$

### Step 1 — Apply the Z-Transform
Using the **time-shift property** of the z-transform: $\mathcal{Z}\{y[n-k]\} = z^{-k} Y(z)$ (assuming zero initial conditions — this is standard for system analysis).

Applying z-transform to **both sides**:

$$\mathcal{Z}\left\{\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k \, y[n-k]\right\} = \mathcal{Z}\left\{\sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k \, x[n-k]\right\}$$

### Step 2 — Use Linearity of Z-Transform
Pull the z-transform inside the summation (linearity):

$$\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k \, \mathcal{Z}\{y[n-k]\} = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k \, \mathcal{Z}\{x[n-k]\}$$

### Step 3 — Apply Time-Shift Property
Substitute $\mathcal{Z}\{y[n-k]\} = z^{-k} Y(z)$ and $\mathcal{Z}\{x[n-k]\} = z^{-k} X(z)$:

$$\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k \, z^{-k} Y(z) = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k \, z^{-k} X(z)$$

### Step 4 — Factor Out $Y(z)$ and $X(z)$
Both $Y(z)$ and $X(z)$ are independent of the summation index $k$, so they factor out:

$$Y(z) \sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k \, z^{-k} = X(z) \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k \, z^{-k}$$

### Step 5 — Solve for the Ratio $H(z) = Y(z)/X(z)$

$$H(z) = \frac{Y(z)}{X(z)} = \frac{\sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k \, z^{-k}}{\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k \, z^{-k}}$$

> [!NOTE] **Interpretation:** The numerator polynomial contains the **zeros** of the system. The denominator polynomial contains the **poles** of the system. The presence of a non-trivial denominator (i.e., any $a_k \neq 0$) is what makes a system **recursive**.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 1: Recursive (IIR) System

**System:** $y[n] - \dfrac{1}{2} y[n-1] = x[n]$

**Step 1 — Identify coefficients:** $a_0 = 1, \; a_1 = -\dfrac{1}{2}, \; b_0 = 1, \; M = 0, \; N = 1$

**Step 2 — Apply the system function formula:**

$$H(z) = \frac{b_0 \, z^{0}}{a_0 \, z^{0} + a_1 \, z^{-1}} = \frac{1}{1 - \dfrac{1}{2} z^{-1}}$$

**Step 3 — Multiply numerator and denominator by $z$ to get positive powers:**

$$H(z) = \frac{z}{z - \dfrac{1}{2}}$$

**Step 4 — Pole location:** Pole at $z = \dfrac{1}{2}$. Since $\left\vert \dfrac{1}{2} \right\vert < 1$, the system is **BIBO stable**.

**Step 5 — Find impulse response via inverse z-transform:**

$$H(z) = \frac{z}{z - \dfrac{1}{2}} \quad \xrightarrow{\mathcal{Z}^{-1}} \quad h[n] = \left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right)^{n} u[n]$$

> [!IMPORTANT] **Observation:** $h[n]$ is non-zero for **infinitely many values of $n$** — hence the name *Infinite Impulse Response (IIR)*. The system is recursive because of the feedback term $\dfrac{1}{2} y[n-1]$.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 2: Non-Recursive (FIR) System

**System:** $y[n] = x[n] + \dfrac{1}{2} x[n-1] + \dfrac{1}{3} x[n-2]$

**Step 1 — Identify coefficients:** $a_0 = 1, \; a_k = 0 \; \forall k \geq 1, \; b_0 = 1, \; b_1 = \dfrac{1}{2}, \; b_2 = \dfrac{1}{3}, \; M = 2, \; N = 0$

**Step 2 — Apply the system function formula (denominator is 1 since all $a_k = 0$ for $k \geq 1$):**

$$H(z) = b_0 + b_1 z^{-1} + b_2 z^{-2} = 1 + \dfrac{1}{2} z^{-1} + \dfrac{1}{3} z^{-2}$$

**Step 3 — Pole location:** Denominator is 1, so there are **no finite poles** (poles only at $z = 0$, inside the unit circle). The system is **always BIBO stable**.

**Step 4 — Impulse response:** Substitute $x[n] = \delta[n]$:

$$h[n] = \delta[n] + \dfrac{1}{2} \delta[n-1] + \dfrac{1}{3} \delta[n-2]$$

$$h[n] = \begin{cases} 1, & n = 0 \\ \dfrac{1}{2}, & n = 1 \\ \dfrac{1}{3}, & n = 2 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

> [!NOTE] **Observation:** $h[n]$ is non-zero only for $n = 0, 1, 2$ — three samples. Hence *Finite Impulse Response (FIR)*. The system is non-recursive because output depends **only** on inputs.

---

## 3.4 Summary Comparison Table

| Property | Recursive (IIR) | Non-Recursive (FIR) |
|---|---|---|
| Feedback? | **Yes** (output fed back) | **No** |
| General form | $y[n] = -\sum a_k y[n-k] + \sum b_k x[n-k]$ | $y[n] = \sum b_k x[n-k]$ |
| Poles | Finite, possibly outside origin | All at $z=0$ (or no finite poles) |
| Impulse response | Infinite in length | Finite length $M+1$ |
| Stability | Conditional (poles inside unit circle) | **Always stable** |
| Phase response | Non-linear (in general) | Can be designed **linear phase** |
| Computational cost per output | Low | High (especially for sharp filters) |
| Memory needed | Stores past outputs **and** inputs | Stores past inputs only |
| Typical application | Audio EQ, control systems, sharp filters | Data communication, image processing |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Non-Recursive (FIR) System — Direct Form

This shows an FIR filter $y[n] = b_0 x[n] + b_1 x[n-1] + b_2 x[n-2]$ using delay elements $z^{-1}$ (a delay by one sample), multipliers (triangles), and a summer.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef inputStyle fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#0277BD,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef delayStyle fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef multStyle fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef sumStyle fill:#FCE4EC,stroke:#C2185B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef outputStyle fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#6A1B9A,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    xNode["x of n INPUT"]:::inputStyle
    b0Mul["b0 MULTIPLY BY b0"]:::multStyle
    dly1["z-1 DELAY 1 SAMPLE"]:::delayStyle
    b1Mul["b1 MULTIPLY BY b1"]:::multStyle
    dly2["z-1 DELAY 1 SAMPLE"]:::delayStyle
    b2Mul["b2 MULTIPLY BY b2"]:::multStyle
    sumA["SUM ADD ALL PATHS"]:::sumStyle
    yNode["y of n OUTPUT"]:::outputStyle

    xNode -- x of n --> b0Mul
    xNode -- x of n-1 --> dly1
    dly1 -- x of n-1 --> b1Mul
    dly1 -- x of n-2 --> dly2
    dly2 -- x of n-2 --> b2Mul
    b0Mul -- b0 x of n --> sumA
    b1Mul -- b1 x of n-1 --> sumA
    b2Mul -- b2 x of n-2 --> sumA
    sumA -- y of n --> yNode
```

> [!NOTE] **Reading the diagram:** The signal flows left-to-right. Each $z^{-1}$ block delays its input by one sample. Each triangular multiplier scales the signal. The summer adds the three scaled branches. **No signal flows backward — hence no feedback, hence non-recursive.**

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Recursive (IIR) System — Direct Form I

This shows the system $y[n] = x[n] + a_1 y[n-1] + a_2 y[n-2]$ — output feedback is present.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef inputStyle fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#0277BD,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef delayStyle fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef multStyle fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef sumStyle fill:#FCE4EC,stroke:#C2185B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef outputStyle fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#6A1B9A,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    xNode["x of n INPUT"]:::inputStyle
    bSum["SUM ADD INPUT AND FEEDBACK"]:::sumStyle
    yOut["y of n OUTPUT NODE"]:::outputStyle
    aMul1["a1 MULTIPLY BY a1"]:::multStyle
    dlyY1["z-1 OUTPUT DELAY 1"]:::delayStyle
    dlyY2["z-1 OUTPUT DELAY 2"]:::delayStyle
    aMul2["a2 MULTIPLY BY a2"]:::multStyle

    xNode -- x of n --> bSum
    bSum -- internal y --> yOut
    yOut -- feedback y of n-1 --> aMul1
    aMul1 -- a1 y of n-1 --> dlyY1
    yOut -- feedback y of n-1 --> dlyY1
    dlyY1 -- y of n-1 --> aMul1
    aMul1 -- a1 y of n-2 --> dlyY2
    dlyY2 -- y of n-2 --> aMul2
    aMul2 -- a2 y of n-2 --> bSum
    aMul1 -- a1 y of n-1 --> bSum
```

> [!IMPORTANT] **Reading the diagram:** The output $y[n]$ is taken from the summer and **looped back** through delay elements $z^{-1}$ and multipliers $a_1, a_2$, then re-injected into the same summer. This **closed loop** is the hallmark of a **recursive system**.

---

## 4.3 Functional Architecture Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef topStyle fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#0D47A1,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef firStyle fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef iirStyle fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef propStyle fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#4A148C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    topNode["DT SYSTEM CLASSIFICATION"]:::topStyle
    firNode["NON RECURSIVE FIR SYSTEM"]:::firStyle
    iirNode["RECURSIVE IIR SYSTEM"]:::iirStyle

    propF1["Output depends ONLY on inputs"]:::propStyle
    propF2["No feedback path"]:::propStyle
    propF3["Finite impulse response h n of length M+1"]:::propStyle
    propF4["Always BIBO stable"]:::propStyle
    propF5["Can achieve exact linear phase"]:::propStyle

    propI1["Output depends on inputs AND past outputs"]:::propStyle
    propI2["Contains feedback path through z-1 delays"]:::propStyle
    propI3["Infinite impulse response h n"]:::propStyle
    propI4["BIBO stable iff all poles inside unit circle"]:::propStyle
    propI5["Computationally efficient for sharp filters"]:::propStyle

    topNode --> firNode
    topNode --> iirNode
    firNode --> propF1
    firNode --> propF2
    firNode --> propF3
    firNode --> propF4
    firNode --> propF5
    iirNode --> propI1
    iirNode --> propI2
    iirNode --> propI3
    iirNode --> propI4
    iirNode --> propI5
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Differentiate between recursive and non-recursive discrete-time systems. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Recursive DT System | Non-Recursive DT System |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Output depends on past outputs and present/past inputs | Output depends only on present/past inputs |
| Feedback | Present | Absent |
| Impulse response | Infinite length (IIR) | Finite length (FIR) |
| Stability | Conditional (poles inside unit circle) | Always stable |

**Examples:**
- *Recursive:* $y[n] = y[n-1] + x[n]$ (accumulator)
- *Non-Recursive:* $y[n] = x[n] + 0.5 x[n-1] + 0.25 x[n-2]$ (3-tap FIR)

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for the comparison, 1 mark for examples)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**State the condition for BIBO stability of a recursive discrete-time LTI system in terms of its system function $H(z)$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A recursive LTI discrete-time system with system function

$$H(z) = \frac{\sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k \, z^{-k}}{\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k \, z^{-k}}$$

is **BIBO stable if and only if all the poles of $H(z)$ lie strictly inside the unit circle** in the z-plane, i.e.,

$$\left\vert p_k \right\vert < 1, \quad \text{for all poles } p_k \text{ of } H(z)$$

Equivalently, the impulse response $h[n]$ must be **absolutely summable:**

$$\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \left\vert h[n] \right\vert < \infty$$

*(1 mark for stating the system function form, 1 mark for the pole condition, 1 mark for the equivalent impulse-response condition)*

---

## 📝 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A — [KTU University Exam — June 2024] (14 Marks)**

**(a)** For the discrete-time system described by the difference equation
$$y[n] - \tfrac{1}{4} y[n-1] = x[n] + x[n-1]$$
determine:
  (i) the system function $H(z)$,
  (ii) the pole-zero plot description,
  (iii) whether the system is recursive or non-recursive. Justify.
**(7 marks)**

**(b)** Determine the impulse response $h[n]$ of the system in part (a) and state whether the system is BIBO stable.
**(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Question A

##### Part (a) (7 marks)

**(i) System function $H(z)$ — 3 marks**

**Step 1:** Identify coefficients: $a_0 = 1, \; a_1 = -\dfrac{1}{4}, \; b_0 = 1, \; b_1 = 1, \; M = 1, \; N = 1$.

**Step 2:** Apply the system function formula:

$$H(z) = \frac{b_0 + b_1 z^{-1}}{a_0 + a_1 z^{-1}} = \frac{1 + z^{-1}}{1 - \tfrac{1}{4} z^{-1}}$$

**Step 3:** Multiply numerator and denominator by $z$ to express in positive powers of $z$:

$$H(z) = \frac{z + 1}{z - \tfrac{1}{4}}$$

**(ii) Pole-Zero Plot — 2 marks**

- **Zeros:** Solve $z + 1 = 0 \Rightarrow z = -1$
- **Poles:** Solve $z - \dfrac{1}{4} = 0 \Rightarrow z = \dfrac{1}{4}$
- The zero lies **on the unit circle** at $z = -1$ and the pole lies **inside the unit circle** at $z = \dfrac{1}{4}$ on the positive real axis.

**(iii) Recursive or Non-Recursive — 2 marks**

The system is **Recursive (IIR)** because:
- The coefficient $a_1 = -\dfrac{1}{4} \neq 0$, meaning $y[n]$ depends on $y[n-1]$.
- Equivalently, the system has a **non-trivial pole** at $z = \dfrac{1}{4}$ (not at the origin).
- The system function has a non-constant denominator polynomial.

##### Part (b) (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Expand $H(z)$ using partial fractions:**

$$H(z) = \frac{z + 1}{z - \tfrac{1}{4}}$$

Express as: $H(z) = A + \dfrac{B}{z - \tfrac{1}{4}}$. Long division: $z + 1 = 1 \cdot \left(z - \dfrac{1}{4}\right) + \dfrac{5}{4}$, so

$$H(z) = 1 + \frac{\tfrac{5}{4}}{z - \tfrac{1}{4}}$$

**Step 2 — Apply inverse z-transform using the standard pair $\dfrac{z}{z-a} \leftrightarrow a^n u[n]$:**

Rewrite: $\dfrac{\tfrac{5}{4}}{z - \tfrac{1}{4}} = z^{-1} \cdot \dfrac{\tfrac{5}{4} z}{z - \tfrac{1}{4}}$

Therefore:

$$h[n] = \delta[n] + \tfrac{5}{4} \left(\tfrac{1}{4}\right)^{n-1} u[n-1]$$

Equivalently:

$$h[n] = \begin{cases} 1, & n = 0 \\ \tfrac{5}{4} \left(\tfrac{1}{4}\right)^{n-1}, & n \geq 1 \\ 0, & n < 0 \end{cases}$$

**Step 3 — BIBO Stability — 2 marks:**

The only pole is at $z = \dfrac{1}{4}$, and $\left\vert \dfrac{1}{4} \right\vert < 1$. Therefore, the system **is BIBO stable**.

Alternatively, verify absolute summability:
$$\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \left\vert h[n] \right\vert = 1 + \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \tfrac{5}{4} \left(\tfrac{1}{4}\right)^{n-1} = 1 + \tfrac{5}{4} \cdot \dfrac{1}{1 - 1/4} = 1 + \tfrac{5}{3} = \tfrac{8}{3} < \infty \quad \checkmark$$

---

### **Question B — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] (Alternative to Question A) (14 Marks)**

**(a)** A non-recursive discrete-time system is described by
$$y[n] = 3 x[n] - 2 x[n-1] + x[n-2]$$
Find the system function $H(z)$ and the impulse response $h[n]$. Verify that the system is BIBO stable.
**(7 marks)**

**(b)** A recursive DT system is given by
$$y[n] = \tfrac{1}{2} y[n-1] + 2 x[n]$$
Determine: (i) the difference equation in standard form, (ii) the system function $H(z)$, (iii) the impulse response $h[n]$ for $n = 0, 1, 2, 3$, and (iv) whether the system is stable.
**(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Question B

##### Part (a) (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Identify coefficients:** $b_0 = 3, \; b_1 = -2, \; b_2 = 1, \; M = 2$, all $a_k = 0$ for $k \geq 1$.

**Step 2 — System function (2 marks):**

$$H(z) = 3 - 2 z^{-1} + z^{-2} = \frac{3 z^{2} - 2 z + 1}{z^{2}}$$

**Step 3 — Impulse response (3 marks):** Apply $x[n] = \delta[n]$:

$$h[n] = 3 \delta[n] - 2 \delta[n-1] + \delta[n-2]$$

In tabular form:

| $n$ | $h[n]$ |
|---|---|
| 0 | 3 |
| 1 | $-2$ |
| 2 | 1 |
| $\geq 3$ | 0 |

**Step 4 — BIBO stability (2 marks):**

$$\sum_{n=0}^{2} \left\vert h[n] \right\vert = 3 + 2 + 1 = 6 < \infty$$

Since the impulse response is absolutely summable and all poles ($z = 0$ with multiplicity 2) lie inside the unit circle, the system is **BIBO stable**. This is also expected because **every FIR system is unconditionally stable**.

##### Part (b) (7 marks)

**(i) Standard difference equation (1 mark):**

$$y[n] - \tfrac{1}{2} y[n-1] = 2 x[n]$$

**(ii) System function (2 marks):**

$$H(z) = \frac{2}{1 - \tfrac{1}{2} z^{-1}} = \frac{2 z}{z - \tfrac{1}{2}}$$

**(iii) Impulse response for $n = 0, 1, 2, 3$ (3 marks):**

Set $x[n] = \delta[n]$, assume zero initial conditions:

- $n = 0$: $h[0] = \tfrac{1}{2} h[-1] + 2 \delta[0] = 0 + 2 = 2$
- $n = 1$: $h[1] = \tfrac{1}{2} h[0] + 2 \delta[1] = \tfrac{1}{2}(2) + 0 = 1$
- $n = 2$: $h[2] = \tfrac{1}{2} h[1] + 0 = \tfrac{1}{2}$
- $n = 3$: $h[3] = \tfrac{1}{2} h[2] = \tfrac{1}{4}$

| $n$ | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $h[n]$ | 2 | 1 | 0.5 | 0.25 |

General form: $h[n] = 2 \left(\tfrac{1}{2}\right)^{n} u[n]$.

**(iv) Stability (1 mark):**

The pole is at $z = \dfrac{1}{2}$, with $\left\vert \dfrac{1}{2} \right\vert < 1$. The system **is BIBO stable**.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE MARKS on this topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $z^{-1}$ with $-z$:** The $z^{-1}$ operator represents a *unit delay*, not a sign inversion or a reciprocal. Drawing block diagrams with the wrong delay semantics is a common error.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to normalize $a_0$:** Many students write the system function as $\dfrac{Y(z)}{X(z)} = \dfrac{\sum b_k z^{-k}}{\sum a_k z^{-k}}$ without enforcing $a_0 = 1$. The correct formula assumes $a_0 = 1$. If your difference equation has $a_0 \neq 1$, divide both sides by $a_0$ first.
>
> 3. **Stability misjudgment for FIR systems:** A student once wrote *"FIR systems can be unstable"* — this is **always wrong** in the BIBO sense. All FIR systems are unconditionally stable.
>
> 4. **Mixing up impulse response with output:** $h[n]$ is the response to $\delta[n]$, not to $x[n] = u[n]$. The step response $s[n]$ is $\sum h[k]$.
>
> 5. **Skipping the pole-zero check:** For recursive systems, always mention pole locations explicitly. Board examiners award the "stability" mark only when pole magnitudes are stated numerically.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ A **non-recursive (FIR) system** has $y[n]$ dependent only on inputs — no $y[n-k]$ terms.
- ✅ A **recursive (IIR) system** has at least one $y[n-k]$ term on the RHS — i.e., at least one $a_k \neq 0$ for $k \geq 1$.
- ✅ **Master formula:** $H(z) = \dfrac{\sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k z^{-k}}{\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k z^{-k}}$ with $a_0 = 1$.
- ✅ **FIR is always stable** (poles only at $z=0$). **IIR is stable iff $\vert p_k \vert < 1$ for all poles**.
- ✅ FIR impulse response has finite support; IIR impulse response has infinite support.
- ✅ For exam derivations, always show: coefficient identification → z-transform → algebraic manipulation → inverse z-transform → stability check.
- ✅ Block diagram of an IIR contains a **feedback loop**; block diagram of an FIR has **no feedback path**.
- ✅ Linear phase is achievable with FIR but generally not with IIR.
- ✅ BIBO stability test: $\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert h[n] \vert < \infty$ is **necessary and sufficient** for LTI BIBO stability.
- ✅ Always state pole magnitudes numerically, not just "inside the unit circle", in board answers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Relaxed System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST416 - SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Relaxed System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the rigorous framework of **Signals and Systems** (per the KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 3 syllabus), a system is said to be **Relaxed** at an initial time instant $t = t_0$ if and only if its output $y(t)$ is identically zero for all $t \leq t_0$ whenever the corresponding input $x(t)$ is identically zero for all $t \leq t_0$.

Mathematically, the relaxed condition is expressed as:

$$y(t) = 0 \quad \text{for all } t \leq t_0, \quad \text{whenever} \quad x(t) = 0 \quad \text{for all } t \leq t_0$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECST416 – Module 3):**
> A *relaxed system* is a system that possesses **zero initial conditions** (or zero initial state) at the reference time instant. This means the system has no residual memory, no stored energy, and no latent response before an excitation is applied. Relaxed systems form the **foundational prerequisite** for defining the impulse response $h(t)$ of an LTI system, for executing the convolution integral, and for analyzing system behavior using zero-state and zero-input responses.

### 1.2 Concept of Initial Conditions

The "initial conditions" of a system are the values (and derivatives) of the output signal stored in the system memory **just before** the input is applied. For a continuous-time (CT) system, these typically include:

$$y(t_0^-), \; \frac{dy}{dt}\bigg|_{t_0^-}, \; \frac{d^2 y}{dt^2}\bigg|_{t_0^-}, \dots, \frac{d^{n-1} y}{dt^{n-1}}\bigg|_{t_0^-}$$

A system is **relaxed** if and only if **all** these initial conditions are zero.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Empty Bucket**
> Imagine a brand-new, empty **bucket** sitting at the edge of a garden. This bucket represents your system.
> - **The empty bucket** = a **relaxed system**. It has no water (no energy, no memory, no prior response).
> - **Pouring water into it** = applying an **input signal** $x(t)$.
> - **The water level** = the **output signal** $y(t)$.
>
> The output $y(t)$ that you observe in the bucket is **entirely** due to the water you have just poured in. There is no "old water" hiding at the bottom, no leaks from a previous session. The bucket's behavior is **purely determined by the current input**, starting from a known zero state.
>
> **Counter-example:** A bucket that already has 2 liters of stale water at the bottom is a **non-relaxed system**. Its response to a new input will be contaminated by the initial 2 liters — you cannot predict the true output behavior of the system without first knowing this hidden initial condition.

### 1.4 Why the Concept of "Relaxed" is Critical in KTU

In the KTU 2024 Scheme Signals and Systems course, the word "relaxed" is the *gateway assumption* for almost every analytical tool you will use:

| Topic in Module 3 | Why "Relaxed" Matters |
|---|---|
| Impulse Response $h(t)$ | Defined only for a relaxed LTI system |
| Convolution Integral | $y(t) = x(t) * h(t)$ is valid for relaxed systems |
| Zero-State Response | By definition, output when system is relaxed |
| Zero-Input Response | Output due to initial conditions (non-zero) |
| Linearity & Time-Invariance | Assumes the relaxed condition for unique decomposition |

---

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Mnemonic for Exams:** A relaxed system is a system with **No Past, No Memory, No Energy** at $t = t_0^-$. Its output is *causally* and *uniquely* determined solely by the input applied for $t > t_0$.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown

The notion of a relaxed system is built on three pillars. Let us deconstruct each.

### Pillar 1: The "If-and-Only-If" Condition
A system is relaxed **if and only if**:
- **Condition A (Sufficient):** $x(t) = 0$ for $t \leq t_0$ $\Longrightarrow$ $y(t) = 0$ for $t \leq t_0$
- **Condition B (Necessary):** Whenever the output is observed to be zero prior to $t_0$, it must be because the input was zero. (Equivalent to: there is no hidden internal source producing output.)

### Pillar 2: The Reference Time Instant $t_0$
The reference instant $t_0$ is *arbitrary* but is usually chosen to be $t_0 = 0$ or $t_0 = -\infty$ in KTU problems.
- If $t_0 = -\infty$: The system is relaxed at $-\infty$, meaning the system was at rest at the beginning of time.
- If $t_0 = 0$: The system is relaxed at $t = 0^-$, meaning the system was at rest just before the input was switched on at $t = 0$.

### Pillar 3: The Output Decomposition Theorem
For a general (not necessarily relaxed) LTI system, the total output is the superposition of two components:

$$y(t) = y_{zi}(t) + y_{zs}(t)$$

where:
- $y_{zi}(t)$ = **Zero-Input Response** — the output produced *solely* by the initial conditions (with $x(t) = 0$ for $t > t_0$).
- $y_{zs}(t)$ = **Zero-State Response** — the output produced *solely* by the input $x(t)$ (with the system initially relaxed).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** The *zero-state response* $y_{zs}(t)$ is by definition the response of a **relaxed system**. The convolution integral $y(t) = x(t) * h(t)$ computes exactly this zero-state response. This is why the impulse response $h(t)$ is defined exclusively on a relaxed LTI system.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **#** | **Concept / Condition** | **Mathematical Expression** | **Physical / Engineering Meaning** |
|:---:|---|---|---|
| 1 | Relaxed System Definition | $y(t) = 0 \; \forall \; t \leq t_0$ when $x(t) = 0 \; \forall \; t \leq t_0$ | System at rest; no stored energy |
| 2 | Zero Initial Condition (CT) | $y(0^-) = 0$ | Output is zero just before $t=0$ |
| 3 | All Initial Conditions Zero (n-th order) | $y^{(k)}(0^-) = 0$ for $k = 0, 1, \dots, n-1$ | Complete relaxation of n-th order system |
| 4 | Zero-State Response | $y_{zs}(t) = x(t) * h(t)$ | Output for a relaxed LTI system |
| 5 | Zero-Input Response | $y_{zi}(t) = $ solution of homogeneous eqn with ICs | Output due to non-zero ICs only |
| 6 | Total Output Decomposition | $y(t) = y_{zi}(t) + y_{zs}(t)$ | Superposition principle applied |
| 7 | Impulse Response (LTI, Relaxed) | $h(t) = T[\{\delta(t)\}, t]$ | Response of relaxed LTI system to $\delta(t)$ |
| 8 | Causal LTI Relaxed System | $h(t) = 0$ for $t < 0$ | No output before input is applied |
| 9 | Step Response (Relaxed) | $s(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{t} h(\tau) \, d\tau$ | Cumulative response from rest |

> **Note on the table:** Vertical bar symbols (used in the absolute value / condition notation above) have been replaced with their LaTeX equivalents to preserve the markdown table structure.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The concept of relaxation is not merely academic. It is foundational in:

- **Circuit Analysis (EEE/ECE):** When analyzing an RC/RLC circuit that has been disconnected for a long time, the capacitor is assumed fully discharged ($v_C(0^-) = 0$) and the inductor is current-free ($i_L(0^-) = 0$). This is the *relaxed circuit* assumption.
- **Control Systems:** State-space analysis uses the *zero-state response* matrix to compute output purely from input — this requires the initial state vector $\mathbf{x}(0) = \mathbf{0}$.
- **Digital Signal Processing (DSP):** When implementing an FIR filter, the delay line is initialized to zero — the filter starts in a relaxed state.
- **Communication Systems:** Matched filters in radar/sonar are designed assuming the receiver is initially relaxed (no residual echoes).

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Uniqueness of Response for a Relaxed LTI System

**Objective:** To prove that for a *relaxed* LTI system, the output $y(t)$ for $t > t_0$ is *uniquely* determined by the input $x(t)$ for $t > t_0$.

### Step 1 — Setup the Input as a Combination of Two Signals
Decompose an arbitrary input $x(t)$ (defined for $t > t_0$) into two parts:

$$x(t) = x_1(t) + x_2(t)$$

where $x_1(t)$ and $x_2(t)$ are two *different* input signals that are **identical** for $t > t_0$ but **different** for $t \leq t_0$.

**Logic:** We want to show that no matter how we choose the past (i.e., for $t \leq t_0$), the future output (for $t > t_0$) remains the same.

### Step 2 — Define the Past Difference Signal
Let us define the difference signal:

$$x_d(t) = x_1(t) - x_2(t)$$

By construction, $x_d(t) = 0$ for all $t > t_0$ (since the two inputs are identical in the future). However, for $t \leq t_0$, $x_d(t)$ may be non-zero.

### Step 3 — Apply Linearity and Time-Invariance
Let $y_1(t)$ and $y_2(t)$ be the outputs corresponding to inputs $x_1(t)$ and $x_2(t)$ respectively. By linearity:

$$y_d(t) = y_1(t) - y_2(t) = \mathcal{T}\{x_d(t)\}$$

where $\mathcal{T}\{\cdot\}$ denotes the system operator.

### Step 4 — Invoke the Relaxed Condition
Since $x_d(t) = 0$ for all $t > t_0$, and assuming $x_d(t) = 0$ for $t \leq t_0$ as well (worst case, if the system is even relaxed under the difference signal), the relaxed system condition forces:

$$y_d(t) = 0 \quad \text{for all } t$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Step:** Even if $x_d(t)$ is non-zero for $t \leq t_0$ (because the two inputs differ in the past), a *relaxed* LTI system still produces a well-defined response. However, the *uniqueness* property specifically requires the past outputs to coincide. The relaxed condition ensures that the system's response for $t > t_0$ depends **only** on the input for $t > t_0$, regardless of what happened before $t_0$.

### Step 5 — Conclude Uniqueness
Since $y_d(t) = 0$ for $t > t_0$, we have $y_1(t) = y_2(t)$ for all $t > t_0$. This proves that the output of a relaxed LTI system is **uniquely** determined by the input applied after $t_0$, irrespective of the input history before $t_0$.

$$\boxed{\; y(t) \text{ for } t > t_0 \text{ is uniquely determined by } x(\tau) \text{ for } \tau > t_0 \;}$$

## 3.2 Worked Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** Consider a system described by the differential equation:

$$y'(t) + 2y(t) = x(t)$$

Determine whether the system is relaxed at $t = 0$ given that $x(t) = 0$ for $t < 0$ and $y(0^-) = 5$.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Check the input condition. Given $x(t) = 0$ for $t < 0$, this satisfies the prerequisite of the relaxed system definition (input is zero for $t \leq t_0 = 0$).

**Step 2:** Solve the homogeneous equation (since input is zero for $t < 0$):

$$y_h(t) = C e^{-2t}$$

**Step 3:** Apply the initial condition $y(0^-) = 5$:

$$y_h(0^-) = C e^{0} = C = 5$$

So $y_h(t) = 5 e^{-2t}$ for $t < 0$.

**Step 4:** Evaluate the output for $t < 0$:

$$y(t) = 5 e^{-2t} \neq 0 \quad \text{for } t < 0$$

**Step 5:** Conclude:

> [!WARNING]
> The output $y(t) \neq 0$ for $t \leq 0$ even though $x(t) = 0$ for $t \leq 0$. Therefore, the system is **NOT relaxed** at $t = 0$. The non-zero initial condition $y(0^-) = 5$ violates the relaxed condition.

**Step 6 — Remedy:** For the system to be relaxed, the initial condition must be:

$$y(0^-) = 0$$

With $y(0^-) = 0$, the solution to the homogeneous equation becomes $y_h(t) = 0$, and the system satisfies the relaxed condition.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Relaxed vs Non-Relaxed Systems

| **Property** | **Relaxed System** | **Non-Relaxed System** |
|---|---|---|
| Initial Conditions | All zero ($y^{(k)}(0^-) = 0$) | At least one is non-zero |
| Output for $t \leq t_0$ when $x(t) = 0$ | $y(t) = 0$ | $y(t) \neq 0$ (generally) |
| Response Uniqueness | Output uniquely determined by input for $t > t_0$ | Output depends on both input and ICs |
| Decomposition Applicability | Only $y_{zs}(t)$ is relevant | Both $y_{zs}(t)$ and $y_{zi}(t)$ contribute |
| Impulse Response Valid? | Yes, $h(t)$ is well-defined | Impulse response is not the *total* response |
| Memory / Stored Energy | None at $t_0^-$ | Present (capacitors charged, inductors carry current) |

This schematic shows how a general (non-relaxed) system's total output is decomposed into the zero-input response (which is absent in a relaxed system) and the zero-state response (which is the *only* component in a relaxed system).

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define a relaxed system. What are the necessary conditions for a continuous-time LTI system to be relaxed at $t = 0$?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A continuous-time system is said to be **relaxed** at $t = t_0$ if its output $y(t) = 0$ for all $t \leq t_0$ whenever the input $x(t) = 0$ for all $t \leq t_0$.

**Necessary conditions for an LTI system to be relaxed at $t = 0$:**

$$y(0^-) = 0, \quad y'(0^-) = 0, \quad y''(0^-) = 0, \quad \dots, \quad y^{(n-1)}(0^-) = 0$$

i.e., **all initial conditions on the output (and its derivatives up to order $n-1$) must be zero**, where $n$ is the order of the system. This ensures no stored energy or memory in the system at the reference instant.

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**Distinguish between the zero-input response and the zero-state response of an LTI system. Which one is relevant for a relaxed system?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Zero-Input Response $y_{zi}(t)$ | Zero-State Response $y_{zs}(t)$ |
|---|---|---|
| Cause | Non-zero initial conditions | Input signal $x(t)$ |
| Input | $x(t) = 0$ for $t > t_0$ | Arbitrary, with ICs = 0 |
| System State | May have stored energy | **Relaxed** (zero ICs) |

For a **relaxed system**, all initial conditions are zero, hence the zero-input response $y_{zi}(t) = 0$. The **total output equals the zero-state response** alone:

$$y(t) = y_{zs}(t) = x(t) * h(t)$$

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Pattern (2024 Scheme):** Part B questions carry 14 marks and offer an internal choice. Each question typically has two sub-parts (a) and (b), each carrying 7 marks. Students must answer either the full Question A or the full Question B.

---

### ❖ QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**Consider the continuous-time LTI system described by the differential equation:**

$$y''(t) + 5y'(t) + 6y(t) = 2x'(t) + x(t)$$

**(a)** Determine whether the system is relaxed at $t = 0$ given that $x(t) = 0$ for $t < 0$ and the initial conditions are $y(0^-) = 0$ and $y'(0^-) = 0$. Justify your answer with a complete mathematical analysis. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Now suppose the initial conditions are modified to $y(0^-) = 2$ and $y'(0^-) = -1$, with the same input $x(t) = 0$ for $t < 0$. Compute the output $y(t)$ for $t < 0$ and verify whether the system remains relaxed. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

##### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Verify the input condition.**
Given $x(t) = 0$ for $t < 0$, the input is zero for $t \leq 0^-$. This satisfies the prerequisite of the relaxed system definition.

**Step 2: Check the initial conditions.**
Given $y(0^-) = 0$ and $y'(0^-) = 0$. All initial conditions are zero.

**Step 3: Form the homogeneous equation for $t < 0$.**
With $x(t) = 0$ for $t < 0$, the differential equation becomes:

$$y''(t) + 5y'(t) + 6y(t) = 0$$

**Step 4: Solve the characteristic equation.**

$$s^2 + 5s + 6 = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad (s+2)(s+3) = 0$$

Roots: $s_1 = -2$ and $s_2 = -3$.

**Step 5: Write the homogeneous solution.**

$$y_h(t) = C_1 e^{-2t} + C_2 e^{-3t}$$

**Step 6: Apply initial conditions.**

At $t = 0^-$:

$$y_h(0^-) = C_1 + C_2 = 0$$

$$y_h'(t) = -2C_1 e^{-2t} - 3C_2 e^{-3t}$$

$$y_h'(0^-) = -2C_1 - 3C_2 = 0$$

**Step 7: Solve the system of equations.**

From the first equation: $C_2 = -C_1$. Substituting into the second:

$$-2C_1 - 3(-C_1) = -2C_1 + 3C_1 = C_1 = 0$$

Therefore $C_1 = 0$ and $C_2 = 0$.

**Step 8: Conclude.**

$$y(t) = 0 \quad \text{for all } t < 0$$

Since the output is zero for $t \leq 0$ whenever the input is zero for $t \leq 0$, **the system is RELAXED at $t = 0$**. ✅ **(7 marks)**

---

##### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Same homogeneous equation and solution form.**

$$y_h(t) = C_1 e^{-2t} + C_2 e^{-3t}$$

**Step 2: Apply the new initial conditions $y(0^-) = 2$ and $y'(0^-) = -1$.**

$$C_1 + C_2 = 2$$

$$-2C_1 - 3C_2 = -1$$

**Step 3: Solve the system.**

From the first equation: $C_2 = 2 - C_1$. Substituting:

$$-2C_1 - 3(2 - C_1) = -1$$

$$-2C_1 - 6 + 3C_1 = -1$$

$$C_1 = 5$$

Then $C_2 = 2 - 5 = -3$.

**Step 4: Write the output for $t < 0$.**

$$y(t) = 5 e^{-2t} - 3 e^{-3t} \quad \text{for } t < 0$$

**Step 5: Verify non-zero output.**

At $t = 0^-$:

$$y(0^-) = 5 - 3 = 2 \neq 0$$

Since $y(t) \neq 0$ for $t \leq 0$ even though $x(t) = 0$ for $t \leq 0$, **the system is NOT RELAXED at $t = 0$**. ❌

**Step 6: Identify the zero-input response.**

The total response in the relaxed case (Part a) was 0, so the **zero-input response** (the part due to non-zero ICs alone) is:

$$y_{zi}(t) = 5 e^{-2t} - 3 e^{-3t}, \quad t \geq 0$$ **(7 marks)**

---

### ❖ QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**Consider the causal LTI system whose impulse response is given by:**

$$h(t) = e^{-3t} u(t)$$

**(a)** If the system is initially relaxed, determine the output $y(t)$ when the input is $x(t) = u(t)$ (unit step), using the convolution integral. Show all integration steps. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Verify that your answer in part (a) is consistent with the relaxed system condition. Also determine the zero-input response of the system. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

##### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: State the convolution formula for a relaxed LTI system.**

$$y(t) = x(t) * h(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(\tau) h(t - \tau) \, d\tau$$

**Step 2: Substitute $x(t) = u(t)$ and $h(t) = e^{-3t} u(t)$.**

$$y(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} u(\tau) \cdot e^{-3(t-\tau)} u(t - \tau) \, d\tau$$

**Step 3: Simplify the integrand using the unit step functions.**

- $u(\tau) = 1$ for $\tau \geq 0$, else $0$.
- $u(t - \tau) = 1$ for $\tau \leq t$, else $0$.

The integrand is non-zero only when $0 \leq \tau \leq t$ (this requires $t \geq 0$).

**Step 4: Restrict the integration limits.**

$$y(t) = \int_{0}^{t} e^{-3(t-\tau)} \, d\tau = e^{-3t} \int_{0}^{t} e^{3\tau} \, d\tau$$

**Step 5: Evaluate the inner integral.**

$$\int_{0}^{t} e^{3\tau} \, d\tau = \left[\frac{e^{3\tau}}{3}\right]_{0}^{t} = \frac{e^{3t} - 1}{3}$$

**Step 6: Substitute back.**

$$y(t) = e^{-3t} \cdot \frac{e^{3t} - 1}{3} = \frac{1 - e^{-3t}}{3}$$

**Step 7: Express the final answer with the unit step.**

$$y(t) = \frac{1}{3}\left(1 - e^{-3t}\right) u(t)$$ **(7 marks)**

---

##### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Verify the relaxed system condition.**

For $t < 0$: $y(t) = 0$ (since $u(t) = 0$). For $t \leq 0$, the input $x(t) = u(t) = 0$. The output is identically zero for $t \leq 0$ when the input is zero for $t \leq 0$. The relaxed condition is **satisfied**. ✅

**Step 2: Determine the zero-input response.**

For a relaxed system, the initial conditions are all zero. Therefore:

$$y_{zi}(t) = 0 \quad \text{for all } t$$

**Step 3: Confirm via the decomposition theorem.**

The total response of a general LTI system is:

$$y(t) = y_{zi}(t) + y_{zs}(t)$$

Since the system is relaxed, $y_{zi}(t) = 0$ and $y(t) = y_{zs}(t)$. The result from part (a) is purely the **zero-state response**, computed via convolution. This is consistent. ✅

**Step 4: Physical interpretation.**

The output $\frac{1}{3}(1 - e^{-3t})$ starts from 0 at $t = 0$ and asymptotically approaches $\frac{1}{3}$ as $t \to \infty$. This is the classic **first-order low-pass step response** of a relaxed RC-like system with time constant $\tau = \frac{1}{3}$ and DC gain $\frac{1}{3}$. **(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes KTU Students Make (and How to Avoid Losing Marks):**
>
> 1. **Confusing "zero input" with "relaxed":** A system with $x(t) = 0$ is **not necessarily relaxed**. You must check the initial conditions. A common error is to declare a system relaxed just because the input is zero, earning a 2-mark deduction.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to check ALL initial conditions:** For an n-th order system, you must verify $y(0^-) = 0$, $y'(0^-) = 0$, ..., $y^{(n-1)}(0^-) = 0$. Missing even one leads to a non-relaxed classification.
>
> 3. **Applying convolution to non-relaxed systems:** The formula $y(t) = x(t) * h(t)$ is **only valid** for relaxed LTI systems. If the system has non-zero ICs, you must add the zero-input response separately. A frequent 4-mark deduction.
>
> 4. **Sign errors in characteristic equation roots:** When solving $s^2 + 5s + 6 = 0$, students often write the roots as $s = 2, 3$ (positive) instead of $s = -2, -3$. Always double-check by substitution.
>
> 5. **Not stating the unit step $u(t)$ in the final answer:** Even if your mathematical expression is correct, omitting $u(t)$ for a causal signal in KTU exams often results in a 1-mark deduction because the *support* of the signal is not clearly specified.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — "Relaxed System" (Module 3)**

- ✅ **Definition:** A system is relaxed at $t_0$ if $y(t) = 0$ for $t \leq t_0$ whenever $x(t) = 0$ for $t \leq t_0$.
- ✅ **Equivalent condition:** All initial conditions on $y$ and its derivatives are zero at $t_0^-$.
- ✅ **Physical meaning:** No stored energy, no memory, no latent response.
- ✅ **Why it matters:** The impulse response $h(t)$, convolution $y(t) = x(t) * h(t)$, and zero-state response are all defined exclusively for relaxed systems.
- ✅ **Decomposition Theorem:** $y(t) = y_{zi}(t) + y_{zs}(t)$ — for a relaxed system, $y_{zi}(t) = 0$ and $y(t) = y_{zs}(t)$.
- ✅ **Common reference time:** $t_0 = 0$ (with conditions at $t = 0^-$) is the most frequently used in KTU problems.
- ✅ **Determination procedure:** (1) Check input is zero for $t \leq 0$; (2) Check all ICs are zero; (3) Solve the homogeneous equation and verify $y(t) = 0$ for $t < 0$.
- ✅ **Counter-intuitive point:** A system can be relaxed even with non-zero input, as long as the *initial conditions* are zero. Relaxation is a property of the system's *state*, not of the input signal.
- ✅ **Uniqueness theorem:** For a relaxed LTI system, the output for $t > t_0$ is uniquely determined by the input for $t > t_0$ alone (independent of past input).
- ✅ **Exam keyword to use:** Always write "*the system is relaxed at $t = 0$ since the input is zero for $t \leq 0$ and all initial conditions are zero*" — this single sentence is worth at least 1-2 marks in a 7-mark sub-question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Linearity and Time Invariance Property of a DT System. Discrete Time LTI Systems - Discrete Time Convolution
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST416 - SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Linearity and Time Invariance Property of a DT System. Discrete Time LTI Systems - Discrete Time Convolution

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Linearity of a Discrete-Time (DT) System

A discrete-time system $T\{\cdot\}$ that maps an input sequence $x[n]$ to an output sequence $y[n]$ is said to be **linear** if and only if it satisfies the principle of **superposition**. This principle is the conjunction of two fundamental sub-properties: **Additivity** and **Homogeneity (Scaling)**.

Mathematically, for any arbitrary input sequences $x_1[n]$ and $x_2[n]$, and arbitrary complex scalars $a$ and $b$:

$$T\{a \cdot x_1[n] + b \cdot x_2[n]\} = a \cdot T\{x_1[n]\} + b \cdot T\{x_2[n]\}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A system is linear if and only if the response to a weighted sum of inputs equals the corresponding weighted sum of the individual responses. Both additivity and homogeneity **must** be satisfied simultaneously. A system that satisfies only one of them is **NOT** linear.

### 1.1.1 Additivity
A system is additive if the response to a sum of inputs is equal to the sum of the responses to each individual input acting alone:

$$T\{x_1[n] + x_2[n]\} = T\{x_1[n]\} + T\{x_2[n]\}$$

This is obtained from the linearity equation by setting the scalars $a = b = 1$.

### 1.1.2 Homogeneity (Scaling Property)
A system exhibits homogeneity (or scaling) if a scalar multiplication of the input results in an identical scalar multiplication of the output:

$$T\{a \cdot x[n]\} = a \cdot T\{x[n]\}$$

This is obtained from the linearity equation by setting $x_1[n] = x_2[n] = x[n]$ and $b = 0$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition):**
> Imagine a simple amplifier in your home audio system. Linearity means: "If you play song 1 at volume 5, and song 2 at volume 5, then the amplifier's output to (song 1 + song 2) at volume 5 should be exactly equal to (output of song 1) + (output of song 2)." It is essentially the **"no surprises"** rule. The system does not generate any new frequency components, distort amplitudes inconsistently, or cross-mix the signals in a non-additive manner. A linear system behaves like a perfect, transparent, and predictable mathematical operator.

### 1.1.3 Why Linearity Matters in Engineering

Linearity is the **backbone of modern signal processing**. Because linear systems obey superposition, engineers can decompose any complex input signal (like speech, music, or biomedical data) into elementary basis signals (such as impulses, sines, or exponentials). The total response is then obtained by simply summing the individual elementary responses. This drastically simplifies analysis, design, and real-time implementation of systems in fields like audio equalizers, digital filters, communication receivers, and control systems.

---

## 1.2 Time Invariance (TI) of a Discrete-Time System

A discrete-time system is **time-invariant (TI)** if a time shift in the input signal causes a corresponding identical time shift in the output signal. The system does not change its operational behavior over time; its characteristics remain constant.

For any input $x[n]$ producing output $y[n] = T\{x[n]\}$, and for any arbitrary integer shift $n_0$:

$$T\{x[n - n_0]\} = y[n - n_0]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** A time-invariant system treats all times uniformly. Whether you input a signal today or five years from now, the system's internal response pattern is identical. The system has **no internal "clock"** that modifies how it processes data depending on when the data arrived.

### 1.2.1 Intuitive Analogy for Time Invariance

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition):**
> Consider a digital camera applying a specific sharpening filter to your photographs. Time invariance means: "If you take a photo at 10:00 AM and apply the filter, the sharpened result is the same as if you took the *same* photo at 10:00 PM and applied the filter." The camera does not suddenly decide to blur more aggressively at night — its algorithm is fixed. The system has a "constant character."

### 1.2.2 Engineering Significance of Time Invariance

Time invariance guarantees that a system designed and tested in a laboratory will behave **identically** when deployed in the real world, regardless of the moment of operation. This predictability is critical for **reproducible engineering design** — without TI, every system would have to be recalibrated continuously, which is practically impossible.

---

## 1.3 The Discrete-Time Linear Time-Invariant (LTI) System

A system is classified as **Discrete-Time LTI** if and only if it satisfies **both** linearity **and** time invariance simultaneously. The LTI class is the most extensively studied, well-understood, and widely applied category of systems in signal processing because:

1. They can be **completely characterized** by a single function called the **impulse response $h[n]$**.
2. The output $y[n]$ for any arbitrary input $x[n]$ can be computed using the **convolution sum**.
3. A vast library of analytical and numerical techniques (z-transform, DTFT, difference equations) is available exclusively for LTI systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Point:** The LTI system is the cornerstone of the entire course. Every concept from this point forward — convolution, stability, causality, z-transform — is built on the LTI assumption. Always verify Linearity and Time Invariance **separately** in exam problems before applying convolution.

---

## 1.4 Discrete-Time Convolution: Definition

For an LTI system with impulse response $h[n]$, the output $y[n]$ in response to an arbitrary input $x[n]$ is given by the **linear convolution sum**:

$$y[n] = x[n] * h[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{+\infty} x[k] \cdot h[n - k]$$

The symbol $*$ denotes the **linear convolution operator**. The variable $k$ is a dummy summation index.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy for Convolution:**
> Convolution is like a **sliding weighted average**. Imagine $h[n]$ as a small "sliding window" of past memory. As this window slides across the input signal $x[n]$, at every position $n$, it picks up the past input samples $x[k]$, multiplies each by the corresponding memory coefficient $h[n-k]$, and sums them all up. The output $y[n]$ at time $n$ is therefore a **weighted blend** of past input values, where the weights are dictated by the system's impulse response.

### 1.4.1 Convolution as a Two-Step Process

The convolution operation is computed using two sequential operations:
1. **Folding (Time-Reversal):** Reflect $h[k]$ about the vertical axis to obtain $h[-k]$.
2. **Shifting:** Translate $h[-k]$ by $n$ units along the time axis to obtain $h[n-k]$.
3. **Multiplication and Summation:** Multiply $x[k]$ by $h[n-k]$ point-by-point and add all the products.

This folding-shifting-multiplying-summing process is then repeated for every integer value of $n$ from $-\infty$ to $+\infty$.

---

## 1.5 The Discrete-Time Unit Impulse $\delta[n]$

The **unit impulse** (also called the Kronecker delta or unit sample) is the foundational elementary signal in discrete-time analysis:

$$\delta[n] = \begin{cases} 1, & n = 0 \\ 0, & n \neq 0 \end{cases}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sifting Property of $\delta[n]$:** Any arbitrary sequence $x[n]$ can be expressed as a weighted sum of shifted impulses: $$x[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{+\infty} x[k] \cdot \delta[n - k]$$ This decomposition is what enables the **derivation of the convolution sum** for LTI systems, as we will see in Section 3.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Procedure: Testing Linearity of a DT System

To determine if a system $y[n] = T\{x[n]\}$ is linear, follow this structured procedure:

**Step 1:** Identify the system equation relating $y[n]$ to $x[n]$.

**Step 2:** Compute the response to two arbitrary distinct inputs $x_1[n]$ and $x_2[n]$. Let these be $y_1[n] = T\{x_1[n]\}$ and $y_2[n] = T\{x_2[n]\}$.

**Step 3:** Construct a combined input $x_3[n] = a \cdot x_1[n] + b \cdot x_2[n]$ for arbitrary scalars $a$ and $b$.

**Step 4:** Compute the response to the combined input: $y_3[n] = T\{x_3[n]\}$.

**Step 5:** Compute the weighted sum of individual responses: $y_4[n] = a \cdot y_1[n] + b \cdot y_2[n]$.

**Step 6:** Compare $y_3[n]$ and $y_4[n]$:
- If $y_3[n] = y_4[n]$ for **all** $n$, for **all** possible $x_1[n]$, $x_2[n]$, $a$, and $b$ $\Rightarrow$ **System is LINEAR**.
- If $y_3[n] \neq y_4[n]$ for at least one set of values $\Rightarrow$ **System is NON-LINEAR**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** A common error is to test only with $a = b = 1$. This only verifies **additivity**. A complete linearity test **must** include arbitrary non-unit scalars to verify **homogeneity**. For example, test with $a = 2, b = 3$ as well.

---

## 2.2 Detailed Procedure: Testing Time Invariance of a DT System

**Step 1:** Identify the system equation $y[n] = T\{x[n]\}$.

**Step 2:** Compute the response $y[n]$ to a general input $x[n]$.

**Step 3:** Delay the input by $n_0$ units to get $x[n - n_0]$, then compute the response: $y_{\text{delayed-input}}[n] = T\{x[n - n_0]\}$.

**Step 4:** Delay the original output $y[n]$ by $n_0$ units to get $y[n - n_0]$.

**Step 5:** Compare the two:
- If $y_{\text{delayed-input}}[n] = y[n - n_0]$ for **all** $n$ and **all** $n_0$ $\Rightarrow$ **System is TIME-INVARIANT**.
- If the equality fails for some $n_0$ $\Rightarrow$ **System is TIME-VARYING**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Tip for KTU Exams:** When shifting inside a system equation, the shift must be applied **directly to the argument** of $x[\cdot]$. For example, if $y[n] = n \cdot x[n]$, then $T\{x[n-n_0]\} = n \cdot x[n-n_0]$, but $y[n-n_0] = (n-n_0) \cdot x[n-n_0]$. These are unequal due to the explicit $n$ multiplying $x[n]$ — so the system is time-varying.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Property / Concept** | **Mathematical Expression** | **Conditions / Notes** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Linearity (Superposition) | $T\{a x_1[n] + b x_2[n]\} = a y_1[n] + b y_2[n]$ | Holds for all $x_1, x_2, a, b$ |
| Additivity | $T\{x_1[n] + x_2[n]\} = y_1[n] + y_2[n]$ | Special case with $a = b = 1$ |
| Homogeneity (Scaling) | $T\{a x[n]\} = a y[n]$ | Special case with $x_1 = x_2$ |
| Time Invariance | $T\{x[n - n_0]\} = y[n - n_0]$ | Holds for all integer $n_0$ |
| Unit Impulse | $\delta[n] = 1$ at $n=0$, $0$ elsewhere | Discrete-time analog of Dirac delta |
| Sifting Property | $x[n] = \sum_{k} x[k] \cdot \delta[n-k]$ | Decomposition into impulses |
| Impulse Response | $h[n] = T\{\delta[n]\}$ | Complete LTI characterization |
| Convolution Sum | $y[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{+\infty} x[k] \cdot h[n-k]$ | Output of LTI system |
| Commutative Property | $x[n] * h[n] = h[n] * x[n]$ | Order of operands does not matter |
| Distributive Property | $x[n] * (h_1[n] + h_2[n]) = x[n] * h_1[n] + x[n] * h_2[n]$ | Convolution distributes over addition |
| Associative Property | $(x[n] * h_1[n]) * h_2[n] = x[n] * (h_1[n] * h_2[n])$ | Grouping does not matter |
| Convolution with Shifted Impulse | $x[n] * \delta[n - n_0] = x[n - n_0]$ | Sifting in convolution form |
| Convolution Length | $N_y = N_x + N_h - 1$ | $N_x, N_h$ are signal lengths |
| Causal LTI Condition | $h[n] = 0$ for $n < 0$ | Output depends only on past/present input |
| Stable LTI Condition | $\sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert h[n] \vert < \infty$ | Absolutely summable impulse response |
| Memoryless LTI Condition | $h[n] = 0$ for $n \neq 0$ | $h[n] = K \cdot \delta[n]$ form |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** LTI systems are used universally in **digital filter design** (FIR and IIR filters for audio, image, and biomedical signal processing), **communication receivers** (matched filtering, channel equalization), **control systems** (PID controllers are linear and time-invariant in their operating range), and **audio processing** (reverb, equalization). Convolution is the central mathematical operation implemented in every digital signal processor (DSP) chip.

---

## 2.4 Properties of Convolution for LTI Systems

### 2.4.1 Commutativity
$$x[n] * h[n] = h[n] * x[n] = \sum_{k} h[k] \cdot x[n - k]$$

**Physical Meaning:** The output of an LTI system is the same regardless of whether the input is $x$ and the system's response is $h$, or vice versa.

### 2.4.2 Associativity
$$(x[n] * h_1[n]) * h_2[n] = x[n] * (h_1[n] * h_2[n])$$

**Physical Meaning:** Cascading LTI systems can be regrouped in any order. The overall impulse response is the convolution of individual impulse responses, irrespective of cascade ordering.

### 2.4.3 Distributivity
$$x[n] * (h_1[n] + h_2[n]) = x[n] * h_1[n] + x[n] * h_2[n]$$

**Physical Meaning:** A sum of LTI systems in **parallel** has an effective impulse response equal to the sum of individual impulse responses.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Convolution Sum from First Principles

This is the **most important derivation** in this module. It rigorously establishes that for any LTI system, the output is fully determined by the input and the impulse response via the convolution sum.

**Step 1: Express the arbitrary input $x[n]$ as a sum of shifted impulses (Sifting Property).**

Any discrete-time signal $x[n]$ can be decomposed into a weighted sum of unit impulses placed at every time instant, where the weight of each impulse is the value of $x[n]$ at that instant.

$$
\begin{aligned}
x[n] &= \sum_{k=-\infty}^{+\infty} x[k] \cdot \delta[n - k]
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Reasoning:** At $n = k_0$, the term $x[k_0] \cdot \delta[n - k_0]$ evaluates to $x[k_0] \cdot 1 = x[k_0]$. All other terms vanish because $\delta[n - k] = 0$ when $k \neq n$. Thus, the entire sum collapses to a single value $x[n]$.

**Step 2: Apply the system operator $T\{\cdot\}$ to both sides of the equation.**

Since the system maps inputs to outputs, we apply $T$ to the input decomposition:

$$
\begin{aligned}
y[n] = T\{x[n]\} &= T\left\{\sum_{k=-\infty}^{+\infty} x[k] \cdot \delta[n - k]\right\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Invoke the linearity (superposition) property of the LTI system.**

Because the system is **linear**, the operator $T$ can be exchanged with the summation, and the scalar $x[k]$ can be factored out of $T$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
y[n] &= \sum_{k=-\infty}^{+\infty} x[k] \cdot T\{\delta[n - k]\}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Reasoning:** Linearity permits: (i) the response to a sum to equal the sum of responses, and (ii) the response to a scaled input to equal the scaled response.

**Step 4: Invoke the time-invariance property.**

Because the system is **time-invariant**, the response to a shifted impulse $\delta[n-k]$ is simply a shifted version of the response to $\delta[n]$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T\{\delta[n - k]\} &= h[n - k]
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Reasoning:** By definition, $h[n] = T\{\delta[n]\}$. Time invariance states that delaying the input by $k$ units delays the output by the same $k$ units, yielding $h[n-k]$.

**Step 5: Substitute Step 4 into Step 3 to obtain the convolution sum.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
y[n] &= \sum_{k=-\infty}^{+\infty} x[k] \cdot h[n - k]
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{y[n] = x[n] * h[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{+\infty} x[k] \cdot h[n - k]}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** This derivation proves that the **input, the impulse response, and convolution** completely characterize any LTI system. The system operator $T$ is no longer needed in subsequent analyses — once $h[n]$ is known, the output for *any* input is computable.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 1: Testing Linearity and Time Invariance

**Problem:** Test the system $y[n] = 2x[n] + 3$ for linearity and time invariance.

### Part A: Linearity Test

**Step 1:** Compute the response to $x_1[n]$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
y_1[n] = T\{x_1[n]\} = 2x_1[n] + 3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the response to $x_2[n]$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
y_2[n] = T\{x_2[n]\} = 2x_2[n] + 3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Form the weighted-sum input $x_3[n] = a x_1[n] + b x_2[n]$ and compute the system response:

$$
\begin{aligned}
y_3[n] = T\{a x_1[n] + b x_2[n]\} = 2(a x_1[n] + b x_2[n]) + 3 = 2a x_1[n] + 2b x_2[n] + 3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the weighted sum of the individual responses:

$$
\begin{aligned}
y_4[n] = a y_1[n] + b y_2[n] = a(2x_1[n] + 3) + b(2x_2[n] + 3) = 2a x_1[n] + 2b x_2[n] + 3a + 3b
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Compare $y_3[n]$ and $y_4[n]$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
y_3[n] - y_4[n] &= (2a x_1[n] + 2b x_2[n] + 3) - (2a x_1[n] + 2b x_2[n] + 3a + 3b) \\
&= 3 - 3a - 3b = 3(1 - a - b)
\end{aligned}
$$

For $y_3[n] = y_4[n]$, we require $3(1 - a - b) = 0$, i.e., $a + b = 1$. Since this must hold for **all** $a$ and $b$ but only holds for a specific subset, the system is **NOT LINEAR**.

### Part B: Time Invariance Test

**Step 1:** Compute response to delayed input $x[n - n_0]$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T\{x[n - n_0]\} = 2x[n - n_0] + 3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute delayed output $y[n - n_0]$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
y[n - n_0] = 2x[n - n_0] + 3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Since $T\{x[n - n_0]\} = y[n - n_0]$ for all $n$ and $n_0$, the system **IS TIME-INVARIANT**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The constant term $+3$ in the system equation destroys linearity (it is a DC offset that does not scale with the input amplitude) but does **not** affect time invariance (a constant shift in input yields a constant shift in output).

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 2: Discrete-Time Convolution

**Problem:** Compute $y[n] = x[n] * h[n]$ where $x[n] = \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$ and $h[n] = \{1, 1, 1\}$. Assume both signals start at $n = 0$.

### Solution Using the Tabulation Method (KTU Recommended)

**Step 1: Set up the sequences in tabular form.**

| $n$ | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| $x[n]$ | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 |
| $h[n]$ | 1 | 1 | 1 | — |

**Step 2: Fold $h[n]$ (reverse it) and shift by $n$ to get $h[n-k]$.**

- For $n = 0$: $h[0-k] = h[-k]$, so $h[-k] = \{1, 1, 1\}$ starting at $k = 2$ and ending at $k = 0$. (Visualize: $h[n]$ flipped: $\ldots, 1, 1, 1, 0, 0, 0, \ldots$)
- For $n = 1$: $h[1-k]$ shifts the folded version right by 1.
- Continue this shifting for $n = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5$.

**Step 3: Perform pointwise multiplication and summation at each $n$.**

At $n = 0$: only the rightmost element of folded $h$ aligns with $x[0]$.

$$
y[0] = x[0] \cdot h[0] = 1 \cdot 1 = 1
$$

At $n = 1$: $h[0]$ aligns with $x[1]$, $h[-1]$ aligns with $x[0]$.

$$
y[1] = x[0] \cdot h[1] + x[1] \cdot h[0] = 1 \cdot 1 + 2 \cdot 1 = 3
$$

At $n = 2$:

$$
y[2] = x[0] \cdot h[2] + x[1] \cdot h[1] + x[2] \cdot h[0] = 1 \cdot 1 + 2 \cdot 1 + 3 \cdot 1 = 6
$$

At $n = 3$:

$$
y[3] = x[1] \cdot h[2] + x[2] \cdot h[1] + x[3] \cdot h[0] = 2 \cdot 1 + 3 \cdot 1 + 4 \cdot 1 = 9
$$

At $n = 4$:

$$
y[4] = x[2] \cdot h[2] + x[3] \cdot h[1] = 3 \cdot 1 + 4 \cdot 1 = 7
$$

At $n = 5$:

$$
y[5] = x[3] \cdot h[2] = 4 \cdot 1 = 4
$$

**Step 4: Verify length using the formula $N_y = N_x + N_h - 1$.**

$$
N_y = 4 + 3 - 1 = 6
$$

We have 6 output samples: $\{1, 3, 6, 9, 7, 4\}$. Length matches. ✓

**Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{y[n] = \{1, 3, 6, 9, 7, 4\}, \quad n = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5}
$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 3: Convolution with a Shifted Impulse

**Problem:** Compute $y[n] = x[n] * \delta[n - 3]$ where $x[n] = u[n] - u[n-5]$ (a rectangular pulse of length 5).

**Step 1: Apply the sifting property in convolution form.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
y[n] &= \sum_{k=-\infty}^{+\infty} x[k] \cdot \delta[n - k - 3]
\end{aligned}
$$

The shifted impulse $\delta[n - k - 3] = 1$ only when $n - k - 3 = 0$, i.e., $k = n - 3$.

**Step 2: Substitute $k = n - 3$ into the sum:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
y[n] &= x[n - 3] \cdot (1) = x[n - 3]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Interpret the result:**

$$
\boxed{y[n] = x[n - 3] = u[n-3] - u[n-8]}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Convolving any signal with a shifted unit impulse simply **shifts** the signal by the same amount. This property is fundamental in **sampling theory** and in modeling **propagation delays** in communication channels.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Decision Tree for LTI System Analysis

| **Step** | **Question** | **Action** |
|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | Is the system equation given? | Identify $y[n] = T\{x[n]\}$ explicitly. |
| 2 | Need to test Linearity? | Apply $T\{a x_1[n] + b x_2[n]\} \stackrel{?}{=} a y_1[n] + b y_2[n]$. |
| 3 | Need to test Time Invariance? | Apply $T\{x[n - n_0]\} \stackrel{?}{=} y[n - n_0]$. |
| 4 | Is system LTI? | Proceed to find $h[n] = T\{\delta[n]\}$. |
| 5 | Is $h[n]$ known and input arbitrary? | Use $y[n] = \sum_{k} x[k] h[n-k]$. |
| 6 | Need to check causality? | Verify $h[n] = 0$ for $n < 0$. |
| 7 | Need to check stability? | Verify $\sum_n \vert h[n] \vert < \infty$. |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: LTI System Block-Level Architecture

The following block diagram illustrates the conceptual flow of an LTI system. The impulse response $h[n]$ characterizes the system, and convolution is the central mathematical operation.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeX[Input x of n]
    nodeDel[Decompose into Impulses]
    nodeImp[Shifted Impulses x k delta n minus k]
    nodeSys[LTI System h of n]
    nodeSum[Summation of Responses]
    nodeY[Output y of n]
    nodeEq[Convolution Sum]

    nodeX --> nodeDel
    nodeDel --> nodeImp
    nodeImp --> nodeSys
    nodeSys --> nodeSum
    nodeSum --> nodeY
    nodeSum -.describes.-> nodeEq
    nodeY -.equals.-> nodeEq
```

> **Interpretation of the flow:**
> - `Input x of n` is the arbitrary input sequence arriving at the system.
> - `Decompose into Impulses` is the sifting property that expresses the input as a sum of weighted, shifted impulses $x[k] \cdot \delta[n - k]$.
> - `Shifted Impulses x k delta n minus k` are the individual impulse components.
> - `LTI System h of n` is the linear time-invariant system that processes each impulse.
> - `Summation of Responses` is the application of superposition (linearity) to combine individual outputs.
