> - `Output y of n` is the final response, which by derivation equals the convolution sum.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Linearity Test Flowchart

This flowchart guides the student through the rigorous procedure of testing whether a system is linear.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start: System Equation Given]
    pickX1[Choose Two Arbitrary Inputs x1 of n and x2 of n]
    pickAB[Choose Arbitrary Scalars a and b]
    compY1[Compute y1 = T of x1]
    compY2[Compute y2 = T of x2]
    compY3[Compute y3 = T of a x1 + b x2]
    compY4[Compute y4 = a y1 + b y2]
    compare{y3 equals y4 for all cases?}
    linearRes[System is LINEAR]
    nonlinRes[System is NONLINEAR]

    startA --> pickX1 --> pickAB
    pickAB --> compY1
    pickAB --> compY2
    pickAB --> compY3
    compY1 --> compY4
    compY2 --> compY4
    compY3 --> compare
    compY4 --> compare
    compare -- Yes --> linearRes
    compare -- No --> nonlinRes
```

> **Interpretation:** The student must compute three outputs ($y_1$, $y_2$, and $y_3$) and one weighted combination ($y_4$). If $y_3 = y_4$ universally, the system passes the linearity test; otherwise, it fails.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Convolution as a Sliding-Window Operation

The following diagram conceptualizes the convolution sum $y[n] = \sum_k x[k] \cdot h[n-k]$ as a sliding-window weighted-average process.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sigX[Signal x k: x0 x1 x2 x3 x4]
    winH[Window h n minus k: Reversed and Shifted]
    mult[Pointwise Multiplication: x k times h n minus k]
    summ[Summation over k]
    outY[Output y of n]

    sigX --> mult
    winH --> mult
    mult --> summ
    summ --> outY
    winH -.slides across n.-> winH
```

> **Interpretation:**
> - The **signal $x[k]$** is fixed.
> - The **window $h[n-k]$** is the time-reversed impulse response, which slides one step to the right as $n$ increases.
> - At each step, the products $x[k] \cdot h[n-k]$ are summed to yield the output $y[n]$.
> - This sliding-window mental model is the most intuitive way to understand convolution in engineering applications like moving-average filters, where $h[n]$ acts as a smoothing kernel.

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Parallel and Cascade LTI Interconnections

LTI systems can be interconnected in two fundamental ways. The block diagram below illustrates both.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inNode[Input x of n]
    h1A[System h1]
    h2A[System h2]
    sumA[Adder]
    outA[Output y of n equals x times h1 + h2]

    inNode --> h1A --> sumA
    inNode --> h2A --> sumA
    sumA --> outA

    inB[Input x of n]
    h1B[System h1]
    h2B[System h2]
    outB[Output y of n equals x times h1 times h2]
    inB --> h1B --> h2B --> outB
```

> **Interpretation:**
> - **Parallel connection (top):** Input is fed to both $h_1$ and $h_2$; outputs are summed. The effective impulse response is $h_{\text{eff}} = h_1 + h_2$ (distributive property).
> - **Cascade connection (bottom):** Output of $h_1$ becomes input to $h_2$. The effective impulse response is $h_{\text{eff}} = h_1 * h_2$ (associative property).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: Define linearity of a discrete-time system. State the two conditions that must be satisfied for a system to be linear.**

**Model Answer:**

A discrete-time system $T\{\cdot\}$ is said to be **linear** if it satisfies the **principle of superposition**, which states that the response to a weighted sum of inputs equals the weighted sum of the responses to each individual input. Mathematically:

$$T\{a x_1[n] + b x_2[n]\} = a y_1[n] + b y_2[n]$$

The two essential sub-conditions are:

1. **Additivity:** $T\{x_1[n] + x_2[n]\} = T\{x_1[n]\} + T\{x_2[n]\}$
2. **Homogeneity (Scaling):** $T\{a x[n]\} = a T\{x[n]\}$

Both conditions must hold **simultaneously** for all inputs and all scalars; otherwise, the system is non-linear. (3 Marks)

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: Define the impulse response of a discrete-time LTI system. How is the output related to the input via convolution?**

**Model Answer:**

The **impulse response** $h[n]$ of a discrete-time LTI system is defined as the output of the system when the input is the unit impulse $\delta[n]$:

$$h[n] = T\{\delta[n]\}$$

For an arbitrary input $x[n]$, the output of the LTI system is given by the **linear convolution sum**:

$$y[n] = x[n] * h[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{+\infty} x[k] \cdot h[n - k]$$

The convolution operation involves three steps: time-reversal of $h[k]$ to obtain $h[-k]$, shifting by $n$ to obtain $h[n-k]$, pointwise multiplication with $x[k]$, and summation over $k$. The impulse response $h[n]$ is a **complete characterization** of the LTI system. (3 Marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]`

**Q: (a)** Test whether the discrete-time system described by $y[n] = n \cdot x[n] + x[n-1]$ is **(i)** linear and **(ii)** time-invariant. Justify your answer with rigorous mathematical proof. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For an LTI system with impulse response $h[n] = \{1, 2, 1\}$ for $n = 0, 1, 2$ and $h[n] = 0$ otherwise, compute the output $y[n]$ when the input is $x[n] = \{1, 1, 1, 1\}$ for $n = 0, 1, 2, 3$ using the convolution sum. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**(i) Linearity Test:**

Let $x_1[n] \to y_1[n] = n \cdot x_1[n] + x_1[n-1]$ and $x_2[n] \to y_2[n] = n \cdot x_2[n] + x_2[n-1]$.

The response to a weighted sum $x_3[n] = a x_1[n] + b x_2[n]$ is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
y_3[n] &= T\{a x_1[n] + b x_2[n]\} \\
&= n \cdot (a x_1[n] + b x_2[n]) + (a x_1[n-1] + b x_2[n-1]) \\
&= a \cdot n x_1[n] + b \cdot n x_2[n] + a \cdot x_1[n-1] + b \cdot x_2[n-1]
\end{aligned}
$$

The weighted sum of individual responses is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
y_4[n] &= a y_1[n] + b y_2[n] \\
&= a (n x_1[n] + x_1[n-1]) + b (n x_2[n] + x_2[n-1]) \\
&= a n x_1[n] + a x_1[n-1] + b n x_2[n] + b x_2[n-1]
\end{aligned}
$$

Comparing $y_3[n]$ and $y_4[n]$, we observe they are **identical**. Thus, the system is **LINEAR**. ✓

**(ii) Time Invariance Test:**

The response to a delayed input $x[n - n_0]$ is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T\{x[n - n_0]\} &= n \cdot x[n - n_0] + x[n - n_0 - 1]
\end{aligned}
$$

The delayed output is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
y[n - n_0] &= (n - n_0) \cdot x[n - n_0] + x[n - n_0 - 1]
\end{aligned}
$$

Comparing the two:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T\{x[n-n_0]\} - y[n-n_0] &= n \cdot x[n-n_0] - (n - n_0) x[n-n_0] \\
&= n_0 \cdot x[n - n_0]
\end{aligned}
$$

For the system to be time-invariant, this difference must be zero for all $x[n]$ and all $n_0$. Since $n_0 \cdot x[n-n_0] \neq 0$ in general, the system is **NOT TIME-INVARIANT**. ✗

**Conclusion:** The system is **linear** but **time-varying**. Hence, it is **not LTI**. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:** $x[n] = \{1, 1, 1, 1\}$ for $n = 0, 1, 2, 3$ and $h[n] = \{1, 2, 1\}$ for $n = 0, 1, 2$.

**Step 1: Determine output length.**

$$
N_y = N_x + N_h - 1 = 4 + 3 - 1 = 6
$$

So $y[n]$ is defined for $n = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5$.

**Step 2: Apply the convolution sum for each $n$.**

For $n = 0$:

$$
y[0] = \sum_k x[k] \cdot h[0-k] = x[0] \cdot h[0] = 1 \cdot 1 = 1
$$

For $n = 1$:

$$
y[1] = x[0] \cdot h[1] + x[1] \cdot h[0] = 1 \cdot 2 + 1 \cdot 1 = 3
$$

For $n = 2$:

$$
y[2] = x[0] \cdot h[2] + x[1] \cdot h[1] + x[2] \cdot h[0] = 1 \cdot 1 + 1 \cdot 2 + 1 \cdot 1 = 4
$$

For $n = 3$:

$$
y[3] = x[1] \cdot h[2] + x[2] \cdot h[1] + x[3] \cdot h[0] = 1 \cdot 1 + 1 \cdot 2 + 1 \cdot 1 = 4
$$

For $n = 4$:

$$
y[4] = x[2] \cdot h[2] + x[3] \cdot h[1] = 1 \cdot 1 + 1 \cdot 2 = 3
$$

For $n = 5$:

$$
y[5] = x[3] \cdot h[2] = 1 \cdot 1 = 1
$$

**Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{y[n] = \{1, 3, 4, 4, 3, 1\}, \quad n = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5}
$$

(7 Marks)

---

### Question B (14 Marks - Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: (a)** State and prove the commutative and associative properties of discrete-time convolution. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compute the convolution $y[n] = x[n] * h[n]$ where $x[n] = u[n] - u[n-3]$ and $h[n] = \delta[n] + 2\delta[n-1] + 3\delta[n-2]$. Express your answer in terms of unit step and impulse functions. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Statement of Commutative Property:**

For any two sequences $x[n]$ and $h[n]$:

$$
x[n] * h[n] = h[n] * x[n]
$$

**Proof:**

Starting from the definition of convolution:

$$
x[n] * h[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{+\infty} x[k] \cdot h[n - k]
$$

Substitute $m = n - k$, which implies $k = n - m$. As $k$ ranges from $-\infty$ to $+\infty$, $m$ also ranges from $-\infty$ to $+\infty$:

$$
x[n] * h[n] = \sum_{m=-\infty}^{+\infty} x[n - m] \cdot h[m]
$$

Since the dummy variable $m$ can be renamed to $k$ without loss of generality:

$$
x[n] * h[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{+\infty} h[k] \cdot x[n - k] = h[n] * x[n] \quad \blacksquare
$$

**Statement of Associative Property:**

For any three sequences $x[n]$, $h_1[n]$, and $h_2[n]$:

$$
(x[n] * h_1[n]) * h_2[n] = x[n] * (h_1[n] * h_2[n])
$$

**Proof:**

Let $y[n] = (x[n] * h_1[n]) * h_2[n]$. Then:

$$
y[n] = \sum_{m=-\infty}^{+\infty} \left( \sum_{k=-\infty}^{+\infty} x[k] \cdot h_1[m - k] \right) \cdot h_2[n - m]
$$

Assuming absolute summability to exchange summation order:

$$
y[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{+\infty} x[k] \left( \sum_{m=-\infty}^{+\infty} h_1[m-k] \cdot h_2[n-m] \right)
$$

Let $p = m - k$, so $m = p + k$:

$$
y[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{+\infty} x[k] \left( \sum_{p=-\infty}^{+\infty} h_1[p] \cdot h_2[(n-k) - p] \right) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{+\infty} x[k] \cdot (h_1 * h_2)[n - k]
$$

Therefore:

$$
y[n] = x[n] * (h_1[n] * h_2[n]) \quad \blacksquare
$$

(7 Marks)

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1: Identify the sequences.**

$x[n] = u[n] - u[n-3]$ is a rectangular pulse of height 1, defined for $n = 0, 1, 2$ and zero elsewhere.

$h[n] = \delta[n] + 2\delta[n-1] + 3\delta[n-2]$ has values $\{1, 2, 3\}$ for $n = 0, 1, 2$.

**Step 2: Compute the convolution using the sifting property of impulses.**

Using the property $x[n] * \delta[n - n_0] = x[n - n_0]$ and distributivity:

$$
\begin{aligned}
y[n] &= x[n] * \{\delta[n] + 2\delta[n-1] + 3\delta[n-2]\} \\
&= x[n] * \delta[n] + 2 \cdot x[n] * \delta[n-1] + 3 \cdot x[n] * \delta[n-2] \\
&= x[n] + 2x[n-1] + 3x[n-2]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Substitute $x[n] = u[n] - u[n-3]$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
y[n] &= (u[n] - u[n-3]) + 2(u[n-1] - u[n-4]) + 3(u[n-2] - u[n-5]) \\
&= u[n] + 2u[n-1] + 3u[n-2] - u[n-3] - 2u[n-4] - 3u[n-5]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Express the result compactly.**

$$
\boxed{y[n] = u[n] + 2u[n-1] + 3u[n-2] - u[n-3] - 2u[n-4] - 3u[n-5]}
$$

**Verification by sample values:**

- $n = 0$: $y[0] = 1 \cdot 1 = 1$
- $n = 1$: $y[1] = 1 + 2 = 3$
- $n = 2$: $y[2] = 1 + 2 + 3 = 6$
- $n = 3$: $y[3] = -1 + 1 + 2 + 3 = 5$ (re-evaluate: $u[0]+2u[1]+3u[2]-u[3]-2u[4]-3u[5] = 1+2+3-1-0-0 = 5$) ✓
- $n = 4$: $y[4] = 1+2+3-1-2-0 = 3$ ✓
- $n = 5$: $y[5] = 1+2+3-1-2-3 = 0$ ✓

Output sequence: $y[n] = \{1, 3, 6, 5, 3\}$ for $n = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4$. (7 Marks)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting to Test Both Sub-Properties of Linearity:** A common error is to verify additivity alone (e.g., setting $a = b = 1$) and conclude the system is linear. You **must** also test homogeneity with non-unit scalars (e.g., $a = 2$, $b = 3$). Loss of up to **2 marks**.
> 2. **Misapplying the Time-Invariance Shift:** Students often shift only the input and forget to compare with the delayed output $y[n-n_0]$. The explicit dependence on $n$ in coefficients (e.g., $n \cdot x[n]$, $\cos(n\pi/2) x[n]$) almost always indicates time-variance. Loss of up to **2 marks**.
> 3. **Convolution Range Errors:** Forgetting that the summation index $k$ must be such that **both** $x[k]$ and $h[n-k]$ are non-zero. Many students either over-extend the summation range or terminate it prematurely. Always use the **length formula** $N_y = N_x + N_h - 1$ as a sanity check. Loss of up to **2 marks**.
> 4. **Confusing Sifting Property with Convolution Property:** The expression $x[n] = \sum_k x[k] \delta[n-k]$ is the *sifting property of the impulse*, not the convolution. The convolution form $x[n] * \delta[n-n_0] = x[n-n_0]$ is derived *from* the sifting property. Do not interchange them. Loss of up to **1 mark**.
> 5. **Sign Errors in Folded Sequences:** When folding $h[k]$ to get $h[-k]$, students often misplace the index. Always sketch the sequence on a number line, mark the origin, and visualize the reflection explicitly before performing arithmetic. Loss of up to **2 marks**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Linearity** demands both **additivity** AND **homogeneity** simultaneously. A system failing either condition is non-linear.
- **Time Invariance** demands that a delayed input produces an equally delayed output. Explicit dependence on $n$ in system coefficients is a tell-tale sign of time-variance.
- An **LTI system** is fully characterized by its **impulse response $h[n]$**. The output for any arbitrary input is computed via the **convolution sum**: $y[n] = \sum_k x[k] h[n-k]$.
- The convolution sum is derived from the **sifting property** combined with linearity and time invariance. Always present this derivation in full to secure full marks.
- **Convolution Procedure:** (1) Folding $h[k]$, (2) Shifting by $n$, (3) Multiplying pointwise, (4) Summing. Repeat for all valid $n$.
- **Output Length Formula:** $N_y = N_x + N_h - 1$. Always use this as a verification step.
- **Convolution Properties** to memorize for KTU exams: Commutative ($x*h = h*x$), Associative ($(x*h_1)*h_2 = x*(h_1*h_2)$), Distributive ($x*(h_1+h_2) = x*h_1 + x*h_2$).
- **Sifting in Convolution Form:** $x[n] * \delta[n-n_0] = x[n-n_0]$. This shortcut saves significant time in numerical problems.
- **Causality Check:** An LTI system is causal if and only if $h[n] = 0$ for all $n < 0$.
- **Stability Check:** An LTI system is BIBO stable if and only if $\sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert h[n] \vert < \infty$ (absolutely summable impulse response).
- **System Interconnections:** Parallel $\Rightarrow$ sum the impulse responses; Cascade $\Rightarrow$ convolve the impulse responses.
- **Common Non-Linear Systems:** $y[n] = x^2[n]$, $y[n] = \vert x[n] \vert$, $y[n] = x[n] \cdot \sin(x[n])$, $y[n] = x[n] + c$ (where $c$ is a non-zero constant).
- **Common Time-Varying Systems:** $y[n] = n \cdot x[n]$, $y[n] = x[2n]$, $y[n] = x[\sin(n)]$, $y[n] = n \cdot x[n-1] + (n-1) \cdot x[n]$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Properties of Convolution
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST416 - SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Properties of Convolution

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Convolution

In the context of **Linear Time-Invariant (LTI) systems**, the convolution operation between two continuous-time signals $x(t)$ and $h(t)$ is formally defined by the mathematical integral:

$$y(t) = x(t) * h(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(\tau) \cdot h(t-\tau) \, d\tau$$

Similarly, for discrete-time sequences $x[n]$ and $h[n]$, the convolution is given by the summation:

$$y[n] = x[n] * h[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x[k] \cdot h[n-k]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Convolution is the cornerstone of LTI system analysis. The response $y(t)$ of any LTI system is mathematically guaranteed to be the convolution of the input signal $x(t)$ with the system's impulse response $h(t)$. This is not just a definition — it is a fundamental theorem.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are **pushing a heavy wooden block** across a rough floor with a rubber mallet. The motion of the block at any instant $t$ depends on:
- **Every single tap** you have ever given it in the past (input history).
- **How the block "remembers" and "responds"** to each tap (system memory / impulse response).

Convolution is the mathematical way of saying: *"The output right now is a weighted sum of all past inputs, where the weight comes from the system's memory of how it reacts to an impulse applied at different times in the past."*

A simpler analogy: **Rolling average (sliding window)**. Each new data point gets multiplied by the window coefficients and summed. Convolution generalizes this idea using a continuous, time-reversed, and time-shifted version of the impulse response.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Mapping:**
> - $x(\tau)$ → Past input value at time $\tau$.
> - $h(t-\tau)$ → Memory / weighting given to that past input when evaluating the output at time $t$.
> - Integration over $\tau$ → Summing the contribution of *all* past inputs.

### 1.3 Role in Signal Processing

Convolution captures **three physical effects simultaneously**:
1. **Scaling** — Multiply $x(\tau)$ by the response coefficient.
2. **Time-Reversal** — Fold $h(\tau)$ to get $h(-\tau)$.
3. **Time-Shifting** — Slide the flipped response across the time axis.

The output is built up by continuously shifting and integrating.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Six Fundamental Properties of Convolution

Convolution is **not** ordinary multiplication. It is a binary operation with its own algebraic structure. The following six properties are considered **high-yield** for KTU examinations:

#### Property 1 — Commutative Law
$$x(t) * h(t) = h(t) * x(t)$$

The order of the two signals does not affect the output. Physically, this means *an LTI system with impulse response $h(t)$ driven by input $x(t)$* behaves identically to *a system with impulse response $x(t)$ driven by input $h(t)$*.

#### Property 2 — Associative Law
$$\big[x(t) * h_1(t)\big] * h_2(t) = x(t) * \big[h_1(t) * h_2(t)\big]$$

This property allows cascaded LTI systems to be **reordered arbitrarily**. The overall impulse response of two cascaded systems is the convolution of their individual impulse responses.

#### Property 3 — Distributive Law (over Addition)
$$x(t) * \big[h_1(t) + h_2(t)\big] = x(t) * h_1(t) + x(t) * h_2(t)$$

A single input passing through a **parallel combination** of LTI systems is equivalent to the sum of individual responses. It allows block-diagram simplification.

#### Property 4 — Identity Element
$$x(t) * \delta(t) = x(t)$$

The unit impulse $\delta(t)$ is the identity for convolution. Convolving any signal with $\delta(t)$ returns the original signal unchanged.

#### Property 5 — Zero Element
$$x(t) * 0 = 0$$

Convolving any signal with zero yields zero (the absorbing element).

#### Property 6 — Shift Property
$$x(t) * \delta(t - t_0) = x(t - t_0)$$

Convolving a signal with a shifted impulse **shifts the signal** by the same amount. This is the workhorse property behind time-shift analysis.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below consolidates all convolution properties required for the KTU 2024 Scheme ESE. Memorize the operator name, the algebraic form, and the engineering interpretation together.

| **Property** | **Continuous-Time Form** | **Discrete-Time Form** | **Engineering Meaning** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Commutative | $x * h = h * x$ | $x[n] * h[n] = h[n] * x[n]$ | Order of LTI blocks can be swapped |
| Associative | $(x * h_1) * h_2 = x * (h_1 * h_2)$ | $(x[n] * h_1[n]) * h_2[n] = x[n] * (h_1[n] * h_2[n])$ | Cascaded LTI systems can be re-grouped |
| Distributive | $x * (h_1 + h_2) = x*h_1 + x*h_2$ | $x[n]*(h_1[n]+h_2[n]) = x[n]*h_1[n]+x[n]*h_2[n]$ | Parallel systems add their outputs |
| Identity | $x(t) * \delta(t) = x(t)$ | $x[n] * \delta[n] = x[n]$ | $\delta$ leaves signal unchanged |
| Shift | $x(t) * \delta(t-t_0) = x(t-t_0)$ | $x[n] * \delta[n-n_0] = x[n-n_0]$ | Impulse shift shifts the signal |
| Time Scaling | $x(at) * h(at) = \tfrac{1}{\vert a \vert} y(at)$ | $x[kn] * h[kn] = \tfrac{1}{k} y[kn]$ | Time-axis compression/expansion |
| Differentiation | $\dfrac{d}{dt}\big[x(t)*h(t)\big] = x'(t)*h(t) = x(t)*h'(t)$ | $y[n] - y[n-1] = x[n] * h[n] - x[n-1] * h[n]$ | Derivative distributes over convolution |
| Integration | $\displaystyle\int_{-\infty}^{t} y(\tau)d\tau = \left[\int_{-\infty}^{t} x(\tau)d\tau\right] * h(t)$ | $\sum_{k=-\infty}^{n} y[k] = \left[\sum_{k=-\infty}^{n} x[k]\right] * h[n]$ | Integral distributes over convolution |

### 2.3 Real-World Utility of These Properties

- **Audio Equalizers** — Built from cascaded LTI filters; commutativity and associativity guarantee identical output regardless of filter order.
- **Image Processing** — Gaussian blur kernels are associative, enabling **separable filtering** (2D convolution broken into two 1D convolutions), drastically reducing computation.
- **Control Systems** — Feedback loops rely on the associative property to collapse complex block diagrams into a single transfer function.
- **Communication Receivers** — Matched filters use the shift property to align received signals with locally generated templates.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Proof of the Commutative Property

**Claim:** $x(t) * h(t) = h(t) * x(t)$

**Derivation:**

By definition, the convolution $x(t) * h(t)$ is given by:

$$y(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(\tau) \cdot h(t-\tau) \, d\tau$$

Apply the substitution $\lambda = t - \tau$. Then $\tau = t - \lambda$ and $d\tau = -d\lambda$. When $\tau \to -\infty$, then $\lambda \to +\infty$, and when $\tau \to +\infty$, then $\lambda \to -\infty$. Substituting:

$$y(t) = \int_{+\infty}^{-\infty} x(t-\lambda) \cdot h(\lambda) \, (-d\lambda)$$

The two minus signs (one from the limits reversed, one from $d\tau$) cancel out:

$$y(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} h(\lambda) \cdot x(t-\lambda) \, d\lambda$$

By renaming the dummy variable $\lambda$ as $\tau$, we obtain:

$$y(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} h(\tau) \cdot x(t-\tau) \, d\tau = h(t) * x(t)$$

Hence proved. $\blacksquare$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The proof rests entirely on the *dummy variable* nature of $\tau$. The limits being infinite and symmetric is what allows the sign flip from $d\tau = -d\lambda$ to be absorbed by reversing integration limits.

### 3.2 Proof of the Associative Property

**Claim:** $\big[x(t) * h_1(t)\big] * h_2(t) = x(t) * \big[h_1(t) * h_2(t)\big]$

**Derivation (Left-Hand Side):**

Let $g(t) = x(t) * h_1(t) = \displaystyle\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(\tau_1) h_1(t - \tau_1) \, d\tau_1$

Then the LHS becomes:

$$\big[x(t) * h_1(t)\big] * h_2(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} g(\lambda) h_2(t - \lambda) \, d\lambda$$

Substituting the expression for $g(\lambda)$:

$$\text{LHS} = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \left[ \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(\tau_1) h_1(\lambda - \tau_1) \, d\tau_1 \right] h_2(t - \lambda) \, d\lambda$$

Since the integrand is absolutely summable (a standard KTU assumption for LTI systems), we can **swap the order of integration**:

$$\text{LHS} = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(\tau_1) \left[ \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} h_1(\lambda - \tau_1) h_2(t - \lambda) \, d\lambda \right] d\tau_1$$

**Derivation (Right-Hand Side):**

Now consider the inner bracket. Apply the substitution $u = \lambda - \tau_1$, so $\lambda = u + \tau_1$ and $d\lambda = du$. When $\lambda \to -\infty$, $u \to -\infty$; when $\lambda \to +\infty$, $u \to +\infty$. The limits remain unchanged:

$$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} h_1(\lambda - \tau_1) h_2(t - \lambda) \, d\lambda = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} h_1(u) h_2\big((t - \tau_1) - u\big) \, du$$

This is precisely the definition of $h_1(t - \tau_1) * h_2(t - \tau_1)$. Plugging back:

$$\text{LHS} = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(\tau_1) \left[ h_1(t - \tau_1) * h_2(t - \tau_1) \right] d\tau_1$$

Now define $h(t) = h_1(t) * h_2(t)$. Then $h(t - \tau_1) = h_1(t - \tau_1) * h_2(t - \tau_1)$, so:

$$\text{LHS} = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(\tau_1) \, h(t - \tau_1) \, d\tau_1 = x(t) * h(t) = x(t) * \big[h_1(t) * h_2(t)\big]$$

Hence proved. $\blacksquare$

### 3.3 Proof of the Distributive Property

**Claim:** $x(t) * \big[h_1(t) + h_2(t)\big] = x(t) * h_1(t) + x(t) * h_2(t)$

**Derivation:**

Starting from the LHS:

$$\text{LHS} = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(\tau) \big[ h_1(t - \tau) + h_2(t - \tau) \big] \, d\tau$$

By linearity of integration, the integral of a sum equals the sum of the integrals:

$$\text{LHS} = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(\tau) h_1(t - \tau) \, d\tau + \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(\tau) h_2(t - \tau) \, d\tau$$

$$\text{LHS} = x(t) * h_1(t) + x(t) * h_2(t)$$

Hence proved. $\blacksquare$

### 3.4 Proof of the Identity Property

**Claim:** $x(t) * \delta(t) = x(t)$

**Derivation:**

Using the sifting property of the Dirac delta, $\delta(t - \tau)$ is zero everywhere except at $\tau = t$, where it integrates to unity. Therefore:

$$x(t) * \delta(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(\tau) \, \delta(t - \tau) \, d\tau = x(t)$$

For discrete-time, using the Kronecker delta $\delta[n]$ which equals $1$ when $n=0$ and $0$ otherwise:

$$x[n] * \delta[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x[k] \, \delta[n - k] = x[n]$$

since $\delta[n - k]$ is nonzero only when $k = n$. Hence proved. $\blacksquare$

### 3.5 Proof of the Shift Property

**Claim:** $x(t) * \delta(t - t_0) = x(t - t_0)$

**Derivation:**

$$x(t) * \delta(t - t_0) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(\tau) \, \delta(t - t_0 - \tau) \, d\tau$$

The argument of the delta is zero when $\tau = t - t_0$. Applying the sifting property:

$$= x(t - t_0)$$

Hence proved. $\blacksquare$

### 3.6 Property Verification: Time-Domain Derivative of Convolution

**Claim:** $\dfrac{d}{dt}\big[x(t) * h(t)\big] = \dfrac{dx(t)}{dt} * h(t) = x(t) * \dfrac{dh(t)}{dt}$

**Derivation:**

$$y(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(\tau) h(t - \tau) \, d\tau$$

Differentiating both sides with respect to $t$ (assuming $x$ and $h$ are differentiable and Leibniz's rule applies):

$$\frac{dy(t)}{dt} = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(\tau) \frac{\partial}{\partial t} h(t - \tau) \, d\tau$$

By the chain rule, $\dfrac{\partial}{\partial t} h(t - \tau) = \dfrac{dh(t - \tau)}{d(t-\tau)} = h'(t - \tau)$. Therefore:

$$\frac{dy(t)}{dt} = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(\tau) h'(t - \tau) \, d\tau = x(t) * h'(t)$$

Using the commutative property, $x(t) * h'(t) = h'(t) * x(t) = \dfrac{dh(t)}{dt} * x(t) = x'(t) * h(t)$. Hence proved. $\blacksquare$

### 3.7 Analytical Matrix Summary

| **Property** | **Primary Tool Used** | **Key Mathematical Step** |
|---|---|---|
| Commutative | Change of variable $\lambda = t - \tau$ | Reversal of integration limits cancels $-d\lambda$ |
| Associative | Fubini's theorem (swap integrals) | Substitution $u = \lambda - \tau_1$ inside inner integral |
| Distributive | Linearity of integration | Split sum inside integral into two integrals |
| Identity | Sifting property of $\delta$ | $\delta(t - \tau)$ picks value at $\tau = t$ |
| Shift | Sifting property of shifted $\delta$ | Argument of delta zeroed at $\tau = t - t_0$ |
| Derivative | Leibniz integral rule | Derivative inside integral with respect to parameter $t$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture: Convolution as LTI System

The following Mermaid diagram represents the conceptual architecture of how the convolution operation fits into the LTI system framework — the foundation for understanding why each property matters.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Signal x of t] --> B[Time Reversal h of minus tau]
    B --> C[Time Shift h of t minus tau]
    C --> D[Multiply x tau times h t minus tau]
    D --> E[Integrate over tau from minus infinity to infinity]
    E --> F[Output y of t equals x convolve h]
    A2[LTI System with Impulse Response h of t] --> F
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Cascaded LTI Systems (Illustrating Associativity)

The associativity property guarantees that cascading two LTI systems in any order or grouping yields the **same overall response**.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X[Input x of t] --> S1[System h1 of t]
    S1 --> S2[System h2 of t]
    S2 --> Y1[Output y of t]

    X2[Input x of t] --> S3[Equivalent System h1 convolve h2]
    S3 --> Y2[Output y of t]
```

> [!NOTE]
> Both paths from input to output produce **identical** outputs because of the associative property. This is why block diagrams in control systems can be simplified without changing system behavior.

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Parallel LTI Systems (Illustrating Distributivity)

When a single input drives multiple parallel LTI branches, the total output is the sum of individual outputs — a direct consequence of the distributive property.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X[Input x of t] --> SPLIT{Parallel Split}
    SPLIT --> SYS1[h1 of t]
    SPLIT --> SYS2[h2 of t]
    SYS1 --> ADD[/Summation Node/]
    SYS2 --> ADD
    ADD --> Y[Output y of t equals x convolve h1 plus x convolve h2]
```

### 4.4 Decision Tree: Selecting the Right Property

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{What is the problem asking?} -->|Swap order of two signals| P1[Use Commutative Property]
    Q1 -->|Combine cascaded LTI systems| P2[Use Associative Property]
    Q1 -->|Combine parallel LTI systems| P3[Use Distributive Property]
    Q1 -->|Convolve with an impulse| P4[Use Identity or Shift Property]
    Q1 -->|Differentiate a convolution| P5[Use Derivative Property]
    Q1 -->|Compress or expand time axis| P6[Use Time Scaling Property]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
> **State and prove the commutative property of convolution for continuous-time LTI systems.**

**Model Answer:**

The commutative property states that for any two continuous-time signals $x(t)$ and $h(t)$:

$$x(t) * h(t) = h(t) * x(t)$$

**Proof:** By definition,

$$x(t) * h(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(\tau) h(t - \tau) \, d\tau$$

Substituting $\lambda = t - \tau$, so $\tau = t - \lambda$ and $d\tau = -d\lambda$, with limits reversed (which contributes another negative sign):

$$= \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t - \lambda) h(\lambda) \, d\lambda = h(t) * x(t)$$

Hence proved.

---

> **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
> **Explain the identity property of convolution. What is its physical significance in LTI system analysis?**

**Model Answer:**

The identity property states:

$$x(t) * \delta(t) = x(t)$$

where $\delta(t)$ is the unit impulse (Dirac delta). **Physical Significance:** It establishes that $\delta(t)$ acts as the identity element for convolution, analogous to how $1$ acts as the identity for multiplication. More importantly, since the response of any LTI system to $\delta(t)$ is its impulse response $h(t)$, the identity property guarantees that **convolving any input with the system's impulse response produces the output signal unchanged in shape** — only shifted/scaled by the system dynamics. It is the foundation of the result $y(t) = x(t) * h(t)$.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> **Note on KTU Pattern:** The KTU 2024 Scheme End Semester Examination awards **7 marks per sub-part**. Students are expected to write the formal property statement, the complete derivation, the physical interpretation, and a numerical or graphical example where relevant.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]`

**a)** State and prove the **associative property** of convolution for continuous-time LTI systems. Mention one practical engineering scenario where this property is applied. **(7 Marks)**

**b)** Using the distributive property, show that a single input $x(t)$ passing through a parallel combination of two LTI systems with impulse responses $h_1(t)$ and $h_2(t)$ is equivalent to passing $x(t)$ through a single LTI system with impulse response $h_1(t) + h_2(t)$. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

#### Part (a) — Associative Property

**Statement:** For three continuous-time signals $x(t), h_1(t), h_2(t)$:

$$\big[x(t) * h_1(t)\big] * h_2(t) = x(t) * \big[h_1(t) * h_2(t)\big]$$

**Proof:** Let $g(t) = x(t) * h_1(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(\tau_1) h_1(t - \tau_1) \, d\tau_1$.

Now consider $g(t) * h_2(t)$:

$$g(t) * h_2(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} g(\lambda) h_2(t - \lambda) \, d\lambda$$

Substituting the expression for $g(\lambda)$:

$$= \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \left[ \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(\tau_1) h_1(\lambda - \tau_1) \, d\tau_1 \right] h_2(t - \lambda) \, d\lambda$$

Swapping the order of integration (justified by absolute integrability):

$$= \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(\tau_1) \left[ \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} h_1(\lambda - \tau_1) h_2(t - \lambda) \, d\lambda \right] d\tau_1$$

In the inner integral, substitute $u = \lambda - \tau_1$, giving $\lambda = u + \tau_1$ and $d\lambda = du$:

$$= \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(\tau_1) \left[ \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} h_1(u) h_2(t - \tau_1 - u) \, du \right] d\tau_1$$

The inner integral is exactly $h_1(t - \tau_1) * h_2(t - \tau_1)$. Letting $h(t) = h_1(t) * h_2(t)$:

$$= \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(\tau_1) \, h(t - \tau_1) \, d\tau_1 = x(t) * h(t) = x(t) * \big[h_1(t) * h_2(t)\big]$$

Hence proved.

**Practical Engineering Scenario:** In multi-stage audio equalizers, several first-order filter stages are cascaded. The associative property guarantees that the order in which we apply the filters does not alter the final sound output, allowing flexible hardware/software design.

---

#### Part (b) — Distributive Property Application

**Given:** Input $x(t)$ enters a parallel combination of systems with impulse responses $h_1(t)$ and $h_2(t)$.

**Output of Branch 1:** $y_1(t) = x(t) * h_1(t)$

**Output of Branch 2:** $y_2(t) = x(t) * h_2(t)$

**Total Output at the summation node:**

$$y(t) = y_1(t) + y_2(t) = x(t) * h_1(t) + x(t) * h_2(t)$$

By the **distributive property** of convolution over addition:

$$x(t) * h_1(t) + x(t) * h_2(t) = x(t) * \big[h_1(t) + h_2(t)\big]$$

Therefore:

$$y(t) = x(t) * \big[h_1(t) + h_2(t)\big]$$

This is **identical** to the output that would be obtained by passing $x(t)$ through a single equivalent LTI system with impulse response:

$$h_{\text{eq}}(t) = h_1(t) + h_2(t)$$

Hence proved. The parallel combination collapses into one equivalent system.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]`

**a)** State and prove the **derivative property** of convolution. Demonstrate its use by computing $\dfrac{dy(t)}{dt}$ where $y(t) = x(t) * h(t)$, and show that the derivative can be taken on either operand. **(7 Marks)**

**b)** Two LTI systems have impulse responses $h_1(t) = e^{-2t} u(t)$ and $h_2(t) = e^{-3t} u(t)$. Using the commutative and associative properties, find the overall impulse response when they are cascaded. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

#### Part (a) — Derivative Property

**Statement:** If $y(t) = x(t) * h(t)$, then:

$$\frac{dy(t)}{dt} = x'(t) * h(t) = x(t) * h'(t)$$

where $x'(t) = \dfrac{dx(t)}{dt}$ and $h'(t) = \dfrac{dh(t)}{dt}$.

**Proof:** Starting from the definition:

$$y(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(\tau) h(t - \tau) \, d\tau$$

Differentiating both sides with respect to $t$ using Leibniz's rule (the limits are constants):

$$\frac{dy(t)}{dt} = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(\tau) \cdot \frac{\partial}{\partial t} h(t - \tau) \, d\tau$$

By the chain rule, $\dfrac{\partial}{\partial t} h(t - \tau) = h'(t - \tau)$. Therefore:

$$\frac{dy(t)}{dt} = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(\tau) h'(t - \tau) \, d\tau = x(t) * h'(t)$$

Applying the **commutative property** to this result:

$$x(t) * h'(t) = h'(t) * x(t) = \left[\frac{dh(t)}{dt}\right] * x(t) = x'(t) * h(t)$$

Hence $\dfrac{dy(t)}{dt} = x'(t) * h(t) = x(t) * h'(t)$. The derivative may be taken on either signal.

---

#### Part (b) — Cascaded LTI System Impulse Response

**Given:** $h_1(t) = e^{-2t} u(t)$ and $h_2(t) = e^{-3t} u(t)$.

**Step 1:** The overall impulse response of two cascaded LTI systems is the convolution of their individual impulse responses:

$$h(t) = h_1(t) * h_2(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} h_1(\tau) h_2(t - \tau) \, d\tau$$

**Step 2:** Substituting the given forms:

$$h(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} e^{-2\tau} u(\tau) \cdot e^{-3(t-\tau)} u(t - \tau) \, d\tau$$

**Step 3:** The unit-step functions restrict the limits: $u(\tau)$ requires $\tau \geq 0$, and $u(t - \tau)$ requires $\tau \leq t$. Combined: $0 \leq \tau \leq t$, valid only when $t \geq 0$. For $t < 0$, $h(t) = 0$.

**Step 4:** For $t \geq 0$:

$$h(t) = \int_{0}^{t} e^{-2\tau} \cdot e^{-3(t-\tau)} \, d\tau = e^{-3t} \int_{0}^{t} e^{-2\tau} e^{3\tau} \, d\tau = e^{-3t} \int_{0}^{t} e^{\tau} \, d\tau$$

**Step 5:** Evaluate the inner integral:

$$\int_{0}^{t} e^{\tau} \, d\tau = e^{\tau} \Big|_{0}^{t} = e^{t} - 1$$

**Step 6:** Substitute back:

$$h(t) = e^{-3t} (e^{t} - 1) = e^{-2t} - e^{-3t}$$

**Step 7:** Apply the unit-step function to enforce the $t \geq 0$ condition:

$$\boxed{h(t) = \big[e^{-2t} - e^{-3t}\big] u(t)}$$

**Verification using properties:** By the commutative property, we could equivalently compute $h_2 * h_1$ and obtain the same result. By the associative property, if a third system $h_3(t)$ were cascaded, we could group it as $(h_1 * h_2) * h_3$ or $h_1 * (h_2 * h_3)$ and get identical responses — this is essential for simplifying multi-stage systems.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Convolution Property Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Sifting Property Justification** — When proving identity or shift properties, merely stating the result without invoking the sifting property of $\delta(t)$ will cost **at least 1 mark**. Always write: *"By the sifting property of the unit impulse..."*
> 2. **Ignoring the Step-Function Constraint** — In numerical cascaded-system problems, students often forget to multiply by $u(t)$ at the end, losing **1 to 2 marks**.
> 3. **Skipping the Substitution Step** — In the commutative proof, students jump from $x(t) * h(t)$ directly to $h(t) * x(t)$ without showing the substitution $\lambda = t - \tau$ and the limit reversal. This is a **3-mark deduction**.
> 4. **Confusing Commutative with Associative** — A frequent error: claiming commutativity justifies re-grouping cascaded systems. Use **associativity** for regrouping; use **commutativity** only for reordering.
> 5. **Missing the Physical Interpretation** — KTU 2024 Scheme emphasizes outcome-based learning. For every formal property, you are expected to include a **one-line physical interpretation** in terms of LTI systems.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Convolution is the operation that fully characterizes any LTI system** — given input $x(t)$ and impulse response $h(t)$, the output is uniquely determined as $y(t) = x(t) * h(t)$.
- **Six core properties:** Commutative, Associative, Distributive, Identity, Zero, Shift — these are tested every KTU cycle without exception.
- **Commutativity** ⇒ swapping input and system gives the same output.
- **Associativity** ⇒ cascading order of LTI blocks can be arbitrarily rearranged.
- **Distributivity** ⇒ parallel LTI systems can be combined by summing their impulse responses.
- **Identity property** uses the **sifting property** of $\delta(t)$ — memorize the integral form: $\int x(\tau) \delta(t - \tau) d\tau = x(t)$.
- **Shift property** generalizes identity: $\delta(t - t_0)$ acts as a delay operator.
- **Derivative property** allows differentiation to be moved inside the convolution onto either operand — this is critical for solving differential equations using impulse responses.
- **Time-scaling property** introduces a $\tfrac{1}{\vert a \vert}$ factor — forgetting this factor is a common error.
- **For KTU numerical problems**, always end with the unit-step function $u(t)$ to enforce causality, even if it is not explicitly asked.
- **Graphical interpretation:** Convolution = **flip, slide, multiply, integrate (area)**. The four-step mnemonic is essential for graphical KTU problems.
- **Practice tip:** Draw the time-reversed signal $h(-\tau)$, slide it across $x(\tau)$, and shade the overlap region before integrating — this visual method is consistently rewarded with full marks in KTU evaluations.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Characterizing LTI Systems and Convolution - Impulse Response of an LTI System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST416 - SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Characterizing LTI Systems and Convolution - Impulse Response of an LTI System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition: The Impulse Response

In the rigorous framework of **Linear Time-Invariant (LTI) Systems** analysis, the **Impulse Response** is the single most important characterizing function of any continuous-time or discrete-time LTI system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** The *impulse response* $h(t)$ of a continuous-time LTI system is defined as the output of the system when the input is a unit impulse $\delta(t)$. Similarly, for a discrete-time LTI system, the impulse response $h[n]$ is the output when the input is a unit sample $\delta[n]$.

Mathematically, this characterization is expressed as:

$$h(t) \triangleq \mathcal{T}\{\delta(t)\}$$

$$h[n] \triangleq \mathcal{T}\{\delta[n]\}$$

where $\mathcal{T}\{\cdot\}$ represents the system transformation operator.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is the impulse response so critical?**
> Because LTI systems are both **Linear** and **Time-Invariant**, knowledge of the response to *one specific input* (the impulse) is **sufficient to determine the response to ANY arbitrary input** via the convolution operation. This is a profound result in systems theory.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **church bell (the LTI system)**. If you strike it with a single, sharp tap (the impulse $\delta(t)$), it produces a characteristic "dong" sound that slowly fades away (this is $h(t)$).

Now, instead of one tap, you push the bell continuously with a varying force for 10 seconds (an arbitrary input $x(t)$). Because the bell behaves *linearly* (double the force → double the response amplitude) and *time-invariant* (a tap at $t=2s$ produces the same "dong" shape as a tap at $t=0$, just delayed), the resulting sound is simply the **sum of many tiny "dongs"** — each triggered by a tiny tap — and each tap produces its own little response that starts at the moment of the tap and fades.

The total sound is the **convolution** of the pushing force $x(t)$ with the bell's characteristic "dong" $h(t)$. This is precisely what convolution means!

### 1.3 The Unit Impulse — Foundation of Everything

The continuous-time unit impulse $\delta(t)$ (also called the **Dirac Delta Function**) is formally defined by two properties:

$$1. \quad \delta(t) = 0 \quad \text{for } t \neq 0$$

$$2. \quad \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \delta(t)\,dt = 1$$

For discrete-time, the unit sample (or unit impulse) $\delta[n]$ is:

$$\delta[n] = \begin{cases} 1, & n = 0 \\ 0, & n \neq 0 \end{cases}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Sifting Property of $\delta(t)$:** The most useful property — any signal can be decomposed into a continuum of weighted, shifted impulses:
> $$x(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(\tau)\, \delta(t-\tau)\,d\tau$$
> This is the cornerstone of why LTI systems are characterized by $h(t)$.

### 1.4 Significance in KTU 2024 Scheme

The impulse response is a **high-weightage** topic in Module 3, bridging Module 2 (LTI system properties) and Module 3 (Convolution). Every subsequent concept in Fourier/Laplace/Z-transforms depends on $h(t)$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic: From Impulse Decomposition to System Response

The derivation of why $h(t)$ characterizes the entire LTI system follows a clean logical chain:

**Step 1 — Decompose the input using the sifting property:**
An arbitrary input $x(t)$ can be expressed as a continuous superposition of shifted, weighted impulses:
$$x(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(\tau)\, \delta(t-\tau)\,d\tau$$

**Step 2 — Apply the system operator (using Linearity):**
The system transforms the input:
$$y(t) = \mathcal{T}\{x(t)\} = \mathcal{T}\left\{\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(\tau)\, \delta(t-\tau)\,d\tau\right\}$$

**Step 3 — Bring the operator inside the integral (using Linearity):**
$$y(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(\tau)\, \mathcal{T}\{\delta(t-\tau)\}\,d\tau$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Time-Invariance property:**
Since the system is time-invariant, the response to $\delta(t-\tau)$ is simply the response to $\delta(t)$ delayed by $\tau$, which is exactly $h(t-\tau)$:
$$\mathcal{T}\{\delta(t-\tau)\} = h(t-\tau)$$

**Step 5 — Assemble the final result (Convolution Integral):**
$$y(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(\tau)\, h(t-\tau)\,d\tau \triangleq (x * h)(t)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The 'Why' behind it:** Linearity allows superposition (breaking the input into pieces, transforming each, summing). Time-Invariance guarantees that the *shape* of the response to a shifted impulse is identical — just shifted in time. The combination is the **Convolution Integral**, and it shows that $h(t)$ alone determines the system.

### 2.2 The Discrete-Time Analog

For a discrete-time LTI system with input $x[n]$ and impulse response $h[n]$:

$$y[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{+\infty} x[k]\, h[n-k] \triangleq (x * h)[n]$$

This is the **Convolution Sum** — a discrete weighted sum rather than a continuous integral.

### 2.3 Properties of LTI Systems Characterized by $h(t)$

| Property | Continuous-Time Condition | Discrete-Time Condition | Engineering Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Memoryless** | $h(t) = 0$ for $t \neq 0$ (i.e., $h(t) = K\delta(t)$) | $h[n] = 0$ for $n \neq 0$ | Output depends only on current input |
| **Causal** | $h(t) = 0$ for $t < 0$ | $h[n] = 0$ for $n < 0$ | Output does not depend on future inputs |
| **Stable (BIBO)** | $\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert h(t) \vert \, dt < \infty$ | $\sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert h[n] \vert < \infty$ | Bounded input → Bounded output |
| **Invertible** | Exists $h_i(t)$ s.t. $h * h_i = \delta(t)$ | Exists $h_i[n]$ s.t. $h * h_i = \delta[n]$ | System has a well-defined inverse |
| **Identity System** | $h(t) = \delta(t)$ | $h[n] = \delta[n]$ | Output equals input |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** For a causal LTI system, the convolution integral simplifies to:
> $$y(t) = \int_{0}^{t} x(\tau)\, h(t-\tau)\,d\tau \quad \text{for } t \geq 0$$
> because $h(t-\tau) = 0$ when $\tau > t$.

### 2.4 Step Response — A Companion to Impulse Response

The **Step Response** $s(t)$ is the output when the input is a unit step $u(t)$. Since $u(t)$ is the running integral of $\delta(t)$, and LTI systems commute with integration:

$$s(t) = (h * u)(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{t} h(\tau)\,d\tau$$

Differentiating both sides:
$$h(t) = \frac{d s(t)}{d t}$$

> This duality is heavily tested in KTU exams.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $h(t) = \mathcal{T}\{\delta(t)\}$ | Definition of impulse response |
| 2 | $y(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(\tau) h(t-\tau)\,d\tau$ | Convolution integral (CT) |
| 3 | $y[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{+\infty} x[k] h[n-k]$ | Convolution sum (DT) |
| 4 | $x(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(\tau) \delta(t-\tau)\,d\tau$ | Sifting property |
| 5 | $h(t) = \dfrac{d s(t)}{d t}$ | Step response ↔ Impulse response |
| 6 | $s(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{t} h(\tau)\,d\tau$ | Step response from impulse response |
| 7 | $\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert h(t) \vert \, dt < \infty$ | BIBO stability condition (CT) |
| 8 | $\sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert h[n] \vert < \infty$ | BIBO stability condition (DT) |
| 9 | $h(t) = 0, \ t < 0$ | Causality condition (CT) |
| 10 | $h[n] = 0, \ n < 0$ | Causality condition (DT) |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The impulse response framework is foundational across all of electrical engineering:

- **Filter Design:** The behavior of every analog/digital filter (low-pass, high-pass, band-pass) is completely specified by $h(t)$ or $h[n]$.
- **Communications:** Channel equalization in wireless systems uses the *inverse* impulse response to undo signal distortion.
- **Control Systems:** The unit-impulse response determines transient behavior, settling time, and stability margins.
- **Audio & Image Processing:** Echo cancellation and deconvolution (e.g., Hubble telescope image restoration) rely on $h(t)$ characterization.
- **Biomedical (ECG/EEG):** Deconvolution separates overlapping physiological signals.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: The Convolution Integral from First Principles

We rigorously derive the **convolution integral** starting from the sifting property and the LTI axioms.

**Starting Point — Sifting property of $\delta(t)$:**

$$x(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(\tau)\, \delta(t-\tau)\,d\tau$$

**Step 1 — Apply the system operator $\mathcal{T}\{\cdot\}$ to both sides:**

$$y(t) = \mathcal{T}\{x(t)\} = \mathcal{T}\left\{\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(\tau)\, \delta(t-\tau)\,d\tau\right\}$$

This means: pass the entire decomposed input through the system.

**Step 2 — Exploit Linearity to bring $\mathcal{T}$ inside the integral:**

A linear system obeys:
$$\mathcal{T}\left\{\int \alpha(\tau)\, p(t,\tau)\,d\tau\right\} = \int \alpha(\tau)\, \mathcal{T}\{p(t,\tau)\}\,d\tau$$

Applying this:

$$y(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(\tau)\, \mathcal{T}\{\delta(t-\tau)\}\,d\tau$$

**Interpretation:** The system output is the weighted integral of the system's response to *each individual shifted impulse*, weighted by $x(\tau)$.

**Step 3 — Apply Time-Invariance:**

A time-invariant system satisfies: if $x(t) \to y(t)$, then $x(t-t_0) \to y(t-t_0)$.

So if $\delta(t) \to h(t)$, then $\delta(t-\tau) \to h(t-\tau)$.

Substituting:

$$y(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(\tau)\, h(t-\tau)\,d\tau$$

**Step 4 — Recognize the operation as convolution:**

$$\boxed{\,y(t) = (x * h)(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(\tau)\, h(t-\tau)\,d\tau\,}$$

This is the **Continuous-Time Convolution Integral**. The discrete-time analog follows identically using a summation:

$$\boxed{\,y[n] = (x * h)[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{+\infty} x[k]\, h[n-k]\,}$$

### 3.2 Worked Analytical Example — Causal LTI System

> **Problem:** An LTI system has impulse response $h(t) = e^{-2t} u(t)$. Determine whether the system is (a) causal and (b) BIBO stable. Then find the output $y(t)$ for input $x(t) = e^{-t} u(t)$.

**Solution:**

**(a) Causality Check:**

$$h(t) = e^{-2t} u(t) = \begin{cases} e^{-2t}, & t \geq 0 \\ 0, & t < 0 \end{cases}$$

Since $h(t) = 0$ for $t < 0$, the system is **causal**. ✓

**(b) BIBO Stability Check:**

$$\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert h(t) \vert\, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-2t}\, dt = \left[ -\frac{1}{2} e^{-2t} \right]_{0}^{\infty} = 0 - \left(-\frac{1}{2}\right) = \frac{1}{2}$$

Since the integral converges to a finite value ($\frac{1}{2} < \infty$), the system is **BIBO stable**. ✓

**(c) Compute $y(t)$ using the convolution integral:**

Because both $x(t)$ and $h(t)$ are causal, the limits of integration reduce to $0$ to $t$:

$$y(t) = \int_{0}^{t} x(\tau)\, h(t-\tau)\, d\tau = \int_{0}^{t} e^{-\tau} \cdot e^{-2(t-\tau)}\, d\tau$$

Factor out constants independent of $\tau$:

$$y(t) = e^{-2t} \int_{0}^{t} e^{-\tau} \cdot e^{2\tau}\, d\tau = e^{-2t} \int_{0}^{t} e^{\tau}\, d\tau$$

Integrate:

$$y(t) = e^{-2t} \left[ e^{\tau} \right]_{0}^{t} = e^{-2t} \left( e^{t} - 1 \right)$$

Simplify:

$$y(t) = e^{-t} - e^{-2t}, \quad t \geq 0$$

Express with unit step:

$$\boxed{\,y(t) = \left(e^{-t} - e^{-2t}\right) u(t)\,}$$

### 3.3 Worked Example — Discrete-Time Convolution

> **Problem:** A discrete-time LTI system has impulse response $h[n] = \{1, 2, 1\}$ (with $h[0]=1, h[1]=2, h[2]=1$) and zero elsewhere. Find the output $y[n]$ for input $x[n] = \{1, 1\}$ (with $x[0]=1, x[1]=1$).

**Solution using the Tabular Method (Flipping & Sliding):**

The discrete convolution is:
$$y[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{1} x[k] h[n-k]$$

**Compute $y[0]$:**
$$y[0] = x[0]h[0] + x[1]h[-1] = (1)(1) + (1)(0) = 1$$

**Compute $y[1]$:**
$$y[1] = x[0]h[1] + x[1]h[0] = (1)(2) + (1)(1) = 3$$

**Compute $y[2]$:**
$$y[2] = x[0]h[2] + x[1]h[1] = (1)(1) + (1)(2) = 3$$

**Compute $y[3]$:**
$$y[3] = x[0]h[3] + x[1]h[2] = (1)(0) + (1)(1) = 1$$

**Result:**
$$y[n] = \{1, 3, 3, 1\} \quad \text{with } y[0]=1, y[1]=3, y[2]=3, y[3]=1$$

The output length is $(2 + 3 - 1) = 4$ samples. ✓

### 3.4 Verification: Associativity Property of Convolution

The convolution operator is **associative**:
$$(x * h_1) * h_2 = x * (h_1 * h_2)$$

This can be verified directly:

$$\begin{aligned}
((x * h_1) * h_2)(t) &= \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \left[\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(\tau) h_1(\sigma - \tau)\, d\tau \right] h_2(t - \sigma)\, d\sigma \\
&= \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(\tau) \left[\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} h_1(\sigma - \tau) h_2(t - \sigma)\, d\sigma \right] d\tau \\
&= \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(\tau) (h_1 * h_2)(t - \tau)\, d\tau \\
&= (x * (h_1 * h_2))(t) \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Importance:** Associativity implies that cascading LTI systems $h_1 \to h_2$ is equivalent to a *single* LTI system with impulse response $h_1 * h_2$. This is foundational in filter design and system interconnection analysis.

### 3.5 Summary of Key Mathematical Relationships

| Relationship | Continuous-Time | Discrete-Time |
|---|---|---|
| Output | $y(t) = (x * h)(t)$ | $y[n] = (x * h)[n]$ |
| Causality | $h(t) = 0$ for $t < 0$ | $h[n] = 0$ for $n < 0$ |
| Stability | $\int \vert h(t) \vert dt < \infty$ | $\sum \vert h[n] \vert < \infty$ |
| Step Response | $s(t) = h(t) * u(t)$ | $s[n] = h[n] * u[n]$ |
| Commutativity | $x * h = h * x$ | $x * h = h * x$ |
| Associativity | $(x * h_1) * h_2 = x * (h_1 * h_2)$ | Same form |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Block Diagram — LTI System Characterized by Impulse Response

```mermaid
graph LR
    src[Input Signal x of t] --> decomp[Sifting Decomposition]
    decomp --> impulse1[Weighted Impulse at tau 1]
    decomp --> impulse2[Weighted Impulse at tau 2]
    decomp --> impulse3[Weighted Impulse at tau 3]
    impulse1 --> resp1[Response h of t minus tau 1]
    impulse2 --> resp2[Response h of t minus tau 2]
    impulse3 --> resp3[Response h of t minus tau 3]
    resp1 --> sum[Superposition Integral]
    resp2 --> sum
    resp3 --> sum
    sum --> out[Output y of t]
```

### 4.2 Convolution Operation — Sliding, Flipping, Multiplying, Integrating

```mermaid
graph TD
    start[Initialize tau equals 0] --> flip[Flip h of tau to get h of minus tau]
    flip --> slide[Slide h of minus tau by t to get h of t minus tau]
    slide --> mul[Multiply x of tau and h of t minus tau]
    mul --> integ[Integrate product over all tau]
    integ --> store[Store result as y of t]
    store --> next[Increment t and repeat]
    next --> done{All t covered?}
    done -->|No| slide
    done -->|Yes| finish[Complete Output y of t]
```

### 4.3 System Interconnection Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    in1[Input x of t] --> sys1[LTI System h1 of t]
    sys1 --> mid1[Intermediate Signal y1]
    mid1 --> sys2[LTI System h2 of t]
    sys2 --> out1[Output y of t]
    in2[Input x of t] --> equiv[Equivalent System h1 star h2]
    equiv --> out2[Output y of t]
    out1 -.->|Identical| out2
```

### 4.4 Causal LTI System — Time-Domain Response Mapping

```mermaid
graph TD
    delta[Unit Impulse delta of t] --> sys[LTI System Operator T]
    sys --> h[Impulse Response h of t]
    step[Unit Step u of t] --> sys2[Same LTI System T]
    sys2 --> s[Step Response s of t]
    h --> diff[Differentiation ds dt]
    diff --> recover[Recovered h of t]
    h --> integ[Integration 0 to t]
    integ --> s
    s --> diff2[Differentiation]
    diff2 --> recover
```

### 4.5 Sequence Diagram — Evaluating Convolution Step-by-Step

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Student
    participant System
    participant Math
    Student->>System: Input x of t
    System->>Math: Compute h of t from delta of t
    Math-->>System: h of t equals T of delta of t
    System->>Math: Form sifting decomposition of x of t
    Math-->>System: x of t equals integral of x tau delta t minus tau d tau
    System->>Math: Apply T and use Time Invariance
    Math-->>System: Output y of t equals integral of x tau h t minus tau d tau
    System-->>Student: y of t equals x star h of t
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
> **Define the impulse response of a continuous-time LTI system. What is the significance of the impulse response in characterizing an LTI system?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **impulse response** $h(t)$ of a continuous-time LTI system is defined as the output of the system when the input is a unit impulse $\delta(t)$, i.e., $h(t) = \mathcal{T}\{\delta(t)\}$.

**Significance (2 marks):** The impulse response is a complete characterization of an LTI system because, by virtue of **Linearity** and **Time-Invariance**, the response $y(t)$ to *any* arbitrary input $x(t)$ can be computed using the **convolution integral**:
$$y(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(\tau) h(t-\tau)\, d\tau$$
Thus, a single function $h(t)$ fully determines the system's behavior for all possible inputs. **(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for significance and convolution formula)**

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
> **State and explain the BIBO stability condition for an LTI system in terms of its impulse response.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A continuous-time LTI system is **Bounded-Input Bounded-Output (BIBO) stable** if and only if its impulse response is **absolutely integrable**, i.e.,
$$\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert h(t) \vert \, dt < \infty$$
For discrete-time LTI systems, the condition becomes: $\sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert h[n] \vert < \infty$.

**Explanation (2 marks):** This condition ensures that when a bounded input $|x(t)| \leq M$ is applied, the output magnitude $|y(t)| = \left| \int x(\tau) h(t-\tau)\, d\tau \right| \leq M \int \vert h(\tau) \vert\, d\tau$ also remains bounded. **(1 mark for stating condition in both CT/DT + 2 marks for explanation)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 3]`

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Starting from the sifting property of the unit impulse, derive the convolution integral for a continuous-time LTI system. Clearly state where linearity and time-invariance are used in the derivation.

> **(b) [7 Marks]** An LTI system is described by the impulse response $h(t) = e^{-3t} u(t)$. Determine: (i) whether the system is causal, (ii) whether it is BIBO stable, and (iii) the step response $s(t)$ of the system.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation [7 marks]:**

**Step 1 [1 mark]:** Express the input $x(t)$ using the sifting property:
$$x(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(\tau)\, \delta(t-\tau)\, d\tau$$

**Step 2 [1 mark]:** Pass $x(t)$ through the LTI system:
$$y(t) = \mathcal{T}\{x(t)\} = \mathcal{T}\left\{\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(\tau)\, \delta(t-\tau)\, d\tau\right\}$$

**Step 3 [2 marks]:** Apply **Linearity** to bring $\mathcal{T}$ inside the integral:
$$y(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(\tau)\, \mathcal{T}\{\delta(t-\tau)\}\, d\tau$$

**Step 4 [2 marks]:** Apply **Time-Invariance**. Since $\delta(t) \to h(t)$, by time-invariance $\delta(t-\tau) \to h(t-\tau)$:
$$y(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(\tau)\, h(t-\tau)\, d\tau$$

**Step 5 [1 mark]:** Identify as convolution:
$$y(t) = (x * h)(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(\tau)\, h(t-\tau)\, d\tau \quad \blacksquare$$

**(b) Solution [7 marks]:**

**(i) Causality [2 marks]:** $h(t) = e^{-3t} u(t)$ is zero for $t < 0$. Hence the system is **causal**. ✓

**(ii) BIBO Stability [2 marks]:** 
$$\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert h(t) \vert\, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-3t}\, dt = \frac{1}{3} < \infty$$
Hence the system is **BIBO stable**. ✓

**(iii) Step Response [3 marks]:**
$$s(t) = (h * u)(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{t} h(\tau)\, d\tau = \int_{0}^{t} e^{-3\tau}\, d\tau$$
$$s(t) = \left[ -\frac{1}{3} e^{-3\tau} \right]_{0}^{t} = \frac{1}{3}\left(1 - e^{-3t}\right), \quad t \geq 0$$
$$\boxed{\,s(t) = \frac{1}{3}\left(1 - e^{-3t}\right) u(t)\,}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative choice*

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Define the impulse response of a discrete-time LTI system. With the help of a block diagram, explain the procedure for computing the convolution sum $y[n] = (x * h)[n]$ for finite-length sequences.

> **(b) [7 Marks]** Consider an LTI system with impulse response $h[n] = (0.5)^n u[n]$. Compute the output $y[n]$ for the input $x[n] = (0.8)^n u[n]$. Identify whether the system is stable and causal.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Definition + Procedure [7 marks]:**

**Definition [2 marks]:** The impulse response $h[n]$ of a discrete-time LTI system is the output of the system when the input is the unit sample $\delta[n]$, i.e., $h[n] = \mathcal{T}\{\delta[n]\}$.

**Procedure [5 marks]:** The convolution sum is:
$$y[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{+\infty} x[k] h[n-k]$$

**Steps:**
1. **Plot** $x[k]$ and $h[k]$ versus $k$.
2. **Flip** $h[k]$ about $k = 0$ to obtain $h[-k]$.
3. **Shift** $h[-k]$ by $n$ units to get $h[n-k]$.
4. **Multiply** $x[k]$ and $h[n-k]$ pointwise.
5. **Sum** all products to get $y[n]$.
6. **Repeat** for each $n$.

**Block Diagram (textual) [1 mark]:**
```
x[k]  --+--> [Multiplied by] h[n-k] --> [Summing block] --> y[n]
        (using flipped and shifted h)
```

**(b) Solution [7 marks]:**

**Causality [1 mark]:** $h[n] = (0.5)^n u[n] = 0$ for $n < 0$. Hence the system is **causal**. ✓

**Stability [1 mark]:** 
$$\sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert h[n] \vert = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} (0.5)^n = \frac{1}{1 - 0.5} = 2 < \infty$$
Hence the system is **BIBO stable**. ✓

**Output Computation [5 marks]:**
$$y[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{n} x[k] h[n-k] = \sum_{k=0}^{n} (0.8)^k (0.5)^{n-k}$$

**Factor out $(0.5)^n$:**
$$y[n] = (0.5)^n \sum_{k=0}^{n} \left(\frac{0.8}{0.5}\right)^k = (0.5)^n \sum_{k=0}^{n} (1.6)^k$$

**Apply finite geometric sum formula:** $\sum_{k=0}^{n} r^k = \dfrac{1 - r^{n+1}}{1 - r}$ for $r \neq 1$:

$$y[n] = (0.5)^n \cdot \frac{1 - (1.6)^{n+1}}{1 - 1.6} = (0.5)^n \cdot \frac{(1.6)^{n+1} - 1}{0.6}$$

Simplify:
$$y[n] = \frac{(0.5)^n (1.6)^{n+1} - (0.5)^n}{0.6} = \frac{1.6 \cdot (0.8)^n - (0.5)^n}{0.6}$$

$$\boxed{\,y[n] = \frac{8}{3} (0.8)^n - \frac{5}{3} (0.5)^n, \quad n \geq 0\,}$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting to check causality limits** — Students often write the convolution integral from $-\infty$ to $+\infty$ for a causal system, losing **1–2 marks** for not simplifying limits to $[0, t]$.
> 2. **Confusing $h(t-\tau)$ with $h(\tau-t)$** — The flip in the convolution integral is on the *impulse response* argument relative to the dummy variable $\tau$, NOT the absolute time $t$. This sign error is the **#1 mistake** in KTU scripts.
> 3. **Absolute value in stability test** — Writing $\int h(t) dt < \infty$ instead of $\int \vert h(t) \vert dt < \infty$ is a **common error**. The integral of $h(t)$ being finite does NOT guarantee stability; absolute integrability is required.
> 4. **Missing $u(t)$ or $u[n]$ in final answer** — The output of a causal system must be expressed as $y(t) = (\text{expression}) u(t)$ or $y[n] = (\text{expression}) u[n]$ for completeness. Omitting this loses **1 mark** consistently.
> 5. **Confusing $h(t)$ with $H(\omega)$** — $h(t)$ is time-domain impulse response, while $H(\omega)$ is its Fourier transform (frequency response). Mixing these in derivations is a serious conceptual error.
> 6. **Not stating the property (L or TI) used** — During derivations, KTU examiners expect students to **explicitly mention "by Linearity"** and **"by Time-Invariance"** at the right step. Skipping these annotations loses easy marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ **Impulse Response $h(t) = \mathcal{T}\{\delta(t)\}$** — the system's response to a unit impulse.
- ✅ **Convolution Integral:** $y(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(\tau) h(t-\tau)\, d\tau$
- ✅ **Convolution Sum:** $y[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{+\infty} x[k] h[n-k]$
- ✅ **Sifting Property:** $x(t) = \int x(\tau) \delta(t-\tau) d\tau$ — basis for LTI system characterization.
- ✅ **Linearity** allows the system operator to move inside integrals/sums.
- ✅ **Time-Invariance** converts $\delta(t-\tau)$ to $h(t-\tau)$.
- ✅ **Causal LTI system:** $h(t) = 0$ for $t < 0$ (or $h[n] = 0$ for $n < 0$).
- ✅ **BIBO Stable LTI system:** $\int \vert h(t) \vert dt < \infty$ (or $\sum \vert h[n] \vert < \infty$).
- ✅ **Step Response:** $s(t) = h(t) * u(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{t} h(\tau) d\tau$
- ✅ **Duality:** $h(t) = \dfrac{ds(t)}{dt}$
- ✅ **Memoryless system:** $h(t) = K\delta(t)$ or $h[n] = K\delta[n]$.
- ✅ **Identity system:** $h(t) = \delta(t)$ (output = input).
- ✅ **Cascade of LTI systems:** Equivalent impulse response = convolution of individual responses ($h_{eq} = h_1 * h_2$).
- ✅ **Convolution Properties:** Commutative, Associative, Distributive over addition.
- ✅ **Output length** (DT finite sequences): $\text{len}(y) = \text{len}(x) + \text{len}(h) - 1$.
- ✅ **Causal convolution limits** reduce to $[0, t]$ (or $[0, n]$ in DT).
- ✅ The impulse response is the **complete** description of an LTI system — a single function captures *all* input-output behavior.

> **One-line takeaway:** *If you know $h(t)$, you know the LTI system — because every output is just the convolution of the input with $h(t)$.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST416 - SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Difference Equation (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):**
> A **difference equation** is a mathematical equation that relates the discrete-time samples of a sequence $y[n]$ to its past values $y[n-1], y[n-2], \dots$ and/or to the input sequence $x[n]$ and its past values. It is the discrete-time counterpart of a differential equation and is the fundamental governing law for **Linear Time-Invariant (LTI) Discrete-Time Systems (LTI-DTS)**.

A **Linear Constant-Coefficient Difference Equation (LCCDE)** has the canonical form:

$$\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k \, y[n-k] = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k \, x[n-k]$$

where $a_k$ and $b_k$ are constant (time-invariant) real coefficients, with $a_0$ conventionally normalized to **1**.

Expanding this:

$$y[n] = -\sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k \, y[n-k] + \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k \, x[n-k]$$

This is the **recursive form** used in KTU board problems to compute $y[n]$ given initial conditions $y[-1], y[-2], \dots, y[-N]$.

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy: The "Monthly Bank Account"

Imagine a savings account where:
- $y[n]$ = balance at the end of month $n$
- $x[n]$ = deposit made in month $n$
- $a_1$ = fraction of previous balance retained (e.g., $0.9$ if $10\%$ is withdrawn monthly)
- $b_0, b_1$ = portions of the deposit that go into the account this month and next month

Then the account balance obeys:

$$y[n] = 0.9 \, y[n-1] + x[n] + 0.5 \, x[n-1]$$

> **The past month’s balance ($y[n-1]$) influences the current balance, and the current input ($x[n]$) plus delayed input ($x[n-1]$) also contribute.** This memory-and-input dependence is exactly what a difference equation captures.

## 1.3 Physical & Engineering Significance

Difference equations are not abstract math; they are the **backbone of every digital filter, DSP algorithm, controller, and recursive computer program**:

| Engineering Domain | Use of Difference Equation |
|---|---|
| **Digital Signal Processing (DSP)** | FIR, IIR filters — $y[n] = \sum b_k x[n-k] - \sum a_k y[n-k]$ |
| **Digital Control Systems** | Discrete-time PID controllers in microcontrollers |
| **Biomedical Engineering** | Modeling heart-rate variability, ECG digital filtering |
| **Economics & Finance** | Recursive interest, loan amortization |
| **Communications** | Channel equalizers, echo cancellers |
| **Computer Science** | Recursion, dynamic programming recurrences |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight (KTU Board Favourite):** In Signals & Systems, the difference equation is the **time-domain description** of an LTI discrete system, equivalent to the **transfer function $H(z)$ in the Z-domain** and the **frequency response $H(e^{j\omega})$ in the DTFT domain**. The three views are interchangeable.

---

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Difference Equations

### 2.1.1 By Linearity

**Linear Difference Equation** — dependent variable $y[n]$ and its shifts appear to the **first power only**, with **no products** like $y[n] \cdot y[n-1]$ or $y^2[n]$.

$$y[n] - 0.5 \, y[n-1] = x[n] \quad \text{(Linear)}$$

**Non-Linear Difference Equation** — contains products, powers, or nonlinear functions.

$$y[n] - y[n-1] \cdot y[n-2] = x[n] \quad \text{(Non-Linear)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Standard:** Most exam problems are strictly **Linear Constant-Coefficient Difference Equations (LCCDE)**.

### 2.1.2 By Order

The **order** of a difference equation is the difference between the highest and lowest delays of the output.

$$y[n] - 0.5 \, y[n-1] + 0.3 \, y[n-3] = x[n] \implies \text{Order} = 3$$

### 2.1.3 By Homogeneity

- **Homogeneous:** All input terms are zero. $\sum a_k y[n-k] = 0$
- **Non-Homogeneous (Forced):** Input $x[n]$ is present. $\sum a_k y[n-k] = \sum b_k x[n-k]$

## 2.2 The Complete Solution Structure (Why and How)

For a linear system, by the **principle of superposition**, the total response is the sum of two independent parts:

> $$y[n] = y_h[n] + y_p[n]$$

| Component | Name | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| $y_h[n]$ | **Homogeneous / Natural / Zero-Input Response** | System's intrinsic behavior due to initial conditions (memory) |
| $y_p[n]$ | **Particular / Forced / Zero-State Response** | System's response purely due to the external input $x[n]$ |

### Step-by-Step Logic of $y_h[n]$ (Natural Response)

1. Set $x[n] = 0$ in the LCCDE.
2. Assume a trial solution of the form $y_h[n] = \lambda^n$ (an exponential).
3. Substitute into the homogeneous equation.
4. This yields the **Characteristic Equation**: $a_0 \lambda^N + a_1 \lambda^{N-1} + \cdots + a_N = 0$.
5. Find the $N$ roots $\lambda_1, \lambda_2, \dots, \lambda_N$ (real or complex conjugates).
6. Form the general homogeneous solution based on the root multiplicity.

### Step-by-Step Logic of $y_p[n]$ (Forced Response)

The particular solution **assumes the form of the input**:

| Input $x[n]$ | Trial Form of $y_p[n]$ |
|---|---|
| Constant $C$ | Constant $K$ |
| $A^n$ | $K \cdot A^n$ (if $A$ is not a root of characteristic equation) |
| $\cos(\omega_0 n)$ or $\sin(\omega_0 n)$ | $K_1 \cos(\omega_0 n) + K_2 \sin(\omega_0 n)$ |
| $n$ (ramp) | $K_1 n + K_2$ |

If the trial form overlaps with a root, **multiply by $n^r$** where $r$ is the multiplicity of the overlapping root (method of undetermined coefficients — similar to ODEs).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **LCCDE (General Form)** | $\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k y[n-k] = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k x[n-k]$ | $a_0 = 1$ by convention |
| 2 | **Recursive Output** | $y[n] = -\sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k y[n-k] + \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k x[n-k]$ | Direct computation form |
| 3 | **Total Response** | $y[n] = y_h[n] + y_p[n]$ | Superposition principle |
| 4 | **Characteristic Equation** | $\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k \lambda^{N-k} = 0$ | Set $x[n]=0$, substitute $\lambda^n$ |
| 5 | **Distinct Real Roots** | $y_h[n] = \sum_{i=1}^{N} C_i \lambda_i^n$ | $C_i$ fixed by initial conditions |
| 6 | **Repeated Real Root $\lambda$ (multiplicity $r$)** | $(C_1 + C_2 n + \cdots + C_r n^{r-1}) \lambda^n$ | Multiply by $n^{r-1}$ terms |
| 7 | **Complex Conjugate Pair** $\lambda = \alpha \pm j\beta$ | $A \cdot \rho^n \cos(\Omega n + \phi)$ | $\rho = \sqrt{\alpha^2 + \beta^2}, \quad \Omega = \tan^{-1}(\beta/\alpha)$ |
| 8 | **Transfer Function (Z-domain)** | $H(z) = \dfrac{\sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k z^{-k}}{\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k z^{-k}}$ | Direct equivalence to LCCDE |
| 9 | **Zero-Input Response Initial Conditions** | Need $y[-1], y[-2], \dots, y[-N]$ | $N$ initial conditions for $N$th order |
| 10 | **Stability Condition** | $\vert \lambda_i \vert < 1 \;\; \forall i$ | All characteristic roots inside unit circle |

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick Memory Aid:** "**H**omogeneous = **H**istorical memory" — it tells you what the system does on its own. "**P**articular = **P**ushed by input" — it tells you how the input shapes the output.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

A real **IIR digital filter** (Infinite Impulse Response) is implemented exactly as an LCCDE in a microcontroller or DSP chip. For example, a 2nd-order Butterworth low-pass filter running on an audio codec uses:

$$y[n] = 1.83 \, y[n-1] - 0.90 \, y[n-2] + 0.0017 \, x[n] + 0.0034 \, x[n-1] + 0.0017 \, x[n-2]$$

This tiny equation, evaluated 44,100 times per second on a smartphone, is precisely what removes hiss from your music. That is the power of difference equations in modern engineering.
