# 📒 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State the motivation for introducing the Z Transform in the analysis of discrete-time signals and systems.**

**Model Answer:**

The Z Transform is introduced in discrete-time signal processing due to the following motivations:

1. **Generalization of the DTFT:** The Z Transform generalizes the Discrete-Time Fourier Transform (DTFT) by introducing a complex variable $z = re^{j\omega}$. The DTFT is obtained as a special case of the Z Transform when $|z| = 1$ (on the unit circle).

2. **Convergence for unstable systems:** The DTFT fails to converge for many important signals such as growing exponentials ($a^n u[n]$ for $a > 1$) and un-damped sinusoids. The Z Transform handles these signals by exploiting a Region of Convergence (ROC) that can extend beyond the unit circle.

3. **Pole-zero analysis:** The Z Transform provides a convenient pole-zero representation of discrete LTI systems in the z-plane, which simplifies stability analysis and digital filter design.

4. **Solution of difference equations:** It converts linear constant-coefficient difference equations into algebraic equations in $z$, which is computationally efficient.

5. **Comprehensive tool for DSP:** It is the fundamental mathematical framework for digital filter design, spectrum analysis, and digital control systems.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the Region of Convergence (ROC) in the Z Transform? Mention any two of its properties.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Region of Convergence (ROC)** of $X(z)$ is the set of all values of the complex variable $z$ for which the Z Transform $X(z) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] z^{-n}$ converges, i.e., the infinite sum is finite.

$$ROC = \{ z \in \mathbb{C} : \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n] \vert \vert z \vert^{-n} < \infty \}$$

**Two important properties of the ROC:**

1. **The ROC is a ring (annulus) in the z-plane** of the form $r_1 < \vert z \vert < r_2$, possibly extending to infinity or contracting to zero.

2. **The ROC does not contain any poles** of $X(z)$. Poles are the values of $z$ that make $X(z)$ infinite; these mark the boundaries of the ROC.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** In Part B, Module 4 questions offer an internal choice between **Question A** and **Question B** (each carrying 14 marks with sub-parts of 7 + 7 marks). Both alternatives are provided below.

---

### 🔷 Question A (14 Marks): Motivation & DTFT Limitation Analysis

#### Part (a) `[7 Marks]` `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`
**Explain in detail the limitations of the Discrete-Time Fourier Transform (DTFT) and discuss how the Z Transform overcomes these limitations.**

**Model Solution:**

The DTFT of a discrete-time signal $x[n]$ is defined as:

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] e^{-j\omega n}$$

**Convergence Requirement:** The DTFT exists only if the signal is **absolutely summable**:

$$\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n] \vert < \infty$$

**Major Limitations of DTFT:**

**Limitation 1: Failure for Growing Signals**
Consider $x[n] = a^n u[n]$ with $a > 1$ (e.g., $a = 2$).
- The DTFT series becomes $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} (a e^{-j\omega})^n$.
- Since $\vert a e^{-j\omega} \vert = a > 1$, the geometric series **diverges**.
- Hence the DTFT **does not exist** for such signals.

**Limitation 2: Failure for Un-damped Sinusoids**
For $x[n] = \cos(\omega_0 n) u[n]$, the signal neither decays nor grows, and the series is not absolutely summable. The DTFT is undefined in the strict sense (only defined in the distributional sense via impulses).

**Limitation 3: Single Convergence Criterion**
The DTFT demands convergence at a single radius ($|z|=1$), which excludes many physically meaningful signals.

**How the Z Transform Overcomes These Limitations:**

The Z Transform introduces a **weighting factor** $r^{-n}$ to the summation:

$$X(z) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] r^{-n} e^{-j\omega n} = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] z^{-n}$$

- By **choosing the appropriate $r$**, we can force even growing signals to converge.
- For $x[n] = a^n u[n]$ with $a > 1$, choosing $r > a$ makes the weighted series converge. The Z Transform is $\dfrac{z}{z-a}$ with ROC $|z| > a$.
- The convergence is now a **region** (the ROC), not a single point, allowing analysis of a **much wider class** of signals including unstable systems.

**Conclusion:** The Z Transform acts as a **generalized framework** that subsumes the DTFT as a special case (when evaluated on the unit circle), enabling analysis of all discrete-time LTI systems.

---

#### Part (b) `[7 Marks]` `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`
**Derive the bilateral Z Transform from the DTFT. Show that the DTFT is a special case of the Z Transform.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Start with the DTFT definition.**

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] e^{-j\omega n}$$

**Step 2: Multiply $x[n]$ by a real factor $r^{-n}$ to introduce a generalized weighting.**

We define a new function:

$$X(r, \omega) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} (x[n] r^{-n}) e^{-j\omega n}$$

**Step 3: Combine the exponentials.**

$$X(r, \omega) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] r^{-n} e^{-j\omega n} = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] (r e^{j\omega})^{-n}$$

**Step 4: Define a new complex variable $z = r e^{j\omega}$.**

Then $z^{-n} = (r e^{j\omega})^{-n} = r^{-n} e^{-j\omega n}$. Substituting:

$$X(z) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] z^{-n}$$

This is the **bilateral Z Transform**. ✅

**Step 5: Show that the DTFT is a special case.**

Substitute $r = 1$ (i.e., $\vert z \vert = 1$) into the Z Transform:

$$X(z)\big\vert_{r=1, z=e^{j\omega}} = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] (1)^{-n} e^{-j\omega n} = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] e^{-j\omega n} = X(e^{j\omega})$$

Therefore, the DTFT is the Z Transform evaluated on the **unit circle**, which is valid when the unit circle lies within the ROC.

$$\boxed{\text{DTFT} = \text{Z Transform} \big\vert_{\vert z \vert = 1}}$$

---

### 🔷 Question B (14 Marks): ROC Properties & Stability

#### Part (a) `[7 Marks]` `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
**Determine the Z Transform and ROC of the signal $x[n] = 3^n u[n] + 4^n u[-n-1]$. Comment on the existence of its DTFT.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Split the signal into right-sided and left-sided parts.**

$$x[n] = \underbrace{3^n u[n]}_{x_1[n]} + \underbrace{4^n u[-n-1]}_{x_2[n]}$$

**Step 2: Z Transform of $x_1[n] = 3^n u[n]$ (causal, right-sided).**

$$X_1(z) = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} 3^n z^{-n} = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \left(\frac{3}{z}\right)^n$$

This geometric series converges if $\left\vert \dfrac{3}{z} \right\vert < 1$, i.e., $\vert z \vert > 3$.

$$X_1(z) = \frac{1}{1 - 3z^{-1}} = \frac{z}{z - 3}, \quad \text{ROC: } \vert z \vert > 3$$

**Step 3: Z Transform of $x_2[n] = 4^n u[-n-1]$ (anti-causal, left-sided).**

$$X_2(z) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{-1} 4^n z^{-n}$$

Let $k = -n$, so $n = -k$. As $n: -\infty \to -1$, we have $k: \infty \to 1$:

$$X_2(z) = \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} 4^{-k} z^{k} = \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} \left(\frac{z}{4}\right)^{k}$$

This converges when $\left\vert \dfrac{z}{4} \right\vert < 1$, i.e., $\vert z \vert < 4$. The closed form is:

$$X_2(z) = \frac{z/4}{1 - z/4} = \frac{-1}{1 - 0.25 z} = \frac{-4}{z - 4}, \quad \text{ROC: } \vert z \vert < 4$$

**Step 4: Combine using linearity.**

$$X(z) = X_1(z) + X_2(z) = \frac{z}{z - 3} - \frac{4}{z - 4}$$

Poles: $z = 3$ and $z = 4$.

**Step 5: Overall ROC = intersection of individual ROCs.**

$$ROC = \{\vert z \vert > 3\} \cap \{\vert z \vert < 4\} = \{3 < \vert z \vert < 4\}$$

This is a **ring-shaped ROC**.

**Step 6: Comment on DTFT existence.**

The DTFT exists if and only if the unit circle ($\vert z \vert = 1$) lies within the ROC. Since the ROC is $3 < \vert z \vert < 4$, the unit circle ($\vert z \vert = 1$) is **outside** this ROC.

**Therefore, the DTFT does NOT exist** for this signal. This is precisely why we need the Z Transform — it can handle signals whose DTFT does not exist.

---

#### Part (b) `[7 Marks]` `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
**Explain the relationship between the Z Transform and the Laplace Transform. How is the Z Transform obtained from the Laplace Transform of a sampled signal?**

**Model Solution:**

**Relationship Overview:**

The Z Transform is the **discrete-time counterpart** of the Laplace Transform in the continuous-time domain. Both are powerful tools that generalize the Fourier Transform by introducing a complex variable.

| Feature | Laplace Transform | Z Transform |
|---|---|---|
| **Domain** | Continuous-time $t$ | Discrete-time $n$ |
| **Variable** | $s = \sigma + j\Omega$ | $z = re^{j\omega}$ |
| **Definition** | $X(s) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) e^{-st} dt$ | $X(z) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] z^{-n}$ |
| **Fourier counterpart** | FT when $\sigma = 0$ | DTFT when $r = 1$ |
| **ROC** | Vertical strip in $s$-plane | Annular region in $z$-plane |

**Derivation: Z Transform from Laplace Transform via Sampling**

**Step 1: Consider an ideal impulse-sampled continuous signal.**

$$x_s(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT) \, \delta(t - nT)$$

where $T$ is the sampling period.

**Step 2: Take the Laplace Transform.**

$$X_s(s) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \left[ \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT) \, \delta(t - nT) \right] e^{-st} dt$$

**Step 3: Exchange the integral and summation, and use the sifting property of the delta function.**

$$X_s(s) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT) e^{-snT}$$

**Step 4: Define the new complex variable $z = e^{sT}$.**

Then $z^{-n} = e^{-snT}$. Substituting:

$$X_s(s)\big\vert_{z=e^{sT}} = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT) z^{-n} = X(z)$$

**Conclusion:** The Z Transform of a discrete sequence $x[n] = x(nT)$ is the Laplace Transform of its impulse-sampled version, with the mapping $z = e^{sT}$.

$$\boxed{z = e^{sT} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad s = \frac{1}{T} \ln(z)}$$

This mapping converts vertical strips in the $s$-plane (ROC of Laplace) into annular rings in the $z$-plane (ROC of Z Transform).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to specify the ROC:** Many students compute $X(z)$ correctly but forget to state the ROC. Since the **ROC is as important as $X(z)$ itself** (a unique signal ↔ unique $X(z)$ + ROC), this loses **at least 2 marks** in a 7-mark question.
>
> 2. **Confusing bilateral and unilateral forms:** Bilateral is for $n$ from $-\infty$ to $\infty$; unilateral is for $n$ from $0$ to $\infty$. Mixing these up is a frequent error.
>
> 3. **Missing the unit circle check for DTFT existence:** Whenever asked "does the DTFT exist?", students often answer "Yes" without verifying that the ROC contains $|z|=1$. Always **explicitly check** $\vert z \vert = 1$ against the ROC.
>
> 4. **Wrong ROC shape for two-sided signals:** Two-sided signals have a **ring** ROC; don't write it as $|z| > r$ or $|z| < r$ alone.
>
> 5. **Skipping the "motivation" in essays:** If the question is *"Discuss the motivation for Z Transform,"* don't just define it. **Start with DTFT limitations** — this is the examiner's expected entry point.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision)

- 🎯 **Z Transform** is defined as $X(z) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] z^{-n}$, with $z = re^{j\omega}$.
- 🎯 The **primary motivation** is that the **DTFT fails** for growing/un-damped signals; the Z Transform handles them via the **ROC**.
- 🎯 The **DTFT is a special case** of the Z Transform on the **unit circle** ($|z|=1$).
- 🎯 The **ROC is a ring** (annulus) in the $z$-plane, bounded by poles.
- 🎯 **Causal signals** → ROC is **outside** the outermost pole: $|z| > r_1$.
- 🎯 **Anti-causal signals** → ROC is **inside** the innermost pole: $|z| < r_2$.
- 🎯 **Two-sided signals** → ROC is a **ring**: $r_1 < |z| < r_2$.
- 🎯 **Finite-duration signals** → ROC is the **entire z-plane** (except possibly $z=0$ or $\infty$).
- 🎯 The **DTFT exists** iff the **unit circle lies in the ROC**.
- 🎯 The **mapping** between Laplace and Z domains: $z = e^{sT}$.
- 🎯 For a **causal LTI system**, stability requires **all poles inside** the unit circle.
- 🎯 The ROC **never contains any pole** of $X(z)$.
- 🎯 **Linearity** holds: $a x_1[n] + b x_2[n] \leftrightarrow a X_1(z) + b X_2(z)$, with ROC being the **intersection** of individual ROCs (excluding poles).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Relationship Between Z Transform and Discrete-Time Fourier Transform
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST416 - SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Relationship Between Z Transform and Discrete-Time Fourier Transform

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of the Z-Transform

The **Z-Transform** of a discrete-time sequence $x[n]$ is a polynomial-like infinite series representation that maps a sequence from the discrete-time domain into the complex $z$-plane. It is defined as:

$$X(z) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] \, z^{-n}$$

where $z$ is a complex variable, typically written in polar form as $z = r \, e^{j\omega}$. The transform converges only over a specific region of the $z$-plane known as the **Region of Convergence (ROC)**.

### 1.2 Formal Definition of the DTFT

The **Discrete-Time Fourier Transform (DTFT)** is the frequency-domain representation of a discrete-time sequence, obtained by evaluating the discrete-time signal's frequency content as a continuous function of the digital frequency $\omega$. It is formally defined as:

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] \, e^{-j\omega n}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The DTFT gives a *continuous* function of the angular frequency $\omega \in [-\pi, \pi]$, while the Z-transform gives a function of the complex variable $z$. The two are intimately related through the concept of evaluating $X(z)$ on a specific contour.

### 1.3 The Core Relationship — The Heart of This Module

The **Discrete-Time Fourier Transform** is simply the **Z-Transform evaluated on the unit circle** in the $z$-plane, provided the unit circle $z = e^{j\omega}$ lies within the **Region of Convergence (ROC)** of $X(z)$.

Mathematically, this central relationship is:

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = \left. X(z) \right|_{z = e^{j\omega}} = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] \, e^{-j\omega n}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (Plain English):**
> Think of the Z-transform as a complete **3D landscape map** of a mountain, where height represents the magnitude of the transform and the ground plane is the complex $z$-plane. The DTFT is simply the **edge-on silhouette** of that mountain as you walk around the unit circle (the path at $r = 1$). You don't get the whole mountain — you only get the *slice* taken along that special circular path. If the mountain (ROC) doesn't include that path, your walk (DTFT) is impossible — the transform won't exist.
>
> In short: **DTFT is a special slice of the Z-transform.**

### 1.4 Why This Relationship Matters in KTU Syllabus

| Aspect | Z-Transform | DTFT |
|---|---|---|
| Domain Variable | Complex $z = re^{j\omega}$ | Real $\omega$ |
| Representation | 2D (full $z$-plane) | 1D (frequency axis) |
| ROC Required | Yes | Implicit (unit circle inside ROC) |
| Information Content | Complete (magnitude + phase + ROC) | Reduced (only $r=1$ slice) |

> [!TIP]
> **Physical Intuition:** If you know $X(z)$ and the ROC includes the unit circle, then you automatically know the DTFT — you just set $r = 1$ in $z = re^{j\omega}$. This is the most-tested relationship in Module 4.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Breaking Down the Relationship Step-by-Step

**Step 1 — Express $z$ in Polar Form**

Any complex number on the unit circle can be written as $z = e^{j\omega}$ with magnitude $r = 1$.

$$z = r \, e^{j\omega} \quad \xrightarrow{r = 1} \quad z = e^{j\omega}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the Z-Transform Definition**

Plug $z = e^{j\omega}$ into the Z-transform expression:

$$X(z) \Big|_{z = e^{j\omega}} = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] \, (e^{j\omega})^{-n} = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] \, e^{-j\omega n}$$

**Step 3 — Recognize the DTFT Form**

The result is exactly the definition of the DTFT:

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = \left. X(z) \right|_{z = e^{j\omega}}$$

### 2.2 Existence Conditions — The Critical "Why"

The DTFT exists **if and only if** the unit circle $|z| = 1$ is entirely contained within the ROC of $X(z)$. This is the single most important condition for the KTU exam.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** A sequence can have a perfectly valid Z-transform (ROC non-empty) but a DTFT that *does not exist*. For example, a causal exponential $x[n] = a^n u[n]$ has ROC $|z| > \vert a \vert$. If $\vert a \vert > 1$, the ROC is *outside* the unit circle and the DTFT **diverges**.

### 2.3 Geometric Interpretation in the Z-Plane

- The **$z$-plane** is a 2D complex plane with real axis $\sigma$ and imaginary axis $j\omega$.
- The **unit circle** is the contour $|z| = 1$, parameterized as $z = e^{j\omega}$ for $\omega \in [-\pi, \pi]$.
- A **pole** inside the unit circle means the system/sequence is **stable** in the BIBO sense — and *vice versa*.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Key Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Z-Transform (Bilateral) | $X(z) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] z^{-n}$ | ROC must be non-empty |
| 2 | Z-Transform (Unilateral) | $X(z) = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} x[n] z^{-n}$ | Used for causal sequences |
| 3 | DTFT | $X(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] e^{-j\omega n}$ | Absolute summability |
| 4 | **Master Relationship** | $X(e^{j\omega}) = X(z) \big\vert_{z = e^{j\omega}}$ | **Unit circle $\subset$ ROC** |
| 5 | Polar form of $z$ | $z = re^{j\omega}, \quad r = \vert z \vert$ | $r = 1$ on unit circle |
| 6 | Magnitude spectrum | $\vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert$ | Real & even for real $x[n]$ |
| 7 | Phase spectrum | $\angle X(e^{j\omega})$ | Odd for real $x[n]$ |
| 8 | Periodicity of DTFT | $X(e^{j(\omega + 2\pi)}) = X(e^{j\omega})$ | Always true |
| 9 | Stability Condition | All poles $\vert p_k \vert < 1$ | Equivalent to ROC containing unit circle |
| 10 | Inverse Relationship | $x[n] = \frac{1}{2\pi j} \oint_{C} X(z) z^{n-1} dz$ | Contour $C$ inside ROC |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Note:** Formula #4 is the *defining* relationship of this module. Questions are frequently asked asking to **derive the DTFT from a given $X(z)$** by substitution.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Digital Filter Design:** IIR and FIR filters are designed via $H(z)$; the magnitude response is $\vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert$.
- **Spectral Analysis:** Audio processing, biomedical signals (ECG, EEG) are analyzed by taking $r=1$ slice of the Z-transform.
- **Communication Systems:** Modulation/demodulation uses DTFT magnitude to analyze signal bandwidth.
- **Control Systems:** Discrete-time controllers are analyzed in the $z$-plane, and frequency response is read on the unit circle.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: From Z-Transform to DTFT

**Given:** Z-Transform of a sequence $x[n]$ is
$$X(z) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] \, z^{-n}$$

**To Prove:** The DTFT of $x[n]$ is given by $X(e^{j\omega})$ when the unit circle lies within the ROC.

---

**Derivation Step 1:** Start with the definition of the Z-transform.

$$X(z) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] \, z^{-n}$$

**Derivation Step 2:** Express the complex variable $z$ in polar form. Any point in the $z$-plane can be written as:

$$z = r \, e^{j\omega}$$

where $r = \vert z \vert$ is the magnitude and $\omega$ is the angle from the positive real axis.

**Derivation Step 3:** Substitute this polar form into the Z-transform.

$$X(z) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] \, (r \, e^{j\omega})^{-n} = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] \, r^{-n} \, e^{-j\omega n}$$

**Derivation Step 4:** Factor out the terms that depend on $r$ and $\omega$ separately.

$$X(z) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \big( x[n] \, r^{-n} \big) \, e^{-j\omega n}$$

**Derivation Step 5:** Recognize this as the DTFT of the modified sequence $x[n] r^{-n}$.

$$X(re^{j\omega}) = \mathcal{F}\big\{x[n] r^{-n}\big\}$$

This is the **general relationship** between Z-transform and DTFT — they are linked by a real exponential weighting $r^{-n}$.

**Derivation Step 6:** Now impose the condition for the DTFT — set $r = 1$ (i.e., evaluate on the unit circle).

$$r = 1 \implies z = e^{j\omega}$$

Substitute into the expression from Step 3:

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] \, (1)^{-n} \, e^{-j\omega n} = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] \, e^{-j\omega n}$$

**Derivation Step 7:** This is the DTFT definition. Q.E.D.

$$\boxed{\,X(e^{j\omega}) = \left. X(z) \right|_{z = e^{j\omega}}\,}$$

---

### 3.2 Worked Example — KTU Standard Problem

**Problem:** Given the Z-transform $X(z) = \dfrac{1}{1 - 0.5 z^{-1}}$ with ROC $\vert z \vert > 0.5$, find the DTFT $X(e^{j\omega})$.

**Solution Step 1:** Verify ROC includes unit circle.

$$\text{ROC: } \vert z \vert > 0.5 \implies \text{Unit circle } \vert z \vert = 1 \text{ is inside ROC} \quad \checkmark$$

So the DTFT exists.

**Solution Step 2:** Substitute $z = e^{j\omega}$ into $X(z)$.

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{1}{1 - 0.5 (e^{j\omega})^{-1}} = \frac{1}{1 - 0.5 e^{-j\omega}}$$

**Solution Step 3:** Rationalize the denominator to obtain magnitude and phase (optional for KTU).

Multiply numerator and denominator by the complex conjugate of the denominator:

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{1}{1 - 0.5 e^{-j\omega}} \cdot \frac{1 - 0.5 e^{j\omega}}{1 - 0.5 e^{j\omega}}$$

**Solution Step 4:** Expand the denominator.

$$1 - 0.5 e^{j\omega} \cdot 1 - 0.5 e^{-j\omega} = 1 - 0.5(e^{j\omega} + e^{-j\omega}) + 0.25$$

Using Euler's identity $e^{j\omega} + e^{-j\omega} = 2\cos\omega$:

$$= 1 - 0.5(2\cos\omega) + 0.25 = 1.25 - \cos\omega$$

**Solution Step 5:** Final DTFT form.

$$\boxed{\,X(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{1 - 0.5 e^{j\omega}}{1.25 - \cos\omega}\,}$$

**Magnitude spectrum:**

$$\vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert = \frac{\sqrt{1.25 - \cos\omega}}{1.25 - \cos\omega} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1.25 - \cos\omega}}$$

**Phase spectrum:**

$$\angle X(e^{j\omega}) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{0.5 \sin\omega}{1 - 0.5 \cos\omega}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Validation:** At $\omega = 0$: $\vert X(e^{j0}) \vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1.25 - 1}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{0.25}} = 2$. This matches the DC gain of the original system $H(1) = \frac{1}{1 - 0.5} = 2$. ✓
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — The Z-Plane and Unit Circle Relationship

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Discrete-Time Sequence x[n]"] --> B["Apply Z-Transform Definition"]
    B --> C["Complex z-Plane Representation X(z)"]
    C --> D{"Does Unit Circle Lie Inside ROC?"}
    D -- "Yes" --> E["DTFT Exists: X(e^jω) = X(z)|z=e^jω"]
    D -- "No" --> F["DTFT Does NOT Exist (Diverges)"]
    E --> G["Frequency Domain: Magnitude and Phase"]
    G --> H["Real-World Use: Filter Design, Spectral Analysis"]
    F --> I["Use only z-Plane Analysis"]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Contour Evaluation in the z-Plane

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["z-Plane Full View"] --> B["ROC Boundary Circles"]
    B --> C["Outer Boundary |z| = R2"]
    B --> D["Inner Boundary |z| = R1"]
    B --> E["Unit Circle |z| = 1"]
    E --> F{"Is 1 Inside [R1, R2]?"}
    F -- "Yes" --> G["DTFT Exists"]
    F -- "No" --> H["DTFT Does Not Exist"]
    G --> I["Sample Along Contour: ω from -π to π"]
    I --> J["Continuous Spectrum X(e^jω)"]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Input x[n]"] --> B["Multiplication by r^-n Weighting"]
    B --> C["Apply DTFT Operation"]
    C --> D["Output: X(re^jω) General Form"]
    D --> E["Special Case: Set r = 1"]
    E --> F["Output: X(e^jω) = DTFT"]
    F --> G["Magnitude Plot |X(e^jω)|"]
    F --> H["Phase Plot angle X(e^jω)"]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** The first diagram shows the decision tree for DTFT existence. The second shows where the unit circle sits relative to the ROC. The third shows the *mathematical* operation: weighting by $r^{-n}$ converts Z-transform into DTFT; setting $r=1$ gives the pure DTFT.

### 4.4 Conceptual Mapping Table

| Z-Transform Concept | DTFT Equivalent | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Complex variable $z$ | $e^{j\omega}$ on unit circle | Frequency evaluation |
| ROC: $r_1 < \vert z \vert < r_2$ | $r_1 < 1 < r_2$ | Existence condition |
| Pole at $z = p$ | Resonance at $\omega$ where $e^{j\omega} = p$ | Frequency peak |
| Zero at $z = q$ | Null at $\omega$ where $e^{j\omega} = q$ | Frequency notch |
| Stable system | All poles inside unit circle | BIBO stability |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**State the relationship between the Z-transform and the Discrete-Time Fourier Transform (DTFT) of a sequence. Under what condition does this relationship hold?**

**Model Answer:**

The Z-transform of a sequence $x[n]$ is defined as:
$$X(z) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] z^{-n}$$

The DTFT is obtained by evaluating the Z-transform on the **unit circle** in the $z$-plane, i.e., by substituting $z = e^{j\omega}$:
$$X(e^{j\omega}) = X(z)\big\vert_{z=e^{j\omega}} = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] e^{-j\omega n}$$

**Condition:** This relationship holds **if and only if the unit circle $\vert z \vert = 1$ lies within the Region of Convergence (ROC) of $X(z)$**. If the unit circle is on or outside the ROC boundary, the DTFT does not exist.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often *forget to state the ROC condition*. Writing only the formula without the existence condition costs **1 mark** in KTU valuation.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**A causal sequence $x[n] = a^n u[n]$ has Z-transform $X(z) = \dfrac{1}{1 - a z^{-1}}$ with ROC $\vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$. For what values of $a$ does the DTFT of $x[n]$ exist?**

**Model Answer:**

The DTFT exists if and only if the unit circle lies within the ROC.

Given: ROC is $\vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$.

Condition for DTFT existence: Unit circle $\vert z \vert = 1$ must be inside ROC.

$$1 > \vert a \vert \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \vert a \vert < 1$$

**Therefore, the DTFT exists only when $\vert a \vert < 1$** (i.e., the sequence is absolutely summable). For such $a$:

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{1}{1 - a e^{-j\omega}}$$

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Expected Pattern]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the relationship between the Z-transform and the Discrete-Time Fourier Transform of a discrete-time sequence $x[n]$. State clearly the conditions under which this relationship is valid.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For the sequence $x[n] = (0.5)^n u[n]$, find:
   1. The Z-transform $X(z)$ and its ROC.
   2. The DTFT $X(e^{j\omega})$ from $X(z)$.
   3. The magnitude spectrum $\vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert$ at $\omega = 0$ and $\omega = \pi$.

**Model Solution for (a):**

*Step 1:* The bilateral Z-transform of $x[n]$ is:
$$X(z) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] z^{-n}$$

*Step 2:* Writing $z$ in polar form $z = re^{j\omega}$:
$$X(re^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] r^{-n} e^{-j\omega n}$$

*Step 3:* This is the DTFT of the weighted sequence $x[n] r^{-n}$.

*Step 4:* For the DTFT of the original sequence, set $r = 1$:
$$X(e^{j\omega}) = \left. X(z) \right|_{z = e^{j\omega}} = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] e^{-j\omega n}$$

*Step 5 — Condition:* This holds **only if the unit circle $\vert z \vert = 1$ lies within the ROC of $X(z)$**.

*Step 6 — Geometric Meaning:* The DTFT is a *slice* of the Z-transform along the unit circle contour in the $z$-plane.

**Model Solution for (b):**

*Part (b.1):* For $x[n] = (0.5)^n u[n]$, the Z-transform is the well-known geometric series:
$$X(z) = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} (0.5)^n z^{-n} = \frac{1}{1 - 0.5 z^{-1}}, \quad \text{ROC: } \vert z \vert > 0.5$$

*Part (b.2):* Since $\vert 0.5 \vert = 0.5 < 1$, the unit circle is inside the ROC. Substitute $z = e^{j\omega}$:
$$X(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{1}{1 - 0.5 e^{-j\omega}}$$

*Part (b.3):*

At $\omega = 0$:
$$X(e^{j0}) = \frac{1}{1 - 0.5} = 2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \vert X(e^{j0}) \vert = 2$$

At $\omega = \pi$ (so $e^{-j\pi} = -1$):
$$X(e^{j\pi}) = \frac{1}{1 - 0.5(-1)} = \frac{1}{1.5} = 0.667 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \vert X(e^{j\pi}) \vert = 0.667$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a clear derivation, show that the DTFT of a sequence $x[n]$ is the Z-transform evaluated on the unit circle. Mention the role of the Region of Convergence (ROC).

**(b) [7 Marks]** Consider the discrete-time system with transfer function:
$$H(z) = \frac{1 - z^{-1}}{1 - 0.8 z^{-1}}$$
   1. Determine the ROC of $H(z)$ assuming causality.
   2. Check whether the system is stable.
   3. Find the frequency response $H(e^{j\omega})$ and compute $\vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert$ at $\omega = 0, \pi/2, \pi$.

**Model Solution for (a):**

*Refer to the detailed derivation in Section 3.1 of these notes. Key points to write:*

1. Start with Z-transform definition.
2. Substitute $z = re^{j\omega}$.
3. Identify the general form $X(re^{j\omega})$ as DTFT of $x[n] r^{-n}$.
4. Apply $r = 1$ to obtain the DTFT.
5. State the ROC condition: unit circle must lie inside ROC.
6. Note the geometric interpretation: DTFT is a 1D slice of the 2D Z-transform surface.

**Model Solution for (b):**

*Part (b.1):* The pole is at $z = 0.8$. For a causal system, the ROC is outside the outermost pole:
$$\text{ROC: } \vert z \vert > 0.8$$

*Part (b.2):* The pole at $0.8$ has magnitude $< 1$, so it lies *inside* the unit circle. The unit circle $\vert z \vert = 1$ is therefore inside the ROC. **The system is stable.**

*Part (b.3):* Substitute $z = e^{j\omega}$:
$$H(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{1 - e^{-j\omega}}{1 - 0.8 e^{-j\omega}}$$

At $\omega = 0$: $e^{-j0} = 1$
$$H(e^{j0}) = \frac{1 - 1}{1 - 0.8} = \frac{0}{0.2} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \vert H(e^{j0}) \vert = 0$$

(This is expected — the numerator $(1 - z^{-1})$ is a *differentiator* that blocks DC.)

At $\omega = \pi/2$: $e^{-j\pi/2} = -j$
$$H(e^{j\pi/2}) = \frac{1 - (-j)}{1 - 0.8(-j)} = \frac{1 + j}{1 + 0.8j}$$
$$\vert H(e^{j\pi/2}) \vert = \frac{\sqrt{1^2 + 1^2}}{\sqrt{1^2 + 0.8^2}} = \frac{\sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{1.64}} = \frac{1.414}{1.281} \approx 1.104$$

At $\omega = \pi$: $e^{-j\pi} = -1$
$$H(e^{j\pi}) = \frac{1 - (-1)}{1 - 0.8(-1)} = \frac{2}{1.8} = 1.111 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \vert H(e^{j\pi}) \vert = 1.111$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to verify ROC includes the unit circle** before writing $H(e^{j\omega})$ — costs 2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing the bilinear transform** $s \to z$ with the unit-circle substitution $z = e^{j\omega}$ — they are *different* operations.
> 3. **Not simplifying the DTFT** to magnitude/phase form when the question asks for the spectrum — costs 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Missing the stability conclusion** explicitly tied to pole location — examiners look for the *verbal* statement, not just the calculation.
> 5. **Sign errors** when computing $e^{-j\pi} = -1$ or $e^{-j\pi/2} = -j$ — common in numerical evaluation.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Z-Transform to DTFT Relationship**

- [x] **Master Formula:** $X(e^{j\omega}) = X(z)\big\vert_{z = e^{j\omega}}$ — *memorize verbatim.*
- [x] **Unit Circle:** Defined by $\vert z \vert = 1$, parameterized as $z = e^{j\omega}$ for $\omega \in [-\pi, \pi]$.
- [x] **Existence Condition:** The unit circle **must lie inside the ROC** of $X(z)$ for the DTFT to exist.
- [x] **Stability Equivalence:** A causal LTI system is BIBO stable **iff all its poles lie inside the unit circle** (iff the ROC includes the unit circle).
- [x] **Generalized Form:** $X(re^{j\omega})$ is the DTFT of $x[n] r^{-n}$ — reduces to ordinary DTFT at $r = 1$.
- [x] **Periodicity:** $X(e^{j\omega})$ is always **periodic with period $2\pi$** in $\omega$.
- [x] **Poles & Zeros on Unit Circle:** If a pole lies *exactly* on $\vert z \vert = 1$, the DTFT **diverges** at that frequency.
- [x] **Causal Exponential:** $x[n] = a^n u[n]$ has DTFT only if $\vert a \vert < 1$.
- [x] **Anti-Causal Exponential:** $x[n] = -a^n u[-n-1]$ has DTFT only if $\vert a \vert > 1$ (ROC $\vert z \vert < \vert a \vert$ must include unit circle).
- [x] **Common Sequences' DTFTs to Know:**
  - $\delta[n] \leftrightarrow 1$ (all frequencies)
  - $u[n] \leftrightarrow \frac{1}{1 - e^{-j\omega}} + \pi \sum_k \delta(\omega - 2\pi k)$ (impulse train at multiples of $2\pi$)
  - $a^n u[n] \leftrightarrow \frac{1}{1 - a e^{-j\omega}}$ for $\vert a \vert < 1$
- [x] **Inverse DTFT:** $x[n] = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} X(e^{j\omega}) e^{j\omega n} d\omega$
- [x] **Inverse Z-Transform:** $x[n] = \frac{1}{2\pi j} \oint_C X(z) z^{n-1} dz$ (contour $C$ inside ROC)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Memory Trick:** "**DTFT lives on the rim, Z-transform lives on the plate.**" The DTFT is the *rim* (unit circle), and the Z-transform is the entire *plate* (full $z$-plane).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Region of Convergence for the Z Transform. Properties of Z Transform - Translation (Time Shifting)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST416 - SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Region of Convergence for the Z Transform. Properties of Z Transform - Translation (Time Shifting)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Z-Transform — The Foundation

The **Z-Transform** of a discrete-time signal $x[n]$ is a powerful mathematical tool that converts a sequence in the **time domain** ($n$) into a function in the **complex frequency domain** ($z$). It is the discrete-time equivalent of the Laplace Transform and is formally defined as:

$$X(z) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] \, z^{-n}$$

where $z$ is a complex variable expressed in polar form as $z = r e^{j\omega}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bilateral Z-Transform** is defined for all $n$ from $-\infty$ to $\infty$. The **Unilateral Z-Transform** is defined only for $n \ge 0$, i.e., $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} x[n] z^{-n}$. KTU 2024 Scheme places strong emphasis on the **bilateral** form for ROC analysis.

## 1.2 Region of Convergence (ROC) — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> The **Region of Convergence (ROC)** of $X(z)$ is the set of all values of the complex variable $z$ (in the $z$-plane) for which the Z-transform summation **converges** to a finite value. In other words, it is the domain in which:
> $$\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n] \, z^{-n} \vert < \infty$$

The ROC is **not** an arbitrary region — it is determined entirely by the **location and nature of the poles** of $X(z)$, and it is always bounded by them.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Valid Listening Zone of a Radio"

Imagine the Z-plane is a vast landscape, and $z$ is a radio frequency. **Not every frequency will let you "hear" the signal** $x[n]$ — only a specific range of frequencies produces a clean, finite response. This valid range is the **ROC**.

- If $x[n]$ grows as $n \to \infty$ (e.g., $x[n] = 2^n u[n]$), the transform diverges for small $\vert z \vert$. We need **large** $\vert z \vert$ to suppress the growth → ROC is the **exterior** of a circle.
- If $x[n]$ grows as $n \to -\infty$ (e.g., $x[n] = 2^n u[-n-1]$), the transform diverges for large $\vert z \vert$. We need **small** $\vert z \vert$ to suppress the growth → ROC is the **interior** of a circle.
- If the signal is bounded in **both** directions, the ROC is an **annular ring** between two circles.

## 1.4 Why is ROC Critical in KTU Examinations?

Two different signals can produce the **same algebraic expression** for $X(z)$ but differ in their ROC. Hence, $X(z)$ alone is **incomplete** without its ROC. For example:

- $a^n u[n] \;\longleftrightarrow\; \dfrac{1}{1-az^{-1}}, \quad \text{ROC: } \vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$
- $-a^n u[-n-1] \;\longleftrightarrow\; \dfrac{1}{1-az^{-1}}, \quad \text{ROC: } \vert z \vert < \vert a \vert$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaway:** The same rational function $X(z) = \frac{1}{1-az^{-1}}$ represents **two different signals** depending on whether the ROC is the **exterior** or **interior** of the pole circle. KTU examiners routinely test this concept.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Conditions for Convergence — The Mathematical Setup

For $X(z)$ to converge, the series $\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] z^{-n}$ must be **absolutely summable**. Splitting the sum into causal ($n \ge 0$) and anti-causal ($n < 0$) parts:

$$\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} x[n] z^{-n} \; + \; \sum_{n=-\infty}^{-1} x[n] z^{-n} \; < \; \infty$$

The first sum requires $\vert z \vert$ to be **large enough**; the second sum requires $\vert z \vert$ to be **small enough**. The intersection (if non-empty) is the ROC.

## 2.2 The Eleven Golden Properties of ROC (KTU High-Yield)

| # | Property | Engineering Interpretation |
|---|----------|----------------------------|
| 1 | The ROC is a **ring** (or disk) in the $z$-plane: $R_1 < \vert z \vert < R_2$ | Bounded by the magnitudes of poles |
| 2 | The ROC **does not contain any pole** of $X(z)$ | Poles cause divergence |
| 3 | The ROC is **bounded by poles** (circles passing through poles) | Pole location dictates boundary |
| 4 | For a **right-sided** sequence ($x[n]=0$ for $n<N_1$), ROC is **outside the outermost pole**: $\vert z \vert > R_{\max}$ | Causal signals |
| 5 | For a **left-sided** sequence ($x[n]=0$ for $n>N_2$), ROC is **inside the innermost pole**: $\vert z \vert < R_{\min}$ | Anti-causal signals |
| 6 | For a **two-sided** (finite-duration or non-causal) sequence, ROC is an **annular ring**: $R_1 < \vert z \vert < R_2$ | Bilateral signals |
| 7 | For a **finite-duration** sequence, ROC is the **entire z-plane** except possibly $z=0$ or $z=\infty$ | Truncated signals |
| 8 | If $x[n]$ is **right-sided** and the unit circle is in ROC, the system is **causal and stable** | Stability criterion |
| 9 | The ROC is a **connected region** (no isolated islands) | Continuity of analyticity |
| 10 | The ROC of a **linear combination** of signals is at least the **intersection** of individual ROCs | LTI systems |
| 11 | Adding a pole to $X(z)$ **contracts** the ROC; adding a zero has **no effect** on ROC | Pole-zero analysis |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Causality + Stability Criterion:** A discrete-time LTI system is **causal and stable** if and only if **all poles lie strictly inside the unit circle** ($\vert z \vert = 1$) and the ROC includes the unit circle. This is the **single most-tested ROC concept** in KTU board exams.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Z-Transform Pairs & Properties

### Table A: Standard Z-Transform Pairs

| Signal $x[n]$ | Z-Transform $X(z)$ | ROC |
|---------------|---------------------|-----|
| $\delta[n]$ | $1$ | All $z$ |
| $u[n]$ | $\dfrac{1}{1-z^{-1}}$ | $\vert z \vert > 1$ |
| $a^n u[n]$ | $\dfrac{1}{1-a z^{-1}}$ | $\vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$ |
| $-a^n u[-n-1]$ | $\dfrac{1}{1-a z^{-1}}$ | $\vert z \vert < \vert a \vert$ |
| $n a^n u[n]$ | $\dfrac{a z^{-1}}{(1-a z^{-1})^2}$ | $\vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$ |
| $\cos(\omega_0 n) u[n]$ | $\dfrac{1 - z^{-1}\cos\omega_0}{1 - 2z^{-1}\cos\omega_0 + z^{-2}}$ | $\vert z \vert > 1$ |
| $\sin(\omega_0 n) u[n]$ | $\dfrac{z^{-1}\sin\omega_0}{1 - 2z^{-1}\cos\omega_0 + z^{-2}}$ | $\vert z \vert > 1$ |

### Table B: Z-Transform Property Reference

| Property | Time Domain | Z-Domain | ROC Effect |
|----------|-------------|----------|------------|
| Linearity | $a x_1[n] + b x_2[n]$ | $a X_1(z) + b X_2(z)$ | $\text{ROC} \supseteq \text{ROC}_1 \cap \text{ROC}_2$ |
| **Time Shifting** | $x[n - n_0]$ | $z^{-n_0} X(z)$ | Same ROC; may add/remove $z=0$ or $z=\infty$ |
| Scaling in $z$ | $a^n x[n]$ | $X(a^{-1} z)$ | $\vert a \vert R_1 < \vert z \vert < \vert a \vert R_2$ |
| Time Reversal | $x[-n]$ | $X(z^{-1})$ | $1/R_2 < \vert z \vert < 1/R_1$ |
| Differentiation | $n x[n]$ | $-z \dfrac{dX(z)}{dz}$ | Same ROC |
| Convolution | $x_1[n] * x_2[n]$ | $X_1(z) X_2(z)$ | $\text{ROC} \supseteq \text{ROC}_1 \cap \text{ROC}_2$ |
| Initial Value | $x[0] = \lim_{z \to \infty} X(z)$ | — | Requires causal $x[n]$ |

> [!NOTE]
> All absolute value signs use LaTeX `\vert` to maintain markdown table integrity. Read `\vert z \vert` as $\vert z \vert$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — ROC of a Right-Sided Exponential Signal

**Problem:** Find the Z-transform and ROC of $x[n] = a^n u[n]$, where $u[n]$ is the unit step.

**Step 1 — Write the Definition:**

$$X(z) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} a^n u[n] \, z^{-n}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Unit Step (limits change from $0$ to $\infty$):**

Since $u[n] = 0$ for $n < 0$ and $u[n] = 1$ for $n \ge 0$:

$$X(z) = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} a^n \, z^{-n} = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} (a z^{-1})^n$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Infinite Geometric Series Formula:**

The series $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} r^n$ converges if and only if $\vert r \vert < 1$. Therefore:

$$X(z) = \frac{1}{1 - a z^{-1}}, \quad \text{provided } \vert a z^{-1} \vert < 1$$

**Step 4 — Solve the Inequality for $\vert z \vert$:**

$$\vert a z^{-1} \vert < 1 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \vert a \vert \cdot \vert z \vert^{-1} < 1 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$$

**Step 5 — State the Final Result:**

$$\boxed{X(z) = \frac{1}{1 - a z^{-1}} = \frac{z}{z - a}, \quad \text{ROC: } \vert z \vert > \vert a \vert}$$

The pole is at $z = a$, and the ROC is the **exterior** of a circle of radius $\vert a \vert$ — consistent with the **right-sided** nature of the signal.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — ROC of a Left-Sided Exponential Signal

**Problem:** Find the Z-transform and ROC of $x[n] = -a^n u[-n-1]$.

**Step 1 — Recall the Unit Step Reflection Property:**

$u[-n-1] = 1$ for $n \le -1$ and $0$ for $n \ge 0$. Hence:

$$X(z) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{-1} -a^n \, z^{-n}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $m = -n$ (so $m$ runs from $1$ to $\infty$):**

$$X(z) = \sum_{m=1}^{\infty} -a^{-m} \, z^{m} = -\sum_{m=1}^{\infty} (a^{-1} z)^m$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Geometric Series Formula (starting at $m=1$):**

$$X(z) = -\frac{a^{-1} z}{1 - a^{-1} z}, \quad \text{provided } \vert a^{-1} z \vert < 1$$

**Step 4 — Solve the Convergence Condition:**

$$\vert a^{-1} z \vert < 1 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \vert z \vert < \vert a \vert$$

**Step 5 — Simplify Algebraically:**

$$\begin{aligned}
X(z) &= -\frac{z/a}{1 - z/a} = -\frac{z}{a - z} = \frac{z}{z - a} = \frac{1}{1 - a z^{-1}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — State the Final Result:**

$$\boxed{X(z) = \frac{1}{1 - a z^{-1}} = \frac{z}{z - a}, \quad \text{ROC: } \vert z \vert < \vert a \vert}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Punchline:** $a^n u[n]$ and $-a^n u[-n-1]$ have the **same** $X(z)$ expression but **different ROCs**! The ROC uniquely identifies the time-domain signal. This duality is a **board-favorite** question.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Proof of the Time Shifting (Translation) Property

**Statement:** If $x[n] \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{Z}} X(z)$ with ROC $= R_1 < \vert z \vert < R_2$, then:

$$x[n - n_0] \;\xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{Z}}\; z^{-n_0} X(z)$$

with the same ROC (except for possible addition/removal of $z=0$ or $z=\infty$).

**Step 1 — Start with the Z-Transform of the Shifted Signal:**

$$Z\{x[n - n_0]\} = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n - n_0] \, z^{-n}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $m = n - n_0$ (so $n = m + n_0$):**

When $n \to -\infty$, $m \to -\infty$; when $n \to \infty$, $m \to \infty$:

$$Z\{x[n - n_0]\} = \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} x[m] \, z^{-(m + n_0)}$$

**Step 3 — Factor Out the Term Independent of Summation Index:**

$$Z\{x[n - n_0]\} = z^{-n_0} \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} x[m] \, z^{-m}$$

**Step 4 — Recognize the Sum as $X(z)$:**

$$Z\{x[n - n_0]\} = z^{-n_0} \cdot X(z)$$

**Step 5 — Conclude the Proof:**

$$\begin{aligned}
Z\{x[n - n_0]\} = z^{-n_0} X(z) \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Analyze the Effect on ROC:**

Since the summation is identical to the original (only the index variable changed), the convergence condition is unchanged:

- **Case $n_0 > 0$ (Right Shift / Delay):** Multiplying by $z^{-n_0}$ adds a pole of order $n_0$ at $z=0$. Hence, $z=0$ is **removed** from the ROC.
- **Case $n_0 < 0$ (Left Shift / Advance):** Multiplying by $z^{\vert n_0 \vert}$ adds poles at $z=\infty$. Hence, $z=\infty$ is **removed** from the ROC.
- **Case $n_0 = 0$:** No change in ROC.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example — Applying Time Shifting

**Problem:** Find the Z-transform and ROC of $x[n] = u[n-3]$ (a unit step delayed by 3 samples).

**Step 1 — Identify the Known Pair:**

From the standard table: $u[n] \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{Z}} \dfrac{1}{1 - z^{-1}}$ with ROC $\vert z \vert > 1$.

**Step 2 — Apply the Time Shifting Property with $n_0 = 3$:**

$$Z\{u[n-3]\} = z^{-3} \cdot \frac{1}{1 - z^{-1}} = \frac{z^{-3}}{1 - z^{-1}}$$

**Step 3 — Determine the ROC:**

Original ROC: $\vert z \vert > 1$. Since $n_0 = 3 > 0$ (right shift), the point $z=0$ is removed. The ROC becomes:

$$\text{ROC: } \vert z \vert > 1, \quad z \neq 0$$

Equivalently, the ROC is $\vert z \vert > 1$ with the exclusion of the origin.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example — Two-Sided Signal ROC

**Problem:** Find the Z-transform and ROC of $x[n] = (1/2)^n u[n] + (1/3)^n u[-n-1]$.

**Step 1 — Transform the Causal Part:**

$$Z\{(1/2)^n u[n]\} = \frac{1}{1 - (1/2) z^{-1}}, \quad \text{ROC}_1: \vert z \vert > 1/2$$

**Step 2 — Transform the Anti-Causal Part:**

$$Z\{(1/3)^n u[-n-1]\} = \frac{-1}{1 - (1/3) z^{-1}}, \quad \text{ROC}_2: \vert z \vert < 1/3$$

Wait — for a left-sided signal $-a^n u[-n-1]$, the Z-transform is $\dfrac{1}{1-az^{-1}}$. Applying directly with $a = 1/3$:

$$Z\{(1/3)^n u[-n-1]\} = \frac{1}{1 - (1/3) z^{-1}}, \quad \text{ROC}_2: \vert z \vert < 1/3$$

**Step 3 — Combine by Linearity (Pole Expansion):**

$$X(z) = \frac{1}{1 - (1/2) z^{-1}} - \frac{1}{1 - (1/3) z^{-1}}$$

**Step 4 — Combine over a Common Denominator:**

$$\begin{aligned}
X(z) &= \frac{[1 - (1/3) z^{-1}] - [1 - (1/2) z^{-1}]}{[1 - (1/2) z^{-1}][1 - (1/3) z^{-1}]} \\
&= \frac{-(1/3) z^{-1} + (1/2) z^{-1}}{[1 - (1/2) z^{-1}][1 - (1/3) z^{-1}]} \\
&= \frac{(1/6) z^{-1}}{[1 - (1/2) z^{-1}][1 - (1/3) z^{-1}]}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Determine the Combined ROC (Intersection):**

$$\text{ROC} = \text{ROC}_1 \cap \text{ROC}_2 = \{\vert z \vert > 1/2\} \cap \{\vert z \vert < 1/3\}$$

Since $1/3 < 1/2$, this intersection is **empty**! The signal $x[n]$ **does not have a Z-transform** (it does not converge anywhere).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Not every signal has a Z-transform. The ROC must be **non-empty** for the Z-transform to exist. This is analogous to the convergence of a power series in calculus.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 ROC Topologies in the Z-Plane

The following Mermaid flowchart maps the decision logic for determining the ROC topology based on signal characteristics.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Given xn] --> B{Is xn finite-duration?}
    B -- Yes --> C[ROC: Entire z-plane except possibly z=0 or z=infinity]
    B -- No --> D{Is xn right-sided only?}
    D -- Yes --> E[ROC: Exterior of outermost pole, vert z vert greater than Rmax]
    D -- No --> F{Is xn left-sided only?}
    F -- Yes --> G[ROC: Interior of innermost pole, vert z vert less than Rmin]
    F -- No --> H[ROC: Annular ring R1 less than vert z vert less than R2]
    E --> I[Check: Unit circle in ROC implies Stability]
    G --> J[Check: System is Anti-causal if ROC excludes infinity]
    H --> K[Check: Two-sided ROC implies Bilateral Signal]
    C --> L[Result: Tabulate Xz and ROC]
    I --> L
    J --> L
    K --> L
```

## 4.2 Time Shifting Property — Operational Flow

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates how the time-shifting operator transforms a signal and its associated ROC in the analysis pipeline.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Signal xn] --> B[Apply Time Shift n0]
    B --> C[Compute Z-Transform Definition]
    C --> D[Substitute m equals n minus n0]
    D --> E[Factor Out z to power minus n0]
    E --> F[Identify Original Xz]
    F --> G[Output: z to power minus n0 times Xz]
    G --> H{n0 greater than 0}
    H -- Yes --> I[ROC loses z equals 0]
    H -- No --> J{n0 less than 0}
    J -- Yes --> K[ROC loses z equals infinity]
    J -- No --> L[ROC Unchanged]
    I --> M[Final Result with Modified ROC]
    K --> M
    L --> M
```

## 4.3 Pole-Zero Plot vs ROC — Conceptual Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Construct Xz in Rational Form] --> B[Factor Numerator and Denominator]
    B --> C[Identify Zeros: Roots of Numerator]
    B --> D[Identify Poles: Roots of Denominator]
    D --> E{Determine Signal Duration}
    E -- Right-sided --> F[Shade Region EXTERIOR to outermost pole]
    E -- Left-sided --> G[Shade Region INTERIOR to innermost pole]
    E -- Two-sided --> H[Shade ANNULAR RING between poles]
    F --> I[Pole-Zero Diagram Complete]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J{Is unit circle vert z vert equals 1 in shaded region?}
    J -- Yes --> K[System is STABLE]
    J -- No --> L[System is UNSTABLE]
```

## 4.4 Causal vs Anti-Causal ROC Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Right-Sided
        A1[xn equals a to power n un] --> A2[ROC: vert z vert greater than vert a vert]
        A2 --> A3[Exterior of circle radius vert a vert]
    end
    subgraph Left-Sided
        B1[xn equals minus a to power n u of minus n minus 1] --> B2[ROC: vert z vert less than vert a vert]
        B2 --> B3[Interior of circle radius vert a vert]
    end
    subgraph Two-Sided
        C1[xn with poles at a and b] --> C2[ROC: vert b vert less than vert z vert less than vert a vert]
        C2 --> C3[Annular Ring between two circles]
    end
```
