# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define Region of Convergence (ROC) of the Z-transform. State any four properties of ROC.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** The Region of Convergence (ROC) of $X(z)$ is the set of all complex values of $z$ for which the Z-transform summation $\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] z^{-n}$ converges to a finite value.

**Four Properties of ROC:**

1. The ROC is a ring-shaped region in the $z$-plane, bounded by circles passing through the poles of $X(z)$.
2. The ROC does **not contain any pole** of $X(z)$.
3. For a **right-sided** sequence, the ROC is the region **outside the outermost pole**.
4. For a **left-sided** sequence, the ROC is the region **inside the innermost pole**.
5. (Bonus) The ROC of a linear combination of signals contains the **intersection** of the individual ROCs.

**Word Count Check:** ~80 words → Suitable for a 3-mark answer (target: 60–100 words).

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**State the time-shifting property of the Z-transform. What is its effect on the ROC?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Statement:** If $x[n] \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{Z}} X(z)$ with ROC $R_1 < \vert z \vert < R_2$, then:
> $$x[n - n_0] \;\xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{Z}}\; z^{-n_0} X(z)$$
> **Effect on ROC:** The ROC remains the **same** as that of $X(z)$, with two exceptions:
> - If $n_0 > 0$ (right shift / delay), the point $z = 0$ is **removed** from the ROC.
> - If $n_0 < 0$ (left shift / advance), the point $z = \infty$ is **removed** from the ROC.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]`

**(a)** Determine the Z-transform and ROC of the signal:

$$x[n] = 3^n u[n] - 2^n u[n]$$

**(b)** State and prove the time-shifting property of the Z-transform. Discuss its effect on the ROC with a suitable example.

---

#### Solution to Question A(a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Transform the First Term:**

Using the standard pair $a^n u[n] \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{Z}} \dfrac{1}{1 - a z^{-1}}$, $\text{ROC: } \vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$:

$$Z\{3^n u[n]\} = \frac{1}{1 - 3 z^{-1}}, \quad \text{ROC}_1: \vert z \vert > 3$$

**Step 2 — Transform the Second Term:**

$$Z\{2^n u[n]\} = \frac{1}{1 - 2 z^{-1}}, \quad \text{ROC}_2: \vert z \vert > 2$$

**Step 3 — Apply Linearity:**

$$X(z) = \frac{1}{1 - 3 z^{-1}} - \frac{1}{1 - 2 z^{-1}}$$

**Step 4 — Common Denominator:**

$$\begin{aligned}
X(z) &= \frac{(1 - 2 z^{-1}) - (1 - 3 z^{-1})}{(1 - 3 z^{-1})(1 - 2 z^{-1})} \\
&= \frac{z^{-1}}{(1 - 3 z^{-1})(1 - 2 z^{-1})}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — ROC Calculation (Intersection):**

$$\text{ROC} = \text{ROC}_1 \cap \text{ROC}_2 = \{\vert z \vert > 3\} \cap \{\vert z \vert > 2\} = \vert z \vert > 3$$

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{X(z) = \frac{z^{-1}}{(1 - 3z^{-1})(1 - 2z^{-1})}, \quad \text{ROC: } \vert z \vert > 3}$$

**Mark Distribution:** Definition of Z-transform (1 mark) + Step-by-step computation (4 marks) + ROC determination (2 marks).

---

#### Solution to Question A(b) — 7 Marks

**Statement:** As derived in Section 3.3 above.

**Proof (Concise Version for 7 Marks):**

Given $x[n] \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{Z}} X(z) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] z^{-n}$, we need to find $Z\{x[n - n_0]\}$.

$$Z\{x[n - n_0]\} = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n - n_0] z^{-n}$$

Let $m = n - n_0 \Rightarrow n = m + n_0$:

$$Z\{x[n - n_0]\} = \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} x[m] z^{-(m + n_0)} = z^{-n_0} \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} x[m] z^{-m} = z^{-n_0} X(z)$$

**Effect on ROC:** The summation bounds and the structure of the series are unchanged. Hence the ROC is identical, with the following edge cases:
- $n_0 > 0 \Rightarrow$ ROC loses $z = 0$
- $n_0 < 0 \Rightarrow$ ROC loses $z = \infty$

**Example:** $x[n] = u[n]$ has $X(z) = \frac{1}{1 - z^{-1}}$ with ROC $\vert z \vert > 1$. For $x[n-3] = u[n-3]$, the Z-transform is $\frac{z^{-3}}{1 - z^{-1}}$ with ROC $\vert z \vert > 1$, $z \neq 0$.

**Mark Distribution:** Statement (1) + Proof (4) + ROC discussion with example (2).

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model]`

**(a)** Find the Z-transform and ROC of the two-sided signal:

$$x[n] = \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^n u[n] + \left(\frac{1}{3}\right)^n u[-n-1]$$

**(b)** Using the time-shifting property, determine the Z-transform and ROC of $x[n] = u[n-5] - u[n-10]$.

---

#### Solution to Question B(a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Transform the Causal Part:**

$$Z\left\{\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^n u[n]\right\} = \frac{1}{1 - \frac{1}{2} z^{-1}}, \quad \text{ROC}_1: \vert z \vert > \frac{1}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Transform the Anti-Causal Part:**

Using the pair $-a^n u[-n-1] \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{Z}} \frac{1}{1 - a z^{-1}}$, with $a = 1/3$:

$$Z\left\{-\left(\frac{1}{3}\right)^n u[-n-1]\right\} = \frac{1}{1 - \frac{1}{3} z^{-1}}, \quad \text{ROC}_2: \vert z \vert < \frac{1}{3}$$

So:

$$Z\left\{\left(\frac{1}{3}\right)^n u[-n-1]\right\} = -\frac{1}{1 - \frac{1}{3} z^{-1}}, \quad \text{ROC}_2: \vert z \vert < \frac{1}{3}$$

**Step 3 — Combine by Linearity:**

$$X(z) = \frac{1}{1 - \frac{1}{2} z^{-1}} - \frac{1}{1 - \frac{1}{3} z^{-1}}$$

**Step 4 — Common Denominator:**

$$\begin{aligned}
X(z) &= \frac{\left(1 - \frac{1}{3} z^{-1}\right) - \left(1 - \frac{1}{2} z^{-1}\right)}{\left(1 - \frac{1}{2} z^{-1}\right)\left(1 - \frac{1}{3} z^{-1}\right)} \\
&= \frac{\left(-\frac{1}{3} + \frac{1}{2}\right) z^{-1}}{\left(1 - \frac{1}{2} z^{-1}\right)\left(1 - \frac{1}{3} z^{-1}\right)} \\
&= \frac{\frac{1}{6} z^{-1}}{\left(1 - \frac{1}{2} z^{-1}\right)\left(1 - \frac{1}{3} z^{-1}\right)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — ROC (Intersection):**

$$\text{ROC} = \{\vert z \vert > 1/2\} \cap \{\vert z \vert < 1/3\} = \text{Empty Set (No ROC!)}$$

**Step 6 — Conclusion:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Z-transform of $x[n] = (1/2)^n u[n] + (1/3)^n u[-n-1]$ **does not exist**, because the two individual ROCs do not overlap. The signal grows faster in the anti-causal direction than the causal direction can compensate.

> **Note for examiner insight:** This problem is intentionally designed to test whether students blindly compute $X(z)$ without verifying ROC non-emptiness. The answer hinges on recognizing the **empty intersection**.

---

#### Solution to Question B(b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Recognize the Signal Structure:**

The signal $x[n] = u[n-5] - u[n-10]$ is a **rectangular pulse** of unit amplitude, extending from $n = 5$ to $n = 9$ (inclusive).

**Step 2 — Apply Time Shifting to the Unit Step:**

From the standard pair $u[n] \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{Z}} \frac{1}{1 - z^{-1}}$, $\text{ROC: } \vert z \vert > 1$:

$$Z\{u[n-5]\} = \frac{z^{-5}}{1 - z^{-1}}, \quad \text{ROC: } \vert z \vert > 1, \; z \neq 0$$

$$Z\{u[n-10]\} = \frac{z^{-10}}{1 - z^{-1}}, \quad \text{ROC: } \vert z \vert > 1, \; z \neq 0$$

**Step 3 — Apply Linearity:**

$$X(z) = \frac{z^{-5}}{1 - z^{-1}} - \frac{z^{-10}}{1 - z^{-1}} = \frac{z^{-5} - z^{-10}}{1 - z^{-1}}$$

**Step 4 — Simplify the ROC:**

Since both terms share the same ROC ($\vert z \vert > 1$, $z \neq 0$), the combined ROC is:

$$\text{ROC: } \vert z \vert > 1, \; z \neq 0$$

**Step 5 — Verification via Direct Computation:**

Directly, $x[n] = 1$ for $5 \le n \le 9$, else $0$:

$$X(z) = \sum_{n=5}^{9} z^{-n} = z^{-5} + z^{-6} + z^{-7} + z^{-8} + z^{-9} = z^{-5}(1 + z^{-1} + z^{-2} + z^{-3} + z^{-4})$$

This is a finite-duration sequence, so the ROC is the entire $z$-plane except $z = 0$ and $z = \infty$ (the latter is excluded since the sequence does not extend to $-\infty$). This matches the time-shifting result.

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{X(z) = \frac{z^{-5} - z^{-10}}{1 - z^{-1}}, \quad \text{ROC: } \vert z \vert > 0 \; (\text{i.e., } z \neq 0, \infty)}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting to specify ROC** — In KTU board exams, providing $X(z)$ **without** its ROC leads to a deduction of **2 to 3 marks** out of 7. Always end with "ROC: ___".
> 2. **Confusing the sign of $n_0$** — A right shift (delay, $n_0 > 0$) gives $z^{-n_0}$; a left shift (advance, $n_0 < 0$) gives $z^{|n_0|}$. Many students invert this and lose 1 mark.
> 3. **Empty ROC trap** — For two-sided signals, always check that the intersection of ROCs is **non-empty**. Computing $X(z)$ algebraically is futile if the ROC is empty.
> 4. **Forgetting the minus sign in the anti-causal pair** — The Z-transform of $a^n u[-n-1]$ is **NOT** $\frac{1}{1 - a z^{-1}}$; it is $\frac{-1}{1 - a z^{-1}}$. This is the **most common** sign error in KTU papers.
> 5. **Mixing $z$ and $z^{-1}$ forms** — Some texts write poles at $z = a$, others at $z = 1/a$. Be consistent; KTU board convention uses the **$z^{-1}$ polynomial form** for ROC analysis.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4 (ROC & Time Shifting)**

### A. Core Concepts
- ☐ **ROC Definition** — Set of $z$ values for which $X(z) = \sum x[n] z^{-n}$ converges.
- ☐ **ROC is a ring** in the $z$-plane, bounded by poles, never containing a pole.
- ☐ **Right-sided signal** → ROC is **exterior** of outermost pole ($\vert z \vert > R_{\max}$).
- ☐ **Left-sided signal** → ROC is **interior** of innermost pole ($\vert z \vert < R_{\min}$).
- ☐ **Two-sided signal** → ROC is an **annular ring** ($R_1 < \vert z \vert < R_2$).
- ☐ **Finite-duration signal** → ROC is the **entire $z$-plane** (except possibly $z=0$ or $z=\infty$).

### B. Stability Criterion
- ☐ **Causal + Stable** ⟺ All poles strictly **inside** the unit circle **AND** ROC includes $\vert z \vert = 1$.
- ☐ **Anti-causal + Stable** ⟺ All poles strictly **outside** the unit circle **AND** ROC includes $\vert z \vert = 1$.
- ☐ **Two-sided + Stable** ⟺ ROC **contains** the unit circle (i.e., $R_1 < 1 < R_2$).

### C. Time Shifting Property — Quick Recall
- ☐ $x[n - n_0] \;\xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{Z}}\; z^{-n_0} X(z)$
- ☐ **Right shift** ($n_0 > 0$) → ROC **loses** $z = 0$
- ☐ **Left shift** ($n_0 < 0$) → ROC **loses** $z = \infty$
- ☐ Standard trick: $u[n - k] = z^{-k}/(1 - z^{-1})$, $\text{ROC: } \vert z \vert > 1$, $z \neq 0$.

### D. Standard Pairs to Memorize
- ☐ $\delta[n] \leftrightarrow 1$, ROC: all $z$
- ☐ $u[n] \leftrightarrow \frac{1}{1 - z^{-1}}$, ROC: $\vert z \vert > 1$
- ☐ $a^n u[n] \leftrightarrow \frac{1}{1 - a z^{-1}}$, ROC: $\vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$
- ☐ $-a^n u[-n-1] \leftrightarrow \frac{1}{1 - a z^{-1}}$, ROC: $\vert z \vert < \vert a \vert$
- ☐ $n a^n u[n] \leftrightarrow \frac{a z^{-1}}{(1 - a z^{-1})^2}$, ROC: $\vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$

### E. Common Exam Pitfalls to Avoid
- ☐ Always write the ROC explicitly — never omit it.
- ☐ Verify ROC is non-empty for two-sided signals.
- ☐ For the anti-causal exponential, include the **negative sign** in the time-domain expression.
- ☐ For stability checks, evaluate the **modulus** of the pole, not the pole itself.
- ☐ When applying time shifting, double-check whether the shift is **right** or **left**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Complex Modulation (Z-Domain Scaling)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST416 - SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Complex Modulation (Z-Domain Scaling)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU Definition

**Complex Modulation (Z-Domain Scaling)** is a fundamental bilateral property of the Z-Transform that establishes the equivalence between multiplication of a discrete-time sequence by a complex exponential sequence in the **time domain** and a corresponding *frequency-domain rotation* (scaling) in the **z-plane**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Statement (Verbatim Concept):**
> If $x(n) \xleftrightarrow{Z} X(z)$ with Region of Convergence $R_1 < \vert z \vert < R_2$, then
> $$\boxed{e^{j\omega_0 n}\,x(n) \xleftrightarrow{Z} X(e^{-j\omega_0}z)}$$
> and more generally, for a complex constant $z_0$,
> $$\boxed{z_0^{\,n}\,x(n) \xleftrightarrow{Z} X\!\left(\frac{z}{z_0}\right)}$$
> with scaled ROC: $R_1\,\vert z_0 \vert < \vert z \vert < R_2\,\vert z_0 \vert$.

The name **"Complex Modulation"** comes from communications engineering, where multiplying a baseband signal by $e^{j\omega_0 n}$ shifts its spectrum to a new centre frequency $\omega_0$, i.e., it acts as a *digital up-converter*.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Spinning Vinyl Record" Model**
> Imagine the Z-plane as a spinning vinyl record, and the ROC as the playable groove region. A sequence $x(n)$ has a "groove pattern" $X(z)$ etched onto this record. If we **modulate** the time-domain signal by $e^{j\omega_0 n}$, it is exactly equivalent to **physically rotating the record by angle $\omega_0$** — the groove pattern looks identical, but every point on it has now moved to a new angular position. Mathematically, we represent this rotation by substituting $z \to e^{-j\omega_0} z$, which corresponds to a contraction/expansion + rotation in the complex plane.

- **Why "scaling"?** Because $e^{-j\omega_0} z$ is a complex number whose magnitude is $\vert z \vert$ but whose *argument* is shifted by $-\omega_0$. The unit circle ($|z|=1$) is rotated, while radial distances are preserved.
- **Why is it important?** It is the discrete-time twin of the **frequency-shifting property** of the Fourier Transform, and it underpins every digital modulator/demodulator in modern communication systems (GSM, OFDM, QPSK).

### 1.3 Standard Notational Conventions

- **Sampling frequency** is denoted $F_s$ in **Hz**, while digital angular frequency is $\omega_0 = 2\pi f_0 / F_s$ in **radians/sample**.
- The complex exponential $e^{j\omega_0 n}$ has magnitude **1**, hence it does not change the energy/power *scale* of the signal — only its frequency content.
- A special case is $(-1)^n = e^{j\pi n}$, which produces a sign inversion (highest possible digital frequency $\omega_0 = \pi$).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Decomposition

1. **Identify the input pair.** We start with a known Z-transform pair $x(n) \leftrightarrow X(z)$ and a specified ROC.
2. **Form the modulated sequence.** Construct $y(n) = e^{j\omega_0 n} x(n)$, which is a complex-valued sequence in general.
3. **Apply the Z-transform definition.** Substitute $y(n)$ into the summation $Y(z) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} y(n)\,z^{-n}$.
4. **Factor the exponential.** Pull $e^{j\omega_0 n}$ into the kernel: $e^{j\omega_0 n} z^{-n} = (e^{-j\omega_0} z)^{-n}$.
5. **Recognise the original transform.** The remaining summation is exactly $X(\cdot)$ evaluated at the argument $e^{-j\omega_0} z$.
6. **Scale the ROC.** Each radial bound is multiplied by $\vert e^{-j\omega_0} \vert = 1$, so the ROC is **invariant in shape** but rotated about the origin. For the general form $z_0^n x(n)$, the ROC boundaries are *scaled* by $\vert z_0 \vert$.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Property

- **Why does it correspond to frequency shifting?** Because on the unit circle ($z = e^{j\omega}$), the substitution gives $Y(e^{j\omega}) = X(e^{j(\omega - \omega_0)})$, which is a pure shift of the DTFT.
- **Why does ROC scaling work the way it does?** Because $|e^{-j\omega_0} z| = |z|$, so distances from the origin are preserved for a pure $e^{j\omega_0 n}$ modulator. For a general $z_0$, the modulus $|z_0|$ becomes a radial scale factor.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarises every KTU-expected formula for this topic. Note the use of `\vert` instead of the literal pipe symbol to preserve table formatting.

| # | Property / Formula | Time-Domain Statement | Z-Domain Result | ROC Transformation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Complex Modulation (frequency shift) | $e^{j\omega_0 n}\,x(n)$ | $X(e^{-j\omega_0} z)$ | ROC unchanged ($|z|$ bounds preserved) |
| 2 | General Z-domain Scaling | $z_0^{\,n}\,x(n)$ | $X(z / z_0)$ | $R_1 \vert z_0 \vert < \vert z \vert < R_2 \vert z_0 \vert$ |
| 3 | Real Modulation ($+j\omega_0$) | $\cos(\omega_0 n)\,x(n)$ | $\tfrac{1}{2}\left[X(e^{-j\omega_0}z) + X(e^{j\omega_0}z)\right]$ | Intersection of both ROCs |
| 4 | Real Modulation (sine) | $\sin(\omega_0 n)\,x(n)$ | $\tfrac{1}{2j}\left[X(e^{-j\omega_0}z) - X(e^{j\omega_0}z)\right]$ | Intersection of both ROCs |
| 5 | Sign Inversion Special Case | $(-1)^n x(n)$ | $X(-z)$ | ROC: $R_1 < \vert z \vert < R_2$ (preserved) |
| 6 | Exponential Multiplication | $a^n x(n)$ (real $a$) | $X(z/a)$ | $R_1 a < \vert z \vert < R_2 a$ (for $a>0$) |

### 2.4 Engineering Utility — Where This Property is Used in Industry

- **Digital Communications:** BPSK/QPSK modulators map bits to $\pm 1$ sequences, which are exactly $(-1)^n$-modulated baseband signals.
- **Digital Up-conversion / Down-conversion (DUC/DDC):** Modern software-defined radios multiply incoming samples by $e^{j\omega_0 n}$ to shift spectra in real-time.
- **Filter Design:** The bilinear transform for IIR filter design relies on frequency warping which is mathematically identical to Z-domain scaling.
- **Audio & Speech Processing:** Pitch shifting and vibrato effects are achieved via complex modulation in the frequency domain.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Complex Modulation Property

**Given:** $x(n) \xleftrightarrow{Z} X(z) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(n)\,z^{-n}$, ROC: $R_1 < \vert z \vert < R_2$.

**To Prove:** $y(n) = e^{j\omega_0 n}\,x(n) \xleftrightarrow{Z} Y(z) = X(e^{-j\omega_0} z)$.

**Step 1 — Definition of the Z-transform of $y(n)$:**
By definition,
$$Y(z) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} y(n)\,z^{-n}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $y(n) = e^{j\omega_0 n}\,x(n)$:**
$$Y(z) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} e^{j\omega_0 n}\,x(n)\,z^{-n}$$

**Step 3 — Combine the exponentials using exponent laws:**
Recognising that $e^{j\omega_0 n}\,z^{-n} = (e^{j\omega_0})^{-n}\,z^{-n}$, we rewrite:
$$e^{j\omega_0 n}\,z^{-n} = (e^{-j\omega_0} z)^{-n}$$
This is valid because $a^n \cdot b^n = (ab)^n$ and $z^{-n} = (e^{-j\omega_0})^{-n}\,(e^{-j\omega_0} z)^{-n}$.

**Step 4 — Substitute back into the summation:**
$$Y(z) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(n)\,\left(e^{-j\omega_0} z\right)^{-n}$$

**Step 5 — Identify the original Z-transform:**
The summation is precisely the definition of $X(\cdot)$ evaluated at $z' = e^{-j\omega_0} z$:
$$Y(z) = X(e^{-j\omega_0} z)$$

**Step 6 — Determine the new ROC:**
The ROC condition $R_1 < \vert z' \vert < R_2$ becomes:
$$R_1 < \vert e^{-j\omega_0} z \vert < R_2$$
Since $\vert e^{-j\omega_0} \vert = 1$, this simplifies to:
$$R_1 < \vert z \vert < R_2$$
Hence **the ROC is preserved** (a pure rotation in the z-plane). $\blacksquare$

### 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Z-Transform of $(-1)^n u(n)$

**Problem:** Find the Z-transform of $x(n) = (-1)^n u(n)$ using the complex modulation property.

**Step 1 — Identify the base pair:**
We know the standard pair
$$u(n) \xleftrightarrow{Z} \frac{z}{z-1}, \quad \text{ROC: } \vert z \vert > 1$$

**Step 2 — Express the modulator as a complex exponential:**
$(-1)^n = (e^{j\pi})^n = e^{j\pi n}$, so $\omega_0 = \pi$ and $z_0 = e^{j\pi} = -1$.

**Step 3 — Apply the complex modulation property:**
$$X_{\text{new}}(z) = U(e^{-j\pi} z) = U(-z)$$

**Step 4 — Substitute:**
$$X_{\text{new}}(z) = \frac{(-z)}{(-z) - 1}$$

**Step 5 — Simplify algebraically:**
$$X_{\text{new}}(z) = \frac{-z}{-z - 1} = \frac{z}{z + 1}$$

**Step 6 — State the ROC:**
$$\boxed{X(z) = \frac{z}{z+1}, \quad \text{ROC: } \vert z \vert > 1}$$

### 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Z-Transform of $2^n \cos\!\left(\tfrac{\pi}{3} n\right) u(n)$

**Step 1 — Identify the base pair and modulator:**
Base signal: $\cos\!\left(\tfrac{\pi}{3} n\right) u(n)$ requires two applications of complex modulation on $u(n)$.

Base pair: $u(n) \leftrightarrow \dfrac{z}{z-1}$, ROC $\vert z \vert > 1$.

**Step 2 — Apply modulation by $e^{j\pi n / 3}$ and $e^{-j\pi n / 3}$:**
Using Euler's identity:
$$\cos\!\left(\tfrac{\pi}{3} n\right) = \tfrac{1}{2}\left[e^{j\pi n/3} + e^{-j\pi n/3}\right]$$

$$Z\{e^{j\pi n/3} u(n)\} = \frac{e^{-j\pi/3} z}{e^{-j\pi/3} z - 1}$$
$$Z\{e^{-j\pi n/3} u(n)\} = \frac{e^{j\pi/3} z}{e^{j\pi/3} z - 1}$$

**Step 3 — Sum the two results and apply the $z_0^n$ scaling for $z_0 = 2$:**
The exponential modulator $2^n$ further scales $z \to z/2$ (general Z-domain scaling with $z_0 = 2$).

After simplification (combining fractions over a common denominator), the final closed-form expression is:
$$\boxed{X(z) = \frac{z(z - 1)}{z^2 - z + 4}, \quad \text{ROC: } \vert z \vert > 2}$$

This is the canonical KTU form for $a^n \cos(\omega_0 n) u(n)$:
$$\frac{z(z - a\cos\omega_0)}{z^2 - 2a\cos\omega_0 \, z + a^2}$$
With $a = 2$, $\omega_0 = \pi/3$, $\cos(\pi/3) = 1/2$, so $2a\cos\omega_0 = 2$ and $a^2 = 4$. ✓

### 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Inverse Z-Transform via Complex Modulation

**Problem:** Find $x(n)$ given $X(z) = \dfrac{z}{z^2 - 1}$, with ROC $\vert z \vert > 1$.

**Step 1 — Partial-fraction expansion:**
$$\frac{z}{z^2 - 1} = \frac{z}{(z-1)(z+1)} = \frac{A}{z-1} + \frac{B}{z+1}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $A$ and $B$ (cover-up method):**
$$A = \left.\frac{z}{z+1}\right|_{z=1} = \frac{1}{2}, \quad B = \left.\frac{z}{z-1}\right|_{z=-1} = \frac{-1}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Rewrite into standard Z-transform form:**
$$X(z) = \frac{1}{2}\cdot\frac{z}{z-1} - \frac{1}{2}\cdot\frac{z}{z+1}$$

**Step 4 — Use the complex modulation (sign-inversion) property in reverse:**
We know $u(n) \leftrightarrow \dfrac{z}{z-1}$. By complex modulation with $z_0 = -1$:
$$(-1)^n u(n) \leftrightarrow \frac{z}{z+1}$$

**Step 5 — Apply linearity to obtain the inverse Z-transform:**
$$\boxed{x(n) = \tfrac{1}{2}\left[u(n) - (-1)^n u(n)\right]}$$

**Verification:** This is exactly $\tfrac{1}{2}[1 - (-1)^n]$, which equals $0$ for even $n$ and $1$ for odd $n$. ✓
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Time-Domain Modulator vs Z-Domain Equivalent

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **dual equivalence** between time-domain modulation and z-domain scaling that KTU examiners love to test as a conceptual question.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Input Sequence xn"] -->|"Multiply by z0 to power n"| B["Modulated Sequence yn = z0 to power n times xn"]
    C["Z-Transform Xz"] -->|"Replace z with z divided by z0"| D["Scaled Transform Yz = X of z over z0"]
    B -.->|"Z Transform Pair"| C
    D -.->|"Inverse Z Transform"| B
    E["Original ROC R1 less than mod z less than R2"] -->|"Multiply bounds by mod z0"| F["New ROC R1 mod z0 less than mod z less than R2 mod z0"]
    C -.-> E
    D -.-> F
```

### 4.2 Special-Case Schematic — Sign Inversion $(-1)^n$

A frequently-tested KTU sub-case is the multiplication by $(-1)^n$, which is a pure $180°$ rotation in the z-plane.

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1["Xn with ROC R1 to R2"] -->|"Apply Complex Modulation z0 equals -1"| P2["Xn replaced by X of minus z"]
    P3["xn"] -->|"Multiply by minus 1 to power n"| P4["yn = minus 1 to power n times xn"]
    P3 -.->|"Z Transform"| P1
    P4 -.->|"Z Transform"| P2
    Q1["Pole at radius r and angle theta"] -->|"Rotate by 180 degrees"| Q2["Pole at radius r and angle theta plus pi"]
    P1 -.-> Q1
    P2 -.-> Q2
```

### 4.3 Operator-Level Mapping Table (Verbal Schematic)

| Operation in Time-Domain | Operator Symbol | Result in Z-Domain | Geometric Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| $y(n) = x(n)$ | Identity | $Y(z) = X(z)$ | No change |
| $y(n) = e^{j\omega_0 n} x(n)$ | Frequency Shift | $Y(z) = X(e^{-j\omega_0} z)$ | Pure rotation by $-\omega_0$ |
| $y(n) = z_0^{\,n} x(n)$ | General Scaling | $Y(z) = X(z/z_0)$ | Rotation + radial scaling by $\vert z_0 \vert$ |
| $y(n) = (-1)^n x(n)$ | Sign Inversion | $Y(z) = X(-z)$ | $180°$ rotation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading Guide for KTU Students:** The arrows in the diagram show two parallel "worlds" — the time-domain on the left and the z-domain on the right. The dotted arrows are the Z-transform links, while the solid arrows represent the **physical operation** (multiplication or substitution). Understanding which operation corresponds to which arrow is what KTU examiners test in Part A.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*State the complex modulation (Z-domain scaling) property of the Z-transform. Clearly mention the ROC transformation.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> **Statement:** If $x(n) \xleftrightarrow{Z} X(z)$ with ROC $R_1 < \vert z \vert < R_2$, then for any complex constant $z_0$:
> $$z_0^{\,n}\,x(n) \xleftrightarrow{Z} X\!\left(\frac{z}{z_0}\right)$$
> **ROC Transformation:** $R_1\,\vert z_0 \vert < \vert z \vert < R_2\,\vert z_0 \vert$.
> **Special Case** (modulation by unit-magnitude exponential $z_0 = e^{j\omega_0}$): ROC boundaries remain unchanged in magnitude, only the angular position of poles/zeros is rotated by $-\omega_0$. **[3 Marks]**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sample]**
*Using the complex modulation property, find the Z-transform of $x(n) = (-1)^n u(n)$.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> We know $u(n) \leftrightarrow \dfrac{z}{z-1}$, ROC $\vert z \vert > 1$.
> Since $(-1)^n = e^{j\pi n}$, applying complex modulation with $z_0 = -1$:
> $$X(z) = \frac{z/(-1)}{z/(-1) - 1} = \frac{-z}{-z-1} = \frac{z}{z+1}, \quad \text{ROC: } \vert z \vert > 1$$ **[3 Marks]**

---

### Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the ROC transformation** — Part (a) proofs often lose 2 marks if the ROC line is missing.
> 2. **Incorrect substitution direction** — When $y(n) = z_0^n x(n)$, students often write $X(z \cdot z_0)$ instead of $X(z/z_0)$. Memorise: *"Divide the argument by $z_0$."*
> 3. **Missing factor of $1/2$ or $1/(2j)$** — When applying Euler's identity for real cosine/sine modulation, the factor must be retained.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) State and prove the complex modulation property of the Z-transform.** *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Statement (1 Mark):** If $x(n) \xleftrightarrow{Z} X(z)$ with ROC $R_1 < \vert z \vert < R_2$, then for a complex constant $z_0$:
$$z_0^{\,n}\,x(n) \xleftrightarrow{Z} X\!\left(\frac{z}{z_0}\right), \quad \text{ROC: } R_1\,\vert z_0 \vert < \vert z \vert < R_2\,\vert z_0 \vert$$

**Proof (6 Marks):**

Starting from the definition:
$$Y(z) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} z_0^{\,n}\,x(n)\,z^{-n}$$

Rearranging the exponentials:
$$Y(z) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(n)\left(\frac{z}{z_0}\right)^{-n}$$

Recognising this as the definition of $X(\cdot)$ evaluated at $z' = z/z_0$:
$$Y(z) = X\!\left(\frac{z}{z_0}\right)$$

ROC: $|z'| = |z|/|z_0|$ must lie in $(R_1, R_2)$, hence:
$$R_1 < \frac{\vert z \vert}{\vert z_0 \vert} < R_2 \implies R_1 \vert z_0 \vert < \vert z \vert < R_2 \vert z_0 \vert \qquad \blacksquare$$

---

**(b) Using the complex modulation property, find the Z-transform of $x(n) = 2^n \cos\!\left(\tfrac{\pi n}{3}\right) u(n)$.** *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** Decompose the cosine using Euler's identity:
$$\cos\!\left(\tfrac{\pi n}{3}\right) = \tfrac{1}{2}\left[e^{j\pi n/3} + e^{-j\pi n/3}\right]$$

**Step 2 (1 Mark):** Therefore:
$$x(n) = \tfrac{1}{2}\cdot 2^n e^{j\pi n/3} u(n) + \tfrac{1}{2}\cdot 2^n e^{-j\pi n/3} u(n)$$

**Step 3 (2 Marks):** Start from $u(n) \leftrightarrow \dfrac{z}{z-1}$, ROC $\vert z \vert > 1$. Apply complex modulation with $z_0 = 2e^{j\pi/3}$:
$$Z\{2^n e^{j\pi n/3} u(n)\} = \frac{z/(2e^{j\pi/3})}{z/(2e^{j\pi/3}) - 1}$$

**Step 4 (2 Marks):** Similarly for $z_0 = 2e^{-j\pi/3}$:
$$Z\{2^n e^{-j\pi n/3} u(n)\} = \frac{z/(2e^{-j\pi/3})}{z/(2e^{-j\pi/3}) - 1}$$

**Step 5 (1 Mark):** After combining the two fractions over a common denominator $z^2 - 2z\cos(\pi/3) + 4 = z^2 - z + 4$, we obtain the standard form.

**Final Answer:**
$$\boxed{X(z) = \frac{z(z-1)}{z^2 - z + 4}, \quad \text{ROC: } \vert z \vert > 2}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a) State the Z-domain scaling property and explain what happens to a pole located at $z = re^{j\theta}$ when the sequence is multiplied by $(-1)^n$.** *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Statement (3 Marks):** As above — if $x(n) \leftrightarrow X(z)$ with ROC $R_1 < \vert z \vert < R_2$, then
$$(-1)^n x(n) \xleftrightarrow{Z} X(-z), \quad \text{ROC preserved.}$$

**Pole Movement Analysis (4 Marks):** A pole originally at $z_p = re^{j\theta}$ satisfies $X(z_p) = \infty$. After applying the property, the new transform $X(-z)$ has a pole at $z_p'$ where $-z_p' = z_p$, i.e.,
$$z_p' = -re^{j\theta} = re^{j(\theta + \pi)}$$

**Geometric Interpretation:** The pole migrates from radius $r$ at angle $\theta$ to **radius $r$ at angle $\theta + \pi$**. Since $\vert z_p' \vert = r$ is unchanged, the ROC is preserved. This is a **pure $180°$ rotation** about the origin in the z-plane. For a zero-pole plot, every pole/zero simply rotates half-way around the unit circle.

---

**(b) Find the inverse Z-transform of $X(z) = \dfrac{z}{z^2 - 1}$ with ROC $\vert z \vert > 1$, using the complex modulation property.** *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** Partial-fraction decomposition:
$$\frac{z}{z^2 - 1} = \frac{z}{(z-1)(z+1)} = \frac{A}{z-1} + \frac{B}{z+1}$$

**Step 2 (2 Marks):** Cover-up method gives $A = 1/2$ and $B = -1/2$.

**Step 3 (1 Mark):** Rewrite in standard Z-transform form:
$$X(z) = \tfrac{1}{2}\cdot\frac{z}{z-1} - \tfrac{1}{2}\cdot\frac{z}{z+1}$$

**Step 4 (2 Marks):** Identify the modulated pair. From the standard pair $u(n) \leftrightarrow z/(z-1)$, and by complex modulation with $z_0 = -1$:
$$(-1)^n u(n) \leftrightarrow \frac{z}{z+1}$$

**Step 5 (1 Mark):** Apply linearity for the inverse Z-transform:
$$\boxed{x(n) = \tfrac{1}{2}\left[u(n) - (-1)^n u(n)\right] = \tfrac{1}{2}\left[1 - (-1)^n\right]u(n)}$$

This sequence is $0$ for even $n \geq 0$ and $1$ for odd $n \geq 0$.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 4 / Complex Modulation**
>
> - **Core Property:** $z_0^n x(n) \xleftrightarrow{Z} X(z/z_0)$, ROC scaled by $\vert z_0 \vert$.
> - **Unit-Magnitude Special Case:** $e^{j\omega_0 n} x(n) \xleftrightarrow{Z} X(e^{-j\omega_0} z)$ — ROC preserved.
> - **Sign Inversion Trap:** $(-1)^n x(n) \leftrightarrow X(-z)$ is the most-frequently tested special case; remember the $180°$ pole rotation.
> - **Real Modulator Trick:** For $\cos(\omega_0 n) x(n)$ and $\sin(\omega_0 n) x(n)$, decompose into $e^{\pm j\omega_0 n}$ and apply the property twice with a factor of $1/2$ or $1/(2j)$.
> - **ROC Direction:** When $|z_0| > 1$, ROC expands outward; when $|z_0| < 1$, ROC contracts inward.
> - **Inverse Application:** Use the property in reverse to recognise $(-1)^n u(n)$, $a^n u(n)$, etc., inside partial-fraction decompositions.
> - **Examiner Favourite:** A 14-mark Part B almost always pairs a 7-mark *proof* with a 7-mark *numerical application* — practise both flows.
> - **Mnemonic:** *"Modulate in time ⇒ Scale the argument in $z$"* — the operation is *divided* by $z_0$, not multiplied.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Conjugation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST416 - SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Conjugation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In **Signals and Systems**, *conjugation* refers to the operation of taking the **complex conjugate** of a signal and analyzing its structural symmetries. For a complex-valued continuous-time signal $x(t) = a(t) + jb(t)$, the **complex conjugate** is defined as:

$$x^*(t) = a(t) - jb(t)$$

where $a(t)$ and $b(t)$ are the real and imaginary components of $x(t)$, respectively. The conjugate operation inverts the sign of the imaginary part while preserving the real part. This operation is foundational for understanding spectral symmetry, energy relations, and orthogonality in signal processing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Conjugation is a prerequisite for understanding *Hermitian Symmetry* in the Discrete-Time Fourier Transform (DTFT) and is heavily tested in problems involving the decomposition of real signals into even and odd components.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a complex number plotted on the **2D Argand plane**. If the point $z = a + jb$ lies in the upper half-plane, its conjugate $z^* = a - jb$ is its **mirror image** reflected across the horizontal (real) axis. 

Imagine a swinging pendulum clock viewed through a horizontal glass pane:
- The **real part** ($a(t)$) is the back-and-forth swing of the pendulum (visible on both sides).
- The **imaginary part** ($b(t)$) is a separate, perpendicular motion.
- Taking the conjugate is like **flipping only the imaginary swing** while keeping the real swing intact.

Mathematically, two key structural concepts follow from this reflection idea:
- **Conjugate Symmetric (Even) Signal:** $x(t) = x^*(-t)$ → mirror symmetry across the time axis with sign inversion only in the imaginary part.
- **Conjugate Antisymmetric (Odd) Signal:** $x(t) = -x^*(-t)$ → mirror antisymmetry combined with sign inversion.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants

| Property | Symbol | Notes |
|----------|--------|-------|
| Complex conjugate | $x^*(t)$ | Inverts imaginary sign |
| Time reversal | $x(-t)$ | Reflects the signal across $t = 0$ |
| Combined operation | $x^*(-t)$ | Used in Hermitian symmetry tests |
| Modulus squared | $\vert x(t) \vert^2 = x(t) x^*(t)$ | Real, non-negative quantity |
| Magnitude invariance | $\vert x^*(t) \vert = \vert x(t) \vert$ | Always preserved |

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants:** No universal physical constants apply; conjugation is a pure mathematical transformation. However, it directly impacts **energy computations** ($E = \int \vert x(t) \vert^2 dt$) and **Parseval's theorem** in the frequency domain.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Logical Breakdown of Conjugation in Signals

### Step 1 — Component Decomposition
Any complex signal can be split into real and imaginary parts:

$$x(t) = x_R(t) + jx_I(t)$$

The conjugate operation flips the sign of $x_I(t)$:

$$x^*(t) = x_R(t) - jx_I(t)$$

### Step 2 — Conjugate Symmetry (Hermitian Symmetry)
A signal is **conjugate symmetric** (also called **Hermitian symmetric** or **even**) if:

$$x(t) = x^*(-t)$$

Substituting the decomposed form:

$$x_R(t) + jx_I(t) = x_R(-t) - jx_I(-t)$$

Equating real and imaginary parts:
- Real part is **even**: $x_R(t) = x_R(-t)$
- Imaginary part is **odd**: $x_I(t) = -x_I(-t)$

### Step 3 — Conjugate Antisymmetry (Anti-Hermitian Symmetry)
A signal is **conjugate antisymmetric** (also called **skew-Hermitian** or **odd**) if:

$$x(t) = -x^*(-t)$$

This forces:
- Real part is **odd**: $x_R(t) = -x_R(-t)$
- Imaginary part is **even**: $x_I(t) = x_I(-t)$

### Step 4 — General Decomposition Theorem
**Every arbitrary signal** $x(t)$ can be uniquely decomposed as:

$$x(t) = x_e(t) + x_o(t)$$

where:
- $x_e(t) = \dfrac{x(t) + x^*(-t)}{2}$ → **Conjugate symmetric part**
- $x_o(t) = \dfrac{x(t) - x^*(-t)}{2}$ → **Conjugate antisymmetric part**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favorite:** This decomposition formula is asked almost every year. Note carefully — it is **$x^*(-t)$** and NOT simply $x(-t)$, because we are working with complex signals. The star handles the imaginary sign flip, while the $(-t)$ handles time reversal.

### Step 5 — Frequency-Domain Conjugate Symmetry (Fourier Transform Pair)
For a **real-valued** signal $x(t)$, the Fourier transform exhibits **conjugate symmetry**:

$$X(-\omega) = X^*(\omega)$$

This is the **Hermitian property** of the spectrum of a real signal. Consequences:
- $\vert X(\omega) \vert$ is **even** in $\omega$.
- $\angle X(\omega)$ is **odd** in $\omega$.

For a **purely imaginary** signal, the spectrum is **anti-Hermitian**:

$$X(-\omega) = -X^*(\omega)$$

## 2.2 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application of Conjugation |
|-------|----------------------------|
| **Telecommunications** | QAM modulation/demodulation uses conjugate symmetry to pack data in I/Q channels. |
| **Radar Systems** | Matched filtering uses conjugate of the transmitted chirp signal. |
| **MRI Imaging** | k-space data uses Hermitian symmetry to halve acquisition time. |
| **Audio Processing** | Real signals have conjugate-symmetric FFT, enabling single-sided spectrum display. |
| **OFDM** | Pilot subcarriers rely on conjugate symmetry for channel estimation. |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description |
|---|---------|-------------|
| 1 | $x^*(t) = x_R(t) - jx_I(t)$ | Complex conjugate |
| 2 | $\vert x(t) \vert^2 = x(t) \cdot x^*(t)$ | Modulus squared (real-valued) |
| 3 | $x_e(t) = \frac{x(t) + x^*(-t)}{2}$ | Conjugate symmetric part |
| 4 | $x_o(t) = \frac{x(t) - x^*(-t)}{2}$ | Conjugate antisymmetric part |
| 5 | $x(t) = x_e(t) + x_o(t)$ | Unique decomposition |
| 6 | $x_e^*(t) = x_e(-t)$ | Property of conjugate symmetric signals |
| 7 | $x_o^*(t) = -x_o(-t)$ | Property of conjugate antisymmetric signals |
| 8 | $X(-\omega) = X^*(\omega)$ | Hermitian symmetry (real signals) |
| 9 | $X(-\omega) = -X^*(\omega)$ | Anti-Hermitian symmetry (imaginary signals) |
| 10 | $\mathcal{F}\{x^*(t)\} = X^*(-\omega)$ | Conjugate property of Fourier transform |
| 11 | $\mathcal{F}\{x^*(-t)\} = X^*(\omega)$ | Dual conjugate reversal property |
| 12 | $\mathcal{F}\{x(-t)\} = X(-\omega)$ | Time reversal property |

> [!NOTE]
> **Board Tip:** Memorize properties #10 and #11. These are the most commonly asked properties in the KTU End Semester Examinations. Property #11 directly leads to the Hermitian symmetry statement for real signals.

## 2.4 The "Why" Behind Conjugation in Engineering

The deep reason engineers care about conjugation is **spectral efficiency** and **recoverability**. Whenever a physical sensor outputs a **real-valued** signal (every microphone, antenna, accelerometer, and camera), its Fourier spectrum automatically carries **redundant information** — the negative frequency content is just the conjugate mirror of the positive frequencies. This redundancy means:
- We only need to transmit/store **half** the spectrum.
- We can use **single-sideband modulation** for bandwidth efficiency.
- Channel estimators can interpolate missing subcarriers using **Hermitian symmetry**.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: General Decomposition into Conjugate Symmetric and Antisymmetric Parts

**Goal:** Show that any signal $x(t)$ can be uniquely written as $x(t) = x_e(t) + x_o(t)$, with $x_e$ conjugate symmetric and $x_o$ conjugate antisymmetric.

### Step 1 — Start with the proposed expressions

$$x_e(t) = \frac{x(t) + x^*(-t)}{2}$$

$$x_o(t) = \frac{x(t) - x^*(-t)}{2}$$

### Step 2 — Add the two parts

$$\begin{aligned}
x_e(t) + x_o(t) &= \frac{x(t) + x^*(-t)}{2} + \frac{x(t) - x^*(-t)}{2} \\
&= \frac{x(t) + x^*(-t) + x(t) - x^*(-t)}{2} \\
&= \frac{2x(t)}{2} \\
&= x(t)
\end{aligned}$$

This confirms the decomposition identity. The $-x^*(-t)$ and $+x^*(-t)$ cancel perfectly.

### Step 3 — Verify $x_e(t)$ is conjugate symmetric

We must show $x_e(t) = x_e^*(-t)$.

Compute $x_e^*(-t)$ by first finding $x_e(-t)$, then conjugating:

$$\begin{aligned}
x_e(-t) &= \frac{x(-t) + x^*(t)}{2} \\
x_e^*(-t) &= \left(\frac{x(-t) + x^*(t)}{2}\right)^* \\
&= \frac{x^*(-t) + x(t)}{2} \\
&= \frac{x(t) + x^*(-t)}{2} \\
&= x_e(t)
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore, $x_e(t) = x_e^*(-t)$. ✅ **Proved.**

### Step 4 — Verify $x_o(t)$ is conjugate antisymmetric

We must show $x_o(t) = -x_o^*(-t)$.

$$\begin{aligned}
x_o(-t) &= \frac{x(-t) - x^*(t)}{2} \\
x_o^*(-t) &= \frac{x^*(-t) - x(t)}{2} \\
-x_o^*(-t) &= -\frac{x^*(-t) - x(t)}{2} = \frac{x(t) - x^*(-t)}{2} = x_o(t)
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore, $x_o(t) = -x_o^*(-t)$. ✅ **Proved.**

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Hermitian Symmetry of Real Signals

**Given:** $x(t)$ is real-valued, i.e., $x(t) = x^*(t)$.

**Goal:** Show $X(-\omega) = X^*(\omega)$.

### Step 1 — Apply the definition of the Fourier transform

$$X(\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) e^{-j\omega t} \, dt$$

### Step 2 — Compute $X(-\omega)$

$$X(-\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) e^{j\omega t} \, dt$$

### Step 3 — Take the conjugate of $X(\omega)$

$$X^*(\omega) = \left(\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) e^{-j\omega t} \, dt\right)^* = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x^*(t) e^{j\omega t} \, dt$$

Note: We can bring the conjugate inside the integral because $x(t)$ is well-behaved.

### Step 4 — Apply the reality condition

Since $x(t) = x^*(t)$:

$$X^*(\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) e^{j\omega t} \, dt$$

### Step 5 — Compare with Step 2

$$X(-\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) e^{j\omega t} \, dt = X^*(\omega)$$

✅ **Proved:** The Fourier transform of any real signal is Hermitian symmetric.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Fourier Transform of $x^*(t)$

**Goal:** Prove $\mathcal{F}\{x^*(t)\} = X^*(-\omega)$.

### Step 1 — Apply the Fourier transform definition

$$\mathcal{F}\{x^*(t)\} = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x^*(t) e^{-j\omega t} \, dt$$

### Step 2 — Conjugate the integral (valid for proper integrals)

$$\mathcal{F}\{x^*(t)\} = \left(\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) e^{j\omega t} \, dt\right)^*$$

### Step 3 — Recognize the integral inside as $X(-\omega)$

The integrand $\int x(t) e^{j\omega t} dt$ is exactly $X(-\omega)$ by definition.

$$\mathcal{F}\{x^*(t)\} = X^*(-\omega)$$

✅ **Proved.**

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** Given $x(t) = (1+j) e^{j3t}$, find the conjugate symmetric and conjugate antisymmetric parts.

### Step 1 — Compute $x^*(t)$

$$x^*(t) = (1-j) e^{-j3t}$$

### Step 2 — Compute $x^*(-t)$

$$x^*(-t) = (1-j) e^{j3t}$$

### Step 3 — Compute $x_e(t)$

$$\begin{aligned}
x_e(t) &= \frac{x(t) + x^*(-t)}{2} \\
&= \frac{(1+j)e^{j3t} + (1-j)e^{j3t}}{2} \\
&= \frac{(1+j+1-j)e^{j3t}}{2} \\
&= \frac{2 e^{j3t}}{2} = e^{j3t}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4 — Compute $x_o(t)$

$$\begin{aligned}
x_o(t) &= \frac{x(t) - x^*(-t)}{2} \\
&= \frac{(1+j)e^{j3t} - (1-j)e^{j3t}}{2} \\
&= \frac{(1+j-1+j)e^{j3t}}{2} \\
&= \frac{2j e^{j3t}}{2} = j e^{j3t}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5 — Verify

$$x(t) = x_e(t) + x_o(t) = e^{j3t} + je^{j3t} = (1+j)e^{j3t} \checkmark$$

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix of Conjugate Properties

| Property | Time Domain | Frequency Domain | Condition |
|----------|-------------|------------------|-----------|
| Conjugation | $x^*(t)$ | $X^*(-\omega)$ | Always |
| Time reversal | $x(-t)$ | $X(-\omega)$ | Always |
| Combined | $x^*(-t)$ | $X^*(\omega)$ | Always |
| Real signal | $x(t) \in \mathbb{R}$ | $X(-\omega) = X^*(\omega)$ | Real-only |
| Imaginary signal | $x(t) \in j\mathbb{R}$ | $X(-\omega) = -X^*(\omega)$ | Imaginary-only |
| Conjugate symmetric | $x(t) = x^*(-t)$ | $X(\omega) \in \mathbb{R}$ | — |
| Conjugate antisymmetric | $x(t) = -x^*(-t)$ | $X(\omega) \in j\mathbb{R}$ | — |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Conjugation in Signal Processing

The following diagram illustrates how a complex signal flows through conjugation operations in a typical DSP receiver chain.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcA[Complex Signal x of t] --> splitA{Split Component}
    splitA --> realA[Real Part xR of t]
    splitA --> imagA[Imaginary Part xI of t]
    realA --> conjA[Conjugate Operation]
    imagA --> conjA
    conjA --> sigStar[x star of t]
    sigStar --> flowA{Decision Branch}
    flowA --> symm[Conjugate Symmetric x of t equals x star minus t]
    flowA --> antis[Conjugate Antisymmetric x of t equals minus x star minus t]
    symm --> fwdA[Apply Forward FT]
    antis --> fwdA
    fwdA --> spec[X of omega]
    spec --> check{Is x of t Real}
    check --> yesA[Yes: Hermitian Symmetry X minus omega equals X star omega]
    check --> noA[No: No Symmetry Guarantee]
    yesA --> outA[Single Sided Spectrum Display]
    noA --> outA
```

## 4.2 Decision Flowchart: Identifying Signal Symmetry Type

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start With Signal x of t] --> q1{Is x of t Real Valued}
    q1 -- Yes --> realBranch[Real Signal Path]
    q1 -- No --> q2{Is x of t Purely Imaginary}
    q2 -- Yes --> imagBranch[Imaginary Signal Path]
    q2 -- No --> complexBranch[Complex Signal Path]
    realBranch --> rSym[Check x of t equals x of minus t: Even]
    realBranch --> rOdd[Check x of t equals minus x of minus t: Odd]
    imagBranch --> iSym[Check x of t equals minus x of minus t: Even Imag]
    imagBranch --> iOdd[Check x of t equals x of minus t: Odd Imag]
    complexBranch --> cDecomp[Use x e and x o Formulas]
    cDecomp --> cEven[Conjugate Symmetric Part x e]
    cDecomp --> cOdd[Conjugate Antisymmetric Part x o]
```

## 4.3 Conjugate Symmetry in the Frequency Domain (Conceptual View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    tdom["TIME DOMAIN x of t"] -->|"Fourier Transform"| fdom["FREQUENCY DOMAIN X of omega"]
    fdom --> posFreq["POSITIVE FREQUENCIES omega greater than 0"]
    fdom --> negFreq["NEGATIVE FREQUENCIES omega less than 0"]
    posFreq --> mag1["Magnitude M of omega"]
    negFreq --> mag2["Magnitude M of omega"]
    mag1 -.->|"Hermitian Symmetry"| mag2
    posFreq --> phase1["Phase phi of omega"]
    negFreq --> phase2["Phase minus phi of omega"]
    phase1 -.->|"Odd Symmetry"| phase2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** Arrows with solid lines represent direct transformations. Dotted arrows labeled "Hermitian Symmetry" represent the constraint that connects the positive and negative frequency halves of the spectrum. For a real signal, knowing only the positive frequencies is sufficient to reconstruct the entire spectrum.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define conjugate symmetry and conjugate antisymmetry of a signal. State the conditions for each.**

**Model Answer:**

Conjugate symmetry (Hermitian symmetry) of a signal $x(t)$ is defined as:

$$x(t) = x^*(-t)$$

where $x^*(-t)$ denotes the time-reversed complex conjugate. Equivalently:
- Real part is **even**: $x_R(t) = x_R(-t)$
- Imaginary part is **odd**: $x_I(t) = -x_I(-t)$

Conjugate antisymmetry (anti-Hermitian symmetry) is defined as:

$$x(t) = -x^*(-t)$$

Equivalently:
- Real part is **odd**: $x_R(t) = -x_R(-t)$
- Imaginary part is **even**: $x_I(t) = x_I(-t)$

**(3 marks)** ✅

### Question A2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State and prove the Fourier transform property that relates $x^*(t)$ to $X(\omega)$.**

**Model Answer:**

**Statement:** If $x(t) \overset{\mathcal{F}}{\longleftrightarrow} X(\omega)$, then

$$x^*(t) \overset{\mathcal{F}}{\longleftrightarrow} X^*(-\omega)$$

**Proof:**

By definition:

$$X(\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) e^{-j\omega t} \, dt$$

Replace $\omega$ by $-\omega$:

$$X(-\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) e^{j\omega t} \, dt$$

Taking the conjugate:

$$X^*(-\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x^*(t) e^{-j\omega t} \, dt = \mathcal{F}\{x^*(t)\}$$

Hence proved. **(3 marks)** ✅

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

> `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** Given the complex signal $x(t) = e^{(1+j2)t} u(t)$, decompose it into conjugate symmetric and conjugate antisymmetric components. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a real-valued signal $x(t)$, prove that its Fourier transform satisfies $X(-\omega) = X^*(\omega)$ and discuss the consequences for the magnitude and phase spectra. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A:

#### Part (a) — Decomposition

**Step 1:** Write $x(t)$ explicitly.

$$x(t) = e^t \cdot e^{j2t} \cdot u(t) = e^t [\cos(2t) + j\sin(2t)] u(t)$$

So $x_R(t) = e^t \cos(2t) u(t)$ and $x_I(t) = e^t \sin(2t) u(t)$.

**Step 2:** Compute $x^*(t)$.

$$x^*(t) = e^t [\cos(2t) - j\sin(2t)] u(t) = e^t e^{-j2t} u(t)$$

**Step 3:** Compute $x^*(-t)$.

$$x^*(-t) = e^{-t} e^{j2t} u(-t)$$

Note: Because of $u(t)$, the support is restricted, so $x^*(-t)$ is **non-zero only for $t \le 0$**.

**Step 4:** Compute the conjugate symmetric part $x_e(t)$.

$$x_e(t) = \frac{x(t) + x^*(-t)}{2} = \frac{1}{2}\left[e^t e^{j2t} u(t) + e^{-t} e^{j2t} u(-t)\right]$$

$$x_e(t) = \frac{e^{j2t}}{2}\left[e^t u(t) + e^{-t} u(-t)\right]$$

**Step 5:** Compute the conjugate antisymmetric part $x_o(t)$.

$$x_o(t) = \frac{x(t) - x^*(-t)}{2} = \frac{1}{2}\left[e^t e^{j2t} u(t) - e^{-t} e^{j2t} u(-t)\right]$$

$$x_o(t) = \frac{e^{j2t}}{2}\left[e^t u(t) - e^{-t} u(-t)\right]$$

**Step 6:** Verification — Add the two parts:

$$x_e(t) + x_o(t) = \frac{e^{j2t}}{2}\left[2 e^t u(t)\right] = e^t e^{j2t} u(t) = x(t) \checkmark$$

**(7 marks awarded)** ✅

#### Part (b) — Hermitian Symmetry Proof

**Step 1:** Begin with the Fourier transform definition:

$$X(\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) e^{-j\omega t} \, dt$$

**Step 2:** Replace $\omega$ with $-\omega$:

$$X(-\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) e^{j\omega t} \, dt$$

**Step 3:** Take the complex conjugate of $X(\omega)$:

$$X^*(\omega) = \left[\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) e^{-j\omega t} \, dt\right]^* = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x^*(t) e^{j\omega t} \, dt$$

**Step 4:** Apply the reality condition $x^*(t) = x(t)$:

$$X^*(\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) e^{j\omega t} \, dt$$

**Step 5:** Compare with Step 2:

$$X(-\omega) = X^*(\omega) \quad \blacksquare$$

**Consequences:**

Let $X(\omega) = \vert X(\omega) \vert e^{j\angle X(\omega)}$.

Since $X(-\omega) = X^*(\omega) = \vert X(\omega) \vert e^{-j\angle X(\omega)}$, we also have $X(-\omega) = \vert X(-\omega) \vert e^{j\angle X(-\omega)}$.

Equating magnitudes:

$$\vert X(-\omega) \vert = \vert X(\omega) \vert \quad \text{(Magnitude is even)}$$

Equating phases:

$$\angle X(-\omega) = -\angle X(\omega) \quad \text{(Phase is odd)}$$

**(7 marks awarded)** ✅

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> `[KTU University Exam - Sample Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** For the signal $x(t) = \cos(\omega_0 t) + j \sin(\omega_0 t)$, show that $x(t)$ is **not** conjugate symmetric but is conjugate antisymmetric after suitable manipulation. Find the conjugate symmetric and antisymmetric parts. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Prove the property $\mathcal{F}\{x^*(-t)\} = X^*(\omega)$ and use it to show that the Fourier transform of a real, even signal is real and even. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B:

#### Part (a)

**Step 1:** Identify $x(t) = e^{j\omega_0 t}$.

**Step 2:** Compute $x^*(-t)$.

$$x^*(t) = e^{-j\omega_0 t} \implies x^*(-t) = e^{j\omega_0 t} = x(t)$$

**Step 3:** Check conjugate symmetry condition $x(t) = x^*(-t)$:

$$x(t) = e^{j\omega_0 t} = x^*(-t) \quad \text{✓}$$

Actually, $x(t) = e^{j\omega_0 t}$ **is** conjugate symmetric because $x^*(-t) = e^{j\omega_0 t} = x(t)$.

**Step 4:** Compute the parts.

$$x_e(t) = \frac{x(t) + x^*(-t)}{2} = \frac{e^{j\omega_0 t} + e^{j\omega_0 t}}{2} = e^{j\omega_0 t}$$

$$x_o(t) = \frac{x(t) - x^*(-t)}{2} = \frac{e^{j\omega_0 t} - e^{j\omega_0 t}}{2} = 0$$

So the entire signal is conjugate symmetric, with zero antisymmetric part.

**(7 marks awarded)** ✅

#### Part (b)

**Step 1:** Prove $\mathcal{F}\{x^*(-t)\} = X^*(\omega)$.

By definition:

$$\mathcal{F}\{x^*(-t)\} = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x^*(-t) e^{-j\omega t} \, dt$$

**Step 2:** Apply the substitution $\tau = -t$, so $d\tau = -dt$ and limits flip:

$$\mathcal{F}\{x^*(-t)\} = \int_{\infty}^{-\infty} x^*(\tau) e^{j\omega \tau} (-d\tau) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x^*(\tau) e^{j\omega \tau} d\tau$$

**Step 3:** Conjugate the integral:

$$= \left[\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(\tau) e^{-j\omega \tau} d\tau\right]^* = X^*(\omega) \quad \blacksquare$$

**Step 4:** Application — Real, even signal.

If $x(t)$ is real, then $x^*(t) = x(t)$, so:

$$X^*(\omega) = \mathcal{F}\{x^*(-t)\} = \mathcal{F}\{x(-t)\} = X(-\omega)$$

If $x(t)$ is also even, then $x(-t) = x(t)$, so $X(-\omega) = X(\omega)$.

Combining:

$$X^*(\omega) = X(\omega) \implies X(\omega) \in \mathbb{R}$$

And $X(-\omega) = X(\omega)$ means $X(\omega)$ is even. Therefore, the FT of a real, even signal is **real and even**. ✅

**(7 marks awarded)** ✅

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Alert:**
> 1. **Most common mistake:** Students write $x(-t)$ instead of $x^*(-t)$ in the decomposition formula. The asterisk is **mandatory** for complex signals. Failing this costs **2 marks** outright.
> 2. **Second common mistake:** Forgetting the factor of $\frac{1}{2}$ in the decomposition formulas. Always include $\frac{1}{2}$ — without it, the reconstruction $x_e + x_o = x$ fails.
> 3. **Third common mistake:** Confusing "real even" with "conjugate symmetric." A purely real even signal is a *special case* of conjugate symmetry where the imaginary part is zero. The general Hermitian condition is $x(t) = x^*(-t)$.
> 4. **Fourth mistake:** When computing $X^*(\omega)$, students forget to flip the sign inside: it is $X^*(-\omega)$ for $\mathcal{F}\{x^*(t)\}$ and $X^*(\omega)$ for $\mathcal{F}\{x^*(-t)\}$. These are **different** properties.
