Using the noble identity, the structure: $H(z^M)$ followed by $\downarrow M$ can be replaced by $M$ parallel branches, each consisting of a downsampler $\downarrow M$ followed by a polyphase subfilter $E_k(z)$, all operating at the lower rate. This reduces computation by a factor of $M$.

**Part (b)(i) — Maximum M (3 marks):**

The original signal is band-limited to $f_{max} = 2.5$ kHz. After downsampling by $M$, the new sampling rate is $F_s' = 8000/M$ Hz, and the new Nyquist frequency is $F_s'/2 = 4000/M$ Hz. To avoid aliasing, the new Nyquist must be $\geq 2.5$ kHz:

$$\frac{4000}{M} \geq 2500 \Rightarrow M \leq \frac{4000}{2500} = 1.6$$

Since $M$ must be a positive integer $\geq 2$, **no integer $M \geq 2$ avoids aliasing** with the given bandwidth. We must either (a) first filter the signal to a lower bandwidth, or (b) use $M=1$ (no downsampling). 

**Correct interpretation:** If we first pre-filter to, say, $f_c = 1.9$ kHz, then $M = \lfloor 4000/1900 \rfloor = 2$ is feasible. With current $2.5$ kHz bandwidth, $M=1$ only.

**Part (b)(ii) — Anti-aliasing filter specification (4 marks):**

Assuming we filter to $f_c = 1.9$ kHz for $M=2$:
- **Cutoff frequency (Hz):** $f_c = F_s / (2M) = 8000/4 = 2000$ Hz. (1 mark)
- **Normalized digital frequency:** $\omega_c = 2\pi f_c / F_s = 2\pi \cdot 2000/8000 = \pi/2$. ✓ (1 mark)
- **Passband gain:** $H_{LP}(0) = M = 2$ (compensates for the $1/M$ factor). (1 mark)
- **Filter type:** Lowpass FIR or IIR with stopband edge $\leq F_s/2 - f_c = 2000$ Hz to ensure alias rejection. (1 mark)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls in Downsampling Problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting the 1/M factor** in the frequency-domain downsampling formula → Lose 1 mark per occurrence.
> 2. **Skipping the anti-aliasing filter** in block diagrams → Lose 2 marks (the pre-filter is part of a complete decimator).
> 3. **Confusing downsampling factor direction** ($x[Mn]$ vs $x[n/M]$) → Lose up to 3 marks.
> 4. **Not stating the Nyquist condition** explicitly in derivations → Lose 1 mark.
> 5. **Confusing time-domain and frequency-domain scaling** (downsampling in time ↔ stretching in frequency, with $M$ shifted replicas).
> 6. **Incorrect polyphase indexing** — the convention $H(z) = \sum_{k=0}^{M-1} z^{-k} E_k(z^M)$ must be used consistently.
> 7. **Mixing up Noble Identity direction** — it applies to $\downarrow M$ and $H(z^M)$, NOT $H(z)$ and $\downarrow M$ directly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Sheet for Downsampling — KTU Module 4**

- **Time-domain equation:** $y_D[n] = x[nM]$ — keeps every $M$-th sample.
- **Frequency-domain equation:** $Y_D(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{1}{M} \sum_{k=0}^{M-1} X(e^{j(\omega - 2\pi k)/M})$ — the "Golden Formula" for board exams.
- **Decimator block** = Lowpass filter (cutoff $\pi/M$, gain $M$) **followed by** $\downarrow M$.
- **Anti-aliasing filter is mandatory** unless the input is already band-limited to $\pi/M$.
- **Spectrum stretching rule:** Downsampling in time by $M$ stretches the spectrum by $M$ (so bandwidth multiplies by $M$).
- **Number of replicas in $[-\pi, \pi]$ after downsampling by $M$** is exactly $M$, each shifted by $2\pi k$, $k = 0, 1, \ldots, M-1$.
- **Nyquist condition** for no aliasing: $\vert \omega \vert_{max, x} \leq \pi/M$.
- **Noble Identity (Downsampler):** $H(z^M) \rightarrow \downarrow M \equiv \downarrow M \rightarrow H(z)$ — enables efficient polyphase implementation.
- **Polyphase decomposition:** $H(z) = \sum_{k=0}^{M-1} z^{-k} E_k(z^M)$, where $E_k$ are the $M$ polyphase subfilters.
- **Computational savings:** Polyphase decimator uses $N/M$ multiplications per output sample (vs $N$ for direct implementation), where $N$ is the filter length.
- **Z-domain expression:** $Y_D(z) = \frac{1}{M} \sum_{k=0}^{M-1} X(z^{1/M} W_M^k)$ with $W_M = e^{-j2\pi/M}$.
- **Multistage downsampling:** $M_{total} = M_1 M_2 \cdots M_K$; reduces overall filter complexity.
- **Applications:** Audio compression, image resizing, software defined radio, biomedical signal compression.
- **Most-tested KTU sub-topic:** Frequency-domain derivation (5–7 marks); block diagram with anti-aliasing filter (3–4 marks); polyphase/Noble identity (4–5 marks).
- **Don't forget:** The factor $1/M$ in the spectrum — examiners **always** check this.
- **Keyword for high marks:** Always explicitly mention "**anti-aliasing**" when describing a decimator.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Convolution
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST416 - SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Convolution # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Convolution** is a mathematical operation that combines two signals to produce a third signal, expressing how the shape of one signal is modified by the other. In the context of Linear Time-Invariant (LTI) systems, convolution describes the output $y(t)$ or $y[n]$ of an LTI system as the convolution of its input $x(t)$ or $x[n]$ with its impulse response $h(t)$ or $h[n]$. ### Continuous-Time (CT) Convolution Integral $$y(t) = x(t) * h(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(\tau)\, h(t - \tau)\, d\tau$$ ### Discrete-Time (DT) Convolution Sum $$y[n] = x[n] * h[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{+\infty} x[k]\, h[n - k]$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The convolution integral and convolution sum are designated as **high-weightage** topics. Mastery of the graphical method and the four standard properties (Commutative, Associative, Distributive, Identity) is mandatory for ESE preparation. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Echo in a Cave" Imagine shouting inside a large cave. You hear your original shout, but you also hear a faint, delayed, smeared-out echo bouncing off the walls. - The **original shout** is the input signal $x(t)$. - The **way the cave distorts and delays the sound** is the impulse response $h(t)$ (a short echo that gets stretched and faded by the walls). - The **total sound reaching your ear** (original shout + echo + smearing) is the output $y(t) = x(t) * h(t)$. The cave is "smearing" the input by taking infinitely many tiny, time-shifted, scaled copies of itself and adding them together — that summation process **is** convolution. ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Convolution Operator:** $*$ (asterisk) - **Integration/Summation Variable:** Conventionally $\tau$ (CT) or $k$ (DT) to avoid confusion with the running time index $t$ or $n$. - **Units:** The output's units equal (input units) × (impulse response units) × (time), because of the integration/summation step. > [!NOTE] > **Memory Aid:** Convolution is the *mathematical fingerprint* of every LTI system. If you know $h(t)$ or $h[n]$, you can predict the system's response to **any** input — past, present, or future — without re-solving the differential/difference equation. ## 1.4 Why Convolution Matters in Engineering | Engineering Domain | Role of Convolution | |---|---| | **Communication Systems** | Channel equalization, intersymbol interference modeling | | **Digital Signal Processing** | FIR/IIR filter design and output computation | | **Image Processing** | Blurring, sharpening, edge detection (2-D convolution) | | **Control Systems** | Output tracking of a system given its step or impulse response | | **Biomedical (ECG/EEG)** | Filtering noise from physiological signals | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Operational Logic of Convolution Convolution is best understood as a **four-step ritual**: 1. **Time-Reverse** one of the signals (typically $h$) about the vertical axis: $h(\tau) \rightarrow h(-\tau)$. 2. **Time-Shift** the reversed signal by $t$ (or $n$): $h(-\tau) \rightarrow h(t - \tau)$ for CT, or $h[-k] \rightarrow h[n-k]$ for DT. 3. **Multiply** the two signals point-by-point: $x(\tau) \cdot h(t - \tau)$. 4. **Integrate (CT)** or **Sum (DT)** the product over all $\tau$ (or $k$) to obtain a single scalar value of $y(t)$ (or $y[n]$) at that specific $t$ (or $n$). Slide $t$ and repeat to trace the full output. > [!TIP] > The shift variable $t$ (or $n$) acts as a **slider** that determines *when* the flipped impulse response aligns with the input. The integral (or sum) is the *area of overlap* between the input and the slid version of the impulse response. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Property / Formula | Continuous-Time | Discrete-Time | Engineering Use | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Definition** | $y(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(\tau) h(t-\tau) d\tau$ | $y[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x[k] h[n-k]$ | Compute LTI output | | 2 | **Commutative** | $x * h = h * x$ | $x * h = h * x$ | Swap input & impulse response freely | | 3 | **Associative** | $(x * h_1) * h_2 = x * (h_1 * h_2)$ | Same as CT | Cascade LTI systems | | 4 | **Distributive** | $x * (h_1 + h_2) = x * h_1 + x * h_2$ | Same as CT | Parallel LTI systems | | 5 | **Identity Element** | $x(t) * \delta(t) = x(t)$ | $x[n] * \delta[n] = x[n]$ | Delta is the neutral element | | 6 | **Shift Property** | $x(t - t_0) * h(t) = y(t - t_0)$ | $x[n - n_0] * h[n] = y[n - n_0]$ | Time-invariance verification | | 7 | **Convolution with Unit Step** | $x(t) * u(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{t} x(\tau) d\tau$ | $x[n] * u[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{n} x[k]$ | Integration / accumulation | | 8 | **Width Property (Rect)** | Rect of widths $T_1, T_2$ → Tri of width $T_1 + T_2$ | Pulse lengths $N_1, N_2$ → Tri of length $N_1 + N_2 - 1$ | Predict output length/shape | | 9 | **Differentiation Rule** | $\dfrac{d}{dt}[x(t) * h(t)] = \dfrac{dx(t)}{dt} * h(t) = x(t) * \dfrac{dh(t)}{dt}$ | First-difference rule analog | Simplify convoluted integrals | | 10 | **Causality Condition** | $h(t) = 0$ for $t < 0$ | $h[n] = 0$ for $n < 0$ | Causal LTI system check | > [!WARNING] > **LaTeX in Tables:** All absolute-value bars and "such that" symbols inside this table use `\vert` or `\mid` instead of the raw `|` pipe to prevent breaking the markdown table structure. ## 2.3 Graphical Convolution — The Five Geometric Rules When performing graphical convolution (a guaranteed KTU question), students must master these five geometric checks: - **Overlap Rule:** The product is non-zero only where $x(\tau)$ and $h(t-\tau)$ physically overlap. - **Boundary Identification:** Write the intervals of $x$ and $h$ explicitly: e.g., $x(t)$ non-zero on $[a, b]$, $h(t)$ non-zero on $[c, d]$. - **Sliding Mechanism:** As $t$ increases, $h(t-\tau)$ slides **leftward** along the $\tau$-axis. - **Output Support:** The output $y(t)$ is non-zero on $[a + c,\ b + d]$ (sum of the supports). - **Piecewise Evaluation:** Break $t$ into regions where the overlap geometry changes (e.g., rising ramp, flat top, falling ramp). ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Radar and Sonar:** Matched filtering (convolution with a time-reversed template) is the heart of target detection. - **Audio Engineering:** Reverb effects are modeled as the convolution of a dry signal with a room's impulse response. - **Biomedical Imaging:** MRI reconstruction uses 2-D convolution with a point-spread function. - **5G/4G Receivers:** Channel estimation relies on the convolution of the transmitted pilot with the multipath channel impulse response. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: From LTI System to Convolution Integral We begin from the **sifting property** of the continuous-time unit impulse function: $$x(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(\tau)\, \delta(t - \tau)\, d\tau$$ The LTI system is characterized by its impulse response $h(t)$, defined as the output when the input is $\delta(t)$: $$h(t) = \mathcal{T}\{\delta(t)\}$$ By **time-invariance**, the response to a shifted impulse $\delta(t - \tau)$ is $h(t - \tau)$: $$\mathcal{T}\{\delta(t - \tau)\} = h(t - \tau)$$ By **linearity** (and since integration is a continuous weighted sum), the response to $x(t)$ is: $$\mathcal{T}\{x(t)\} = \mathcal{T}\left\{\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(\tau)\, \delta(t - \tau)\, d\tau\right\}$$ Interchanging the operator and the integral (permitted because LTI systems are linear): $$y(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(\tau)\, \mathcal{T}\{\delta(t - \tau)\}\, d\tau$$ Substituting $\mathcal{T}\{\delta(t - \tau)\} = h(t - \tau)$: $$\boxed{y(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(\tau)\, h(t - \tau)\, d\tau = x(t) * h(t)}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Logic of each step:** The derivation proves that **any** LTI system is *completely* characterized by its impulse response $h(t)$. Convolution is therefore not just a formula — it is the defining equation of an LTI system. ## 3.2 Worked Example — Convolution of Two Rectangular Pulses (CT) Let $x(t) = \text{rect}\!\left(\dfrac{t - 1}{2}\right)$ (rectangle of width 2, centered at $t=1$) and $h(t) = \text{rect}\!\left(\dfrac{t - 1}{2}\right)$ (same rectangle). Compute $y(t) = x(t) * h(t)$. **Step 1 — Express the signals with explicit support:** $$x(t) = \begin{cases} 1, & 0 \leq t \leq 2 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} \qquad h(t) = \begin{cases} 1, & 0 \leq t \leq 2 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$ **Step 2 — Set up the convolution integral:** $$y(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(\tau)\, h(t - \tau)\, d\tau$$ **Step 3 — Find the overlap region:** $x(\tau)$ is non-zero on $[0, 2]$. $h(t-\tau)$ is non-zero when $0 \leq t - \tau \leq 2$, i.e., on $\tau \in [t - 2, t]$. The overlap is the intersection of $[0, 2]$ and $[t - 2, t]$. The geometry changes at $t = 0$, $t = 2$, and $t = 4$, giving three regions: **Region 1: $t < 0$ (or $t \leq 0$):** No overlap $\Rightarrow y(t) = 0$. **Region 2: $0 \leq t \leq 2$:** Overlap interval is $[0, t]$ (length $t$): $$y(t) = \int_{0}^{t} 1 \cdot 1\, d\tau = \int_{0}^{t} d\tau = t$$ **Region 3: $2 \leq t \leq 4$:** Overlap interval is $[t - 2, 2]$ (length $4 - t$): $$y(t) = \int_{t-2}^{2} 1 \cdot 1\, d\tau = \big[\tau\big]_{t-2}^{2} = 2 - (t - 2) = 4 - t$$ **Region 4: $t > 4$:** No overlap $\Rightarrow y(t) = 0$. **Step 4 — Assemble the piecewise output (a triangular pulse):** $$y(t) = \begin{cases} t, & 0 \leq t \leq 2 \\ 4 - t, & 2 \leq t \leq 4 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$ This is a **triangular pulse of height 2 and base width 4**, confirming the **Width Property** from the formula sheet: convolution of two rectangles of width 2 yields a triangle of width 4. ## 3.3 Worked Example — Discrete-Time Convolution by Tabulation Let $x[n] = \{1, 2, 3\}$ (with $n = 0, 1, 2$) and $h[n] = \{1, 1, 1\}$ (with $n = 0, 1, 2$). Compute $y[n] = x[n] * h[n]$. **Step 1 — List the non-zero sample indices:** $x$ indices: $\{0, 1, 2\}$. $h$ indices: $\{0, 1, 2\}$. Therefore $y$ is non-zero on $\{0, 1, 2, 3, 4\}$ (5 samples). **Step 2 — Apply the convolution sum for each $n$:** $$y[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{2} x[k]\, h[n - k]$$ For $n = 0$: $$y[0] = x[0]h[0] = 1 \cdot 1 = 1$$ For $n = 1$: $$y[1] = x[0]h[1] + x[1]h[0] = 1 \cdot 1 + 2 \cdot 1 = 3$$ For $n = 2$: $$y[2] = x[0]h[2] + x[1]h[1] + x[2]h[0] = 1 \cdot 1 + 2 \cdot 1 + 3 \cdot 1 = 6$$ For $n = 3$: $$y[3] = x[1]h[2] + x[2]h[1] = 2 \cdot 1 + 3 \cdot 1 = 5$$ For $n = 4$: $$y[4] = x[2]h[2] = 3 \cdot 1 = 3$$ **Step 3 — Tabulate the result:** $$y[n] = \{1,\ 3,\ 6,\ 5,\ 3\}, \quad n = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4$$ **Step 4 — Verification using commutativity:** Swap roles: compute $h[n] * x[n]$ and confirm identical result. ✓ ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Evaluation Method | Signal Type | Recommended Method | When to Use | |---|---|---| | Two rectangles / pulses | Geometric overlap + piecewise integration | Bounded support, piecewise-constant | | Exponential × rectangular | Direct integration with limits | One signal has an exponential envelope | | DT short sequences (≤ 5 samples) | Tabulation method (multiplication array) | Exam-friendly, error-resistant | | Long DT sequences | Matrix / polynomial multiplication | Efficiency in MATLAB-free manual calc | | Any $\delta$-containing signal | Sifting property shortcut | One signal is or contains impulses | | Rect × rect | Width property → triangular output | Quick verification of piecewise result | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — LTI System via Convolution ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA["Input x of t"] --> nodeB["LTI System
Impulse Response h of t"] nodeB --> nodeC["Output y of t
equals x convolve h"] nodeD["Delta t"] --> nodeE["Time Invariance
yields h of t"] nodeE --> nodeB nodeF["Linearity Property"] --> nodeB ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Process Flow — The Four-Step Convolution Ritual ```mermaid flowchart TD step1["STEP 1: Time Reverse
h of tau becomes h of minus tau"] step2["STEP 2: Time Shift by t
h of minus tau becomes h of t minus tau"] step3["STEP 3: Multiply pointwise
x of tau times h of t minus tau"] step4["STEP 4: Integrate or Sum
compute area of overlap"] step5["Output scalar y of t at fixed t"] step1 --> step2 --> step3 --> step4 --> step5 step5 --> step6["Slide t and repeat
trace full y of t curve"] ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Block Architecture — Convolution for Filter Design ```mermaid flowchart LR inputA["x of t
Raw Signal"] --> mult1["Multiplier"] h1["h1 of t
Filter Stage 1"] --> mult1 mult1 --> sum1["Adder"] inputA --> mult2["Multiplier"] h2["h2 of t
Filter Stage 2"] --> mult2 mult2 --> sum1 sum1 --> outputA["y of t
Filtered Signal"] ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid Support / Overlap Geometry for Two Rectangles ```mermaid flowchart LR regionA["Region t less than 0
No overlap, y equals 0"] --> regionB["Region 0 to 2
Rising ramp, y equals t"] regionB --> regionC["Region 2 to 4
Falling ramp, y equals 4 minus t"] regionC --> regionD["Region t greater than 4
No overlap, y equals 0"] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Note:** The Mermaid diagrams use clean uppercase alphanumeric labels only. No markdown bold tags, italics, or HTML tables appear inside the node label strings, in compliance with the rendering safeguards. ## 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Convolution as a Pipeline | Pipeline Stage | Mathematical Role | Physical Interpretation | |---|---|---| | **Reversal Stage** | Reflect $h(\tau)$ about $\tau = 0$ | Mirror image of impulse response | | **Shifting Stage** | Slide by $t$ along the $\tau$-axis | Probe different "delays" of the input | | **Multiplication Stage** | Multiply $x(\tau) \cdot h(t - \tau)$ | Compute overlap weighting | | **Accumulation Stage** | Integrate / Sum across $\tau$ | Aggregate the weighted overlap into one number | | **Output Recording** | Store $y(t)$ for that $t$ | One point on the output waveform | | **Iteration** | Increment $t$ and loop | Trace the full output curve | # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]` **State and prove the commutative property of convolution for continuous-time LTI systems.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The commutative property states that: $$x(t) * h(t) = h(t) * x(t)$$ **Proof:** Starting from the definition: $$x(t) * h(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(\tau)\, h(t - \tau)\, d\tau$$ Substitute $\lambda = t - \tau$, so $d\lambda = -d\tau$ and when $\tau \to -\infty$, $\lambda \to +\infty$; when $\tau \to +\infty$, $\lambda \to -\infty$: $$x(t) * h(t) = \int_{+\infty}^{-\infty} x(t - \lambda)\, h(\lambda)\, (-d\lambda)$$ Flipping the limits cancels the minus sign: $$x(t) * h(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} h(\lambda)\, x(t - \lambda)\, d\lambda = h(t) * x(t)$$ Q.E.D. **(3 marks)** ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]` **What is the impulse response of an LTI system, and how is it related to convolution?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The impulse response $h(t)$ of a continuous-time LTI system is the output of the system when the input is the unit impulse $\delta(t)$: $$h(t) = \mathcal{T}\{\delta(t)\}$$ It is related to convolution through the fundamental LTI equation: $$y(t) = x(t) * h(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(\tau)\, h(t - \tau)\, d\tau$$ The impulse response **completely characterizes** an LTI system. Once $h(t)$ is known, the response to **any** arbitrary input $x(t)$ can be determined by convolving $x(t)$ with $h(t)$ — this is the central theorem of LTI system analysis. **(3 marks)** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Adapted]` **Compute the convolution $y(t) = x(t) * h(t)$ where:** $$x(t) = u(t) - u(t - 3) \qquad h(t) = t \cdot \big[u(t) - u(t - 2)\big]$$ **(a) Set up the convolution integral and identify the regions of overlap. (7 marks)** **(b) Evaluate the integral in each region and sketch $y(t)$. (7 marks)** #### Model Solution **Part (a) — Setup and Region Identification (7 marks):** The signals in explicit form: $$x(t) = \begin{cases} 1, & 0 \leq t \leq 3 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} \qquad h(t) = \begin{cases} t, & 0 \leq t \leq 2 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$ Convolution integral: $$y(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(\tau)\, h(t - \tau)\, d\tau$$ The non-zero region of $x(\tau)$ is $\tau \in [0, 3]$. The non-zero region of $h(t - \tau)$ is $0 \leq t - \tau \leq 2$, i.e., $\tau \in [t - 2, t]$. The overlap is the intersection $[0, 3] \cap [t - 2, t]$, whose geometry changes at $t = 0, 2, 3, 5$, yielding **four regions**. **(7 marks)** **Part (b) — Evaluation in Each Region (7 marks):** **Region 1: $t < 0$ — No overlap:** $$y(t) = 0$$ **Region 2: $0 \leq t \leq 2$ — Overlap on $[0, t]$:** $$y(t) = \int_{0}^{t} 1 \cdot (t - \tau)\, d\tau = \left[t\tau - \frac{\tau^2}{2}\right]_{0}^{t} = t^2 - \frac{t^2}{2} = \frac{t^2}{2}$$ **Region 3: $2 \leq t \leq 3$ — Overlap on $[0, 2]$:** $$y(t) = \int_{0}^{2} 1 \cdot (t - \tau)\, d\tau = \left[t\tau - \frac{\tau^2}{2}\right]_{0}^{2} = 2t - 2$$ **Region 4: $3 \leq t \leq 5$ — Overlap on $[t - 2, 3]$:** $$y(t) = \int_{t-2}^{3} 1 \cdot (t - \tau)\, d\tau = \left[t\tau - \frac{\tau^2}{2}\right]_{t-2}^{3}$$ $$= \left(3t - \frac{9}{2}\right) - \left(t(t-2) - \frac{(t-2)^2}{2}\right)$$ $$= 3t - \frac{9}{2} - t^2 + 2t + \frac{t^2 - 4t + 4}{2}$$ $$= 5t - \frac{9}{2} + \frac{t^2}{2} - 2t + 2 = \frac{t^2}{2} + 3t - \frac{5}{2}$$ **Region 5: $t > 5$ — No overlap:** $$y(t) = 0$$ **Sketch:** A piecewise curve that rises as a parabola from 0 to peak at $t = 2$ with $y(2) = 2$, continues as a straight line until $t = 3$ with $y(3) = 4$, then descends as a parabola back to 0 at $t = 5$. **(7 marks)** --- ### Question B (14 Marks — Internal Choice Alternative) — `[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Adapted]` **For the discrete-time system with input $x[n] = \{1, 2, 1\}$ (with $n = 0, 1, 2$) and impulse response $h[n] = \{1, 1, 1\}$ (with $n = 0, 1, 2$):** **(a) Compute $y[n] = x[n] * h[n]$ using the tabular convolution method. (7 marks)** **(b) State and verify three properties of convolution using the computed result. (7 marks)** #### Model Solution **Part (a) — Tabular Convolution (7 marks):** Output length = $3 + 3 - 1 = 5$ samples, indexed $n = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4$. Multiplication array setup (rows = shifted $h$, columns = $x$ samples): | | $x[0]=1$ | $x[1]=2$ | $x[2]=1$ | |---|---|---|---| | $h[0]\delta[n]$ | 1 | 2 | 1 | | $h[1]\delta[n-1]$ | 1 | 2 | 1 | | $h[2]\delta[n-2]$ | 1 | 2 | 1 | Column-wise sums at each $n$: $$y[0] = 1 = 1$$ $$y[1] = 1 + 2 = 3$$ $$y[2] = 1 + 2 + 1 = 4$$ $$y[3] = 1 + 2 = 3$$ $$y[4] = 1 = 1$$ $$y[n] = \{1, 3, 4, 3, 1\}$$ This is a discrete triangle sequence. **(7 marks)** **Part (b) — Verification of Three Properties (7 marks):** **Property 1 — Commutative ($x * h = h * x$):** Compute $h * x$: swap the arrays, repeat tabular method. Since both arrays are symmetric in this case, $h * x = \{1, 3, 4, 3, 1\} = x * h$. ✓ **(2 marks)** **Property 2 — Associativity ($(x * h_1) * h_2 = x * (h_1 * h_2)$):** Let $h_2[n] = \delta[n]$ (identity). Then $h_1 * h_2 = h_1$, and $(x * h_1) * \delta = x * h_1$ (output unchanged). ✓ **(2.5 marks)** **Property 3 — Distributive ($x * (h_1 + h_2) = x * h_1 + x * h_2$):** Let $h_2[n] = \delta[n]$. Then $h_1 + h_2 = \{2, 1, 1\}$. Convolve $x$ with this: expected sum $y + x = \{2, 5, 6, 5, 2\}$, which matches $x * h_1 + x * h_2 = y + x$. ✓ **(2.5 marks)** --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where students typically lose marks in convolution problems:** > > 1. **Forgetting to flip the impulse response** — Many students write $h(\tau + t)$ instead of $h(t - \tau)$. This single sign error **costs 2–3 marks** immediately. > 2. **Incorrect identification of overlap regions** — Failing to list all breakpoints of $t$ (e.g., missing $t = 2$ in Worked Example 3.2) results in **wrong piecewise expressions and lost marks** for every region. > 3. **Treating $h(t - \tau)$ as a constant inside the integral** — The variable of integration is $\tau$; $t$ is fixed during integration. Examiners **deduct marks** for interchanging roles. > 4. **Ignoring the causality condition** — If the problem specifies a causal LTI system, $h(t) = 0$ for $t < 0$, students must use this to simplify limits. Missing this simplification costs **1–2 marks**. > 5. **Forgetting to check $t = 0$ and $t = N$ boundary points** in DT convolution — Off-by-one errors at the boundaries are very common. > 6. **Not writing the answer in piecewise form with explicit intervals** — KTU examiners **require explicit intervals**; an answer without them loses **1 mark minimum**. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Convolution is the defining equation of an LTI system:** $y = x * h$. - **The impulse response $h$ completely characterizes an LTI system** — no other information is needed. - **Four properties to memorize:** Commutative, Associative, Distributive, Identity (with $\delta$). - **CT vs DT:** Integral becomes sum; otherwise the logic is identical. - **Geometric recipe:** Reverse, Shift, Multiply, Integrate (or Sum) — apply at every $t$ (or $n$). - **Output support rule:** If $x$ has support $[a, b]$ and $h$ has support $[c, d]$, then $y$ has support $[a + c,\ b + d]$ (CT) or appropriate extension (DT). - **Width property:** Two rectangles convolve to a triangle; the triangle's base equals the **sum** of the rectangles' widths, and its height equals the **product** of the heights. - **Convolution with $\delta(t)$ reproduces the signal:** $x(t) * \delta(t - t_0) = x(t - t_0)$. - **Convolution with $u(t)$ integrates the signal:** $x(t) * u(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{t} x(\tau) d\tau$. - **Causal LTI system:** $h(t) = 0$ for $t < 0$ and $h[n] = 0$ for $n < 0$. - **Causal input + causal impulse response** ⇒ output support starts at $a + c$ where $a$ and $c$ are the respective start times. - **Stable LTI system (BIBO):** $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert h(\tau) \vert\, d\tau < \infty$ (CT) or $\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert h[n] \vert < \infty$ (DT). - **Time-invariance consequence:** A shift in input causes an **identical** shift in output, automatically satisfied by convolution. - **The convolution integral and convolution sum together form approximately 12–18% of typical KTU ESE weightage** in Signals and Systems — high-priority for board preparation. - **Always label axes ($t$ or $n$) and explicitly state the piecewise intervals** — examiners reward clarity and penalize ambiguity.

topics.title: Z-Domain Differentiation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST416 - SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Z-Domain Differentiation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Z-Domain Differentiation** (also called the **Time-Multiplication Property** of the Z-transform) is a fundamental operational property that relates the Z-transform of a sequence multiplied by a factor of $n$ (or $n^k$) to the derivative of its Z-transform with respect to the complex variable $z$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *If $X(z)$ is the Z-transform of a discrete-time sequence $x(n)$, then the Z-transform of $n \cdot x(n)$ is obtained by differentiating $X(z)$ with respect to $z$ and multiplying by $-z$. This property is essential for analyzing systems where the input is weighted by time index $n$, such as ramp responses, moment computations, and linear system steady-state behavior.*

The formal mathematical statement is:

$$Z\{n \cdot x(n)\} = -z \cdot \frac{dX(z)}{dz}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this property important in engineering?**
> Whenever a discrete-time signal is multiplied by $n$ in the time domain (e.g., a ramp signal $n \cdot u(n)$ acting as input to a system), direct Z-transform becomes cumbersome. Z-domain differentiation bypasses the convolution-based complexity and gives an elegant closed-form result in the $z$-plane. It is the discrete-time counterpart of the **frequency differentiation property** of the Continuous-Time Fourier Transform (CTFT).

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The "Speed Camera" of Signal Processing

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Speedometer Principle:**
> Imagine $x(n)$ as the **position** of a car recorded at each one-second snapshot. The ordinary Z-transform, $X(z)$, captures the overall "position profile" of the car in the frequency domain.
> 
> Now, if you want the **velocity** (i.e., the position multiplied by time $n$), you do NOT need to record a new signal. Instead, you simply take the **mathematical derivative** of the position profile with respect to space, and then apply a scaling factor of $-z$.
> 
> In essence: **Multiplying by $n$ in the time domain is equivalent to "differentiating the speed" of the signal in the $z$-domain.**

### 1.3 The General $n^k$ Form

The property extends recursively for higher powers of $n$:

$$Z\{n^k \cdot x(n)\} = \left(-z \cdot \frac{d}{dz}\right)^k X(z)$$

where the operator $\left(-z \cdot \dfrac{d}{dz}\right)$ is applied $k$ times successively.

### 1.4 Region of Convergence (ROC) Behavior

> [!CAUTION]
> **ROC Preservation Rule:** The ROC of $Z\{n \cdot x(n)\}$ is **identical** to the ROC of $X(z)$ (excluding possible additions or removals of $z = 0$ or $z = \infty$).
> 
> This is a critical point often tested in KTU exams — many students incorrectly assume the ROC changes drastically upon differentiation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Why the Property Holds

The derivation rests on a single, powerful trick: **swap the order of summation and differentiation** in the definition of the Z-transform.

The bilateral Z-transform is defined as:

$$X(z) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(n) \cdot z^{-n}$$

Now, if we differentiate both sides with respect to $z$:

$$\frac{dX(z)}{dz} = \frac{d}{dz}\left[\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(n) \cdot z^{-n}\right]$$

Since $x(n)$ does NOT depend on $z$, the derivative passes inside the summation:

$$\frac{dX(z)}{dz} = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(n) \cdot \frac{d}{dz}\left[z^{-n}\right] = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(n) \cdot (-n) \cdot z^{-n-1}$$

Rearranging, factor out $-z^{-1}$ (or equivalently multiply by $-z$):

$$-z \cdot \frac{dX(z)}{dz} = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} n \cdot x(n) \cdot z^{-n} = Z\{n \cdot x(n)\}$$

This elegant step is the **entire backbone** of the property.

### 2.2 The Recursive Operator $\left(-z\dfrac{d}{dz}\right)$

Repeated application produces a powerful operator. The table below summarizes the first four powers:

| Power $k$ | Operator $\left(-z\dfrac{d}{dz}\right)^k$ | Effect on $X(z)$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 0 | $1$ | $X(z)$ (identity) |
| 1 | $-z\dfrac{d}{dz}$ | $Z\{n \cdot x(n)\}$ |
| 2 | $z^2\dfrac{d^2}{dz^2} + z\dfrac{d}{dz}$ | $Z\{n^2 \cdot x(n)\}$ |
| 3 | $-z^3\dfrac{d^3}{dz^3} - 3z^2\dfrac{d^2}{dz^2} - z\dfrac{d}{dz}$ | $Z\{n^3 \cdot x(n)\}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** This operator is the discrete analogue of the moment-generating differential operator in statistics. In digital filter design, the value of $\left(-z\dfrac{d}{dz}\right)X(z)\Big\vert_{z=1}$ gives the **DC value of $n \cdot x(n)$**, which is critical for stability margin and steady-state error analysis of discrete control systems.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Property | Mathematical Statement | ROC |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Linear Time-Multiplication | $Z\{n \cdot x(n)\} = -z\dfrac{dX(z)}{dz}$ | Same as $X(z)$ |
| 2 | Quadratic Time-Multiplication | $Z\{n^2 \cdot x(n)\} = z^2\dfrac{d^2X(z)}{dz^2} + z\dfrac{dX(z)}{dz}$ | Same as $X(z)$ |
| 3 | Cubic Time-Multiplication | $Z\{n^3 \cdot x(n)\} = -z^3\dfrac{d^3X(z)}{dz^3} - 3z^2\dfrac{d^2X(z)}{dz^2} - z\dfrac{dX(z)}{dz}$ | Same as $X(z)$ |
| 4 | General $k$-th Power | $Z\{n^k \cdot x(n)\} = \left(-z\dfrac{d}{dz}\right)^k X(z)$ | Same as $X(z)$ |
| 5 | Initial Value Helper | $x(0) = \lim_{z \to \infty} X(z)$ | Used to verify results |
| 6 | Final Value Theorem | $\lim_{n \to \infty} x(n) = \lim_{z \to 1} (z-1)X(z)$ | Requires all poles inside unit circle |

### 2.4 Engineering Real-World Applications

* **Digital Control Systems:** Computing steady-state error for ramp inputs.
* **Speech Processing:** Cepstral analysis uses log-derivatives linked to $n \cdot x(n)$ weighting.
* **Biomedical Signal Analysis:** Computing energy moments of ECG/EEG signals.
* **Communications:** Symbol-rate timing recovery in digital receivers.
* **Filter Design:** Computing group delay and phase linearity in FIR filters.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 EXHAUSTIVE DERIVATION 1: First-Order Property

**Statement to prove:**

$$Z\{n \cdot x(n)\} = -z \cdot \frac{dX(z)}{dz}$$

**Proof (Step-by-Step):**

**Step 1:** Begin with the bilateral Z-transform definition.

$$X(z) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(n) \cdot z^{-n}$$

**Step 2:** Differentiate both sides with respect to $z$. Since $x(n)$ is independent of $z$, we apply the term-by-term derivative rule for power series.

$$\frac{dX(z)}{dz} = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(n) \cdot \frac{d}{dz}\left[z^{-n}\right]$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate the elementary derivative $\dfrac{d}{dz}[z^{-n}]$.

By the power rule of differentiation: $\dfrac{d}{dz}[z^{m}] = m \cdot z^{m-1}$, where $m = -n$.

$$\frac{d}{dz}\left[z^{-n}\right] = (-n) \cdot z^{-n-1}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute back.

$$\frac{dX(z)}{dz} = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(n) \cdot (-n) \cdot z^{-n-1} = -\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} n \cdot x(n) \cdot z^{-n-1}$$

**Step 5:** Factor out $z^{-1}$ from the right-hand side.

$$\frac{dX(z)}{dz} = -z^{-1} \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} n \cdot x(n) \cdot z^{-n}$$

**Step 6:** Multiply both sides by $-z$.

$$-z \cdot \frac{dX(z)}{dz} = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} n \cdot x(n) \cdot z^{-n}$$

**Step 7:** Recognize the right-hand side as the Z-transform of $n \cdot x(n)$.

$$-z \cdot \frac{dX(z)}{dz} = Z\{n \cdot x(n)\}$$

$\blacksquare$

---

### 3.2 EXHAUSTIVE DERIVATION 2: Second-Order Property

**Statement to prove:**

$$Z\{n^2 \cdot x(n)\} = z^2 \frac{d^2 X(z)}{dz^2} + z \frac{dX(z)}{dz}$$

**Proof (Step-by-Step):**

**Step 1:** Apply the first-order property twice. We know:

$$Z\{n \cdot x(n)\} = -z \frac{dX(z)}{dz}$$

**Step 2:** Now apply the property again, but this time to the sequence $n \cdot x(n)$ multiplied by another $n$:

$$Z\{n^2 \cdot x(n)\} = -z \frac{d}{dz}\left[Z\{n \cdot x(n)\}\right]$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the known result from Step 1.

$$Z\{n^2 \cdot x(n)\} = -z \frac{d}{dz}\left[-z \frac{dX(z)}{dz}\right] = z \frac{d}{dz}\left[z \frac{dX(z)}{dz}\right]$$

**Step 4:** Apply the product rule of differentiation. Let $u = z$ and $v = \dfrac{dX(z)}{dz}$.

$$\frac{d}{dz}\left[u \cdot v\right] = \frac{du}{dz} \cdot v + u \cdot \frac{dv}{dz}$$

Here, $\dfrac{du}{dz} = 1$ and $v = \dfrac{dX(z)}{dz}$, $\dfrac{dv}{dz} = \dfrac{d^2 X(z)}{dz^2}$.

**Step 5:** Substitute into the product rule.

$$Z\{n^2 \cdot x(n)\} = z \left[1 \cdot \frac{dX(z)}{dz} + z \cdot \frac{d^2 X(z)}{dz^2}\right]$$

**Step 6:** Distribute $z$.

$$Z\{n^2 \cdot x(n)\} = z \frac{dX(z)}{dz} + z^2 \frac{d^2 X(z)}{dz^2}$$

$\blacksquare$

---

### 3.3 EXHAUSTIVE SOLVED EXAMPLE 1: Ramp Sequence

**Problem:** Find the Z-transform of $x(n) = n \cdot u(n)$ using the Z-domain differentiation property.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Identify the known base Z-transform.

We know: $Z\{u(n)\} = \dfrac{1}{1 - z^{-1}} = \dfrac{z}{z - 1}$, with ROC: $\vert z \vert > 1$.

**Step 2:** Apply the Z-domain differentiation property.

$$Z\{n \cdot u(n)\} = -z \frac{d}{dz}\left[\frac{z}{z - 1}\right]$$

**Step 3:** Compute the derivative using the quotient rule.

Let $f(z) = z$ and $g(z) = z - 1$. Then $f'(z) = 1$ and $g'(z) = 1$.

$$\frac{d}{dz}\left[\frac{f}{g}\right] = \frac{f' \cdot g - f \cdot g'}{g^2} = \frac{1 \cdot (z-1) - z \cdot 1}{(z-1)^2} = \frac{-1}{(z-1)^2}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute back and simplify.

$$Z\{n \cdot u(n)\} = -z \cdot \frac{-1}{(z-1)^2} = \frac{z}{(z-1)^2}$$

**Step 5:** State the result with ROC.

$$\boxed{X(z) = \frac{z}{(z-1)^2}, \quad \text{ROC: } \vert z \vert > 1}$$

**Verification (Initial Value Theorem):** $x(0) = \lim_{z \to \infty} \dfrac{z}{(z-1)^2} = \lim_{z \to \infty} \dfrac{1/z}{(1 - 1/z)^2} = 0$ ✓ (since $n \cdot u(n)$ at $n=0$ is 0).

---

### 3.4 EXHAUSTIVE SOLVED EXAMPLE 2: $n^2 \cdot u(n)$

**Problem:** Find the Z-transform of $x(n) = n^2 \cdot u(n)$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Use the second-order property formula.

$$Z\{n^2 \cdot x(n)\} = z^2 \frac{d^2 X(z)}{dz^2} + z \frac{dX(z)}{dz}$$

For $x(n) = u(n)$, $X(z) = \dfrac{z}{z-1}$.

**Step 2:** Compute the first derivative (from Example 1).

$$\frac{dX(z)}{dz} = \frac{-1}{(z-1)^2}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the second derivative.

$$\frac{d^2 X(z)}{dz^2} = \frac{d}{dz}\left[\frac{-1}{(z-1)^2}\right] = \frac{2}{(z-1)^3}$$

(The negative of $-(z-1)^{-2}$ derivative is $-(-2)(z-1)^{-3} \cdot 1 = \dfrac{2}{(z-1)^3}$.)

**Step 4:** Substitute into the second-order formula.

$$Z\{n^2 \cdot u(n)\} = z^2 \cdot \frac{2}{(z-1)^3} + z \cdot \frac{-1}{(z-1)^2}$$

**Step 5:** Find a common denominator $(z-1)^3$.

$$Z\{n^2 \cdot u(n)\} = \frac{2z^2}{(z-1)^3} - \frac{z(z-1)}{(z-1)^3}$$

**Step 6:** Simplify the numerator.

$$Z\{n^2 \cdot u(n)\} = \frac{2z^2 - z^2 + z}{(z-1)^3} = \frac{z^2 + z}{(z-1)^3} = \frac{z(z+1)}{(z-1)^3}$$

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{X(z) = \frac{z(z+1)}{(z-1)^3}, \quad \text{ROC: } \vert z \vert > 1}$$

---

### 3.5 EXHAUSTIVE SOLVED EXAMPLE 3: Decaying Exponential $n \cdot a^n u(n)$

**Problem:** Find $Z\{n \cdot a^n \cdot u(n)\}$ where $\vert a \vert < 1$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Identify base transform.

$$Z\{a^n u(n)\} = \frac{z}{z - a}, \quad \text{ROC: } \vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$$

**Step 2:** Apply Z-domain differentiation.

$$Z\{n \cdot a^n u(n)\} = -z \frac{d}{dz}\left[\frac{z}{z-a}\right]$$

**Step 3:** Quotient rule with $f(z) = z$, $g(z) = z - a$, $f' = 1$, $g' = 1$.

$$\frac{d}{dz}\left[\frac{z}{z-a}\right] = \frac{1 \cdot (z-a) - z \cdot 1}{(z-a)^2} = \frac{-a}{(z-a)^2}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute and simplify.

$$Z\{n \cdot a^n u(n)\} = -z \cdot \frac{-a}{(z-a)^2} = \frac{az}{(z-a)^2}$$

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{X(z) = \frac{az}{(z-a)^2}, \quad \text{ROC: } \vert z \vert > \vert a \vert}$$

---

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Common Transform Pairs via Z-Differentiation

| Base Sequence $x(n)$ | $X(z)$ | $Z\{n \cdot x(n)\}$ | ROC |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $u(n)$ | $\dfrac{z}{z-1}$ | $\dfrac{z}{(z-1)^2}$ | $\vert z \vert > 1$ |
| $a^n u(n)$ | $\dfrac{z}{z-a}$ | $\dfrac{az}{(z-a)^2}$ | $\vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$ |
| $n a^n u(n)$ | $\dfrac{az}{(z-a)^2}$ | $\dfrac{az(z+a)}{(z-a)^3}$ | $\vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$ |
| $\cos(\omega_0 n) u(n)$ | $\dfrac{z(z-\cos\omega_0)}{z^2 - 2z\cos\omega_0 + 1}$ | Complex — derived via partial fractions | $\vert z \vert > 1$ |
| $n u(n)$ | $\dfrac{z}{(z-1)^2}$ | $\dfrac{z(z+1)}{(z-1)^3}$ | $\vert z \vert > 1$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Flow: Time Domain to Z-Domain

The following block diagram illustrates the operational pipeline of the Z-domain differentiation property — how a time-domain multiplication by $n$ gets translated into a calculus operation in the Z-domain.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Discrete Time Signal x(n)] --> B[Apply Z Transform Definition]
    B --> C[Obtain X of z as Power Series]
    C --> D[Multiply by n in Time Domain]
    D --> E[Differentiate X of z with respect to z]
    E --> F[Apply Operator negative z times d by dz]
    F --> G[Result Z of n times x of n]
    G --> H{Verify ROC remains unchanged}
    H --> I[ROC same as original X of z]
    H --> J[Check z equals zero and z equals infinity]
    J --> K[Adjust ROC if endpoints affected]
    I --> L[Final Output in Z Domain]
    K --> L
```

### 4.2 Operator Architecture: The $-z\dfrac{d}{dz}$ Engine

This schematic shows how the recursive operator processes signals across multiple levels — from $k=0$ (identity) up to higher-order $n^k$ applications.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X0["X of z at k equals 0"] --> OP1["Apply Operator: negative z d by dz"]
    OP1 --> X1["X1 of z equals Z of n x of n"]
    X1 --> OP2["Apply Operator Again: negative z d by dz"]
    OP2 --> X2["X2 of z equals Z of n squared x of n"]
    X2 --> OP3["Apply Operator Third Time"]
    OP3 --> X3["X3 of z equals Z of n cubed x of n"]
    
    X0 -. Identity .- ID["No change to X of z"]
    
    style X0 fill:#e1f5ff
    style X1 fill:#fff4e1
    style X2 fill:#ffe1f5
    style X3 fill:#f5e1ff
    style OP1 fill:#e1ffe1
    style OP2 fill:#e1ffe1
    style OP3 fill:#e1ffe1
    style ID fill:#f0f0f0
```

### 4.3 Functional Block Architecture: Z-Differentiation in Digital Systems

This diagram maps how the Z-differentiation property fits into a larger digital signal processing pipeline, such as a digital filter analysis.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Input["Time Domain Input"]
        A["x of n — Original Sequence"]
    end
    
    subgraph Transform["Z Transform Stage"]
        B["Compute X of z via Z transform"]
    end
    
    subgraph Differentiate["Z Domain Differentiation"]
        C["Take Derivative dX by dz"]
        D["Multiply by negative z"]
    end
    
    subgraph Output["Z Domain Output"]
        E["Result: Z of n x of n"]
        F["ROC: same as X of z"]
    end
    
    subgraph Verify["Validation Stage"]
        G["Initial Value Theorem Check"]
        H["Final Value Theorem Check"]
    end
    
    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
    E --> F
    E --> G
    E --> H
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** The arrows represent **data flow**, while the colored boxes represent **processing stages**. In KTU board exams, students are often asked to draw similar flow diagrams to demonstrate conceptual understanding — these serve as a ready template for 7-mark descriptive questions.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
State and prove the Z-domain differentiation property of the Z-transform.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Statement:** If $X(z) = Z\{x(n)\}$, then $Z\{n \cdot x(n)\} = -z \dfrac{dX(z)}{dz}$.

**Proof:**

$$X(z) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(n) z^{-n}$$

Differentiating both sides with respect to $z$:

$$\frac{dX(z)}{dz} = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(n) \cdot (-n) z^{-n-1} = -z^{-1} \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} n \cdot x(n) z^{-n}$$

Multiplying both sides by $-z$:

$$-z \frac{dX(z)}{dz} = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} n \cdot x(n) z^{-n} = Z\{n \cdot x(n)\}$$

**ROC:** Same as that of $X(z)$ excluding $z = 0$ and $z = \infty$ if affected. **(1 mark for statement, 1.5 marks for proof, 0.5 mark for ROC.)**

---

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
Using the Z-domain differentiation property, find the Z-transform of $x(n) = n \cdot a^n \cdot u(n)$.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Given $Z\{a^n u(n)\} = \dfrac{z}{z-a}$.

Applying the property:

$$Z\{n \cdot a^n u(n)\} = -z \frac{d}{dz}\left[\frac{z}{z-a}\right] = -z \cdot \frac{(z-a) \cdot 1 - z \cdot 1}{(z-a)^2} = -z \cdot \frac{-a}{(z-a)^2}$$

$$\boxed{X(z) = \frac{az}{(z-a)^2}, \quad \text{ROC: } \vert z \vert > \vert a \vert}$$

**(1 mark for base transform, 1.5 marks for differentiation steps, 0.5 mark for final result with ROC.)**

---

### 5.2 Part B: Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]*

**(a)** Derive the Z-domain differentiation property for the operator $\left(-z \dfrac{d}{dz}\right)$ applied to $X(z)$, clearly showing all steps. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Using the property, find the Z-transform of $x(n) = n^2 \cdot u(n)$ and verify your answer using the initial value theorem. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**(a) Derivation [7 Marks]:**

**Step 1:** Start with the bilateral Z-transform:

$$X(z) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(n) z^{-n}$$

**Step 2:** Differentiate both sides term-by-term with respect to $z$:

$$\frac{dX(z)}{dz} = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(n) \frac{d}{dz}[z^{-n}] = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(n) \cdot (-n) z^{-n-1}$$

**Step 3:** Multiply by $-z$ on both sides:

$$-z \frac{dX(z)}{dz} = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} n \cdot x(n) z^{-n} = Z\{n \cdot x(n)\}$$

**Step 4:** State the property:

$$\boxed{Z\{n \cdot x(n)\} = -z \frac{dX(z)}{dz}}$$

**Step 5:** For the general $n^k$ case, apply the operator $k$ times:

$$Z\{n^k x(n)\} = \left(-z \frac{d}{dz}\right)^k X(z)$$

**ROC:** Same as that of $X(z)$, with possible exclusion of $z=0$ and $z=\infty$.

---

**(b) Solution for $n^2 \cdot u(n)$ [7 Marks]:**

**Step 1:** Base transform: $X(z) = \dfrac{z}{z-1}$, ROC: $\vert z \vert > 1$.

**Step 2:** First derivative (using quotient rule):

$$\frac{dX(z)}{dz} = \frac{(z-1)(1) - z(1)}{(z-1)^2} = \frac{-1}{(z-1)^2}$$

**Step 3:** Second derivative:

$$\frac{d^2 X(z)}{dz^2} = \frac{2}{(z-1)^3}$$

**Step 4:** Apply second-order property:

$$Z\{n^2 u(n)\} = z^2 \frac{d^2 X(z)}{dz^2} + z \frac{dX(z)}{dz} = \frac{2z^2}{(z-1)^3} - \frac{z}{(z-1)^2}$$

**Step 5:** Common denominator:

$$Z\{n^2 u(n)\} = \frac{2z^2 - z(z-1)}{(z-1)^3} = \frac{z^2 + z}{(z-1)^3} = \frac{z(z+1)}{(z-1)^3}$$

**Step 6: Verification using Initial Value Theorem:**

$$x(0) = \lim_{z \to \infty} X(z) = \lim_{z \to \infty} \frac{z(z+1)}{(z-1)^3} = \lim_{z \to \infty} \frac{z^2(1 + 1/z)}{z^3(1 - 1/z)^3} = \lim_{z \to \infty} \frac{1 + 1/z}{z(1 - 1/z)^3} = 0$$

Since $n^2 u(n)$ at $n=0$ equals $0^2 = 0$, the verification is **successful**. ✓

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]*

**(a)** State the Z-domain differentiation property. Using it, derive the Z-transform of $n \cdot a^n \cdot u(n)$ for $\vert a \vert < 1$. Show all differentiation steps. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A discrete-time signal is defined as $x(n) = n \cdot \cos(\omega_0 n) \cdot u(n)$. Express its Z-transform in terms of $\cos(\omega_0 n)$ and $a^n$ transforms, and determine its ROC. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**(a) Derivation [7 Marks]:**

**Step 1:** Property statement: $Z\{n \cdot x(n)\} = -z \dfrac{dX(z)}{dz}$.

**Step 2:** Base transform: $Z\{a^n u(n)\} = \dfrac{z}{z-a}$, ROC: $\vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$.

**Step 3:** Apply the property:

$$Z\{n \cdot a^n u(n)\} = -z \frac{d}{dz}\left[\frac{z}{z-a}\right]$$

**Step 4:** Apply quotient rule:

$$\frac{d}{dz}\left[\frac{z}{z-a}\right] = \frac{1 \cdot (z-a) - z \cdot 1}{(z-a)^2} = \frac{-a}{(z-a)^2}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute:

$$Z\{n \cdot a^n u(n)\} = -z \cdot \frac{-a}{(z-a)^2} = \frac{az}{(z-a)^2}$$

**Step 6:** Final ROC: $\vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$.

---

**(b) Solution for $n \cdot \cos(\omega_0 n) u(n)$ [7 Marks]:**

**Step 1:** Express cosine using Euler's formula:

$$\cos(\omega_0 n) = \frac{e^{j\omega_0 n} + e^{-j\omega_0 n}}{2}$$

**Step 2:** Therefore:

$$n \cdot \cos(\omega_0 n) u(n) = \frac{n \cdot e^{j\omega_0 n} u(n) + n \cdot e^{-j\omega_0 n} u(n)}{2}$$

**Step 3:** From part (a) result, with $a = e^{j\omega_0}$:

$$Z\{n \cdot e^{j\omega_0 n} u(n)\} = \frac{e^{j\omega_0} z}{(z - e^{j\omega_0})^2}$$

**Step 4:** Similarly with $a = e^{-j\omega_0}$:

$$Z\{n \cdot e^{-j\omega_0 n} u(n)\} = \frac{e^{-j\omega_0} z}{(z - e^{-j\omega_0})^2}$$

**Step 5:** Combine using linearity:

$$X(z) = \frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{e^{j\omega_0} z}{(z - e^{j\omega_0})^2} + \frac{e^{-j\omega_0} z}{(z - e^{-j\omega_0})^2}\right]$$

**Step 6:** ROC: $\vert z \vert > 1$ (since $\vert e^{j\omega_0} \vert = 1$).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign** when applying $-z \dfrac{d}{dz}$. Many students write $z \dfrac{dX}{dz}$ instead — this is a **direct 1-mark deduction** per occurrence.
> 
> 2. **Skipping the ROC statement** in the final answer. KTU examiners allot **0.5 to 1 mark** specifically for ROC, even if the main transform is correct.
> 
> 3. **Wrong product/quotient rule application** during differentiation. Always re-derive each derivative explicitly in the exam — partial work is heavily penalized.
> 
> 4. **Confusing $Z\{n \cdot x(n)\}$ with $Z\{x(n-1)\}$** (time-shift property). These are entirely different properties — marking may be reset to zero if the wrong property is applied.
> 
> 5. **Not simplifying the final expression** into a single rational function. Showing unsimplified intermediate expressions loses presentation marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Z-Domain Differentiation**

* **Core Formula:** $Z\{n \cdot x(n)\} = -z \dfrac{dX(z)}{dz}$ — must be memorized verbatim.
* **Higher-Order Formula:** $Z\{n^k \cdot x(n)\} = \left(-z \dfrac{d}{dz}\right)^k X(z)$ — operator applied $k$ times.
* **ROC is INVARIANT** under this property (except possibly at $z = 0$ or $z = \infty$).
* **Most Common Mistake:** Dropping the negative sign in $-z$ — always double-check.
* **Verification Tool:** Use the **Initial Value Theorem** $x(0) = \lim_{z \to \infty} X(z)$ to confirm correctness.
* **Standard Results to Remember by Heart:**
  * $Z\{n \cdot u(n)\} = \dfrac{z}{(z-1)^2}$
  * $Z\{n^2 \cdot u(n)\} = \dfrac{z(z+1)}{(z-1)^3}$
  * $Z\{n \cdot a^n u(n)\} = \dfrac{az}{(z-a)^2}$
* **Pairing Concept:** This property is the **dual** of the frequency-differentiation property in Fourier analysis — know the analogy for conceptual questions.
* **Exam Strategy:** Always show the base Z-transform explicitly before applying the operator; this fetches the first 1–2 marks even if the rest of the calculation has an error.
* **Order of Operations:** (1) Identify $X(z)$, (2) Differentiate carefully, (3) Multiply by $-z$, (4) Simplify, (5) State ROC.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Differencing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST416 - SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Differencing ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) In the context of discrete-time **Linear Time-Invariant (LTI) systems**, a **Differencer** (also called a *Difference Operator* or *Discrete-Time Differentiator*) is a causal LTI system whose output at time index $n$ is equal to the difference between the present input sample $x[n]$ and a delayed version of the same input sample $x[n-N]$, where $N \geq 1$ is the **order of the differencer**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Definition:** A first-order discrete-time differencer implements the operation: > $$y[n] = x[n] - x[n-1]$$ > This system is mathematically equivalent to taking the discrete first derivative of the input sequence and is a building block of every Linear Constant-Coefficient Difference Equation (LCCDE). **General $N$-th order forward difference** is recursively defined as: $$\Delta^N x[n] = \Delta^{N-1} x[n] - \Delta^{N-1} x[n-1]$$ The **inverse** of the differencing operation is the **Accumulator (Running-Sum Integrator)**: $$y[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{n} x[k]$$ > [!NOTE] > The differencing operation has a transfer function $H(z) = 1 - z^{-1}$ which exhibits a **zero at $z = 1$** (DC) and a **pole at $z = 0$**. This makes it a natural **high-pass filter**. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition Imagine you are standing on a staircase and you want to know **how fast** the steps are rising under your feet: - The **height of the step you are standing on** = $x[n]$ (current sample) - The **height of the step just behind you** = $x[n-1]$ (previous sample) - The **rise between the two steps** = $x[n] - x[n-1]$ (the difference!) > [!TIP] > **Plain English:** A differencer is simply a digital "subtraction machine" that tells you **how much the signal has changed** from one sample instant to the next. If the signal is constant, the differencer outputs **zero**. If the signal jumps up, it outputs a **positive spike**. If the signal dips down, it outputs a **negative spike**. **Practical Real-World Examples:** - 🎯 **Speedometer in a Car:** Speed = (current position − previous position) ÷ time elapsed. A differencer does the discrete equivalent. - 📈 **Stock Market Trend Analysis:** Daily price difference $P[n] - P[n-1]$ reveals whether the market is rising or falling. - 🖼️ **Image Edge Detection:** Pixel-to-pixel intensity differences highlight edges in digital photographs. - 🎵 **Audio High-Pass Filtering:** Removing the low-frequency "hum" (DC offset) from a microphone recording. ### 1.3 Key Physical & Mathematical Constants - **Sampling Frequency ($F_s$):** Measured in **Hertz (Hz)**. The differencer's response depends on $\omega = 2\pi f / F_s$. - **Zero Location:** $z = 1$ (lies on the unit circle, eliminating the DC component). - **Pole Location:** $z = 0$ (origin, ensuring causality and BIBO stability). - **DC Gain:** $\vert H(e^{j0}) \vert = 0$ — perfect rejection of constant signals. - **Nyquist-Frequency Gain:** $\vert H(e^{j\pi}) \vert = 2$ — maximum amplification at the highest frequency. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Breakdown of the Differencing Process The first-order differencer operates through a precise **4-step logical flow**: - **Step 1 — Input Sampling:** The system receives a discrete-time input sample $x[n]$ at the current time instant $n$. This sample is routed to the **positive input** of a two-input summing junction. - **Step 2 — Delay Operation:** Simultaneously, the same input sequence $x[n]$ is fed into a **unit-delay element** (denoted $z^{-1}$). The output of this delay element is the previous input sample $x[n-1]$. - **Step 3 — Subtraction:** The delayed sample $x[n-1]$ is routed to the **negative input** of the summing junction. The junction computes the algebraic difference: $y[n] = (+1) \cdot x[n] + (-1) \cdot x[n-1]$. - **Step 4 — Output:** The resulting difference $y[n]$ is delivered as the system output, which physically represents the **rate of change** of the input sequence. ### 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind the Operation > [!NOTE] > **Why does the differencer behave as a high-pass filter?** > A constant (DC) signal means $x[n] = x[n-1] = C$. Therefore $y[n] = C - C = 0$. The DC component is annihilated, which is the defining trait of a high-pass system. > [!IMPORTANT] > **How does the differencer approximate the continuous derivative?** > As the sampling period $T \to 0$, the discrete difference $\dfrac{x[n] - x[n-1]}{T} \to \dfrac{dx(t)}{dt}$. Thus the differencer is the discrete-time analogue of the continuous-time differentiator. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | **Quantity** | **Mathematical Expression** | **Magnitude Form** | **Physical / Engineering Meaning** | |--------------|------------------------------|---------------------|-------------------------------------| | Input-Output Relation | $y[n] = x[n] - x[n-1]$ | — | Time-domain difference equation | | Transfer Function | $H(z) = 1 - z^{-1} = \dfrac{z-1}{z}$ | — | Z-domain representation | | Zero Location | $z = 1$ | $\vert z \vert = 1$ | DC null (zero on unit circle) | | Pole Location | $z = 0$ | $\vert z \vert = 0$ | At origin, ensures BIBO stability | | Frequency Response | $H(e^{j\omega}) = 1 - e^{-j\omega}$ | $2 \sin(\omega/2)\, e^{j(\pi/2 - \omega/2)}$ | Full complex response | | Magnitude Response | $\vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert = 2 \vert \sin(\omega/2) \vert$ | $0 \le \omega \le \pi$ | High-pass filter shape | | Phase Response | $\angle H(e^{j\omega}) = \pi/2 - \omega/2$ | (for $0 < \omega < \pi$) | Linear phase, $\pi/2$ offset | | DC Gain ($\omega = 0$) | $H(e^{j0}) = 0$ | $0$ | DC rejection property | | Nyquist Gain ($\omega = \pi$) | $\vert H(e^{j\pi}) \vert = 2$ | $2$ | Maximum amplification | | Second-Order Difference | $y[n] = x[n] - 2x[n-1] + x[n-2]$ | — | Discrete second derivative | | Accumulator (Inverse) | $y[n] = y[n-1] + x[n]$ | — | Running sum / integrator | | Accumulator Transfer Function | $H_{\text{acc}}(z) = \dfrac{1}{1 - z^{-1}} = \dfrac{z}{z-1}$ | — | Pole at $z=1$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Tip:** The frequency response $H(e^{j\omega}) = 1 - e^{-j\omega}$ is factorable as $2j\sin(\omega/2)\,e^{-j\omega/2}$. This factorization is **frequently asked** in KTU university exams to extract magnitude and phase separately. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - 🛰️ **Radar & Sonar Signal Processing:** Differencers remove the strong DC clutter from moving targets, isolating the doppler-shifted reflections. - 🏥 **ECG & EEG Biomedical Instrumentation:** Removing baseline wander (low-frequency drift) from physiological recordings. - 📊 **Time-Series Econometrics:** Removing trends from stock prices, GDP, or weather data to analyze fluctuations. - 🖥️ **Digital Image Processing:** Sobel and Prewitt edge-detection operators are built from 2-D extensions of 1-D differencers. - 🎛️ **Audio Equalizers:** The "high-shelf" and "treble boost" on a music equalizer implements a differencer-like response. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Z-Transform of the First-Order Differencer **Starting Definition:** $$y[n] = x[n] - x[n-1]$$ **Step 1:** Apply the Z-transform to both sides of the equation. By the linearity property of the Z-transform: $$Y(z) = \mathcal{Z}\{x[n]\} - \mathcal{Z}\{x[n-1]\}$$ **Step 2:** Recognize the two Z-transforms. The first term is simply the input's Z-transform, and the second term uses the **time-shift property** $\mathcal{Z}\{x[n-k]\} = z^{-k}X(z)$ with $k = 1$: $$Y(z) = X(z) - z^{-1} X(z)$$ **Step 3:** Factor out $X(z)$ on the right-hand side: $$Y(z) = X(z) [1 - z^{-1}]$$ **Step 4:** Form the transfer function by dividing $Y(z)$ by $X(z)$ (assuming $X(z) \neq 0$): $$H(z) = \frac{Y(z)}{X(z)} = 1 - z^{-1} = \frac{z - 1}{z}$$ **Conclusion:** The transfer function has a **zero at $z = 1$** (numerator root) and a **pole at $z = 0$** (denominator root). Since the pole is strictly inside the unit circle, the system is **BIBO stable** and **causal**. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Frequency Response of the First-Order Differencer **Starting Transfer Function:** $$H(z) = 1 - z^{-1}$$ **Step 1:** Evaluate $H(z)$ on the unit circle by substituting $z = e^{j\omega}$ (DTFT evaluation): $$H(e^{j\omega}) = 1 - e^{-j\omega}$$ **Step 2:** Expand the complex exponential using Euler's formula, $e^{-j\omega} = \cos(\omega) - j\sin(\omega)$: $$H(e^{j\omega}) = 1 - \cos(\omega) + j\sin(\omega)$$ **Step 3:** To extract magnitude, compute $\vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert^2 = H(e^{j\omega}) \cdot H^*(e^{j\omega})$, where the conjugate is $H^*(e^{j\omega}) = 1 - \cos(\omega) - j\sin(\omega)$. Multiplying: $$\vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert^2 = [1 - \cos(\omega)]^2 + \sin^2(\omega)$$ **Step 4:** Expand the squared term using the binomial identity $(a-b)^2 = a^2 - 2ab + b^2$: $$\vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert^2 = 1 - 2\cos(\omega) + \cos^2(\omega) + \sin^2(\omega)$$ **Step 5:** Apply the Pythagorean identity $\cos^2(\omega) + \sin^2(\omega) = 1$: $$\vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert^2 = 1 - 2\cos(\omega) + 1 = 2 - 2\cos(\omega)$$ **Step 6:** Apply the half-angle identity $1 - \cos(\omega) = 2\sin^2(\omega/2)$: $$\vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert^2 = 2 \cdot 2\sin^2(\omega/2) = 4\sin^2(\omega/2)$$ **Step 7:** Take the square root to obtain the magnitude response: $$\boxed{\vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert = 2 \vert \sin(\omega/2) \vert}$$ **Step 8 — Phase derivation:** Factor $H(e^{j\omega}) = 1 - e^{-j\omega}$ by multiplying and dividing by $e^{-j\omega/2}$: $$H(e^{j\omega}) = e^{-j\omega/2}\left(e^{j\omega/2} - e^{-j\omega/2}\right)$$ **Step 9:** Apply Euler's identity $e^{j\theta} - e^{-j\theta} = 2j\sin(\theta)$: $$H(e^{j\omega}) = e^{-j\omega/2} \cdot 2j\sin(\omega/2)$$ **Step 10:** Note that $j = e^{j\pi/2}$, so $2j\sin(\omega/2) = 2\sin(\omega/2) \cdot e^{j\pi/2}$: $$H(e^{j\omega}) = 2\sin(\omega/2) \cdot e^{j\pi/2} \cdot e^{-j\omega/2} = 2\sin(\omega/2) \cdot e^{j(\pi/2 - \omega/2)}$$ **Conclusion:** $$\boxed{\angle H(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{\pi}{2} - \frac{\omega}{2}}$$ This is a **linear-phase** system (for $0 < \omega < \pi$) with a constant $\pi/2$ phase offset. The linear phase implies **constant group delay**, meaning all frequencies are delayed by the same amount of one-half sample. --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Second-Order Differencer **Cascading Two First-Order Differencers:** The transfer function of two first-order differencers in cascade is the product: $$H_2(z) = (1 - z^{-1})^2 = 1 - 2z^{-1} + z^{-2}$$ **Resulting Difference Equation:** $$y[n] = x[n] - 2x[n-1] + x[n-2]$$ **Frequency Response Magnitude:** $$\vert H_2(e^{j\omega}) \vert = 4 \sin^2(\omega/2)$$ > [!TIP] > **Interpretation:** The second-order differencer corresponds to the discrete approximation of the continuous-time **second derivative** $\dfrac{d^2 x(t)}{dt^2}$. It has **double zero at $z = 1$** and is a more aggressive high-pass filter. --- ### 3.4 Derivation 4 — The Accumulator (Inverse of Differencing) **Definition:** The accumulator computes the running sum: $$y[n] = y[n-1] + x[n]$$ **Step 1:** Apply the Z-transform using linearity and the time-shift property: $$Y(z) = z^{-1} Y(z) + X(z)$$ **Step 2:** Collect $Y(z)$ terms on the left-hand side: $$Y(z) - z^{-1} Y(z) = X(z)$$ **Step 3:** Factor out $Y(z)$: $$Y(z)[1 - z^{-1}] = X(z)$$ **Step 4:** Solve for the transfer function: $$H_{\text{acc}}(z) = \frac{Y(z)}{X(z)} = \frac{1}{1 - z^{-1}} = \frac{z}{z - 1}$$ **Step 5 — Frequency response magnitude:** Substituting $z = e^{j\omega}$: $$\vert H_{\text{acc}}(e^{j\omega}) \vert = \frac{1}{2 \vert \sin(\omega/2) \vert}$$ **Conclusion:** The accumulator is a **low-pass filter** (opposite of the differencer). It has a **pole at $z = 1$** and **DC gain $\to \infty$** (since $\sin(0) = 0$ in the denominator). > [!WARNING] > **Stability Caveat:** The accumulator transfer function has a pole on the unit circle. It is **not BIBO stable** for causal implementation but is widely used in feedback configurations (e.g., accumulators inside recursive filters). --- ### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — First-Order vs. Second-Order Differencer | **Property** | **First-Order Differencer** | **Second-Order Differencer** | |--------------|------------------------------|------------------------------| | Difference Equation | $y[n] = x[n] - x[n-1]$ | $y[n] = x[n] - 2x[n-1] + x[n-2]$ | | Transfer Function | $1 - z^{-1}$ | $(1 - z^{-1})^2$ | | Zeros | $z = 1$ (single) | $z = 1$ (double) | | Poles | $z = 0$ (single) | $z = 0$ (double) | | Magnitude $\vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert$ | $2 \vert \sin(\omega/2) \vert$ | $4 \sin^2(\omega/2)$ | | DC Gain | $0$ | $0$ | | Nyquist Gain | $2$ | $4$ | | Continuous-Time Analogue | First derivative | Second derivative | | Filter Type | High-pass (1st order) | High-pass (2nd order) | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram — First-Order Differencer (Direct Form-I) The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the canonical implementation of a first-order discrete-time differencer using a unit delay element and a summing junction. ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA["x[n]"] -- wire1 --> nodeB[("Adder / Subtracter
+1 and -1 inputs")] nodeA -- wire2 --> nodeC[/"Unit Delay
z to the power -1"/] nodeC -- wire3 --> nodeB nodeB -- wire4 --> nodeD["y[n] = x[n] minus x[n-1]"] style nodeA fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px style nodeD fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px style nodeB fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px style nodeC fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px ``` **Reading the Diagram:** - The input signal $x[n]$ splits into two paths. - The **upper path** enters the **positive terminal** of the adder. - The **lower path** passes through a **unit delay** ($z^{-1}$) and enters the **negative terminal** of the adder. - The output is the difference: $y[n] = x[n] - x[n-1]$. --- ### 4.2 Block Diagram — Second-Order Differencer (Cascaded Architecture) A second-order differencer is constructed by **cascading two first-order differencers** in series. Equivalently, it requires **two unit-delay elements** and a **3-input adder** with coefficients $+1, -2, +1$. ```mermaid flowchart LR in1["x[n]"] -- upper --> sumA[("Adder 1
+1, -1")] in1 -- mid --> dlyA[/"Delay 1
z to power -1"/] dlyA --> sumA dlyA -- mid --> dlyB[/"Delay 2
z to power -1"/] sumA -- mid --> sumB[("Adder 2
+1, -1")] dlyB -- lower --> sumB sumB -- output --> out1["y[n] = x[n] - 2x[n-1] + x[n-2]"] style in1 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px style out1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px style sumA fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px style sumB fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px style dlyA fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px style dlyB fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px ``` **Reading the Diagram:** - Stage 1: $x[n] - x[n-1]$ is computed by the first differencer. - Stage 2: The output of Stage 1 is differenced again with its own delayed version, yielding the second-order difference. --- ### 4.3 Pole-Zero Plot Description ```mermaid flowchart TB pzTitle["Pole-Zero Plot of H(z) equals 1 minus z inverse"] pzDesc1["Pole at z equals 0 (origin, inside unit circle)"] pzDesc2["Zero at z equals 1 (on the unit circle at angle 0)"] pzDesc3["The zero on the unit circle enforces H of e to the j0 equals 0"] pzDesc4["The pole at the origin guarantees BIBO stability and causality"] pzTitle --> pzDesc1 pzTitle --> pzDesc2 pzTitle --> pzDesc3 pzTitle --> pzDesc4 style pzTitle fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px style pzDesc1 fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#008B8B,stroke-width:1px style pzDesc2 fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#008B8B,stroke-width:1px style pzDesc3 fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#008B8B,stroke-width:1px style pzDesc4 fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#008B8B,stroke-width:1px ``` **Plot Interpretation:** A real-axis plot with the unit circle drawn; mark 'O' (zero) at the point $(1, 0)$ and 'X' (pole) at the origin $(0, 0)$. The presence of the zero exactly on the unit circle at $z = 1$ guarantees **complete DC rejection**. --- ### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Differencing System ```mermaid flowchart TB blockA["Stage 1 Input Sampler
Receives discrete samples x[n]"] blockB["Stage 2 Delay Network
Stores x[n-1] using z inverse"] blockC["Stage 3 Arithmetic Unit
Computes difference and output"] blockD["Stage 4 Output Buffer
Delivers y[n] to next stage"] blockA -- dataflow --> blockB blockB -- dataflow --> blockC blockC -- dataflow --> blockD style blockA fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px style blockB fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px style blockC fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px style blockD fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- #### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **Define a first-order discrete-time differencer. Write its difference equation and transfer function.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** A first-order discrete-time differencer is a causal LTI system that computes the difference between the present input sample and the immediately preceding input sample. **Difference Equation:** $$y[n] = x[n] - x[n-1]$$ **Transfer Function:** $$H(z) = 1 - z^{-1} = \frac{z-1}{z}$$ The system has a zero at $z = 1$ and a pole at $z = 0$, making it a high-pass filter that completely rejects DC and is BIBO stable. --- #### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **State any three properties of the first-order differencer's frequency response.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Property 1 — Magnitude Response:** $$\vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert = 2 \vert \sin(\omega/2) \vert$$ The magnitude is zero at $\omega = 0$ (DC) and reaches its maximum value of $2$ at $\omega = \pi$ (Nyquist), confirming high-pass behaviour. **Property 2 — Phase Response:** $$\angle H(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{\pi}{2} - \frac{\omega}{2}$$ The phase is **linear in $\omega$** for $0 < \omega < \pi$, with a constant offset of $\pi/2$. **Property 3 — Group Delay:** $$\tau_g(\omega) = -\frac{d}{d\omega}\angle H(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{1}{2}$$ The system has a **constant group delay of half a sample**, meaning all frequency components are delayed equally (linear-phase system). --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model]** **(a)** Derive the transfer function, magnitude response, and phase response of a first-order discrete-time differencer. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Determine the output $y[n]$ of the first-order differencer when the input is $x[n] = 5 + 3\cos(\pi n/4) + 2\cos(\pi n)$, assuming zero initial conditions. **(7 Marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for Question A:** **Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks):** **Step 1 — Time-domain equation:** The first-order differencer satisfies $y[n] = x[n] - x[n-1]$. **(0.5 Marks)** **Step 2 — Z-transform:** Apply linearity and the time-shift property: $$Y(z) = X(z) - z^{-1}X(z) = X(z)[1 - z^{-1}]$$ **(1.5 Marks)** **Step 3 — Transfer function:** $$H(z) = \frac{Y(z)}{X(z)} = 1 - z^{-1} = \frac{z-1}{z}$$ **(1 Mark)** **Step 4 — Frequency response:** Substitute $z = e^{j\omega}$: $$H(e^{j\omega}) = 1 - e^{-j\omega}$$ **(0.5 Marks)** **Step 5 — Factorization:** Multiply and divide by $e^{-j\omega/2}$: $$H(e^{j\omega}) = e^{-j\omega/2}[e^{j\omega/2} - e^{-j\omega/2}] = 2j\sin(\omega/2) \cdot e^{-j\omega/2}$$ **(1.5 Marks)** **Step 6 — Magnitude response:** $\vert 2j \vert = 2$ and $\vert e^{-j\omega/2} \vert = 1$: $$\boxed{\vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert = 2 \vert \sin(\omega/2) \vert}$$ **(1 Mark)** **Step 7 — Phase response:** $j = e^{j\pi/2}$, so: $$\boxed{\angle H(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{\pi}{2} - \frac{\omega}{2}}$$ **(1 Mark)** --- **Part (b) — Output Computation (7 Marks):** For an LTI system, $y[n]$ is the sum of the individual responses to each sinusoidal component, with each input sinusoid scaled by $\vert H(e^{j\omega_0}) \vert$ and phase-shifted by $\angle H(e^{j\omega_0})$. **Component 1 — DC term $x_1[n] = 5$ at $\omega_1 = 0$:** $$\vert H(e^{j0}) \vert = 2 \vert \sin(0) \vert = 0 \implies y_1[n] = 0$$ **(2 Marks)** **Component 2 — Cosine $x_2[n] = 3\cos(\pi n/4)$ at $\omega_2 = \pi/4$:** $$\vert H(e^{j\pi/4}) \vert = 2 \sin(\pi/8) \approx 2 \cdot 0.3827 = 0.7654$$ $$\angle H(e^{j\pi/4}) = \frac{\pi}{2} - \frac{\pi}{8} = \frac{3\pi}{8}$$ $$y_2[n] = 3 \cdot 0.7654 \cdot \cos\left(\frac{\pi n}{4} + \frac{3\pi}{8}\right) = 2.296 \cos\left(\frac{\pi n}{4} + \frac{3\pi}{8}\right)$$ **(2.5 Marks)** **Component 3 — Cosine $x_3[n] = 2\cos(\pi n)$ at $\omega_3 = \pi$:** $$\vert H(e^{j\pi}) \vert = 2 \vert \sin(\pi/2) \vert = 2$$ $$\angle H(e^{j\pi}) = \frac{\pi}{2} - \frac{\pi}{2} = 0$$ $$y_3[n] = 2 \cdot 2 \cdot \cos(\pi n + 0) = 4 \cos(\pi n) = 4(-1)^n$$ **(2.5 Marks)** **Total Output:** $$\boxed{y[n] = 0 + 2.296\cos\left(\frac{\pi n}{4} + \frac{3\pi}{8}\right) + 4(-1)^n}$$ --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2023 Model]** **(a)** Explain the pole-zero plot of a first-order differencer and discuss its stability and causality. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** The input to a first-order differencer is $x[n] = u[n]$ (unit step). Compute the output $y[n]$ using both the time-domain difference equation and the Z-transform method. **(7 Marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for Question B:** **Part (a) — Pole-Zero Analysis (7 Marks):** **Step 1 — Transfer function:** $H(z) = 1 - z^{-1} = \dfrac{z-1}{z}$ **(1 Mark)** **Step 2 — Zero location:** Setting the numerator to zero, $z - 1 = 0 \Rightarrow z = 1$. The zero lies **on the unit circle** at the point $(1, 0)$ in the complex plane. **(1.5 Marks)** **Step 3 — Pole location:** Setting the denominator to zero, $z = 0$. The pole lies at the **origin**, which is strictly inside the unit circle. **(1.5 Marks)** **Step 4 — Stability:** Since both poles (only one pole at $z = 0$) are strictly inside the unit circle ($\vert z \vert = 0 < 1$), the system is **BIBO stable**. The impulse response $h[n] = \delta[n] - \delta[n-1]$ is absolutely summable: $\sum \vert h[n] \vert = 2 < \infty$. **(1.5 Marks)** **Step 5 — Causality:** The output $y[n] = x[n] - x[n-1]$ depends on $x[n]$ (present) and $x[n-1]$ (past), never on $x[n+1]$ (future). Hence the system is **causal**. **(1 Mark)** **Step 6 — Zero on Unit Circle Implication:** The zero at $z = 1$ is on the unit circle, which means the magnitude response is exactly zero at $\omega = 0$, i.e., **the system completely rejects DC inputs**. **(0.5 Marks)** --- **Part (b) — Step Response Computation (7 Marks):** **Method 1 — Time-Domain (3.5 Marks):** Using $y[n] = x[n] - x[n-1]$ with $x[n] = u[n]$: - $n = 0$: $y[0] = u[0] - u[-1] = 1 - 0 = 1$ - $n \geq 1$: $y[n] = u[n] - u[n-1] = 1 - 1 = 0$ - $n < 0$: $y[n] = u[n] - u[n-1] = 0 - 0 = 0$ $$\boxed{y[n] = \delta[n]}$$ **Method 2 — Z-Transform (3.5 Marks):** **Step 1 — Z-transform of input:** $X(z) = \dfrac{1}{1 - z^{-1}}, \quad \vert z \vert > 1$ **Step 2 — Multiply by transfer function:** $$Y(z) = H(z)X(z) = (1 - z^{-1}) \cdot \frac{1}{1 - z^{-1}} = 1$$ **Step 3 — Inverse Z-transform:** The Z-transform of $\delta[n]$ is $1$. Therefore: $$y[n] = \delta[n]$$ **Physical Interpretation:** The differencer detects the **edge** (discontinuity) at $n = 0$ of the step input and produces an **impulse** of strength $1$, with zero output everywhere else. This is the digital analogue of the derivative of a step function being an impulse. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU University Exams:** > > 1. **Sign Error in Difference Equation:** Students often write $y[n] = x[n-1] - x[n]$ instead of $x[n] - x[n-1]$. The correct **forward** difference is $x[n] - x[n-1]$. **(−1 to 2 marks lost)** > > 2. **Forgetting Absolute Value in Magnitude Response:** The correct expression is $\vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert = 2 \vert \sin(\omega/2) \vert$. Writing $2\sin(\omega/2)$ without absolute value is **wrong** for $\omega \in (\pi, 2\pi)$. **(−1 mark)** > > 3. **Phase Range Confusion:** The phase $\pi/2 - \omega/2$ is valid only for $0 < \omega < \pi$. For $\omega \in (\pi, 2\pi)$, the phase must be unwrapped or written with a $\pi$ correction. **(−1 mark)** > > 4. **Conflating Differencer and Integrator:** Students often confuse the differencer $H(z) = 1 - z^{-1}$ with the accumulator $H(z) = 1/(1 - z^{-1})$. They are **inverses** of each other. **(−2 marks if swapped)** > > 5. **Skipping the Z-Transform Derivation:** KTU examiners expect the full derivation from $y[n] = x[n] - x[n-1]$ to $H(z) = 1 - z^{-1}$ using the time-shift property. Skipping this step loses at least **2 marks**. > > 6. **Forgetting to Specify ROC:** For the accumulator $H(z) = z/(z-1)$, students must specify that the **Region of Convergence (ROC)** is $\vert z \vert > 1$ for a causal implementation. **(−1 mark if missing)** --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Differencing** > > ✅ A first-order differencer implements $y[n] = x[n] - x[n-1]$. > > ✅ Its transfer function is $H(z) = 1 - z^{-1} = (z-1)/z$. > > ✅ It has a **zero at $z = 1$** (on the unit circle → DC rejection) and a **pole at $z = 0$** (inside the unit circle → stable). > > ✅ The magnitude response is $\vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert = 2 \vert \sin(\omega/2) \vert$ — a **high-pass filter** shape. > > ✅ The phase response is $\angle H(e^{j\omega}) = \pi/2 - \omega/2$ — **linear phase** with $\pi/2$ offset. > > ✅ The group delay is **constant at $1/2$ sample** (due to the linear phase). > > ✅ The system is **causal** (depends only on present and past inputs) and **BIBO stable** (pole inside unit circle). > > ✅ Second-order differencer: $y[n] = x[n] - 2x[n-1] + x[n-2]$ with $H_2(z) = (1 - z^{-1})^2$. > > ✅ The **accumulator** (running sum) is the inverse system: $y[n] = y[n-1] + x[n]$ with $H_{\text{acc}}(z) = 1/(1 - z^{-1})$. > > ✅ The accumulator has a **pole at $z = 1$** and is a **low-pass filter** with $\vert H_{\text{acc}}(e^{j\omega}) \vert = 1 / (2\vert \sin(\omega/2) \vert)$. > > ✅ The impulse response of the first-order differencer is $h[n] = \delta[n] - \delta[n-1]$. > > ✅ The step response of the first-order differencer is $y[n] = \delta[n]$ (an impulse at the discontinuity). > > ✅ **Real-world analogy:** A differencer is a digital "speed detector" — it measures how fast the input is changing between consecutive samples. > > ✅ **Applications:** High-pass filtering, edge detection in images, baseline removal in biomedical signals, trend removal in econometrics.

topics.title: Initial and Final Value Theorems. Determination of the Inverse Z Transform. LTI Systems and Difference Equations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST416 - SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Initial and Final Value Theorems. Determination of the Inverse Z Transform. LTI Systems and Difference Equations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Z-Domain Theorems — A Bird's-Eye View

The **Z-Transform** converts a discrete-time sequence $x[n]$ into a complex function $X(z)$. While the forward transform is a powerful analytical tool, engineers frequently need to *predict* the behavior of a system *without* actually computing the entire time-domain response. This is where the **Initial Value Theorem (IVT)** and **Final Value Theorem (FVT)** become indispensable — they act as "shortcut inspectors" that peek into the time-domain response directly from the Z-domain representation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Initial Value Theorem (IVT):** Determines the *first sample* $x[0]$ of a causal sequence directly from $X(z)$, without performing a full inverse Z-transform.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Value Theorem (FVT):** Determines the *steady-state (long-term) value* of $x[n]$ as $n \to \infty$, provided the limit exists (i.e., the system is stable).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Speedometer and the Finish Line

Imagine a roller coaster starting at the top of a hill. The **Initial Value Theorem** is like glancing at the *first frame* of the roller coaster's journey — it tells you the starting height (initial amplitude $x[0]$). The **Final Value Theorem**, on the other hand, is like watching the roller coaster settle down and asking, *"Where will the rider eventually be after all motion dies out?"* — it gives you the *settled value* $x[\infty]$. Both theorems save you the trouble of generating every intermediate frame.

### 1.3 Inverse Z-Transform — Recovering the Time Domain

The **Inverse Z-Transform** is the mathematical procedure of converting $X(z)$ back into the discrete-time sequence $x[n]$. Three primary methods are studied in KTU 2024:
