- **Long Division (Power Series) Method** — best for causal sequences when only a few samples are needed.
- **Partial Fraction Expansion (PFE) Method** — most popular; decomposes $X(z)$ into simpler standard pairs.
- **Residue (Contour Integration) Method** — rigorous, based on Cauchy's residue theorem.

### 1.4 LTI Systems and Difference Equations

A **Linear Time-Invariant (LTI) discrete-time system** is fully characterized by a **linear constant-coefficient difference equation** of the form:

$$y[n] + a_1 y[n-1] + \cdots + a_N y[n-N] = b_0 x[n] + b_1 x[n-1] + \cdots + b_M x[n-M]$$

Taking the Z-transform of both sides converts this difference equation into an algebraic equation in $z$, yielding the **transfer function** $H(z)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECST416, Module 4):** Students must be able to (i) apply IVT and FVT, (ii) compute the inverse Z-transform by at least two methods, and (iii) derive $H(z)$ from a difference equation and determine stability.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Initial Value Theorem (IVT) — Step-by-Step Logic

**Statement:** If $X(z)$ is the Z-transform of a causal sequence $x[n]$, then:

$$x[0] = \lim_{z \to \infty} X(z)$$

**Why this works — the logic behind it:**
1. Recall the definition: $X(z) = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} x[n] \, z^{-n} = x[0] + x[1]z^{-1} + x[2]z^{-2} + \cdots$
2. As $z \to \infty$, every term containing $z^{-1}, z^{-2}, \ldots$ vanishes because $z^{-k} \to 0$ for $k \geq 1$.
3. The only term that survives is $x[0]$, leaving $X(z) \to x[0]$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Validity Condition for IVT:** The sequence must be **causal** ($x[n] = 0$ for $n < 0$) and $X(z)$ must exist for $\vert z \vert > R$, where $R$ is the radius of convergence.

**Generalized form** (for $x[m]$):

$$x[m] = \lim_{z \to \infty} z^m \left[ X(z) - \sum_{k=0}^{m-1} x[k] z^{-k} \right]$$

For board exams, the most-tested variant is:

$$x[0] = \lim_{z \to \infty} X(z), \quad x[1] = \lim_{z \to \infty} z\bigl[X(z) - x[0]\bigr]$$

### 2.2 Final Value Theorem (FVT) — Step-by-Step Logic

**Statement:** If $X(z)$ is the Z-transform of $x[n]$ and all poles of $(1 - z^{-1})X(z)$ lie *strictly inside* the unit circle ($\vert z \vert < 1$), then:

$$\lim_{n \to \infty} x[n] = \lim_{z \to 1} (1 - z^{-1}) X(z) = \lim_{z \to 1} \frac{(z-1)}{z} X(z)$$

**Why this works — the logic behind it:**
1. The unilateral Z-transform of the first difference is $\mathcal{Z}\{x[n] - x[n-1]\} = (1 - z^{-1})X(z)$.
2. By the time-limit property, the sum of all first differences equals the final value: $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \{x[n] - x[n-1]\} = x[\infty] - x[-1]$.
3. Taking the Z-domain limit $z \to 1$ recovers this sum, yielding the final value.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake (FVT):** Applying FVT when poles of $(1-z^{-1})X(z)$ lie *on* or *outside* the unit circle. FVT is **invalid** for unstable or marginally stable systems — the limit $x[\infty]$ does not exist in such cases.

### 2.3 Inverse Z-Transform — The Three Pillars

**Method A — Long Division (Power Series)**
Divide the numerator polynomial by the denominator polynomial in descending powers of $z^{-1}$. The coefficients of $z^0, z^{-1}, z^{-2}, \ldots$ give $x[0], x[1], x[2], \ldots$. Best suited for **causal** sequences.

**Method B — Partial Fraction Expansion (PFE) — *Most Popular for KTU Board Exams***
1. Express $X(z)/z$ in partial fractions (this is the standard textbook approach).
2. Multiply each fraction back by $z$ to get a recognizable form.
3. Use the standard Z-transform pair table to identify each term.

**Method C — Residue Method**
$$x[n] = \sum \text{Residues of } X(z) z^{n-1} \text{ at the poles of } X(z)$$
Used when $X(z)$ has complex poles or repeated poles.

### 2.4 LTI Systems from Difference Equations

A general **Nth-order LTI difference equation**:

$$\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k \, y[n-k] = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k \, x[n-k] \quad (a_0 = 1)$$

Taking the unilateral Z-transform (assuming zero initial conditions for simplicity):

$$Y(z) \sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k z^{-k} = X(z) \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k z^{-k}$$

**Transfer Function:**

$$H(z) = \frac{Y(z)}{X(z)} = \frac{\sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k z^{-k}}{\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k z^{-k}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stability Criterion:** An LTI system described by $H(z)$ is **BIBO stable** if and only if *all poles* of $H(z)$ lie *strictly inside* the unit circle (i.e., $\vert p_k \vert < 1$ for every pole $p_k$).

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Validity / Condition |
|---|---------|---------|----------------------|
| 1 | **IVT** (initial sample) | $x[0] = \lim\limits_{z \to \infty} X(z)$ | Causal sequence |
| 2 | **IVT** (general) | $x[m] = \lim\limits_{z \to \infty} z^m X(z)$ — only if $x[n]=0$ for $0<n<m$ | Special form |
| 3 | **FVT** | $\lim\limits_{n \to \infty} x[n] = \lim\limits_{z \to 1} (1 - z^{-1})X(z)$ | Poles of $(1-z^{-1})X(z)$ inside unit circle |
| 4 | **Inverse ZT (PFE step 1)** | Express $\dfrac{X(z)}{z} = \sum \dfrac{A_k}{z - p_k}$ | Standard textbook trick |
| 5 | **Inverse ZT (Residue)** | $x[n] = \sum_{k} \text{Res}\bigl[X(z) z^{n-1}, p_k\bigr]$ | All poles inside ROC |
| 6 | **Long Division** | $X(z) = \dfrac{N(z)}{D(z)} = c_0 + c_1 z^{-1} + c_2 z^{-2} + \cdots$ | $x[n] = c_n$ for $n \geq 0$ |
| 7 | **Transfer Function** | $H(z) = \dfrac{Y(z)}{X(z)} = \dfrac{\sum b_k z^{-k}}{\sum a_k z^{-k}}$ | Zero initial conditions |
| 8 | **BIBO Stability** | All poles of $H(z)$ satisfy $\vert p_k \vert < 1$ | Inside unit circle |
| 9 | **Causality** | ROC is outside the outermost pole | $\vert z \vert > R$ |
| 10 | **Difference Equation → TF** | $H(z) = \dfrac{\sum b_k z^{-k}}{\sum a_k z^{-k}}$ | Apply unilateral ZT |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** IVT and FVT are routinely used in **digital control systems** (predicting the startup and steady-state response of digital controllers) and **digital filter design** (verifying stability of recursive filters such as IIR filters used in audio processing and biomedical instrumentation).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Derivation — Initial Value Theorem

We begin with the definition of the unilateral Z-transform of a causal sequence $x[n]$:

$$X(z) = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} x[n] \, z^{-n}$$

Expanding the sum explicitly:

$$X(z) = x[0] + x[1] z^{-1} + x[2] z^{-2} + x[3] z^{-3} + \cdots$$

Now, take the limit as $z \to \infty$:

$$\lim_{z \to \infty} X(z) = \lim_{z \to \infty} \Bigl[ x[0] + x[1] z^{-1} + x[2] z^{-2} + \cdots \Bigr]$$

As $z \to \infty$, the quantities $z^{-1}, z^{-2}, z^{-3}, \ldots$ all approach zero, so every term except the first vanishes:

$$\lim_{z \to \infty} X(z) = x[0] + 0 + 0 + \cdots = x[0]$$

Thus, the formal statement is:

$$x[0] = \lim_{z \to \infty} X(z) \quad \blacksquare$$

### 3.2 Detailed Derivation — Final Value Theorem

Consider the unilateral Z-transform of the first backward difference $w[n] = x[n] - x[n-1]$:

$$W(z) = \mathcal{Z}\{x[n] - x[n-1]\} = X(z) - z^{-1} X(z) = (1 - z^{-1}) X(z)$$

By the definition of the Z-transform:

$$W(z) = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \{x[n] - x[n-1]\} \, z^{-n}$$

Now apply the IVT trick — take the limit as $z \to 1$:

$$\lim_{z \to 1} W(z) = \lim_{z \to 1} (1 - z^{-1}) X(z)$$

On the right-hand side, expand the sum at $z = 1$:

$$\lim_{z \to 1} \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \{x[n] - x[n-1]\} z^{-n} = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \{x[n] - x[n-1]\}$$

(using $\lim_{z \to 1} z^{-n} = 1$).

The infinite series telescopes:

$$\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \{x[n] - x[n-1]\} = x[0] - x[-1] + x[1] - x[0] + x[2] - x[1] + \cdots$$

All intermediate terms cancel, leaving:

$$\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \{x[n] - x[n-1]\} = \lim_{n \to \infty} x[n] - x[-1]$$

Assuming a causal sequence, $x[-1] = 0$, so:

$$\lim_{n \to \infty} x[n] = \lim_{z \to 1} (1 - z^{-1}) X(z) \quad \blacksquare$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Caveat:** The interchange of the limit and the infinite sum (i.e., $\lim_{z \to 1} \sum \to \sum \lim$) is valid **only if** the sum converges uniformly. This convergence requires that all poles of $(1 - z^{-1})X(z)$ lie strictly inside the unit circle. If any pole lies on the unit circle, the FVT cannot be applied.

### 3.3 Worked Example — PFE Method for Inverse Z-Transform

**Problem:** Find the inverse Z-transform of:

$$X(z) = \frac{z^2 + z}{(z - 1)(z - 0.5)}, \quad \vert z \vert > 1$$

**Step 1 — Divide by $z$:**

$$\frac{X(z)}{z} = \frac{z^2 + z}{z(z - 1)(z - 0.5)} = \frac{z + 1}{(z - 1)(z - 0.5)}$$

**Step 2 — Partial fraction expansion:**

$$\frac{z + 1}{(z - 1)(z - 0.5)} = \frac{A}{z - 1} + \frac{B}{z - 0.5}$$

Multiply both sides by $(z-1)(z-0.5)$:

$$z + 1 = A(z - 0.5) + B(z - 1)$$

**Step 3 — Solve for A and B using the cover-up method.**

Set $z = 1$:

$$1 + 1 = A(1 - 0.5) \implies 2 = 0.5 A \implies A = 4$$

Set $z = 0.5$:

$$0.5 + 1 = B(0.5 - 1) \implies 1.5 = -0.5 B \implies B = -3$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back and multiply by $z$:**

$$X(z) = z \left[ \frac{4}{z - 1} - \frac{3}{z - 0.5} \right] = \frac{4z}{z - 1} - \frac{3z}{z - 0.5}$$

**Step 5 — Identify the standard pairs.**

Recall: $\mathcal{Z}^{-1}\left\{\dfrac{z}{z - a}\right\} = a^n u[n]$.

Therefore:

$$x[n] = 4 (1)^n u[n] - 3 (0.5)^n u[n] = \left[ 4 - 3 (0.5)^n \right] u[n]$$

**Verification via IVT:** $x[0] = 4 - 3(1) = 1$. Check: $X(z) = \dfrac{z^2+z}{z^2-1.5z+0.5}$, so $\lim_{z \to \infty} X(z) = 1$ (ratio of leading coefficients). ✓

**Verification via FVT:** $x[\infty] = 4 - 0 = 4$. Check: $\lim_{z \to 1} (1 - z^{-1}) X(z) = \lim_{z \to 1} \dfrac{(z-1)}{z} \cdot \dfrac{z^2+z}{(z-1)(z-0.5)} = \lim_{z \to 1} \dfrac{z+1}{z-0.5} = \dfrac{2}{0.5} = 4$. ✓

### 3.4 Worked Example — Long Division Method

**Problem:** Find the first four samples of $x[n]$ for:

$$X(z) = \frac{1}{1 - 0.5 z^{-1} + 0.2 z^{-2}}$$

**Step 1 — Set up polynomial long division, arranged in descending powers of $z^{-1}$:**

```
                       1 + 0.5 z⁻¹ + 0.05 z⁻² + ...
                 ___________________________________________
1 - 0.5z⁻¹ + 0.2z⁻² | 1
                   - (1 - 0.5z⁻¹ + 0.2z⁻²)
                   _________________________
                          0.5z⁻¹ - 0.2z⁻²
                   -    (0.5z⁻¹ - 0.25z⁻² + 0.1z⁻³)
                   _________________________
                              0.05z⁻² - 0.1z⁻³
```

**Step 2 — Read off the coefficients:**

$$X(z) = 1 + 0.5 z^{-1} + 0.05 z^{-2} + \cdots$$

Therefore:

$$x[0] = 1, \quad x[1] = 0.5, \quad x[2] = 0.05, \quad x[3] = \text{(next term from continued division)}$$

### 3.5 Worked Example — LTI System from Difference Equation

**Problem:** A causal LTI system is described by:

$$y[n] - 0.5 y[n-1] = x[n] + 0.2 x[n-1]$$

Determine $H(z)$ and check BIBO stability.

**Step 1 — Take the Z-transform of both sides (with zero initial conditions):**

$$Y(z) - 0.5 z^{-1} Y(z) = X(z) + 0.2 z^{-1} X(z)$$

**Step 2 — Factor:**

$$Y(z)(1 - 0.5 z^{-1}) = X(z)(1 + 0.2 z^{-1})$$

**Step 3 — Transfer function:**

$$H(z) = \frac{Y(z)}{X(z)} = \frac{1 + 0.2 z^{-1}}{1 - 0.5 z^{-1}} = \frac{z + 0.2}{z - 0.5}$$

**Step 4 — Stability check:**

The single pole is at $p = 0.5$. Since $\vert 0.5 \vert < 1$, the pole lies **inside the unit circle** ⟹ the system is **BIBO stable**. ✓

**Step 5 — Impulse response (optional):**

$$H(z) = \frac{z}{z - 0.5} + \frac{0.2 z^{-1}}{1 - 0.5 z^{-1}} = \frac{z}{z - 0.5} + 0.2 \cdot \frac{1}{z - 0.5}$$

Using standard pairs and the time-shift property:

$$h[n] = (0.5)^n u[n] + 0.2 (0.5)^{n-1} u[n-1]$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Inverse Z-Transform Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Input: X of z, rational function] --> decideA{Identify ROC and causal anti-causal}
    decideA --> pathA1[Causal: ROC is exterior]
    decideA --> pathA2[Anti-causal: ROC is interior]

    pathA1 --> methodA1[Long Division: descending powers of z inverse]
    pathA2 --> methodA2[Long Division: ascending powers of z]

    methodA1 --> outA1[Output: first N samples x 0 x 1 x 2 ...]
    methodA2 --> outA2[Output: samples for negative n]

    decideA --> methodB[Partial Fraction Expansion via X of z divided by z]
    methodB --> idPoles[Identify poles: real, repeated, complex]
    idPoles --> tableLookup[Match with standard Z-transform pairs]
    tableLookup --> assemble[Assemble h of n as sum of terms a to the n times u of n]
    assemble --> outB[Final output: x of n in closed form]

    decideA --> methodC[Residue Method: sum of residues of X of z times z to the power n minus 1 at each pole]
    methodC --> computeRes[Compute residue for each pole]
    computeRes --> outC[Final output: x of n]

    outA1 --> recap[Combine with IVT and FVT for verification]
    outA2 --> recap
    outB --> recap
    outC --> recap
    recap --> finalAns[Verified answer for x of n]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Architecture — LTI System Identification via Difference Equation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputNode[Input x of n] --> delayBankX[Delay Line: x of n minus 1, x of n minus 2, ..., x of n minus M]
    inputNode --> coefMul0[b0 multiplier]
    delayBankX --> coefMul1[b1 multiplier]
    delayBankX --> coefMul2[b2 multiplier]
    delayBankX --> coefMulM[bM multiplier]

    coefMul0 --> summerTop[Summer upper rail]
    coefMul1 --> summerTop
    coefMul2 --> summerTop
    coefMulM --> summerTop

    summerTop --> outputNode[Output y of n]
    outputNode --> feedbackDelay[Delay Line: y of n minus 1, ..., y of n minus N]
    feedbackDelay --> coefNegA1[minus a1 multiplier]
    feedbackDelay --> coefNegA2[minus a2 multiplier]
    feedbackDelay --> coefNegAN[minus aN multiplier]
    coefNegA1 --> summerBottom[Summer lower rail]
    coefNegA2 --> summerBottom
    coefNegAN --> summerBottom
    summerBottom --> outputNode

    outputNode --> ztBlock[Apply Z-transform: derive H of z]
    ztBlock --> poleCheck[Check pole locations relative to unit circle]
    poleCheck --> stableDecide{All poles inside unit circle?}
    stableDecide --> yesStable[System is BIBO stable]
    stableDecide --> noUnstable[System is unstable: FVT cannot be applied]
```

### 4.3 Decision Flowchart — When to Use IVT vs. FVT

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    q1{Given X of z for a causal sequence?}
    q1 -- No --> notValid[Theorems may not apply: check ROC and causality]
    q1 -- Yes --> q2{What do you need to find?}

    q2 -- Initial sample x of 0 --> ivtPath[Apply IVT: take limit as z approaches infinity of X of z]
    ivtPath --> ivtOut[Result: x of 0 directly from X of z without inverse transform]

    q2 -- Steady state limit as n approaches infinity --> q3{All poles of one minus z inverse times X of z inside unit circle?}
    q3 -- Yes --> fvtPath[Apply FVT: take limit as z approaches 1 of one minus z inverse times X of z]
    fvtPath --> fvtOut[Result: x of infinity as steady state value]
    q3 -- No --> fvtInvalid[FVT is invalid: limit does not exist or system is unstable]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*State and prove the Initial Value Theorem for Z-transforms.*

**Model Answer:**

**Statement:** If $X(z)$ is the unilateral Z-transform of a causal sequence $x[n]$ with $\text{ROC: } \vert z \vert > R$, then the initial value is given by:

$$x[0] = \lim_{z \to \infty} X(z)$$

**Proof:** Starting from the definition of the unilateral Z-transform:

$$X(z) = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} x[n] z^{-n} = x[0] + x[1] z^{-1} + x[2] z^{-2} + \cdots$$

Taking the limit as $z \to \infty$:

$$\lim_{z \to \infty} X(z) = \lim_{z \to \infty} \left[ x[0] + x[1] z^{-1} + x[2] z^{-2} + \cdots \right] = x[0]$$

since $z^{-k} \to 0$ for $k \geq 1$. Hence proved. *(3 marks: 1 for statement, 2 for proof)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*What is the condition for applying the Final Value Theorem? Why is it important?*

**Model Answer:**

**Condition:** The FVT can be applied only if **all poles of $(1 - z^{-1}) X(z)$ lie strictly inside the unit circle** (i.e., $\vert p_k \vert < 1$ for all poles $p_k$). Additionally, the sequence $x[n]$ must be causal.

**Importance:** FVT allows engineers to predict the *long-term steady-state behavior* of a discrete-time system **without computing the entire time response**. This is critical in:
- Digital control systems (verifying steady-state error).
- Stability analysis of digital filters.
- Determining DC gain of recursive systems.

If the condition is violated, the limit $\lim_{n \to \infty} x[n]$ does not exist (the system response either grows unboundedly or oscillates indefinitely), so FVT cannot be used. *(3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Find the inverse Z-transform of $X(z) = \dfrac{z^2}{(z - 0.5)(z - 1)^2}}$ for $\vert z \vert > 1$ using partial fraction expansion.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Apply the Initial and Final Value Theorems to verify your answer, and hence comment on the stability of the corresponding system.

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1 — Write $X(z)/z$:**

$$\frac{X(z)}{z} = \frac{z}{(z - 0.5)(z - 1)^2}$$

**Step 2 — Partial fraction expansion:**

$$\frac{z}{(z - 0.5)(z - 1)^2} = \frac{A}{z - 0.5} + \frac{B}{z - 1} + \frac{C}{(z - 1)^2}$$

Multiply both sides by $(z - 0.5)(z - 1)^2$:

$$z = A(z - 1)^2 + B(z - 0.5)(z - 1) + C(z - 0.5)$$

**Step 3 — Solve for A, B, C using the cover-up method.**

Set $z = 0.5$:

$$0.5 = A(0.5 - 1)^2 = A(0.25) \implies A = 2$$

Set $z = 1$:

$$1 = C(1 - 0.5) = 0.5 C \implies C = 2$$

Compare coefficients of $z^2$ on both sides (or substitute another value, e.g., $z = 0$):

$$0 = A + B \implies B = -A = -2$$

**Verification at $z = 0$:** LHS = $0$. RHS = $A(1) + B(0.5)(-1) + C(-0.5) = 2 + (-2)(-0.5) + 2(-0.5) = 2 + 1 - 1 = 2$. Hmm, let me recheck.

Actually, expanding the right side:
$A(z^2 - 2z + 1) + B(z^2 - 1.5z + 0.5) + C(z - 0.5)$

- $z^2$ coefficient: $A + B = 0 \implies B = -2$.
- $z$ coefficient: $-2A - 1.5B + C = 1 \implies -4 + 3 + 2 = 1$ ✓
- constant: $A + 0.5B - 0.5C = 0 \implies 2 - 1 - 1 = 0$ ✓

So $A = 2$, $B = -2$, $C = 2$.

**Step 4 — Multiply back by $z$:**

$$X(z) = \frac{2z}{z - 0.5} - \frac{2z}{z - 1} + \frac{2z}{(z - 1)^2}$$

**Step 5 — Apply standard Z-transform pairs:**

- $\mathcal{Z}^{-1}\left\{\dfrac{z}{z - a}\right\} = a^n u[n]$, so $\mathcal{Z}^{-1}\left\{\dfrac{2z}{z - 0.5}\right\} = 2 (0.5)^n u[n]$.
- $\mathcal{Z}^{-1}\left\{\dfrac{2z}{z - 1}\right\} = 2 (1)^n u[n] = 2 u[n]$.
- $\mathcal{Z}^{-1}\left\{\dfrac{2z}{(z - 1)^2}\right\} = 2 n (1)^n u[n] = 2n \, u[n]$ (using the pair $\dfrac{az}{(z-a)^2} \leftrightarrow n a^n u[n]$ with $a = 1$).

**Final Answer:**

$$x[n] = \left[ 2(0.5)^n - 2 + 2n \right] u[n] = \left[ 2n - 2 + 2(0.5)^n \right] u[n]$$

**(7 marks: 2 for setup, 3 for solving A/B/C, 2 for the final inverse transform)**

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**IVT Verification:**

$$x[0] = \lim_{z \to \infty} X(z) = \lim_{z \to \infty} \frac{z^2}{(z - 0.5)(z - 1)^2} = 1$$

(from the ratio of leading coefficients: $1 / 1 = 1$).

From our derived expression: $x[0] = 2(0) - 2 + 2(0.5)^0 = -2 + 2 = 0$. 

*Wait — discrepancy! Let me recheck the PFE.*

Re-examining: $X(z) = \dfrac{z^2}{(z-0.5)(z-1)^2}$. The numerator and denominator both have degree 2, so $X(z) \to 1$ as $z \to \infty$. Thus $x[0] = 1$. Let me recheck the PFE arithmetic.

Setting $z = 0$ in $z = A(z-1)^2 + B(z-0.5)(z-1) + C(z-0.5)$:

$$0 = A(1) + B(-0.5)(-1) + C(-0.5) = A + 0.5B - 0.5C$$

With $A = 2, C = 2$: $0 = 2 + 0.5B - 1 \implies 0.5B = -1 \implies B = -2$. ✓

Now plug $n = 0$: $x[0] = 2(1) - 2(1) + 2(0) = 0$. But IVT says $x[0] = 1$.

**Recheck IVT:** $\lim_{z \to \infty} \dfrac{z^2}{z^3} = \lim_{z \to \infty} \dfrac{1}{z} = 0$. Yes, $x[0] = 0$, which matches our PFE result. (The denominator is degree 3, numerator degree 2 — so the limit is indeed 0.)

**So the correct IVT result:** $x[0] = 0$ ✓ (matches PFE).

**FVT Verification:**

$$\lim_{z \to 1} (1 - z^{-1}) X(z) = \lim_{z \to 1} \frac{(z-1)}{z} \cdot \frac{z^2}{(z - 0.5)(z - 1)^2} = \lim_{z \to 1} \frac{z}{(z - 0.5)(z - 1)}$$

This limit is **infinite** (the pole at $z = 1$ is not cancelled in the denominator after the simplification — there is a remaining $(z-1)$ term in the denominator). Thus, the FVT limit does not exist as a finite value, which is consistent with $x[n] = 2n - 2 + 2(0.5)^n \to \infty$ as $n \to \infty$.

**Stability Comment:** The pole at $z = 1$ lies **on the unit circle** (a repeated pole at $z = 1$ of order 2). Hence the system is **not BIBO stable** — the response grows without bound, which confirms why FVT cannot be applied. *(7 marks: 2 for IVT, 2 for FVT, 3 for the stability discussion)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** An LTI system is governed by the difference equation:

$$y[n] - 0.8 y[n-1] + 0.15 y[n-2] = x[n] + 0.5 x[n-1]$$

Determine (i) the transfer function $H(z)$, (ii) the impulse response $h[n]$, and (iii) whether the system is causal and stable.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For the input $x[n] = u[n]$, determine $y[n]$ using the partial fraction method, and verify using the Final Value Theorem.

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1 — Take the Z-transform of the difference equation (zero initial conditions):**

$$Y(z) - 0.8 z^{-1} Y(z) + 0.15 z^{-2} Y(z) = X(z) + 0.5 z^{-1} X(z)$$

$$Y(z) (1 - 0.8 z^{-1} + 0.15 z^{-2}) = X(z) (1 + 0.5 z^{-1})$$

**Step 2 — Transfer function:**

$$H(z) = \frac{Y(z)}{X(z)} = \frac{1 + 0.5 z^{-1}}{1 - 0.8 z^{-1} + 0.15 z^{-2}}$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $z^2$:

$$H(z) = \frac{z^2 + 0.5 z}{z^2 - 0.8 z + 0.15} = \frac{z(z + 0.5)}{(z - 0.3)(z - 0.5)}$$

**Step 3 — Impulse response via PFE:**

$$\frac{H(z)}{z} = \frac{z + 0.5}{(z - 0.3)(z - 0.5)} = \frac{A}{z - 0.3} + \frac{B}{z - 0.5}$$

Cover-up at $z = 0.3$:

$$A = \frac{0.3 + 0.5}{0.3 - 0.5} = \frac{0.8}{-0.2} = -4$$

Cover-up at $z = 0.5$:

$$B = \frac{0.5 + 0.5}{0.5 - 0.3} = \frac{1}{0.2} = 5$$

Therefore:

$$H(z) = -\frac{4z}{z - 0.3} + \frac{5z}{z - 0.5}$$

$$h[n] = \left[ -4 (0.3)^n + 5 (0.5)^n \right] u[n]$$

**Step 4 — Causality & Stability:**

- **Causality:** $h[n] = 0$ for $n < 0$ (the unit step $u[n]$ enforces this) — so the system is **causal**. ✓
- **Stability:** Poles are at $z = 0.3$ and $z = 0.5$. Since $\vert 0.3 \vert < 1$ and $\vert 0.5 \vert < 1$, both poles lie **inside the unit circle** — the system is **BIBO stable**. ✓

*(7 marks: 2 for $H(z)$, 3 for $h[n]$, 2 for causality and stability)*

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Find $Y(z) = H(z) X(z)$:**

Recall $\mathcal{Z}\{u[n]\} = \dfrac{z}{z - 1}$, so $X(z) = \dfrac{z}{z - 1}$.

$$Y(z) = H(z) X(z) = \frac{z(z + 0.5)}{(z - 0.3)(z - 0.5)} \cdot \frac{z}{z - 1} = \frac{z^2 (z + 0.5)}{(z - 0.3)(z - 0.5)(z - 1)}$$

**Step 2 — Partial fraction expansion of $Y(z)/z$:**

$$\frac{Y(z)}{z} = \frac{z(z + 0.5)}{(z - 0.3)(z - 0.5)(z - 1)} = \frac{A}{z - 0.3} + \frac{B}{z - 0.5} + \frac{C}{z - 1}$$

Cover-up at $z = 0.3$:

$$A = \frac{0.3 (0.3 + 0.5)}{(0.3 - 0.5)(0.3 - 1)} = \frac{0.3 \times 0.8}{(-0.2)(-0.7)} = \frac{0.24}{0.14} = \frac{12}{7}$$

Cover-up at $z = 0.5$:

$$B = \frac{0.5 (0.5 + 0.5)}{(0.5 - 0.3)(0.5 - 1)} = \frac{0.5 \times 1}{(0.2)(-0.5)} = \frac{0.5}{-0.1} = -5$$

Cover-up at $z = 1$:

$$C = \frac{1 (1 + 0.5)}{(1 - 0.3)(1 - 0.5)} = \frac{1.5}{(0.7)(0.5)} = \frac{1.5}{0.35} = \frac{30}{7}$$

**Step 3 — Multiply back by $z$ and apply inverse Z-transform:**

$$Y(z) = \frac{12}{7} \cdot \frac{z}{z - 0.3} - 5 \cdot \frac{z}{z - 0.5} + \frac{30}{7} \cdot \frac{z}{z - 1}$$

$$y[n] = \left[ \frac{12}{7} (0.3)^n - 5 (0.5)^n + \frac{30}{7} (1)^n \right] u[n] = \left[ \frac{12}{7}(0.3)^n - 5(0.5)^n + \frac{30}{7} \right] u[n]$$

**Step 4 — Verify using FVT:**

Apply FVT: $\lim_{n \to \infty} y[n] = \lim_{z \to 1} (1 - z^{-1}) Y(z)$.

$$\lim_{z \to 1} \frac{(z-1)}{z} \cdot \frac{z^2(z+0.5)}{(z-0.3)(z-0.5)(z-1)} = \lim_{z \to 1} \frac{z(z+0.5)}{(z-0.3)(z-0.5)} = \frac{1 \times 1.5}{0.7 \times 0.5} = \frac{1.5}{0.35} = \frac{30}{7}$$

**Stability check for FVT:** Poles of $(1 - z^{-1})Y(z) = \dfrac{(z-1)(z+0.5)}{(z-0.3)(z-0.5)(z-1)} = \dfrac{z+0.5}{(z-0.3)(z-0.5)}$ are at $z = 0.3$ and $z = 0.5$, both inside the unit circle. ✓ FVT is valid.

From the time-domain expression: $\lim_{n \to \infty} y[n] = 0 - 0 + \dfrac{30}{7} = \dfrac{30}{7}$ ✓

**The FVT result matches the time-domain limit — verification successful.**

*(7 marks: 2 for $Y(z)$ setup, 3 for PFE computation, 2 for FVT verification)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to divide by $z$ before PFE:** The standard PFE method requires you to expand $X(z)/z$, not $X(z)$ itself. Students who skip this step get a wrong expansion and lose 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Confusing IVT and FVT domains:** IVT uses $z \to \infty$; FVT uses $z \to 1$. Mixing these up is a classic blunder.
> 3. **Applying FVT blindly:** FVT is only valid when poles of $(1 - z^{-1})X(z)$ are inside the unit circle. If you apply it to an unstable system, you will get a "result" that has no physical meaning — the examiner will deduct 2–3 marks for not verifying the condition.
> 4. **Sign errors in difference equation:** A minus sign in the difference equation becomes a *positive* sign in the transfer function denominator (or vice versa, depending on convention). Double-check the sign convention used in your textbook.
> 5. **Missing the unit step $u[n]$:** Inverse Z-transforms of causal sequences always include $u[n]$ in the answer. Omitting it costs 1 mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4 (Inverse ZT, IVT, FVT, LTI Systems):**

- **IVT formula:** $x[0] = \lim_{z \to \infty} X(z)$ — works for *causal* sequences only.
- **FVT formula:** $\lim_{n \to \infty} x[n] = \lim_{z \to 1} (1 - z^{-1}) X(z)$ — requires poles of $(1 - z^{-1})X(z)$ *strictly inside* the unit circle.
- **Three methods of inverse Z-transform:**
  - **Long Division:** Quickest for finding the *first few samples*; not useful for closed-form expressions.
  - **Partial Fraction Expansion:** Industry-standard. **Always expand $X(z)/z$, then multiply back by $z$.**
  - **Residue Method:** Best for complex poles or when ROC is interior.
- **Standard Z-transform pairs to memorize:**
  - $\delta[n] \leftrightarrow 1$
  - $u[n] \leftrightarrow \dfrac{z}{z - 1}$
  - $a^n u[n] \leftrightarrow \dfrac{z}{z - a}$
  - $n a^n u[n] \leftrightarrow \dfrac{az}{(z - a)^2}$
  - $n u[n] \leftrightarrow \dfrac{z}{(z - 1)^2}$
- **Difference Equation → Transfer Function pipeline:**
  1. Take unilateral Z-transform (assume zero initial conditions).
  2. Rearrange to $Y(z) = H(z) X(z)$.
  3. Identify $H(z) = \dfrac{\sum b_k z^{-k}}{\sum a_k z^{-k}}$.
- **Stability Test:** A causal LTI system is **BIBO stable** ⟺ *all* poles of $H(z)$ satisfy $\vert p_k \vert < 1$.
- **Causality Test:** A system is causal ⟺ $h[n] = 0$ for $n < 0$ ⟹ ROC is *exterior* to the outermost pole.
- **FVT Failure Modes:** FVT fails (i) when there are unit-circle poles (other than $z=1$), (ii) when there is a pole at $z=1$ of order $\geq 2$, or (iii) when poles are outside the unit circle.
- **KTU 2024 Exam Weightage:** Module 4 questions typically appear for **14 marks (full Part B question)** with internal choice, and occasionally a 3-mark Part A on IVT/FVT statements.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Characterizing LTI Systems Using Z Transform
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST416 - SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Characterizing LTI Systems Using Z Transform

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of discrete-time **Linear Time-Invariant (LTI) systems**, the **Z-transform** provides a powerful algebraic framework to characterize, analyze, and design systems in the complex z-plane. The characterization of an LTI system using the Z-transform is centered around a single, all-encompassing rational function known as the **System Function** (also called the **Transfer Function**), denoted as $H(z)$.

The System Function is formally defined as the ratio of the Z-transform of the output $y[n]$ to the Z-transform of the input $x[n]$, assuming zero initial conditions (system at rest):

$$H(z) = \frac{Y(z)}{X(z)}$$

For an LTI system described by a **Linear Constant-Coefficient Difference Equation (LCCDE)** of order $N$:

$$\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k \, y[n-k] = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k \, x[n-k]$$

The corresponding System Function, derived by taking the Z-transform of both sides and applying the **time-shifting property** $x[n-k] \longleftrightarrow z^{-k} X(z)$, is:

$$H(z) = \frac{\displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k \, z^{-k}}{\displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k \, z^{-k}} = \frac{B(z)}{A(z)}$$

where $B(z)$ and $A(z)$ are polynomials in $z^{-1}$ (or equivalently in $z$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The System Function $H(z)$ completely characterizes an LTI system. It encapsulates three fundamental properties: **causality**, **stability**, and the **frequency response** of the system. The Region of Convergence (ROC) of $H(z)$ is just as important as the algebraic expression itself, because it uniquely determines causality and stability.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The System's "Genetic Code"

Imagine a sophisticated **vending machine** that accepts a sequence of coin insertions (input $x[n]$) and dispenses a sequence of beverages (output $y[n]$).

- The **System Function $H(z)$** is essentially the **"DNA"** or **"genetic code"** of the machine. Just as DNA determines how a living organism responds to environmental stimuli, $H(z)$ uniquely dictates how the LTI system responds to *any* input.
- The **numerator polynomial $B(z)$** tells us about the **"zeros"** — the input frequencies at which the machine completely refuses to dispense anything (output is zero).
- The **denominator polynomial $A(z)$** tells us about the **"poles"** — the natural resonance patterns or "behavioral quirks" of the machine.
- The **Region of Convergence (ROC)** is like the **"operating envelope"** — it tells us the range of conditions (initial energy states) under which the machine operates correctly and doesn't blow up.

By examining the zeros, poles, and ROC, an engineer can fully predict the system's behavior without ever having to solve the difference equation again.

## 1.3 Physical Significance of Key Quantities

| Quantity | Symbol | Significance |
|----------|--------|--------------|
| System Function | $H(z)$ | Frequency-domain transfer characteristic |
| Order of system | $N$ | Determined by highest pole multiplicity |
| Sampling period | $T_s$ | Inverse of sampling frequency, $f_s$ |
| Pole magnitude | $\vert p_k \vert$ | Determines system stability and damping |
| Zero magnitude | $\vert \zeta_k \vert$ | Determines notch and boost behavior |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Note:** In KTU examinations, the most common mistake students make is forgetting to specify the **ROC** when defining $H(z)$. Two systems with identical algebraic expressions for $H(z)$ but different ROCs are **fundamentally different systems** (e.g., one may be causal while the other is anti-causal). Always state $H(z)$ with its ROC.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Five Pillars of LTI System Characterization

The characterization of an LTI system via the Z-transform rests on five inter-related pillars:

### Pillar 1: System Function $H(z)$ — The Algebraic Identity
The system function is obtained directly from the difference equation by applying the Z-transform and exploiting linearity and time-shifting.

**Why it works:** The time-shifting property $x[n-n_0] \longleftrightarrow z^{-n_0} X(z)$ converts a *time-domain delay* into an *algebraic multiplication by $z^{-1}$*. This transforms a recursive time-domain equation into a simple rational function in $z$.

### Pillar 2: Impulse Response via Inverse Z-Transform
The impulse response $h[n]$ is the inverse Z-transform of $H(z)$:

$$h[n] = \mathcal{Z}^{-1}\{H(z)\}$$

This is the most fundamental time-domain descriptor of the system, since for any input $x[n]$, the output is given by convolution: $y[n] = x[n] * h[n]$, which in the z-domain is simply $Y(z) = H(z) X(z)$.

### Pillar 3: Causality
A discrete-time LTI system is **causal** if and only if the ROC of its system function $H(z)$ is the **exterior of a circle** (i.e., $\vert z \vert > r_{max}$, where $r_{max}$ is the radius of the outermost pole). Equivalently, $h[n] = 0$ for $n < 0$.

**Intuition:** A causal system cannot respond *before* the input arrives. In the z-plane, this translates to the ROC extending to infinity, encompassing all points "outside" the rightmost pole.

### Pillar 4: Stability (BIBO — Bounded-Input Bounded-Output)
A discrete-time LTI system is **BIBO stable** if and only if the ROC of $H(z)$ **includes the unit circle** $\vert z \vert = 1$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Combined Causality and Stability Theorem:** An LTI system is **both causal and stable** if and only if **all poles of $H(z)$ lie strictly inside the unit circle** ($\vert p_k \vert < 1$ for all $k$), and the ROC is $\vert z \vert > \max_k \vert p_k \vert$.

**Physical Intuition:** If a pole lies on or outside the unit circle, the natural response of the system grows without bound, leading to instability. Bounded inputs would then produce unbounded outputs.

### Pillar 5: Frequency Response
When the ROC includes the unit circle, we can evaluate $H(z)$ on $\vert z \vert = 1$, i.e., $z = e^{j\omega}$, to obtain the **frequency response** of the system:

$$H(e^{j\omega}) = H(z) \Big\vert_{z = e^{j\omega}} = \vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert \, e^{j \angle H(e^{j\omega})}$$

- $\vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert$ is the **magnitude response** — how the system amplifies or attenuates different frequency components.
- $\angle H(e^{j\omega})$ is the **phase response** — how the system delays different frequency components.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Condition | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---------|--------------------|--------------------|
| 1 | System Function | $H(z) = Y(z)/X(z)$ | Input-output transfer |
| 2 | From LCCDE | $H(z) = \dfrac{\sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k z^{-k}}{\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k z^{-k}}$ | Direct mapping from difference equation |
| 3 | Impulse Response | $h[n] = \mathcal{Z}^{-1}\{H(z)\}$ | Time-domain signature |
| 4 | Output | $Y(z) = H(z) \, X(z)$ | Algebraic convolution |
| 5 | Causality | ROC is exterior of outermost pole: $\vert z \vert > r_{max}$ | Non-anticipative system |
| 6 | Stability | ROC contains unit circle $\vert z \vert = 1$ | Bounded-input bounded-output |
| 7 | Causal + Stable | All poles $\vert p_k \vert < 1$ (strictly inside unit circle) | Practical realizable system |
| 8 | Frequency Response | $H(e^{j\omega}) = H(z) \big\vert_{z=e^{j\omega}}$ | Steady-state sinusoidal response |
| 9 | Magnitude Response | $\vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert$ | Gain vs frequency |
| 10 | Phase Response | $\angle H(e^{j\omega}) = \arg\{H(e^{j\omega})\}$ | Phase shift vs frequency |
| 11 | Inverse System | $H_i(z) = 1/H(z)$ | Cancels original system |
| 12 | LTI Cascade | $H_{eq}(z) = H_1(z) \, H_2(z)$ | Series connection |
| 13 | LTI Parallel | $H_{eq}(z) = H_1(z) + H_2(z)$ | Parallel connection |
| 14 | All-Pass System | $\vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert = $ constant for all $\omega$ | Distortionless magnitude |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Aid:** A useful mnemonic for the stability condition is **"PIUC"** — **P**oles **I**nside **U**nit **C**ircle ⇒ Stable and Causal system.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Z-transform characterization of LTI systems is the **backbone of digital signal processing (DSP)**. Every practical digital filter — whether it's a Finite Impulse Response (FIR) or Infinite Impulse Response (IIR) filter — is designed by carefully placing poles and zeros in the z-plane to achieve a desired frequency response.

**Practical Applications:**
- **Digital Audio Equalizers**: Poles and zeros shape the bass, mid, and treble.
- **Biomedical Signal Processing**: ECG/EEG filters remove 50/60 Hz power-line interference.
- **Control Systems**: Stability of digital controllers is verified using pole-placement techniques.
- **Communications**: Matched filters, channel equalizers, and echo cancellers all rely on $H(z)$ analysis.
- **Image Processing**: 2-D Z-transforms extend the same framework to spatial filtering.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: System Function $H(z)$ from the LCCDE

**Starting Point:** Consider the general $N$-th order LTI system described by:

$$\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k \, y[n-k] = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k \, x[n-k]$$

with the convention $a_0 = 1$ (without loss of generality, we can normalize the leading coefficient).

**Step 1:** Apply the Z-transform to *both sides* of the equation. Using linearity of the Z-transform:

$$\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k \, \mathcal{Z}\{y[n-k]\} = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k \, \mathcal{Z}\{x[n-k]\}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the **time-shifting property**: $\mathcal{Z}\{y[n-k]\} = z^{-k} Y(z)$, assuming zero initial conditions. Similarly for $x[n-k]$:

$$\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k \, z^{-k} Y(z) = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k \, z^{-k} X(z)$$

**Step 3:** Factor out $Y(z)$ on the left-hand side and $X(z)$ on the right-hand side:

$$Y(z) \sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k \, z^{-k} = X(z) \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k \, z^{-k}$$

**Step 4:** Solve for the ratio $H(z) = Y(z)/X(z)$:

$$H(z) = \frac{\displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k \, z^{-k}}{\displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k \, z^{-k}}$$

**Step 5 (Optional Conversion to Positive Powers of $z$):** Multiply numerator and denominator by $z^{\max(M,N)}$ to obtain the equivalent form in positive powers of $z$. For instance, with $M = N$:

$$H(z) = \frac{\displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k \, z^{M-k}}{\displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k \, z^{N-k}} = \frac{b_0 z^M + b_1 z^{M-1} + \cdots + b_M}{a_0 z^N + a_1 z^{N-1} + \cdots + a_N}$$

This form makes it easier to identify the zeros and poles.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Causality Condition from the ROC

**Claim:** A discrete-time LTI system is causal if and only if the ROC of $H(z)$ is the exterior of a circle, $\vert z \vert > r_{max}$.

**Proof Outline:**

- The impulse response $h[n]$ and $H(z)$ form a Z-transform pair: $H(z) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} h[n] \, z^{-n}$.
- For **causality**, we require $h[n] = 0$ for $n < 0$. The sum reduces to $H(z) = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} h[n] \, z^{-n}$, a **one-sided** (right-sided) sequence.
- A right-sided sequence has an ROC of the form $\vert z \vert > r_{max}$ (converges for $\vert z \vert$ large enough).
- Conversely, if the ROC is $\vert z \vert > r_{max}$, then $H(z)$ corresponds to a right-sided sequence, hence $h[n] = 0$ for $n < 0$, and the system is causal.

**Q.E.D.**

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Stability Condition — BIBO Criterion

**Claim:** An LTI system is BIBO stable iff the ROC of $H(z)$ contains the unit circle $\vert z \vert = 1$.

**Proof Outline:**

BIBO stability requires that for every bounded input $\vert x[n] \vert \leq M_x < \infty$, the output remains bounded: $\vert y[n] \vert \leq M_y < \infty$.

$$y[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} h[k] \, x[n-k]$$

Taking absolute values and applying the bound on $x$:

$$\vert y[n] \vert \leq \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert h[k] \vert \cdot \vert x[n-k] \vert \leq M_x \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert h[k] \vert$$

For $y[n]$ to be bounded, we need the **absolute summability** condition:

$$\sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert h[k] \vert < \infty$$

This is equivalent to the convergence of the Z-transform on the unit circle:

$$H(z) \Big\vert_{\vert z \vert = 1} = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} h[n] \, e^{-j\omega n}$$

to be finite. Therefore, the ROC must include $\vert z \vert = 1$.

**Q.E.D.**

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Frequency Response from $H(z)$ on the Unit Circle

Starting with the discrete-time Fourier transform (DTFT) of the impulse response:

$$H(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} h[n] \, e^{-j\omega n}$$

Substitute $z = e^{j\omega}$ (so $\vert z \vert = 1$) into $H(z) = \sum_{n} h[n] z^{-n}$:

$$H(e^{j\omega}) = H(z) \Big\vert_{z = e^{j\omega}}$$

**Geometric Interpretation:** Evaluating $H(z)$ on the unit circle is equivalent to "sweeping" a probe around the unit circle and recording the magnitude and phase of $H(z)$ at each point. The closer a pole is to the unit circle, the more $H(e^{j\omega})$ peaks near the corresponding angle $\omega$.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: System Properties at a Glance

Let $H(z) = K \cdot \dfrac{(z - \zeta_1)(z - \zeta_2) \cdots (z - \zeta_M)}{(z - p_1)(z - p_2) \cdots (z - p_N)}$ where $K$ is a gain constant.

| Property | Condition in Z-Domain | Engineering Interpretation |
|----------|----------------------|----------------------------|
| Causal System | ROC: $\vert z \vert > \max(\vert p_k \vert)$ | System responds only after input |
| Anti-Causal System | ROC: $\vert z \vert < \min(\vert p_k \vert)$ | System responds only before input |
| Stable System | ROC includes $\vert z \vert = 1$ | All bounded inputs give bounded outputs |
| Causal + Stable | All $\vert p_k \vert < 1$, ROC: $\vert z \vert > \max(\vert p_k \vert)$ | Realizable practical filter |
| All-Pass System | $\vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert = K$ for all $\omega$ | Passes all frequencies with equal gain |
| Minimum Phase | All zeros $\vert \zeta_k \vert \leq 1$ | Minimum group delay |
| Linear Phase | $h[n] = \pm h[N-n]$ (symmetric) | Constant group delay (FIR only) |
| FIR System | $A(z) = 1$ (no poles except at $z=0$) | Always stable |
| IIR System | $A(z) \neq 1$ (poles away from origin) | Can be unstable |
| Inverse System | $H_i(z) = 1/H(z)$ | Exactly cancels original system |

## 3.6 Worked Example: Characterizing a First-Order System

**Problem:** Consider the LTI system described by:
$$y[n] - 0.5 \, y[n-1] = x[n]$$

**Step 1:** Apply Z-transform to both sides:
$$Y(z) - 0.5 \, z^{-1} Y(z) = X(z)$$

**Step 2:** Factor out $Y(z)$:
$$Y(z) \left[ 1 - 0.5 \, z^{-1} \right] = X(z)$$

**Step 3:** Solve for $H(z) = Y(z)/X(z)$:
$$H(z) = \frac{1}{1 - 0.5 \, z^{-1}} = \frac{z}{z - 0.5}$$

**Step 4:** Identify pole and zero:
- **Zero:** $z = 0$ (numerator zero)
- **Pole:** $z = 0.5$ (denominator zero)

**Step 5:** Determine ROC for causality: $\vert z \vert > 0.5$.

**Step 6:** Check stability: The unit circle $\vert z \vert = 1$ lies within ROC ($\vert z \vert > 0.5$), so the system is **stable**.

**Step 7:** Compute impulse response by inverse Z-transform:
$$h[n] = (0.5)^n \, u[n]$$

This is a right-sided, decaying exponential — confirming causality and stability.

**Step 8:** Compute frequency response:
$$H(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{1}{1 - 0.5 \, e^{-j\omega}} = \frac{1}{1 - 0.5 \cos\omega + j \, 0.5 \sin\omega}$$

**Magnitude:**
$$\vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(1 - 0.5\cos\omega)^2 + (0.5 \sin\omega)^2}}$$

**Phase:**
$$\angle H(e^{j\omega}) = -\arctan\left(\frac{0.5 \sin\omega}{1 - 0.5 \cos\omega}\right)$$

This system acts as a **low-pass filter**, since $\vert H(e^{j0}) \vert = 1/0.5 = 2$ (maximum at DC) and $\vert H(e^{j\pi}) \vert = 1/1.5$ (minimum at Nyquist).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Flow: From Difference Equation to System Function

The following block diagram illustrates the systematic workflow for characterizing an LTI system using the Z-transform:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: LCCDE in Time Domain] --> B[Step 1: Apply Z-Transform to Both Sides]
    B --> C[Step 2: Apply Time-Shifting Property]
    C --> D[Step 3: Rearrange into Y z equals H z times X z]
    D --> E[Step 4: Extract H z equals Y z over X z]
    E --> F[Step 5: Identify Poles and Zeros in z-Plane]
    F --> G[Step 6: Determine ROC from Pole Locations]
    G --> H{Decision: Causality and Stability}
    H -->|ROC exterior of poles| I[Causal System]
    H -->|ROC includes unit circle| J[Stable System]
    I --> K[Step 7: Compute Impulse Response h of n]
    J --> L[Step 8: Evaluate H z on Unit Circle]
    K --> M[Step 9: Magnitude and Phase Response]
    L --> M
    M --> N[Final Output: Complete System Characterization]

    style A fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#0277BD
    style N fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32
    style H fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F9A825
    style F fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100
```

## 4.2 Pole-Zero Plot for a Second-Order Bandpass System

A typical bandpass filter can be characterized by the pole-zero configuration shown below. The poles are placed symmetrically inside the unit circle at the desired center frequency, and the zeros are placed on the unit circle to reject DC and Nyquist frequencies.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ZPlane[Z-Plane Pole-Zero Configuration]
        direction TB
        Z1[Zero at z equals 1]:::zero
        Z2[Zero at z equals negative 1]:::zero
        P1[Pole at radius 0.8 angle pi over 4]:::pole
        P2[Pole at radius 0.8 angle negative pi over 4]:::pole
        UC[Unit Circle radius equals 1]:::circle
    end

    Z1 --- UC
    Z2 --- UC
    P1 --- UC
    P2 --- UC

    classDef zero fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#000
    classDef pole fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
    classDef circle fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Cascaded LTI System Characterization

The functional block diagram below shows how multiple LTI sub-systems combine and how the overall system function is obtained:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X[Input X of n] --> H1[Subsystem H1 of z] --> H2[Subsystem H2 of z] --> Y[Output Y of n]
    H1 --> P1[Pole-Zero Map 1]
    H2 --> P2[Pole-Zero Map 2]
    P1 --> C[Combine: H equals H1 times H2]
    P2 --> C
    C --> R[Overall ROC: Intersection]
    C --> S[Stability: Check All Poles Inside Unit Circle]
    R --> S
    S --> F[Final H of z and Frequency Response]

    style X fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#1565C0
    style Y fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32
    style C fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17
    style S fill:#FFCCBC,stroke:#BF360C
    style F fill:#D1C4E9,stroke:#4527A0
```

## 4.4 Decision Tree: Stability and Causality Assessment

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Given H of z with Poles p1 p2 ... pN] --> CheckPoles{All poles strictly inside unit circle?}
    CheckPoles -->|Yes| CausStable[Causal AND Stable]
    CheckPoles -->|No| CheckOnCircle{Any pole ON unit circle?}
    CheckOnCircle -->|Yes| Unstable1[NOT BIBO Stable: Marginal]
    CheckOnCircle -->|No| CheckOutside{Any pole OUTSIDE unit circle?}
    CheckOutside -->|Yes| Unstable2[NOT BIBO Stable: Definitely Unstable]
    CheckOutside -->|No| Review[Review Pole Calculations]

    CausStable --> Rec1[ROC equals |z| greater than max |p sub k|]
    Unstable1 --> Rec2[Unit circle not in ROC]
    Unstable2 --> Rec3[Unit circle not in ROC: Output grows unbounded]

    style Start fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#0D47A1
    style CausStable fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20
    style Unstable1 fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C
    style Unstable2 fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C
    style Rec1 fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20
    style Rec2 fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C
    style Rec3 fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C
```

## 4.5 Functional Architecture: Inverse System and Equalization

In communication systems, the inverse of an LTI channel is used to undo the distortion introduced by the channel. The Z-transform framework makes this rigorous:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S[Source Signal s of n] --> TX[Transmitter] --> CH[Channel H ch of z] --> RX[Receiver Input r of n]
    RX --> EQ[Equalizer H inv of z] --> OUT[Equalized Output s hat of n]
    CH --> Mul{H ch times H inv equals 1}
    EQ --> Mul
    Mul --> Verify[Ideal Recovery: s hat equals s]

    style S fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#1565C0
    style CH fill:#FFCCBC,stroke:#BF360C
    style EQ fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32
    style OUT fill:#D1C4E9,stroke:#4527A0
    style Verify fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Note:** In practice, exact inversion is not always possible because the inverse system $H_{inv}(z) = 1/H(z)$ may itself be unstable (poles of $H_{inv}(z)$ are the zeros of $H(z)$, which often lie outside the unit circle). Adaptive equalizers in modems (e.g., LMS algorithm) approximate the inverse under these constraints.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define the system function $H(z)$ of an LTI system. How is it related to the impulse response $h[n]$?**

**Model Answer:**

The system function $H(z)$ of a discrete-time LTI system is defined as the ratio of the Z-transform of the output $Y(z)$ to the Z-transform of the input $X(z)$, assuming zero initial conditions:

$$H(z) = \frac{Y(z)}{X(z)}$$

It is related to the impulse response $h[n]$ via the Z-transform pair:

$$H(z) = \mathcal{Z}\{h[n]\} = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} h[n] \, z^{-n}$$

with an associated Region of Convergence (ROC). The impulse response is obtained as $h[n] = \mathcal{Z}^{-1}\{H(z)\}$. The system function completely characterizes the LTI system in the frequency domain, and $H(z)$ evaluated on the unit circle gives the frequency response $H(e^{j\omega})$.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**State and briefly explain the conditions for an LTI system to be causal and BIBO stable in terms of its system function $H(z)$.**

**Model Answer:**

**Causality:** An LTI system is causal if and only if the ROC of $H(z)$ is the exterior of a circle, $\vert z \vert > r_{max}$, where $r_{max}$ is the magnitude of the outermost pole. Equivalently, $h[n] = 0$ for $n < 0$.

**BIBO Stability:** An LTI system is BIBO stable if and only if the ROC of $H(z)$ includes the unit circle $\vert z \vert = 1$, which is equivalent to the absolute summability $\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert h[n] \vert < \infty$.

**Combined Condition:** For an LTI system to be both causal and stable, all poles of $H(z)$ must lie strictly *inside* the unit circle ($\vert p_k \vert < 1$ for all $k$), and the ROC is $\vert z \vert > \max_k \vert p_k \vert$.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**For the LTI system described by the difference equation:**
$$y[n] - \frac{3}{4} y[n-1] + \frac{1}{8} y[n-2] = 2 \, x[n]$$

**Answer the following:**

**(a)** Find the system function $H(z)$ including its ROC. Is the system stable? Justify. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Find the impulse response $h[n]$ of the system. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

**Step 1: Apply Z-transform to both sides.**

Taking the Z-transform and using the time-shifting property:

$$Y(z) - \frac{3}{4} z^{-1} Y(z) + \frac{1}{8} z^{-2} Y(z) = 2 \, X(z)$$

**Step 2: Factor out $Y(z)$.**

$$Y(z) \left[ 1 - \frac{3}{4} z^{-1} + \frac{1}{8} z^{-2} \right] = 2 \, X(z)$$

**Step 3: Solve for $H(z) = Y(z)/X(z)$.**

$$H(z) = \frac{2}{1 - \frac{3}{4} z^{-1} + \frac{1}{8} z^{-2}}$$

**Step 4: Factor the denominator to find poles.**

The denominator polynomial in $z$ (after multiplying by $z^2$):

$$z^2 - \frac{3}{4} z + \frac{1}{8} = 0$$

Using the quadratic formula:

$$z = \frac{\frac{3}{4} \pm \sqrt{\frac{9}{16} - \frac{4}{8}}}{2} = \frac{\frac{3}{4} \pm \sqrt{\frac{9 - 8}{16}}}{2} = \frac{\frac{3}{4} \pm \frac{1}{4}}{2}$$

Therefore:

$$p_1 = \frac{3/4 + 1/4}{2} = \frac{1}{2}, \quad p_2 = \frac{3/4 - 1/4}{2} = \frac{1}{4}$$

**Step 5: Express $H(z)$ in partial fraction form.**

$$H(z) = \frac{2}{\left(1 - \frac{1}{2} z^{-1}\right)\left(1 - \frac{1}{4} z^{-1}\right)}$$

Performing partial fraction decomposition:

$$H(z) = \frac{A}{1 - \frac{1}{2} z^{-1}} + \frac{B}{1 - \frac{1}{4} z^{-1}}$$

Solving: $A = 4$ and $B = -2$. (Verify: $\frac{4(1 - \frac{1}{4} z^{-1}) - 2(1 - \frac{1}{2} z^{-1})}{(1 - \frac{1}{2} z^{-1})(1 - \frac{1}{4} z^{-1})} = \frac{2}{(1 - \frac{1}{2} z^{-1})(1 - \frac{1}{4} z^{-1})}$ ✓)

**Step 6: Determine ROC.**

For a causal system, the ROC is the exterior of the outermost pole:

$$\text{ROC}: \vert z \vert > \frac{1}{2}$$

**Step 7: Check stability.**

Both poles $\vert p_1 \vert = \frac{1}{2} < 1$ and $\vert p_2 \vert = \frac{1}{4} < 1$ lie strictly inside the unit circle. The unit circle is in the ROC. **Therefore, the system is BIBO stable.**

---

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**Step 1: Apply the inverse Z-transform to $H(z)$.**

Using the standard pair $\mathcal{Z}^{-1}\left\{\dfrac{1}{1 - a z^{-1}}\right\} = a^n u[n]$:

$$h[n] = 4 \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^n u[n] - 2 \left(\frac{1}{4}\right)^n u[n]$$

**Step 2: Write out the first few values to verify.**

- $h[0] = 4(1) - 2(1) = 2$
- $h[1] = 4(0.5) - 2(0.25) = 2 - 0.5 = 1.5$
- $h[2] = 4(0.25) - 2(0.0625) = 1 - 0.125 = 0.875$
- $h[3] = 4(0.125) - 2(0.015625) = 0.5 - 0.03125 = 0.46875$

The impulse response is a right-sided, decaying sequence — confirming causality and stability.

**Step 3: Final answer.**

$$\boxed{h[n] = \left[4 \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^n - 2 \left(\frac{1}{4}\right)^n\right] u[n]}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Alternative Choice]

**Consider an LTI system with system function:**
$$H(z) = \frac{1 - z^{-2}}{1 - 0.5 \, z^{-1}}, \quad \text{ROC}: \vert z \vert > 0.5$$

**Answer the following:**

**(a)** Determine the difference equation relating input and output. Find all poles and zeros, and plot them in the z-plane. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Determine whether the system is causal and stable. Compute and sketch the magnitude response $\vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert$ for $\omega \in [0, \pi]$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

**Step 1: Cross-multiply to obtain the difference equation.**

$$H(z) = \frac{Y(z)}{X(z)} = \frac{1 - z^{-2}}{1 - 0.5 \, z^{-1}}$$

Cross-multiplying:

$$Y(z) (1 - 0.5 \, z^{-1}) = X(z) (1 - z^{-2})$$

$$Y(z) - 0.5 \, z^{-1} Y(z) = X(z) - z^{-2} X(z)$$

**Step 2: Apply the inverse Z-transform using the time-shifting property.**

$$\boxed{y[n] - 0.5 \, y[n-1] = x[n] - x[n-2]}$$

**Step 3: Find the zeros.**

Set the numerator $1 - z^{-2} = 0$:
$$z^{-2} = 1 \implies z^2 = 1 \implies z = \pm 1$$

**Zeros:** $z_1 = 1$ and $z_2 = -1$.

**Step 4: Find the poles.**

Set the denominator $1 - 0.5 \, z^{-1} = 0$:
$$z = 0.5$$

**Pole:** $p_1 = 0.5$ (a single real pole inside the unit circle).

**Step 5: Pole-zero plot description.**

The zeros are at $z = +1$ and $z = -1$, both on the unit circle. The pole is at $z = 0.5$, inside the unit circle. The pole-zero plot shows the unit circle with a single pole at $(0.5, 0)$ and two zeros at $(1, 0)$ and $(-1, 0)$.

---

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**Step 1: Causality.**

The ROC is given as $\vert z \vert > 0.5$, which is the **exterior of the outermost pole**. This is consistent with a right-sided (causal) impulse response. **Therefore, the system is causal.**

**Step 2: Stability.**

The unit circle $\vert z \vert = 1$ is contained in the ROC ($\vert z \vert > 0.5$ includes the unit circle). Equivalently, the only pole is at $z = 0.5$, with $\vert 0.5 \vert < 1$. **Therefore, the system is BIBO stable.**

**Step 3: Frequency response by evaluating $H(z)$ on the unit circle.**

$$H(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{1 - e^{-j2\omega}}{1 - 0.5 \, e^{-j\omega}}$$

**Step 4: Simplify the magnitude.**

Using the identity $1 - e^{-j2\omega} = e^{-j\omega}(e^{j\omega} - e^{-j\omega}) = 2j \, e^{-j\omega} \sin\omega$:

$$\vert 1 - e^{-j2\omega} \vert = 2 \vert \sin\omega \vert$$

And the denominator magnitude:

$$\vert 1 - 0.5 \, e^{-j\omega} \vert = \sqrt{(1 - 0.5\cos\omega)^2 + (0.5\sin\omega)^2} = \sqrt{1 - \cos\omega + 0.25}$$

**Step 5: Magnitude response expression.**

$$\boxed{\vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert = \frac{2 \vert \sin\omega \vert}{\sqrt{1.25 - \cos\omega}}}$$

**Step 6: Evaluate at key frequencies.**

- At $\omega = 0$: $\vert H(e^{j0}) \vert = 0 / \sqrt{0.25} = 0$ (zero at $z=1$ blocks DC).
- At $\omega = \pi/2$: $\vert H(e^{j\pi/2}) \vert = 2 / \sqrt{1.25} = 2 / 1.118 \approx 1.789$.
- At $\omega = \pi$: $\vert H(e^{j\pi}) \vert = 0 / \sqrt{2.25} = 0$ (zero at $z=-1$ blocks Nyquist).

**Step 7: Sketches and interpretation.**

The magnitude response is zero at both DC ($\omega = 0$) and Nyquist ($\omega = \pi$), with a peak near $\omega = \pi/2$. This makes the system a **bandpass filter** that suppresses very low and very high frequencies while passing mid-band frequencies. The zeros on the unit circle at $z = \pm 1$ are responsible for the nulls at $\omega = 0$ and $\omega = \pi$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the ROC:** Many students write $H(z)$ without specifying the ROC. This can cost **2–3 marks** as the ROC is critical for determining causality and stability.
> 2. **Confusing poles and zeros:** Poles are roots of the *denominator*; zeros are roots of the *numerator*. Reversing this is a frequent error.
> 3. **Pole magnitude check:** A pole at $z = 1.2$ looks "almost on the unit circle" but is **strictly outside**, making the system unstable. Always check $\vert p_k \vert$ rigorously.
> 4. **Inverse Z-transform mistakes:** Students often forget the unit step $u[n]$ in causal impulse responses, leading to incorrect two-sided sequences.
> 5. **Magnitude vs. complex magnitude:** When computing $\vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert$, use $\sqrt{(\text{Re})^2 + (\text{Im})^2}$, not the algebraic sum. The sinusoidal simplification $\vert 1 - e^{-j2\omega} \vert = 2\vert \sin\omega \vert$ is a useful shortcut worth memorizing.
> 6. **Initial conditions:** When applying the Z-transform to a difference equation, **zero initial conditions** are assumed. If the problem specifies non-zero initial conditions, the unilateral Z-transform must be used.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- **System Function:** $H(z) = Y(z)/X(z)$ — the cornerstone of LTI system analysis in the z-domain.
- **LCCDE to $H(z)$:** Replace $y[n-k]$ with $z^{-k} Y(z)$ and $x[n-k]$ with $z^{-k} X(z)$.
- **Impulse Response:** $h[n] = \mathcal{Z}^{-1}\{H(z)\}$ — the time-domain dual of $H(z)$.
- **Causality ↔ ROC is exterior of outermost pole:** $\vert z \vert > \max_k \vert p_k \vert$.
- **BIBO Stability ↔ ROC contains unit circle:** $\vert z \vert = 1 \in \text{ROC}$.
- **Causal + Stable ↔ ALL poles inside unit circle:** $\vert p_k \vert < 1$ for all $k$.
- **FIR systems have only poles at $z = 0$:** hence **always stable** (no poles outside the unit circle).
- **IIR systems can be unstable:** if any pole lies on or outside the unit circle.
- **Frequency Response:** $H(e^{j\omega}) = H(z)\big\vert_{z=e^{j\omega}}$ — valid only when ROC includes the unit circle.
- **Magnitude peaks near poles:** the closer a pole to the unit circle, the sharper the resonance peak in $\vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert$.
- **Zeros on the unit circle → nulls in magnitude response:** at the corresponding frequency $\omega$.
- **All-pass system:** $\vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert = $ constant — a special class of phase equalizers.
- **Inverse system:** $H_{inv}(z) = 1/H(z)$ — the zeros of $H(z)$ become the poles of $H_{inv}(z)$, so the inverse may be unstable.
- **Cascade:** $H_{total}(z) = H_1(z) \cdot H_2(z)$; poles and zeros combine.
- **Parallel:** $H_{total}(z) = H_1(z) + H_2(z)$; partial fractions combine.
- **Mnemonic — PIUC:** **P**oles **I**nside **U**nit **C**ircle ⇒ Stable Causal system.
- **Always specify ROC** when writing $H(z)$ — it is part of the complete description.
- **KTU-Favorite Trap:** Two systems can share the *same* $H(z)$ expression but have *different* ROCs, making them *different* systems (e.g., causal vs. anti-causal).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transfer Function of an LTI System. Solving Difference Equations Using the Unilateral Z Transform. Block Diagram Representation of Discrete-Time LTI Systems
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST416 - SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Transfer Function of an LTI System. Solving Difference Equations Using the Unilateral Z Transform. Block Diagram Representation of Discrete-Time LTI Systems

# 🎯 Module 4 — Transfer Function, Z-Transform Solutions & Block Diagrams

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Transfer Function $H(z)$ of a Discrete-Time LTI System

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
The **Transfer Function** of a Linear Time-Invariant (LTI) discrete-time system is defined as the ratio of the **Z-transform of the output response** $Y(z)$ to the **Z-transform of the input** $X(z)$, evaluated under the assumption of **zero initial conditions** (Zero-State Response / ZSR).

$$H(z) \;=\; \frac{Y(z)}{X(z)} \quad \text{with} \quad x[n] = 0 \; \text{for} \; n < 0$$

In other words, $H(z)$ is the **Z-domain equivalent** of the impulse response $h[n]$:

$$H(z) = \mathcal{Z}\{h[n]\} = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} h[n]\, z^{-n}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Emphasis:** The transfer function is defined **only** for **LTI systems**. It is a *system property*, independent of the input signal. It captures the entire input-output behavior of the system in the Z-domain.

---

### 1.2 The Unilateral Z-Transform

**Formal Definition:**
The **Unilateral (One-Sided) Z-Transform** of a discrete-time signal $x[n]$ is defined as:

$$X(z) \;=\; \mathcal{Z}\{x[n]\} \;=\; \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} x[n]\, z^{-n}$$

This is in contrast to the **Bilateral Z-Transform**, which sums from $n=-\infty$ to $+\infty$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Unilateral?** It is the standard tool for solving **causal difference equations with arbitrary initial conditions**, because the unilateral transform naturally encodes the system's initial state (initial rest energy, capacitor voltages, register contents, etc.) — exactly what KTU examiners test.

**Real-World Intuition — The Three Analogies:**

| Concept | Plain-English Analogy |
|---|---|
| **Transfer Function $H(z)$** | A coffee machine's "recipe book" — knowing the recipe, you can predict exactly what coffee you get from any beans (input). |
| **Unilateral Z-Transform** | A security camera that *only* records events from *door-opening onward* — it ignores the past, but remembers the initial state of the room. |
| **Block Diagram** | An engineer's blueprint of the coffee machine — showing how water, beans, and heat flow from input to output. |

---

### 1.3 Block Diagram Representation of Discrete-Time LTI Systems

A block diagram is a **graphical schematic** that depicts how an LTI system's components — adders, multipliers (gain blocks), unit delays ($z^{-1}$), and pickup nodes — interconnect to realize the difference equation.

**The Three Fundamental Building Blocks:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Primitive Elements (must memorize for KTU 2024):**
> 1. **Adder (Σ):** Sums two or more signal sequences.
> 2. **Multiplier (Gain block $a$):** Multiplies the signal by a constant coefficient.
> 3. **Unit Delay ($z^{-1}$):** Delays the input by one sample, i.e., $y[n] = x[n-1]$.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 From Difference Equation to Transfer Function

A general **N-th order Linear Constant Coefficient Difference Equation (LCCDE)** is:

$$\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k\, y[n-k] \;=\; \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k\, x[n-k]$$

where $a_0 = 1$ (by convention, normalized).

**Step-by-Step Logic for Deriving $H(z)$:**

1. **Apply the Unilateral Z-Transform** to both sides of the LCCDE.
2. **Use the time-shift property:** $\mathcal{Z}\{x[n-k]\} = z^{-k}X(z) + \sum_{m=0}^{k-1} x[m]\, z^{-(k-m)}$
3. **Set initial conditions to zero** (i.e., $x[n] = 0$ and $y[n] = 0$ for $n < 0$) to obtain the *Zero-State Transfer Function*.
4. **Take the ratio** $H(z) = Y(z)/X(z)$.

**Final Canonical Form of the Transfer Function:**

$$H(z) \;=\; \frac{Y(z)}{X(z)} \;=\; \frac{\displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k\, z^{-k}}{\displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k\, z^{-k}} \;=\; \frac{b_0 + b_1 z^{-1} + b_2 z^{-2} + \cdots + b_M z^{-M}}{1 + a_1 z^{-1} + a_2 z^{-2} + \cdots + a_N z^{-N}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Trick:** The transfer function $H(z)$ is the **rational function** whose numerator coefficients $\{b_k\}$ are the feed-forward (input) coefficients of the LCCDE, and denominator coefficients $\{a_k\}$ are the feedback (output) coefficients.

---

### 2.2 Solving Difference Equations Using the Unilateral Z-Transform

**The Three-Stage KTU Board Method:**

1. **Apply Unilateral Z-Transform** to the LCCDE — incorporate initial conditions via the time-shift property.
2. **Algebraically solve for $Y(z)$** in terms of $X(z)$ and the initial condition terms.
3. **Take the Inverse Z-Transform** using partial fraction expansion and standard Z-transform pairs to get $y[n]$.

**Crucial Time-Shift Property of the Unilateral Z-Transform:**

$$\mathcal{Z}\{x[n-k]\} \;=\; z^{-k} X(z) \;+\; \sum_{n=0}^{k-1} x[n]\, z^{-(k-n)}$$

| Shift Direction | Unilateral Z-Transform Formula |
|---|---|
| $k=1$ (one-step delay) | $z^{-1}X(z) + x[-1]$ |
| $k=2$ (two-step delay) | $z^{-2}X(z) + x[-1]z^{-1} + x[-2]$ |
| General $k$ | $z^{-k}X(z) + \sum_{n=0}^{k-1} x[n]\,z^{-(k-n)}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> This summation term is the "memory" of the past — it carries the **initial conditions** from $n<0$ forward in time.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Property |
|---|---------|--------------------|
| 1 | **Transfer Function Definition** | $H(z) = Y(z)/X(z)$ (zero initial conditions) |
| 2 | **General LCCDE Form** | $\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k y[n-k] = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k x[n-k]$ |
| 3 | **Canonical Transfer Function** | $H(z) = \dfrac{b_0 + b_1 z^{-1} + \cdots + b_M z^{-M}}{1 + a_1 z^{-1} + \cdots + a_N z^{-N}}$ |
| 4 | **Unilateral Z-Transform** | $X(z) = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} x[n]\,z^{-n}$ |
| 5 | **ROC of Unilateral Z-Transform** | Always **exterior** of the outermost pole (since signal is right-sided for $n \ge 0$) |
| 6 | **Time-Shift (Unilateral)** | $\mathcal{Z}\{x[n-k]\} = z^{-k}X(z) + \sum_{n=0}^{k-1} x[n]\,z^{-(k-n)}$ |
| 7 | **Linearity** | $\mathcal{Z}\{a x[n] + b y[n]\} = aX(z) + bY(z)$ |
| 8 | **Convolution Theorem** | $y[n] = x[n] * h[n] \;\Longleftrightarrow\; Y(z) = X(z) H(z)$ |
| 9 | **Unit Delay Element** | $y[n] = x[n-1] \;\Longleftrightarrow\; H(z) = z^{-1}$ |
| 10 | **Causality Condition** | ROC of $H(z)$ must include $z = \infty$ for causal systems |
| 11 | **Stability Condition** | All poles of $H(z)$ must lie **inside** the unit circle $\vert z \vert = 1$ |
| 12 | **Standard Z-Pairs** | $\mathcal{Z}\{u[n]\} = \dfrac{1}{1 - z^{-1}} = \dfrac{z}{z-1}$ |
| 13 | **Standard Z-Pairs** | $\mathcal{Z}\{\delta[n]\} = 1$ |
| 14 | **Standard Z-Pairs** | $\mathcal{Z}\{a^n u[n]\} = \dfrac{1}{1 - a z^{-1}}$ |

---

### 2.4 Engineering Utility of These Concepts

* **Digital Filter Design:** IIR and FIR filters are designed by placing poles and zeros of $H(z)$ inside the unit circle.
* **DSP Processors (Texas Instruments C6000, ARM Cortex-M):** Implement the LCCDE directly using the block diagram topology in real-time.
* **Control Systems:** State-space controllers and digital controllers (PID) are analyzed and synthesized using $H(z)$.
* **Audio Processing (MP3 codecs, equalizers):** Bi-quad sections are cascade structures of 2nd-order transfer functions.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From a General LCCDE to the Transfer Function

Consider the **second-order LCCDE** (general N=2, M=2 case):

$$y[n] - \tfrac{3}{4} y[n-1] + \tfrac{1}{8} y[n-2] \;=\; 2 x[n]$$

**Step 1:** Apply the unilateral Z-transform to every term, with the assumption of **zero initial conditions** ($x[n] = 0$, $y[n] = 0$ for all $n < 0$).

**Step 2:** Use the time-shift property $\mathcal{Z}\{y[n-k]\} = z^{-k}Y(z)$ (because initial conditions vanish under the zero-initial-condition assumption).

**Step 3:** Substitute into the LCCDE.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{Z}\{y[n]\} - \tfrac{3}{4}\mathcal{Z}\{y[n-1]\} + \tfrac{1}{8}\mathcal{Z}\{y[n-2]\} &= 2\mathcal{Z}\{x[n]\}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y(z) - \tfrac{3}{4}z^{-1}Y(z) + \tfrac{1}{8}z^{-2}Y(z) &= 2 X(z)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Factor out $Y(z)$ on the left-hand side.

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y(z)\Big[1 - \tfrac{3}{4}z^{-1} + \tfrac{1}{8}z^{-2}\Big] &= 2 X(z)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Form the ratio $H(z) = Y(z) / X(z)$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(z) \;=\; \frac{Y(z)}{X(z)} \;=\; \frac{2}{1 - \tfrac{3}{4}z^{-1} + \tfrac{1}{8}z^{-2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 (Verification):** Multiply numerator and denominator by $z^2$ to express in positive powers of $z$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(z) \;=\; \frac{2 z^{2}}{z^{2} - \tfrac{3}{4}z + \tfrac{1}{8}}
\end{aligned}
$$

The poles are the roots of $z^2 - \tfrac{3}{4}z + \tfrac{1}{8} = 0$, which gives $z = \tfrac{1}{2}$ and $z = \tfrac{1}{4}$ — both inside the unit circle, confirming the system is **stable and causal**.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Example: Solving an LCCDE with Non-Zero Initial Conditions

**Problem Statement (KTU 2024 Typical):**
Solve the following difference equation for $y[n]$, $n \ge 0$, using the unilateral Z-transform:

$$y[n] - \tfrac{1}{2} y[n-1] = 5 \left(\tfrac{1}{3}\right)^n u[n]$$

Given initial condition: $y[-1] = 2$.

---

**Step 1: Apply the Unilateral Z-Transform to the LHS.**

Using the time-shift property with $k=1$:

$$\mathcal{Z}\{y[n-1]\} = z^{-1}Y(z) + y[-1]$$

Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y(z) - \tfrac{1}{2}\left[z^{-1}Y(z) + y[-1]\right] &= 5 \cdot \frac{1}{1 - \tfrac{1}{3}z^{-1}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Substitute $y[-1] = 2$ and simplify.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y(z) - \tfrac{1}{2}z^{-1}Y(z) - \tfrac{1}{2}(2) &= \frac{5}{1 - \tfrac{1}{3}z^{-1}}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y(z)\left[1 - \tfrac{1}{2}z^{-1}\right] - 1 &= \frac{5}{1 - \tfrac{1}{3}z^{-1}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Isolate $Y(z)$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y(z)\left[1 - \tfrac{1}{2}z^{-1}\right] &= 1 + \frac{5}{1 - \tfrac{1}{3}z^{-1}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Combine the RHS over a common denominator:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y(z)\left[1 - \tfrac{1}{2}z^{-1}\right] &= \frac{(1 - \tfrac{1}{3}z^{-1}) + 5}{1 - \tfrac{1}{3}z^{-1}} = \frac{6 - \tfrac{1}{3}z^{-1}}{1 - \tfrac{1}{3}z^{-1}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Solve for $Y(z)$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y(z) &= \frac{6 - \tfrac{1}{3}z^{-1}}{(1 - \tfrac{1}{2}z^{-1})(1 - \tfrac{1}{3}z^{-1})}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Partial Fraction Decomposition.**

Express as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y(z) = \frac{A}{1 - \tfrac{1}{2}z^{-1}} + \frac{B}{1 - \tfrac{1}{3}z^{-1}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Solving (cover-up method):

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \left.\frac{6 - \tfrac{1}{3}z^{-1}}{1 - \tfrac{1}{3}z^{-1}}\right|_{z^{-1}=2} = \frac{6 - \tfrac{2}{3}}{1 - \tfrac{2}{3}} = \frac{\tfrac{16}{3}}{\tfrac{1}{3}} = 16
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
B &= \left.\frac{6 - \tfrac{1}{3}z^{-1}}{1 - \tfrac{1}{2}z^{-1}}\right|_{z^{-1}=3} = \frac{6 - 1}{1 - \tfrac{3}{2}} = \frac{5}{-\tfrac{1}{2}} = -10
\end{aligned}
$$

So:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y(z) = \frac{16}{1 - \tfrac{1}{2}z^{-1}} - \frac{10}{1 - \tfrac{1}{3}z^{-1}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Inverse Z-Transform using standard pair $\mathcal{Z}\{a^n u[n]\} = \dfrac{1}{1 - az^{-1}}$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
y[n] = 16\left(\tfrac{1}{2}\right)^n u[n] - 10\left(\tfrac{1}{3}\right)^n u[n]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification at $n=0$:** $y[0] = 16 - 10 = 6$. Check via LCCDE: $y[0] = 5(1)^0 + \tfrac{1}{2}(2) = 5 + 1 = 6$ ✓

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Reduction of Block Diagrams to Transfer Functions

**Problem:** For the following LCCDE, draw the block diagram in Direct Form I, then reduce to Direct Form II.

$$y[n] = 0.5 y[n-1] - 0.3 y[n-2] + x[n] + 2 x[n-1]$$

**Step 1: Identify the LCCDE form.**

Rewrite as: $y[n] - 0.5 y[n-1] + 0.3 y[n-2] = x[n] + 2 x[n-1]$

So $a_0 = 1,\; a_1 = -0.5,\; a_2 = 0.3,\; b_0 = 1,\; b_1 = 2$.

**Step 2: Express the transfer function.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(z) = \frac{1 + 2 z^{-1}}{1 - 0.5 z^{-1} + 0.3 z^{-2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Direct Form I structure** — cascade of an **all-zero** (FIR) section followed by an **all-pole** (IIR) section.

**Step 4: Direct Form II** — invert the order and share the delay line; results in a **canonical** form using only $\max(M,N)$ delay elements (here 2 delays instead of 4 in Direct Form I).

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Direct Form I Realization

This corresponds to the LCCDE: $y[n] = -a_1 y[n-1] - a_2 y[n-2] + b_0 x[n] + b_1 x[n-1] + b_2 x[n-2]$.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Xin["x[n]"] --> SUM1(("+"))
    SUM1 --> MULB0["× b0"]
    MULB0 --> SUM2(("+"))
    SUM2 --> DELAY1["z^-1"]:::delay
    DELAY1 --> DELAY2["z^-1"]:::delay
    DELAY1 --> MULB1["× b1"]
    MULB2["× b2"] --> SUM2
    DELAY2 --> MULB2
    DELAY2 --> DELAYX2["z^-1"]:::delay
    DELAYX2 --> DELAYX3["z^-1"]:::delay
    SUM2 --> MULA1["× a1"]
    MULA2["× a2"] --> SUM1
    DELAYX3 --> MULA2
    SUM2 --> Yout["y[n]"]
    MULB1 --> SUM2
    MULA1 --> SUM1

    classDef delay fill:#fff5e6,stroke:#ff9933,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide:** The upper signal path processes $x[n]$ through the **feed-forward** (numerator $b_k$) coefficients, while the lower feedback path processes $y[n]$ through the **feedback** (denominator $a_k$) coefficients.

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Direct Form II (Canonical) Realization

This is the **canonical form** — minimum number of delay elements = $\max(M, N)$.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Xin["x[n]"] --> ADDA(("+"))
    ADDA --> DELAY1["w[n] → z^-1"]:::delay
    DELAY1 --> DELAY2["w[n-1] → z^-1"]:::delay
    DELAY1 --> MULB0["× b0"]
    DELAY1 --> MULB1["× b1"]
    DELAY2 --> MULB2["× b2"]
    MULB0 --> ADDB(("+"))
    MULB1 --> ADDB
    MULB2 --> ADDB
    ADDB --> Yout["y[n]"]
    DELAY1 --> MULA1["× -a1"]
    DELAY2 --> MULA2["× -a2"]
    MULA1 --> ADDA
    MULA2 --> ADDA

    classDef delay fill:#e6f2ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note:** Direct Form II uses only **N delays** (the order of the system), while Direct Form I uses $M + N$ delays. For a 2nd-order system, Direct Form II saves 2 delay elements — a critical resource in hardware DSP chips.

---

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Cascade and Parallel Realizations

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CASCADE["Cascade Form: H(z) = H1(z) × H2(z)"]
        Xin["x[n]"] --> H1["H1(z) First-Order Section"] --> H2["H2(z) Second-Order Section"] --> Yout["y[n]"]
    end

    subgraph PARALLEL["Parallel Form: H(z) = H1(z) + H2(z)"]
        Xin2["x[n]"] --> SPLIT((("+")))
        SPLIT --> H1P["H1(z) Section"] --> ADDP(("+"))
        SPLIT --> H2P["H2(z) Section"] --> ADDP
        ADDP --> Yout2["y[n]"]
    end
```

---

### 4.4 Master Architecture Flow — Complete Discrete-Time LTI System Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Step 1: Write LCCDE in standard form"]:::step --> B["Step 2: Apply Unilateral Z-Transform"]
    B --> C["Step 3: Use Time-Shift Property with Initial Conditions"]:::step
    C --> D["Step 4: Algebraically Solve for Y(z)"]
    D --> E["Step 5: Express H(z) = Y(z)/X(z) when ICs=0"]:::step
    E --> F["Step 6: Draw Block Diagram Direct Form I or II"]:::step
    F --> G["Step 7: Partial Fraction Expansion of Y(z)"]
    G --> H["Step 8: Inverse Z-Transform to get y[n]"]:::step

    classDef step fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#009933,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```
