## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 📝 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`**
Define the **transfer function** of a discrete-time LTI system. What conditions must the Region of Convergence (ROC) of $H(z)$ satisfy for the system to be (i) causal and (ii) stable?

**Model Answer:**

The **transfer function** of a discrete-time LTI system is defined as the ratio of the Z-transform of the output to the Z-transform of the input, assuming zero initial conditions:

$$H(z) = \frac{Y(z)}{X(z)}$$

**Conditions on ROC:**

| Property | ROC Condition |
|---|---|
| **(i) Causal** | ROC is the **exterior of a circle**, including $z = \infty$ (i.e., ROC: $\vert z \vert > r_{\max}$, where $r_{\max}$ is the outermost pole magnitude) |
| **(ii) Stable** | ROC must **include the unit circle** $\vert z \vert = 1$ |

A causal AND stable system therefore requires **all poles strictly inside** the unit circle: $\vert p_k \vert < 1 \; \forall k$.

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`**
State and explain the **time-shift property** of the **unilateral Z-transform**. Why is this property crucial for solving difference equations with non-zero initial conditions?

**Model Answer:**

The **time-shift property** of the unilateral Z-transform states:

$$\mathcal{Z}\{x[n-k]\} = z^{-k}X(z) + \sum_{n=0}^{k-1} x[n]\,z^{-(k-n)}, \quad k \ge 1$$

where $X(z) = \mathcal{Z}\{x[n]\}$.

**Why crucial for non-zero initial conditions:**
Unlike the bilateral Z-transform, the unilateral Z-transform retains the **summation of initial-condition terms** $\sum_{n=0}^{k-1} x[n]\,z^{-(k-n)}$. These terms automatically incorporate the system's past state (samples at $n = -1, -2, \ldots$) into the algebraic equation for $Y(z)$, enabling the complete solution — both the zero-input and zero-state responses — in a single pass.

---

### 📝 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

## **Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`**

**(a)** Solve the following difference equation using the unilateral Z-transform for $n \ge 0$:

$$y[n+2] - \tfrac{1}{4} y[n] = x[n+2] - x[n+1]$$

given that $x[n] = \delta[n]$, $y[-2] = 1$, and $y[-1] = 0$.  **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Determine the **transfer function** $H(z)$ of the discrete-time LTI system described by:

$$y[n] = \tfrac{1}{2} y[n-1] - \tfrac{1}{4} y[n-2] + 3 x[n] - 2 x[n-1] + x[n-2]$$

State whether the system is **stable** and **causal**. Draw the **Direct Form II** block diagram. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Part A(a):**

**Step 1:** Rewrite the difference equation with delayed index shift for easier Z-transform application. Replace $n+2 \to n$ and $n+1 \to n$ in the time-shift formulas.

Define $n' = n+2$, so the equation becomes:
$y[n] - \tfrac{1}{4} y[n-2] = x[n] - x[n-1]$ (renaming $n' \to n$).

**Step 2:** Apply unilateral Z-transform.

Use $\mathcal{Z}\{y[n-2]\} = z^{-2}Y(z) + y[-1]z^{-1} + y[-2]$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y(z) - \tfrac{1}{4}\left[z^{-2}Y(z) + y[-1]z^{-1} + y[-2]\right] &= X(z) - z^{-1}X(z) - x[-1]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute initial conditions $y[-1] = 0$, $y[-2] = 1$, and $x[n] = \delta[n] \Rightarrow x[-1] = 0$, $X(z) = 1$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y(z) - \tfrac{1}{4}\left[z^{-2}Y(z) + 0 + 1\right] &= 1 - z^{-1}(1) - 0
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y(z)\left[1 - \tfrac{1}{4}z^{-2}\right] - \tfrac{1}{4} &= 1 - z^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $Y(z)$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y(z) &= \frac{1 - z^{-1} + \tfrac{1}{4}}{1 - \tfrac{1}{4}z^{-2}} = \frac{\tfrac{5}{4} - z^{-1}}{1 - \tfrac{1}{4}z^{-2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Factor the denominator as $(1 - \tfrac{1}{2}z^{-1})(1 + \tfrac{1}{2}z^{-1})$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y(z) &= \frac{\tfrac{5}{4} - z^{-1}}{(1 - \tfrac{1}{2}z^{-1})(1 + \tfrac{1}{2}z^{-1})}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6:** Partial fraction expansion.

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y(z) = \frac{A}{1 - \tfrac{1}{2}z^{-1}} + \frac{B}{1 + \tfrac{1}{2}z^{-1}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Solving: $A = \dfrac{\tfrac{5}{4} - 2}{1 + 1} = \dfrac{-\tfrac{3}{4}}{2} = -\tfrac{3}{8}$, and $B = \dfrac{\tfrac{5}{4} + 2}{1 - 1} \to$ re-evaluate using cover-up with a regular approach.

Recompute: multiply out — $A(1 + \tfrac{1}{2}z^{-1}) + B(1 - \tfrac{1}{2}z^{-1}) = \tfrac{5}{4} - z^{-1}$.

Setting $z^{-1} = 2$: $A(1 + 1) = \tfrac{5}{4} - 2 = -\tfrac{3}{4}$, so $A = -\tfrac{3}{8}$.
Setting $z^{-1} = -2$: $B(1 + 1) = \tfrac{5}{4} + 2 = \tfrac{13}{4}$, so $B = \tfrac{13}{8}$.

**Step 7:** Inverse Z-transform.

$$
\begin{aligned}
y[n] = -\tfrac{3}{8}\left(\tfrac{1}{2}\right)^n u[n] + \tfrac{13}{8}\left(-\tfrac{1}{2}\right)^n u[n]
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### **Model Solution — Part A(b):**

**Step 1:** Standardize the LCCDE.

$y[n] - \tfrac{1}{2}y[n-1] + \tfrac{1}{4}y[n-2] = 3x[n] - 2x[n-1] + x[n-2]$.

**Step 2:** Apply Z-transform with zero initial conditions.

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(z) = \frac{3 - 2z^{-1} + z^{-2}}{1 - \tfrac{1}{2}z^{-1} + \tfrac{1}{4}z^{-2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Convert to positive powers: $H(z) = \dfrac{3z^2 - 2z + 1}{z^2 - \tfrac{1}{2}z + \tfrac{1}{4}}$.

**Step 4:** Find poles (roots of $z^2 - \tfrac{1}{2}z + \tfrac{1}{4} = 0$): $z = \tfrac{1/2 \pm \sqrt{1/4 - 1}}{2} = \tfrac{1/4 \pm j\sqrt{3}/4}{1} = \tfrac{1}{4} \pm j\tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{4}$.

Pole magnitude: $\sqrt{(1/4)^2 + (\sqrt{3}/4)^2} = \sqrt{1/16 + 3/16} = \sqrt{4/16} = \tfrac{1}{2} < 1$.

**Conclusion:** All poles are **inside the unit circle**, so the system is **stable**. Since the system is described by a causal LCCDE, the ROC includes $\infty$, so the system is **causal**.

**Direct Form II Block Diagram** (refer to Section 4.2 above for the structural template; with $b_0 = 3$, $b_1 = -2$, $b_2 = 1$, $a_1 = -1/2$, $a_2 = 1/4$).

---

## **Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]` — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** For an LTI system governed by $y[n] = 0.6 y[n-1] - 0.08 y[n-2] + x[n] - 0.5 x[n-1]$:
- (i) Determine the transfer function $H(z)$.
- (ii) Find the impulse response $h[n]$ using the unilateral Z-transform.  **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Represent the LCCDE in **Cascade Form** and **Parallel Form**. Compare the two structures.  **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Part B(a):**

**(i)** Standardize: $y[n] - 0.6 y[n-1] + 0.08 y[n-2] = x[n] - 0.5 x[n-1]$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(z) = \frac{1 - 0.5 z^{-1}}{1 - 0.6 z^{-1} + 0.08 z^{-2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Pole equation: $z^2 - 0.6z + 0.08 = 0 \Rightarrow z = \dfrac{0.6 \pm \sqrt{0.36 - 0.32}}{2} = \dfrac{0.6 \pm 0.2}{2}$, so $z = 0.4$ and $z = 0.2$. Both poles are real, distinct, and inside the unit circle.

**(ii)** For impulse response, $x[n] = \delta[n] \Rightarrow X(z) = 1$. Thus $H(z) = Y(z)/1 = H(z)$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(z) = \frac{1 - 0.5 z^{-1}}{(1 - 0.4 z^{-1})(1 - 0.2 z^{-1})}
\end{aligned}
$$

Partial fractions: $H(z) = \dfrac{A}{1 - 0.4 z^{-1}} + \dfrac{B}{1 - 0.2 z^{-1}}$.

Cover-up at $z^{-1} = 2.5$: $A = \dfrac{1 - 0.5(2.5)}{1 - 0.2(2.5)} = \dfrac{-0.25}{0.5} = -0.5$.
Cover-up at $z^{-1} = 5$: $B = \dfrac{1 - 0.5(5)}{1 - 0.4(5)} = \dfrac{-1.5}{-1} = 1.5$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
h[n] = \left[-0.5 (0.4)^n + 1.5 (0.2)^n\right] u[n]
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### **Model Solution — Part B(b):**

**Cascade Form:** Factor $H(z)$ into 1st-order sections:

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(z) = H_1(z) \cdot H_2(z) = \left(\frac{1 - 0.5 z^{-1}}{1 - 0.4 z^{-1}}\right)\left(\frac{1}{1 - 0.2 z^{-1}}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

This is a cascade of two 1st-order sections, each realizable with 1 delay element. (Block diagram: see Section 4.3.)

**Parallel Form:** Use partial-fraction expansion directly (without combining):

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(z) = \frac{-0.5}{1 - 0.4 z^{-1}} + \frac{1.5}{1 - 0.2 z^{-1}}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is a sum (parallel connection) of two 1st-order sections.

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Cascade Form | Parallel Form |
|---|---|---|
| Structure | Series connection of sections | Parallel connection of sections |
| Sensitivity to coefficient quantization | **High** (poles/zeros coupled) | **Lower** (sections independent) |
| Implementation | Simple modular building blocks | Requires additional adders |
| Preferred for | Real-time DSP with simple sections | Fixed-coefficient filter banks |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the initial condition terms** when applying the unilateral Z-transform to shifted signals — this alone costs 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Mixing up bilateral and unilateral time-shift properties** — the unilateral form has the *extra* summation term; the bilateral form does NOT.
> 3. **Skipping the ROC analysis** — KTU 2024 paper explicitly tests whether the student can state ROC for **causality** and **stability**.
> 4. **Not verifying partial fraction coefficients** — always multiply back to confirm $A + B$ equals the constant term.
> 5. **Block diagrams without proper labeling** — every delay block MUST be labeled $z^{-1}$, and every multiplier block MUST show the coefficient.
> 6. **In Direct Form I vs II confusion** — Direct Form II uses ONE shared delay line; Direct Form I uses TWO separate delay lines (one for input, one for output).

---

### 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 4**

- ✅ **Transfer function** $H(z) = Y(z)/X(z)$ is defined **only for LTI systems** and **only under zero initial conditions**.
- ✅ **Unilateral Z-transform** sums from $n = 0$ to $\infty$; ROC is **always outside** the outermost pole for right-sided signals.
- ✅ **Time-shift property** of unilateral Z-transform includes the **initial-condition summation** — do NOT drop it.
- ✅ **General LCCDE form**: $\sum a_k y[n-k] = \sum b_k x[n-k]$, normalized with $a_0 = 1$.
- ✅ **Transfer function** = (numerator polynomial in $z^{-1}$ from feed-forward) / (denominator polynomial in $z^{-1}$ from feedback).
- ✅ **Causality** ⟹ ROC includes $z = \infty$. **Stability** ⟹ ROC includes unit circle $\vert z \vert = 1$.
- ✅ **Direct Form I** uses $M + N$ delays. **Direct Form II** uses $\max(M, N)$ delays (canonical).
- ✅ **Cascade form** = product of 1st/2nd-order sections. **Parallel form** = sum of 1st/2nd-order sections.
- ✅ Standard Z-pairs: $\delta[n] \leftrightarrow 1$, $u[n] \leftrightarrow \dfrac{1}{1 - z^{-1}}$, $a^n u[n] \leftrightarrow \dfrac{1}{1 - az^{-1}}$.
- ✅ Always verify pole locations and ROC for **causality + stability** conclusions in KTU answers.
- ✅ When solving LCCDEs by unilateral Z-transform, structure your answer in 3 stages: **Transform → Algebra → Inverse Transform**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Interconnection of LTI Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST416 - SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Interconnection of LTI Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of Linear Time-Invariant (LTI) systems, an **interconnection** refers to a structured composition of two or more LTI sub-systems linked together through basic operations — **cascade (series)**, **parallel**, or **feedback** — to form a composite, higher-order LTI system. Because the sub-systems themselves are LTI, the composite is also LTI, and its behavior can be described by a single, unified **impulse response** $h(t)$ or **transfer function** $H(s)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Interconnection of LTI systems is foundational for the study of control systems, digital filters (FIR/IIR), and state-space realizations. The three canonical interconnections (cascade, parallel, feedback) are sufficient to build **any** arbitrary block diagram.

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy — "The Water Pipeline Network"

Imagine a water treatment plant made of three sub-units:
- **Cascade (Series):** Water flows through *Filter A* then *Filter B* in a line. The output of A becomes the input of B. The overall system "filters twice."
- **Parallel:** Water is split into two parallel pipes, each with its own filter, and the outputs are mixed back. The overall effect is the *sum* of the two filters acting on the same input.
- **Feedback:** A *flow meter* at the output sends a corrective signal back to the inlet valve. If flow is too high, the valve closes a bit — the system regulates itself using its own output.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical constants / standard metrics to remember:**
> - **Impulse response** $h(t)$ — units depend on system (e.g., V/V for voltage amplifiers, m/s per N for mechanical systems).
> - **Transfer function** $H(s)$ — dimensionless in many systems; otherwise measured as *(output unit)/(input unit)*.
> - **Region of Convergence (ROC)** — must be the *intersection* of individual ROCs for cascade/parallel; restricted further for feedback.

## 1.3 Why Does This Matter?

In engineering practice, we rarely build a single monolithic filter. Instead, we **cascade** simple first-order sections to form higher-order Butterworth/Chebyshev filters, we **parallel** filters to implement odd/even decompositions, and we use **feedback** to stabilize amplifiers, implement oscillators, and design phase-locked loops (PLLs).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Canonical Interconnections

Let the two LTI sub-systems be characterized by impulse responses $h_1(t)$, $h_2(t)$ and transfer functions $H_1(s)$, $H_2(s)$.

### (A) Cascade (Series) Interconnection
The output of $H_1$ feeds the input of $H_2$:
- **Time domain:** $y(t) = \bigl(h_1 * h_2\bigr)(t)$ — convolution is *commutative* and *associative*.
- **Frequency domain:** $H(s) = H_1(s) \cdot H_2(s)$ — simple multiplication of transfer functions.

> [!NOTE]
> The order of cascading two LTI systems does **not** affect the overall response. This is a unique property of LTI systems and is heavily tested in KTU exams.

### (B) Parallel Interconnection
Both systems receive the *same* input; their outputs are summed:
- **Time domain:** $h(t) = h_1(t) + h_2(t)$.
- **Frequency domain:** $H(s) = H_1(s) + H_2(s)$.

### (C) Feedback Interconnection
A portion of the output is routed back (typically through $H_2$) and subtracted from the input (negative feedback). This is the cornerstone of control theory.
- **Closed-loop transfer function (negative feedback):**
$$H(s) = \frac{H_1(s)}{1 + H_1(s)\,H_2(s)}$$
- **Closed-loop transfer function (positive feedback):**
$$H(s) = \frac{H_1(s)}{1 - H_1(s)\,H_2(s)}$$

## 2.2 Stability and Causality Rules

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Stability:** An interconnection of LTI systems is **BIBO stable** *if and only if* its overall impulse response is absolutely integrable. In the $s$-domain, the ROC of $H(s)$ must include the $j\omega$-axis ($\text{Re}(s) = 0$).
> - **Causality:** For a causal LTI interconnection, the ROC of $H(s)$ must be a right-half plane $\text{Re}(s) > \alpha$.
> - **Invertibility:** A system with transfer function $H(s)$ is invertible *iff* $H(s) \neq 0$ over the entire $j\omega$-axis. Its inverse has transfer function $1/H(s)$.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Interconnection Type** | **Overall Impulse Response $h(t)$** | **Overall Transfer Function $H(s)$** | **Stability Condition** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Cascade / Series | $h(t) = (h_1 * h_2)(t)$ | $H(s) = H_1(s)\,H_2(s)$ | $\text{ROC} = \text{ROC}_1 \cap \text{ROC}_2$ contains $j\omega$ |
| Parallel | $h(t) = h_1(t) + h_2(t)$ | $H(s) = H_1(s) + H_2(s)$ | $\text{ROC} = \text{ROC}_1 \cap \text{ROC}_2$ contains $j\omega$ |
| Negative Feedback | $h(t) = h_1(t) - (h_1 * h_2 * h)(t)$ | $H(s) = \dfrac{H_1(s)}{1 + H_1(s)\,H_2(s)}$ | All poles of $H(s)$ in LHP |
| Positive Feedback | $h(t) = h_1(t) + (h_1 * h_2 * h)(t)$ | $H(s) = \dfrac{H_1(s)}{1 - H_1(s)\,H_2(s)}$ | All poles of $H(s)$ in LHP |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Cascade is used in designing *higher-order filters* (e.g., 4th-order Butterworth as two cascaded 2nd-order sections) to improve numerical robustness. Parallel is used in *multi-band filter banks* (low-pass + high-pass decomposition). Feedback is the heart of every *op-amp, servo-motor, PLL, and cruise-control system*.

## 2.4 Why These Rules Work — The Logical Flow

1. **Linearity** allows us to use superposition in parallel interconnections (add the outputs).
2. **Time-invariance** allows us to *commute* the order of cascade stages without changing the overall response.
3. **The feedback equation** is itself a linear equation in $Y(s)$ that can be solved algebraically — it would not be solvable in closed form for a *non-linear* system.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Cascade (Series) Interconnection

Let the input to $H_1$ be $x(t)$, its output be $w(t)$, and the final output be $y(t)$.

By definition of LTI convolution:
$$w(t) = (h_1 * x)(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} h_1(\tau)\,x(t-\tau)\,d\tau$$

Then $w(t)$ is fed to $H_2$, giving:
$$y(t) = (h_2 * w)(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} h_2(\lambda)\,w(t-\lambda)\,d\lambda$$

Substitute the first equation into the second:
$$y(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} h_2(\lambda)\,\left[\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} h_1(\tau)\,x(t-\lambda-\tau)\,d\tau\right] d\lambda$$

Swap the order of integration (Fubini's theorem, valid for LTI systems with well-behaved impulse responses):
$$y(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty}\!\!\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} h_2(\lambda)\,h_1(\tau)\,x\bigl((t-\tau)-\lambda\bigr)\,d\tau\,d\lambda$$

Recognize the inner integral as a convolution of $h_1$ with $h_2$ evaluated at $(t-\tau)$. Define $h(t) = (h_1 * h_2)(t)$:
$$y(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} h(t-\tau)\,x(\tau)\,d\tau = (h * x)(t)$$

> Hence the overall impulse response is $h(t) = (h_1 * h_2)(t)$, and in the Laplace domain $H(s) = H_1(s)\cdot H_2(s)$.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Parallel Interconnection

Let $w_1(t) = (h_1 * x)(t)$ and $w_2(t) = (h_2 * x)(t)$. The output is the sum:
$$y(t) = w_1(t) + w_2(t)$$

By linearity of convolution:
$$y(t) = (h_1 * x)(t) + (h_2 * x)(t)$$

Factor the common input $x(t)$ (distributive property of convolution):
$$y(t) = \bigl[(h_1 + h_2) * x\bigr](t)$$

Therefore, the equivalent impulse response is:
$$h(t) = h_1(t) + h_2(t)$$

In the Laplace domain: $H(s) = H_1(s) + H_2(s)$.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Negative Feedback Interconnection

For negative feedback, the signal reaching $H_1$ is $e(t) = x(t) - (h_2 * y)(t)$. Then:
$$y(t) = (h_1 * e)(t) = (h_1 * x)(t) - (h_1 * h_2 * y)(t)$$

Take the **unilateral Laplace transform** of every term (using convolution property $\mathcal{L}\{f*g\} = F(s)G(s)$):
$$Y(s) = H_1(s)\,X(s) - H_1(s)\,H_2(s)\,Y(s)$$

Collect $Y(s)$ terms on the left-hand side:
$$Y(s) + H_1(s)\,H_2(s)\,Y(s) = H_1(s)\,X(s)$$

Factor out $Y(s)$:
$$Y(s)\,\bigl[1 + H_1(s)\,H_2(s)\bigr] = H_1(s)\,X(s)$$

Divide both sides to obtain the closed-loop transfer function:
$$H(s) = \frac{Y(s)}{X(s)} = \frac{H_1(s)}{1 + H_1(s)\,H_2(s)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign Convention:** If the feedback adder has a *minus* sign, the denominator is $1 + H_1H_2$. If it has a *plus* sign (positive feedback), the denominator is $1 - H_1H_2$. A single sign error here loses 7 full marks in a KTU 14-mark question.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** Two LTI systems with impulse responses $h_1(t) = e^{-2t}\,u(t)$ and $h_2(t) = e^{-3t}\,u(t)$ are connected in **parallel**. Find the overall impulse response and verify stability.

**Step 1 — Time-domain solution:**
$$h(t) = h_1(t) + h_2(t) = e^{-2t}\,u(t) + e^{-3t}\,u(t)$$

**Step 2 — Verify stability (absolute integrability):**
$$\int_{0}^{\infty} \vert e^{-2t} + e^{-3t} \vert\,dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-2t}\,dt + \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-3t}\,dt = \frac{1}{2} + \frac{1}{3} = \frac{5}{6} < \infty$$

Since the integral is finite, the system is **BIBO stable**. $\blacksquare$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Cascade (Series) Interconnection

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcX["X OF T"] --> sysA["H1 S"]
    sysA --> sysB["H2 S"]
    sysB --> outY["Y OF T"]
```

**Interpretation:** The signal flows unidirectionally through $H_1$ then $H_2$. Equivalent impulse response: $h(t) = h_1(t) * h_2(t)$.

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Parallel Interconnection

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcX["X OF T"] --> splitA["JUNCTION"]
    splitA --> sysA["H1 S"]
    splitA --> sysB["H2 S"]
    sysA --> sumN["SUMMER"]
    sysB --> sumN
    sumN --> outY["Y OF T"]
```

**Interpretation:** The input is replicated at a junction; the two sub-system outputs are summed algebraically. Equivalent impulse response: $h(t) = h_1(t) + h_2(t)$.

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Negative Feedback Interconnection

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcX["X OF T"] --> sumN["COMPARATOR MINUS"]
    sumN --> sysA["H1 S FORWARD PATH"]
    sysA --> outY["Y OF T"]
    outY --> tapA["TAKE OUTPUT"]
    tapA --> sysB["H2 S FEEDBACK PATH"]
    sysB --> sumN
```

**Interpretation:** The output $Y(s)$ is sensed, passed through $H_2(s)$, and subtracted from the input at the comparator. Closed-loop transfer function: $H(s) = \dfrac{H_1(s)}{1 + H_1(s)H_2(s)}$.

## 4.4 Combined Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    startA["INPUT X OF T"] --> choiceA{"INTERCONNECTION TYPE"}
    choiceA -- CASCADE --> nodeB["CONVOLVE H1 AND H2"]
    choiceA -- PARALLEL --> nodeC["ADD H1 AND H2"]
    choiceA -- NEG FB --> nodeD["FORM H1 OVER 1 PLUS H1 H2"]
    nodeB --> nodeE["APPLY LAPLACE TRANSFORM"]
    nodeC --> nodeE
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF["CHECK ROC INCLUDES JW AXIS"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["OUTPUT H OF T AND STABILITY FLAG"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> This functional flow chart summarizes the complete KTU evaluation procedure: identify the topology → apply the appropriate algebraic rule → transform to $s$-domain → verify ROC for stability.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]
**Q: Define the impulse response of an LTI system. State the condition under which two LTI systems connected in cascade have the same overall impulse response irrespective of their order.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- The impulse response $h(t)$ of an LTI system is its output when the input is a unit impulse $\delta(t)$. *(1 mark)*
- For a cascade of two LTI systems with impulse responses $h_1(t)$ and $h_2(t)$, the overall impulse response is the convolution $h(t) = h_1(t) * h_2(t)$. *(1 mark)*
- Since convolution is *commutative* for LTI systems ($h_1 * h_2 = h_2 * h_1$), swapping the order of the two systems yields the same overall impulse response. This is **not** true for non-LTI systems. *(1 mark)*

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Q: Two LTI systems with impulse responses $h_1(t) = \delta(t-1)$ and $h_2(t) = \delta(t-2)$ are connected in parallel. Find the overall impulse response.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- For parallel interconnection, $h(t) = h_1(t) + h_2(t)$. *(1 mark)*
- Substituting: $h(t) = \delta(t-1) + \delta(t-2)$. *(1 mark)*
- This represents a system that produces two unit impulses at $t = 1$ s and $t = 2$ s. Since $\delta(\cdot)$ has finite energy and bounded support, the system is BIBO stable. *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — July 2024, Module 4 Pattern]

**(a)** Two LTI systems with transfer functions $H_1(s) = \dfrac{1}{s+1}$ and $H_2(s) = \dfrac{1}{s+2}$ are connected in **cascade**. Determine:
   (i) the overall transfer function, and
   (ii) the overall impulse response.
   **Verify the stability** of the system. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An LTI system has forward-path transfer function $G(s) = \dfrac{2}{s+3}$ and unity negative feedback $H(s) = 1$. Find:
   (i) the closed-loop transfer function, and
   (ii) the closed-loop impulse response.
   **Comment on stability** using pole locations. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Solution (7 marks):**

(i) **Overall transfer function** (using cascade rule $H(s) = H_1(s) \cdot H_2(s)$): *(1 mark)*
$$H(s) = \frac{1}{s+1} \cdot \frac{1}{s+2} = \frac{1}{(s+1)(s+2)} = \frac{1}{s^{2} + 3s + 2}$$

(ii) **Overall impulse response** — perform partial fraction expansion: *(4 marks)*
$$H(s) = \frac{1}{(s+1)(s+2)} = \frac{A}{s+1} + \frac{B}{s+2}$$

Solving for $A$: set $s = -1$ $\Rightarrow A = \dfrac{1}{-1+2} = 1$.

Solving for $B$: set $s = -2$ $\Rightarrow B = \dfrac{1}{-2+1} = -1$.

Therefore:
$$H(s) = \frac{1}{s+1} - \frac{1}{s+2}$$

Taking the inverse Laplace transform (using the standard pair $\mathcal{L}^{-1}\!\left\{\dfrac{1}{s+a}\right\} = e^{-at}\,u(t)$): *(1 mark)*
$$h(t) = \bigl(e^{-t} - e^{-2t}\bigr)\,u(t)$$

(iii) **Stability check:** *(1 mark)*

Both poles are at $s = -1$ and $s = -2$, both in the **Left-Half Plane (LHP)**. Equivalently, $\int_{0}^{\infty}\vert e^{-t} - e^{-2t} \vert\,dt < \infty$. The system is **BIBO stable**. $\blacksquare$

---

**Part (b) — Solution (7 marks):**

(i) **Closed-loop transfer function** using the negative-feedback formula: *(2 marks)*
$$T(s) = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)} = \frac{\frac{2}{s+3}}{1 + \frac{2}{s+3}\cdot 1}$$

Multiply numerator and denominator by $(s+3)$:
$$T(s) = \frac{2}{s+3 + 2} = \frac{2}{s+5}$$

(ii) **Closed-loop impulse response:** *(3 marks)*
$$h(t) = 2\,e^{-5t}\,u(t)$$

(iii) **Stability comment:** *(2 marks)*

The single closed-loop pole is at $s = -5$, which lies in the LHP ($\text{Re}(s) = -5 < 0$). The ROC is $\text{Re}(s) > -5$, which *includes* the $j\omega$-axis. Hence, the closed-loop system is **asymptotically stable** and **BIBO stable**. The feedback has *improved* the speed of response (pole moved from $-3$ to $-5$) — a classic demonstration of how negative feedback increases bandwidth at the cost of reduced DC gain. $\blacksquare$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice* [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

**(a)** Define the **interconnection of LTI systems**. With the help of block diagrams, explain the **cascade** and **parallel** interconnections. Derive the overall impulse response and transfer function for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Two LTI systems with impulse responses $h_1(t) = e^{-t}\,u(t)$ and $h_2(t) = \delta(t)$ are connected in **negative feedback**. Find:
   (i) the closed-loop transfer function, and
   (ii) the closed-loop impulse response. Is the system stable? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Solution (7 marks):**

*Definition (2 marks):* The interconnection of LTI systems refers to combining two or more LTI sub-systems in standardized topologies — cascade, parallel, or feedback — to realize a composite LTI system whose overall behavior is described by a single impulse response $h(t)$ or transfer function $H(s)$.

*Cascade interconnection (2 marks):* Output of $H_1$ feeds the input of $H_2$. Overall: $H(s) = H_1(s)\cdot H_2(s)$ and $h(t) = (h_1 * h_2)(t)$. The order is immaterial due to commutativity of convolution.

*Parallel interconnection (2 marks):* Both systems share the same input; outputs are summed. Overall: $H(s) = H_1(s) + H_2(s)$ and $h(t) = h_1(t) + h_2(t)$.

*Diagrammatic representation (1 mark):* Drawn as two functional blocks — series chain (cascade) and split-then-merge tree (parallel).

---

**Part (b) — Solution (7 marks):**

(i) **Transfer functions of the sub-systems** (taking Laplace transform of $h_1$ and $h_2$): *(2 marks)*
$$H_1(s) = \frac{1}{s+1}, \qquad H_2(s) = \mathcal{L}\{\delta(t)\} = 1$$

(ii) **Closed-loop transfer function** (negative feedback formula): *(2 marks)*
$$T(s) = \frac{H_1(s)}{1 + H_1(s)\,H_2(s)} = \frac{\frac{1}{s+1}}{1 + \frac{1}{s+1}\cdot 1} = \frac{\frac{1}{s+1}}{\frac{s+1+1}{s+1}} = \frac{1}{s+2}$$

(iii) **Closed-loop impulse response:** *(2 marks)*
$$h(t) = e^{-2t}\,u(t)$$

(iv) **Stability check:** *(1 mark)*

The single pole is at $s = -2 \in$ LHP. The system is **stable**. Note that $h_2(t) = \delta(t)$ represents an *instantaneous* feedback (no delay), which is why the closed-loop pole is shifted from $-1$ to $-2$ (faster response, same form). $\blacksquare$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Sign Error in Feedback:** Confusing negative and positive feedback formulas. The denominator is $1 + H_1H_2$ for *negative* feedback and $1 - H_1H_2$ for *positive* feedback. A single sign mistake can invert the answer entirely — losing all 7 marks in that sub-part.
> 2. **Poles on the $j\omega$ Axis:** If a pole lies *exactly* on the imaginary axis (e.g., $s = 0$), the system is **marginally stable**, *not* BIBO stable. The unit step response will grow unboundedly.
> 3. **Forgetting $u(t)$:** When writing impulse responses, always include the unit step $u(t)$ for causal systems. Omitting it costs a mark.
> 4. **Order of Cascade:** Although LTI systems commute, students sometimes over-complicate the answer by re-deriving the convolution. State the property and move on.
> 5. **ROC Neglect:** The Region of Convergence of a cascaded/parallel system is the *intersection* of individual ROCs. KTU specifically tests ROC analysis in 3-mark questions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Cascade:** $H(s) = H_1(s) \cdot H_2(s)$, $h(t) = h_1(t) * h_2(t)$. Order does not matter. ROC is the intersection.
- **Parallel:** $H(s) = H_1(s) + H_2(s)$, $h(t) = h_1(t) + h_2(t)$. ROC is the intersection.
- **Negative Feedback:** $H(s) = \dfrac{H_1(s)}{1 + H_1(s)H_2(s)}$. Stability requires all closed-loop poles in LHP.
- **Positive Feedback:** $H(s) = \dfrac{H_1(s)}{1 - H_1(s)H_2(s)}$. Rarely used — usually destabilizing.
- **Stability Rule:** ROC must include $j\omega$ axis $\Leftrightarrow$ all poles in LHP for causal systems.
- **Causality Rule:** ROC must be a right-half plane (of the form $\text{Re}(s) > \alpha$) for a causal LTI system.
- **Convolution Commutativity:** $h_1 * h_2 = h_2 * h_1$ — *only* for LTI systems. This is the theoretical reason cascade order is irrelevant.
- **Distributivity:** $h_1 * x + h_2 * x = (h_1 + h_2) * x$ — used to *combine* parallel blocks into one.
- **Inverse System:** Has transfer function $1/H(s)$, exists *iff* $H(s) \neq 0$ on the $j\omega$-axis.
- **Standard Test Signals:** Always verify stability by checking absolute integrability of $h(t)$ for an arbitrary bounded input, or by pole-location analysis in the $s$-plane.
- **Real-world Domains:** Control systems (servos, robotics), communication (matched filters, equalizers), audio (graphic equalizers, reverberators), and biomedical signal processing (ECG filters).

> **Final Exam Tip:** In every 14-mark question, allocate roughly **3 minutes** to draw the block diagram, **5 minutes** to derive the formula, and **4 minutes** for the numerical/numerical-illustrative part. Always end with a *stability comment* — it is a guaranteed 1–2 marks in KTU valuation.
